☸️ Kubernetes CKA — Certified Kubernetes Administrator

Complete Learning Guide with anihpj Django Project (jobpost app)

From complete beginner to CKA-certified Kubernetes administrator. Learn cluster architecture, installation, workloads, networking, storage, security, maintenance, troubleshooting, CRDs, operators, security hardening, and policies — all through hands-on examples with a real Django application.

🟢 Beginner Friendly ☸️ Kubernetes v1.31+ 🐍 Django / Python 📚 40 Chapters ⏱️ ~50 Hours 🎓 CKA Exam — 100% Coverage 📋 9 Appendices

🚀 Your CKA Learning Journey

🎯 Welcome! This guide takes you from complete beginner to CKA-certified Kubernetes administrator. Every concept is explained with real examples using the anihpj/jobpost Django project — you'll learn by doing, not just reading. Covers all CKA exam domains: Cluster Architecture (25%), Workloads & Scheduling (15%), Services & Networking (20%), Storage (10%), and Troubleshooting (30%).

📚 Complete Table of Contents

📚 🟢 Beginner

Part 1: Foundation

Understanding the Basics

What is Kubernetes, Docker & Containers, Architecture deep dive, Core Objects every admin must know.

Ch 1-4 ~7 hours
🛠️ 🟢 Beginner

Part 2: Getting Started

Setting Up and Deploying

Environment setup, mastering kubectl, writing YAML manifests, and deploying anihpj to Kubernetes.

Ch 5-8 ~8 hours
🏗️ 🟡 CKA 25%

Part 3: Cluster Installation

Building Clusters from Scratch

kubeadm clusters, kubeconfig management, etcd operations, and control plane administration.

Ch 9-12 ~6 hours
⚙️ 🟡 CKA 15%

Part 4: Workloads & Scheduling

Managing Applications

Deployments, scheduling, resource management, Jobs, CronJobs, DaemonSets, and StatefulSets.

Ch 13-16 ~5 hours
🌐 🟡 CKA 20%

Part 5: Services & Networking

Connecting Everything

Networking model, Services, DNS, CoreDNS, Ingress controllers, and Network Policies.

Ch 17-20 ~5 hours
💾 🟡 CKA 10%

Part 6: Storage

Persistent Data

Volumes, PersistentVolumes, PersistentVolumeClaims, StorageClasses, ConfigMaps, and Secrets.

Ch 21-23 ~4 hours
🔐 🟡 Intermediate

Part 7: Security

Securing the Cluster

RBAC, Service Accounts, TLS/PKI certificates, Pod Security, and Admission Controllers.

Ch 24-27 ~5 hours
🔧 🔴 Advanced

Part 8: Cluster Maintenance

Upgrades & Backups

Cluster upgrades with kubeadm, etcd backup and restore, node maintenance with drain and cordon.

Ch 28-30 ~4 hours
🔍 🔴 CKA 30%

Part 9: Troubleshooting

Debug Like a Pro

Debug pods, nodes, cluster components, logging, monitoring, and CKA exam troubleshooting scenarios.

Ch 31-34 ~4 hours
🚀 🔴 Advanced

Part 10: Production & Beyond

Enterprise Ready

HA clusters, managed Kubernetes (EKS/GKE/AKS), CI/CD pipelines, GitOps with ArgoCD, and Helm charts.

Ch 35-37 ~4 hours
🔧 🔴 Advanced

Part 11: Extending & Hardening

CRDs, Security & Policies

Custom Resource Definitions, Operators, API extensions, security hardening, Pod Security Standards, LimitRange, ResourceQuota, and policy engines.

Ch 38-40 ~5 hours

Master Summary & Implementation Roadmap

Your Complete CKA Certification Journey — From Zero to Certified Kubernetes Administrator

This comprehensive guide takes you from absolute beginner to CKA-certified Kubernetes administrator. With 11 Parts, 40 Chapters, 9 Appendices, and 400+ exam-style practice questions, you'll master cluster architecture, workloads, networking, security, storage, troubleshooting, production operations, CRDs & Operators, security hardening, and policy engines — all using a real Django project (anihpj/jobpost) as your hands-on example throughout.

📚 11 Learning Parts
📖 40 Chapters
📝 400+ Practice Questions
📎 9 Appendices
🎯 CKA Exam Ready
🚀 1 Real Project

📜 The Evolution That Led to Kubernetes

Understanding the journey from bare metal to orchestrated containers

2000s
Bare Metal Servers

One app per physical server. No isolation, massive hardware waste, manual scaling — anihpj would need its own dedicated box.

❌ Inefficient & rigid
2008-12
Virtual Machines

Hypervisors enabled multi-tenancy and better density. Still heavy — each VM runs a full OS kernel, slow to provision.

⚠️ Heavy overhead
2013-14
Docker Containers

Lightweight process-level isolation sharing the host kernel. Fast startup, portable images — but managing hundreds manually? Nightmare.

🟡 Missing orchestration
2014-15
Kubernetes Born

Google open-sources Borg's successor. CNCF founded. K8s becomes the industry-standard orchestrator for containers.

✅ Orchestration solved
2020+
Cloud-Native Era

EKS, GKE, AKS managed services. GitOps, service mesh, eBPF networking. The CKA certifies you to manage all of this.

🚀 You Are Here

🎯 CKA Exam Domain Coverage

Every domain is fully covered — here's how this guide maps to the actual exam

🔧 Troubleshooting (30%) Ch 28-34 · 70+ Questions
🏗️ Cluster Architecture (25%) Ch 1-6, 9-12, 38 · 120+ Questions
🌐 Services & Networking (20%) Ch 16-21 · 60+ Questions
📦 Workloads & Scheduling (15%) Ch 7-8, 13-15, 22-27, 39-40 · 120+ Questions
💾 Storage (10%) Ch 13-15 · 30+ Questions

📋 Prerequisites Before You Begin

💻 Basic Terminal/CLI — Comfortable with Linux command line
🐳 Docker Basics — Chapter 2 covers Docker fundamentals
🐍 Any Programming — Examples use Python/Django
📝 Text Editor — VS Code recommended (vim on exam)
💾 8GB+ RAM — For running local K8s cluster
🌐 Internet Access — For pulling images & docs

✨ What Makes This Guide Different

Not just another K8s tutorial — here's why this guide works

🏗️
Real Project Throughout

Every concept demonstrated with anihpj/jobpost — not abstract examples. You deploy a real Django application from scratch to production.

🎯
CKA Exam Aligned

400+ practice questions mapped to all 5 CKA domains. Every chapter includes exam tips, gotchas, and time-saving imperative commands.

📊
Progressive Complexity

Start with "What is Kubernetes?" and end with HA clusters, GitOps, CRDs & Operators, security hardening, and policy engines. Each chapter builds on the previous one naturally.

🔧
Hands-On, Not Theoretical

Every section includes kubectl commands, YAML manifests, and verification steps you can run on your own cluster. Learn by doing.

🧪
Full Exam Simulator

Appendix H provides a complete timed CKA exam simulation with real-world tasks across all 5 domains — your final readiness check.

🗺️
10-Week Study Plan

Appendix G maps out a detailed week-by-week study plan so you never wonder "what should I study next?"

⏱️
2 Hours

Exam duration — performance-based tasks in a live K8s environment

🎯
66% to Pass

Passing score — roughly 11 of ~17 questions must be correct

🌐
Open Book

kubernetes.io docs allowed — but speed matters, know where to find things

🔄
Free Retake

One free retake included — valid for 12 months from purchase

🏆 Kubernetes Certifications — Which One Is Right for You?

This guide covers CKA — but here's how all three CNCF certifications compare

Aspect 🎯 CKA (This Guide) 📦 CKAD 🔒 CKS
Focus Cluster administration & operations Application development on K8s Kubernetes security hardening
Duration 2 hours 2 hours 2 hours
Pass Score 66% 66% 67%
Prerequisites None None CKA required first
Key Topics kubeadm, etcd backup, networking, RBAC, cluster upgrades, troubleshooting Pods, Deployments, Services, ConfigMaps, volumes, health probes, Jobs Pod security, supply chain, audit logging, runtime security, network policies
Target Role SRE, Platform Engineer, DevOps Backend Developer, App Dev Security Engineer, SRE
Validity 3 years 3 years 2 years
💡 Recommended Certification Path

Start with CKA (this guide) to master the platform → then CKAD if you develop apps on K8s → finally CKS for security specialization. CKA is the foundation for all three certifications.

☸️ Kubernetes Architecture at a Glance

🌐 External
👤
Users & Traffic HTTP/HTTPS requests
🔧
kubectl CLI management tool
🔗
CI/CD Pipelines ArgoCD, GitHub Actions
🎛️ Control Plane
📡
API Server Central hub (port 6443)
💾
etcd Cluster state store
📋
Scheduler Pod placement
🔄
Controller Manager Reconciliation loops
⚙️ Worker Nodes
🤖
kubelet Node agent
🌐
kube-proxy Service networking
📦
containerd Container runtime
📦 Workloads
🫛
Pods Smallest unit
🔄
Deployments Stateless apps
🗄️
StatefulSets Databases
🔌
Services Load balancing

🔄 The Kubernetes Deployment Lifecycle

From code on your laptop to running in production — the complete pipeline this guide teaches

✍️
Write Code

Django app with models, views, and APIs

python manage.py runserver
🐳
Containerize

Dockerfile with multi-stage builds

docker build -t anihpj .
📝
Write YAML

Deployment, Service, Ingress manifests

vim deployment.yaml
🚀
Deploy

Apply manifests to the cluster

kubectl apply -f .
⚡
Running

Pods scheduled, health checks pass

kubectl get pods -w
📊
Monitor

Logs, metrics, alerts, autoscaling

kubectl logs -f pod/app

🗺️ Kubernetes Object Relationship Map

How the core Kubernetes objects connect — from user requests down to running containers

🌍 Ingress Routes HTTP(S) traffic by host/path rules
▼
routes to →
🔌 Service (ClusterIP) Internal LB — stable IP & DNS
🌐 Service (NodePort) Exposes on node's IP:port
⚖️ Service (LoadBalancer) Cloud external LB → Pod
▼
selects pods via labels →
🔄 Deployment Manages ReplicaSets
🗄️ StatefulSet Stable identities
🔁 DaemonSet One pod per node
⚡ Job / CronJob Batch tasks
▼
creates & manages →
🫛 Pod Smallest unit — containers sharing network & storage
▼
uses config & storage from →
⚙️ ConfigMap Non-sensitive config
🔒 Secret Sensitive data
💾 PVC → PV Persistent storage
🛡️ ServiceAccount Pod identity (RBAC)
🌐 How a User Request Flows Through Kubernetes
   USER REQUEST (https://jobs.example.com/api/posts)
   │
   ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  🌍 EXTERNAL LAYER                                               │
│  DNS → Load Balancer (cloud) → NodePort / Ingress Controller     │
└──────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
               │  :443 (TLS terminated)
               ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  🔀 INGRESS (Layer 7 Routing)                                    │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐         │
│  │  Rule: host=jobs.example.com, path=/api/*           │         │
│  │  → backend: anihpj-service:8000                     │         │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘         │
└──────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
               │  matched route
               ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  🔌 SERVICE (ClusterIP: 10.96.42.100:8000)                       │
│  ┌────────────┐                                                  │
│  │  Endpoints │→ 10.244.1.5:8000, 10.244.2.8:8000               │
│  │  (iptables)│  kube-proxy maintains rules                      │
│  └────────────┘                                                  │
└──────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
               │  round-robin to healthy pod
               ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  🫚 POD (10.244.1.5 — managed by Deployment/ReplicaSet)          │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐        │
│  │  📦 Container: anihpj (Django + Gunicorn)            │        │
│  │     ├── ConfigMap → DJANGO_SETTINGS, ALLOWED_HOSTS   │        │
│  │     ├── Secret → DATABASE_URL, SECRET_KEY            │        │
│  │     ├── PVC → /app/media (persistent storage)        │        │
│  │     └── ServiceAccount → RBAC permissions            │        │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘        │
│  Health: ✅ Liveness (/healthz) | ✅ Readiness (/ready)          │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
               │  Django processes the request
               ▼
          📤 HTTP 200 Response → User sees job listings
🚀

anihpj — Your Hands-On Project Throughout This Guide

A real-world Django job posting application that you'll containerize, deploy, scale, secure, and manage on Kubernetes

🐍 Python 3.x 🌐 Django 4.x 🗄️ SQLite → PostgreSQL 🐳 Docker ☸️ Kubernetes 🔒 RBAC & Secrets 📊 Monitoring 🔄 CI/CD (ArgoCD)
📁
Django + Models Job posting app with views, URLs, and admin — the app you'll deploy
🐳
Dockerfile Multi-stage build optimized for production — small image, fast startup
📝
K8s Manifests 13+ YAML files — Deployments, Services, ConfigMaps, Secrets, Ingress, HPA
🗄️
Database Layer Start with SQLite, migrate to PostgreSQL with PVCs and StatefulSets
🔐
Security Stack Secrets, RBAC, Network Policies, Pod Security Standards, encryption at rest
📈
Observability Prometheus metrics, centralized logging, health probes, resource monitoring

📈 Your CKA Learning Journey

Follow this structured path — 11 parts from beginner fundamentals to production mastery

1

🟢 Parts 1-3: Foundation & Basics

BEGINNER

Build a rock-solid foundation — understand Kubernetes, set up your cluster, master kubectl, and deploy your first application with proper configuration and storage.

📖 Ch 1
What is Kubernetes?

Why K8s exists, evolution from bare metal → containers → orchestration

Why K8sBenefitsUse Cases
📖 Ch 2
Docker Prerequisites

Containers vs VMs, Dockerfile for Django, essential Docker commands

DockerImagesCompose
📖 Ch 3
Kubernetes Architecture

Control plane vs worker nodes, 5 control plane components, 8-step pod creation

Control Planeetcdkubelet
📖 Ch 4
Core Kubernetes Objects

Pods, Deployments, ReplicaSets, Services, Namespaces, Labels

PodsDeploymentsServices
📖 Ch 5
Environment Setup

Minikube, kubectl, first cluster, kubeconfig, contexts

MinikubekubectlContexts
📖 Ch 6
kubectl Command Center

30+ essential commands, output formats, imperative vs declarative

CommandsJSON/YAMLFiltering
📖 Ch 7
YAML Configuration Files

YAML syntax, 4 required fields, multi-document YAML, validation

YAMLManifestsapiVersion
📖 Ch 8
Deploying anihpj

End-to-end: Namespace, ConfigMap, Secret, Deployment, Service

DeployDjangoFull Stack
📖 Ch 9
Cluster Setup with kubeadm

kubeadm init, join, networking, multi-node clusters from scratch

kubeadmInitJoin
📖 Ch 10
Control Plane Components

API server, scheduler, controller-manager, static pods, kubelet config

Static PodsManifestsConfig
📖 Ch 11
etcd — Cluster State Store

Backup, restore, snapshots, encryption at rest, TLS certs

BackupRestoreEncrypt
📖 Ch 12
Node Management

Cordon, drain, uncordon, OS upgrades, kubeadm upgrade workflow

DrainUpgradeMaintain
2

🟡 Parts 4-7: Intermediate Skills

INTERMEDIATE

Master storage, workloads, networking, scaling, deployment strategies, security, and observability — the CKA core.

📖 Ch 13
Volumes & Persistent Storage

emptyDir, hostPath, PV, PVC, StorageClasses, dynamic provisioning

PV/PVCStorageDynamic
📖 Ch 14
ConfigMaps & Secrets

Creation methods, consumption patterns, immutable configs, encoding

ConfigMapSecretsEnv
📖 Ch 15
Scheduling & Placement

nodeSelector, nodeAffinity, taints/tolerations, topology spread

AffinityTaintsPriority
📖 Ch 16

Flat network model, pod-to-pod, CoreDNS, CNI plugins

DNSCNINetwork
📖 Ch 17

ClusterIP, NodePort, LoadBalancer, ExternalName, Headless

ClusterIPNodePortLB
📖 Ch 18

Path/host routing, TLS termination, Ingress controllers

Ingress/egress rules, default deny, microsegmentation

NetPolDenyAllow
📖 Ch 20
Deployments & Strategies

Rolling updates, recreate, rollbacks, revision history

RollingRollbackHistory
📖 Ch 21

Liveness, readiness, startup probes; HTTP/TCP/Exec

ProbesLivenessReady
📖 Ch 22
Resource Management

Requests vs limits, QoS classes, Resource Quotas, LimitRanges

CPU/MemQoSQuotas
📖 Ch 23
Workload Types

StatefulSets, DaemonSets, Jobs, CronJobs, ReplicaSets

StatefulSetDaemonSetJobs
📖 Ch 24

Roles, ClusterRoles, Bindings, ServiceAccounts, least privilege

📖 Ch 25

Manual scaling, HPA (CPU/memory/custom), PDB

HPAScalePDB
📖 Ch 26

SecurityContext, Pod Security Standards, AppArmor, Seccomp

🔴 Parts 8-10: Advanced & Production

ADVANCED

Troubleshooting, logging, monitoring, multi-container patterns, HA clusters, cloud deployments, CI/CD, and Helm.

🛠️ How to Use This Guide — Implementation Strategy

A 6-step plan from opening the guide to passing the CKA exam

1

Read the Chapter Content

Each chapter explains concepts from first principles using the anihpj Django project as a concrete example. Read the theory, understand the "why" behind each Kubernetes object.

📖
Theory First

Understand concepts before touching the keyboard

🎯
Learning Objectives

Check the objectives at the start of each chapter

2

Practice the Code Examples

Every chapter includes real YAML manifests and kubectl commands. Don't just read — type them out, apply them to your cluster, and observe what happens.

⌨️
Type, Don't Copy

Muscle memory matters on the 2-hour exam

🔬
Break Things

Intentionally cause errors to learn troubleshooting

3

Attempt the Practice Questions

Each chapter ends with 10-14 CKA-style practice questions. Try them without looking at the answer first. Time yourself — the real exam gives ~7 minutes per question.

⏱️
Time Yourself

Target under 5 minutes for each question

📝
400+ Questions

Every CKA domain is thoroughly covered

4

Use the Appendices as References

Appendices A-I are your reference library: kubectl cheat sheet, YAML templates, common errors, glossary, manifests, exam prep, study plan, a full exam simulator, and a labels/annotations/taints reference.

📋
Exam Day

Appendix F has your exam day checklist

🗓️
Study Plan

Appendix G has a 10-week learning path

5

Run the Exam Simulator

Appendix H contains a complete CKA exam simulator with timed tasks across all 5 domains. Use it as your final readiness check before booking the real exam.

🧪
Simulate

Full 2-hour timed exam environment

✅
Score 75%+

If you score 75%+ on the simulator, you're ready

6

Pass the CKA Exam!

Book the exam, use Appendix F's checklist, and trust your preparation. Remember: the exam is open-book (kubernetes.io), and you get one free retake.

🎓
Get Certified

CKA valid for 3 years (previously 2)

🚀
Career Impact

Top K8s certification — recognized globally

Before vs After This Guide

❌ Before

😰K8s is overwhelming — too many objects, too many YAML fields
🤷kubectl is confusing — which command does what?
😱Networking is scary — Services, Ingress, DNS, CNI?
🔓Security is unclear — RBAC, PSS, NetworkPolicy?
❓Debugging is guessing — pod is crashlooping, now what?
📝CKA exam seems impossible — 2 hours for 17+ tasks?

✅ After

💪K8s clicks — you understand how every object fits together
⚡kubectl is muscle memory — create, apply, debug in seconds
🌐Networking makes sense — ClusterIP → NodePort → Ingress chain
🔒Security is systematic — RBAC + PSS + NetPol layered defense
🔍5-step debug flow — events → logs → describe → exec → fix
🎓CKA certified! — 400+ practice questions and exam simulator ready

📅 10-Week Study Plan at a Glance

A week-by-week roadmap to CKA certification — see Appendix G for full details

🟢 Weeks 1-2: Foundation

Chapters 1-6 — Understand Kubernetes, Docker prerequisites, architecture, core objects, environment setup, and kubectl mastery.

🟢 Weeks 3-4: Core Skills

Chapters 7-12 — YAML manifests, deploy anihpj end-to-end, kubeadm clusters, control plane deep dive, etcd backup/restore, node management.

🟡 Weeks 5-6: Storage & Networking

Chapters 13-19 — Volumes & PVCs, ConfigMaps & Secrets, scheduling, flat network model, Services deep dive, Ingress, Network Policies.

🟡 Weeks 7-8: Workloads & Security

Chapters 20-26 — Deployment strategies, health probes, resource management, workload types, RBAC, scaling & HPA, pod security.

🔴 Week 9: Advanced Operations

Chapters 27-34 — Logging, monitoring with Prometheus, troubleshooting, multi-container patterns, cluster upgrades, advanced scheduling, certificates, backup & DR.

🟣 Week 10: Production, Extending & Exam Prep

Chapters 35-40 + Appendices F-I — HA clusters, cloud Kubernetes, CI/CD & GitOps, CRDs & Operators, security hardening, policies deep dive, exam checklist, 10-week plan review, full exam simulator, and labels/annotations/taints reference.

Choose your starting point based on your current experience level

🟢

Complete Beginner

 🟡

Know Docker + Basics

 🔴

Experienced, Need CKA Prep

 🎯

Ready for Exam Sim

Jump to Appendix H — full CKA exam simulator with timed tasks

💡 Top CKA Exam Pro Tips

Maximize your score with these battle-tested strategies

⌨️
Set Aliases First

alias k=kubectl and export do="--dry-run=client -o yaml" — saves minutes

📋
Bookmark Docs

Pre-bookmark key pages on kubernetes.io — cheat sheet, API reference, task pages

⏩
Skip & Return

Stuck over 5 min? Flag it and move on. Easy questions first, hard ones last.

✅
Verify Everything

After every task: kubectl get, describe, curl to confirm it works

🔀
Check Context

Each question may use a different cluster. Always run kubectl config use-context first

🎓 Your Journey Starts Now

You have everything you need: 40 chapters of in-depth content, 400+ practice questions covering all 5 CKA exam domains, a real Django project (anihpj) to deploy end-to-end, a 9-appendix reference library, and a full exam simulator. Whether you're starting from zero or polishing your skills for exam day, this guide will take you from "What is Kubernetes?" to CKA certified. Scroll down to begin — or use the sidebar navigation to jump to any chapter.

🎯

CKA Exam Strategy — Your Battle Plan

A Comprehensive, No-Fluff Guide to Passing the CKA on Your First Attempt

The CKA is a performance-based exam — you solve real tasks in a live Kubernetes terminal, not answer multiple-choice questions. Knowing Kubernetes isn't enough; you need speed, accuracy, and a systematic approach. This section distills the strategies that separate first-attempt passers from those who retake.

⏱️ 120 Minutes Total
📝 15-20 Tasks / Questions
🎯 66% Pass Score
⚡ ~6-7 Min per Task
🌐 Open kubernetes.io Allowed
🔄 1 Free Retake

⚡ The First 5 Minutes — Your Setup Ritual

Do these IMMEDIATELY when the exam starts — before touching a single question

🏁 Why the First 5 Minutes Matter

The CKA terminal is bare. No aliases, no autocomplete shortcuts. Investing 5 minutes upfront saves 15-20 minutes over the exam. Every top scorer does this.

1

Set Up Aliases & Variables

These save you from typing kubectl thousands of times. Paste these into your terminal immediately:

~/.bashrc — Paste & source 
# Essential aliases — MEMORIZE THESE
alias k=kubectl
alias kgp='kubectl get pods'
alias kgs='kubectl get svc'
alias kgn='kubectl get nodes'
alias kgd='kubectl get deploy'
alias kd='kubectl describe'
alias kaf='kubectl apply -f'
alias kdel='kubectl delete'
alias kns='kubectl config set-context --current --namespace'

# Dry-run shortcuts — generate YAML without creating
export do="--dry-run=client -o yaml"
export now="--force --grace-period 0"

# Apply changes
source ~/.bashrc
2

Enable kubectl Autocompletion

Tab-completion for resource names, flags, and namespaces. Massive time saver:

bash autocompletion 
source <(kubectl completion bash)
complete -o default -F __start_kubectl k
3

Set Up Your Editor (vim)

The exam uses vim. Set indentation for YAML — one wrong indent = broken manifest:

~/.vimrc 
cat <<EOF > ~/.vimrc
set tabstop=2
set shiftwidth=2
set expandtab
set number
set autoindent
EOF
4

Bookmark Key Documentation Pages

You're allowed to browse kubernetes.io during the exam. Pre-bookmark these pages:

📋
Cheat Sheet

kubernetes.io/docs/reference/kubectl/cheatsheet/

📖
Tasks Index

kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/ — step-by-step guides

🔍
API Reference

kubernetes.io/docs/reference/ — exact field names

🔧
kubeadm Ref

kubernetes.io/docs/reference/setup-tools/kubeadm/

5

Verify Your Environment

Confirm your tools work and check which cluster/context each question uses:

Quick environment check 
# Verify basics work
k version --short
k get nodes
k config get-contexts    # See all available clusters
k cluster-info           # Confirm API server is reachable

⏱️ Time Management — The 3-Pass Strategy

Don't solve questions in order — use this proven approach to maximize your score

⚠️ The #1 Reason People Fail

Getting stuck on a hard question for 15+ minutes, panicking, rushing through the rest, and running out of time. The 3-pass strategy prevents this completely.

1

🟢 Pass 1: Quick Wins (0-40 min)

EASY FIRST

Scan ALL questions first. Solve everything you can do in under 4 minutes. These are your guaranteed points.

⚡ Target
Quick imperatives

Create pods, deployments, services, configmaps with kubectl create / kubectl run

~2 minHigh yield
⚡ Target
Label/annotate tasks

Add labels, selectors, annotations — simple one-liners

~1 minFree points
⚡ Target
Scale / expose

kubectl scale, kubectl expose — quick and easy

~1 minFree points
⚡ Target
Node drain/cordon

kubectl drain --ignore-daemonsets — one command

~2 minHigh yield
2

🟡 Pass 2: Medium Tasks (40-90 min)

MEDIUM NEXT

Now tackle the 5-8 minute tasks. These require YAML editing or multi-step procedures but you know how to do them.

🟡 Target
RBAC setup

Create Role, RoleBinding, ServiceAccount — need YAML but straightforward pattern

~5 minCKA staple
🟡 Target
Network Policies

Write ingress/egress rules — use kubernetes.io template, adjust selectors

~6 minTricky YAML
🟡 Target
PV + PVC + Pod

Create PersistentVolume, claim it, mount in a pod — 3 resources

~6 minMulti-step
🟡 Target
Ingress rules

Create Ingress with path/host routing — refer to docs for exact API

~5 minAPI changes
3

🔴 Pass 3: Hard Tasks (90-120 min)

HARD LAST

Final 30 minutes for the hardest tasks. Even partial solutions get partial credit. Never leave a question blank.

🔴 Target
etcd backup/restore

Snapshot, restore with correct flags — must know cert paths and endpoints

~8 minHigh weight
🔴 Target
Cluster upgrades

kubeadm upgrade plan → apply → drain → upgrade kubelet — multi-step

~10 minCritical
🔴 Target
Troubleshooting

Fix a broken cluster/node/deployment — require systematic debugging

~8 min30% of exam
🔴 Target
Multi-step scenarios

Complex tasks combining multiple objects — even partial = partial credit

~10 minPartial OK

📊 Time Budget Breakdown

⚡ Setup (aliases, vim, bookmarks) 5 min (4%)
🟢 Pass 1 — Quick wins (guaranteed points) 35 min (29%)
🟡 Pass 2 — Medium tasks (bulk of score) 50 min (42%)
🔴 Pass 3 — Hard tasks + review 30 min (25%)

✅❌ The Complete Do's & Don'ts

Habits that separate passers from failers — burned into muscle memory from 400+ practice questions

Exam Behaviour — What to Do vs What to Avoid

✅ DO — Always

🔀Switch context FIRST — Every question specifies a cluster. Run kubectl config use-context <name> before ANYTHING else.
📦Check the namespace — Read the question carefully. Use -n <namespace> or kns <namespace> to set default. Wrong namespace = 0 points.
⚡Use imperative commands first — kubectl run, kubectl create, kubectl expose are 10x faster than writing YAML from scratch.
📝Generate YAML, then edit — k run nginx --image=nginx $do > pod.yaml then vim pod.yaml to add volumes, probes, etc.
✔️Verify after EVERY task — kubectl get, kubectl describe, curl the service. Never assume it worked.
⏩Skip and return — Stuck for 5+ minutes? Flag it, move on, come back later. Points per minute matters.
🔍Use kubectl explain — Forgot a field name? k explain pod.spec.containers.livenessProbe gives you the exact YAML structure.
📄Use docs search — On kubernetes.io, use the search bar. "etcd backup" → exact procedure. Faster than scrolling.
💾Save partial work — Even an incomplete answer may earn partial credit. Apply what you have before moving on.
📐Read the ENTIRE question — Questions often have specific names, namespaces, labels, or constraints buried in the text. Miss one = wrong answer.

❌ DON'T — Never

🚫Don't write YAML from scratch — Always generate with --dry-run=client -o yaml or copy from docs. Hand-writing YAML wastes time and causes indent errors.
🚫Don't spend 10+ min on one question — If you're stuck, you're stuck. Mark it, move on. Come back with fresh eyes in Pass 3.
🚫Don't forget the context switch — Solving a task on the wrong cluster = 0 points AND you might break another cluster. Always check.
🚫Don't panic on errors — kubectl apply failed? Read the error. 90% of the time it's a typo, wrong indent, or missing field. Fix and retry.
🚫Don't delete resources carelessly — You might delete something another question depends on. Only delete what the question asks you to.
🚫Don't memorize YAML — Memorize the commands to generate YAML and the fields to add. The structure comes from --dry-run.
🚫Don't ignore the question weight — A 2% question isn't worth 15 minutes. A 7% question might be worth 10 minutes. Prioritize by value.
🚫Don't browse unrelated docs — Only kubernetes.io is allowed. Opening other sites will be flagged by the proctor and can void your exam.
🚫Don't use deprecated APIs — extensions/v1beta1 is gone. Always check kubectl api-resources for current API versions.
🚫Don't leave questions blank — Even a partially correct YAML or a created namespace can earn partial credit. Something is always better than nothing.

🔀 Imperative vs Declarative — When to Use Which

The fastest path depends on the task complexity

⚡ Imperative (Fast Path)

Use when: the task is simple enough for a single command

  • k run nginx --image=nginx — create a pod
  • k create deploy web --image=nginx --replicas=3
  • k expose deploy web --port=80 --type=NodePort
  • k create cm myconfig --from-literal=key=val
  • k create secret generic mysec --from-literal=pw=123
  • k create ns production
  • k create sa myaccount -n production
  • k label node worker1 disk=ssd
  • k scale deploy web --replicas=5
  • k set image deploy/web nginx=nginx:1.25

⏱️ Time: 10-30 seconds each

📝 Declarative (YAML Path)

Use when: the task requires fields you can't set imperatively

  • Adding volumes / volumeMounts to a pod
  • Adding liveness / readiness probes
  • Writing NetworkPolicy rules
  • Creating PersistentVolume manifests
  • Setting nodeAffinity or tolerations
  • Creating Ingress with path rules
  • Setting securityContext fields
  • Creating Role / ClusterRole with specific rules
  • Multi-container pods (sidecar patterns)
  • Resource requests & limits

⏱️ Time: 3-8 minutes each

💡 The Hybrid Approach (Best of Both Worlds)

For YAML tasks, don't write from scratch. Generate the base imperatively, then add what's needed:

k run mypod --image=nginx $do > pod.yaml → vim pod.yaml → add volumeMounts, probes, etc. → k apply -f pod.yaml

This eliminates 80% of indent errors and gives you correct metadata, apiVersion, and kind for free.

🏗️ Domain-by-Domain Strategy

How to approach each CKA exam domain — weighted by importance

🔧
Troubleshooting — 30% (Highest Weight)

What to expect: Broken clusters, crashed pods, failed services, node issues.
Strategy: Follow a systematic 5-step debug flow: kubectl get → kubectl describe → kubectl logs → kubectl exec → fix & verify. Always check events first (kubectl get events --sort-by=.metadata.creationTimestamp). Common fixes: wrong image name, missing ConfigMap/Secret, resource limits too low, wrong label selectors, certificate expiry.
Key commands: k describe pod, k logs -p (previous crash), k get events, crictl ps, journalctl -u kubelet

🏛️
Cluster Architecture, Installation & Configuration — 25%

What to expect: kubeadm init/join, etcd backup/restore, cluster upgrades, RBAC, certificate management.
Strategy: Practice kubeadm upgrade workflow until it's muscle memory. Know where etcd certs live (/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/). For etcd backup: etcdctl snapshot save with --endpoints, --cacert, --cert, --key. RBAC: know the difference between Role vs ClusterRole, RoleBinding vs ClusterRoleBinding.
Key commands: kubeadm upgrade plan, etcdctl snapshot save/restore, k create role, k create clusterrolebinding

🌐
Services & Networking — 20%

What to expect: Create Services (ClusterIP/NodePort/LoadBalancer), Ingress resources, Network Policies, CoreDNS troubleshooting.
Strategy: Memorize: k expose deploy NAME --port=80 --target-port=8080 --type=NodePort. For NetworkPolicy, bookmark the kubernetes.io page — the YAML is complex and easy to get wrong. Know DNS format: svc-name.namespace.svc.cluster.local.
Key commands: k expose, k get svc, k get ep, k run tmp --image=busybox -it --rm -- wget -qO- svc:port

📦
Workloads & Scheduling — 15%

What to expect: Create/modify Deployments, scale, rolling updates, Jobs, CronJobs, nodeSelector, taints/tolerations, resource requests/limits.
Strategy: All deployment tasks are fast with imperative commands. For scheduling: remember nodeSelector is one line in YAML, tolerations are 4 fields (key, operator, value, effect). Use k explain pod.spec.tolerations if you forget the structure.
Key commands: k create deploy, k scale, k rollout undo, k taint, k label node

💾
Storage — 10%

What to expect: Create PV, PVC, mount volumes in pods, StorageClasses, access modes.
Strategy: Storage questions are almost always YAML-based. Know the PV→PVC→Pod chain. Key fields: capacity, accessModes, hostPath/nfs, storageClassName. For PVC: must match PV's accessModes and storageClassName. Mount path in pod with volumeMounts.
Key commands: k get pv,pvc, k explain pv.spec, k explain pod.spec.volumes

📊 Understanding Scoring & Partial Credit

How the CKA exam is scored — and how to maximize every point

⚖️
Weighted Questions

Each question has a weight (2%-13%). Focus energy on high-weight tasks first.

🧩
Partial Credit

Multi-step tasks give partial credit. Create the namespace even if you can't finish the deployment.

🤖
Auto-Graded

A script checks your cluster state. Exact names, namespaces, and labels matter. Read the question twice.

🎯
66% to Pass

You can miss ~34% and still pass. Don't aim for perfection — aim for maximum points per minute.

📈 Score Maximization Decision Flow
  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │            READ THE QUESTION                     │
  │    Note: cluster context, namespace, names       │
  └────────────────────┬────────────────────────────┘
                       │
                       ▼
           ┌───────────────────────┐
           │ Can I do this in      │──── YES ──→ DO IT NOW (Pass 1)
           │ under 4 minutes?      │              ✅ Verify → Next
           └───────────┬───────────┘
                       │ NO
                       ▼
           ┌───────────────────────┐
           │ Do I know HOW to      │──── YES ──→ DO IT (Pass 2, 5-8 min)
           │ solve this?           │              📝 Use docs if needed
           └───────────┬───────────┘              ✅ Verify → Next
                       │ NO
                       ▼
           ┌───────────────────────┐
           │ Can I do PART of it?  │──── YES ──→ DO THE PARTS YOU CAN
           │ (namespace, SA, etc.) │              💾 Save partial work
           └───────────┬───────────┘              🔖 Flag for Pass 3
                       │ NO
                       ▼
              🔖 FLAG IT → Skip to next
              Return in Pass 3 with
              remaining time. Use docs
              to look up the procedure.

💀 Top 10 Mistakes That Cost Points

Real errors from real exam-takers — learn from their pain so you don't repeat it

1
Wrong Cluster Context

Solving a task on the wrong cluster. The grading script checks the specified cluster — if your work is on a different one, it's invisible. Always run kubectl config use-context first.

2
Wrong Namespace

Creating resources in default when the question says production. Either use -n production on every command or set it with kns production.

3
Typos in Resource Names

Question says create pod web-server but you create webserver. The auto-grader checks exact names. Copy-paste from the question when possible.

4
YAML Indentation Errors

YAML is whitespace-sensitive. One extra space = broken manifest. Use kubectl apply --dry-run=client -f file.yaml to validate before applying.

5
Not Verifying After Apply

You kubectl apply and move on without checking. The resource might be in CrashLoopBackOff or Pending state. Always verify: k get pods → k describe pod.

6
Time Wasted Writing YAML From Scratch

Hand-writing a 40-line YAML when k run nginx --image=nginx $do > pod.yaml gives you 90% of it in 2 seconds. Always generate first.

7
Forgetting --force --grace-period 0

Deleting a pod and waiting 30 seconds for graceful shutdown. Use k delete pod NAME $now (your alias) for instant deletion during the exam.

8
etcd Restore Without Stopping API Server

Restoring etcd without stopping kube-apiserver can corrupt the cluster. Follow the exact procedure: stop → restore → update data-dir → start.

9
Using Deprecated API Versions

extensions/v1beta1 for Ingress is long gone. Use networking.k8s.io/v1. Check k api-resources | grep ingress to confirm.

10
Spending Too Long on One Question

Getting stuck on a 4% question for 15 minutes while leaving 7% questions unsolved. Calculate: points per minute. Always maximize total score, not individual perfection.

📋 Exam Day Checklist

Everything to do before, during, and after the exam

🏠 Before the Exam (Day Before & Morning Of)

🪪 Government ID ready — passport or driver's license must be valid and visible on webcam
🖥️ System check done — run the PSI browser compatibility check 24 hours before
📡 Stable internet — wired connection preferred. Have a mobile hotspot as backup
🧹 Clean desk & room — proctor will ask you to show your workspace via webcam. Remove all papers, books, phones
🔇 Quiet environment — no one else in the room. Close doors. Disable notifications on your machine
🌐 Chrome browser only — the PSI Secure Browser requires Chrome. Close all other tabs/apps

⚡ During the Exam (120 Minutes)

⌨️ First 5 min: setup — aliases, vim config, bash completion, bookmarks (see above)
🔀 Context switch EVERY question — read the cluster name at the top, run kubectl config use-context
📖 Use kubernetes.io — search for procedures (etcd backup, kubeadm upgrade). Don't memorize, know where to find
⏱️ Watch the clock — 3-pass strategy. Quick wins first, medium next, hard last. Never go over 10 min on one task
✅ Verify every answer — kubectl get, describe, test with curl. If it's not running, it's not scored
🧘 Stay calm — if you blank on something, breathe, check docs, try kubectl explain. Panic wastes time

🏁 After the Exam

📧 Results in 24 hours — emailed as a link to the Linux Foundation portal. Sometimes faster.
🔄 Free retake if needed — valid for 12 months. Review weak areas using this guide's chapter questions
🎓 Certificate valid 3 years — download your PDF cert and digital badge from the portal
🚀 Next step: CKAD or CKS — CKA unlocks CKS (which requires CKA as prerequisite)

🏆 The Bottom Line

The CKA is not about knowing everything — it's about solving tasks efficiently under time pressure. You don't need to memorize every YAML field; you need to know where to find it fast (kubectl explain, kubernetes.io docs) and how to generate it fast (--dry-run=client -o yaml). Follow the 3-pass strategy, use your aliases, verify every answer, and never leave a question blank. With the 40 chapters and 400+ practice questions in this guide behind you, you are more than ready. Go pass that exam.

🟢 Part 1: Foundation Chapters 1-4

Chapter 1: What is Kubernetes? — Why Do Administrators Need It?

Before diving into cluster builds and config files, let's understand why Kubernetes exists, what problems it solves for operations teams, and why the CKA certification focuses on the platform itself rather than just deploying apps on it. We'll use our anihpj/jobpost Django app to make every concept concrete.

🟢 Beginner ⏱️ ~1.5 hours 📖 Concepts 🎯 Foundation

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ The problems that exist before Kubernetes — manual scaling, 3 AM crashes, single points of failure
  • ✅ What Kubernetes actually is — the simple, clear definition
  • ✅ CKA vs CKAD perspective — why administrators own the cluster layer
  • ✅ Kubernetes core concepts — clusters, control plane, worker nodes, pods, desired state
  • ✅ The Kubernetes ecosystem — CNCF, CNI plugins, Helm, GitOps, service mesh
  • ✅ How companies of different sizes use Kubernetes in real production
🎓 CKA Relevance
Cluster Architecture 25% Foundation ALL DOMAINS
Understanding "Why" Matters on the CKA

The CKA is a performance-based exam (2 hours, 15-20 tasks in a live terminal). While Chapter 1 isn't directly tested, understanding why Kubernetes works the way it does helps you troubleshoot faster and reason through unfamiliar scenarios. Key concepts to internalize:

  • Desired State vs Actual State — K8s controllers constantly reconcile these. This explains why the control plane exists and why etcd is so critical.
  • Self-Healing — If a node goes down, K8s reschedules pods to healthy nodes. The CKA tests your ability to manage this.
  • Control Plane Components — Understanding what the API server, scheduler, controller-manager, and etcd do is directly tested on the CKA (25% of the exam).

1.1 The Problem Before Kubernetes

Imagine you've built anihpj — a Django app with a jobpost module where employers list jobs filtered by Skills, Location, and Author. It works perfectly on your laptop. But how do you run it in production so thousands of users can access it reliably?

📖 Key Term: Container Orchestration

Container orchestration is the automated management of containerized applications across multiple machines — including deployment, scaling, networking, and availability. Kubernetes is the industry-standard container orchestrator, and as a CKA administrator, you are responsible for building and maintaining the orchestration platform itself.

The Evolution of Deployment

Software deployment has evolved through four major eras. Each solved problems of the previous one — but introduced new challenges:

🖥️
2000s
Bare Metal

One app per physical server. No isolation. Massive resource waste. anihpj would need its own dedicated box.

❌ Wasteful & rigid
💻
2010s
Virtual Machines

Multiple VMs per server. Better isolation. Still heavy — each VM runs a full OS. Slow to provision.

⚠️ Heavy overhead
📦
2015+
Containers

Lightweight, fast, shared kernel. But managing hundreds of containers manually across servers? Nightmare.

🟡 Almost there...
☸️
2018+
Kubernetes

Orchestrated containers. Auto-healing, auto-scaling, zero-downtime deploys. The admin builds and maintains this platform.

✅ Production-ready

The Scaling Problem — Before vs After

Without Kubernetes vs With Kubernetes
  ❌ WITHOUT KUBERNETES                    ✅ WITH KUBERNETES
  ┌──────────────────────────┐            ┌──────────────────────────┐
  │                          │            │                          │
  │  ┌──────────────┐        │            │  ┌────┐ ┌────┐ ┌────┐   │
  │  │  🖥️ SERVER    │        │            │  │ 📦 │ │ 📦 │ │ 📦 │   │
  │  │              │        │            │  │app │ │app │ │app │   │
  │  │  anihpj      │  ← All │            │  └────┘ └────┘ └────┘   │
  │  │  postgres    │  on    │            │    ↑ auto-heal & scale  │
  │  │  nginx       │  ONE   │            │                          │
  │  │              │  box   │            │  ┌──────┐ ┌──────┐      │
  │  │  💀 crash    │        │            │  │Node 1│ │Node 2│      │
  │  │  = TOTAL     │        │            │  │ ┌──┐ │ │ ┌──┐ │      │
  │  │    DOWNTIME  │        │            │  │ │📦│ │ │ │📦│ │      │
  │  └──────────────┘        │            │  │ └──┘ │ │ └──┘ │      │
  │                          │            │  └──────┘ └──────┘      │
  │  Manual restart at 3AM 😱│            │  Self-healing 24/7 🎉    │
  └──────────────────────────┘            └──────────────────────────┘

Why Containers Alone Aren't Enough

Docker gives you a container. Kubernetes gives you an army of containers that manage themselves. Here's what you'd have to do manually without an orchestrator:

😰 Managing anihpj WITHOUT Kubernetes
  • 🔴 SSH into the server manually at 3 AM when the process crashes
  • 🔴 Restart the crashed Django process by hand — python manage.py runserver
  • 🔴 No load balancing — one server handles ALL traffic to /jobs/
  • 🔴 Deploying = copy files via SCP + pray nothing breaks
  • 🔴 Scaling = buy a new server, install everything from scratch, configure nginx
  • 🔴 Rolling back a bad deploy = restore from backup (if you remembered to make one)
🎉 Managing anihpj WITH Kubernetes
  • ✅ Pod crashes at 3 AM → K8s restarts it in seconds, you sleep through the night
  • ✅ Traffic spikes → K8s spins up more pods automatically via HPA
  • ✅ Deploy new version → K8s does a rolling update with zero downtime
  • ✅ Bad deploy? → kubectl rollout undo — instant rollback
  • ✅ Node fails → K8s migrates all pods to healthy nodes automatically
  • ✅ Config changes? → Update a ConfigMap, pods reload automatically
💡 CKA Admin Perspective

As a CKA administrator, your job isn't just deploying apps — it's building and maintaining the platform that makes all of the above possible. You install the cluster, manage certificates, upgrade versions, backup etcd, and troubleshoot when things go wrong at the infrastructure level.

1.2 What is Kubernetes? (The Simple Answer)

📖 Official Definition

"An open-source system for automating deployment, scaling, and management of containerized applications."

In plain English: Kubernetes is an operating system for your containers. You tell it what you want ("3 copies of anihpj, always running") — and it figures out how to make that happen across your cluster.

The Kubernetes Elevator Pitch

Declarative Management — You Declare, K8s Delivers
  📝 YOU DECLARE (YAML):             ⚙️ KUBERNETES ENSURES:
  ┌───────────────────────┐         ┌────────────────────────────────┐
  │ replicas: 3           │         │ ✅ 3 containers running        │
  │ image: anihpj:v2      │  ────▶  │ ✅ All using v2 image          │
  │ memory: 256Mi         │         │ ✅ Each limited to 256Mi       │
  └───────────────────────┘         └────────────────────────────────┘

  Pod crashes?     K8s restarts it automatically  🔄
  Node dies?       K8s moves pods to other nodes  🔀
  Traffic spikes?  K8s can auto-scale up          📈
  New version?     K8s rolls out with 0 downtime  🚀

  💡 You never say "start a container on node 2"
     You say "I want 3 copies" — K8s figures out the HOW

Quick Facts

☸️
Name Origin

K8s = K + 8 letters + s. Greek κυβερνήτης means "helmsman" — steering the ship.

🏛️
Born at Google

Evolved from Google's internal Borg system. Open-sourced in 2014, donated to CNCF.

🔤
Written in Go

Built with Google's Go language for high concurrency, compiled performance, and clean APIs.

📜
Apache 2.0 License

Fully open-source. Free to use, modify, and distribute commercially.

Kubernetes Lineage — From Borg to K8s
  Google Borg (2003)           Google Omega (2013)          Kubernetes (2014)
 ┌─────────────────────┐     ┌─────────────────────┐     ┌─────────────────────┐
 │ Internal cluster     │────▶│ Next-gen scheduler  │────▶│ Open-source for     │
 │ manager at Google    │     │ research project    │     │ everyone! ☸️         │
 │ Ran Gmail, Search,   │     │ Flexible queuing,   │     │ CNCF graduated 2018 │
 │ YouTube, Maps        │     │ multi-scheduler     │     │ Largest OSS after   │
 └─────────────────────┘     └─────────────────────┘     │ Linux kernel        │
                                                          └─────────────────────┘

Kubernetes by the Numbers

CNCF Survey — K8s Adoption Rate 96%
Fortune 100 Companies Using K8s 77%
GitHub Stars (Top 10 OSS Projects) 112K+
Active GitHub Contributors 3,500+

1.3 The CKA Perspective: Administrator vs Developer

There are two Kubernetes certifications from the Linux Foundation — and they test fundamentally different skills. Understanding the boundary between them is crucial for knowing what to study.

👨‍💻
CKAD — Developer

"I deploy and configure apps on a cluster someone else built."

  • Create Deployments, Services, ConfigMaps
  • Configure health checks and resource limits
  • Build multi-container pods
  • Manage application lifecycle
🔧
CKA — Administrator ← YOU

"I build, manage, and troubleshoot the cluster itself."

  • Install clusters with kubeadm
  • Upgrade cluster versions safely
  • Backup & restore etcd
  • Manage certificates & RBAC
  • Troubleshoot broken nodes
  • Configure networking (CNI, DNS)
CKA Administrator Responsibilities Map
 ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
 │  THE K8s ADMINISTRATOR OWNS EVERYTHING BELOW THE APP       │
 │                                                            │
 │  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
 │  │  👨‍💻 Developer's World (CKAD)                         │  │
 │  │  Pods, Deployments, Services, ConfigMaps, Secrets     │  │
 │  ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤  │
 │  │  🔧 Administrator's World (CKA) ← YOU                │  │
 │  │  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │  │
 │  │  │ ⚙️  Cluster Installation (kubeadm)              │  │  │
 │  │  │ 🔐 Certificate Management (PKI/TLS)            │  │  │
 │  │  │ 💾 etcd Backup & Restore                       │  │  │
 │  │  │ ⬆️  Cluster Upgrades (version management)       │  │  │
 │  │  │ 🖥️  Node Management (drain, cordon, uncordon)   │  │  │
 │  │  │ 🛡️  RBAC & Security Policies                   │  │  │
 │  │  │ 🌐 Networking (CNI, CoreDNS, kube-proxy)       │  │  │
 │  │  │ 💿 Storage (PV, PVC, StorageClasses)            │  │  │
 │  │  │ 🔍 Troubleshooting (nodes, pods, cluster)      │  │  │
 │  │  │ 🏗️  High Availability & Disaster Recovery       │  │  │
 │  │  └────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │  │
 │  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
 └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

CKA Exam Domain Weights

The CKA exam tests five domains. As a CKA candidate, you need to know exactly where each topic fits:

Cluster Architecture, Installation & Configuration 25%
Workloads & Scheduling 15%
Services & Networking 20%
Storage 10%
Troubleshooting 30%
⚠️ Troubleshooting is 30% of the CKA!

Unlike the CKAD (which focuses on building apps), the CKA dedicates 30% of the exam to troubleshooting broken clusters, failed nodes, crashed control plane components, and networking issues. You must know how to diagnose and fix problems under time pressure.

Aspect CKAD (Developer) CKA (Administrator) ← YOU
Focus Application deployment & configuration Cluster lifecycle & infrastructure
Duration 2 hours, ~15-20 tasks 2 hours, ~15-20 tasks
Passing Score 66% 66%
etcd Not tested Backup, restore, snapshot
kubeadm Not tested Install, upgrade, configure
Certificates Not tested CSR, TLS, certificate rotation
Node Management Not tested Drain, cordon, troubleshoot
Networking Services, Ingress basics CNI, CoreDNS, kube-proxy, NetworkPolicy

CKA Exam Logistics — Quick Reference

📋
Exam Format
  • Duration: 2 hours
  • Questions: 15-20 performance tasks
  • Passing: 66%
  • Environment: Linux terminal (browser-based)
  • K8s version: Latest stable (check CNCF site)
  • Retake: 1 free retake included
📚
Allowed Resources
  • ✅ kubernetes.io/docs — official docs
  • ✅ kubernetes.io/blog — official blog
  • ✅ github.com/kubernetes — source code
  • ❌ No other websites
  • ❌ No notes, bookmarks, or AI tools
  • 💡 Tip: Practice navigating docs fast!

Kubernetes Release Cycle

K8s releases 3 minor versions per year (~every 4 months). As a CKA admin, you manage cluster upgrades and must understand the support window:

🆕
Month 0
New Release

e.g., v1.32 releases. Includes new features, deprecations, and bug fixes.

✅ Latest
🛡️
0-14 months
Supported

Receives patch releases (security/bug fixes). CKA tests within this window.

✅ Patch support
⚠️
14+ months
End of Life

No more patches. Running EOL versions = security risk. Must upgrade.

❌ Unsupported
💡 CKA Upgrade Rule: One Minor Version at a Time

Kubernetes only supports upgrading one minor version at a time (e.g., 1.30 → 1.31 → 1.32). You cannot skip versions. The CKA tests this: you'll be asked to upgrade a cluster from, say, 1.31 to 1.32 using kubeadm upgrade. Attempting to jump from 1.30 to 1.32 will fail.

1.4 Kubernetes Core Concepts — The Big Picture

Before getting hands-on, you need to understand five fundamental concepts that everything else in Kubernetes builds upon. These form the mental model you'll use for the entire CKA journey.

🏗️
Cluster

A set of machines (nodes) managed by Kubernetes as a single unit.

🧠
Control Plane

The "brain" that makes all scheduling & orchestration decisions.

💪
Worker Nodes

The "muscles" that actually run your containerized applications.

📦
Pod

Smallest deployable unit — one or more containers sharing network & storage.

🎯
Desired State

You declare what you want — K8s continuously ensures it stays that way.

The Kubernetes Architecture — Bird's Eye View

Complete Cluster Architecture
 ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
 │                        CLUSTER                                  │
 │                                                                 │
 │  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
 │  │              🧠 CONTROL PLANE ("The Brain")                │  │
 │  │                                                            │  │
 │  │  ┌─────────────┐ ┌────────┐ ┌────────────┐ ┌───────────┐ │  │
 │  │  │ API Server   │ │  etcd  │ │ Scheduler  │ │ Controller│ │  │
 │  │  │ (Front Door) │ │ (Brain │ │ (Matchmaker│ │  Manager  │ │  │
 │  │  │              │ │  Memory│ │  "Pod→Node"│ │ (Watchdog)│ │  │
 │  │  │ kubectl ──▶  │ │ DB)    │ │            │ │           │ │  │
 │  │  └─────────────┘ └────────┘ └────────────┘ └───────────┘ │  │
 │  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
 │                          │                                      │
 │                   kubelet / kube-proxy                           │
 │                          │                                      │
 │  ┌───────────────────────▼───────────────────────────────────┐  │
 │  │              💪 WORKER NODES ("The Muscles")               │  │
 │  │                                                            │  │
 │  │  ┌──────────────────┐         ┌──────────────────┐        │  │
 │  │  │   Worker Node 1  │         │   Worker Node 2  │        │  │
 │  │  │  ┌──────────┐    │         │  ┌──────────┐    │        │  │
 │  │  │  │📦 Pod     │    │         │  │📦 Pod     │    │        │  │
 │  │  │  │ anihpj   │    │         │  │ anihpj   │    │        │  │
 │  │  │  │ :8000    │    │         │  │ :8000    │    │        │  │
 │  │  │  └──────────┘    │         │  └──────────┘    │        │  │
 │  │  │  ┌──────────┐    │         │  ┌──────────┐    │        │  │
 │  │  │  │📦 Pod     │    │         │  │📦 Pod     │    │        │  │
 │  │  │  │ postgres │    │         │  │ redis    │    │        │  │
 │  │  │  │ :5432    │    │         │  │ :6379    │    │        │  │
 │  │  │  └──────────┘    │         │  └──────────┘    │        │  │
 │  │  └──────────────────┘         └──────────────────┘        │  │
 │  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
 │                                                                 │
 │  You say: "I want 3 anihpj pods" (Desired State)               │
 │  K8s ensures: 3 pods are ALWAYS running (Actual State)         │
 └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Control Plane Components — The Admin Must Know These

🧠 Control Plane Components (CKA Exam: 25%)

👨‍✈️ kube-apiserver
The front door — ALL communication goes through the API server. kubectl commands, node heartbeats, controller actions. If this goes down, the cluster is blind.
📋 etcd
The cluster's brain memory — a distributed key-value store holding ALL cluster state. CKA tests etcd backup & restore. Lose etcd without a backup = lose your entire cluster.
🎯 kube-scheduler
The matchmaker — watches for new Pods with no node assigned, then picks the best node based on resources, affinity, taints/tolerations.
🔄 kube-controller-manager
The watchdog — runs controller loops that continuously compare desired state vs actual state and take corrective action (e.g., "only 2 of 3 replicas running → create 1 more").
☁️ cloud-controller-manager
Cloud glue (optional) — integrates with AWS/Azure/GCP APIs for load balancers, persistent disks, and node lifecycle. Not present in bare-metal clusters.

Worker Node Components

💪 Worker Node Components

🤖 kubelet
The node agent — receives pod specs from the API server and ensures the containers are running and healthy on that node. Reports node status back.
🔀 kube-proxy
The network plumber — maintains network rules on each node so pods can communicate with each other and with external traffic via Services.
🐋 Container Runtime
The engine — actually runs containers. Kubernetes supports containerd (default), CRI-O, or any CRI-compatible runtime. Docker was removed in v1.24.

Desired State vs Actual State — The Declarative Magic

The Reconciliation Loop
  📝 YOU DECLARE (YAML):           ⚙️ KUBERNETES ENSURES:
  ┌──────────────────────┐        ┌──────────────────────────────┐
  │ replicas: 3          │        │ 📦 📦 📦  running             │
  │ image: anihpj:v2     │ ─────▶ │ all v2 images                │
  │ memory: 256Mi        │        │ each ≤ 256Mi                  │
  └──────────────────────┘        └──────────────────────────────┘
                                            │
                                   Controller Loop 🔄
                                            │
                              ┌─────────────▼──────────────┐
                              │ Pod crashes?  → Restart it  │
                              │ Node dies?    → Reschedule  │
                              │ Too few pods? → Create more │
                              │ Too many?     → Terminate   │
                              └────────────────────────────┘
💡 Why Declarative Matters for CKA

The CKA exam expects you to work declaratively (YAML manifests) rather than imperatively. You declare the desired state, apply it with kubectl apply -f, and trust Kubernetes controllers to make it happen. Understanding this model is essential for troubleshooting — when something breaks, you compare desired state (in etcd) against actual state (on nodes).

1.5 The Kubernetes Ecosystem

Kubernetes doesn't work alone. It sits at the center of a massive ecosystem of tools, plugins, and platforms — all governed by the Cloud Native Computing Foundation (CNCF). As a CKA administrator, you'll work with many of these tools daily.

Kubernetes Ecosystem Orbit
                         Helm 📦 (Package Manager)
                    ╱                               ╲
              ArgoCD 🔄                         Calico 🌐
            (GitOps)   ╲                      ╱  (CNI)
                        ┌─────────────────┐
               ─────────│   KUBERNETES    │─────── Prometheus 📊
                        │     ☸️ Core      │        (Monitoring)
                        └─────────────────┘
            containerd 🐋╱                     ╲  Istio 🕸️
            (Runtime)                           (Service Mesh)
                    ╲                               ╱
                        Grafana 📈            Cilium 🔒
                      (Dashboards)          (eBPF Networking)

              All governed by CNCF (Cloud Native Computing Foundation)

Key Ecosystem Categories for CKA

🐋
Container Runtimes

containerd (default since K8s 1.24), CRI-O (Red Hat). Docker engine was deprecated — Kubernetes uses the Container Runtime Interface (CRI) directly.

🌐
CNI Plugins (Networking)

Calico (network policies), Cilium (eBPF-based), Flannel (simple overlay), Weave. CKA requires you to install a CNI plugin during cluster setup.

📦
Package Management

Helm — the "apt/yum for Kubernetes." Packages K8s manifests into reusable charts. Essential for installing complex applications like Prometheus, cert-manager, ingress controllers.

🔄
GitOps

ArgoCD, FluxCD — automatically sync cluster state from a Git repository. Changes in Git trigger deployments. The source of truth lives in version control.

🕸️
Service Mesh

Istio, Linkerd — adds observability, traffic management, and mTLS encryption between services without changing application code.

📊
Monitoring & Observability

Prometheus (metrics), Grafana (dashboards), Loki (logs), Jaeger (tracing). The CKA expects you to know kubectl top and metrics-server.

📖 CNCF — Who Governs Kubernetes?

The Cloud Native Computing Foundation (part of the Linux Foundation) governs Kubernetes and 170+ related projects. Projects go through three maturity levels: Sandbox → Incubating → Graduated. Kubernetes was the first project to graduate (2018). CNCF also manages the CKA and CKAD certifications.

The CNCF Landscape — Maturity Levels

🎓 Graduated Production-proven, widely adopted
Kubernetes Prometheus containerd CoreDNS etcd Helm
🧪 Incubating Growing adoption, maturing features
ArgoCD Cilium Knative Kyverno Backstage
🌱 Sandbox Early-stage, experimental projects
Headlamp OpenCost Inspektor Gadget

1.6 How Companies Use Kubernetes in Real Life

Kubernetes isn't just for tech giants. Companies of every size use it — the scale of their setup and admin tasks varies dramatically.

Company Size K8s Setup Admin Tasks Team
🏠 Startup (5-20 people)
e.g., anihpj		 1 cluster, 3 nodes. Start with Minikube, graduate to managed cloud (EKS/AKS/GKE). Deploy apps, monitor health, basic troubleshooting, set up CI/CD. 1 admin does it all
🏢 Mid-size (50-200 people) 2-5 clusters. Separate dev/staging/prod. Multi-team RBAC. CI/CD pipelines, RBAC, cluster upgrades, monitoring, cost optimization, security policies. 2-4 admins
🏙️ Enterprise (500+ people) 10-100+ clusters. Multi-region, HA, service mesh, custom networking. HA & disaster recovery, security compliance, GitOps, multi-cluster management, platform engineering. Dedicated platform team (5-20+)

A Day in the Life of a Kubernetes Administrator

1
Morning Check

Review monitoring dashboards (Grafana/Prometheus). Check node health, pod restarts, resource usage. Look for alerts triggered overnight.

2
Troubleshooting

Investigate any failed pods or node issues. kubectl describe node, kubectl logs, check system journals with journalctl -u kubelet.

3
Planned Changes

Apply planned upgrades, RBAC changes, or new namespace configurations. Always test in staging first, then promote to production.

4
Security & Compliance

Review recent audit logs, rotate certificates if due, verify NetworkPolicies, check RBAC for over-permissioned accounts.

5
Capacity Planning

Monitor resource trends. Are nodes nearing capacity? Do we need to add nodes or right-size pod requests/limits?

Real Companies Running Kubernetes at Scale

🎵
Spotify

Migrated 150+ services to K8s. Reduced deployment time from hours to minutes. Custom platform built on top of K8s.

🛒
Shopify

Handles Black Friday traffic (millions of requests/sec) on K8s clusters that auto-scale to handle 10x normal load.

🎮
Pokemon GO

Launched on GKE. Scaled from expected 1x traffic to 50x in days. K8s handled the scaling that would have crashed traditional infrastructure.

🏦
Capital One

Runs critical banking services on K8s with strict compliance requirements. Proves K8s works in highly regulated industries.

💡 Where You Fit In — The anihpj Journey

Throughout this guide, we'll follow the journey of anihpj — our Django job board application — from a single-server deployment to a production-grade Kubernetes cluster. You'll learn to build the infrastructure that makes reliable, scalable deployments possible. By the end, you'll have the skills to pass the CKA exam and administer real-world clusters.

⚠️ CKA Gotcha: Conceptual Traps from Chapter 1

  • Kubernetes ≠ Docker — K8s is the orchestrator; containerd/CRI-O is the runtime. Docker as a runtime was removed in v1.24
  • Desired State ≠ Current State — The exam tests whether you understand the reconciliation loop: controllers constantly compare desired vs actual, and self-heal
  • Control Plane ≠ Master Node — Modern K8s avoids the term "master." The CKA uses "control plane node." Know the four components: API Server, Scheduler, Controller Manager, etcd
  • Pods ≠ Containers — A Pod can contain multiple containers sharing the same network namespace. On the exam, you'll see sidecar and init container patterns

Chapter 1 CKA Quick Wins: When asked about architecture on the exam, remember: kube-apiserver → front door, etcd → brain, kube-scheduler → which-node, kube-controller-manager → reconciliation. All run as static pods in /etc/kubernetes/manifests/.

📝 Chapter 1 — CKA Practice Questions

Q1
A newly deployed Kubernetes cluster has the control plane running, but worker nodes show NotReady. You SSH into a worker node and find that kubelet is not running. Which component is responsible for registering the node with the API server and managing pod lifecycle on that node?
Show Answer
Answer

The kubelet is responsible. It runs on every worker node and performs two critical functions: (1) registers the node with the kube-apiserver, and (2) watches for Pod specs assigned to its node, then ensures containers are running and healthy.

# Start kubelet on the worker node
sudo systemctl start kubelet
sudo systemctl enable kubelet

# Verify node status from control plane
kubectl get nodes
Explanation

The kubelet is the primary node agent. Without it, the node cannot communicate with the API server, so it appears NotReady. On the CKA exam, if a node is NotReady, always check systemctl status kubelet and journalctl -u kubelet first. The kubelet receives PodSpecs from the API server (via a watch) and ensures the described containers are running via the container runtime (containerd).

Q2
What are the four core control plane components in Kubernetes, and what is each one's primary responsibility? List them and briefly describe their roles.
Show Answer
Answer
Component Role
kube-apiserver Front door — all communication goes through it. RESTful API, authentication, authorization, admission control
etcd Distributed key-value store — the cluster's single source of truth for all state
kube-scheduler Assigns unscheduled pods to nodes based on resource requirements, affinity, taints/tolerations
kube-controller-manager Runs reconciliation loops — ensures desired state matches actual state (Deployment, ReplicaSet, Node controllers, etc.)
Explanation

These four components make up the control plane. On the CKA exam, they run as static pods in /etc/kubernetes/manifests/ on the control plane node. The API server is the only component that communicates directly with etcd. All other components (scheduler, controller-manager, kubelet) communicate through the API server. This star topology is a key architectural concept tested on the exam.

Q3
Explain the reconciliation loop (control loop) in Kubernetes. A Deployment specifies 3 replicas, but you manually delete one pod. What happens and why?
Show Answer
Answer

The ReplicaSet controller (running inside kube-controller-manager) detects that the current state (2 pods) doesn't match the desired state (3 replicas). It immediately creates a new pod to restore the count to 3.

# Demonstrate the reconciliation loop
kubectl create deployment nginx --image=nginx --replicas=3
kubectl get pods  # 3 pods running

# Delete one pod
kubectl delete pod nginx-xxxxx-yyyyy
kubectl get pods  # Still 3 pods — a new one was created!
Explanation

This is the declarative model in action. You declare "I want 3 replicas" and Kubernetes continuously enforces that state. The control loop runs: Observe (watch current state) → Diff (compare to desired) → Act (create/delete resources to converge). This self-healing behavior is fundamental to Kubernetes and a heavily tested concept on the CKA. Controllers never stop watching — they run indefinitely.

Q4
What is the difference between the control plane and a worker node? Which components run exclusively on each?
Show Answer
Answer

Control plane node: Runs the cluster management components — kube-apiserver, etcd, kube-scheduler, kube-controller-manager. These are deployed as static pods.

Worker node: Runs application workloads. Has kubelet (pod lifecycle), kube-proxy (networking rules), and a container runtime (containerd or CRI-O).

Both: kubelet and kube-proxy also run on control plane nodes (control plane nodes are also nodes, just tainted).

Explanation

The CKA exam avoids the term "master node" — use control plane node. By default, control plane nodes have a taint (node-role.kubernetes.io/control-plane:NoSchedule) that prevents regular workloads from being scheduled there. The kubelet runs on ALL nodes because it's needed to manage static pods (like the API server itself) on control plane nodes too.

Q5
You run kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml. Trace the complete path of this request through the Kubernetes architecture — which components are involved and in what order?
Show Answer
Answer

The request flows through these components in order:

  1. kubectl → sends HTTPS request to the API server
  2. kube-apiserver → authenticates, authorizes, runs admission controllers, validates the object
  3. etcd → API server persists the Deployment object to etcd
  4. Deployment controller (in kube-controller-manager) → creates a ReplicaSet
  5. ReplicaSet controller → creates Pod objects (with nodeName empty)
  6. kube-scheduler → watches for unscheduled pods, assigns them to nodes by setting nodeName
  7. kubelet (on assigned node) → detects the pod, pulls images via container runtime, starts containers
Explanation

This request flow demonstrates the asynchronous, event-driven nature of Kubernetes. No single component orchestrates the entire process. Each component watches for changes relevant to it (via the API server's watch mechanism) and acts independently. This loose coupling is what makes Kubernetes resilient — if the scheduler goes down temporarily, pods queue up and get scheduled when it recovers. Understanding this flow is essential for CKA troubleshooting scenarios.

Q6
What is etcd and why is backing it up critical for cluster recovery? What command would you use to take an etcd snapshot?
Show Answer
Answer

etcd is a distributed, consistent key-value store that holds all cluster state — every pod, service, secret, configmap, RBAC rule, and custom resource. If etcd is lost without a backup, the entire cluster state is gone.

# Take an etcd snapshot (CKA exam tested!)
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot save /tmp/etcd-backup.db \
  --endpoints=https://127.0.0.1:2379 \
  --cacert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt \
  --cert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt \
  --key=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key

# Verify the snapshot
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot status /tmp/etcd-backup.db --write-table
Explanation

etcd backup/restore is a guaranteed CKA exam topic. Key points: (1) Always use ETCDCTL_API=3 — version 2 API is deprecated. (2) You need three TLS flags: --cacert, --cert, --key — find them in the etcd pod spec at /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml. (3) The endpoint is typically https://127.0.0.1:2379. On the exam, check the etcd static pod manifest for the exact values.

Q7
What is the role of kube-proxy on a Kubernetes node? If kube-proxy is not running on a node, what symptoms would you observe?
Show Answer
Answer

kube-proxy maintains network rules on nodes that allow network communication to pods via Services. It runs as a DaemonSet and operates in one of three modes: iptables (default), IPVS, or userspace (legacy).

Symptoms if kube-proxy is down:

  • Services (ClusterIP, NodePort) stop forwarding traffic to pods
  • Pod-to-pod communication via pod IPs still works (handled by CNI)
  • DNS resolution via CoreDNS may fail (CoreDNS is accessed via a Service)
  • kubectl exec and kubectl logs still work (direct API server → kubelet)
Explanation

kube-proxy is a network proxy that implements the Kubernetes Service concept. It watches the API server for Service and Endpoint objects, then programs iptables/IPVS rules so that traffic to a Service's ClusterIP gets load-balanced across the backend pods. Important distinction: kube-proxy handles Service networking, while the CNI plugin (Calico, Flannel, Cilium) handles pod-to-pod networking. On the CKA, if services aren't reachable but pods can ping each other by IP, suspect kube-proxy.

Q8
What is the Container Runtime Interface (CRI)? Name two CRI-compliant runtimes. Why was Docker's direct support removed in Kubernetes v1.24?
Show Answer
Answer

The CRI is a plugin interface that allows the kubelet to communicate with any container runtime without needing runtime-specific code. Two CRI-compliant runtimes:

  • containerd — the most widely used, extracted from Docker
  • CRI-O — purpose-built for Kubernetes, popular in OpenShift

Docker was removed because it doesn't implement CRI directly. Kubernetes used a shim layer called dockershim to translate CRI calls to Docker API calls, adding unnecessary complexity. Docker internally uses containerd anyway, so removing the shim layer simplified the architecture.

Explanation

This is a commonly tested conceptual question. Key point: Docker images still work perfectly — OCI (Open Container Initiative) images are universal. Only the runtime changed, not the image format. After v1.24, you use crictl (not docker commands) to inspect containers directly on nodes. The CKA expects you to use crictl ps, crictl logs, and crictl inspect for node-level troubleshooting.

Q9
What is the difference between imperative and declarative management in Kubernetes? Which approach is recommended for the CKA exam and why?
Show Answer
Answer

Imperative: Tell Kubernetes what to do step by step — kubectl create, kubectl run, kubectl expose. Fast, one-off, no YAML needed.

Declarative: Tell Kubernetes what you want — write a YAML manifest and kubectl apply -f. Idempotent, version-controllable, repeatable.

CKA strategy: Use imperative commands to generate YAML, then edit and apply declaratively:

# Generate YAML without creating the resource
kubectl run nginx --image=nginx --dry-run=client -o yaml > pod.yaml

# Edit the YAML as needed, then apply
kubectl apply -f pod.yaml
Explanation

On the CKA exam, speed matters. The hybrid approach — imperative generation + declarative application — is the gold standard. Use --dry-run=client -o yaml to scaffold YAML instantly, then tweak it in vim. This saves massive time compared to writing YAML from scratch. Pure imperative commands (kubectl create) work for simple tasks, but for complex configurations (multi-container pods, volumes, probes), you'll need the YAML approach.

Q10
You've been asked to verify the health of a Kubernetes cluster during the CKA exam. Write the sequence of commands you would run and explain what each reveals about cluster health.
Show Answer
Answer
# 1. Check all nodes are Ready
kubectl get nodes

# 2. Check control plane component pods
kubectl get pods -n kube-system

# 3. Check cluster endpoint info
kubectl cluster-info

# 4. Check for cluster-wide events/issues
kubectl get events -A --sort-by='.lastTimestamp' | tail -20

# 5. Check system pod health across all namespaces
kubectl get pods -A | grep -v Running

# 6. Verify DNS is working
kubectl run dns-test --image=busybox --rm -it --restart=Never -- nslookup kubernetes

# 7. If a node is NotReady, SSH and check kubelet
ssh node01
sudo systemctl status kubelet
sudo journalctl -u kubelet --no-pager | tail -30
Explanation

Cluster health verification is a fundamental CKA skill. The commands above form a systematic top-down diagnosis: nodes → control plane pods → system events → DNS → individual node logs. Key things to look for: (1) All nodes Ready, (2) All kube-system pods Running, (3) No CrashLoopBackOff or Error pods, (4) DNS resolving the kubernetes service. If a component is down, check its static pod manifest in /etc/kubernetes/manifests/ and the kubelet logs on that node.

Chapter 2: Docker and Containers — The Building Blocks

Learn containers, Docker basics, and container runtimes — the foundation Kubernetes builds on. As a CKA administrator, you must understand how containers work because your cluster runs them.

🟢 Beginner ⏱️ ~2 hours 📖 Hands-On 🐳 Docker

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ Why containers solve the "works on my machine" problem
  • ✅ Containers vs Virtual Machines — when to use each
  • ✅ Docker basics: Dockerfile, images, containers, registries
  • ✅ Building a Docker image for the anihpj Django app
  • ✅ Essential Docker commands every admin needs
  • ✅ Container runtimes: containerd, CRI-O, and why Docker was removed from K8s
🎓 CKA Relevance
Cluster Architecture 25% Troubleshooting 30%
Container Runtime Knowledge on the CKA

The CKA doesn't test Docker commands directly, but you must understand container runtimes because:

  • Cluster installation — you configure containerd/CRI-O as part of kubeadm setup
  • Troubleshooting — when pods fail, you may need to check runtime logs with crictl
  • Node issues — container runtime failures cause node NotReady status
  • Image management — understanding image layers helps debug ImagePullBackOff errors

2.1 Why Containers? The "Works on My Machine" Problem

Every developer has lived this nightmare: the app works perfectly on your laptop, but crashes in production. Containers solve this by packaging everything the app needs into a single portable unit.

The "Works on My Machine" Problem
  👩‍💻 Developer laptop:  "anihpj works perfectly!"
  🖥️ Production server:  "ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'django'" 💀

  WHY? Different Python version, missing packages,
  different OS, missing environment variables...

  SOLUTION: Pack EVERYTHING into a container 📦
  ┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
  │ 📦 Docker Container                  │
  │ ┌─────────────────────────────────┐ │
  │ │ Python 3.12                     │ │
  │ │ Django 5.0                      │ │
  │ │ gunicorn, psycopg2              │ │
  │ │ anihpj source code              │ │
  │ │ settings & config files         │ │
  │ └─────────────────────────────────┘ │
  │ Runs IDENTICALLY everywhere         │
  │ Laptop ✅  Staging ✅  Production ✅ │
  └─────────────────────────────────────┘
📖 Key Term: Container

A container is a lightweight, standalone, executable package that includes everything needed to run a piece of software: code, runtime, system tools, libraries, and settings. Containers share the host's OS kernel, making them much lighter than virtual machines.

📦
Portable

Same container runs on Linux, Mac, Windows, cloud, bare metal — identical behavior everywhere.

🔒
Isolated

Each container has its own filesystem, network, and process space. No conflicts between apps.

⚡
Fast

Start in seconds (not minutes like VMs). Share host kernel — no full OS boot needed.

📐
Lightweight

Megabytes (not gigabytes). Run dozens of containers on a single machine.

2.2 Containers vs Virtual Machines

Both containers and VMs provide isolation, but they work at fundamentally different levels. Understanding this helps you troubleshoot as a CKA admin.

VM Architecture vs Container Architecture
  VIRTUAL MACHINES                    CONTAINERS
  ┌─────────────────────┐            ┌─────────────────────┐
  │ ┌─────┐  ┌─────┐    │            │ ┌─────┐  ┌─────┐    │
  │ │App A│  │App B│    │            │ │App A│  │App B│    │
  │ ├─────┤  ├─────┤    │            │ ├─────┤  ├─────┤    │
  │ │Libs │  │Libs │    │            │ │Libs │  │Libs │    │
  │ ├─────┤  ├─────┤    │            │ └──┬──┘  └──┬──┘    │
  │ │Guest│  │Guest│    │            │    │ Shared  │       │
  │ │ OS  │  │ OS  │    │            │ ┌──▼────────▼──┐    │
  │ └─────┘  └─────┘    │            │ │Container Eng.│    │
