kubectl scale deployment myapp --replicas=5
kubectl set image deployment/myapp nginx=nginx:1.25
kubectl create namespace dev
kubectl label pod mypod env=prod
kubectl annotate pod mypod description="my pod"
📝 Chapter 4 Practice Drill

Timed challenge (target: 5 minutes):

  • 1. Create namespace drill-ns
  • 2. Create a deployment web in drill-ns with image nginx:1.24, 3 replicas
  • 3. Expose it as a ClusterIP service on port 80
  • 4. Update the image to nginx:1.25
  • 5. Verify the rollout: kubectl rollout status deployment/web -n drill-ns
  • 6. Roll back to previous version: kubectl rollout undo deployment/web -n drill-ns
  • 7. Verify the image is back to nginx:1.24

Key insight: This drill covers 3 CKA domains — Workloads (create/scale), Services (expose), and Troubleshooting (rollout history).

📝 Chapter 4 — CKA Practice Questions

Q1
Create a pod named web-pod in namespace production using the image nginx:1.25-alpine with the label app=web and a resource request of 100m CPU and 128Mi memory. Write the imperative command and the equivalent YAML.
Show Answer
Answer
# Imperative approach (generate YAML, then add resources)
kubectl run web-pod --image=nginx:1.25-alpine \
  --labels=app=web \
  -n production \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml > web-pod.yaml

# Then edit to add resources, or use the full YAML:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: web-pod
  namespace: production
  labels:
    app: web
spec:
  containers:
  - name: web-pod
    image: nginx:1.25-alpine
    resources:
      requests:
        cpu: "100m"
        memory: "128Mi"

# Apply it
kubectl apply -f web-pod.yaml
Explanation

This is a fundamental CKA task. The hybrid approach — imperative generation + declarative editing — is the fastest strategy. Key points: (1) kubectl run only creates pods (not deployments since v1.18+). (2) --dry-run=client -o yaml generates YAML without creating the resource. (3) Resource requests affect scheduling — the scheduler only places the pod on nodes with at least 100m CPU and 128Mi memory available. (4) Always create the namespace first if it doesn't exist: kubectl create namespace production.

Q2
What is the relationship between a Deployment, a ReplicaSet, and a Pod? If you create a Deployment with 3 replicas, how many of each object exist?
Show Answer
Answer

The hierarchy is: Deployment → ReplicaSet → Pods

Deployment (nginx-deploy)
  └── ReplicaSet (nginx-deploy-7d4f6b9c8)     ← 1 ReplicaSet
        ├── Pod (nginx-deploy-7d4f6b9c8-abc12)  ← Pod 1
        ├── Pod (nginx-deploy-7d4f6b9c8-def34)  ← Pod 2
        └── Pod (nginx-deploy-7d4f6b9c8-ghi56)  ← Pod 3

Total: 1 Deployment + 1 ReplicaSet + 3 Pods = 5 objects

Ownership chain: Each object has an ownerReference pointing to its parent. Deleting the Deployment cascades down — deletes the ReplicaSet, which deletes the pods.

Explanation

Understanding this hierarchy is critical for CKA troubleshooting. Key points: (1) You should never create ReplicaSets directly — always use Deployments. (2) During rolling updates, the Deployment creates a new ReplicaSet and scales it up while scaling the old one down. (3) Old ReplicaSets are kept (with 0 replicas) for rollback — controlled by spec.revisionHistoryLimit (default: 10). (4) If you see orphaned pods not managed by a Deployment, check for missing ownerReferences.

Q3
Explain the three Service types: ClusterIP, NodePort, and LoadBalancer. For each type, describe who can access the service and provide an example command to create one.
Show Answer
Answer
Type Accessible From Port Range Use Case
ClusterIP Inside cluster only Any Internal services (DB, cache)
NodePort External via node IP 30000-32767 Dev/test external access
LoadBalancer External via cloud LB Any Production external access 
# ClusterIP (default)
kubectl expose deployment nginx --port=80 --target-port=80

# NodePort
kubectl expose deployment nginx --type=NodePort --port=80 --target-port=80

# LoadBalancer
kubectl expose deployment nginx --type=LoadBalancer --port=80 --target-port=80
Explanation

Services provide stable networking for ephemeral pods. Each Service gets a permanent ClusterIP and DNS name. Key CKA concepts: (1) NodePort automatically creates a ClusterIP underneath — it's an extension. (2) LoadBalancer creates both ClusterIP and NodePort underneath. (3) The selector in the Service spec must match labels on the pods — if they don't match, the service has no endpoints. (4) kubectl expose is the fastest way to create a service on the CKA exam.

Q4
You create a Deployment with kubectl create deployment myapp --image=myapp:v1 --replicas=3. Now you need to update the image to myapp:v2. What command do you use, and what happens internally during the rolling update?
Show Answer
Answer
# Update the image
kubectl set image deployment/myapp myapp=myapp:v2

# Watch the rollout
kubectl rollout status deployment/myapp

# What happens internally:
# 1. Deployment controller creates a NEW ReplicaSet (myapp-v2-xxxxx)
# 2. Scales new RS up: 0 → 1
# 3. Scales old RS down: 3 → 2
# 4. Repeats until new RS has 3 and old RS has 0
# (controlled by maxSurge and maxUnavailable)
# Check rollout history
kubectl rollout history deployment/myapp

# Rollback if v2 is broken
kubectl rollout undo deployment/myapp

# Rollback to a specific revision
kubectl rollout undo deployment/myapp --to-revision=1
Explanation

Rolling updates are a core CKA task. The Deployment's strategy.rollingUpdate settings control the pace: maxSurge (how many extra pods above desired count) and maxUnavailable (how many pods can be down during the update). Default is 25% for both. The old ReplicaSet is kept with 0 replicas so you can rollout undo. Pro tip: to record the change cause, add --record (deprecated but still works) or annotate the deployment with kubernetes.io/change-cause.

Q5
What are Labels and Selectors in Kubernetes? How do they connect Services to Pods? Give an example where a Service fails to route traffic because of a label mismatch.
Show Answer
Answer

Labels are key-value pairs attached to objects. Selectors are queries that match labels to connect objects together.

# Pod has label: app=web
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: web-pod
  labels:
    app: web        # ← Label
    version: v1

# Service selects pods with app=web
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: web-svc
spec:
  selector:
    app: web        # ← Must EXACTLY match pod label
  ports:
  - port: 80
    targetPort: 8080

# ❌ BROKEN: Service selector doesn't match pod labels
spec:
  selector:
    app: frontend   # ← "frontend" ≠ "web" → NO endpoints!
# Diagnose: check if service has endpoints
kubectl get endpoints web-svc
# If ENDPOINTS shows <none>, selector doesn't match any pods
Explanation

Label/selector mismatches are one of the most common CKA troubleshooting scenarios. If traffic doesn't reach pods: (1) Check kubectl get endpoints <svc> — empty means no matching pods. (2) Compare kubectl get svc <svc> -o yaml | grep -A 5 selector with kubectl get pods --show-labels. (3) Labels must match exactly — app: web ≠ App: web (case sensitive). ReplicaSets and Deployments also use selectors to find their pods — same matching rules apply.

Q6
Create a Namespace called exam-prep, then create a Deployment named api-server in that namespace with 2 replicas of httpd:2.4. Finally, expose it as a NodePort service on port 80. Write all the imperative commands.
Show Answer
Answer
# Step 1: Create namespace
kubectl create namespace exam-prep

# Step 2: Create deployment
kubectl create deployment api-server \
  --image=httpd:2.4 \
  --replicas=2 \
  -n exam-prep

# Step 3: Expose as NodePort
kubectl expose deployment api-server \
  --type=NodePort \
  --port=80 \
  --target-port=80 \
  -n exam-prep

# Verify everything
kubectl get all -n exam-prep

# Access the service
kubectl get svc api-server -n exam-prep
# Note the NodePort (e.g., 31234)
# Access via: http://<node-ip>:31234
Explanation

This 3-command sequence is a bread-and-butter CKA task. Timing tip: this should take under 60 seconds. Key points: (1) Always specify -n <namespace> or use kubectl config set-context --current --namespace=exam-prep to avoid mistakes. (2) --port is the service port, --target-port is the container port. (3) Kubernetes auto-assigns a NodePort from 30000-32767 unless you specify --node-port=XXXXX. (4) kubectl get all shows deployments, replicasets, pods, and services in one view.

Q7
What is the difference between kubectl create and kubectl apply? When would you use each one on the CKA exam?
Show Answer
Answer
Feature kubectl create kubectl apply
Type Imperative Declarative
If exists Error: "already exists" Updates (merges changes)
Idempotent No Yes
Best for First-time creation, speed Updates, CI/CD, repeatability 
# CKA strategy: use create for speed, apply for edits
kubectl create deployment web --image=nginx --dry-run=client -o yaml > web.yaml
vim web.yaml  # add resources, probes, volumes
kubectl apply -f web.yaml

# Later, edit and re-apply
vim web.yaml  # change replicas, image
kubectl apply -f web.yaml  # applies only the diff
Explanation

On the CKA, use create with --dry-run=client -o yaml to generate quick scaffolds, then apply to submit them. If a question says "create," either works. If a question says "update" or "modify," you need apply or kubectl edit or kubectl patch. Pro tip: kubectl apply stores the last-applied configuration as an annotation, enabling three-way merge diffs on subsequent applies.

Q8
A pod is in Pending state. When you describe it, events show: "0/3 nodes are available: 3 Insufficient cpu". What does this mean and what are your options to fix it?
Show Answer
Answer

The scheduler cannot find any node with enough free CPU to satisfy the pod's resources.requests.cpu. Options to fix:

  1. Reduce the pod's CPU request: 
    kubectl edit pod <name>  # or edit the deployment
# Change resources.requests.cpu to a lower value
  2. Free up resources by deleting/scaling down other workloads: 
    kubectl top pods -A --sort-by=cpu | head -10
kubectl scale deployment heavy-app --replicas=1
  3. Add more nodes to the cluster to provide additional capacity
  4. Check ResourceQuotas — a namespace quota may be artificially limiting available resources: 
    kubectl get resourcequota -n <namespace>
Explanation

The "Insufficient" message is from the scheduler's filtering phase. It checked all 3 nodes and none had enough allocatable CPU. On the CKA, kubectl describe pod events section is your best friend for diagnosing Pending pods. Other common Pending causes: node taints without matching tolerations, node selector/affinity not matching, PVC unbound (waiting for PV). Always read the specific message — it tells you exactly which filter failed and on how many nodes.

Q9
What are Annotations in Kubernetes? How do they differ from Labels, and when would you use annotations instead of labels?
Show Answer
Answer
Feature Labels Annotations
Purpose Identify and select objects Attach non-identifying metadata
Used in selectors Yes (selector: app=web) No
Size limit 63 chars value 256KB total
Example app: web, tier: frontend description: "Main web app" 
# Add annotation
kubectl annotate deployment web kubernetes.io/change-cause="Updated to v2"

# Add label
kubectl label pod web-pod tier=frontend

# Filter by label (annotations can't be used in selectors)
kubectl get pods -l tier=frontend
Explanation

Labels are for identification and grouping. Annotations are for metadata storage. Common annotations you'll see on the CKA: (1) kubernetes.io/change-cause — records why a rollout happened, (2) kubectl.kubernetes.io/last-applied-configuration — stored by kubectl apply, (3) Ingress controller annotations (e.g., nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/rewrite-target). The CKA may ask you to annotate objects or add labels for selection — know the syntax for both.

Q10
You need to scale a Deployment from 3 replicas to 5, then verify the rollout completed. What are three different ways to accomplish this on the CKA exam?
Show Answer
Answer
# Method 1: kubectl scale (fastest)
kubectl scale deployment myapp --replicas=5

# Method 2: kubectl edit (in-place edit)
kubectl edit deployment myapp
# Change spec.replicas from 3 to 5, save and exit

# Method 3: kubectl patch (JSON patch)
kubectl patch deployment myapp -p '{"spec":{"replicas":5}}'

# Verify (all methods)
kubectl rollout status deployment myapp
kubectl get deployment myapp
# Ready: 5/5
Explanation

kubectl scale is the fastest method for the CKA exam. However, kubectl edit is useful when you need to change multiple fields at once. kubectl patch is scriptable and good for automation. Key insight: scaling does NOT trigger a rollout — it only changes the replica count on the current ReplicaSet. Only image changes, env changes, or other spec updates trigger new rollouts. Verify scaling with kubectl get deployment — check that READY shows 5/5, not 3/5 (which would indicate pods are still starting). You can also use kubectl get pods -l app=myapp to see all 5 pods.

🟢 Part 2: Getting Started — Hands-On Basics Chapters 5-8

Chapter 5: Setting Up Your Environment

Before you can manage a Kubernetes cluster, you need one to practice on. This chapter walks you through installing the tools, connecting to a cluster, managing multiple contexts, and getting your environment CKA-exam ready. Everything uses our anihpj/jobpost Django project as the target workload.

🟢 Beginner ⏱️ ~1 hour 🛠️ Setup 💻 Hands-On

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ Choose the right local Kubernetes tool — Minikube, Kind, k3s, kubeadm, Docker Desktop
  • ✅ Install and start Minikube with the optimal settings for CKA practice
  • ✅ Install kubectl and verify it connects to your cluster
  • ✅ Understand kubeconfig — the file that controls all cluster connections
  • ✅ Switch between multiple clusters and contexts (directly tested on CKA)
  • ✅ Verify cluster health and enable essential addons
🎓 CKA Relevance
Cluster Architecture 25% Troubleshooting 30%
The CKA Exam Environment

On the CKA exam you'll be given multiple pre-built clusters and asked to switch between them. You must know kubectl config use-context and how kubeconfig works — it's tested in the very first instruction of most questions. Practice context switching until it's muscle memory.

  • Context switching — Every question starts with: "Use context: k8s-cluster-X". You must switch before answering.
  • kubectl is pre-installed — But knowing aliases and auto-completion saves critical exam time.
  • Bookmark kubernetes.io/docs — The only allowed reference during the exam.

5.1 Choosing a Local Kubernetes Tool

There are several tools to run Kubernetes locally. Each has trade-offs — the right choice depends on what you're practicing for:

Tool Best For Nodes Resources Install CKA Prep?
Minikube Learning, interactive 1 → multi Medium Easy ✅ Start here
Kind CI/CD, testing Multi Low Easy ✅ Good
k3s Edge, lightweight Multi Very Low Easy ⚠️ Differs from full K8s
kubeadm Real clusters Multi High Manual ⚠️ ✅ Must learn
Docker Desktop Windows/Mac dev 1 Medium Very Easy ⚠️ Limited
💡 CKA Learning Path Recommendation

Start with Minikube for Chapters 5-8 (instant setup, great for learning). Then switch to kubeadm in Part 3 (Chapter 9) to practice building clusters from scratch — this is directly tested on the CKA exam.

🖥️ What the CKA Exam Environment Actually Provides

Knowing what's pre-installed in the exam environment helps you practice in realistic conditions:

✅
Pre-Installed Tools
  • • kubectl with bash completion
  • • jq for JSON processing
  • • tmux for terminal multiplexing
  • • curl and wget
  • • vim / nano editors
  • • systemctl for service management
📚
Allowed References
  • • kubernetes.io/docs — Official docs
  • • kubernetes.io/blog — K8s blog
  • • github.com/kubernetes — Source repo
  • • In-browser terminal (no local apps)
  • • One browser tab for docs only
  • • No personal notes or bookmarks
⚙️
Exam Clusters
  • • 6 pre-built clusters provided
  • • Mix of single & multi-node
  • • Different K8s versions possible
  • • Must switch context per question
  • • SSH access to nodes when needed
  • • sudo available on nodes
⏱️
Exam Format
  • • 2 hours total duration
  • • 15-20 questions (performance-based)
  • • 66% passing score
  • • Questions weighted differently
  • • Can flag & return to questions
  • • Free retake if first attempt fails
CKA Tool Progression
  Your CKA Journey:

  Stage 1 (Now)          Stage 2 (Part 3)       Stage 3 (Exam)
  ┌──────────────┐       ┌──────────────┐       ┌──────────────┐
  │  🐣 Minikube  │──────▶│  🔧 kubeadm   │──────▶│  🎯 Exam Env  │
  │  1 command    │       │  Full install │       │  Pre-built    │
  │  start fast   │       │  manual setup │       │  clusters     │
  │  learn basics │       │  real skills  │       │  context switch│
  └──────────────┘       └──────────────┘       └──────────────┘

5.2 Installing Minikube (Quick Start)

Minikube creates a single-node Kubernetes cluster inside a VM or container on your laptop. One command and you have a fully functional cluster.

Installation by OS

🪟
Windows

choco install minikube
or
winget install minikube

🍎
macOS

brew install minikube

🐧
Linux (x86)

curl -LO https://storage.googleapis.com/minikube/releases/latest/minikube-linux-amd64
sudo install minikube-linux-amd64 /usr/local/bin/minikube

🐳
Driver

Requires Docker, VirtualBox, or Hyper-V. Docker is recommended.

Starting Your First Cluster

terminal 
# Start a cluster with optimal CKA practice settings
minikube start --driver=docker --cpus=2 --memory=4096

# Expected output:
# 😄  minikube v1.33.0 on Darwin 14.0
# ✨  Using the docker driver based on user configuration
# 📦  Preparing Kubernetes v1.30.0 on Docker 26.0.1...
# 🔗  Configuring bridge networking...
# 🏄  Done! kubectl is now configured to use "minikube" cluster

# Check status
minikube status
# minikube
# type: Control Plane
# host: Running
# kubelet: Running
# apiserver: Running
# kubeconfig: Configured

# For multi-node practice (CKA-relevant):
minikube start --nodes=3 --driver=docker --cpus=2 --memory=4096
📖 Multi-Node Practice

Use --nodes=3 when practicing node operations like kubectl drain, kubectl cordon, and node troubleshooting — these are heavily tested on the CKA. Single-node is fine for basic learning.

Minikube Architecture — What's Running Inside
  Your Machine (Host OS)
  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                                                                │
  │   Docker Engine / Hypervisor                                   │
  │   ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
  │   │  Minikube Node (VM or Container)                       │   │
  │   │                                                        │   │
  │   │  ┌──────────── Control Plane ──────────────────────┐   │   │
  │   │  │  kube-apiserver    ← All kubectl commands go here│  │   │
  │   │  │  etcd              ← Cluster state database      │  │   │
  │   │  │  kube-scheduler    ← Assigns pods to node        │  │   │
  │   │  │  controller-manager ← Manages deployments, RS    │  │   │
  │   │  │  CoreDNS           ← Internal DNS for Services   │  │   │
  │   │  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │   │
  │   │                                                        │   │
  │   │  ┌──────────── Worker Components ──────────────────┐   │   │
  │   │  │  kubelet           ← Runs pods on this node      │  │   │
  │   │  │  kube-proxy        ← Network rules (Services)    │  │   │
  │   │  │  containerd        ← Container runtime           │  │   │
  │   │  │  ┌─────┐ ┌─────┐ ┌─────┐                       │  │   │
  │   │  │  │Pod 1│ │Pod 2│ │Pod 3│  ← Your workloads      │  │   │
  │   │  │  └─────┘ └─────┘ └─────┘                       │  │   │
  │   │  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │   │
  │   └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
  │                                                                │
  │   kubectl (your laptop) ──HTTPS──▶ API Server :8443            │
  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Key insight: Minikube runs BOTH control plane AND worker on the SAME node.
  Real clusters separate these onto different machines.

🔧 Troubleshooting Common Minikube Issues

If Minikube doesn't start cleanly, try these fixes:

Error Likely Cause Fix
Exiting due to DRV_NOT_DETECTED Docker/Hyper-V not running Start Docker Desktop, then retry
insufficient memory Not enough RAM allocated minikube start --memory=2048 (lower)
Unable to connect to the server Cluster crashed or IP changed minikube delete && minikube start
apiserver not responding Stale cluster state minikube stop && minikube start
Slow startup (> 5 min) First-time image pull Normal — K8s images are large (~1 GB)
minikube-lifecycle.sh 
# Minikube lifecycle commands (know these)
minikube start         # Start cluster
minikube stop          # Stop cluster (preserves state)
minikube delete        # Destroy cluster (fresh start)
minikube pause         # Pause — free CPU but keep state
minikube unpause       # Resume paused cluster
minikube ssh           # SSH into the Minikube node
minikube ip            # Get the Minikube node IP address
minikube logs          # View Minikube system logs (for debugging)

5.3 Installing kubectl

kubectl is the CLI tool you'll use to interact with any Kubernetes cluster — local or remote, Minikube or production. Think of it as the "remote control" for your cluster.

installation-per-os.sh 
# Windows
choco install kubernetes-cli

# macOS
brew install kubectl

# Linux
curl -LO "https://dl.k8s.io/release/$(curl -L -s https://dl.k8s.io/release/stable.txt)/bin/linux/amd64/kubectl"
sudo install kubectl /usr/local/bin/kubectl

# Verify installation
kubectl version --client
# Client Version: v1.30.0
# Kustomize Version: v5.0.4
How kubectl Connects to Your Cluster
  ┌─────────────────┐         HTTPS (TLS)          ┌─────────────────────┐
  │  YOU             │                               │  KUBERNETES CLUSTER │
  │                  │    ~/.kube/config tells        │                     │
  │  $ kubectl get   │    kubectl WHERE and WHO       │  ┌───────────────┐ │
  │    pods          │──────────────────────────────▶ │  │  API Server   │ │
  │                  │    1. Read kubeconfig           │  │  :6443        │ │
  │  ┌────────────┐  │    2. Find server URL           │  └───────┬───────┘ │
  │  │  kubectl   │  │    3. Authenticate (cert/token) │          │         │
  │  └────────────┘  │    4. Send REST request         │          ▼         │
  │                  │                                 │  ┌───────────────┐ │
  │  ┌────────────┐  │    5. Receive JSON response      │  │    etcd       │ │
  │  │ kubeconfig │  │◀ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─  │  │  (data store) │ │
  │  └────────────┘  │    6. Format & display           │  └───────────────┘ │
  └─────────────────┘                                 └─────────────────────┘
💡 CKA Speed Boost: Shell Aliases & Autocomplete

Set these up immediately — they save massive time on the exam:

~/.bashrc 
# Essential CKA aliases — add to ~/.bashrc
alias k='kubectl'
alias kg='kubectl get'
alias kd='kubectl describe'
alias kaf='kubectl apply -f'
alias kdel='kubectl delete'
alias kl='kubectl logs'
alias ke='kubectl exec -it'
alias kns='kubectl config set-context --current --namespace'

# Enable auto-completion (provided on CKA exam)
source <(kubectl completion bash)
complete -o default -F __start_kubectl k

# Quick dry-run alias
alias kdr='kubectl --dry-run=client -o yaml'

# Verify aliases work
k get nodes

🔌 kubectl Plugins Worth Knowing

While not allowed on the CKA exam, these plugins accelerate daily work. Install via krew (the kubectl plugin manager):

Plugin What It Does Install Example
kubectx Fast context switching (interactive) kubectl krew install ctx kubectl ctx dev
kubens Fast namespace switching kubectl krew install ns kubectl ns anihpj-dev
neat Clean up -o yaml output (remove clutter) kubectl krew install neat kubectl get pod nginx -o yaml | kubectl neat
tree Show resource ownership hierarchy kubectl krew install tree kubectl tree deployment nginx

5.4 Understanding kubeconfig

The ~/.kube/config file is the single most important configuration file for kubectl. It tells kubectl where to connect, who you are, and which namespace to use by default. The CKA exam relies heavily on this.

kubeconfig — The 3 Building Blocks
  ~/.kube/config
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                                                          │
  │  clusters:       ← WHERE to connect                     │
  │  ├── minikube    (https://192.168.49.2:8443)            │
  │  ├── staging     (https://staging.k8s.example.com)      │
  │  └── prod        (https://prod.k8s.example.com)         │
  │                                                          │
  │  users:          ← WHO you are                           │
  │  ├── minikube-user   (client certificate)               │
  │  ├── dev-user        (token)                             │
  │  └── admin-user      (client certificate)               │
  │                                                          │
  │  contexts:       ← cluster + user + namespace            │
  │  ├── minikube    (minikube + minikube-user)              │
  │  ├── dev         (staging + dev-user + anihpj-dev)      │
  │  └── prod        (prod + admin-user + anihpj-prod)      │
  │                                                          │
  │  current-context: minikube   ← active connection         │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Context = Cluster + User + Namespace
  ┌────────────┐   ┌────────────┐   ┌──────────────┐
  │  🖥️ Cluster  │ + │  👤 User    │ + │  📂 Namespace │
  │  WHERE      │   │  WHO       │   │  SCOPE        │
  └────────────┘   └────────────┘   └──────────────┘
             \\          │          /
              ▼          ▼        ▼
         ┌──────────────────────────┐
         │  📋 Context = all three   │
         │  "dev" = staging +        │
         │  dev-user + anihpj-dev    │
         └──────────────────────────┘
~/.kube/config 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Config
current-context: minikube        # ← active context

clusters:
- cluster:
    certificate-authority: /home/user/.minikube/ca.crt
    server: https://192.168.49.2:8443
  name: minikube

users:
- name: minikube-user
  user:
    client-certificate: /home/user/.minikube/profiles/minikube/client.crt
    client-key: /home/user/.minikube/profiles/minikube/client.key

contexts:
- context:
    cluster: minikube
    user: minikube-user
    namespace: default           # ← default namespace for this context
  name: minikube
⚠️ CKA Exam Warning: Always Check Your Context

Every CKA question specifies which context to use. Running commands in the wrong context = wrong cluster = wrong answer = zero points. Always run kubectl config current-context before answering.

5.5 Managing Multiple Clusters and Contexts

In real environments (and on the CKA exam), you work with multiple clusters. Context management commands are your lifeline.

context-management.sh 
# List all contexts
kubectl config get-contexts
# CURRENT   NAME       CLUSTER    AUTHINFO        NAMESPACE
# *         minikube   minikube   minikube-user   default
#           dev        staging    dev-user        anihpj-dev
#           prod       prod       admin-user      anihpj-prod

# Show current context
kubectl config current-context
# minikube

# Switch to a different context
kubectl config use-context dev
# Switched to context "dev".

# Set default namespace for current context
kubectl config set-context --current --namespace=anihpj-dev
# Context "dev" modified.

# Create a new context
kubectl config set-context anihpj-staging \
  --cluster=staging \
  --user=dev-user \
  --namespace=anihpj-staging

# Merge multiple kubeconfig files
export KUBECONFIG=~/.kube/config:~/.kube/config-staging:~/.kube/config-prod
kubectl config get-contexts    # shows all merged contexts
📋Read Question
→
🔄Switch Context
→
✅Verify Context
→
⌨️Answer Question
💡 Practice Exercise: Simulate CKA Context Switching

Create multiple contexts on Minikube to practice the exact workflow used on the CKA exam:

cka-context-practice.sh 
# Setup: Create 3 simulated contexts (all point to minikube)
kubectl config set-context cka-cluster1 \
  --cluster=minikube --user=minikube --namespace=default
kubectl config set-context cka-cluster2 \
  --cluster=minikube --user=minikube --namespace=kube-system
kubectl config set-context cka-cluster3 \
  --cluster=minikube --user=minikube --namespace=anihpj-dev

# ── Simulate CKA Question 1 ──
# "Task: Use context: cka-cluster1. List all pods in default namespace."
kubectl config use-context cka-cluster1
kubectl config current-context   # Verify: cka-cluster1
kubectl get pods

# ── Simulate CKA Question 2 ──
# "Task: Use context: cka-cluster2. Find kube-proxy pods."
kubectl config use-context cka-cluster2
kubectl config current-context   # Verify: cka-cluster2
kubectl get pods | grep kube-proxy

# ── Simulate CKA Question 3 ──
# "Task: Use context: cka-cluster3. Scale deployment anihpj to 3 replicas."
kubectl config use-context cka-cluster3
kubectl config current-context   # Verify: cka-cluster3
kubectl scale deployment anihpj --replicas=3

# Cleanup practice contexts
kubectl config delete-context cka-cluster1
kubectl config delete-context cka-cluster2
kubectl config delete-context cka-cluster3
kubectl config use-context minikube

5.6 Verifying Your Cluster

After setting up your environment, verify everything works. These are the same commands you'll use on the CKA to orient yourself in unfamiliar clusters.

cluster-verification.sh 
# 1. Cluster info — where is the API server?
kubectl cluster-info
# Kubernetes control plane is running at https://192.168.49.2:8443
# CoreDNS is running at https://192.168.49.2:8443/api/v1/.../kube-dns:dns

# 2. Node status — are all nodes Ready?
kubectl get nodes -o wide
# NAME       STATUS   ROLES           AGE   VERSION   INTERNAL-IP     OS-IMAGE
# minikube   Ready    control-plane   5m    v1.30.0   192.168.49.2    Ubuntu 22.04

# 3. System pods — is the control plane healthy?
kubectl get pods -n kube-system
# NAME                               READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE
# coredns-7db6d8ff4d-xxxxx           1/1     Running   0          5m
# etcd-minikube                      1/1     Running   0          5m
# kube-apiserver-minikube            1/1     Running   0          5m
# kube-controller-manager-minikube   1/1     Running   0          5m
# kube-proxy-xxxxx                   1/1     Running   0          5m
# kube-scheduler-minikube            1/1     Running   0          5m

# 4. API health check
kubectl get --raw=/healthz
# ok

# 5. All namespaces overview
kubectl get namespaces
# NAME              STATUS   AGE
# default           Active   5m
# kube-node-lease   Active   5m
# kube-public       Active   5m
# kube-system       Active   5m
1
cluster-info

Confirm API server URL is reachable

2
get nodes

All nodes should show STATUS: Ready

3
get pods -n kube-system

All control plane pods should be Running

4
healthz endpoint

API should return "ok"

🚨 CKA Troubleshooting: Node Not Ready?

If kubectl get nodes shows NotReady, use these commands to diagnose:

Symptom Check Command Common Cause
Node STATUS: NotReady kubectl describe node <name> kubelet stopped, network plugin down
Pods stuck in Pending kubectl describe pod <name> No schedulable nodes, resource limits
kube-system pods CrashLoop kubectl logs -n kube-system <pod> etcd disk full, config errors
API server unreachable systemctl status kubelet (on node) kubelet service not running

5.7 Enabling Essential Addons

Minikube addons extend your cluster with features that are standard in production. Enable these for a realistic practice environment:

Addon What It Enables CKA Relevant? Command
metrics-server CPU/memory metrics, kubectl top ✅ Yes minikube addons enable metrics-server
ingress NGINX Ingress controller for HTTP routing ✅ Yes minikube addons enable ingress
dashboard Web UI for visual cluster management ⚠️ Not tested minikube addons enable dashboard
storage-provisioner Dynamic PV provisioning ✅ Yes Enabled by default
enable-addons.sh 
# Enable essential addons for CKA practice
minikube addons enable metrics-server
minikube addons enable ingress

# Verify addons are running
minikube addons list | grep enabled
# |  ingress          | minikube | enabled ✅ |
# |  metrics-server   | minikube | enabled ✅ |
# |  storage-provisioner | minikube | enabled ✅ |

# Verify metrics-server is working (wait ~60 seconds after enabling)
kubectl top nodes
# NAME       CPU(cores)   CPU%   MEMORY(bytes)   MEMORY%
# minikube   250m         12%    1024Mi          25%

📋 Complete Environment Readiness Checklist

Run this one-shot script to verify your entire environment is ready for CKA practice:

environment-readiness-check.sh 
#!/bin/bash
echo "══════════════════════════════════════════════"
echo "  CKA Environment Readiness Check"
echo "══════════════════════════════════════════════"

# 1. kubectl installed?
echo -n "✓ kubectl: "; kubectl version --client --short 2>/dev/null || echo "❌ NOT INSTALLED"

# 2. Cluster reachable?
echo -n "✓ Cluster: "; kubectl cluster-info 2>/dev/null | head -1 || echo "❌ NOT REACHABLE"

# 3. Nodes ready?
echo "✓ Nodes:"
kubectl get nodes -o wide 2>/dev/null || echo "  ❌ Cannot list nodes"

# 4. Control plane pods running?
echo "✓ Control Plane Components:"
kubectl get pods -n kube-system -o custom-columns=\
"NAME:.metadata.name,STATUS:.status.phase,RESTARTS:.status.containerStatuses[0].restartCount" \
2>/dev/null | grep -E "etcd|apiserver|scheduler|controller"

# 5. DNS working?
echo -n "✓ CoreDNS: "
kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns --no-headers 2>/dev/null | \
awk '{print $3}' || echo "❌"

# 6. Metrics server?
echo -n "✓ Metrics: "
kubectl top nodes 2>/dev/null | head -1 || echo "⚠️ Not enabled (run: minikube addons enable metrics-server)"

# 7. Alias configured?
echo -n "✓ Alias 'k': "
type k 2>/dev/null && echo "configured" || echo "⚠️ Add: alias k='kubectl'"

# 8. Bash completion?
echo -n "✓ Completion: "
complete -p kubectl 2>/dev/null && echo "active" || echo "⚠️ Run: source <(kubectl completion bash)"

echo "══════════════════════════════════════════════"
echo "  All checks complete!"
echo "══════════════════════════════════════════════"
📖 Chapter 5 Complete — Your Environment is Ready!

You now have a working Kubernetes cluster with Minikube, kubectl configured with auto-completion, and essential addons enabled. You understand kubeconfig and can switch between contexts. Next: mastering the kubectl commands you'll use every day.

⚠️ CKA Gotcha: Environment Setup Traps

  • Context switching — The CKA exam gives you multiple clusters. You MUST switch context before answering each question: kubectl config use-context <name>. Forgetting this = zero marks for that question
  • Namespace scope — Many questions specify a namespace. If you forget -n <namespace>, you'll create objects in default and lose marks
  • kubectl autocompletion — Pre-configured on the exam, but verify with kubectl <TAB>. If broken: source <(kubectl completion bash)
  • Minikube ≠ kubeadm — The CKA uses kubeadm clusters, NOT Minikube. kubeadm has real systemd services, real certificates, and real multi-node topology

CKA Speed Tip: Set these aliases at the START of your exam (they're usually pre-set but verify):
alias k=kubectl
export do="--dry-run=client -o yaml"
Now you can type: k run nginx --image=nginx $do > pod.yaml

⚡ Context & config commands you'll use every question: kubectl config get-contexts — list all available clusters
kubectl config use-context <name> — switch to the right cluster
kubectl config current-context — confirm which cluster you're on
kubectl config set-context --current --namespace=<ns> — set default namespace

📝 Chapter 5 — CKA Practice Questions

Q1
You've just started the CKA exam and need to connect to the cluster named cluster-prod. The exam question specifies namespace finance. Write the exact commands to switch context and set the default namespace.
Show Answer
Answer
# Step 1: Switch to the correct cluster context
kubectl config use-context cluster-prod

# Step 2: Set the default namespace for this context
kubectl config set-context --current --namespace=finance

# Verify
kubectl config current-context    # Should show: cluster-prod
kubectl config view --minify | grep namespace  # Should show: finance
Explanation

This is the FIRST thing you do for every CKA question. The exam provides ~6 different cluster contexts. If you forget to switch, you'll create resources on the wrong cluster and get zero marks. The --current flag modifies the currently active context. Setting the namespace means you don't have to type -n finance on every subsequent command. Always verify with kubectl config current-context.

Q2
Explain the structure of a kubeconfig file. What are the three main sections and how do they relate to each other?
Show Answer
Answer

A kubeconfig file has three main sections:

Section Contains Purpose
clusters Server URL + CA certificate Where to connect
users Client cert + key, or token Who you are
contexts cluster + user + namespace Links cluster to user 
# View kubeconfig
kubectl config view

# Default location
cat ~/.kube/config

# Use a custom kubeconfig
kubectl --kubeconfig=/path/to/config get pods
# OR
export KUBECONFIG=/path/to/config
Explanation

A context is a triple: (cluster, user, namespace). This is how kubectl knows which cluster to talk to, which credentials to present, and which namespace to use by default. The CKA expects you to understand this structure because you'll work with multiple contexts. You can merge multiple kubeconfig files: export KUBECONFIG=~/.kube/config:/path/to/other-config. The default kubeconfig is at ~/.kube/config.

Q3
Your cluster shows 3 nodes when you run kubectl get nodes, but one node shows NotReady. You need to SSH to that node and diagnose the issue. What are the first three things you check?
Show Answer
Answer
# From control plane: identify the NotReady node
kubectl get nodes -o wide
kubectl describe node <node-name> | grep -A 10 Conditions

# SSH to the node
ssh <node-name>

# Check 1: Is kubelet running?
sudo systemctl status kubelet
# If not: sudo systemctl start kubelet && sudo systemctl enable kubelet

# Check 2: Are there kubelet errors?
sudo journalctl -u kubelet --no-pager | tail -30

# Check 3: Is the container runtime running?
sudo systemctl status containerd
# If not: sudo systemctl start containerd
Explanation

A node shows NotReady when its kubelet hasn't sent a heartbeat to the API server within the timeout (default: 40 seconds). The three most common causes: (1) kubelet stopped — start it with systemctl, (2) kubelet config error — wrong certificate paths, wrong API server address in kubelet config, (3) container runtime down — containerd crashed or is not installed. On the CKA, always check systemctl status kubelet and journalctl -u kubelet — the logs tell you exactly what's wrong.

Q4
What is the difference between Minikube, Kind, k3s, and kubeadm? Which one does the CKA exam use and why does that matter for your practice?
Show Answer
Answer
Tool Nodes Best For CKA?
Minikube Single-node VM Local dev, learning basics ❌
Kind Docker containers CI/CD testing, multi-node ❌
k3s Lightweight VMs Edge, IoT, resource-constrained ❌
kubeadm Real multi-node Production clusters ✅ YES
Explanation

The CKA exam uses kubeadm-bootstrapped clusters with real VMs. This matters because kubeadm clusters have: (1) Real systemd services (kubelet), (2) Real TLS certificates in /etc/kubernetes/pki/, (3) Static pods in /etc/kubernetes/manifests/, (4) Real multi-node networking. Minikube hides all of this complexity. If you only practice on Minikube, you won't be prepared for node-level troubleshooting. Use kubeadm with Vagrant or cloud VMs for realistic practice.

Q5
Set up the essential bash aliases and environment variables for maximum CKA exam speed. Write the commands you would run at the start of the exam.
Show Answer
Answer
# Essential aliases (add to ~/.bashrc)
alias k=kubectl
alias kn='kubectl config set-context --current --namespace'
alias kgp='kubectl get pods'
alias kgn='kubectl get nodes'
alias kga='kubectl get all'
alias kd='kubectl describe'
alias kaf='kubectl apply -f'

# Environment variables for speed
export do="--dry-run=client -o yaml"
export now="--force --grace-period=0"

# Enable autocompletion for alias
complete -o default -F __start_kubectl k

# Verify setup
k get nodes        # Should work like kubectl get nodes
k run test --image=nginx $do   # Should output YAML
Explanation

The CKA exam usually pre-configures alias k=kubectl and bash completion, but always verify. The $do variable saves massive time: k run nginx --image=nginx $do > pod.yaml generates YAML without creating the resource. The $now variable force-deletes pods instantly: k delete pod stuck-pod $now. These shortcuts can save 5-10 minutes over a 2-hour exam. Practice using them so they're muscle memory on exam day.

Q6
You have two kubeconfig files: /tmp/admin.conf (for cluster-A) and ~/.kube/config (for cluster-B). How do you merge them and switch between clusters?
Show Answer
Answer
# Option 1: Merge via KUBECONFIG env variable
export KUBECONFIG=~/.kube/config:/tmp/admin.conf

# View merged config — both clusters visible
kubectl config get-contexts

# Switch between clusters
kubectl config use-context cluster-A
kubectl config use-context cluster-B

# Option 2: Permanently merge into one file
KUBECONFIG=~/.kube/config:/tmp/admin.conf kubectl config view --flatten > ~/.kube/config-merged
mv ~/.kube/config-merged ~/.kube/config

# Option 3: Use --kubeconfig for one-off commands
kubectl --kubeconfig=/tmp/admin.conf get nodes
Explanation

On the CKA exam, the kubeconfig is pre-configured with all exam contexts, but you may need to work with custom kubeconfig files in troubleshooting questions. The KUBECONFIG environment variable accepts a colon-separated list of files (semicolon on Windows). kubectl merges them at runtime. The --flatten flag resolves all relative paths and embedded certificates into a single self-contained file. Always back up before merging: cp ~/.kube/config ~/.kube/config.bak.

Q7
After setting up a local Kubernetes cluster, you run kubectl get nodes and the output shows STATUS: NotReady for all nodes. What is the most likely cause for a brand new cluster?
Show Answer
Answer

The most likely cause is that a CNI (Container Network Interface) plugin is not installed.

# Verify: check node conditions
kubectl describe node <node-name> | grep -i "ready\|network"
# Look for: "NetworkPluginNotReady message:Network plugin returns error: cni plugin not initialized"

# Fix: install a CNI plugin (e.g., Calico)
kubectl apply -f https://docs.projectcalico.org/manifests/calico.yaml

# Or Flannel
kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/flannel-io/flannel/master/Documentation/kube-flannel.yml

# Wait for pods to come up
kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep -E "calico|flannel"

# Check nodes again
kubectl get nodes   # Should now show Ready
Explanation

After kubeadm init, the cluster is initialized but nodes remain NotReady until a CNI plugin is installed. The CNI plugin is responsible for pod-to-pod networking. Without it, the kubelet reports the node as not ready because the network subsystem isn't functional. On the CKA, you may need to install a CNI plugin as part of a cluster setup question. Common choices: Calico (most feature-rich), Flannel (simplest), Cilium (eBPF-based). The pod network CIDR must match what you specified in kubeadm init --pod-network-cidr.

Q8
What Minikube addons are useful for CKA practice? Enable the metrics-server, ingress, and dashboard addons and explain what each provides.
Show Answer
Answer
# Enable addons
minikube addons enable metrics-server
minikube addons enable ingress
minikube addons enable dashboard

# Verify addons
minikube addons list | grep enabled

# What each provides:
# metrics-server → enables: kubectl top pods, kubectl top nodes
#   (resource monitoring — needed for HPA and debugging)
# ingress → NGINX ingress controller
#   (HTTP routing by hostname/path — CKA networking topic)
# dashboard → Web UI for cluster visualization
#   (not used on exam but helpful for learning)
Explanation

metrics-server is essential — without it, kubectl top commands fail, and HorizontalPodAutoscalers can't get CPU metrics. On the CKA exam, metrics-server is pre-installed. The ingress addon installs an NGINX Ingress Controller, which is required for Ingress resource questions. Note: on kubeadm clusters (like the exam), you install these as regular manifests/Helm charts, not Minikube addons. Practice both approaches.

Q9
How do you verify that your cluster is healthy and all essential components are running? Write the commands for a complete health check.
Show Answer
Answer
# 1. All nodes Ready?
kubectl get nodes

# 2. Control plane pods running?
kubectl get pods -n kube-system

# 3. CoreDNS functioning?
kubectl run dns-check --image=busybox --rm -it --restart=Never \
  -- nslookup kubernetes.default
# Should resolve to the API server ClusterIP

# 4. Cluster info
kubectl cluster-info

# 5. Can you create resources?
kubectl run health-check --image=nginx --restart=Never
kubectl get pod health-check
kubectl delete pod health-check

# 6. Metrics working? (if metrics-server installed)
kubectl top nodes
kubectl top pods -A
Explanation

Perform this health check after every cluster setup or modification. On the CKA, if something seems broken, start here. The DNS check is particularly important — many services depend on DNS resolution. If nslookup kubernetes.default fails from inside a pod, check: (1) CoreDNS pods in kube-system, (2) kube-dns Service exists, (3) kube-proxy is running (services need iptables rules). A quick kubectl run + kubectl delete verifies the entire pipeline: API server → scheduler → kubelet → runtime.

Q10
On the CKA exam, you switch to a context but realize you're working in the default namespace instead of kube-system. You've already created a pod in the wrong namespace. How do you fix this?
Show Answer
Answer
# Step 1: Delete the pod from the wrong namespace
kubectl delete pod my-pod -n default

# Step 2: Set the correct namespace
kubectl config set-context --current --namespace=kube-system

# Step 3: Recreate the pod in the correct namespace
kubectl run my-pod --image=nginx -n kube-system
# OR if using your saved YAML:
kubectl apply -f pod.yaml -n kube-system

# Verify it's in the right namespace
kubectl get pod my-pod -n kube-system

# Pro tip: you can also move resources by exporting and re-creating
kubectl get pod my-pod -n default -o yaml | \
  sed 's/namespace: default/namespace: kube-system/' | \
  kubectl apply -f -
kubectl delete pod my-pod -n default
Explanation

You cannot move a pod between namespaces — you must delete and recreate. This is a common CKA mistake that costs marks. Prevention strategies: (1) Always set the namespace at the start of each question: kubectl config set-context --current --namespace=X. (2) Always verify with kubectl config view --minify | grep namespace. (3) Include namespace: in the metadata section of every YAML manifest. Namespaces are a fundamental isolation boundary — resources in different namespaces are independent.

Chapter 6: kubectl — The Administrator's Command Center

kubectl is the single tool you'll use for every CKA exam question and every day as a Kubernetes administrator. This chapter breaks down the syntax, the essential commands, output formatting, and the critical difference between imperative and declarative approaches.

🟢 Beginner ⏱️ ~1.5 hours ⌨️ CLI 🎯 CKA Essential

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ The kubectl command anatomy — verb + resource + name + flags
  • ✅ Essential admin commands organized by category (get, describe, create, debug)
  • ✅ kubectl explain — your built-in API documentation during the exam
  • ✅ Imperative vs Declarative — when to use each and the dry-run trick
  • ✅ Output formatting with JSONPath, custom-columns, and label selectors
🎓 CKA Relevance
All Domains 100%
kubectl Speed = CKA Pass/Fail

The CKA is a timed, hands-on exam. You have 2 hours for 15-20 tasks. Every second counts. Administrators who type full resource names lose time. Administrators who know --dry-run=client -o yaml finish early. This chapter is the most important for exam speed.

  • Use aliases — k instead of kubectl saves ~8 characters per command
  • Use kubectl explain — faster than searching docs.kubernetes.io
  • Use imperative + dry-run — generate YAML skeletons in seconds

6.1 kubectl Syntax Pattern

Every kubectl command follows the same anatomy. Learning this pattern once lets you construct any command from memory:

kubectl Command Anatomy
  kubectl     get       pods      anihpj-xyz     -n anihpj-dev
  ───────    ─────    ───────    ───────────    ──────────────
  command    VERB     RESOURCE     NAME            FLAGS

  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  VERB (what to do):                                        │
  │  get, describe, create, apply, delete, edit, logs, exec,   │
  │  top, patch, label, annotate, scale, rollout, drain,       │
  │  cordon, uncordon, taint, auth, explain, port-forward      │
  │                                                            │
  │  RESOURCE (what to act on):                                │
  │  pods (po), deployments (deploy), services (svc),          │
  │  nodes (no), namespaces (ns), configmaps (cm),             │
  │  secrets, ingress (ing), pv, pvc, sa, roles, clusterroles  │
  │                                                            │
  │  FLAGS (modifiers):                                        │
  │  -n namespace, -o output, -l labels, --all-namespaces (-A),│
  │  --sort-by, --field-selector, -w (watch), -f (file/follow) │
  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
💡 Resource Short Names Save Time

Use abbreviations on the CKA: po=pods, deploy=deployments, svc=services, no=nodes, ns=namespaces, cm=configmaps, ing=ingress, pv=persistentvolumes, pvc=persistentvolumeclaims, sa=serviceaccounts. Find all with kubectl api-resources.

6.2 Essential Admin Commands — Daily Use

These are the commands you'll use every single day as a Kubernetes administrator. They're organized by what you're trying to do:

📋 Get Information

get-commands.sh 
# List resources
kubectl get nodes -o wide              # nodes with IP, OS, runtime
kubectl get pods -A                     # ALL pods in ALL namespaces
kubectl get pods -o yaml                # full YAML definition
kubectl get pods --sort-by=.status.startTime  # sorted by start time
kubectl get all -n anihpj-dev           # everything in namespace

# Resource usage (requires metrics-server)
kubectl top nodes                       # node CPU/memory usage
kubectl top pods -n anihpj-dev          # pod CPU/memory usage

🔍 Inspect Details

describe-commands.sh 
# Detailed info (events, conditions, volumes, etc.)
kubectl describe pod anihpj-xyz -n anihpj-dev
kubectl describe node worker-1
kubectl get events --sort-by=.lastTimestamp -n anihpj-dev

✏️ Create and Modify

create-modify-commands.sh 
# Apply manifests
kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml
kubectl create namespace anihpj-dev

# Edit live objects
kubectl edit deployment anihpj -n anihpj-dev
kubectl patch deployment anihpj -p '{"spec":{"replicas":5}}' -n anihpj-dev

# Labels and taints (CKA-important)
kubectl label node worker-1 disk=ssd
kubectl taint node worker-1 key=value:NoSchedule

🗑️ Delete and Clean Up

delete-commands.sh 
kubectl delete pod anihpj-xyz -n anihpj-dev
kubectl delete -f deployment.yaml
kubectl delete pods --field-selector status.phase=Failed -n anihpj-dev

🐛 Debug and Troubleshoot

debug-commands.sh 
# Logs
kubectl logs anihpj-xyz -n anihpj-dev
kubectl logs anihpj-xyz --previous -n anihpj-dev   # crashed container logs

# Interactive shell
kubectl exec -it anihpj-xyz -n anihpj-dev -- /bin/sh

# Port forwarding
kubectl port-forward pod/anihpj-xyz 8000:8000 -n anihpj-dev

# Debug a node (creates ephemeral debug pod)
kubectl debug node/worker-1 --image=busybox
📋
get

List resources. Add -o wide for extra detail.

🔍
describe

Deep dive. Shows events, conditions, volumes.

📝
logs

Container output. Add --previous for crashes.

🐚
exec -it

Shell into a running container for debugging.

🏷️ Labels & Selectors — The Glue of Kubernetes

Labels are key-value pairs attached to objects. Selectors use them to find and group resources. Services find pods by labels. Deployments manage pods by labels. Understanding this linkage is critical:

labels-and-selectors.sh 
# Add a label to a pod
kubectl label pod anihpj-xyz app=anihpj -n anihpj-dev

# Show labels
kubectl get pods --show-labels -n anihpj-dev
# NAME           READY   STATUS    LABELS
# anihpj-abc12   1/1     Running   app=anihpj,version=v1

# Filter by label (equality)
kubectl get pods -l app=anihpj

# Filter by label (set-based)
kubectl get pods -l 'app in (anihpj, nginx)'
kubectl get pods -l 'app notin (redis)'
kubectl get pods -l 'environment'              # has label
kubectl get pods -l '!environment'             # doesn't have label

# Remove a label (trailing minus sign)
kubectl label pod anihpj-xyz version- -n anihpj-dev

# Overwrite a label
kubectl label pod anihpj-xyz version=v2 --overwrite -n anihpj-dev
How Labels Connect Everything
  Deployment                  Service                    Ingress
  selector:                   selector:                  backend:
    matchLabels:                app: anihpj                service:
      app: anihpj                                           name: anihpj-svc
       │                          │
       │   Labels are the          │
       └───────────────┬──────────┘
                   │
                   ▼
       ┌──────────────────────┐
       │  Pod                      │
       │  labels:                  │
       │    app: anihpj  ◀── match  │
       │    version: v1            │
       └──────────────────────┘

  If labels don't match → Service has 0 endpoints → App unreachable!

⚡ Top 15 CKA Must-Know Commands — Quick Reference

These commands appear in nearly every CKA question. Memorize them:

# Task Command
1 Switch context kubectl config use-context <name>
2 Set default namespace kubectl config set-context --current --namespace=<ns>
3 Generate YAML from command kubectl run nginx --image=nginx --dry-run=client -o yaml
4 Create deployment kubectl create deploy <name> --image=<img> --replicas=<n>
5 Expose as service kubectl expose deploy <name> --port=80 --target-port=8000
6 Scale deployment kubectl scale deploy <name> --replicas=5
7 View pod logs kubectl logs <pod> [-c container] [--previous]
8 Shell into pod kubectl exec -it <pod> -- /bin/sh
9 Get all in namespace kubectl get all -n <namespace>
10 Describe resource kubectl describe <resource> <name> -n <ns>
11 Find field paths kubectl explain <resource>.spec --recursive
12 Drain a node kubectl drain <node> --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data
13 Cordon/uncordon node kubectl cordon <node> / kubectl uncordon <node>
14 Rollout status/undo kubectl rollout status deploy/<name> / kubectl rollout undo
15 Apply a manifest kubectl apply -f <file.yaml>

6.3 kubectl explain — Built-in API Documentation

kubectl explain is your best friend during the CKA exam. It shows you the exact fields available for any resource — no internet search needed. The exam allows access to kubernetes.io/docs, but explain is often faster.

kubectl-explain.sh 
# Top-level fields for a resource
kubectl explain pod
# KIND:     Pod
# VERSION:  v1
# FIELDS:   apiVersion, kind, metadata, spec, status

# Drill into nested fields
kubectl explain pod.spec
kubectl explain pod.spec.containers
kubectl explain pod.spec.containers.resources
kubectl explain pod.spec.containers.resources.limits

# See ALL fields recursively (great for finding field paths)
kubectl explain deployment.spec.strategy --recursive
# strategy     <Object>
#    rollingUpdate     <Object>
#       maxSurge       <string>
#       maxUnavailable <string>
#    type              <string>

# Find specific field names
kubectl explain pod.spec.containers.livenessProbe --recursive
kubectl explain — Drill-Down Navigation
  kubectl explain pod
       │
       ▼
  ┌─────────────────┐
  │ apiVersion       │
  │ kind             │
  │ metadata ────────┼──▶ kubectl explain pod.metadata
  │ spec ────────────┼──▶ kubectl explain pod.spec
  │ status           │         │
  └─────────────────┘         ▼
                         ┌─────────────────────┐
                         │ containers ──────────┼──▶ kubectl explain pod.spec.containers
                         │ volumes              │         │
                         │ nodeName             │         ▼
                         │ serviceAccountName   │    ┌──────────────────┐
                         │ tolerations          │    │ image             │
                         │ nodeSelector         │    │ ports             │
                         └─────────────────────┘    │ env               │
                                                     │ resources ────────┼──▶ .resources.limits
                                                     │ volumeMounts      │
                                                     │ livenessProbe     │
                                                     │ readinessProbe    │
                                                     └──────────────────┘
💡 CKA Pro Tip: Explain + Dry-Run = Speed

Forgot the field name for resource limits? kubectl explain pod.spec.containers.resources --recursive in 2 seconds. Then use kubectl run nginx --image=nginx --dry-run=client -o yaml to generate the skeleton and add the field. This combo is faster than searching docs.

6.4 Imperative vs Declarative — When to Use Each

Kubernetes supports two approaches to managing resources. Understanding when to use each is critical for both the CKA exam and production work.

Imperative vs Declarative — Two Approaches
  🏃 IMPERATIVE: "Do this NOW"               📝 DECLARATIVE: "Make it like THIS"
  ┌──────────────────────────────────┐       ┌──────────────────────────────────┐
  │ $ kubectl run anihpj \            │       │ $ kubectl apply -f deploy.yaml   │
  │     --image=anihpj:v1             │       │                                  │
  │ $ kubectl scale deploy anihpj \   │       │ deploy.yaml defines desired state│
  │     --replicas=5                  │       │ K8s figures out what to change   │
  │ $ kubectl expose deploy anihpj \  │       │                                  │
  │     --port=80                     │       │ Like ordering from a menu:       │
  │                                    │       │ "I want pasta" — kitchen handles │
  │ Like giving step-by-step cooking  │       │ the recipe                       │
  │ instructions to a chef            │       │                                  │
  │                                    │       │                                  │
  │ ✅ Fast for one-off tasks         │       │ ✅ Repeatable, version-controlled │
  │ ✅ Great for CKA exam speed       │       │ ✅ Production standard            │
  │ ❌ No record of what you did      │       │ ✅ GitOps friendly                │
  │ ❌ Hard to reproduce              │       │ ❌ Slower for quick tests         │
  └──────────────────────────────────┘       └──────────────────────────────────┘
Approach When to Use CKA Exam Production
Imperative Quick tasks, generate YAML ✅ Use for speed ❌ Avoid
Declarative Real environments, repeatable ✅ Required for complex tasks ✅ Always

The CKA Power Move: Imperative + Dry-Run = YAML Generator

dry-run-yaml-generators.sh 
# Generate Pod YAML (don't create, just output)
kubectl run anihpj --image=anihpj:v1 --port=8000 \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml > pod.yaml

# Generate Deployment YAML
kubectl create deployment anihpj --image=anihpj:v1 --replicas=3 \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml > deployment.yaml

# Generate Service YAML
kubectl expose deployment anihpj --port=80 --target-port=8000 \
  --type=ClusterIP --dry-run=client -o yaml > service.yaml

# Generate Namespace YAML
kubectl create namespace anihpj-dev \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml > namespace.yaml

# Generate ConfigMap YAML
kubectl create configmap anihpj-config \
  --from-literal=DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE=anihpj.settings \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml > configmap.yaml

# Then edit the generated YAML and apply:
vim deployment.yaml   # add resource limits, env vars, etc.
kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml
⚠️ CKA Exam Critical: --dry-run=client vs --dry-run=server

--dry-run=client validates locally (no API call), always use this for YAML generation. --dry-run=server validates against the API server (checks if resource already exists, validates references). Use server for pre-apply validation.

🎯 Real CKA Scenario: "Create a Deployment with Specific Requirements"

The exam gives you constraints: name, image, replicas, port, namespace, labels. Here's the fastest workflow:

cka-scenario-workflow.sh 
# STEP 1: Generate the skeleton (5 sec)
kubectl create deployment web-app \
  --image=nginx:1.25 \
  --replicas=3 \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml > web-app.yaml

# STEP 2: Edit to add requirements (30 sec)
# vim web-app.yaml
# → Add resource limits, nodeSelector, labels, etc.

# STEP 3: Validate before applying (5 sec)
kubectl apply -f web-app.yaml --dry-run=server
# deployment.apps/web-app created (server dry run)

# STEP 4: Apply for real (2 sec)
kubectl apply -f web-app.yaml

# STEP 5: Verify (5 sec)
kubectl get deployment web-app -o wide
kubectl get pods -l app=web-app

# Total: ~47 seconds → vs 3+ minutes writing YAML from scratch!
⚡Generate
→
✏️Edit
→
✅Validate
→
🚀Apply
→
🔍Verify

6.5 Output Formatting and Filtering

The CKA frequently asks you to extract specific information — pod names, node IPs, container images. Mastering output formatting separates fast solvers from slow ones.

Flag Purpose Example
-o wide Extra columns (IP, node, etc.) kubectl get pods -o wide
-o yaml Full object as YAML kubectl get pod anihpj -o yaml
-o json Full object as JSON kubectl get pod anihpj -o json
-o jsonpath Extract specific fields kubectl get pods -o jsonpath='{.items[*].metadata.name}'
-o custom-columns Custom table output kubectl get pods -o custom-columns=NAME:.metadata.name,STATUS:.status.phase
--sort-by Sort output by field kubectl get pods --sort-by=.metadata.creationTimestamp
-l key=value Filter by label kubectl get pods -l app=anihpj
--field-selector Filter by field value kubectl get pods --field-selector status.phase=Running

JSONPath Examples (CKA Favorite)

jsonpath-examples.sh 
# Get all pod names
kubectl get pods -o jsonpath='{.items[*].metadata.name}'

# Get pod names + their node assignments
kubectl get pods -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.metadata.name}{"\t"}{.spec.nodeName}{"\n"}{end}'

# Get node internal IPs
kubectl get nodes -o jsonpath='{.items[*].status.addresses[?(@.type=="InternalIP")].address}'

# Get container images across all pods
kubectl get pods -o jsonpath='{.items[*].spec.containers[*].image}' -A

# Custom columns — clean table output
kubectl get pods -o custom-columns=\
NAME:.metadata.name,\
STATUS:.status.phase,\
NODE:.spec.nodeName,\
IP:.status.podIP
💡 JSONPath Cheat Sheet for CKA

{.items[*]} = all items, {.metadata.name} = specific field, {range .items[*]}...{end} = loop, {"\n"} = newline, {"\t"} = tab, [?(@.type=="InternalIP")] = filter. Practice these — JSONPath extraction is tested on every CKA exam.

🎯 Common CKA Output Extraction Patterns

These exact patterns appear on CKA exams. Save as muscle memory:

cka-extraction-patterns.sh 
# ── Pattern 1: "List pod names sorted by creation time" ──
kubectl get pods --sort-by=.metadata.creationTimestamp \
  -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.metadata.name}{"\n"}{end}'

# ── Pattern 2: "Find which node each pod is running on" ──
kubectl get pods -o custom-columns=\
POD:.metadata.name,\
NODE:.spec.nodeName

# ── Pattern 3: "Get the IP address of all nodes" ──
kubectl get nodes -o jsonpath=\
'{.items[*].status.addresses[?(@.type=="InternalIP")].address}'

# ── Pattern 4: "List all container images in the cluster" ──
kubectl get pods -A -o jsonpath=\
'{range .items[*]}{.spec.containers[*].image}{"\n"}{end}' | sort -u

# ── Pattern 5: "Find pods not in Running state" ──
kubectl get pods -A --field-selector=status.phase!=Running

# ── Pattern 6: "Count pods per namespace" ──
kubectl get pods -A --no-headers | awk '{print $1}' | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn

# ── Pattern 7: "Save output to file" (CKA often requires this) ──
kubectl get pods -o jsonpath='{.items[*].metadata.name}' > /opt/answers/pod-names.txt
📤
Output to File

Many CKA questions say "save result to /opt/answers/file.txt". Always use > redirect. Double-check with cat after writing.

🔗
Pipe Power

Combine kubectl with grep, awk, sort, wc -l. Example: kubectl get pods -A | grep -c Running counts running pods.

⚠️ CKA Gotcha: kubectl Traps

  • kubectl apply vs kubectl create — create fails if the object exists; apply creates or updates. On the exam, use apply for YAML files and create for imperative one-liners
  • -o wide vs -o yaml — wide shows node placement + IPs (great for verifying scheduling). yaml shows the full spec (great for editing)
  • jsonpath quoting — Use single quotes around the jsonpath expression: -o jsonpath='{.items[*].metadata.name}'. Double quotes break in bash
  • --all-namespaces vs -A — Identical. Use -A to save time. But remember: some resources are cluster-scoped (nodes, PVs, clusterroles) and don't need -A

CKA Speed Tip: Master these output shortcuts — they appear in nearly every question:
k get po -o wide — see which NODE each pod is on
k get po -o yaml | grep -A5 image — quickly find container images
k get po --sort-by=.metadata.creationTimestamp — sort by age
k get po -l app=web — filter by label selector

⚡ kubectl power commands for the exam: kubectl explain pod.spec.containers — in-cluster docs (faster than browser)
kubectl api-resources | grep -i network — find the right resource type
kubectl get events --sort-by='.lastTimestamp' -A — timeline of cluster events
kubectl diff -f manifest.yaml — preview changes before applying

📝 Chapter 6 — CKA Practice Questions

Q1
Write a single kubectl command to list all pods across all namespaces, showing their node assignment, IP address, and status. Then filter to show only pods that are NOT in Running state.
Show Answer
Answer
# List all pods with node and IP info
kubectl get pods -A -o wide

# Filter for non-Running pods
kubectl get pods -A | grep -v Running

# Alternative: use field selector for specific statuses
kubectl get pods -A --field-selector=status.phase!=Running

# JSONPath for custom columns
kubectl get pods -A -o custom-columns=\
NAME:.metadata.name,\
NAMESPACE:.metadata.namespace,\
NODE:.spec.nodeName,\
STATUS:.status.phase,\
IP:.status.podIP
Explanation

-A (or --all-namespaces) is essential for getting a cluster-wide view. -o wide adds NODE and IP columns. The grep -v Running approach is the fastest on the exam. Field selectors work at the API server level (more efficient but limited to certain fields). Custom columns with -o custom-columns let you show exactly the fields you need — useful for complex output formatting questions on the CKA.

Q2
Use kubectl to find the exact YAML field path for a pod's restart policy, resource limits, and liveness probe. Which command acts as an in-cluster documentation system?
Show Answer
Answer
# The kubectl explain command — your in-cluster docs
kubectl explain pod.spec.restartPolicy
kubectl explain pod.spec.containers.resources.limits
kubectl explain pod.spec.containers.livenessProbe

# Recursive view to see ALL fields
kubectl explain pod.spec --recursive | head -80

# Search for specific fields
kubectl explain pod.spec --recursive | grep -i "liveness\|readiness"

# Drill into nested fields
kubectl explain pod.spec.containers.livenessProbe.httpGet
Explanation

kubectl explain is your most valuable tool on the CKA exam. It's faster than the official docs browser and works offline. The --recursive flag shows ALL field paths, and piping to grep helps you find exact field names. On the exam, if you can't remember whether it's livenessProbe or liveness-probe, just run kubectl explain pod.spec.containers --recursive | grep -i probe. Combine with --dry-run=client -o yaml to generate the scaffold, then use explain to add advanced fields.

Q3
Extract the image names of all containers running in the kube-system namespace using JSONPath output. Show two approaches: JSONPath and custom-columns.
Show Answer
Answer
# Method 1: JSONPath
kubectl get pods -n kube-system \
  -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.metadata.name}{"\t"}{.spec.containers[*].image}{"\n"}{end}'

# Method 2: custom-columns
kubectl get pods -n kube-system \
  -o custom-columns='POD:.metadata.name,IMAGES:.spec.containers[*].image'

# Method 3: Quick grep approach (fastest on exam)
kubectl get pods -n kube-system -o yaml | grep "image:"

# Method 4: go-template
kubectl get pods -n kube-system \
  -o go-template='{{range .items}}{{.metadata.name}}: {{range .spec.containers}}{{.image}} {{end}}{{"\n"}}{{end}}'
Explanation

The CKA sometimes asks you to extract specific fields from resources. JSONPath and custom-columns are the two main approaches. JSONPath is more powerful but tricky syntax — practice the {range .items[*]} pattern. Custom-columns is simpler for tabular output. In practice, the grep approach is fastest when you just need to see values quickly. Pro tip: -o json | jq '.items[].spec.containers[].image' also works if jq is available (it usually is on the CKA).

Q4
What is the difference between kubectl edit, kubectl patch, and kubectl replace? When would you use each on the CKA exam?
Show Answer
Answer
Command How Best For
kubectl edit Opens resource in vim for editing Interactive changes, exploring spec
kubectl patch JSON/YAML merge via CLI Quick single-field changes
kubectl replace Replaces entire resource from file Full resource replacement 
# edit — interactive
kubectl edit deployment nginx

# patch — one-liner
kubectl patch deployment nginx -p '{"spec":{"replicas":5}}'

# replace — from file (must include complete resource)
kubectl get deployment nginx -o yaml > nginx.yaml
vim nginx.yaml  # make changes
kubectl replace -f nginx.yaml
Explanation

On the CKA exam: use edit when you need to see the full resource and change multiple fields. Use patch for quick changes to a single field. Use replace when modifying immutable fields (like pod spec) — you may need to delete + replace --force. Note: some fields can't be changed on existing pods (e.g., container image in a standalone pod). In those cases, export, modify, delete the old pod, and create the new one.

Q5
Sort all pods in the default namespace by their restart count (highest first). Then sort all events by timestamp. Show the kubectl commands.
Show Answer
Answer
# Sort pods by restart count (highest first)
kubectl get pods --sort-by='.status.containerStatuses[0].restartCount'

# Sort events by timestamp (most recent first)
kubectl get events --sort-by='.lastTimestamp'

# Sort events across all namespaces
kubectl get events -A --sort-by='.lastTimestamp'

# Sort pods by creation time
kubectl get pods --sort-by='.metadata.creationTimestamp'

# Sort nodes by CPU capacity
kubectl get nodes --sort-by='.status.capacity.cpu'
Explanation

The --sort-by flag accepts a JSONPath expression. This is frequently tested on the CKA for diagnostic questions like "find the pod with the most restarts" or "show the most recent events." Key gotcha: --sort-by sorts in ascending order. To get descending, pipe to tail or tac: kubectl get pods --sort-by='.status.containerStatuses[0].restartCount' | tail -5. For events, the timestamp sort is essential when debugging cascading failures.

Q6
Use kubectl to find all API resources that support the list verb. Then find the short name and API group for NetworkPolicy.
Show Answer
Answer
# List all API resources with their verbs
kubectl api-resources -o wide

# Filter for resources that support 'list'
kubectl api-resources --verbs=list

# Find NetworkPolicy details
kubectl api-resources | grep -i networkpol
# NAME              SHORTNAMES   APIVERSION             NAMESPACED   KIND
# networkpolicies   netpol       networking.k8s.io/v1   true         NetworkPolicy

# Short name: netpol
# API group: networking.k8s.io
# Namespaced: true

# Use the short name
kubectl get netpol -A
Explanation

kubectl api-resources is your cheat sheet for finding resource names, short names, API versions, and whether they're namespaced. On the CKA, if you can't remember the exact resource name or API version, this command gives it to you instantly. Short names save typing: po (pods), svc (services), deploy (deployments), rs (replicasets), ns (namespaces), netpol (networkpolicies), pv/pvc (persistentvolumes/claims), sa (serviceaccounts).

Q7
Execute a command inside a running pod named web-app to: (1) check the default gateway, (2) test DNS resolution, (3) view environment variables. Write the kubectl exec commands.
Show Answer
Answer
# Interactive shell
kubectl exec -it web-app -- /bin/sh

# One-off commands (non-interactive)
# 1. Check default gateway
kubectl exec web-app -- ip route
kubectl exec web-app -- cat /proc/net/route

# 2. Test DNS resolution
kubectl exec web-app -- nslookup kubernetes.default
kubectl exec web-app -- cat /etc/resolv.conf

# 3. View environment variables
kubectl exec web-app -- env
kubectl exec web-app -- printenv

# Multi-container pod: specify container
kubectl exec web-app -c sidecar -- env

# Debugging with a temporary container
kubectl debug web-app -it --image=busybox -- sh
Explanation

kubectl exec is invaluable for CKA troubleshooting. Key syntax: -- separates kubectl args from the command to run inside the container. For multi-container pods, use -c <container-name>. If the container has no shell (distroless images), use kubectl debug to attach an ephemeral debug container with tools like busybox. Common CKA patterns: exec into a pod to test network connectivity, verify mounted volumes (ls /data), or check if ConfigMap/Secret values are properly injected as env vars.

Q8
Write a kubectl command to get the logs of a pod named api-pod that has crashed. The current container has just started (so its logs are empty). How do you see the logs from the previous crash?
Show Answer
Answer
# View logs of the previous (crashed) container instance
kubectl logs api-pod --previous

# View only the last 100 lines
kubectl logs api-pod --previous --tail=100

# Stream logs in real-time (for current container)
kubectl logs api-pod -f

# Multi-container: specify which container's logs
kubectl logs api-pod -c main-app --previous

# All containers in the pod
kubectl logs api-pod --all-containers=true

# Logs from all pods matching a label
kubectl logs -l app=api --previous
Explanation

The --previous flag is critical for CKA troubleshooting. When a container is in CrashLoopBackOff, the current instance may have empty logs or only startup messages. The --previous flag shows logs from the last terminated container instance — this is where the actual error that caused the crash is recorded. On the exam, always try --previous if the current logs don't show the error. Note: Kubernetes only stores the last terminated container's logs — if it has crashed multiple times, you only see the most recent crash.

Q9
Use kubectl to generate a Deployment YAML file for an application named backend with image node:18-alpine, 3 replicas, in namespace staging — without creating the resource. Then explain the dry-run flag.
Show Answer
Answer
# Generate YAML without creating anything
kubectl create deployment backend \
  --image=node:18-alpine \
  --replicas=3 \
  -n staging \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml > backend-deploy.yaml

# Two types of dry-run:
# --dry-run=client  →  generates YAML locally (no API server contact)
# --dry-run=server  →  sends to API server for validation but doesn't persist

# Edit the generated YAML to add more fields
vim backend-deploy.yaml

# Apply when ready
kubectl apply -f backend-deploy.yaml
Explanation

--dry-run=client -o yaml is the CKA exam's most used flag combination. It generates valid YAML for any resource without creating it. client mode doesn't contact the API server (works even if API server is down). server mode validates against the actual API server (catches invalid field names, missing namespaces, quota violations). Strategy: always start with --dry-run=client to generate the scaffold, add advanced fields (resources, probes, volumes), then kubectl apply. This is 10x faster than writing YAML from memory.

Q10
Permanently delete a stuck pod named zombie-pod that has been in Terminating state for 10 minutes. The normal kubectl delete doesn't work. What do you do?
Show Answer
Answer
# Force delete with zero grace period
kubectl delete pod zombie-pod --grace-period=0 --force

# If the pod had a finalizer preventing deletion
kubectl patch pod zombie-pod -p '{"metadata":{"finalizers":null}}'
kubectl delete pod zombie-pod

# If it's a pod managed by a deployment, delete won't permanently remove it
# The replicaset will recreate it. Instead:
kubectl scale deployment <deploy-name> --replicas=0
# OR delete the deployment
kubectl delete deployment <deploy-name>
Explanation

Stuck Terminating pods occur when: (1) The kubelet on the node is down and can't confirm termination, (2) The container's process ignores SIGTERM, (3) finalizers prevent deletion. The --grace-period=0 --force combination tells the API server to remove the pod object immediately from etcd, regardless of whether the kubelet has confirmed container shutdown. On the CKA, this is essential for cleanup when a node is unreachable. Caution: force-deleting doesn't stop the actual container — if the node comes back, the container may still be running (orphaned).

Chapter 7: YAML and Kubernetes Manifests

Every Kubernetes object is defined in YAML. You can't pass the CKA without being able to read, write, and debug YAML manifests quickly. This chapter covers YAML syntax, the 4 required fields every manifest must have, finding the right apiVersion, multi-resource files, and validation.

🟢 Beginner ⏱️ ~1 hour 📝 YAML 🎯 CKA Essential

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ YAML syntax — key-value pairs, lists, nested objects, and the rules that trip people up
  • ✅ The 4 required fields every Kubernetes manifest must have
  • ✅ How to find the right apiVersion for any resource
  • ✅ Combining multiple resources in one YAML file with --- separator
  • ✅ Validating manifests with --dry-run and kubectl diff before applying
🎓 CKA Relevance
All Domains 100%
YAML Mistakes = Failed Tasks

The #1 reason candidates fail CKA tasks isn't lack of knowledge — it's YAML indentation errors. A single wrong indent breaks the entire manifest. Practice writing YAML by hand until it's automatic. On the exam, always use --dry-run=server before applying to catch errors.

  • Spaces only — tabs will break your YAML silently
  • 2-space indentation — Kubernetes convention
  • Colon + space — key: value not key:value

7.1 YAML Basics for Kubernetes

YAML ("YAML Ain't Markup Language") is a human-readable data format. Kubernetes uses it to describe desired state. Here are the three building blocks you need:

YAML Building Blocks
  1️⃣ KEY-VALUE PAIR (scalar):
  ┌─────────────────────────────┐
  │ name: anihpj                │  string
  │ replicas: 3                 │  number
  │ debug: true                 │  boolean
