  │ description: "Job platform" │  quoted string
  └─────────────────────────────┘

  2️⃣ LIST (sequence) — each item starts with "- ":
  ┌─────────────────────────────┐
  │ ports:                      │
  │ - 8000          ◀── item 1  │
  │ - 8080          ◀── item 2  │
  │ - 443           ◀── item 3  │
  └─────────────────────────────┘

  3️⃣ NESTED OBJECT (mapping) — indent = nesting:
  ┌─────────────────────────────┐
  │ metadata:       ◀── parent  │  2 spaces per level
  │   name: anihpj  ◀── child   │
  │   labels:       ◀── child   │
  │     app: anihpj ◀── grandchild
  └─────────────────────────────┘

  ⚠️ CRITICAL RULES:
  ✅ Spaces only      (2 per indent level)
  ❌ NO tabs          (YAML breaks silently!)
  ✅ Colon + space    key: value
  ❌ NOT              key:value  (missing space!)
  ✅ Dash + space     - item
  ❌ NOT              -item  (missing space!)
  ✅ Use ---          to separate multiple documents
yaml-syntax-examples.yaml 
# Key-value pairs
name: anihpj
replicas: 3
enabled: true

# List of strings
skills:
  - Python
  - Django
  - Kubernetes

# List of objects
containers:
  - name: django
    image: anihpj:v1
    ports:
      - containerPort: 8000
  - name: nginx
    image: nginx:alpine
    ports:
      - containerPort: 80

# Inline list (shorthand)
args: ["runserver", "0.0.0.0:8000"]

# Multi-line string (pipe = preserve newlines)
description: |
  anihpj is a Django job posting platform.
  It supports filtering by Skills, Location, and Author.

# Folded string (> = join lines)
summary: >
  This is a single paragraph
  even though it spans multiple lines.
🚨 Common YAML Gotcha: Indentation

These look similar but mean completely different things:

Mistake What You Wrote What K8s Sees Fix
Tab character [TAB]name: anihpj Parse error — YAML fails entirely Use spaces only
No space after colon name:anihpj Treats name:anihpj as a single string name: anihpj
Wrong indent level labels: at root level Top-level field instead of nested under metadata Indent 2 spaces under parent
String vs number replicas: "3" Error: string instead of integer replicas: 3 (no quotes)
Missing dash for list containers:\n name: django Error: expected list, got mapping containers:\n- name: django
Indentation Matters — Same Words, Different Meaning
  ✅ CORRECT — ports is a field of the container:
  containers:
  - name: django
    ports:                    ← indented under the list item
    - containerPort: 8000

  ❌ WRONG — ports is a sibling, not a child:
  containers:
  - name: django
  ports:                      ← NOT indented = separate field!
  - containerPort: 8000

  ✅ CORRECT — labels nested under metadata:
  metadata:
    name: anihpj
    labels:
      app: anihpj

  ❌ WRONG — labels at same level as metadata:
  metadata:
    name: anihpj
  labels:                     ← NOT nested = top-level field!
    app: anihpj

🔄 YAML vs JSON — Why Kubernetes Uses YAML

Kubernetes actually stores everything as JSON internally. YAML is a superset of JSON — easier to write by hand, with comments support:

📝
YAML (What You Write)
# Pod definition
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj
spec:
  containers:
  - name: django
    image: anihpj:v1

✅ Comments • ✅ Readable • ✅ Less typing

{ }
JSON (What K8s Stores)
{
  "apiVersion": "v1",
  "kind": "Pod",
  "metadata": {"name": "anihpj"},
  "spec": {
    "containers": [{
      "name": "django",
      "image": "anihpj:v1"
    }]}}

❌ No comments • ❌ Verbose • ✅ Precise

📖 Both are Valid for kubectl

kubectl apply -f pod.yaml and kubectl apply -f pod.json both work. However, always use YAML for writing manifests (readable, supports comments). Use JSON with -o json for extracting data programmatically.

7.2 The 4 Required Fields — Every Manifest

Every single Kubernetes object — Pod, Deployment, Service, ConfigMap, Secret — requires exactly 4 top-level fields. Memorize these:

🔌
apiVersion

Which API group? v1, apps/v1, networking.k8s.io/v1

📦
kind

What type of object? Pod, Deployment, Service

🏷️
metadata

Identity: name, namespace, labels, annotations

⚙️
spec

Desired state — THE MEAT. Different for every resource type.

anihpj-pod.yaml 
apiVersion: v1                    # ← 1. API version
kind: Pod                         # ← 2. Object type
metadata:                         # ← 3. Identity
  name: anihpj-django
  namespace: anihpj-dev
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    component: web
    environment: dev
spec:                             # ← 4. Desired state
  containers:
  - name: django
    image: anihpj:v1
    ports:
    - containerPort: 8000
    env:
    - name: DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE
      value: "anihpj.settings"
    resources:
      requests:
        cpu: "100m"
        memory: "128Mi"
      limits:
        cpu: "500m"
        memory: "256Mi"
Manifest Structure — Annotated
  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  apiVersion: apps/v1     ← Which API group?          │
  │  kind: Deployment        ← What type of object?      │
  │  metadata:               ← Identity (name, labels)   │
  │    name: anihpj                                      │
  │    namespace: anihpj-dev                             │
  │    labels:                                           │
  │      app: anihpj                                     │
  │  spec:                   ← Desired state (THE MEAT)  │
  │    replicas: 3              ← How many pods?          │
  │    selector:                ← Which pods to manage?   │
  │      matchLabels:                                     │
  │        app: anihpj                                    │
  │    template:                ← Pod template            │
  │      metadata:                                        │
  │        labels:                                        │
  │          app: anihpj        ← MUST match selector!    │
  │      spec:                  ← Pod spec (containers)   │
  │        containers:                                    │
  │        - name: django                                 │
  │          image: anihpj:v1                             │
  │          ports:                                       │
  │          - containerPort: 8000                        │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

7.3 Finding the Right apiVersion

Wrong apiVersion = manifest rejected by the API server. Here's the complete reference and how to look it up:

Resource apiVersion Kind Short Name
Podv1Podpo
Servicev1Servicesvc
ConfigMapv1ConfigMapcm
Secretv1Secret—
Namespacev1Namespacens
PersistentVolumev1PersistentVolumepv
PersistentVolumeClaimv1PersistentVolumeClaimpvc
ServiceAccountv1ServiceAccountsa
Deploymentapps/v1Deploymentdeploy
ReplicaSetapps/v1ReplicaSetrs
DaemonSetapps/v1DaemonSetds
StatefulSetapps/v1StatefulSetsts
Ingressnetworking.k8s.io/v1Ingressing
NetworkPolicynetworking.k8s.io/v1NetworkPolicynetpol
ClusterRolerbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1ClusterRole—
ClusterRoleBindingrbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1ClusterRoleBinding—
find-apiversion.sh 
# List ALL resources with their apiVersion and short names
kubectl api-resources
# NAME               SHORTNAMES   APIVERSION                     NAMESPACED   KIND
# pods               po           v1                             true         Pod
# services           svc          v1                             true         Service
# deployments        deploy       apps/v1                        true         Deployment
# ingresses          ing          networking.k8s.io/v1           true         Ingress

# List all available API versions
kubectl api-versions
# apps/v1
# networking.k8s.io/v1
# rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
# v1

# Quick check: what apiVersion does Ingress use?
kubectl api-resources | grep -i ingress
# ingresses   ing   networking.k8s.io/v1   true   Ingress
💡 CKA Memory Trick

Core resources = v1 (Pod, Service, ConfigMap, Secret, Namespace, PV, PVC, SA). Workloads = apps/v1 (Deployment, ReplicaSet, DaemonSet, StatefulSet). Networking = networking.k8s.io/v1 (Ingress, NetworkPolicy). RBAC = rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1 (Role, ClusterRole, RoleBinding, ClusterRoleBinding).

API Group Hierarchy — Visual Map
  Kubernetes API Groups
  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                                                            │
  │  Core API (v1)  ← No group prefix                         │
  │  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
  │  │ Pod • Service • ConfigMap • Secret • Namespace       │  │
  │  │ PersistentVolume • PersistentVolumeClaim • SA • Node │  │
  │  │ Endpoints • Event • ReplicationController            │  │
  │  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
  │                                                            │
  │  apps/v1  ← Workload controllers                           │
  │  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
  │  │ Deployment • ReplicaSet • DaemonSet • StatefulSet    │  │
  │  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
  │                                                            │
  │  networking.k8s.io/v1  ← Network resources                 │
  │  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
  │  │ Ingress • NetworkPolicy • IngressClass               │  │
  │  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
  │                                                            │
  │  rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1  ← Access control            │
  │  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
  │  │ Role • RoleBinding • ClusterRole • ClusterRoleBinding│  │
  │  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
  │                                                            │
  │  batch/v1  ← Job workloads                                 │
  │  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
  │  │ Job • CronJob                                        │  │
  │  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
  │                                                            │
  │  storage.k8s.io/v1  ← Storage                              │
  │  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
  │  │ StorageClass • CSIDriver • CSINode                   │  │
  │  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
  │                                                            │
  │  policy/v1  ← Pod disruption                               │
  │  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
  │  │ PodDisruptionBudget                                  │  │
  │  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

7.4 Multi-Resource YAML Files

You can define multiple Kubernetes objects in a single YAML file by separating them with ---. This is common for deploying an app with its service together:

anihpj-complete.yaml 
# Namespace
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: anihpj-dev
---
# ConfigMap
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: anihpj-config
  namespace: anihpj-dev
data:
  DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE: "anihpj.settings"
  ALLOWED_HOSTS: "*"
---
# Deployment
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj
  namespace: anihpj-dev
spec:
  replicas: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: django
        image: anihpj:v1
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8000
        envFrom:
        - configMapRef:
            name: anihpj-config
---
# Service
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: anihpj-svc
  namespace: anihpj-dev
spec:
  selector:
    app: anihpj
  ports:
  - port: 80
    targetPort: 8000
apply-strategies.sh 
# Apply a single multi-resource file
kubectl apply -f anihpj-complete.yaml

# Apply all YAML files in a directory
kubectl apply -f k8s/

# Apply with Kustomize
kubectl apply -k overlays/prod/

# Delete everything from the file
kubectl delete -f anihpj-complete.yaml
File Organization — Two Approaches
  Approach A: Single File              Approach B: Directory
  ┌─────────────────────┐             ┌─────────────────────┐
  │ anihpj-complete.yaml │             │ k8s/                 │
  │ ├── Namespace        │             │ ├── namespace.yaml   │
  │ ├── ---              │             │ ├── configmap.yaml   │
  │ ├── ConfigMap        │             │ ├── secret.yaml      │
  │ ├── ---              │             │ ├── deployment.yaml  │
  │ ├── Deployment       │             │ └── service.yaml     │
  │ ├── ---              │             │                      │
  │ └── Service          │             │ $ kubectl apply -f k8s/
  │                      │             │ (applies all files)  │
  │ $ kubectl apply -f   │             └─────────────────────┘
  │ anihpj-complete.yaml │
  └─────────────────────┘
  ✅ Simple, one command              ✅ Better organization
  ❌ Hard to manage large apps        ✅ Each file = one concern
⚠️ Resource Ordering Matters!

When using kubectl apply -f dir/, Kubernetes processes files in alphabetical order. If your Deployment references a ConfigMap that hasn't been created yet, it'll fail. Use numbered filenames to control order: 01-namespace.yaml, 02-configmap.yaml, 03-secret.yaml, 04-deployment.yaml, 05-service.yaml. Or use a single multi-resource file with --- separators — resources are created top-to-bottom.

7.5 YAML Validation and Dry Run

Never apply a manifest blindly. Always validate first — especially on the CKA exam where a bad apply can cost you time and points.

validation-workflow.sh 
# Step 1: Client-side validation (no API call)
kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml --dry-run=client
# deployment.apps/anihpj created (dry run)   ← YAML is valid

# Step 2: Server-side validation (checks against API)
kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml --dry-run=server
# deployment.apps/anihpj created (dry run)   ← API accepted it

# Step 3: Preview changes before applying
kubectl diff -f deployment.yaml
# Shows what will change (like git diff)

# Step 4: Apply for real
kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml
# deployment.apps/anihpj created
1
--dry-run=client

Validates YAML syntax locally. No cluster needed.

2
--dry-run=server

Validates against API server. Catches invalid refs.

3
kubectl diff

Preview what will change. Like git diff for K8s.

4
kubectl apply

Apply for real. K8s reconciles to desired state.

⚠️ CKA Exam Workflow

On the exam, always use this order: generate YAML with --dry-run=client -o yaml > file.yaml → edit the YAML to add required fields → validate with --dry-run=server → apply. This prevents wasted time debugging apply errors.

🚨 Decoding Common Error Messages

When validation fails, K8s gives you error messages. Learn to read them fast:

common-errors.sh 
# ERROR: Wrong apiVersion
error: resource mapping not found for name: "anihpj"
  kind: Deployment  apiVersion: v1
# FIX: Change apiVersion to apps/v1

# ERROR: Missing required field
error: error validating: ValidationError(Deployment.spec):
  missing required field "selector"
# FIX: Add spec.selector.matchLabels

# ERROR: Label mismatch
The Deployment "anihpj" is invalid:
  spec.selector does not match template.metadata.labels
# FIX: Make selector.matchLabels == template.metadata.labels

# ERROR: Invalid YAML syntax
error: error parsing deployment.yaml: yaml: line 12:
  did not find expected key
# FIX: Check indentation at line 12 (probably tabs or wrong indent)

# ERROR: Namespace doesn't exist
Error: namespaces "anihpj-dev" not found
# FIX: Create namespace first: kubectl create ns anihpj-dev

🛠️ Vim Tips for YAML Editing (CKA Exam Editor)

The CKA exam provides vim as the default editor. These settings prevent YAML indent disasters:

~/.vimrc 
# Add to ~/.vimrc FIRST THING on the exam:
set tabstop=2       " Tab = 2 spaces
set shiftwidth=2    " Indent = 2 spaces
set expandtab       " Convert tabs to spaces
set autoindent      " Keep indent level on new line
set smartindent     " Smart indent for code
set number          " Show line numbers (helps find errors)
set paste           " Prevents auto-indent issues when pasting

# Quick setup (one command):
echo 'set ts=2 sw=2 et ai si nu paste' >> ~/.vimrc
Vim Command What It Does When to Use
:set paste Disable auto-indent for pasting YAML Before pasting from docs
:set nopaste Re-enable auto-indent After pasting, for manual editing
gg=G Re-indent entire file When indentation is messed up
Shift+V then > Visual select + indent right Indent a YAML block deeper
Shift+V then < Visual select + indent left Un-indent a YAML block
/searchterm Search for text Find a specific field in large YAML
dd / yy / p Delete/copy/paste line Move or duplicate YAML lines
u Undo last change Revert a mistake instantly

⚠️ CKA Gotcha: YAML & Manifest Traps

  • Indentation kills — YAML uses spaces, NEVER tabs. A single tab character causes a parse error. In vim: :set expandtab shiftwidth=2 tabstop=2
  • apiVersion confusion — Deployments use apps/v1, not v1. CronJobs use batch/v1. Ingress uses networking.k8s.io/v1. When in doubt: kubectl api-resources | grep <kind>
  • Multi-document YAML — Use --- separator between resources. But on the exam, prefer one resource per file for easier debugging
  • kubectl explain is your YAML cheat sheet — kubectl explain pod.spec.containers.resources.limits shows the exact field structure. Faster than docs
  • Pasting YAML in vim — Always :set paste before pasting, otherwise auto-indent corrupts your YAML. :set nopaste after

CKA Speed Tip — Generate, don't type: Never write YAML from scratch. Generate a skeleton then edit:
kubectl run nginx --image=nginx --dry-run=client -o yaml > pod.yaml
kubectl create deploy web --image=nginx --dry-run=client -o yaml > deploy.yaml
Then add fields like resources, nodeSelector, tolerations by referencing kubectl explain.

⚡ YAML validation & debugging commands: kubectl apply -f manifest.yaml --dry-run=server — validate against API server without creating
kubectl apply -f manifest.yaml --validate=true — syntax + schema validation
kubectl explain deployment.spec.strategy — field-level docs
kubectl explain pod.spec --recursive | grep -i volume — find volume-related fields fast

📝 Chapter 7 — CKA Practice Questions

Q1
What are the four mandatory top-level fields in every Kubernetes YAML manifest? Write a minimal valid Pod manifest with all four fields.
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: v1          # 1. API version
kind: Pod               # 2. Resource type
metadata:               # 3. Metadata (name, namespace, labels)
  name: minimal-pod
spec:                   # 4. Specification (desired state)
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx:1.25

The four required fields:

  1. apiVersion — which API group/version (v1, apps/v1, networking.k8s.io/v1)
  2. kind — the resource type (Pod, Deployment, Service, etc.)
  3. metadata — at minimum, name is required
  4. spec — the desired state definition (varies per resource type)
Explanation

Every K8s manifest follows this structure. The most common CKA mistake is getting apiVersion wrong. Quick reference: Pods/Services/ConfigMaps/Secrets → v1. Deployments/ReplicaSets/DaemonSets → apps/v1. Ingress → networking.k8s.io/v1. RBAC → rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1. If unsure: kubectl api-resources | grep <kind> shows the correct API version.

Q2
You paste a YAML manifest into vim on the CKA exam and it comes out garbled — indentation is wrong everywhere. What happened and how do you prevent it?
Show Answer
Answer
# The problem: vim's auto-indent added extra indentation to pasted text

# Prevention: Enter paste mode BEFORE pasting
:set paste       # Disable auto-indent for pasting
# Now paste your YAML

# After pasting, re-enable normal mode  
:set nopaste     # Re-enable auto-indent

# Better approach: use vim's paste toggle
# Add to ~/.vimrc on the exam:
set pastetoggle=<F2>
# Then press F2 before pasting, F2 after

# Essential vim settings for YAML editing
:set tabstop=2 shiftwidth=2 expandtab
# This ensures tabs are converted to 2 spaces
Explanation

YAML is whitespace-sensitive — incorrect indentation means invalid syntax. Vim's auto-indent feature adds extra spaces when you paste, corrupting the YAML. On the CKA exam, this is a common time-waster. Always :set paste before pasting, :set nopaste after. Also useful: :set number (show line numbers), gg=G (re-indent entire file), and :set list (show hidden whitespace). Practice vim YAML editing before the exam — it saves enormous time.

Q3
What is the correct apiVersion for each of these resources: Pod, Deployment, Service, Ingress, NetworkPolicy, PersistentVolumeClaim, Role, ClusterRoleBinding? How do you find the right apiVersion if you can't remember?
Show Answer
Answer
Resource apiVersion
Pod, Service, ConfigMap, Secret, PVC, SA v1
Deployment, ReplicaSet, DaemonSet, StatefulSet apps/v1
Ingress, NetworkPolicy networking.k8s.io/v1
Role, ClusterRole, RoleBinding, ClusterRoleBinding rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
StorageClass storage.k8s.io/v1 
# Can't remember? Use this command:
kubectl api-resources | grep -i deployment
# deployments   deploy   apps/v1   true   Deployment
Explanation

Getting apiVersion wrong causes "no matches for kind" errors. The pattern is: core resources (pods, services) use v1. Workload resources use apps/v1. Networking resources use networking.k8s.io/v1. RBAC uses rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1. On the CKA, kubectl api-resources is the definitive answer — it shows the exact API group and version for every resource. Alternatively, kubectl explain <resource> shows the VERSION at the top of its output.

Q4
Write a YAML manifest that defines BOTH a Deployment and a Service in the same file. How do you separate multiple resources in one YAML file?
Show Answer
Answer
# Multi-resource YAML — separated by ---
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: web-app
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: web
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: web
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: web
        image: nginx:1.25
        ports:
        - containerPort: 80
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: web-svc
spec:
  selector:
    app: web
  ports:
  - port: 80
    targetPort: 80
  type: ClusterIP
# Apply both at once
kubectl apply -f multi-resource.yaml

# Or apply all files in a directory
kubectl apply -f ./manifests/
Explanation

The --- document separator allows multiple resources in one file. Each document is an independent YAML document. On the CKA exam, it's generally faster to use one file per resource for easier editing and debugging. However, multi-document files are common in production. Key gotcha: make sure the --- is at column 0 with no spaces. Also critical: the Deployment's spec.selector.matchLabels MUST match spec.template.metadata.labels — and the Service's spec.selector must match the pod's labels.

Q5
You apply a YAML manifest and get the error: "error validating data: ValidationError(Deployment.spec): unknown field 'replicas' in io.k8s.api.apps.v1.DeploymentSpec". What is wrong with your YAML?
Show Answer
Answer

The replicas field is at the wrong indentation level. It should be under spec: of the Deployment, not nested under another field.

# ❌ WRONG — replicas indented under template
spec:
  template:
    replicas: 3    # ← This is wrong! replicas belongs under spec, not template
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: web

# ✅ CORRECT — replicas at the right level
spec:
  replicas: 3      # ← Correct! Direct child of spec
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: web
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: web
Explanation

YAML indentation errors are the #1 cause of failed applies on the CKA. The Deployment spec has three main sections at the same level: replicas, selector, and template. The template section is a PodTemplateSpec — it has its own metadata and spec. This nested spec (pod spec) is different from the outer spec (deployment spec). Use kubectl explain deployment.spec to see which fields are direct children of the deployment spec. Common trap: putting pod-level fields (like containers) at the deployment level.

Q6
How do you validate a YAML manifest BEFORE applying it? Show three different validation methods available on the CKA exam.
Show Answer
Answer
# Method 1: Client-side dry run (no API server needed)
kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml --dry-run=client
# Validates YAML syntax and basic structure

# Method 2: Server-side dry run (validates against API server)
kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml --dry-run=server
# Validates: schema, RBAC, admission controllers, quotas

# Method 3: kubectl diff (shows what would change)
kubectl diff -f deployment.yaml
# Shows the diff between current state and proposed changes

# Bonus: validate only (no apply)
kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml --validate=true --dry-run=client

# Quick YAML syntax check with python
python3 -c "import yaml; yaml.safe_load(open('deployment.yaml'))"
Explanation

--dry-run=server is the most thorough validation — it sends the request through the entire API pipeline (authentication, authorization, admission control, schema validation) but doesn't persist to etcd. --dry-run=client only validates locally — it won't catch missing namespaces or quota violations. kubectl diff is great for seeing exactly what will change. On the CKA, always use server-side dry-run before applying complex manifests to catch errors without wasting time on failed deployments.

Q7
Explain YAML data types: strings, integers, booleans, lists, and maps. What happens if you forget to quote the string "true" in a ConfigMap value?
Show Answer
Answer
# YAML type coercion gotchas
data:
  debug: true           # ❌ Boolean! Not string "true"
  debug: "true"         # ✅ String "true"
  port: 8080            # ❌ Integer! Not string "8080"
  port: "8080"          # ✅ String "8080"
  version: 1.0          # ❌ Float! Not string "1.0"
  version: "1.0"        # ✅ String "1.0"

# ConfigMap data values MUST be strings
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: app-config
data:                    # All values must be strings
  debug: "true"          # ← Quoted
  port: "8080"           # ← Quoted
  message: "Hello World" # ← Quoted

# Lists (arrays) use - prefix
spec:
  containers:
  - name: app          # ← List item 1
    image: nginx
  - name: sidecar      # ← List item 2
    image: fluentd
Explanation

YAML automatically interprets unquoted values: true/false become booleans, numbers become integers/floats. ConfigMap data: requires string values — unquoted true causes validation errors. This catches many people on the CKA. Also tricky: yes/no are booleans in YAML! And 0666 is octal. Always quote values in ConfigMaps and anywhere you mean a literal string. For binary data, ConfigMap provides binaryData: with base64 encoding.

Q8
What is the Deployment spec.selector.matchLabels field and why is it required? What error do you get if it doesn't match spec.template.metadata.labels?
Show Answer
Answer
# The selector MUST match the template labels
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: web
spec:
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: web          # ← Must match template labels
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: web        # ← Must match selector
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: web
        image: nginx

# ❌ Mismatch causes this error:
# "selector does not match template labels"
Explanation

The selector.matchLabels tells the Deployment which pods it owns. If the selector doesn't match the template labels, the Deployment can never find its own pods. This is a hard requirement since apps/v1 — the API server rejects the manifest immediately. Unlike in older API versions, you cannot omit the selector. On the CKA, when generating with --dry-run=client -o yaml, the selector is automatically set correctly. But if you edit YAML manually, make sure these match. The selector is also immutable — once created, you can't change it.

Q9
Convert this imperative command into a complete YAML manifest: kubectl create deployment redis --image=redis:7 --replicas=2 --port=6379
Show Answer
Answer
# Step 1: Generate the YAML
kubectl create deployment redis --image=redis:7 --replicas=2 --port=6379 \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml

# Result:
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  labels:
    app: redis
  name: redis
spec:
  replicas: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: redis
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: redis
    spec:
      containers:
      - image: redis:7
        name: redis
        ports:
        - containerPort: 6379
Explanation

Understanding the mapping between imperative commands and YAML is essential for the CKA. --image → spec.template.spec.containers[0].image. --replicas → spec.replicas. --port → spec.template.spec.containers[0].ports[0].containerPort. The generated YAML is always a good starting point — add fields like resources, livenessProbe, env, volumeMounts by editing. Remember: kubectl run creates Pods (not Deployments), kubectl create deployment creates Deployments.

Q10
You have a running Deployment and need to add a livenessProbe to it. You don't have the original YAML. How do you extract the current YAML, modify it, and re-apply it?
Show Answer
Answer
# Method 1: Export and edit (safest)
kubectl get deployment myapp -o yaml > myapp-deploy.yaml
vim myapp-deploy.yaml
# Add the livenessProbe under spec.template.spec.containers[0]:
#   livenessProbe:
#     httpGet:
#       path: /healthz
#       port: 8080
#     initialDelaySeconds: 15
#     periodSeconds: 10
kubectl apply -f myapp-deploy.yaml

# Method 2: kubectl edit (faster, in-place)
kubectl edit deployment myapp
# vim opens — add the livenessProbe section, save and exit

# Method 3: kubectl patch (one-liner, no editor)
kubectl patch deployment myapp --type=json \
  -p='[{"op":"add","path":"/spec/template/spec/containers/0/livenessProbe","value":{"httpGet":{"path":"/healthz","port":8080},"initialDelaySeconds":15,"periodSeconds":10}}]'
Explanation

On the CKA, you'll often need to modify running resources. kubectl edit is the fastest for adding a few lines. kubectl get -o yaml > file.yaml is safer as you have a backup. When extracting YAML from a running resource, remove server-generated fields (resourceVersion, uid, creationTimestamp, status) before re-applying — otherwise you may get "resourceVersion conflict" errors. The livenessProbe tells the kubelet how to check if the container is alive. If it fails, the container is killed and restarted.

Chapter 8: Deploying anihpj — Your First Kubernetes Application

Time to put everything together. In this chapter you'll deploy the anihpj/jobpost Django application step by step — creating a Namespace, ConfigMap, Secret, Deployment, and Service. By the end, anihpj will be running on Kubernetes and you'll understand every piece of the deployment flow.

🟢 Beginner ⏱️ ~2 hours 🚀 Deploy 🐍 Django

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ Plan a Kubernetes deployment — what resources you need and in what order
  • ✅ Create a Namespace to isolate your application
  • ✅ Use ConfigMap for non-sensitive configuration and Secret for credentials
  • ✅ Write a complete Deployment manifest with environment variables and resource limits
  • ✅ Expose your app with a Service and access it via port-forward
  • ✅ Verify and troubleshoot your deployment end-to-end
  • ✅ Understand the full flow: kubectl apply → API server → etcd → scheduler → kubelet → container
🎓 CKA Relevance
Workloads & Scheduling 15% Services & Networking 20%
This Is the Pattern for Every CKA Deployment Task

The CKA will ask you to create Deployments, Services, ConfigMaps, and Secrets multiple times. The step-by-step workflow in this chapter is exactly the workflow you'll follow for every deployment question on the exam. Practice this until it's muscle memory.

  • Namespace → ConfigMap → Secret → Deployment → Service — this order avoids reference errors
  • Use imperative + dry-run to generate skeletons, then edit for specific requirements
  • Always verify with kubectl get all -n <namespace> after deploying

8.1 The anihpj Application Overview

Let's review what we're deploying — the anihpj Django project with its jobpost app. Understanding the application architecture helps you make the right Kubernetes decisions.

🐍
Django Project: anihpj

Job posting platform. Models: Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost. Uses SQLite for dev, PostgreSQL for prod.

📦
Docker Image: anihpj:v1

Containerized Django app. Runs on port 8000. Needs environment vars for settings and secret key.

Target Architecture — anihpj in Kubernetes
  NAMESPACE: anihpj-dev
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                                                          │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐            │
  │  │ Deployment: anihpj (replicas: 2)         │            │
  │  │ ┌──────────────────┐ ┌────────────────┐ │            │
  │  │ │ 📦 Pod: anihpj-1  │ │ 📦 Pod: anihpj-2│ │            │
  │  │ │ Image: anihpj:v1 │ │ Image: anihpj:v1│ │            │
  │  │ │ Port: 8000       │ │ Port: 8000      │ │            │
  │  │ └──────────────────┘ └────────────────┘ │            │
  │  └──────────────────┬──────────────────────┘            │
  │                     │                                    │
  │  ┌──────────────────▼───────────────────┐               │
  │  │  Service: anihpj-svc (ClusterIP)      │               │
  │  │  Port: 80 → targetPort: 8000          │               │
  │  └──────────────────────────────────────┘               │
  │                                                          │
  │  ┌───────────────────┐  ┌───────────────────┐           │
  │  │ ConfigMap:         │  │ Secret:            │           │
  │  │ anihpj-config      │  │ anihpj-secret      │           │
  │  │ ├ DJANGO_SETTINGS  │  │ ├ SECRET_KEY       │           │
  │  │ └ ALLOWED_HOSTS    │  │ └ DATABASE_URL     │           │
  │  └───────────────────┘  └───────────────────┘           │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  You (laptop) ──port-forward──▶ Service ──▶ Pod ──▶ Django
📂Namespace
→
⚙️ConfigMap
→
🔐Secret
→
🚀Deployment
→
🌐Service
→
✅Verify

8.2 Step 1: Create the Namespace

Always isolate your application in its own namespace. This keeps resources organized and makes cleanup easy.

01-namespace.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: anihpj-dev
  labels:
    project: anihpj
    environment: dev
terminal 
# Imperative (faster for CKA)
kubectl create namespace anihpj-dev

# Or declarative
kubectl apply -f 01-namespace.yaml

# Set as default namespace
kubectl config set-context --current --namespace=anihpj-dev

# Verify
kubectl get namespaces
# NAME              STATUS   AGE
# anihpj-dev        Active   5s
# default           Active   1h
# kube-system       Active   1h

8.3 Step 2: Create ConfigMap

ConfigMaps hold non-sensitive configuration data. Our Django app needs settings module path, allowed hosts, and debug mode.

02-configmap.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: anihpj-config
  namespace: anihpj-dev
  labels:
    app: anihpj
data:
  DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE: "anihpj.settings"
  ALLOWED_HOSTS: "*"
  DEBUG: "True"
  DATABASE_ENGINE: "django.db.backends.sqlite3"
  DATABASE_NAME: "/app/db.sqlite3"
terminal 
# Apply
kubectl apply -f 02-configmap.yaml

# Verify
kubectl get configmap anihpj-config -n anihpj-dev -o yaml
# data:
#   ALLOWED_HOSTS: "*"
#   DATABASE_ENGINE: django.db.backends.sqlite3
#   DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE: anihpj.settings

# Imperative alternative (CKA speed)
kubectl create configmap anihpj-config \
  --from-literal=DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE=anihpj.settings \
  --from-literal=ALLOWED_HOSTS="*" \
  --from-literal=DEBUG=True \
  -n anihpj-dev
ConfigMap Secret
Purpose Non-sensitive config (URLs, feature flags) Sensitive data (passwords, tokens, keys)
Data format Plain text Base64-encoded
Size limit 1 MiB 1 MiB
Mount as Env vars or volume files Env vars or volume files (tmpfs)
RBAC Less restricted by default Should be RBAC-restricted
Create command kubectl create configmap kubectl create secret generic

8.4 Step 3: Create Secret

Secrets hold sensitive data — passwords, API keys, certificates. Values are base64-encoded (NOT encrypted by default).

03-secret.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: anihpj-secret
  namespace: anihpj-dev
  labels:
    app: anihpj
type: Opaque
data:
  # echo -n 'django-insecure-cka-practice-key-12345' | base64
  SECRET_KEY: ZGphbmdvLWluc2VjdXJlLWNrYS1wcmFjdGljZS1rZXktMTIzNDU=
  # echo -n 'sqlite:///app/db.sqlite3' | base64
  DATABASE_URL: c3FsaXRlOi8vL2FwcC9kYi5zcWxpdGUz
🚨 Security Warning: Secrets Are Base64, NOT Encrypted

By default, Kubernetes Secrets are only base64-encoded — anyone with kubectl get secret -o yaml access can decode them. In production, enable encryption at rest (EncryptionConfiguration) and use RBAC to restrict access. Never commit Secret YAML to Git.

terminal 
# Apply
kubectl apply -f 03-secret.yaml

# Imperative alternative (CKA speed — auto-encodes to base64)
kubectl create secret generic anihpj-secret \
  --from-literal=SECRET_KEY='django-insecure-cka-practice-key-12345' \
  --from-literal=DATABASE_URL='sqlite:///app/db.sqlite3' \
  -n anihpj-dev

# Verify (values are base64)
kubectl get secret anihpj-secret -n anihpj-dev -o yaml

# Decode a value
kubectl get secret anihpj-secret -n anihpj-dev \
  -o jsonpath='{.data.SECRET_KEY}' | base64 -d
# django-insecure-cka-practice-key-12345

📚 Kubernetes Secret Types Reference

Kubernetes has several built-in Secret types. Know which to use for the CKA:

Type Value for type: Purpose CKA?
Opaque Opaque Generic key-value (default) ✅ Most common
Docker Registry kubernetes.io/dockerconfigjson Image pull authentication ✅ Tested
TLS kubernetes.io/tls TLS certificate + private key ✅ Tested
Service Account kubernetes.io/service-account-token Auto-generated SA token ⚠️ Managed by K8s
Basic Auth kubernetes.io/basic-auth Username + password ❌ Rare
secret-types-examples.sh 
# Create docker-registry secret (for private images)
kubectl create secret docker-registry my-registry \
  --docker-server=registry.example.com \
  --docker-username=admin \
  --docker-password=pass123 \
  --docker-email=admin@example.com -n anihpj-dev

# Create TLS secret (for Ingress HTTPS)
kubectl create secret tls my-tls \
  --cert=path/to/tls.crt \
  --key=path/to/tls.key -n anihpj-dev

8.5 Step 4: Create the Deployment

The Deployment is the core of your application — it manages pods, rolling updates, and self-healing. This is the most complete manifest in our deployment.

04-deployment.yaml 
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj
  namespace: anihpj-dev
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    version: v1
spec:
  replicas: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj                       # ← MUST match template labels
  strategy:
    type: RollingUpdate
    rollingUpdate:
      maxSurge: 1
      maxUnavailable: 0
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj                     # ← MUST match selector
        version: v1
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: django
        image: anihpj:v1
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8000
          name: http
        envFrom:
        - configMapRef:
            name: anihpj-config          # ← all ConfigMap keys as env vars
        - secretRef:
            name: anihpj-secret          # ← all Secret keys as env vars
        resources:
          requests:
            cpu: "100m"                  # ← minimum CPU (0.1 core)
            memory: "128Mi"              # ← minimum memory
          limits:
            cpu: "500m"                  # ← maximum CPU (0.5 core)
            memory: "256Mi"              # ← maximum memory
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /
            port: 8000
          initialDelaySeconds: 10
          periodSeconds: 5
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /
            port: 8000
          initialDelaySeconds: 30
          periodSeconds: 10

Deployment Manifest — Field by Field

🔢 replicas: 2
Run 2 identical pods. K8s maintains this count via the ReplicaSet.
🏷️ selector.matchLabels
Links the Deployment to its pods. MUST match template.metadata.labels.
🔄 strategy: RollingUpdate
Zero-downtime updates. maxSurge=1 adds 1 new pod before removing old.
📦 envFrom
Injects ALL keys from ConfigMap/Secret as environment variables at once.
📊 resources
requests = guaranteed minimum. limits = maximum allowed. Prevents resource starvation.
💚 readinessProbe
Is the pod ready to receive traffic? Fails = removed from Service endpoints.
❤️ livenessProbe
Is the pod still alive? Fails = pod gets restarted by kubelet.
terminal 
# Apply the deployment
kubectl apply -f 04-deployment.yaml

# Watch pods being created
kubectl get pods -n anihpj-dev -w
# NAME                      READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE
# anihpj-6d7f8c9b5d-abc12   1/1     Running   0          15s
# anihpj-6d7f8c9b5d-def34   1/1     Running   0          15s
💡 envFrom vs env — Two Ways to Inject Variables

envFrom injects ALL keys from a ConfigMap/Secret as env vars (what we used above — simplest approach). env lets you pick individual keys and optionally rename them. Use env when you need selective injection or renaming:

env-vs-envfrom.yaml 
# envFrom: inject ALL keys from ConfigMap/Secret
envFrom:
- configMapRef:
    name: anihpj-config
- secretRef:
    name: anihpj-secret

# env: inject INDIVIDUAL keys (can rename)
env:
- name: DJANGO_SECRET       # ← name in container
  valueFrom:
    secretKeyRef:
      name: anihpj-secret
      key: SECRET_KEY        # ← name in Secret
- name: SETTINGS_MODULE      # ← renamed!
  valueFrom:
    configMapKeyRef:
      name: anihpj-config
      key: DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE
Rolling Update — Zero-Downtime Deployment
  Initial State (replicas: 2)
  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────┐
  │ Pod v1 ✅ │  │ Pod v1 ✅ │   Both running, serving traffic
  └──────────┘  └──────────┘

  Step 1: maxSurge=1 → Create new pod with v2
  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────┐
  │ Pod v1 ✅ │  │ Pod v1 ✅ │  │ Pod v2 🔄 │  New pod starting
  └──────────┘  └──────────┘  └──────────┘

  Step 2: v2 passes readinessProbe → remove one v1
  ┌──────────┐  ╔══════════╗  ┌──────────┐
  │ Pod v1 ✅ │  ║ Pod v1 ❌ ║  │ Pod v2 ✅ │  v2 ready, old v1 terminating
  └──────────┘  ╚══════════╝  └──────────┘

  Step 3: Start another v2 → remove last v1
  ╔══════════╗  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────┐
  ║ Pod v1 ❌ ║  │ Pod v2 ✅ │  │ Pod v2 🔄 │  Last v1 terminating
  ╚══════════╝  └──────────┘  └──────────┘

  Final State: All v2, zero downtime
  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────┐
  │ Pod v2 ✅ │  │ Pod v2 ✅ │   Update complete!
  └──────────┘  └──────────┘

  Key: maxSurge=1 means at most 3 pods during update (2+1)
       maxUnavailable=0 means traffic always has 2 healthy pods
rolling-update-commands.sh 
# Update the image (triggers rolling update)
kubectl set image deployment/anihpj django=anihpj:v2 -n anihpj-dev

# Watch the rollout in real time
kubectl rollout status deployment/anihpj -n anihpj-dev
# Waiting for deployment "anihpj" rollout to finish: 1 of 2 updated...
# deployment "anihpj" successfully rolled out

# View rollout history
kubectl rollout history deployment/anihpj -n anihpj-dev
# REVISION  CHANGE-CAUSE
# 1         <none>
# 2         <none>

# Rollback to previous version (CKA important!)
kubectl rollout undo deployment/anihpj -n anihpj-dev

# Rollback to specific revision
kubectl rollout undo deployment/anihpj --to-revision=1 -n anihpj-dev

8.6 Step 5: Create the Service

The Service gives your pods a stable IP and DNS name. Without it, you'd need to track individual pod IPs that change on every restart.

05-service.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: anihpj-svc
  namespace: anihpj-dev
  labels:
    app: anihpj
spec:
  type: ClusterIP
  selector:
    app: anihpj                      # ← selects pods with this label
  ports:
  - name: http
    port: 80                         # ← service port (what clients use)
    targetPort: 8000                 # ← container port (where Django listens)
    protocol: TCP
Service → Pod Traffic Flow
  Client Request
       │
       ▼
  ┌──────────────────────────┐
  │  Service: anihpj-svc     │
  │  ClusterIP: 10.96.45.123 │
  │  Port: 80                │
  └──────────┬───────────────┘
             │ selector: app=anihpj
             │ (finds all matching pods)
       ┌─────┴──────┐
       ▼            ▼
  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐
  │ Pod 1   │  │ Pod 2   │
  │ :8000   │  │ :8000   │
  │ app:    │  │ app:    │
  │ anihpj  │  │ anihpj  │
  └─────────┘  └─────────┘
  Round-robin load balancing
terminal 
# Apply
kubectl apply -f 05-service.yaml

# Verify service
kubectl get svc -n anihpj-dev
# NAME         TYPE        CLUSTER-IP     EXTERNAL-IP   PORT(S)   AGE
# anihpj-svc   ClusterIP   10.96.45.123   <none>        80/TCP    5s

# Check endpoints (should list pod IPs)
kubectl get endpoints anihpj-svc -n anihpj-dev
# NAME         ENDPOINTS                         AGE
# anihpj-svc   172.17.0.3:8000,172.17.0.4:8000   5s

📊 All Kubernetes Service Types — Comparison

We used ClusterIP above. Know all 4 types for the CKA:

Type Accessible From Use Case External Access
ClusterIP Inside cluster only Internal microservice communication ❌ No (use port-forward for dev)
NodePort Node IP + static port Dev/testing, direct node access ✅ Via <NodeIP>:<30000-32767>
LoadBalancer Cloud LB → cluster Production on cloud (AWS, GKE, AKS) ✅ Via cloud load balancer IP
ExternalName DNS CNAME redirect Alias to external services N/A (just a DNS alias)
Service Types — Visibility Layers
  External Traffic (Internet)
  ───────────────────────────────────────────────
       │
       ▼
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  LoadBalancer     ← Cloud provider LB     │
  │  (AWS ELB, GCP LB, Azure LB)             │
  └─────────────────────┬────────────────────┘
                        ▼
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  NodePort           ← All nodes listen    │
  │  (Port 30000-32767 on every node)         │
  └─────────────────────┬────────────────────┘
                        ▼
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  ClusterIP          ← Internal only       │
  │  (Virtual IP, accessible within cluster)  │
  │  Service: 10.96.45.123:80                 │
  └─────────────────────┬────────────────────┘
                        ▼
              ┌─────────┴──────────┐
              ▼                    ▼
         ┌─────────┐         ┌─────────┐
         │  Pod 1   │         │  Pod 2   │
         │  :8000   │         │  :8000   │
         └─────────┘         └─────────┘

  Each type INCLUDES the ones below it:
  LoadBalancer = NodePort + ClusterIP + external LB
  NodePort = ClusterIP + exposed port on all nodes

8.7 Step 6: Access Your Application

ClusterIP services are only accessible from inside the cluster. To access from your laptop, use port-forward:

terminal 
# Port-forward: laptop:8000 → service:80 → pod:8000
kubectl port-forward svc/anihpj-svc 8000:80 -n anihpj-dev
# Forwarding from 127.0.0.1:8000 -> 8000

# Now open your browser:
# http://localhost:8000          ← anihpj homepage
# http://localhost:8000/admin    ← Django admin

# Alternative: port-forward directly to a pod
kubectl port-forward pod/anihpj-6d7f8c9b5d-abc12 8000:8000 -n anihpj-dev

# Alternative: Minikube service (opens browser automatically)
minikube service anihpj-svc -n anihpj-dev
Port-Forward — How It Works
  Your Laptop                    Kubernetes Cluster
  ┌────────────────┐            ┌────────────────────────────┐
  │ Browser        │            │                            │
  │ localhost:8000 │───kubectl──│──▶ Service:80 ──▶ Pod:8000 │
  │                │  tunnel    │     anihpj-svc    django   │
  └────────────────┘            └────────────────────────────┘
  
  kubectl creates a secure tunnel from your machine to the cluster.
  Traffic: browser → kubectl → API server → pod

8.8 Step 7: Verify Everything

After deploying, run a comprehensive health check. This is the same verification workflow you'll use on the CKA exam:

verification-commands.sh 
# 1. See EVERYTHING in the namespace
kubectl get all -n anihpj-dev
# NAME                          READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE
# pod/anihpj-6d7f8c9b5d-abc12   1/1     Running   0          2m
# pod/anihpj-6d7f8c9b5d-def34   1/1     Running   0          2m
#
# NAME                 TYPE        CLUSTER-IP     PORT(S)   AGE
# service/anihpj-svc   ClusterIP   10.96.45.123   80/TCP    1m
#
# NAME                     READY   UP-TO-DATE   AVAILABLE   AGE
# deployment.apps/anihpj   2/2     2            2           2m
#
# NAME                                DESIRED   CURRENT   READY   AGE
# replicaset.apps/anihpj-6d7f8c9b5d   2         2         2       2m

# 2. Detailed deployment info
kubectl describe deployment anihpj -n anihpj-dev

# 3. Pod logs (check for errors)
kubectl logs deployment/anihpj -n anihpj-dev
# Watching for Django startup messages...

# 4. Check environment variables are injected
kubectl exec deployment/anihpj -n anihpj-dev -- env | grep DJANGO
# DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE=anihpj.settings

# 5. Check ConfigMap and Secret are mounted
kubectl get configmap,secret -n anihpj-dev
# NAME                         DATA   AGE
# configmap/anihpj-config      5      3m
# NAME                         TYPE     DATA   AGE
# secret/anihpj-secret         Opaque   2      3m
✅
Healthy Deployment Checklist

All pods STATUS=Running, READY=1/1. Deployment AVAILABLE matches replicas. Service has ENDPOINTS listed. No error events.

❌
Common Problems

ImagePullBackOff = wrong image name. CrashLoopBackOff = app crashes on start. Pending = no node has enough resources. 0 endpoints = label mismatch.

Troubleshooting Decision Tree — Pod Not Running?
  kubectl get pods -n anihpj-dev
       │
       ├── STATUS: Pending
       │       └── kubectl describe pod <name>
       │           ├── "Insufficient cpu/memory" → Lower resource requests or add nodes
       │           ├── "no nodes match" → Check nodeSelector, taints, tolerations
       │           └── "Unschedulable" → kubectl uncordon <node>
       │
       ├── STATUS: ImagePullBackOff / ErrImagePull
       │       └── Wrong image name, tag doesn't exist, or registry auth required
       │           └── Fix: check image:tag spelling, create imagePullSecret
       │
       ├── STATUS: CrashLoopBackOff
       │       └── kubectl logs <pod> --previous
       │           ├── App error → Fix application code/config
       │           ├── Missing env var → Check ConfigMap/Secret refs
       │           └── Permission denied → Check securityContext
       │
       ├── STATUS: Running but READY: 0/1
       │       └── Readiness probe failing
       │           └── Check readinessProbe path/port, app might need more startup time
       │
       └── STATUS: Running, READY: 1/1 → ✅ Healthy!

🧹 Cleanup — Tear Down the Deployment

When you're done practicing, clean up all resources with one command:

cleanup.sh 
# Option 1: Delete the namespace (removes EVERYTHING inside)
kubectl delete namespace anihpj-dev
# namespace "anihpj-dev" deleted
# → All pods, services, configmaps, secrets in anihpj-dev are gone

# Option 2: Delete individual resources
kubectl delete -f 05-service.yaml
kubectl delete -f 04-deployment.yaml
kubectl delete -f 03-secret.yaml
kubectl delete -f 02-configmap.yaml
kubectl delete -f 01-namespace.yaml

# Option 3: Delete all from a directory
kubectl delete -f k8s/

# Reset your context namespace
kubectl config set-context --current --namespace=default

🔗 Resource Dependency Chain — What Depends on What

Understanding the dependency order prevents "resource not found" errors:

Deployment Resource Dependencies
  Create Order (top → bottom)         Delete Order (bottom → top)
  ═══════════════════════════         ═════════════════════════════

  1. Namespace                         5. Namespace (deletes ALL inside!)
     └── creates boundary                 ↑
                                          │
  2. ConfigMap + Secret                4. ConfigMap + Secret
     └── config data ready                ↑ (deployment needs these)
                                          │
  3. Deployment                        3. Deployment
     └── refs ConfigMap/Secret            ↑ (service targets its pods)
     └── creates ReplicaSet               │
         └── creates Pods              2. Service
                                          ↑
  4. Service                           1. Ingress (if exists)
     └── selector finds pods
                                     ⚡ Shortcut: 
  5. Ingress (optional)              kubectl delete namespace anihpj-dev
     └── routes to service           → removes EVERYTHING at once!
⚠️
Common Dependency Mistakes
  • • Creating Deployment before ConfigMap → CreateContainerError
  • • Wrong Secret name in Deployment → Pod stuck in Pending
  • • Service selector doesn't match pod labels → 0 endpoints
  • • Deployment in wrong namespace → resource not found
✅
Verification at Each Step
  • • After Namespace: kubectl get ns
  • • After ConfigMap: kubectl get cm -n <ns>
  • • After Deployment: kubectl get pods -n <ns>
  • • After Service: kubectl get ep -n <ns>

8.9 What Just Happened? — Full Walkthrough

When you ran kubectl apply, a complex chain of events happened inside the cluster. Understanding this flow is essential for CKA troubleshooting:

End-to-End Deployment Flow — What K8s Did With Your YAML
  YOU: $ kubectl apply -f 04-deployment.yaml
       $ kubectl apply -f 05-service.yaml
            │
  ┌─────────▼──────────────┐
  │  1. API SERVER           │  Receives YAML, validates schema,
  │     validates & stores   │  authenticates you, authorizes action,
  │     in etcd              │  stores object in etcd
  └─────────┬──────────────┘
            ▼
  ┌────────────────────────┐
  │  2. CONTROLLER MANAGER   │  deployment-controller sees new Deployment →
  │     creates child objects │  creates ReplicaSet → RS creates Pod objects
  └─────────┬──────────────┘
            ▼
  ┌────────────────────────┐
  │  3. SCHEDULER            │  Sees unassigned Pods → evaluates nodes
  │     assigns pods to      │  (resources, taints, affinity) →
  │     worker nodes         │  assigns best worker node
  └─────────┬──────────────┘
            ▼
  ┌────────────────────────┐
  │  4. KUBELET              │  On assigned node: sees pod spec →
  │     (on worker node)     │  pulls anihpj:v1 image →
  │     starts containers    │  starts container via containerd
  └─────────┬──────────────┘
            ▼
  ┌────────────────────────┐
  │  5. KUBE-PROXY           │  Updates iptables/IPVS rules →
  │     (all nodes)          │  Service ClusterIP 10.96.45.123
  │     creates routing      │  now routes to pod IPs
  └─────────┬──────────────┘
            ▼
  ┌────────────────────────┐
  │  6. YOU: port-forward    │  kubectl creates tunnel →
  │     localhost:8000       │  browser → API server → pod
  │     anihpj is LIVE! 🎉  │  Django responds with job listings
  └────────────────────────┘
1
API Server

Validates, authenticates, stores in etcd

2
Controller Manager

Creates ReplicaSet → creates Pod objects

3
Scheduler

Assigns pods to worker nodes

4
Kubelet

Pulls image, starts container

5
Kube-Proxy

Updates networking rules for Service

6
Port-Forward

You access anihpj at localhost:8000 🎉

⚠️ CKA Gotcha: First Deployment Traps

  • Namespace must exist first — kubectl apply -f deploy.yaml -n myns fails if myns doesn't exist. Always kubectl create namespace myns first, or include the namespace in the YAML metadata
  • Secret must exist before the Pod that references it — If a Deployment references a Secret or ConfigMap that doesn't exist, pods will be stuck in CreateContainerConfigError
  • Order matters for multi-resource deployments — Create in this order: Namespace → ConfigMap/Secret → PVC → Deployment → Service. Reversing causes dependency errors
  • port-forward ≠ Service — Port-forward is for local testing only. On the CKA, they expect you to create a proper Service (ClusterIP, NodePort) for connectivity
  • Container port vs Service port — containerPort in the pod spec is informational. The Service's targetPort must match the actual port your app listens on (e.g., Django on 8000)

CKA Speed Tip — Full app deployment in under 3 minutes:
kubectl create ns myapp
kubectl create configmap myapp-config --from-literal=DB_HOST=postgres -n myapp
kubectl create secret generic myapp-secret --from-literal=DB_PASS=s3cret -n myapp
kubectl create deployment myapp --image=myapp:v1 --replicas=2 -n myapp
kubectl expose deployment myapp --port=80 --target-port=8000 -n myapp
Then edit the deployment to add envFrom references if needed.

⚡ Deployment verification commands: kubectl get all -n <namespace> — see all resources in one view
kubectl logs deployment/<name> -n <ns> — check application logs
kubectl exec -it <pod> -n <ns> -- /bin/sh — shell into a running container
kubectl get endpoints <svc> -n <ns> — verify service has backend pods
📝 Chapter 8 Practice Drill

Timed challenge (target: 6 minutes):

  • 1. Create namespace anihpj-drill
  • 2. Create a ConfigMap app-config with DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE=anihpj.settings
  • 3. Create a Secret db-creds with DB_PASSWORD=exampass123
  • 4. Create Deployment anihpj with image python:3.11, 2 replicas, env from ConfigMap and Secret
  • 5. Expose as ClusterIP service on port 8000
  • 6. Verify: kubectl get all,cm,secret -n anihpj-drill

Key insight: This mirrors real CKA questions — create namespace, config, secrets, deployment, service.

🎯 Part 2 Complete — Key Takeaways

You've set up an environment, mastered kubectl, learned YAML, and deployed a real application. Here are the commands you must remember:

must-remember-commands.sh 
# Generate YAML (CKA speed trick)
kubectl run nginx --image=nginx --dry-run=client -o yaml > pod.yaml
kubectl create deploy web --image=nginx --replicas=3 --dry-run=client -o yaml
kubectl expose deploy web --port=80 --type=ClusterIP --dry-run=client -o yaml

# Context management (every CKA question)
kubectl config use-context k8s-cluster-1
kubectl config set-context --current --namespace=anihpj-dev

# API documentation (your exam cheat sheet)
kubectl explain pod.spec.containers
kubectl explain deployment.spec.strategy --recursive

# Deployment verification
kubectl get all -n anihpj-dev
kubectl describe deployment anihpj -n anihpj-dev
kubectl logs deployment/anihpj -n anihpj-dev
kubectl exec -it deployment/anihpj -n anihpj-dev -- /bin/sh

📝 Chapter 8 — CKA Practice Questions

Q1
Walk through the complete workflow to deploy a Django application (anihpj) to Kubernetes. What resources do you need to create, and in what order?
Show Answer
Answer
# Step 1: Create a namespace
kubectl create namespace anihpj-dev

# Step 2: Create ConfigMap for non-sensitive config
kubectl create configmap anihpj-config \
  --from-literal=DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE=anihpj.settings \
  --from-literal=ALLOWED_HOSTS="*" \
  -n anihpj-dev

# Step 3: Create Secret for sensitive data
kubectl create secret generic anihpj-secret \
  --from-literal=SECRET_KEY='your-django-secret-key' \
  --from-literal=DB_PASSWORD='dbpass123' \
  -n anihpj-dev

# Step 4: Create Deployment
kubectl create deployment anihpj \
  --image=anihpj:latest --replicas=2 \
  -n anihpj-dev --dry-run=client -o yaml > deploy.yaml
# Edit deploy.yaml to add envFrom, ports, probes
kubectl apply -f deploy.yaml

# Step 5: Expose with a Service
kubectl expose deployment anihpj \
  --port=80 --target-port=8000 \
  --type=NodePort -n anihpj-dev

Order matters: Namespace → ConfigMap/Secrets → Deployment → Service

Explanation

The order is critical because each resource depends on the previous: the Deployment references ConfigMaps and Secrets (they must exist first or the pod will fail), and the Service targets pods created by the Deployment. On the CKA, questions often present the end state and ask you to recreate it. Always check: does the namespace exist? Are ConfigMaps/Secrets created? Are labels matching between Deployment and Service selectors?

Q2
You deploy an application and the pod stays in CrashLoopBackOff. The kubectl logs output shows: "ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'django'". What is the problem and how do you fix it?
Show Answer
Answer
# Step 1: Verify the logs
kubectl logs pod/anihpj-7f8d6c9b4-x2k9p -n anihpj-dev

# Step 2: Check the image — the issue is the container image
# Either wrong base image or dependencies not installed

# Step 3: Debug inside the container
kubectl run debug-pod --image=anihpj:latest --rm -it \
  --command -- pip list
# If django is not listed, the Dockerfile is wrong

# Fix: Rebuild the Docker image with proper requirements
# In the Dockerfile:
FROM python:3.11-slim
WORKDIR /app
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt  # Must include django
COPY . .
CMD ["python", "manage.py", "runserver", "0.0.0.0:8000"]
Explanation

CrashLoopBackOff means the container starts, crashes, and Kubernetes keeps restarting it with exponential backoff. Common causes: missing dependencies (like here), incorrect CMD/ENTRYPOINT, missing environment variables, or permission issues. On the CKA, the debugging pattern is always the same: kubectl describe pod → check Events section → kubectl logs → check application error. If the container crashes too fast for logs, use kubectl logs --previous to see the previous container's logs.

Q3
How do you pass environment variables from a ConfigMap and a Secret to a Deployment? Show both envFrom and individual env with valueFrom approaches.
Show Answer
Answer
# Method 1: envFrom — loads ALL keys from ConfigMap/Secret
spec:
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: myapp:1.0
    envFrom:
    - configMapRef:
        name: app-config      # All keys become env vars
    - secretRef:
        name: app-secrets     # All keys become env vars

# Method 2: env with valueFrom — load specific keys
spec:
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: myapp:1.0
    env:
    - name: DB_HOST            # Env var name in container
      valueFrom:
        configMapKeyRef:
          name: app-config     # ConfigMap name
          key: database_host   # Key in the ConfigMap
    - name: DB_PASSWORD
      valueFrom:
        secretKeyRef:
          name: app-secrets    # Secret name
          key: db_password     # Key in the Secret
Explanation

envFrom is faster for bulk loading — every key in the ConfigMap/Secret becomes an environment variable with the same name. env.valueFrom gives you control: you can rename keys and pick only specific values. On the CKA, envFrom is faster to type but valueFrom is more common in exam questions. Key gotcha: if a ConfigMap key has a hyphen (like db-host), it becomes an invalid shell variable — use env.valueFrom to rename it (e.g., DB_HOST).

Q4
Your Deployment has replicas: 3 but only 2 pods are running. The third pod is in Pending state. How do you diagnose and fix this?
Show Answer
Answer
# Step 1: Check pod status
kubectl get pods -n anihpj-dev
# NAME                     READY   STATUS    AGE
# anihpj-xxx-abc           1/1     Running   5m
# anihpj-xxx-def           1/1     Running   5m
# anihpj-xxx-ghi           0/1     Pending   5m

# Step 2: Describe the Pending pod — check Events
kubectl describe pod anihpj-xxx-ghi -n anihpj-dev
# Events:
#   Warning  FailedScheduling  0/3 nodes are available:
#   1 node(s) had taint {node-role.kubernetes.io/control-plane},
#   2 node(s) had insufficient cpu

# Step 3: Common causes and fixes:
# a) Insufficient resources → reduce requests or add nodes
# b) Node taints → add tolerations or untaint nodes
# c) NodeSelector/affinity mismatch → fix labels
# d) PVC not bound → check StorageClass/PV availability

# Fix for insufficient CPU:
kubectl top nodes    # Check resource usage
# Edit deployment to reduce CPU requests
kubectl edit deployment anihpj -n anihpj-dev
# Or add a new node to the cluster
Explanation

Pending means the scheduler cannot find a suitable node. The Events section of kubectl describe pod tells you exactly why. The CKA frequently tests this scenario. The diagnosis tree: Pending → FailedScheduling → read the message. Messages include: "insufficient cpu/memory" (resource constraint), "didn't match node selector" (label mismatch), "had taint" (toleration needed), "unbound PVC" (storage issue). Always check kubectl describe Events first — the answer is almost always right there.

Q5
How do you update the container image of a running Deployment from anihpj:v1 to anihpj:v2 without editing YAML? What happens during the rollout?
Show Answer
Answer
# Imperative image update (fastest on CKA)
kubectl set image deployment/anihpj app=anihpj:v2 -n anihpj-dev

# Watch the rollout
kubectl rollout status deployment/anihpj -n anihpj-dev
# Waiting for deployment "anihpj" rollout to finish: 1 out of 3 new replicas updated...
# deployment "anihpj" successfully rolled out

# What happens during the rollout:
# 1. New ReplicaSet created with v2 image
# 2. New pods start coming up (controlled by maxSurge)
# 3. Old pods start terminating (controlled by maxUnavailable)
# 4. Default: RollingUpdate with 25% maxSurge, 25% maxUnavailable

# Verify the update
kubectl get replicasets -n anihpj-dev
# NAME                DESIRED   CURRENT   READY   AGE
# anihpj-7f8d6c9b4   0         0         0       10m   ← old (v1)
# anihpj-9a1b2c3d4   3         3         3       1m    ← new (v2)

# Undo if something goes wrong
kubectl rollout undo deployment/anihpj -n anihpj-dev
Explanation

kubectl set image is the fastest way to update an image on the CKA. The Deployment controller creates a new ReplicaSet, gradually scales it up while scaling the old one down. This is a rolling update — the application has zero downtime because old pods serve traffic until new pods are ready. The old ReplicaSet is kept (scaled to 0) for rollback. kubectl rollout undo simply scales the old ReplicaSet back up and the new one down. You can also rollback to a specific revision: kubectl rollout undo deployment/anihpj --to-revision=2.

Q6
Write a complete Service manifest that exposes the anihpj Deployment on NodePort 30080. What is the relationship between port, targetPort, and nodePort?
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: anihpj-svc
  namespace: anihpj-dev
spec:
  type: NodePort
  selector:
    app: anihpj          # Must match Deployment pod labels
  ports:
  - port: 80             # Service port (cluster-internal)
    targetPort: 8000     # Container port (where app listens)
    nodePort: 30080      # Node port (external access)
    protocol: TCP
Field What It Does Example
port Service's own port (other pods connect here) anihpj-svc:80
targetPort Container port (where the app listens) 8000 (Django default)
nodePort External port on every node (30000-32767) <NodeIP>:30080
Explanation

Traffic flow: External → NodeIP:nodePort → ServiceIP:port → PodIP:targetPort. The selector must match the pod labels from the Deployment. If targetPort is omitted, it defaults to the same value as port. If nodePort is omitted, Kubernetes assigns a random port in 30000-32767. On the CKA, the fastest approach: kubectl expose deployment anihpj --port=80 --target-port=8000 --type=NodePort --dry-run=client -o yaml > svc.yaml, then add the nodePort value.

Q7
You need to deploy the anihpj app to a specific namespace production that doesn't exist yet. Write the complete sequence of commands.
Show Answer
Answer
# Step 1: Create the namespace
kubectl create namespace production

# Step 2: Set context to avoid -n flag on every command
kubectl config set-context --current --namespace=production

# Step 3: Verify context
kubectl config view --minify | grep namespace

# Step 4: Create deployment (now in production namespace)
kubectl create deployment anihpj --image=anihpj:v1 --replicas=3

# Step 5: Expose it
kubectl expose deployment anihpj --port=80 --target-port=8000

# Step 6: Verify everything
kubectl get all
# Shows deployment, replicaset, pods, service all in production

# Alternative: Do it all without switching context
kubectl create namespace production
kubectl create deployment anihpj --image=anihpj:v1 --replicas=3 -n production
kubectl expose deployment anihpj --port=80 --target-port=8000 -n production
Explanation

On the CKA exam, every question specifies a context and namespace. Always switch context first: kubectl config use-context <name>. Then set the namespace: kubectl config set-context --current --namespace=<ns>. This prevents the most common exam mistake — deploying to the wrong namespace. If a namespace doesn't exist, create it first. If you forget -n and deploy to default, you'll need to recreate in the correct namespace. Practice this workflow until it's automatic.

Q8
Your deployment is running but when you curl the Service, you get "Connection refused". How do you systematically debug this?
Show Answer
Answer
# Debug chain: Pod → Service → Endpoint → Network

# Step 1: Is the pod actually running and ready?
kubectl get pods -o wide
# Check READY column: 1/1 means healthy

# Step 2: Can you reach the app directly in the pod?
kubectl exec -it anihpj-xxx -- curl localhost:8000
# If this fails → app issue, not K8s

# Step 3: Does the Service have Endpoints?
kubectl get endpoints anihpj-svc
# If ENDPOINTS is empty → label mismatch!

# Step 4: Check label match
kubectl get pods --show-labels
kubectl describe svc anihpj-svc | grep Selector
# Compare Selector with pod labels — must match

# Step 5: Verify port mapping
kubectl describe svc anihpj-svc
# Check: TargetPort should match containerPort

# Common causes:
# 1. Labels don't match → fix selector or pod labels
# 2. targetPort ≠ containerPort → fix port mapping
# 3. App listening on 127.0.0.1 not 0.0.0.0 → fix app config
# 4. Pod not Ready → check readinessProbe
Explanation

The debug chain for "can't reach service" is: Pod running? → App listening? → Endpoints exist? → Labels match? → Ports correct? The most common issue is empty Endpoints, caused by label mismatch between Service selector and pod labels. The second most common is port mismatch — the Service targetPort must match the container's actual listening port. On the CKA, always check Endpoints first: kubectl get ep <service-name>. No endpoints = no pods match the selector.

Q9
How do you scale a Deployment from 2 replicas to 5, and then verify that all pods are running and healthy?
Show Answer
Answer
# Method 1: kubectl scale (imperative, fastest)
kubectl scale deployment anihpj --replicas=5 -n anihpj-dev

# Method 2: kubectl patch
kubectl patch deployment anihpj -n anihpj-dev \
  -p '{"spec":{"replicas":5}}'

# Method 3: kubectl edit
kubectl edit deployment anihpj -n anihpj-dev
# Change spec.replicas from 2 to 5

# Verification:
kubectl get deployment anihpj -n anihpj-dev
# NAME    READY   UP-TO-DATE   AVAILABLE   AGE
# anihpj  5/5     5            5           10m

kubectl get pods -n anihpj-dev
# All 5 pods should show STATUS: Running, READY: 1/1

# Watch in real-time
kubectl get pods -n anihpj-dev -w
# Shows pods being created live
Explanation

kubectl scale is the fastest method on the CKA. The Deployment controller adjusts the ReplicaSet's replica count, and the ReplicaSet controller creates new pods. To verify health: READY x/x means all containers are ready, UP-TO-DATE means all pods match the current template, AVAILABLE means pods have passed readinessProbe. If AVAILABLE < READY, some pods aren't passing their readiness checks. If pods stay Pending after scaling, it usually means insufficient node resources.

Q10
You need to generate the YAML for a complete application stack (Namespace, ConfigMap, Deployment, Service) from scratch using only imperative commands and redirect to a single file. How?
Show Answer
Answer
# Generate all resources into one file using ---

# Namespace
kubectl create namespace myapp --dry-run=client -o yaml > stack.yaml

# ConfigMap (append with >>)
echo "---" >> stack.yaml
kubectl create configmap myapp-config \
  --from-literal=APP_ENV=production \
  --from-literal=LOG_LEVEL=info \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml >> stack.yaml

# Deployment
echo "---" >> stack.yaml
kubectl create deployment myapp \
  --image=myapp:v1 --replicas=3 \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml >> stack.yaml

# Service
echo "---" >> stack.yaml
kubectl create service clusterip myapp \
  --tcp=80:8000 \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml >> stack.yaml

# Verify the file
cat stack.yaml

# Apply everything at once
kubectl apply -f stack.yaml

# Or validate first
kubectl apply -f stack.yaml --dry-run=server
Explanation

This is the CKA power technique: generate YAML imperatively, combine into one file, edit as needed, apply. The > file.yaml creates/overwrites, >> file.yaml appends. The --- separator between documents is critical. This approach is faster than writing YAML from scratch. After generating, edit the file to add anything imperative commands don't support: envFrom, volumeMounts, probes, resources, nodeSelector, etc. On the CKA, speed matters — generate the boilerplate, then customize.

Environment Setup Ch 5 ✅
kubectl Mastery Ch 6 ✅
YAML & Manifests Ch 7 ✅
First Deployment Ch 8 ✅
CKA Journey Progress Part 2 of 10

🟡 Part 3: Cluster Installation & Configuration Chapters 9-12

Chapter 9: Installing Kubernetes with kubeadm

This is the chapter that separates "I know Kubernetes concepts" from "I can build a Kubernetes cluster from scratch." kubeadm is the official tool for creating production-grade clusters, and the CKA exam heavily tests this skill. You'll walk through every step — from preparing nodes to joining workers — using our anihpj/jobpost project as the target workload.

🟡 Intermediate ⏱️ ~2.5 hours 🔧 kubeadm 📝 CKA EXAM

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ Understand what kubeadm does (and what it doesn't do)
  • ✅ Prepare all nodes — disable swap, load kernel modules, install containerd
  • ✅ Initialize the control plane with kubeadm init
  • ✅ Install a CNI plugin to enable pod-to-pod networking
  • ✅ Join worker nodes to the cluster with kubeadm join
  • ✅ Verify the cluster is fully operational
  • ✅ Practice the complete cluster build workflow under CKA time pressure
🎓 CKA Relevance
Cluster Architecture, Installation & Configuration 25%
kubeadm = 25% of Your CKA Score

The "Cluster Architecture, Installation & Configuration" domain is worth 25% of the CKA exam. You will almost certainly get a question asking you to bootstrap a cluster or join a node. The exact steps in this chapter are what the exam tests.

  • Know the prerequisites — swap off, containerd, kernel modules — the exam may ask you to fix a broken node
  • Know kubeadm init flags — especially --pod-network-cidr and --apiserver-advertise-address
  • Know how to recover the join command — kubeadm token create --print-join-command

9.1 What is kubeadm?

kubeadm is the official Kubernetes tool for bootstrapping clusters. It handles the complex orchestration of generating certificates, starting control plane components, and configuring networking — but it does NOT provision the machines themselves.

kubeadm: What It Does vs What It Doesn't
  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                                                                │
  │  YOU provide:                    kubeadm handles:              │
  │                                                                │
  │  ┌──────────────────────┐       ┌───────────────────────────┐ │
  │  │ 🖥️ VMs / Bare Metal   │       │ ✅ Bootstrap control plane │ │
  │  │ OS installed (Ubuntu) │       │ ✅ Generate TLS certs      │ │
  │  │ Network connectivity  │       │ ✅ Create etcd cluster     │ │
  │  │ Swap disabled         │       │ ✅ Configure kubelet       │ │
  │  │ Container runtime     │       │ ✅ Join worker nodes       │ │
  │  │ (containerd)          │       │ ✅ Set up kube-proxy       │ │
  │  └──────────────────────┘       └───────────────────────────┘ │
  │                                                                │
  │  ❌ NOT kubeadm's job:                                        │
  │     • VM provisioning (use Vagrant, Terraform, cloud console) │
  │     • OS installation / updates                                │
  │     • CNI plugin install (you choose: Calico, Flannel, etc.)  │
  │     • Load balancer setup (for HA control plane)              │
  │     • Application deployment (that's kubectl's job)           │
  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
🔧
kubeadm init

Run on the control plane node. Generates certificates, starts API server, etcd, scheduler, controller manager.

🔗
kubeadm join

Run on worker nodes. Connects the node to an existing cluster using a token + CA cert hash.

🖥️Provision VMs
→
⚙️Prerequisites
→
🔧kubeadm init
→
🌐Install CNI
→
🔗kubeadm join
→
✅Cluster Ready

9.2 Pre-requisites for All Nodes

Before running kubeadm, every node (control plane AND workers) must meet these requirements. On the CKA exam, you may be asked to fix a node that's missing one of these steps.

🖥️
Hardware

2+ CPUs, 2 GB+ RAM, unique hostname/MAC per node, full network connectivity

🚫
Swap Off

Kubernetes requires swap disabled. kubelet won't start with swap on.

🐳
Container Runtime

containerd (recommended), CRI-O, or Docker + cri-dockerd shim.

📦
K8s Packages

kubeadm, kubelet, kubectl — all pinned to the same version.

Step A: Disable Swap

all-nodes.sh 
# Disable swap immediately
sudo swapoff -a

# Disable swap permanently (survives reboot)
sudo sed -i '/swap/d' /etc/fstab

# Verify swap is off
free -h | grep Swap
# Swap:            0B          0B          0B

Step B: Load Kernel Modules

/etc/modules-load.d/k8s.conf 
# Create module config
cat <<EOF | sudo tee /etc/modules-load.d/k8s.conf
overlay
br_netfilter
EOF

# Load modules now
sudo modprobe overlay
sudo modprobe br_netfilter

# Verify
lsmod | grep -E "overlay|br_netfilter"
# br_netfilter           32768  0
# overlay               151552  0

Step C: Configure Sysctl Parameters

/etc/sysctl.d/k8s.conf 
# Enable bridge networking and IP forwarding
cat <<EOF | sudo tee /etc/sysctl.d/k8s.conf
net.bridge.bridge-nf-call-iptables  = 1
net.bridge.bridge-nf-call-ip6tables = 1
net.ipv4.ip_forward                 = 1
EOF

# Apply without reboot
sudo sysctl --system

# Verify
sysctl net.bridge.bridge-nf-call-iptables
# net.bridge.bridge-nf-call-iptables = 1

Step D: Install containerd

install-containerd.sh 
# Install containerd (Ubuntu/Debian)
sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get install -y containerd

# Generate default config
sudo mkdir -p /etc/containerd
containerd config default | sudo tee /etc/containerd/config.toml

# CRITICAL: Enable SystemdCgroup (required for K8s)
sudo sed -i 's/SystemdCgroup = false/SystemdCgroup = true/' /etc/containerd/config.toml

# Restart and enable
sudo systemctl restart containerd
sudo systemctl enable containerd

# Verify
sudo systemctl status containerd
# ● containerd.service - containerd container runtime
#      Active: active (running)
🚨 Critical: SystemdCgroup Must Be true

If SystemdCgroup is false in containerd's config, kubelet will fail to start pods correctly. This is one of the most common "broken node" scenarios on the CKA exam. Always verify this setting after installing containerd.

Step E: Install kubeadm, kubelet, kubectl

install-k8s-packages.sh 
# Add Kubernetes apt repo (Ubuntu/Debian)
sudo apt-get install -y apt-transport-https ca-certificates curl
curl -fsSL https://pkgs.k8s.io/core:/stable:/v1.30/deb/Release.key \
  | sudo gpg --dearmor -o /etc/apt/keyrings/kubernetes-apt-keyring.gpg
echo 'deb [signed-by=/etc/apt/keyrings/kubernetes-apt-keyring.gpg] \
  https://pkgs.k8s.io/core:/stable:/v1.30/deb/ /' \
  | sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/kubernetes.list

# Install packages
sudo apt-get update
