sudo apt-get install -y kubelet kubeadm kubectl

# CRITICAL: Hold versions (prevent auto-upgrade breaking cluster)
sudo apt-mark hold kubelet kubeadm kubectl

# Verify
kubeadm version
# kubeadm version: &version.Info{Major:"1", Minor:"30", ...}
⚠️ CKA Exam Note: Version Pinning

apt-mark hold prevents package upgrades from accidentally breaking your cluster. On the CKA, you may need to upgrade a cluster — in that case you explicitly unhold, upgrade, then hold again. We cover this in Chapter 23.

A
Disable Swap

swapoff -a + remove from fstab

B
Kernel Modules

overlay + br_netfilter

C
Sysctl Params

IP forwarding + bridge-nf-call

D
Container Runtime

containerd + SystemdCgroup=true

E
K8s Packages

kubeadm + kubelet + kubectl (held)

9.3 Initialize the Control Plane Node

With prerequisites done on all nodes, run kubeadm init on the control plane node only. This is the single most important command in cluster creation.

control-plane-init.sh 
# Initialize the control plane
sudo kubeadm init \
  --pod-network-cidr=10.244.0.0/16 \
  --apiserver-advertise-address=192.168.56.10 \
  --kubernetes-version=v1.30.0

# Expected output (abbreviated):
# [init] Using Kubernetes version: v1.30.0
# [certs] Generating certificates...
# [certs]  etcd/ca
# [certs]  etcd/server
# [certs]  apiserver
# [certs]  apiserver-kubelet-client
# [kubeconfig] Writing kubeconfig files...
# [kubelet-start] Starting the kubelet
# [control-plane] Creating static Pod manifests...
# [etcd] Creating static Pod manifest for local etcd
# [upload-config] Storing configuration in ConfigMap
# [mark-control-plane] Marking the node as control-plane
#
# Your Kubernetes control-plane has initialized successfully!
#
# To start using your cluster, run:
#   mkdir -p $HOME/.kube
#   sudo cp -i /etc/kubernetes/admin.conf $HOME/.kube/config
#   sudo chown $(id -u):$(id -g) $HOME/.kube/config
#
# You can now join worker nodes by running:
#   kubeadm join 192.168.56.10:6443 --token abcdef.0123456789abcdef \
#     --discovery-token-ca-cert-hash sha256:abc123...

kubeadm init Flags — What Each One Does

🌐 --pod-network-cidr
IP address range for pods. Must match your CNI plugin. Calico: 192.168.0.0/16, Flannel: 10.244.0.0/16
📡 --apiserver-advertise-address
IP address the API server advertises to other nodes. Use the control plane node's IP.
📌 --kubernetes-version
Pin to an exact version. Prevents version mismatches between components.
🔒 --control-plane-endpoint
For HA clusters: the load balancer address shared by all control plane nodes.

Post-Init: Configure kubectl Access

post-init-kubeconfig.sh 
# MUST run these 3 commands as regular user (NOT root)
mkdir -p $HOME/.kube
sudo cp -i /etc/kubernetes/admin.conf $HOME/.kube/config
sudo chown $(id -u):$(id -g) $HOME/.kube/config

# Verify kubectl works
kubectl get nodes
# NAME           STATUS     ROLES           AGE   VERSION
# control-plane  NotReady   control-plane   30s   v1.30.0
# (NotReady because CNI is not installed yet!)
⚠️ SAVE THE JOIN COMMAND!

The kubeadm join command shown at the end of kubeadm init contains a token that expires in 24 hours. Copy it now! If you lose it, regenerate with kubeadm token create --print-join-command.

What kubeadm init Creates
  $ sudo kubeadm init
       │
       ├── 🔐 Generates Certificates (/etc/kubernetes/pki/)
       │   ├── ca.crt, ca.key                    ← Cluster CA
       │   ├── apiserver.crt, apiserver.key       ← API Server TLS
       │   ├── apiserver-kubelet-client.crt       ← API→Kubelet auth
       │   ├── front-proxy-ca.crt                 ← Aggregation layer
       │   ├── etcd/ca.crt, etcd/server.crt       ← etcd TLS
       │   └── sa.key, sa.pub                     ← ServiceAccount signing
       │
       ├── 📋 Creates kubeconfig Files (/etc/kubernetes/)
       │   ├── admin.conf           ← cluster-admin kubeconfig
       │   ├── kubelet.conf         ← kubelet kubeconfig
       │   ├── controller-manager.conf
       │   └── scheduler.conf
       │
       ├── 📦 Creates Static Pod Manifests (/etc/kubernetes/manifests/)
       │   ├── etcd.yaml
       │   ├── kube-apiserver.yaml
       │   ├── kube-controller-manager.yaml
       │   └── kube-scheduler.yaml
       │
       └── 🚀 Starts Components
           ├── kubelet (systemd service) watches /etc/kubernetes/manifests/
           ├── etcd, API server, scheduler, controller manager start as static pods
           └── kube-proxy deployed as DaemonSet

9.4 Install a CNI Plugin (Pod Networking)

Without a CNI (Container Network Interface) plugin, pods cannot communicate across nodes. The control plane will stay NotReady until you install one. This is a required step the CKA expects you to know.

CNI Plugin Features Performance Complexity CKA Choice?
Calico Network policies, BGP, IPIP 🟢 High 🟡 Medium ✅ Most common
Flannel Simple VXLAN overlay 🟡 Medium 🟢 Low ✅ Good for learning
Cilium eBPF-based, advanced security 🟢 Very High 🔴 High ⚠️ Advanced
Weave Net Simple, encrypted mesh 🟡 Medium 🟢 Low ⚠️ End of life
install-cni.sh 
# Option 1: Calico (recommended for CKA — supports NetworkPolicy)
kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/projectcalico/calico/v3.27.0/manifests/calico.yaml

# Option 2: Flannel (simpler, use --pod-network-cidr=10.244.0.0/16)
kubectl apply -f https://github.com/flannel-io/flannel/releases/latest/download/kube-flannel.yml

# Wait for CNI pods to be Ready
kubectl get pods -n kube-system -w
# NAME                                       READY   STATUS    AGE
# calico-kube-controllers-xxxx               1/1     Running   30s
# calico-node-xxxxx                          1/1     Running   30s
# coredns-7db6d8ff4d-xxxxx                   1/1     Running   60s  ← was Pending!

# NOW the control plane node becomes Ready
kubectl get nodes
# NAME           STATUS   ROLES           AGE    VERSION
# control-plane  Ready    control-plane   5m     v1.30.0
Why CNI is Required — Before vs After
  BEFORE CNI:                         AFTER CNI (Calico):
  ┌──────────────────────┐           ┌──────────────────────┐
  │ Node 1               │           │ Node 1               │
  │ ┌──────┐ ┌──────┐   │           │ ┌──────┐ ┌──────┐   │
  │ │Pod A │ │Pod B │   │           │ │Pod A │ │Pod B │   │
  │ │10.1  │ │10.2  │   │           │ │10.1  │ │10.2  │   │
  │ └──┬───┘ └──┬───┘   │           │ └──┬───┘ └──┬───┘   │
  │    │   ❌    │       │           │    │   ✅    │       │
  │    └────────┘       │           │    └───┬────┘       │
  │                      │           │        │ Calico     │
  │        ❌ No route    │           │        │ creates    │
  │                      │           │        │ routes     │
  │ ┌──────┐             │           │        │            │
  │ │Pod C │ (Node 2)    │           │        ▼            │
  │ │10.3  │ UNREACHABLE │           │  ┌──────┐ (Node 2)  │
  │ └──────┘             │           │  │Pod C │ REACHABLE │
  └──────────────────────┘           │  │10.3  │           │
                                      └──────────────────────┘
💡 CKA Tip: pod-network-cidr Must Match

Flannel requires --pod-network-cidr=10.244.0.0/16. Calico defaults to 192.168.0.0/16 but can be configured. If you use the wrong CIDR in kubeadm init, pods will get IPs but routing won't work. On the exam, use whatever CIDR the question specifies.

9.5 Join Worker Nodes

After the control plane is ready and CNI is installed, join each worker node. Each worker must have completed all prerequisites from Section 9.2.

worker-nodes.sh 
# Run on EACH worker node (use the command from kubeadm init output)
sudo kubeadm join 192.168.56.10:6443 \
  --token abcdef.0123456789abcdef \
  --discovery-token-ca-cert-hash sha256:abc123def456...

# Expected output:
# [preflight] Running pre-flight checks
# [preflight] Reading configuration from the cluster...
# [kubelet-start] Starting the kubelet
# [kubelet-start] Waiting for kubelet to perform TLS Bootstrap...
#
# This node has joined the cluster:
# * Certificate signing request was sent and approved
# * Run 'kubectl get nodes' on the control-plane to see this node

# If token expired (after 24 hours), regenerate on control plane:
sudo kubeadm token create --print-join-command
# kubeadm join 192.168.56.10:6443 --token xyz789... \
#   --discovery-token-ca-cert-hash sha256:newHash...
Complete Cluster Build — Step by Step
  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  KUBEADM CLUSTER CREATION                                       │
  │                                                                 │
  │  Step 1: Prerequisites (ALL nodes)                               │
  │  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
  │  │ swap off │ kernel modules │ sysctl │ containerd │ kubeadm │   │
  │  └──────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────┘   │
  │                              ▼                                   │
  │  Step 2: kubeadm init (CONTROL PLANE only)                      │
  │  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
  │  │ $ sudo kubeadm init --pod-network-cidr=10.244.0.0/16     │   │
  │  │ → Generates certs → Starts API server → Creates configs   │   │
  │  └──────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────┘   │
  │                              ▼                                   │
  │  Step 3: Setup kubeconfig (CONTROL PLANE)                       │
  │  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
  │  │ $ mkdir -p ~/.kube                                        │   │
  │  │ $ sudo cp /etc/kubernetes/admin.conf ~/.kube/config       │   │
  │  └──────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────┘   │
  │                              ▼                                   │
  │  Step 4: Install CNI (CONTROL PLANE)                            │
  │  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
  │  │ $ kubectl apply -f calico.yaml                            │   │
  │  │ → Control plane becomes Ready, CoreDNS pods start         │   │
  │  └──────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────┘   │
  │                              ▼                                   │
  │  Step 5: kubeadm join (EACH WORKER)                             │
  │  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
  │  │ $ sudo kubeadm join <IP>:6443 --token X --discovery...    │   │
  │  │ → Worker registers with API server → becomes Ready        │   │
  │  └──────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────┘   │
  │                              ▼                                   │
  │  Step 6: Verify ✅                                              │
  │  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
  │  │ $ kubectl get nodes                                       │   │
  │  │ control-plane  Ready   control-plane   5m    v1.30.0      │   │
  │  │ worker-1       Ready   <none>          2m    v1.30.0      │   │
  │  │ worker-2       Ready   <none>          1m    v1.30.0      │   │
  │  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
📖 Worker Node ROLES Show <none>

By default, worker nodes show <none> for ROLES. You can label them: kubectl label node worker-1 node-role.kubernetes.io/worker=. This is cosmetic only — it doesn't change any behavior.

9.6 Verifying the Cluster

After all nodes have joined, run these checks to confirm the cluster is fully operational:

verify-cluster.sh 
# 1. All nodes Ready?
kubectl get nodes -o wide
# NAME           STATUS   ROLES           AGE   VERSION   INTERNAL-IP
# control-plane  Ready    control-plane   10m   v1.30.0   192.168.56.10
# worker-1       Ready    <none>          5m    v1.30.0   192.168.56.11
# worker-2       Ready    <none>          3m    v1.30.0   192.168.56.12

# 2. All system pods Running?
kubectl get pods -n kube-system
# NAME                                       READY   STATUS    RESTARTS
# calico-kube-controllers-xxxx               1/1     Running   0
# calico-node-xxxxx (one per node)           1/1     Running   0
# coredns-7db6d8ff4d-xxxx                    1/1     Running   0
# coredns-7db6d8ff4d-yyyy                    1/1     Running   0
# etcd-control-plane                         1/1     Running   0
# kube-apiserver-control-plane               1/1     Running   0
# kube-controller-manager-control-plane      1/1     Running   0
# kube-proxy-xxxxx (one per node)            1/1     Running   0
# kube-scheduler-control-plane               1/1     Running   0

# 3. Cluster info
kubectl cluster-info
# Kubernetes control plane is running at https://192.168.56.10:6443
# CoreDNS is running at https://192.168.56.10:6443/api/v1/.../kube-dns

# 4. Deploy a test pod to confirm everything works
kubectl run test-nginx --image=nginx --port=80
kubectl get pod test-nginx -o wide
# NAME         READY   STATUS    NODE
# test-nginx   1/1     Running   worker-1     ← scheduled to a worker!

# Clean up test
kubectl delete pod test-nginx
✅
Healthy Cluster Checklist

All nodes STATUS=Ready. All kube-system pods Running. CoreDNS pods Running (not Pending). Test pod schedules to a worker node.

❌
Common Issues

Node NotReady = kubelet not running or CNI missing. CoreDNS Pending = CNI not installed. Pod stuck on control plane = no workers joined.

9.7 Practice Exercises

Building clusters from scratch is a muscle memory skill. Practice until you can complete Exercise 9.1 in under 30 minutes.

🏋️
Exercise 9.1: Build a 3-Node Cluster

Create 3 VMs (Vagrant or cloud). Install prerequisites on all. Run kubeadm init, install Calico, join 2 workers. Deploy anihpj and verify. ⏱️ Target: 30 min.

🔑
Exercise 9.2: Token Recovery

Pretend the join token expired. Generate a new one with kubeadm token create --print-join-command. Join a new worker using the fresh token.

🔧
Exercise 9.3: Fix a Broken Node

Intentionally break prerequisites on a worker (enable swap, stop containerd). Troubleshoot and fix it until the node shows Ready.

🎯
Exercise 9.4: Add Control Plane Node

Use kubeadm join --control-plane to add a second control plane. Verify etcd member list shows 2 members. (HA setup)

💡 Vagrant Quick Setup for Practice

Use a Vagrantfile to spin up 3 Ubuntu VMs with one command: vagrant up. Each VM gets a static IP and 2 GB RAM. This is the fastest way to practice kubeadm clusters repeatedly.

🔄 Bonus: kubeadm Cluster Upgrade (CKA Critical Skill)

Upgrading a Kubernetes cluster is a guaranteed CKA topic. The process is sequential: upgrade the control plane first, then each worker node one at a time. Never skip minor versions (e.g., 1.28 → 1.30 is not allowed).

1
Upgrade kubeadm

Update the kubeadm package to the target version on the control plane node.

2
Plan the Upgrade

Run kubeadm upgrade plan to verify compatibility and see available versions.

3
Apply the Upgrade

Run kubeadm upgrade apply v1.XX.Y to upgrade control plane components.

4
Drain & Upgrade kubelet

Drain the node, upgrade kubelet and kubectl packages, restart kubelet.

5
Uncordon & Repeat

Uncordon the node, then repeat steps 1 + 4 on each worker node.

upgrade-control-plane.sh 
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
#  CONTROL PLANE UPGRADE (e.g., 1.29.0 → 1.30.0)
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# Step 1: Update kubeadm to target version
sudo apt-mark unhold kubeadm
sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get install -y kubeadm=1.30.0-1.1
sudo apt-mark hold kubeadm

# Step 2: Verify the upgrade plan
sudo kubeadm upgrade plan
# [upgrade] Found 1 control plane instance...
# Components that will be upgraded:
#   kube-apiserver     v1.29.0 → v1.30.0
#   kube-controller-manager  v1.29.0 → v1.30.0
#   kube-scheduler     v1.29.0 → v1.30.0
#   etcd               3.5.10 → 3.5.12

# Step 3: Apply the upgrade
sudo kubeadm upgrade apply v1.30.0
# [upgrade] Successfully upgraded to v1.30.0!

# Step 4: Drain the control plane node
kubectl drain control-plane --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data

# Step 5: Upgrade kubelet and kubectl
sudo apt-mark unhold kubelet kubectl
sudo apt-get install -y kubelet=1.30.0-1.1 kubectl=1.30.0-1.1
sudo apt-mark hold kubelet kubectl

# Step 6: Restart kubelet
sudo systemctl daemon-reload
sudo systemctl restart kubelet

# Step 7: Uncordon the node
kubectl uncordon control-plane
# node/control-plane uncordoned
upgrade-worker-node.sh 
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
#  WORKER NODE UPGRADE (repeat for each worker)
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# On the CONTROL PLANE — drain the worker first
kubectl drain worker-1 --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data

# SSH to worker-1, then:

# Step 1: Upgrade kubeadm
sudo apt-mark unhold kubeadm
sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get install -y kubeadm=1.30.0-1.1
sudo apt-mark hold kubeadm

# Step 2: Upgrade node config (NOT "apply" — use "node")
sudo kubeadm upgrade node

# Step 3: Upgrade kubelet and kubectl
sudo apt-mark unhold kubelet kubectl
sudo apt-get install -y kubelet=1.30.0-1.1 kubectl=1.30.0-1.1
sudo apt-mark hold kubelet kubectl

# Step 4: Restart kubelet
sudo systemctl daemon-reload
sudo systemctl restart kubelet

# Back on CONTROL PLANE — uncordon the worker
kubectl uncordon worker-1
# node/worker-1 uncordoned

# Verify all nodes are at the new version
kubectl get nodes
# NAME            STATUS   ROLES           AGE   VERSION
# control-plane   Ready    control-plane   90d   v1.30.0
# worker-1        Ready    <none>          90d   v1.30.0
# worker-2        Ready    <none>          90d   v1.29.0  ← still needs upgrade
🚨 CKA Upgrade Rules — Memorize These

1) Always upgrade control plane FIRST, then workers. 2) Never skip minor versions (1.28 → 1.29 → 1.30, not 1.28 → 1.30). 3) Control plane uses kubeadm upgrade apply, workers use kubeadm upgrade node. 4) Always drain before upgrading kubelet, uncordon after.

🔧 Bonus: Troubleshooting Common kubeadm Issues

When things go wrong during cluster setup, these are the errors you'll encounter most. Knowing the fix saves crucial exam time.

Symptom Likely Cause Fix
node NotReady CNI not installed or container runtime stopped Install CNI plugin; systemctl start containerd
kubeadm init hangs Swap enabled or port 6443 already in use swapoff -a; check ss -tlnp | grep 6443
connection refused :6443 API server not running (bad manifest edit) Check /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml; restore backup
kubelet not starting cgroupDriver mismatch or containerd down Match cgroupDriver: systemd in kubelet config AND containerd config
token expired Join tokens expire after 24 hours kubeadm token create --print-join-command
etcd unhealthy etcd cert expired or data corruption Check certs: openssl x509 -in ca.crt -noout -dates; restore from backup
Pods stuck Pending No worker nodes or all nodes tainted Join workers; or remove taint: kubectl taint nodes --all node-role.kubernetes.io/control-plane-
coredns CrashLoopBackOff CNI not installed or DNS loop Install CNI first; check /etc/resolv.conf for loops
kubeadm Troubleshooting Decision Tree
  kubectl get nodes → Node "NotReady"?
       │
       ├── YES ─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
       │   ├── Is CNI installed? ──── NO ──▶ Install CNI plugin │
       │   ├── Is containerd running? NO ──▶ systemctl start     │
       │   └── Is kubelet running? ── NO ──▶ journalctl -u kubelet
       │                                                         │
       ├── kubectl can't connect? ──────────────────────────────┐
       │   ├── Is kubeconfig set? ─── NO ──▶ export KUBECONFIG=  │
       │   ├── API server running? ── NO ──▶ Check static pod    │
       │   └── Firewall blocking? ── YES ──▶ ufw allow 6443     │
       │                                                         │
       └── Pods stuck "Pending"? ───────────────────────────────┐
           ├── Nodes available? ───── NO ──▶ Join worker nodes   │
           ├── Resources enough? ─── NO ──▶ Check requests/limits│
           └── Taints blocking? ──── YES ──▶ Remove/add tolerations
💡 CKA Debugging Lifeline: 3 Commands

When anything fails during cluster setup, these 3 commands reveal 90% of issues:
kubectl get nodes → Are nodes Ready?
kubectl get pods -n kube-system → Are system pods running?
sudo journalctl -u kubelet -f → What is kubelet complaining about?

⚠️ CKA Gotcha: Cluster Installation Traps

  • kubeadm init requires root — All kubeadm commands need sudo. Forgetting it gives cryptic permission errors
  • Swap must be OFF — kubelet refuses to start if swap is enabled: sudo swapoff -a. Also disable in /etc/fstab for persistence
  • CNI must be installed AFTER init — Nodes stay NotReady until you install a CNI plugin (Calico, Flannel, Weave). The CKA gives you which CNI to use
  • Join token expires in 24h — If worker join fails with "token invalid", regenerate: kubeadm token create --print-join-command
  • Port conflicts — API server needs 6443, etcd needs 2379-2380, kubelet needs 10250. Firewalls or other services on these ports = cluster won't start

CKA Speed Tip: When troubleshooting a failed cluster install, check in this order:
1. systemctl status kubelet — is kubelet running?
2. journalctl -u kubelet --no-pager | tail -20 — what's the error?
3. crictl ps — are control plane containers running?
4. ls /etc/kubernetes/manifests/ — are static pod manifests present?

⚡ Cluster installation commands: kubeadm init --pod-network-cidr=10.244.0.0/16 — initialize control plane
kubeadm token create --print-join-command — regenerate join command
kubeadm reset — tear down and start over (nuclear option)
kubectl get nodes -o wide — verify all nodes are Ready

📝 Chapter 9 — CKA Practice Questions

Q1
You run kubeadm init and the initialization fails with: "[ERROR Swap]: running with swap on is not supported." What do you do?
Show Answer
Answer
# Disable swap immediately
sudo swapoff -a

# Disable swap permanently (survives reboot)
sudo sed -i '/ swap / s/^/#/' /etc/fstab

# Verify swap is off
free -h
# Swap line should show zeros: 0B  0B  0B

# Now retry kubeadm init
sudo kubeadm init --pod-network-cidr=10.244.0.0/16
Explanation

Kubernetes requires swap to be disabled because the scheduler's resource calculations assume physical memory only. If pods swap to disk, performance becomes unpredictable and memory limits lose meaning. swapoff -a disables it immediately, but editing /etc/fstab makes it permanent across reboots. This is a common CKA pre-requisite step. If you see any error during kubeadm init, always check the [preflight] section — it lists exactly what failed (swap, ports, container runtime, etc.).

Q2
After running kubeadm init, the output prints three important commands. What are they and why is each critical?
Show Answer
Answer
# Command 1: Set up kubeconfig for the current user
mkdir -p $HOME/.kube
sudo cp -i /etc/kubernetes/admin.conf $HOME/.kube/config
sudo chown $(id -u):$(id -g) $HOME/.kube/config

# Command 2: Install a CNI plugin (e.g., Calico)
kubectl apply -f https://docs.projectcalico.org/manifests/calico.yaml

# Command 3: Join worker nodes (unique per init)
kubeadm join 192.168.1.10:6443 --token abcdef.0123456789abcdef \
    --discovery-token-ca-cert-hash sha256:abc123...
Step Why Critical
kubeconfig setup Without it, kubectl can't authenticate to the API server
CNI plugin Nodes stay NotReady until pod networking is installed
Join command Workers need the token + CA hash to securely join the cluster
Explanation

These three steps complete the cluster setup. The kubeconfig file at ~/.kube/config contains the cluster endpoint, client certificate, and CA certificate — this is how kubectl authenticates. The CNI (Container Network Interface) plugin provides pod-to-pod networking — without it, pods can't communicate and nodes report NotReady. The join token is valid for 24 hours by default. If it expires: kubeadm token create --print-join-command generates a new one.

Q3
All worker nodes show NotReady status. The control plane node is also NotReady. What is the most likely cause?
Show Answer
Answer
# Most likely cause: No CNI plugin installed

# Verify: check node conditions
kubectl describe node worker-1 | grep -A5 Conditions
# Look for: NetworkReady=false, "network plugin is not ready"

# Fix: Install a CNI plugin
kubectl apply -f https://docs.projectcalico.org/manifests/calico.yaml

# Or for Flannel:
kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/flannel-io/flannel/master/Documentation/kube-flannel.yml

# Verify nodes become Ready (may take 30-60 seconds)
kubectl get nodes -w

# Other causes of NotReady:
# - kubelet not running: systemctl status kubelet
# - Container runtime down: systemctl status containerd
# - Node resource pressure: kubectl describe node | grep Conditions
Explanation

If all nodes are NotReady, the cause is almost always a missing CNI plugin. The kubelet reports Ready only when the container runtime AND network plugin are both operational. A single node being NotReady suggests node-specific issues (kubelet crash, disk pressure, memory pressure). On the CKA, always check if CNI is deployed: kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep -i calico\|flannel\|weave. No CNI pods = no networking = all NotReady.

Q4
A worker node failed to join the cluster. The kubeadm join command returns: "token has expired". How do you generate a new join command from the control plane node?
Show Answer
Answer
# On the control plane node:

# Method 1: Create new token and print full join command
kubeadm token create --print-join-command
# Output: kubeadm join 10.0.0.10:6443 --token abc123.xyz789 \
#   --discovery-token-ca-cert-hash sha256:...

# Method 2: List existing tokens
kubeadm token list
# TOKEN                     TTL         EXPIRES
# abcdef.0123456789abcdef   23h         2024-01-02T10:00:00Z

# Method 3: Create token with custom TTL
kubeadm token create --ttl 2h --print-join-command

# Then on the worker node, run the printed join command:
sudo kubeadm join 10.0.0.10:6443 --token <new-token> \
  --discovery-token-ca-cert-hash sha256:<hash>
Explanation

Bootstrap tokens expire after 24 hours by default. The --print-join-command flag is essential — it outputs the complete command including the token AND the CA cert hash. Without the hash, the worker can't verify it's connecting to the legitimate API server (preventing MITM attacks). On the CKA, if a question says "add a worker node," start with kubeadm token create --print-join-command on the control plane, then run the output on the worker.

Q5
What is the --pod-network-cidr flag in kubeadm init, and what happens if you choose a CIDR that overlaps with your node network?
Show Answer
Answer
# The pod-network-cidr defines the IP range for ALL pods in the cluster
kubeadm init --pod-network-cidr=10.244.0.0/16  # Flannel default
kubeadm init --pod-network-cidr=192.168.0.0/16 # Calico default

# Common CIDR allocations:
# Node network:    10.0.0.0/24  (physical/VM IPs)
# Pod network:     10.244.0.0/16 (pod IPs)
# Service network: 10.96.0.0/12  (ClusterIP range, default)

# ❌ OVERLAP PROBLEM:
# Node network: 10.244.1.0/24
# Pod CIDR:     10.244.0.0/16   ← OVERLAPS!
# Result: routing breaks — traffic to pods goes to nodes instead
Explanation

The --pod-network-cidr tells the CNI plugin which IP range to assign to pods. Each node gets a subnet from this range (e.g., node1 gets 10.244.0.0/24, node2 gets 10.244.1.0/24). If the pod CIDR overlaps with your node IPs, the kernel can't distinguish between pod and node traffic — routing tables conflict, and networking breaks silently. On the CKA, the CIDR is usually provided in the question. Use exactly what they specify. Most CNI plugins also require the CIDR to match their default (Flannel = 10.244.0.0/16, Calico = 192.168.0.0/16).

Q6
The kubelet on a worker node is not starting. systemctl status kubelet shows it's "activating (auto-restart)". How do you diagnose this?
Show Answer
Answer
# Step 1: Check kubelet logs
sudo journalctl -u kubelet --no-pager -n 50
# or follow in real-time:
sudo journalctl -u kubelet -f

# Step 2: Common errors and fixes:

# Error: "failed to load kubelet config file"
# Fix: Check /var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml exists and is valid
cat /var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml

# Error: "container runtime is not running"
# Fix: Start containerd
sudo systemctl start containerd
sudo systemctl enable containerd

# Error: "unable to load client CA file"
# Fix: Node hasn't joined yet or certs are corrupted
# Re-join: kubeadm reset && kubeadm join ...

# Step 3: Verify container runtime
crictl info
sudo systemctl status containerd

# Step 4: Check kubelet is enabled at boot
sudo systemctl enable kubelet
Explanation

kubelet crash-looping means it starts, encounters an error, exits, and systemd restarts it. journalctl -u kubelet is your primary tool — the error message tells you exactly what's wrong. The three most common causes: (1) container runtime not running (containerd/CRI-O), (2) kubelet config file missing or corrupt, (3) node hasn't been joined to a cluster yet. On the CKA, always check in this order: systemctl status kubelet → journalctl -u kubelet → systemctl status containerd. This pattern solves 90% of node failures.

Q7
What are the kernel modules and system settings required before installing Kubernetes with kubeadm? List the prerequisites.
Show Answer
Answer
# 1. Load required kernel modules
cat <<EOF | sudo tee /etc/modules-load.d/k8s.conf
overlay
br_netfilter
EOF
sudo modprobe overlay
sudo modprobe br_netfilter

# 2. Set required sysctl parameters
cat <<EOF | sudo tee /etc/sysctl.d/k8s.conf
net.bridge.bridge-nf-call-iptables  = 1
net.bridge.bridge-nf-call-ip6tables = 1
net.ipv4.ip_forward                 = 1
EOF
sudo sysctl --system

# 3. Disable swap
sudo swapoff -a
sudo sed -i '/ swap / s/^/#/' /etc/fstab

# 4. Install container runtime (containerd)
sudo apt-get install -y containerd
sudo mkdir -p /etc/containerd
containerd config default | sudo tee /etc/containerd/config.toml
# Enable SystemdCgroup = true in config.toml
sudo systemctl restart containerd

# 5. Install kubeadm, kubelet, kubectl
sudo apt-get install -y kubeadm kubelet kubectl
sudo apt-mark hold kubeadm kubelet kubectl
Explanation

These prerequisites ensure the kernel can handle container networking. br_netfilter allows iptables to see bridged traffic (pod networking). overlay enables the overlay filesystem for container images. ip_forward=1 allows packet forwarding between interfaces. apt-mark hold prevents accidental upgrades of kubeadm/kubelet/kubectl during apt upgrade — version skew between components breaks clusters. On the CKA, these are usually pre-configured, but understanding them helps troubleshoot: if pod networking breaks, check that br_netfilter is loaded and bridge-nf-call-iptables = 1.

Q8
How do you completely tear down a Kubernetes cluster installed with kubeadm and start fresh?
Show Answer
Answer
# On EACH node (workers first, then control plane):

# Step 1: Reset kubeadm (removes certs, configs, etcd data)
sudo kubeadm reset -f

# Step 2: Clean up networking
sudo rm -rf /etc/cni/net.d
sudo iptables -F && sudo iptables -t nat -F && sudo iptables -t mangle -F
sudo iptables -X

# Step 3: Remove kubeconfig
rm -rf $HOME/.kube

# Step 4: (Optional) Stop kubelet and containerd
sudo systemctl stop kubelet
sudo systemctl stop containerd

# Step 5: Clean up leftover data
sudo rm -rf /var/lib/etcd
sudo rm -rf /var/lib/kubelet
sudo rm -rf /etc/kubernetes

# Then re-initialize:
sudo kubeadm init --pod-network-cidr=10.244.0.0/16
Explanation

kubeadm reset undoes what kubeadm init or kubeadm join did — it removes TLS certificates, the local etcd data member, the kubelet config, and the admin kubeconfig. However, it does NOT clean up CNI config or iptables rules — those linger and can cause issues on re-init. On the CKA, you typically won't need to reset, but knowing this helps if you severely break something. Always reset workers BEFORE the control plane to avoid orphan node objects.

Q9
What ports must be open on control plane nodes vs worker nodes for a Kubernetes cluster to function? List the components and their ports.
Show Answer
Answer
Component Port Where
API Server 6443 Control Plane
etcd 2379-2380 Control Plane
Scheduler 10259 Control Plane
Controller Manager 10257 Control Plane
Kubelet 10250 All Nodes
NodePort Services 30000-32767 All Nodes
Explanation

Port conflicts are a common cause of cluster initialization failures. If a firewall blocks port 6443, workers can't reach the API server. If port 2379 is in use, etcd won't start. If 10250 is blocked, the API server can't reach kubelet (which means kubectl exec and kubectl logs fail). On the CKA exam, firewalls are usually pre-configured, but understanding ports helps with troubleshooting: ss -tlnp | grep 6443 checks if the API server is listening, nc -zv <control-plane-ip> 6443 tests connectivity from a worker.

Q10
You need to set up a cluster with a specific Kubernetes version (v1.29.0). How do you specify the version during installation and ensure all components match?
Show Answer
Answer
# Step 1: Install specific versions of kubeadm, kubelet, kubectl
sudo apt-get install -y kubeadm=1.29.0-1.1 kubelet=1.29.0-1.1 kubectl=1.29.0-1.1
sudo apt-mark hold kubeadm kubelet kubectl

# Step 2: Initialize with version flag
sudo kubeadm init --kubernetes-version=v1.29.0 \
  --pod-network-cidr=10.244.0.0/16

# Step 3: Verify versions match
kubectl version --short
# Client Version: v1.29.0
# Server Version: v1.29.0

kubectl get nodes
# NAME           STATUS   VERSION
# control-plane  Ready    v1.29.0
# worker-1       Ready    v1.29.0

# Check component versions
kubectl -n kube-system get pods -o custom-columns=\
'NAME:.metadata.name,IMAGE:.spec.containers[0].image' | grep kube
# All images should show v1.29.0
Explanation

Version consistency is critical — Kubernetes supports a maximum skew of one minor version between components. kubelet can be one minor version behind the API server, but never ahead. The apt-mark hold command prevents apt upgrade from changing versions. On the CKA, the exam environment usually has specific versions pre-installed, but upgrade questions require you to install exact versions. Always verify with kubectl version and kubeadm version after installation.

Chapter 10: Cluster Configuration and kubeconfig

The kubeconfig file is your key to the cluster — without it, kubectl can't talk to the API server. This chapter dives deep into kubeconfig structure, managing multiple clusters, API server and kubelet configuration, admission controllers, and API groups. These are heavily tested on the CKA.

🟡 Intermediate ⏱️ ~1.5 hours ⚙️ Config 📝 CKA EXAM

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ Understand kubeconfig file structure — clusters, users, contexts
  • ✅ Manage multiple clusters and switch contexts
  • ✅ Configure the API server with custom flags
  • ✅ Understand kubelet configuration and static pods
  • ✅ Explain admission controllers and their role in the API pipeline
  • ✅ Navigate API groups and resource paths
🎓 CKA Relevance
Cluster Architecture, Installation & Configuration 25%
kubeconfig = Top CKA Skill

On the CKA exam, you work with multiple clusters. Switching between them quickly with kubectl config use-context is essential — fumbling with kubeconfig costs precious exam minutes.

  • Know the 3 parts — clusters, users, contexts — and how they link together
  • Know how to switch contexts fast — kubectl config use-context
  • Know where kubeconfig lives — $HOME/.kube/config or $KUBECONFIG

10.1 kubeconfig Deep Dive

The kubeconfig file tells kubectl three things: where to connect (cluster), who to authenticate as (user), and which combination to use (context).

kubeconfig File Structure — The Three Pillars
  ┌─────────────────────── kubeconfig (~/.kube/config) ───────────────────────┐
  │                                                                           │
  │  ┌── clusters ──────────┐  ┌── users ──────────────┐  ┌── contexts ────┐ │
  │  │                      │  │                        │  │                │ │
  │  │  - cluster: prod     │  │  - user: prod-admin    │  │  - context:    │ │
  │  │    server: https://  │  │    client-certificate  │  │    name: prod  │ │
  │  │      prod:6443       │  │    client-key          │  │    cluster:    │ │
  │  │    certificate-      │  │                        │  │      prod     │ │
  │  │      authority       │  │  - user: dev-admin     │  │    user:      │ │
  │  │                      │  │    token: eyJhb...     │  │      prod-    │ │
  │  │  - cluster: dev      │  │                        │  │      admin    │ │
  │  │    server: https://  │  │  - user: staging-      │  │    namespace: │ │
  │  │      dev:6443        │  │      readonly          │  │      default  │ │
  │  │    certificate-      │  │    token: abc123...    │  │               │ │
  │  │      authority       │  │                        │  │  - context:   │ │
  │  │                      │  │                        │  │    name: dev  │ │
  │  │  - cluster: staging  │  │                        │  │    cluster:   │ │
  │  │    server: https://  │  │                        │  │      dev     │ │
  │  │      staging:6443    │  │                        │  │    user:     │ │
  │  │                      │  │                        │  │      dev-    │ │
  │  └──────────┬───────────┘  └────────────┬───────────┘  │      admin  │ │
  │             │                           │              └──────┬─────┘ │
  │             └───────────┐  ┌────────────┘                     │       │
  │                         ▼  ▼                                  │       │
  │  current-context: prod  ◀─────────────────────────────────────┘       │
  │                                                                       │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
~/.kube/config 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Config

# The active context (used when you run kubectl)
current-context: production

# WHERE to connect
clusters:
- cluster:
    certificate-authority-data: LS0tLS1...   # Base64 CA cert
    server: https://192.168.56.10:6443
  name: production

- cluster:
    certificate-authority-data: LS0tLS1...
    server: https://10.0.0.5:6443
  name: development

# WHO to authenticate as
users:
- name: prod-admin
  user:
    client-certificate-data: LS0tLS1...   # Base64 client cert
    client-key-data: LS0tLS1...           # Base64 client key

- name: dev-admin
  user:
    token: eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIs...        # Bearer token

# WHICH cluster + user combination to use
contexts:
- context:
    cluster: production
    user: prod-admin
    namespace: default
  name: production

- context:
    cluster: development
    user: dev-admin
    namespace: dev-apps
  name: development

kubeconfig — Field-by-Field Breakdown

📍 clusters[].server
API server URL. Format: https://<ip>:6443. Port 6443 is the default.
🔐 clusters[].certificate-authority-data
Base64-encoded CA certificate. Kubectl uses this to verify the API server's TLS certificate.
👤 users[].client-certificate-data
Base64-encoded client cert. The API server verifies this to authenticate the user.
🔗 contexts[].cluster + user + namespace
A context links a cluster and user together, plus an optional default namespace.
⭐ current-context
Which context kubectl uses right now. Changed via kubectl config use-context <name>

10.2 Managing Multiple Clusters & Contexts

On the CKA exam, you'll work with multiple clusters — each question tells you which context to use. Switching contexts fast is critical.

context-commands.sh 
# View all contexts
kubectl config get-contexts
# CURRENT   NAME          CLUSTER       AUTHINFO      NAMESPACE
# *         production    production    prod-admin    default
#           development   development   dev-admin     dev-apps
#           staging       staging       stg-viewer    staging

# Switch to a different context
kubectl config use-context development
# Switched to context "development".

# View current context
kubectl config current-context
# development

# Set default namespace for current context
kubectl config set-context --current --namespace=kube-system
# Context "development" modified.

# View the full kubeconfig
kubectl config view
# (displays the merged kubeconfig YAML)

# View just one cluster's details
kubectl config view --minify
# (shows only the active context's config)
🆕
Create a New Context
create-context.sh 
# Add a new cluster
kubectl config set-cluster prod-cluster \
  --server=https://10.0.1.5:6443 \
  --certificate-authority=/path/to/ca.crt

# Add a new user
kubectl config set-credentials jane \
  --client-certificate=/path/to/jane.crt \
  --client-key=/path/to/jane.key

# Create a context linking them
kubectl config set-context jane@prod \
  --cluster=prod-cluster \
  --user=jane \
  --namespace=web-apps
📁
KUBECONFIG Env Variable
kubeconfig-env.sh 
# Use a specific kubeconfig file
export KUBECONFIG=/path/to/custom-config

# Merge multiple kubeconfig files
export KUBECONFIG=~/.kube/config:\
  ~/.kube/prod-config:\
  ~/.kube/dev-config

# View merged result
kubectl config view --flatten

# One-time override (no env change)
kubectl --kubeconfig=/path/to/config \
  get nodes
💡 CKA Shortcut: First Command on Every Question

Every CKA exam question starts with a context switch instruction. Make this muscle memory:
kubectl config use-context k8s-cluster-1
If you forget this step and work on the wrong cluster, you'll lose points for that question.

10.3 API Server Configuration

The kube-apiserver is the front door to the entire cluster. Understanding its flags helps you troubleshoot and is tested on the CKA.

API Server — Static Pod Configuration
  /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml
  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  apiVersion: v1                                                │
  │  kind: Pod                                                     │
  │  metadata:                                                     │
  │    name: kube-apiserver                                        │
  │    namespace: kube-system                                      │
  │  spec:                                                         │
  │    containers:                                                 │
  │    - command:                                                  │
  │      - kube-apiserver                                          │
  │      - --advertise-address=192.168.56.10                       │
  │      - --etcd-servers=https://127.0.0.1:2379                   │
  │      - --service-cluster-ip-range=10.96.0.0/12                │
  │      - --client-ca-file=/etc/kubernetes/pki/ca.crt             │
  │      - --tls-cert-file=/etc/kubernetes/pki/apiserver.crt       │
  │      - --tls-private-key-file=/etc/kubernetes/pki/apiserver.key│
  │      - --enable-admission-plugins=NodeRestriction              │
  │      - --authorization-mode=Node,RBAC                          │
  │      - --enable-bootstrap-token-auth=true                      │
  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                          │
                  kubelet watches this file
                  and auto-restarts the pod
                  when it changes

Key API Server Flags — CKA Must-Know

🏠 --etcd-servers
Where the API server stores all cluster data. Default: https://127.0.0.1:2379
🌐 --service-cluster-ip-range
CIDR for ClusterIP Services. Default: 10.96.0.0/12. Must not overlap pod CIDR.
🔒 --authorization-mode
Node,RBAC is the default. Order matters — first match wins. Always include RBAC.
🛡️ --enable-admission-plugins
Comma-separated list of admission controllers. NodeRestriction is critical for security.
🔑 --client-ca-file
CA certificate for verifying client certificates (mutual TLS). Located in /etc/kubernetes/pki/
⚠️ Static Pod = Auto-Restart on Change

The API server runs as a static pod. If you edit /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml, kubelet automatically restarts it. But a bad edit will crash the API server — always backup the manifest first: cp kube-apiserver.yaml kube-apiserver.yaml.bak

10.4 kubelet Configuration

The kubelet runs on every node. It's the agent that talks to the API server, manages pods, reports node status, and runs static pods. Unlike other control plane components, kubelet is NOT a pod — it's a systemd service.

kubelet — Configuration File Locations
  ┌────────────────── kubelet Configuration Paths ──────────────────┐
  │                                                                 │
  │  /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10-kubeadm.conf          │
  │  └── Environment flags passed to kubelet binary                 │
  │      └── --config=/var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml                  │
  │                                                                 │
  │  /var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml        ← Main config file         │
  │  ├── clusterDNS: ["10.96.0.10"]                                │
  │  ├── clusterDomain: cluster.local                               │
  │  ├── staticPodPath: /etc/kubernetes/manifests   ← Static pods!  │
  │  ├── authentication:                                            │
  │  │   └── x509: clientCAFile: /etc/kubernetes/pki/ca.crt         │
  │  ├── authorization:                                             │
  │  │   └── mode: Webhook                                          │
  │  └── cgroupDriver: systemd          ← Must match containerd     │
  │                                                                 │
  │  /etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf        ← kubelet's kubeconfig     │
  │  └── server, client-certificate, client-key                     │
  │                                                                 │
  │  /etc/kubernetes/manifests/          ← Static pod manifests     │
  │  ├── etcd.yaml                                                  │
  │  ├── kube-apiserver.yaml                                        │
  │  ├── kube-controller-manager.yaml                               │
  │  └── kube-scheduler.yaml                                        │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
kubelet-commands.sh 
# Check kubelet status
sudo systemctl status kubelet
# ● kubelet.service - kubelet: The Kubernetes Node Agent
#    Active: active (running) since...

# View kubelet logs (troubleshooting)
sudo journalctl -u kubelet -f
# (live log stream — look for errors here)

# Restart kubelet (after config changes)
sudo systemctl restart kubelet

# View kubelet config
cat /var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml

# Static pods: drop a manifest in /etc/kubernetes/manifests/
# kubelet will auto-create the pod (no API server needed!)
cat <<EOF | sudo tee /etc/kubernetes/manifests/static-nginx.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: static-nginx
  namespace: default
spec:
  containers:
  - name: nginx
    image: nginx:1.25
    ports:
    - containerPort: 80
EOF

# Verify static pod exists
kubectl get pods | grep static-nginx
# static-nginx-control-plane   1/1   Running   0   10s
📖 Static Pods vs Regular Pods

Static pods are managed directly by kubelet, not the API server. They are created from YAML files in /etc/kubernetes/manifests/. The API server sees a mirror copy but cannot control them. All control plane components (etcd, apiserver, scheduler, controller-manager) run as static pods.

10.5 Admission Controllers

Admission controllers sit in the API request pipeline after authentication and authorization but before the object is persisted. They can validate, mutate, or reject requests.

API Request Pipeline — Where Admission Controllers Sit
  kubectl apply -f pod.yaml
       │
       ▼
  ┌─────────────────┐   ┌──────────────────┐   ┌──────────────────────────┐
  │ 1. AUTHENTICATION│──▶│ 2. AUTHORIZATION  │──▶│ 3. ADMISSION CONTROLLERS │
  │                  │   │                   │   │                          │
  │ WHO are you?     │   │ CAN you do this?  │   │ SHOULD we allow this?    │
  │ • x509 certs     │   │ • RBAC            │   │                          │
  │ • Bearer tokens  │   │ • Node            │   │ ┌────────────────────┐  │
  │ • ServiceAccount │   │ • Webhook         │   │ │ Mutating Webhooks  │  │
  │                  │   │                   │   │ │ (modify request)   │  │
  └─────────────────┘   └──────────────────┘   │ └─────────┬──────────┘  │
                                                 │           ▼            │
                                                 │ ┌────────────────────┐│
                                                 │ │ Validating Webhooks ││
                                                 │ │ (accept or reject) ││
                                                 │ └─────────┬──────────┘│
                                                 └───────────┼───────────┘
                                                              ▼
                                                 ┌──────────────────────┐
                                                 │ 4. PERSIST TO etcd   │
                                                 │ (object is stored)   │
                                                 └──────────────────────┘
Admission Controller Type What It Does CKA?
NamespaceLifecycle Validating Prevents creating objects in non-existent or terminating namespaces ✅
LimitRanger Mutating Enforces LimitRange defaults — auto-sets resource requests/limits ✅
ResourceQuota Validating Rejects requests that exceed namespace ResourceQuota ✅
NodeRestriction Validating Limits kubelet to only modify its own node and pods ✅
DefaultStorageClass Mutating Adds default StorageClass to PVCs that don't specify one 🔹
ServiceAccount Mutating Injects the default ServiceAccount into pods that don't specify one ✅
admission-controller-commands.sh 
# View enabled admission plugins
kubectl -n kube-system describe pod kube-apiserver-control-plane \
  | grep enable-admission
#   --enable-admission-plugins=NodeRestriction

# To enable additional plugins, edit the API server manifest:
sudo vi /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml
# Change:
#   --enable-admission-plugins=NodeRestriction
# To:
#   --enable-admission-plugins=NodeRestriction,NamespaceLifecycle,LimitRanger

# kubelet will auto-restart the API server
# Verify the API server comes back up
kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep apiserver
💡 CKA Tip: Enabling Admission Controllers

If a CKA question asks you to enable an admission controller, you edit /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml, add it to the --enable-admission-plugins flag, save the file, and wait for the API server pod to restart automatically.

10.6 API Groups and Resource Paths

Every Kubernetes resource belongs to an API group. Understanding this helps you write RBAC rules, use kubectl api-resources, and debug API calls.

Kubernetes API Group Hierarchy
  /api/v1  (Core / Legacy group — no group name in apiVersion)
  ├── pods                  apiVersion: v1
  ├── services              apiVersion: v1
  ├── configmaps            apiVersion: v1
  ├── secrets               apiVersion: v1
  ├── namespaces            apiVersion: v1
  ├── nodes                 apiVersion: v1
  ├── persistentvolumes     apiVersion: v1
  └── serviceaccounts       apiVersion: v1

  /apis/apps/v1  (Named group: "apps")
  ├── deployments           apiVersion: apps/v1
  ├── statefulsets           apiVersion: apps/v1
  ├── daemonsets             apiVersion: apps/v1
  └── replicasets            apiVersion: apps/v1

  /apis/networking.k8s.io/v1
  ├── ingresses             apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
  └── networkpolicies       apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1

  /apis/rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
  ├── roles                 apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
  ├── rolebindings          apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
  ├── clusterroles          apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
  └── clusterrolebindings   apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1

  /apis/batch/v1
  ├── jobs                  apiVersion: batch/v1
  └── cronjobs              apiVersion: batch/v1

  /apis/storage.k8s.io/v1
  ├── storageclasses        apiVersion: storage.k8s.io/v1
  └── persistentvolumeclaims apiVersion: v1  (core group!)
api-groups-commands.sh 
# List ALL available API resources
kubectl api-resources
# NAME                  SHORTNAMES   APIVERSION          NAMESPACED   KIND
# pods                  po           v1                  true         Pod
# services              svc          v1                  true         Service
# deployments           deploy       apps/v1             true         Deployment
# nodes                 no           v1                  false        Node

# Filter by API group
kubectl api-resources --api-group=apps
# NAME           SHORTNAMES   APIVERSION   NAMESPACED   KIND
# deployments    deploy       apps/v1      true         Deployment
# statefulsets   sts          apps/v1      true         StatefulSet

# List API versions
kubectl api-versions
# apps/v1
# batch/v1
# networking.k8s.io/v1
# rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
# v1  ← core group

# Explain a resource (shows API group and version)
kubectl explain deployment
# KIND:     Deployment
# VERSION:  apps/v1
# DESCRIPTION: ...
📖 Core Group vs Named Groups

Core group resources (Pod, Service, ConfigMap, Secret) use apiVersion: v1 — no group prefix. Named group resources use apiVersion: groupName/v1 — e.g., apps/v1 for Deployments. When writing RBAC rules, core group resources use apiGroups: [""] (empty string).

🔌 Bonus: Control Plane Component Communication Map

Understanding how control plane components talk to each other is essential for debugging and CKA troubleshooting. Every arrow below represents a real network connection.

Control Plane Architecture — Who Talks to Whom
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                        CONTROL PLANE NODE                               │
  │                                                                          │
  │   ┌──────────────────┐        ┌──────────────────────────┐              │
  │   │  kube-scheduler  │───────▶│      kube-apiserver       │              │
  │   │                  │  HTTPS │  (Central communication   │              │
  │   │ "Where should    │  :6443 │   hub — ALL components    │              │
  │   │  this pod run?"  │        │   talk through me)        │              │
  │   └──────────────────┘        │                           │              │
  │                                │  Ports:                  │              │
  │   ┌──────────────────┐        │   6443 — HTTPS API       │              │
  │   │ kube-controller- │───────▶│                           │              │
  │   │ manager          │  HTTPS │                           │              │
  │   │                  │  :6443 │                           │              │
  │   │ "Are actual and  │        └─────────┬────────────────┘              │
  │   │  desired state   │                  │                                │
  │   │  matching?"      │                  │ HTTPS :2379                    │
  │   └──────────────────┘                  ▼                                │
  │                                ┌──────────────────┐                      │
  │                                │      etcd         │                     │
  │                                │  (All state stored│                     │
  │                                │   here — ONLY the │                     │
  │                                │   API server talks │                    │
  │                                │   to etcd!)        │                    │
  │                                │                   │                     │
  │                                │  Ports:           │                     │
  │                                │   2379 — client   │                     │
  │                                │   2380 — peer     │                     │
  │                                └──────────────────┘                      │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                                        │
                          ┌─────────────┼─────────────┐
                          ▼             ▼             ▼
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                         WORKER NODES                                     │
  │                                                                          │
  │   ┌──────────────────┐    ┌──────────────────┐   ┌────────────────┐     │
  │   │     kubelet       │    │   kube-proxy     │   │   CNI Plugin   │     │
  │   │                  │    │                  │   │  (e.g. Calico) │     │
  │   │ Talks to API     │    │ Watches API      │   │                │     │
  │   │ server to:       │    │ server for       │   │ Manages pod    │     │
  │   │ • Report status  │    │ Service/Endpoint │   │ networking and │     │
  │   │ • Get pod specs  │    │ changes, updates │   │ network policy │     │
  │   │ • Send events    │    │ iptables/IPVS    │   │ enforcement    │     │
  │   └──┬───────────────┘    └──────────────────┘   └────────────────┘     │
  │      │                                                                   │
  │      ▼                                                                   │
  │   ┌──────────────────┐                                                   │
  │   │   containerd     │  kubelet → containerd via CRI (Container          │
  │   │   (container     │  Runtime Interface) to create/stop containers     │
  │   │    runtime)      │                                                   │
  │   └──────────────────┘                                                   │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  KEY RULE: Only the API server talks to etcd. Everything else talks to the API server.

📋 Bonus: KUBECONFIG Merge Priority Order

When multiple kubeconfig sources exist, kubectl merges them in a specific precedence order. Knowing this prevents "wrong cluster" mistakes.

kubeconfig Resolution — Priority Order (Highest → Lowest)
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  PRIORITY 1 (Highest)                                           │
  │  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
  │  │  --kubeconfig flag                                       │   │
  │  │  kubectl --kubeconfig=/path/to/config get pods           │   │
  │  │  → Uses ONLY this file, ignores everything else          │   │
  │  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
  │                          │ if not set                           │
  │                          ▼                                      │
  │  PRIORITY 2                                                     │
  │  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
  │  │  $KUBECONFIG environment variable                        │   │
  │  │  export KUBECONFIG=/a/config:/b/config:/c/config         │   │
  │  │  → Merges ALL files (colon-separated on Linux)           │   │
  │  │  → First context/cluster/user wins on conflict           │   │
  │  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
  │                          │ if not set                           │
  │                          ▼                                      │
  │  PRIORITY 3 (Default)                                           │
  │  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
  │  │  $HOME/.kube/config                                      │   │
  │  │  → The default kubeconfig file                           │   │
  │  │  → Created by kubeadm init (admin.conf copied here)      │   │
  │  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

⚡ Bonus: kubectl config — CKA Speed Reference

Key commands you'll use on exam day, organized for quick recall.

Task Command When to Use
Switch context kubectl config use-context <name> Start of every CKA question
See current context kubectl config current-context Verify you're on the right cluster
List all contexts kubectl config get-contexts Find available cluster names
Set default namespace kubectl config set-context --current --namespace=X Avoid typing -n X repeatedly
View active config kubectl config view --minify Debug connection issues
Flatten merged configs kubectl config view --flatten Export a merged config to a single file
Check API server URL kubectl cluster-info Verify cluster endpoint
Test connectivity kubectl get nodes Confirm kubectl can reach the cluster
💡 Pro Tip: Alias for Speed

The CKA exam pre-configures alias k=kubectl. You can also add: alias kgc='kubectl config get-contexts' and alias kuc='kubectl config use-context' to shave seconds off every question.

⚠️ CKA Gotcha: kubeconfig & Configuration Traps

  • KUBECONFIG env var — The exam may set KUBECONFIG=/path/to/config. If your commands fail, check: echo $KUBECONFIG and kubectl config view
  • Multiple kubeconfig files — Merge with KUBECONFIG=file1:file2 kubectl config view --flatten. The exam may provide separate configs per cluster
  • Admission controllers — Enabled in kube-apiserver.yaml via --enable-admission-plugins. The exam may ask you to enable/disable one — edit the static pod manifest
  • API groups matter — v1 = core group (pods, services). apps/v1 = apps group (deployments). rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1 = RBAC. Wrong group = object won't create

CKA Speed Tip: Quickly check API server flags without opening the manifest:
kubectl -n kube-system get pod kube-apiserver-controlplane -o jsonpath='{.spec.containers[0].command}' | tr ',' '\n'
This lists every flag the API server is running with.

⚡ Config & context commands: kubectl config view --minify — show only current context details
kubectl config set-credentials user --client-certificate=cert.crt --client-key=key.pem — add user
kubectl config set-context myctx --cluster=mycluster --user=myuser --namespace=default — create context
kubectl api-versions — list all available API versions

📝 Chapter 10 — CKA Practice Questions

Q1
What are the three main sections of a kubeconfig file? Draw the relationship between them.
Show Answer
Answer
# The three sections:
# 1. clusters    — WHERE to connect (API server endpoint + CA cert)
# 2. users       — WHO you are (client cert + key, or token)
# 3. contexts    — WHICH combo to use (cluster + user + namespace)

apiVersion: v1
kind: Config
current-context: dev-context    # ← Active context

clusters:
- name: dev-cluster
  cluster:
    server: https://10.0.0.10:6443
    certificate-authority-data: <base64-ca-cert>

users:
- name: dev-admin
  user:
    client-certificate-data: <base64-cert>
    client-key-data: <base64-key>

contexts:
- name: dev-context              # ← Links cluster + user
  context:
    cluster: dev-cluster         # → References clusters[].name
    user: dev-admin              # → References users[].name
    namespace: default           # Optional default namespace
# Relationship:
#  Context ──→ Cluster (server URL)
#     │
#     └──→ User (credentials)
#     │
#     └──→ Namespace (optional default)
Explanation

A kubeconfig is essentially a mapping: contexts bind a cluster to a user with an optional default namespace. You can have multiple clusters, users, and contexts in one file. The current-context field determines which context is active. On the CKA, understanding this structure is critical because exam questions give you different contexts per question. Each context points to a different cluster. If you use the wrong context, you'll work on the wrong cluster and fail the question.

Q2
You have two kubeconfig files: /root/admin.conf (production cluster) and /root/dev.conf (dev cluster). How do you merge them into one and switch between clusters?
Show Answer
Answer
# Method 1: Temporary merge (current session only)
export KUBECONFIG=/root/admin.conf:/root/dev.conf
kubectl config view  # Shows both clusters/contexts

# Method 2: Permanent merge into one file
KUBECONFIG=/root/admin.conf:/root/dev.conf \
  kubectl config view --flatten > ~/.kube/config

# List all available contexts
kubectl config get-contexts
# CURRENT   NAME         CLUSTER    AUTHINFO    NAMESPACE
# *         prod-ctx     prod       prod-admin  default
#           dev-ctx      dev        dev-admin   dev-ns

# Switch between clusters
kubectl config use-context prod-ctx   # → production
kubectl config use-context dev-ctx    # → development

# Verify current context
kubectl config current-context
# dev-ctx
Explanation

KUBECONFIG accepts a colon-separated list of files (semicolon on Windows). The --flatten flag resolves all external file references and outputs a self-contained config. On the CKA, the exam environment has pre-configured kubeconfig with multiple contexts — one per cluster/question. You MUST switch contexts at the start of each question. Always verify you're in the right context: kubectl config current-context. Working in the wrong context is the #1 reason candidates fail otherwise-correct answers.

Q3
What are admission controllers? Name three important ones and explain how to enable/disable them.
Show Answer
Answer
# Admission controllers intercept API requests AFTER auth but BEFORE persistence

# View currently enabled admission controllers:
kubectl -n kube-system get pod kube-apiserver-controlplane \
  -o jsonpath='{.spec.containers[0].command}' | tr ',' '\n' | grep admission

# Important admission controllers:
# 1. NamespaceLifecycle — prevents creating objects in terminating namespaces
# 2. LimitRanger       — enforces LimitRange defaults in namespaces
# 3. ResourceQuota     — enforces ResourceQuota limits
# 4. NodeRestriction   — limits what kubelets can modify
# 5. PodSecurity       — enforces pod security standards

# Enable an admission controller:
# Edit /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml
# Find or add:
- --enable-admission-plugins=NodeRestriction,PodSecurity,LimitRanger

# Disable a specific one:
- --disable-admission-plugins=DefaultStorageClass

# After saving, the API server restarts automatically (static pod)
Explanation

Admission controllers are plugins that govern what's allowed in the cluster. They run in order after authentication and authorization. There are two types: mutating (can modify the request, like adding default resource limits) and validating (can only accept/reject). On the CKA, you may be asked to enable a specific admission controller by editing the API server static pod manifest at /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml. After saving, kubelet restarts the API server — wait 30-60 seconds for it to come back up. Always verify with kubectl get pods -n kube-system.

Q4
How do you find the API group and version for a specific Kubernetes resource? Show multiple methods.
Show Answer
Answer
# Method 1: kubectl api-resources (most useful)
kubectl api-resources | grep -i deployment
# deployments   deploy   apps/v1   true   Deployment
#               ↑alias   ↑apiVersion ↑namespaced

# Method 2: kubectl api-versions (list all)
kubectl api-versions
# apps/v1
# networking.k8s.io/v1
# rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
# v1   ← core group (no prefix)

# Method 3: kubectl explain (shows VERSION header)
kubectl explain deployment
# KIND:     Deployment
# VERSION:  apps/v1

# Method 4: API discovery endpoint
kubectl get --raw /apis/apps/v1 | python3 -m json.tool

# Quick reference — API groups:
# Core (v1):        Pod, Service, ConfigMap, Secret, PVC, SA, Node, Namespace
# apps/v1:          Deployment, ReplicaSet, DaemonSet, StatefulSet
# networking:       Ingress, NetworkPolicy
# rbac:             Role, ClusterRole, RoleBinding, ClusterRoleBinding
# storage:          StorageClass, VolumeAttachment
# batch/v1:         Job, CronJob
Explanation

kubectl api-resources is the definitive answer for the CKA. It shows the short name, API group/version, whether the resource is namespaced, and the kind. The core API group resources (Pod, Service, etc.) use v1 with no group prefix in the apiVersion. All other resources use <group>/<version> format. The kubectl explain command is also invaluable — it shows the VERSION in its header output, plus detailed documentation of every field.

Q5
You need to create a new context called staging-ctx that connects to cluster staging-cluster as user staging-admin with default namespace staging. Then switch to it.
Show Answer
Answer
# Step 1: Create the context
kubectl config set-context staging-ctx \
  --cluster=staging-cluster \
  --user=staging-admin \
  --namespace=staging

# Step 2: Switch to it
kubectl config use-context staging-ctx
# Switched to context "staging-ctx"

# Step 3: Verify
kubectl config current-context
# staging-ctx

kubectl config view --minify
# Shows only staging-ctx details

# Step 4: Test connectivity
kubectl get pods
# Should show pods in the staging namespace
Explanation

kubectl config set-context creates or updates a context entry in the kubeconfig. The --cluster and --user must reference entries that already exist in the kubeconfig's clusters and users sections. Setting --namespace makes every command in this context default to that namespace (no need for -n staging every time). On the CKA, the most common pattern is: kubectl config use-context <name> then kubectl config set-context --current --namespace=<ns> — this sets the namespace for the current context without creating a new one.

Q6
How do you view and modify the API server configuration? What flags control authentication, authorization, and which admission controllers are active?
Show Answer
Answer
# API Server is a static pod — config is in the manifest
sudo cat /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml

# Key flags to know:
# Authentication:
--client-ca-file=/etc/kubernetes/pki/ca.crt
--authentication-token-webhook-config-file=...
--oidc-issuer-url=...

# Authorization (order matters!):
--authorization-mode=Node,RBAC
# Options: AlwaysAllow, AlwaysDeny, Node, RBAC, Webhook, ABAC

# Admission Controllers:
--enable-admission-plugins=NodeRestriction,PodSecurity
--disable-admission-plugins=DefaultStorageClass

# To modify: edit the manifest directly
sudo vim /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml
# kubelet detects the change and restarts the API server

# Wait and verify:
kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep apiserver
# Should show Running with RESTARTS incremented by 1
Explanation

The API server runs as a static pod, so its config is a YAML manifest at /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml. Editing this file triggers kubelet to restart the pod automatically — no kubectl apply needed. On the CKA, you must be comfortable editing this file. The --authorization-mode flag determines the auth chain — requests are checked against each mode in order. Node,RBAC is the default: first check Node authorization (for kubelet), then RBAC for everything else. Be careful editing — a syntax error will take the API server down, and kubectl won't work until you fix it.

Q7
What is the difference between kubectl config view and kubectl config view --minify? When should you use --raw?
Show Answer
Answer
# kubectl config view — shows ENTIRE kubeconfig (all contexts)
kubectl config view
# Shows all clusters, users, contexts — can be very long

# kubectl config view --minify — current context ONLY
kubectl config view --minify
# Shows only the cluster, user, context for current-context

# kubectl config view --raw — shows actual certificate data
kubectl config view --raw
# Normally cert data is replaced with "DATA+OMITTED"
# --raw shows the full base64-encoded certificates

# Practical example: extract the CA cert
kubectl config view --raw -o jsonpath='{.clusters[0].cluster.certificate-authority-data}' | base64 -d > ca.crt

# Extract the current context's server URL
kubectl config view --minify -o jsonpath='{.clusters[0].cluster.server}'
# https://10.0.0.10:6443
Explanation

--minify filters the output to only show what's relevant to the current context — essential on the CKA where kubeconfig has many contexts. --raw shows the actual certificate data instead of the DATA+OMITTED placeholder. You'd use --raw when you need to extract certificates for debugging or copying to another config. On the CKA, combine --minify with -o jsonpath to quickly extract specific values like the server URL, namespace, or user name.

Q8
The kube-apiserver won't start after you edited its manifest. How do you debug without kubectl?
Show Answer
Answer
# kubectl is DEAD when API server is down — use these alternatives:

# 1. Check container status via CRI
sudo crictl ps -a | grep kube-apiserver
# Look for Exited containers with exit codes

# 2. Check API server container logs
sudo crictl logs <container-id>
# Shows the actual error message

# 3. Check kubelet logs (kubelet manages static pods)
sudo journalctl -u kubelet --no-pager -n 30

# 4. Check the manifest for syntax errors
sudo cat /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml
# Look for typos, wrong indentation, missing values

# 5. Validate YAML syntax
python3 -c "import yaml; yaml.safe_load(open('/etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml'))"

# 6. Common mistakes:
# - Extra space in flag: --enable-admission -plugins (wrong!)
# - Missing value: --authorization-mode= (empty!)
# - Wrong file path: --client-ca-file=/wrong/path
# - Typo: --enbale-admission-plugins (misspelled!)

# After fixing, kubelet auto-restarts the API server (30-60s)
Explanation

When you break the API server, you lose kubectl access — this is the most stressful CKA scenario. Your lifeline is crictl (CRI tool that talks directly to containerd) and journalctl -u kubelet. The pattern: (1) crictl ps -a to see the crash-looping container, (2) crictl logs <id> to read the error, (3) fix the manifest, (4) wait for auto-recovery. Don't panic — kubelet keeps trying to restart the static pod. As soon as the manifest is valid, the API server comes back within 30-60 seconds.

Q9
What is the Kubernetes API request flow? List every step from when you type kubectl get pods to when you see the output.
Show Answer
Answer
# Complete API request flow:

# 1. kubectl reads ~/.kube/config
#    → Finds server URL, client cert, CA cert

# 2. kubectl sends HTTPS GET /api/v1/namespaces/default/pods
#    → TLS handshake with API server

# 3. API Server: Authentication
#    → Validates client certificate (or token/OIDC)
#    → Identifies: "This is user kubernetes-admin"

# 4. API Server: Authorization (RBAC)
#    → Checks: Can kubernetes-admin GET pods in default?
#    → Evaluates ClusterRoleBindings/RoleBindings

# 5. API Server: Admission Control
#    → Runs mutating admission controllers (modify request)
#    → Runs validating admission controllers (accept/reject)

# 6. API Server: Read from etcd
#    → Retrieves pod objects from etcd datastore

# 7. Response serialization
#    → Converts internal objects to requested format (JSON/YAML/table)

# 8. kubectl: Output formatting
#    → Applies -o wide, -o yaml, --sort-by, JSONPath filters
Explanation

Understanding this flow helps with debugging: if authentication fails, check certificates. If authorization fails, check RBAC (Role/ClusterRole bindings). If admission fails, check admission controller configuration. For read operations (GET/LIST), admission controllers may still run (e.g., webhook admission). For write operations (CREATE/UPDATE), all three stages are critical. On the CKA, if you get "forbidden" errors, the problem is at step 4 (RBAC). If you get "denied by admission controller," the problem is at step 5.

Q10
How do you check what API groups and resources are available in your cluster? What is the difference between core API group and named API groups?
Show Answer
Answer
# List all API groups and versions
kubectl api-versions
# apps/v1
# batch/v1
# networking.k8s.io/v1
# rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
# v1   ← This is the CORE group (no name prefix)

# List all resources with their API group
kubectl api-resources -o wide
# NAME         SHORTNAMES  APIVERSION                    VERBS
# pods         po          v1                            [create delete get ...]
# deployments  deploy      apps/v1                       [create delete get ...]
# ingresses    ing         networking.k8s.io/v1          [create delete get ...]

# Core group (legacy, no prefix):
# API path: /api/v1
# Resources: Pod, Service, ConfigMap, Secret, PVC, Node, Namespace
# apiVersion in YAML: v1

# Named groups (newer, prefixed):
# API path: /apis/apps/v1, /apis/networking.k8s.io/v1
# Resources: Deployment, Ingress, NetworkPolicy, etc.
# apiVersion in YAML: apps/v1, networking.k8s.io/v1
Explanation

The core group (accessed at /api/v1) contains the original K8s resources and uses just v1 as the apiVersion. Named groups (accessed at /apis/<group>/<version>) were added later for better organization. New features always go into named groups. On the CKA, knowing which group a resource belongs to determines the correct apiVersion in manifests. kubectl api-resources is the ultimate reference — it shows every installed resource, its API group, whether it's namespaced, and its shortname alias.

Q11
How do you view and modify the kubelet configuration on a worker node? Where is the config file, and what must you do after making changes?
Show Answer
Answer
# Step 1: Find the kubelet config file path
ps aux | grep kubelet | grep -oP 'config=\S+'
# --config=/var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml

# Alternative: check systemd unit
systemctl cat kubelet | grep config
# --config=/var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml

# Step 2: View current configuration
cat /var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml
# Key fields:
#   staticPodPath: /etc/kubernetes/manifests
#   clusterDNS: ["10.96.0.10"]
#   clusterDomain: cluster.local
#   cgroupDriver: systemd
#   evictionHard:
#     memory.available: "100Mi"
#     nodefs.available: "10%"

# Step 3: Edit the config
sudo vim /var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml
# Example: change eviction threshold
# evictionHard:
#   memory.available: "200Mi"

# Step 4: Restart kubelet to apply changes
sudo systemctl restart kubelet

# Step 5: Verify kubelet is running
sudo systemctl status kubelet
# Active: active (running)

# ⚠️ Unlike static pod manifests, kubelet config
# is NOT auto-detected — you MUST restart kubelet
Explanation

The kubelet config lives at /var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml and is referenced via --config in the kubelet systemd unit. Unlike static pod manifests (which kubelet auto-detects), changes to the kubelet config file require a systemctl restart kubelet to take effect. Key configurable fields include staticPodPath, clusterDNS, evictionHard thresholds, and cgroupDriver. On the CKA, you may need to find this file to check settings like the static pod path or DNS configuration. The ps aux | grep kubelet → find --config → read file pattern is essential.

Chapter 11: etcd — The Cluster Database

etcd is the single source of truth for your entire Kubernetes cluster. Every object — every pod, service, configmap, secret — is stored in etcd. Lose etcd, lose your cluster. That's why etcd backup is one of the most important admin tasks and is guaranteed to appear on the CKA exam.

🟡 Intermediate ⏱️ ~1.5 hours 💾 etcd 📝 CKA EXAM

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ Understand etcd's role — what it stores and why it's critical
  • ✅ Know etcd's architecture and the Raft consensus algorithm
  • ✅ Backup etcd using etcdctl snapshot save (CKA guaranteed question)
  • ✅ Restore etcd from a backup snapshot
  • ✅ Secure etcd with TLS certificates and access control
🎓 CKA Relevance
Cluster Architecture, Installation & Configuration 25%
etcd Backup = GUARANTEED CKA Question
