Almost every CKA exam includes an etcd backup and/or restore question. This is worth significant points. Memorize the backup command and the certificate paths.

  • Memorize: ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot save with all 3 cert flags
  • Know the cert paths: /etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/
  • Practice restore blindfolded: stop etcd → restore snapshot → update data-dir → restart

11.1 What is etcd?

etcd is a distributed, reliable key-value store that Kubernetes uses to store all cluster state. Every kubectl apply, every pod creation, every secret — all stored in etcd.

🏛️
Key-Value Store

Stores data as key-value pairs, similar to a dictionary. Example: /registry/pods/default/nginx → pod YAML.

🔄
Distributed

Runs on multiple nodes (3 or 5 for HA) and replicates data using the Raft consensus algorithm.

🔒
Strongly Consistent

All reads return the latest write. No stale data ever — critical for cluster state correctness.

📊
Watch API

Supports efficient watchers. The API server subscribes to changes — this powers the entire controller pattern.

What etcd Stores — The Kubernetes Registry
  etcd Key Space (all under /registry/)
  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                                                                 │
  │  /registry/                                                     │
  │  ├── pods/                                                      │
  │  │   ├── default/                                               │
  │  │   │   ├── nginx-deployment-abc123     ← Pod spec + status    │
  │  │   │   └── jobpost-web-xyz789          ← Our anihpj app!      │
  │  │   └── kube-system/                                           │
  │  │       ├── coredns-7db6d8ff4d-xxx      ← System pods          │
  │  │       └── calico-node-yyy                                    │
  │  ├── deployments/                                               │
  │  │   └── default/                                               │
  │  │       └── nginx-deployment            ← Deployment spec      │
  │  ├── services/                                                  │
  │  │   ├── default/kubernetes              ← The kubernetes svc   │
  │  │   └── default/jobpost-service         ← Our app service      │
  │  ├── secrets/                                                   │
  │  │   └── default/db-credentials          ← ⚠️ Stored in etcd!   │
  │  ├── configmaps/                                                │
  │  ├── namespaces/                                                │
  │  ├── nodes/                                                     │
  │  ├── clusterroles/                                              │
  │  └── ... (every K8s object type)                                │
  │                                                                 │
  │  📌 Size: Typically 2-8 GB for a large production cluster       │
  │  📌 Default port: 2379 (client) / 2380 (peer)                  │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
🚨 etcd Contains Secrets in Plain Text

By default, Kubernetes Secrets are stored unencrypted in etcd. Anyone with direct access to etcd can read all secrets. Enable encryption at rest (covered in Section 11.5) to protect sensitive data.

11.2 etcd Architecture & Raft Consensus

In production/HA clusters, etcd runs on 3 or 5 nodes. They elect a leader using the Raft consensus algorithm, ensuring data consistency even if nodes fail.

Raft Consensus — Leader Election & Replication
  ┌─────────────────────── etcd Cluster (3 Members) ───────────────────────┐
  │                                                                         │
  │    ┌─────────────────┐   ┌─────────────────┐   ┌─────────────────┐     │
  │    │   etcd Member 1  │   │   etcd Member 2  │   │   etcd Member 3  │   │
  │    │   ★ LEADER       │   │   FOLLOWER       │   │   FOLLOWER       │   │
  │    │                  │   │                  │   │                  │     │
  │    │  ┌────────────┐  │   │  ┌────────────┐  │   │  ┌────────────┐ │   │
  │    │  │  Log: v101  │  │   │  │  Log: v101  │  │   │  │  Log: v101 │ │   │
  │    │  │  Log: v102  │  │──▶│  │  Log: v102  │  │──▶│  │  Log: v102 │ │   │
  │    │  │  Log: v103  │  │   │  │  Log: v103  │  │   │  │  Log: v103 │ │   │
  │    │  └────────────┘  │   │  └────────────┘  │   │  └────────────┘ │   │
  │    │   Port: 2379     │   │   Port: 2379     │   │   Port: 2379    │   │
  │    │   Peer:  2380    │   │   Peer:  2380    │   │   Peer:  2380   │   │
  │    └─────────────────┘   └─────────────────┘   └─────────────────┘   │
  │                                                                       │
  │    ✅ Write: Client → Leader → Replicate to majority → ACK            │
  │    ✅ Read:  Client → Any member (leader or follower)                  │
  │    ✅ Quorum: 2 of 3 must agree (tolerates 1 failure)                  │
  │                                                                       │
  │    Quorum formula: (N/2) + 1                                           │
  │    ┌─────────┬────────────┬────────────────┬──────────────────┐       │
  │    │ Members │   Quorum   │ Fault Tolerance│    Use Case      │       │
  │    ├─────────┼────────────┼────────────────┼──────────────────┤       │
  │    │    1    │     1      │       0        │ Development      │       │
  │    │    3    │     2      │       1        │ Small production │       │
  │    │    5    │     3      │       2        │ Large production │       │
  │    │    7    │     4      │       3        │ Rare (overhead)  │       │
  │    └─────────┴────────────┴────────────────┴──────────────────┘       │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
✍️Client Write
→
★Leader Receives
→
📤Replicate Log
→
✅Majority ACK
→
💾Commit & Respond
📖 Why Odd Numbers? (3 or 5, Never 4 or 6)

A 3-member cluster tolerates 1 failure (quorum = 2). A 4-member cluster also tolerates only 1 failure (quorum = 3). So 4 members gives no advantage over 3 but adds network overhead. Always use odd numbers.

11.3 Backing Up etcd (CKA Must-Know)

This is the #1 admin task for cluster reliability. If you can back up and restore etcd, you can recover from almost any disaster. The CKA exam will test this.

etcd-backup.sh 
# ╔════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
# ║  MEMORIZE THIS COMMAND — CKA GUARANTEED QUESTION      ║
# ╚════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝

ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot save /opt/etcd-backup.db \
  --endpoints=https://127.0.0.1:2379 \
  --cacert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt \
  --cert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt \
  --key=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key

# Expected output:
# {"level":"info","msg":"created temporary snapshot file",
#  "path":"/opt/etcd-backup.db.part"}
# {"level":"info","msg":"fetching snapshot","endpoint":"https://..."}
# Snapshot saved at /opt/etcd-backup.db

# Verify the backup
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot status /opt/etcd-backup.db \
  --write-out=table
# +----------+----------+------------+------------+
# |   HASH   | REVISION | TOTAL KEYS | TOTAL SIZE |
# +----------+----------+------------+------------+
# | fe01cf57 |    10945 |        771 |    2.1 MB  |
# +----------+----------+------------+------------+

etcdctl snapshot save — Flag Breakdown

💾 /opt/etcd-backup.db
Output file path. Use whatever path the exam question specifies.
📡 --endpoints
etcd endpoint URL. Default: https://127.0.0.1:2379 (local etcd).
🔐 --cacert
etcd CA certificate: /etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt
📜 --cert
etcd server certificate: /etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt
🔑 --key
etcd server key: /etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key

🧠 How to Find the Certificate Paths on the Exam

If you forget the cert paths, extract them from the running etcd pod:

find-etcd-certs.sh 
# Method 1: From the etcd pod spec
kubectl -n kube-system describe pod etcd-control-plane | grep -A5 "cert\|key\|ca"
#   --cert-file=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt
#   --key-file=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key
#   --trusted-ca-file=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt
#   --peer-cert-file=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/peer.crt

# Method 2: From the static pod manifest
cat /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml | grep -E "cert|key|ca"

# Method 3: From the API server (etcd client certs)
cat /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml | grep etcd
💡 CKA Tip: Always Set ETCDCTL_API=3

Some etcdctl versions default to API v2. Always prefix with ETCDCTL_API=3 or export it: export ETCDCTL_API=3. API v3 is what Kubernetes uses. If you forget this, the backup command may fail silently.

11.4 Restoring etcd from Backup

Restoring etcd is more complex than backup because you must stop etcd, restore to a new data directory, update the configuration to point to the new directory, and restart etcd.

1
Restore Snapshot

Use etcdctl snapshot restore to create a new data directory from the backup file.

2
Stop etcd

Move the etcd static pod manifest out of /etc/kubernetes/manifests/ to stop it.

3
Update data-dir

Edit the etcd manifest to point --data-dir to the restored directory.

4
Restart etcd

Move the manifest back. kubelet will auto-start etcd with the restored data.

5
Verify

Check that all previous resources are present in the restored cluster.

etcd-restore.sh 
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════
#  STEP 1: Restore the snapshot to a NEW data directory
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot restore /opt/etcd-backup.db \
  --data-dir=/var/lib/etcd-restored

# Output:
# {"level":"info","msg":"restoring snapshot",
#  "path":"/opt/etcd-backup.db",
#  "wal-dir":"/var/lib/etcd-restored/member/wal",
#  "data-dir":"/var/lib/etcd-restored",
#  "snap-dir":"/var/lib/etcd-restored/member/snap"}

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════
#  STEP 2: Update the etcd static pod manifest
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════
sudo vi /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml

# Change the --data-dir flag:
#   BEFORE: --data-dir=/var/lib/etcd
#   AFTER:  --data-dir=/var/lib/etcd-restored

# ALSO update the hostPath volume:
#   BEFORE: path: /var/lib/etcd
#   AFTER:  path: /var/lib/etcd-restored

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════
#  STEP 3: Wait for etcd to restart automatically
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# kubelet detects the manifest change and restarts etcd

# Watch for the etcd pod to come back
kubectl get pods -n kube-system -w | grep etcd
# etcd-control-plane   1/1   Running   0   10s

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════
#  STEP 4: Verify the restore
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════
kubectl get nodes
kubectl get pods --all-namespaces
kubectl get deployments
# All resources from the backup should be present
etcd Restore Flow — Visual Walkthrough
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  ETCD RESTORE PROCEDURE                                      │
  │                                                              │
  │  /opt/etcd-backup.db                                         │
  │       │                                                      │
  │       ▼                                                      │
  │  etcdctl snapshot restore                                    │
  │       │  --data-dir=/var/lib/etcd-restored                   │
  │       │                                                      │
  │       ▼                                                      │
  │  /var/lib/etcd-restored/         ← New data directory        │
  │  ├── member/                                                 │
  │  │   ├── snap/                   ← Restored snapshots        │
  │  │   └── wal/                    ← Write-ahead logs          │
  │  │                                                           │
  │  │  Now update etcd manifest:                                │
  │  │                                                           │
  │  │  /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml                      │
  │  │  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────┐       │
  │  │  │  --data-dir=/var/lib/etcd-restored    ← Update │       │
  │  │  │                                                │       │
  │  │  │  volumes:                                      │       │
  │  │  │  - hostPath:                                   │       │
  │  │  │      path: /var/lib/etcd-restored     ← Update │       │
  │  │  └────────────────────────────────────────────────┘       │
  │  │                                                           │
  │  └── kubelet detects manifest change → restarts etcd pod     │
  │                                                              │
  │  ✅ Cluster restored to the backup point in time              │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
🚨 Critical: Update BOTH data-dir AND hostPath

When restoring, you must update the --data-dir flag in the etcd command AND the hostPath.path in the volume section of the manifest. Missing either one will cause etcd to fail to start or use stale data.

⚠️ Post-Restore: Everything After the Backup is Lost

A restore rolls the cluster back to the point when the backup was taken. Any resources created, updated, or deleted after the backup will be lost (or reverted). This is why regular, scheduled backups are essential.

11.5 etcd Security & Encryption at Rest

etcd must be secured because it holds every secret, token, and credential in the cluster. Security comes from TLS encryption for communication and encryption at rest for stored data.

🔒
TLS Encryption (In Transit)

Default with kubeadm. All communication between API server ↔ etcd and etcd ↔ etcd peers uses mutual TLS. Certificates are in /etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/.

🔐
Encryption at Rest

Not enabled by default! Without this, secrets are stored as base64 (NOT encrypted) in etcd. You must configure an EncryptionConfiguration.

/etc/kubernetes/enc/enc.yaml 
# Encryption Configuration — encrypts Secrets at rest in etcd
apiVersion: apiserver.config.k8s.io/v1
kind: EncryptionConfiguration
resources:
  - resources:
      - secrets           # Only encrypt secrets (most common)
    providers:
      - aescbc:           # AES-CBC encryption (recommended)
          keys:
            - name: key1
              secret: c2VjcmV0LWtleS1oZXJlLTMyYnl0ZXM=  # Base64 of 32-byte key
      - identity: {}       # Fallback: read unencrypted (for existing secrets)
enable-encryption.sh 
# Step 1: Create the encryption config file
sudo mkdir -p /etc/kubernetes/enc
sudo vi /etc/kubernetes/enc/enc.yaml   # (paste YAML above)

# Step 2: Add flag to API server manifest
sudo vi /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml
# Add this flag to the command:
#   --encryption-provider-config=/etc/kubernetes/enc/enc.yaml
# Add volume mount for the config file:
#   volumeMounts:
#   - name: enc
#     mountPath: /etc/kubernetes/enc
#     readOnly: true
#   volumes:
#   - name: enc
#     hostPath:
#       path: /etc/kubernetes/enc

# Step 3: Wait for API server to restart
kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep apiserver

# Step 4: Re-encrypt ALL existing secrets
kubectl get secrets --all-namespaces -o json | \
  kubectl replace -f -

# Step 5: Verify encryption is working
# Read directly from etcd — should show encrypted data
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl get /registry/secrets/default/my-secret \
  --endpoints=https://127.0.0.1:2379 \
  --cacert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt \
  --cert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt \
  --key=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key | hexdump -C
# Should show encrypted binary data, NOT readable text
etcd Security — Defense In Depth
  ┌────────────────────────── etcd Security Layers ──────────────────────────┐
  │                                                                          │
  │  Layer 1: Network Isolation                                              │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐            │
  │  │ etcd should ONLY be accessible from the control plane    │            │
  │  │ Firewall: Allow port 2379 only from API server IP       │            │
  │  │ Firewall: Allow port 2380 only from other etcd peers    │            │
  │  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘            │
  │                                                                          │
  │  Layer 2: TLS Encryption (In Transit)                                    │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐            │
  │  │ API Server ──TLS──▶ etcd (client certs: server.crt/key) │            │
  │  │ etcd ──TLS──▶ etcd peers (peer certs: peer.crt/key)     │            │
  │  │ All verified against etcd CA: /etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt │      │
  │  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘            │
  │                                                                          │
  │  Layer 3: Encryption at Rest                                             │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐            │
  │  │ EncryptionConfiguration encrypts secrets before storage  │            │
  │  │ Provider: aescbc (AES-256-CBC) or secretbox               │            │
  │  │ Without this: secrets stored as base64 (readable!)       │            │
  │  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘            │
  │                                                                          │
  │  Layer 4: RBAC + Authentication                                          │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐            │
  │  │ Only API server should have etcd client credentials      │            │
  │  │ No user should access etcd directly (always go via API)  │            │
  │  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘            │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
💡 CKA Tip: Encryption at Rest vs TLS

TLS encrypts data in transit (between components). Encryption at rest encrypts data on disk (inside etcd). Both are independent. kubeadm sets up TLS automatically but does NOT enable encryption at rest — you must configure it manually.

🏥 Bonus: etcd Health Check & Monitoring Commands

Knowing how to check etcd's health is essential for cluster troubleshooting. These commands tell you if etcd is running, healthy, and performing well.

etcd-health-check.sh 
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
#  ETCD HEALTH CHECK COMMANDS
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# Set cert variables (reuse across commands)
export ETCDCTL_API=3
CERTS="--endpoints=https://127.0.0.1:2379 \
  --cacert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt \
  --cert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt \
  --key=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key"

# Check cluster health
etcdctl endpoint health $CERTS
# https://127.0.0.1:2379 is healthy: successfully committed
#   proposal: took = 2.345ms

# Check endpoint status (shows leader, DB size, Raft index)
etcdctl endpoint status $CERTS --write-out=table
# +---------------------------+--------+---------+---------+-----------+
# |         ENDPOINT          |   ID   | VERSION | DB SIZE | IS LEADER |
# +---------------------------+--------+---------+---------+-----------+
# | https://127.0.0.1:2379    | abc123 |  3.5.12 |  4.2 MB |   true    |
# +---------------------------+--------+---------+---------+-----------+

# List all etcd cluster members
etcdctl member list $CERTS --write-out=table
# +--------+---------+-------+---------------------------+-----------+
# |   ID   | STATUS  | NAME  |       PEER ADDRS          | IS LEADER |
# +--------+---------+-------+---------------------------+-----------+
# | abc123 | started | etcd0 | https://192.168.56.10:2380|   true    |
# +--------+---------+-------+---------------------------+-----------+

# Check performance (write latency)
etcdctl check perf $CERTS
# 60/60 succeeded, took 1.234s
# PASS: Throughput is 150 writes/s

# Monitor real-time changes (debugging)
etcdctl watch /registry/pods --prefix $CERTS
# (streams all pod changes as they happen)
✅
Healthy etcd Indicators

• endpoint health says "is healthy"
• DB size under 8 GB
• Raft term stable (not incrementing)
• All members show "started"
• Write latency under 10ms
• Leader is elected (one IS LEADER = true)

🚨
Unhealthy etcd Red Flags

• endpoint health timeout/errors
• DB size over 8 GB (needs compaction)
• "no leader" in member list
• High Raft term (frequent elections)
• "mvcc: database space exceeded"
• Members showing "unstarted"

🧹 Bonus: etcd Maintenance — Compaction & Defragmentation

etcd stores all historical revisions of keys. Over time, this grows the database. Compaction removes old revisions, and defragmentation reclaims disk space.

etcd-maintenance.sh 
# Get current revision number
REV=$(etcdctl endpoint status $CERTS --write-out="json" | \
  python3 -c "import sys,json; print(json.load(sys.stdin)[0]['Status']['header']['revision'])")

# Compact old revisions (keep only latest)
etcdctl compact $REV $CERTS
# compacted revision 12345

# Defragment (reclaim disk space after compaction)
etcdctl defrag $CERTS
# Finished defragmenting etcd member[https://127.0.0.1:2379]

# Check new DB size (should be smaller)
etcdctl endpoint status $CERTS --write-out=table
⚠️ Compaction is Irreversible

Once you compact, old revisions are permanently deleted. This means watchers using old revisions will get an error. Always take an etcd backup before compacting in production.

🎯 CKA etcd Quick Reference Card

Print this or memorize it. These are the exact commands and paths you need for the CKA exam.

Item Path / Value Why You Need It
etcd CA cert /etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt --cacert flag for all etcdctl commands
etcd server cert /etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt --cert flag for all etcdctl commands
etcd server key /etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key --key flag for all etcdctl commands
Client port 2379 --endpoints HTTPS URL
Peer port 2380 etcd ↔ etcd communication
Data directory /var/lib/etcd --data-dir for restore
Static pod manifest /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml Edit to change --data-dir after restore
API version env ETCDCTL_API=3 Always set this before etcdctl commands
CKA etcd — Complete Backup & Restore Cheat Sheet
  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │   BACKUP:                                                          │
  │   ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot save /opt/backup.db \             │
  │     --endpoints=https://127.0.0.1:2379 \                           │
  │     --cacert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt \                     │
  │     --cert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt \                   │
  │     --key=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key                      │
  │                                                                     │
  │   VERIFY BACKUP:                                                    │
  │   ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot status /opt/backup.db -w table    │
  │                                                                     │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │   RESTORE:                                                          │
  │   ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot restore /opt/backup.db \          │
  │     --data-dir=/var/lib/etcd-restored                              │
  │                                                                     │
  │   THEN EDIT: /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml                   │
  │     1. --data-dir=/var/lib/etcd-restored                           │
  │     2. volumes.hostPath.path=/var/lib/etcd-restored                │
  │                                                                     │
  │   WAIT: kubelet auto-restarts etcd pod                             │
  │   VERIFY: kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep etcd              │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │   FIND CERT PATHS (if you forget):                                 │
  │   kubectl -n kube-system describe pod etcd-<node> | grep cert     │
  │   cat /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml | grep -E "cert|key|ca"  │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
💡 CKA Exam Strategy: etcd Questions

Step 1: Read the question carefully — does it ask for backup, restore, or both?
Step 2: If you forget cert paths, run: kubectl describe pod etcd-controlplane -n kube-system
Step 3: After restore, always verify with kubectl get nodes and kubectl get pods -A
Step 4: Don't panic if the API server takes 30-60 seconds to restart after a restore.

⚠️ CKA Gotcha: etcd Backup & Restore Traps

  • Certificate paths are REQUIRED — etcdctl won't work without --cacert, --cert, --key. Extract them from /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml
  • ETCDCTL_API=3 — Must be set. API v2 commands look similar but produce incompatible snapshots
  • Restore path ≠ original path — etcdctl snapshot restore creates a NEW data directory. You must update --data-dir in the etcd manifest to point to it
  • After restore, wait — The API server, scheduler, and controller-manager restart automatically when kubelet detects the manifest change. Give it 30-60 seconds
  • Don't confuse endpoint — Default etcd endpoint is https://127.0.0.1:2379. External etcd uses the node's IP

CKA Speed Tip — etcd backup one-liner you MUST memorize:
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot save /tmp/etcd-backup.db \
  --cacert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt \
  --cert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt \
  --key=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key

📝 Chapter 11 Practice Drill

Timed challenge (target: 8 minutes):

  • 1. Find etcd cert paths: cat /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml | grep -E "cert|key|ca"
  • 2. Take a snapshot to /opt/etcd-backup.db
  • 3. Verify snapshot: ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot status /opt/etcd-backup.db --write-table
  • 4. Restore to /var/lib/etcd-restored
  • 5. Update /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml data-dir and hostPath volume
  • 6. Wait and verify: kubectl get pods -A

Key insight: This is a GUARANTEED exam question. Practice until you can do it blindfolded.

📝 Chapter 11 — CKA Practice Questions

Q1
Write the complete etcdctl command to take a snapshot backup of etcd to /opt/etcd-backup.db. Where do you find the required certificate paths?
Show Answer
Answer
# Step 1: Find certificate paths from etcd static pod manifest
cat /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml | grep -E "\-\-cert|\-\-key|\-\-ca"
# --cert-file=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt
# --key-file=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key
# --trusted-ca-file=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt

# Step 2: Take the snapshot
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot save /opt/etcd-backup.db \
  --cacert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt \
  --cert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt \
  --key=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key

# Step 3: Verify the snapshot
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot status /opt/etcd-backup.db --write-table
# +----------+----------+------------+------------+
# |   HASH   | REVISION | TOTAL KEYS | TOTAL SIZE |
# +----------+----------+------------+------------+
# | abcdef12 |    45678 |       1234 |     5.2 MB |
# +----------+----------+------------+------------+
Explanation

This is a guaranteed CKA exam question. The three certificates are mandatory — etcd uses mutual TLS. The trick is knowing where to find them: /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml has the exact paths in its command arguments. Note the flag name differences: etcd uses --cert-file and --trusted-ca-file, but etcdctl uses --cert and --cacert. ETCDCTL_API=3 must be set — API v2 uses different commands and snapshot formats. Always verify with snapshot status to confirm the backup is valid.

Q2
Write the complete procedure to restore etcd from a snapshot at /opt/etcd-backup.db. What must you update after restoring?
Show Answer
Answer
# Step 1: Restore to a NEW data directory
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot restore /opt/etcd-backup.db \
  --data-dir=/var/lib/etcd-restored

# Step 2: Update etcd manifest to use the new data directory
sudo vim /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml

# Change TWO places:
# a) In container command args:
#    --data-dir=/var/lib/etcd-restored

# b) In volumes/volumeMounts hostPath:
#    volumes:
#    - name: etcd-data
#      hostPath:
#        path: /var/lib/etcd-restored    ← Update this
#        type: DirectoryOrCreate

# Step 3: Wait for the API server to recover (30-60 seconds)
# kubelet detects manifest change → restarts etcd → API server reconnects

# Step 4: Verify
kubectl get pods -A
kubectl get nodes
Explanation

The restore creates a brand new data directory — you must NOT restore to the original /var/lib/etcd because the member IDs won't match. After restoring, you update the etcd manifest in two places: (1) the --data-dir flag in the container command, and (2) the hostPath volume mount. kubelet detects the manifest change and restarts etcd with the restored data. The API server, scheduler, and controller-manager automatically reconnect. Give it 30-60 seconds — don't panic if kubectl is temporarily unavailable.

Q3
What is the difference between etcd's --cert-file/--key-file and --peer-cert-file/--peer-key-file?
Show Answer
Answer
Flag Purpose Who Connects
--cert-file Server cert for client connections API server → etcd
--key-file Server key for client connections API server → etcd
--peer-cert-file Cert for etcd-to-etcd communication etcd ↔ etcd (HA cluster)
--peer-key-file Key for etcd-to-etcd communication etcd ↔ etcd (HA cluster) 
# In a single-node etcd, peer certs are still configured
# but only matter in multi-node (HA) etcd clusters

# Find all cert paths:
cat /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml | grep -E "cert|key|ca"
Explanation

etcd uses two sets of TLS certificates: client certs (for API server connections) and peer certs (for etcd member replication). In a single control-plane cluster, peer certs exist but aren't actively used for multi-node communication. In HA clusters with 3 or 5 etcd members, peer certs are critical — they secure the Raft consensus protocol traffic between members. On the CKA, for backup/restore you use the client certs (--cacert, --cert, --key with etcdctl), not the peer certs.

Q4
How do you check the health of the etcd cluster? What commands show the member list and cluster status?
Show Answer
Answer
# Check etcd health
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl endpoint health \
  --cacert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt \
  --cert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt \
  --key=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key
# 127.0.0.1:2379 is healthy: successfully committed proposal

# Check etcd member list (HA clusters)
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl member list \
  --cacert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt \
  --cert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt \
  --key=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key --write-table
# Shows all etcd members, their status, and client URLs

# Check etcd endpoint status (detailed)
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl endpoint status \
  --cacert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt \
  --cert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt \
  --key=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key --write-table
# Shows DB size, leader, raft index, raft term

# Quick check via kubectl (simpler)
kubectl -n kube-system get pods | grep etcd
# etcd-controlplane   1/1   Running   0   5d
Explanation

etcd healthchecks are essential before and after backup/restore operations. endpoint health verifies the etcd instance can accept writes. member list shows all members in the cluster (single or HA). endpoint status gives detailed info including DB size and who's the leader. All etcdctl commands need the three cert flags — without them, the connection is refused. On the CKA, always verify etcd health after restore: if the endpoint is unhealthy, check that the --data-dir path matches the restored directory.

Q5
What is the default endpoint for etcd? How do you find the actual endpoint if it's been changed? What's the difference between stacked and external etcd?
Show Answer
Answer
# Default endpoint
https://127.0.0.1:2379

# Find actual endpoint from etcd manifest
cat /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml | grep listen-client
# --listen-client-urls=https://127.0.0.1:2379,https://10.0.0.10:2379

# Or from API server (how API server connects to etcd)
cat /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml | grep etcd
# --etcd-servers=https://127.0.0.1:2379

# Stacked etcd: runs ON the control plane node (most common)
# - Static pod in /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml
# - Data stored at /var/lib/etcd
# - Managed by kubelet

# External etcd: runs on SEPARATE dedicated nodes
# - Not a static pod — standalone systemd service
# - API server connects via --etcd-servers=https://<external-ip>:2379
# - Common in production HA setups
Explanation

In CKA exam environments, etcd is almost always stacked (running on the control plane). But the exam may present external etcd scenarios for backup/restore questions. The key difference: for stacked etcd, use 127.0.0.1:2379 and certs from /etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/. For external etcd, use the external node's IP and potentially different cert paths. Always check the API server manifest's --etcd-servers flag to confirm the endpoint, and the etcd manifest's --listen-client-urls for what etcd is actually listening on.

Q6
You restored etcd from a backup, but some pods that were created AFTER the backup was taken are now missing. Is this expected? What happened?
Show Answer
Answer
# YES — this is expected behavior

# Timeline:
# T=0:   etcd backup taken (snapshot)
# T=1:   New deployment "webapp" created (pod exists)
# T=2:   New configmap "app-config" created
# T=3:   etcd restore from T=0 snapshot
#
# After restore: cluster state = T=0
# "webapp" deployment is GONE — it didn't exist at T=0
# "app-config" is GONE — same reason

# What IS preserved:
# - All objects that existed at backup time
# - All RBAC rules from backup time
# - All namespaces from backup time

# What IS NOT preserved:
# - Any objects created AFTER the backup
# - Any modifications made AFTER the backup
# - Running pod state (pods restart from desired state)
Explanation

etcd restore is a point-in-time recovery — it rolls the entire cluster state back to when the backup was taken. Everything after that point is lost. This is why regular backups are critical and why the CKA tests this scenario. In production, etcd snapshots are taken every few hours via CronJobs. The lesson: backups are only as good as their frequency. After a restore, you'll need to recreate any resources that were added between the backup and the restore. Running containers are killed and re-created based on the restored desired state.

Q7
During an etcd restore, you get the error: "Error: data-dir '/var/lib/etcd-restored' already exists". What should you do?
Show Answer
Answer
# The restore target directory must be EMPTY or non-existent

# Option 1: Use a different directory name
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot restore /opt/etcd-backup.db \
  --data-dir=/var/lib/etcd-restored-v2

# Option 2: Remove the existing directory first
sudo rm -rf /var/lib/etcd-restored
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot restore /opt/etcd-backup.db \
  --data-dir=/var/lib/etcd-restored

# IMPORTANT: Update etcd manifest to match the new path
sudo vim /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml
# Update --data-dir AND hostPath volume path

# Wait for etcd to restart with new data
# Verify:
kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep etcd
Explanation

etcdctl refuses to restore to an existing directory to prevent accidental data loss. Use a unique directory name each time. On the CKA, if you've already done one restore attempt and need to retry, either remove the directory or use a different name. Remember to update BOTH locations in the etcd manifest: the --data-dir command argument AND the hostPath.path in the volumes section. If they don't match, etcd starts with an empty data directory and the cluster appears empty.

Q8
How do you list all keys stored in etcd? How would you find a specific pod's data in etcd?
Show Answer
Answer
# List all keys in etcd (top-level prefixes)
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl get / --prefix --keys-only | head -20 \
  --cacert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt \
  --cert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt \
  --key=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key

# Kubernetes stores everything under /registry/
# /registry/pods/default/nginx
# /registry/deployments/kube-system/coredns
# /registry/services/specs/default/kubernetes
# /registry/secrets/default/my-secret

# Find a specific pod
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl get /registry/pods/default/nginx \
  --cacert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt \
  --cert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt \
  --key=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key

# Count total keys  
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl get / --prefix --keys-only \
  --cacert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt \
  --cert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt \
  --key=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key | wc -l
Explanation

etcd stores all Kubernetes objects under /registry/<resource-type>/<namespace>/<name>. The data is stored as protocol buffer (protobuf) format, not plain JSON — so the values look like binary. This is rarely needed on the CKA, but understanding the key structure helps debug issues like: "Why does my deleted namespace still show up?" (check if keys still exist). Secrets are stored base64-encoded by default in etcd. If EncryptionConfiguration is set up, they're also encrypted at rest.

Q9
What happens to the Kubernetes cluster if etcd goes down? Which operations continue to work and which don't?
Show Answer
Answer
✅ Still Works ❌ Doesn't Work
Running pods continue running Can't create new pods/resources
Existing Services route traffic Can't update or delete anything
Container processes keep running No scheduling of new pods
Node-local operations (kubelet) No self-healing (crashed pods stay down)
Explanation

etcd is the cluster's source of truth but not the runtime engine. Running workloads continue because kubelet manages containers locally. However, the API server can't write to etcd, so all mutating operations fail. Controllers can't reconcile (no self-healing), the scheduler can't schedule (no new pods), and kubectl commands that modify state fail. Read operations may work briefly from API server cache, then fail. This is why etcd availability is critical in production — and why HA clusters run 3 or 5 etcd members with Raft consensus.

Q10
Write a complete etcd backup and restore script that could be used as a CronJob for automated backups. Include verification steps.
Show Answer
Answer
#!/bin/bash
# etcd-backup.sh — Automated etcd backup script

# Variables
BACKUP_DIR="/opt/etcd-backups"
TIMESTAMP=$(date +%Y%m%d_%H%M%S)
BACKUP_FILE="${BACKUP_DIR}/etcd-snapshot-${TIMESTAMP}.db"
CACERT="/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt"
CERT="/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt"
KEY="/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key"

# Create backup directory
mkdir -p ${BACKUP_DIR}

# Take snapshot
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot save ${BACKUP_FILE} \
  --cacert=${CACERT} --cert=${CERT} --key=${KEY}

# Verify snapshot
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot status ${BACKUP_FILE} \
  --write-table

# Keep only last 7 backups
ls -tp ${BACKUP_DIR}/*.db | tail -n +8 | xargs -r rm --

echo "Backup completed: ${BACKUP_FILE}"

# To restore (manual process):
# ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot restore ${BACKUP_FILE} \
#   --data-dir=/var/lib/etcd-restored
# Then update /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml
Explanation

Automated backups are essential in production. This script takes a timestamped snapshot, verifies it, and keeps only the last 7 backups (rotation). On the CKA, you won't need to write a full script, but understanding the flow is important. The exam tests individual commands — primarily backup (save) and restore. Remember: backup is non-disruptive (online snapshot), but restore requires etcd to restart, which briefly disrupts the cluster. Always take a fresh backup BEFORE performing cluster upgrades or major changes.

Q11
How do you enable encryption at rest for Kubernetes Secrets in etcd? Write the EncryptionConfiguration and show the complete API server modification.
Show Answer
Answer
# Step 1: Generate an encryption key
ENCRYPTION_KEY=$(head -c 32 /dev/urandom | base64)
echo $ENCRYPTION_KEY

# Step 2: Create the EncryptionConfiguration file
cat <<EOF > /etc/kubernetes/enc/encryption-config.yaml
apiVersion: apiserver.config.k8s.io/v1
kind: EncryptionConfiguration
resources:
  - resources:
      - secrets
    providers:
      - aescbc:
          keys:
            - name: key1
              secret: ${ENCRYPTION_KEY}
      - identity: {}    # ← fallback: read unencrypted data
EOF

# Step 3: Add flag to kube-apiserver manifest
sudo vim /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml
# Add to command args:
#   - --encryption-provider-config=/etc/kubernetes/enc/encryption-config.yaml

# Add volumeMount:
#   - mountPath: /etc/kubernetes/enc
#     name: enc-config
#     readOnly: true

# Add volume:
#   - hostPath:
#       path: /etc/kubernetes/enc
#       type: DirectoryOrCreate
#     name: enc-config

# Step 4: Wait for API server restart (30-60s)
kubectl -n kube-system get pod kube-apiserver-controlplane -w

# Step 5: Re-encrypt all existing secrets
kubectl get secrets -A -o json | kubectl replace -f -

# Step 6: Verify encryption is working
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl get /registry/secrets/default/my-secret \
  --cacert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt \
  --cert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt \
  --key=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key
# Should show encrypted (garbled) data, not plaintext
Explanation

By default, Secrets are stored base64-encoded but NOT encrypted in etcd — anyone with etcd access can read them. The EncryptionConfiguration enables actual encryption using aescbc, aesgcm, or secretbox providers. The identity: {} provider as a fallback allows reading old unencrypted Secrets. After enabling, you must re-encrypt existing secrets with kubectl get secrets -A -o json | kubectl replace -f -. On the CKA, the key steps are: create config file → add API server flag + volume mount → restart → re-encrypt. The most common mistake is forgetting the volume mount for the config file.

Chapter 12: Managing the Control Plane

Static pods, control plane health, API server config, scheduler, controller manager.

🟡 Intermediate ⏱️ ~1 hour 🧠 Control Plane 📝 CKA EXAM

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ Static pods — what they are, how kubelet manages them, mirror pod concept
  • ✅ Control plane component architecture — how the four components interact
  • ✅ Configuring control plane components — editing manifests, adding flags safely
  • ✅ Health monitoring — checking component status with kubectl and without it
  • ✅ Troubleshooting control plane failures — systematic diagnosis when components crash
🎓 CKA Relevance
Cluster Architecture 25% Troubleshooting 30%
🎯 Control Plane Mastery is Non-Negotiable

Control plane troubleshooting appears in EVERY CKA exam. You must know where static pod manifests live, how to edit them, and what happens when you do. Combined, cluster architecture (25%) and troubleshooting (30%) cover 55% of the exam — and control plane management sits at the intersection of both domains.

12.1 Understanding Static Pods

🎯 CKA EXAM TOPIC

Static pods are a special category of pods managed directly by the kubelet on a specific node — without any involvement from the API server. The entire Kubernetes control plane runs as static pods.

Static Pods vs Regular Pods — How They're Managed
  REGULAR POD                              STATIC POD
  ┌──────────────────────────┐            ┌──────────────────────────┐
  │                          │            │                          │
  │  kubectl apply ──▶ API   │            │  YAML file placed in    │
  │  Server ──▶ etcd         │            │  /etc/kubernetes/        │
  │        │                 │            │  manifests/ directory    │
  │        ▼                 │            │        │                 │
  │  Scheduler assigns node  │            │        ▼                 │
  │        │                 │            │  kubelet detects file    │
  │        ▼                 │            │  and creates pod         │
  │  kubelet creates pod     │            │  directly on THIS node   │
  │                          │            │                          │
  │  ✅ Managed by API Server│            │  ✅ Managed by kubelet   │
  │  ✅ Can run on any node  │            │  ❌ Only on local node   │
  │  ✅ kubectl delete works │            │  ❌ kubectl delete = noop│
  │  ✅ Can be scaled        │            │  ❌ Cannot be scaled     │
  └──────────────────────────┘            └──────────────────────────┘

  API server creates a "mirror pod" for each static pod so it
  appears in kubectl get pods — but it's READ-ONLY (a shadow copy).
📦
Why Static Pods Exist

The control plane needs to run before the API server exists. You can't use kubectl apply to create the API server because kubectl talks to the API server! Static pods solve this chicken-and-egg problem — kubelet reads YAML files directly from disk.

🪞
Mirror Pods

When the API server starts, kubelet creates "mirror" pods — read-only representations of static pods visible via kubectl. They appear in kubectl get pods -n kube-system with the node name appended (e.g., etcd-controlplane).

The Static Pod Lifecycle

1
File Placed

Admin places a pod manifest YAML in /etc/kubernetes/manifests/ (or the configured staticPodPath).

2
Kubelet Detects

Kubelet watches the directory. Within seconds, it reads the new manifest and starts the pod's containers via the container runtime.

3
Mirror Created

If the API server is running, kubelet creates a mirror pod so the static pod appears in kubectl output (with -nodename suffix).

4
Auto-Restart

If the container crashes, kubelet automatically restarts it. If the manifest is edited, kubelet recreates the pod with updated config (~30-60 seconds).

5
File Removed

When the manifest file is deleted from the directory, kubelet terminates the pod. This is the only way to permanently delete a static pod.

Static Pod Identification Cheat Sheet

Feature Static Pod Regular Pod
Name pattern component-nodename deploy-hash-hash
ownerReferences.kind Node ReplicaSet / DaemonSet / Job
config.source annotation file api
Deleted with kubectl? ❌ Recreated immediately ✅ Permanently deleted
Managed by kubelet (local) API server (central)
Scheduling Always on local node Placed by scheduler
identify-static-pods.sh 
# Quick ways to identify static pods:

# 1. Name ends with node name
kubectl get pods -n kube-system
# etcd-controlplane                    ← static (node suffix)
# kube-apiserver-controlplane          ← static
# coredns-5d78c9869d-x7k2l            ← regular (hash suffix)

# 2. Check ownerReferences
kubectl get pod etcd-controlplane -n kube-system \
  -o jsonpath='{.metadata.ownerReferences[0].kind}'
# Node  ← static!

# 3. Find the static pod path
cat /var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml | grep staticPodPath
# staticPodPath: /etc/kubernetes/manifests

# 4. List all static pod manifests
ls /etc/kubernetes/manifests/
# etcd.yaml  kube-apiserver.yaml
# kube-controller-manager.yaml  kube-scheduler.yaml

12.2 Control Plane Components — How They Interact

🎯 CKA EXAM TOPIC

The four control plane components form a tightly coupled system. Understanding how they interact — and what happens when each one fails — is essential for CKA troubleshooting.

Control Plane Communication Flow
 ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
 │                    CONTROL PLANE NODE                                │
 │                                                                     │
 │  ┌──────────────────┐     ┌──────────────────────────────────────┐  │
 │  │   kube-apiserver │◀───▶│              etcd                    │  │
 │  │   :6443 (HTTPS)  │     │  :2379 (client) / :2380 (peer)      │  │
 │  │                  │     │  Only API server talks to etcd       │  │
 │  │  ┌─────────────┐│     │  All state stored here               │  │
 │  │  │ AuthN/AuthZ ││     └──────────────────────────────────────┘  │
 │  │  │ Admission   ││                                               │
 │  │  │ Validation  ││                                               │
 │  │  └─────────────┘│                                               │
 │  └───────▲──▲──▲───┘                                               │
 │          │  │  │                                                    │
 │     ┌────┘  │  └────┐                                              │
 │     │       │       │                                              │
 │  ┌──▼───┐ ┌─▼─────┐ ┌─▼──────────────────┐                        │
 │  │Sched.│ │Ctrl   │ │ cloud-controller   │  ← optional            │
 │  │:10259│ │Mgr    │ │   -manager         │                        │
 │  │      │ │:10257 │ │ (EKS/GKE/AKS only) │                        │
 │  │Watches│ │Watches│ └────────────────────┘                        │
 │  │unsched│ │desired│                                               │
 │  │pods   │ │vs     │  All components WATCH the API server          │
 │  │→assign│ │actual │  API server is the SINGLE point of contact    │
 │  │node   │ │→fix   │  etcd is ONLY accessed by API server          │
 │  └──────┘ └───────┘                                                │
 │                                                                     │
 └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
         ▲                          ▲
         │ kubelet heartbeats       │ kubectl commands
         │ :10250                   │ :6443
 ┌───────┴──────────────────────────┴───────────────────┐
 │                    WORKER NODES                       │
 │  kubelet → container runtime → pods                  │
 │  kube-proxy → iptables/IPVS rules                    │
 └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Component Responsibilities & Failure Impact

⚙️ Control Plane Component Matrix

👨‍✈️ kube-apiserver
Role: Front door for ALL cluster communication. Authenticates, authorizes, validates, and persists objects to etcd. If down: kubectl stops working, no component can communicate, cluster is effectively blind. Port: 6443
💾 etcd
Role: Distributed key-value store holding ALL cluster state (every pod, service, secret, configmap). If down: API server can't read/write state — cluster becomes read-only then unresponsive. Port: 2379 (client), 2380 (peer)
🎯 kube-scheduler
Role: Watches for unscheduled pods, assigns them to nodes based on resources/affinity/taints. If down: Existing pods run fine, but new pods stay Pending forever. Port: 10259
🔄 kube-controller-manager
Role: Runs reconciliation loops — ReplicaSet, Node, Job, Endpoint, ServiceAccount controllers. If down: No self-healing, failed pods aren't replaced, scaling stops, terminated namespaces pile up. Port: 10257

What Happens When Each Component Fails?

❌ Component Failure Symptoms
  • API server: kubectl returns "connection refused," all other components lose contact
  • etcd: API server returns 500 errors, can't create/update/delete anything
  • Scheduler: New pods stay Pending (no nodeName assigned)
  • Controller-mgr: Crashed pods not replaced, Deployments don't scale, no self-healing
✅ What Still Works During Failure
  • API server down: Existing pods keep running (kubelet manages them locally)
  • etcd down: Existing pods run, Services work (iptables rules cached)
  • Scheduler down: All existing pods unaffected (already assigned to nodes)
  • Controller-mgr down: All currently healthy pods keep running normally
⚠️ CKA Key Insight: Component Independence

Each control plane component is a separate process (static pod). They can fail independently. The CKA often breaks one component and asks you to identify which one is broken and fix it. Understanding the specific symptoms of each failure is how you diagnose fast under time pressure.

12.3 Configuring Control Plane Components

🎯 CKA EXAM TOPIC

All control plane components are configured through their static pod manifests in /etc/kubernetes/manifests/. Understanding the manifest structure and key flags is essential for the CKA.

Static Pod Manifest Structure

/etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: kube-apiserver
  namespace: kube-system
  labels:
    component: kube-apiserver
    tier: control-plane
spec:
  containers:
  - command:                        # ← FLAGS LIVE HERE
    - kube-apiserver
    - --advertise-address=192.168.1.10
    - --allow-privileged=true
    - --authorization-mode=Node,RBAC
    - --client-ca-file=/etc/kubernetes/pki/ca.crt
    - --enable-admission-plugins=NodeRestriction
    - --etcd-cafile=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt
    - --etcd-certfile=/etc/kubernetes/pki/apiserver-etcd-client.crt
    - --etcd-keyfile=/etc/kubernetes/pki/apiserver-etcd-client.key
    - --etcd-servers=https://127.0.0.1:2379
    - --service-cluster-ip-range=10.96.0.0/12
    - --tls-cert-file=/etc/kubernetes/pki/apiserver.crt
    - --tls-private-key-file=/etc/kubernetes/pki/apiserver.key
    image: registry.k8s.io/kube-apiserver:v1.32.0
    volumeMounts:
    - mountPath: /etc/kubernetes/pki  # ← Cert files
      name: k8s-certs
    - mountPath: /etc/ca-certificates
      name: ca-certs
  hostNetwork: true                   # ← Uses node network
  volumes:
  - hostPath:
      path: /etc/kubernetes/pki
    name: k8s-certs

Key API Server Flags to Know

Flag Purpose CKA Relevance
--authorization-mode Authorization methods (Node, RBAC, Webhook) Check/modify RBAC is enabled
--enable-admission-plugins Admission controllers (NodeRestriction, PodSecurity) May need to add/enable plugins
--etcd-servers etcd endpoint URL Identify etcd connection config
--service-cluster-ip-range IP range for ClusterIP Services Networking troubleshooting
--etcd-cafile/certfile/keyfile TLS certs for etcd communication etcd backup command needs these
--audit-log-path Enable API audit logging Security/compliance tasks

Safe Workflow for Editing Control Plane Manifests

📋Backup manifest
→
✏️Edit YAML file
→
💾Save & exit
→
⏳Wait 30-60s
→
✅Verify restart
safe-edit-workflow.sh 
# CKA-safe workflow for modifying control plane components

# Step 1: ALWAYS backup before editing
cp /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml \
   /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml.bak

# Step 2: Edit the manifest
sudo vim /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml
# Add/modify flags under spec.containers[0].command

# Step 3: Save and exit (:wq)
# kubelet auto-detects the change and restarts the pod

# Step 4: Watch for restart (30-60 seconds)
kubectl -n kube-system get pod kube-apiserver-controlplane -w
# NAME                            READY  STATUS   RESTARTS
# kube-apiserver-controlplane     1/1    Running  1  ← restarted!

# Step 5: Verify the change took effect
kubectl -n kube-system get pod kube-apiserver-controlplane \
  -o jsonpath='{.spec.containers[0].command}' | tr ',' '\n'

# ⚠️ If something went wrong:
# - kubectl stops responding = you broke the manifest
# - Use: crictl ps -a | grep apiserver
# - Use: crictl logs <container-id>
# - Restore backup: cp kube-apiserver.yaml.bak kube-apiserver.yaml
🚨 DANGER: Breaking the API Server

If you introduce a syntax error or invalid flag in kube-apiserver.yaml, the API server will crash and kubectl will stop working entirely. You must use crictl (container runtime CLI) and journalctl to debug. Always keep a backup of the manifest before editing on the CKA exam.

12.4 Control Plane Health Monitoring

As a CKA administrator, you need to quickly assess control plane health using multiple methods — some work via kubectl, others work even when the API server is down.

Health Check Methods — From Quick to Deep

⚡
Level 1: Quick Check (5 seconds)
quick-check.sh 
# All 4 components Running?
kubectl get pods -n kube-system \
  | grep -E "apiserver|etcd|scheduler|controller"

# Quick component status
kubectl get cs  # deprecated but fast
🔍
Level 2: Deep Check (30 seconds)
deep-check.sh 
# Check logs for errors
kubectl -n kube-system logs \
  kube-apiserver-controlplane --tail=10

# Check restart counts
kubectl -n kube-system get pods \
  -o custom-columns=\
NAME:.metadata.name,RESTARTS:.status.containerStatuses[0].restartCount

Health Check Endpoints

Component Health Endpoint Secure Port
kube-apiserver curl -k https://localhost:6443/healthz 6443
kube-scheduler curl -k https://localhost:10259/healthz 10259
kube-controller-manager curl -k https://localhost:10257/healthz 10257
etcd curl -k https://localhost:2379/health 2379 / 2380

When kubectl Is Dead — Emergency Diagnostics

kubectl-less Troubleshooting Decision Tree
  kubectl get pods -n kube-system
       │
       ├── ✅ Works → Check pod status, logs, events
       │
       └── ❌ "Connection refused" / timeout
            │
            ├── SSH to control plane node
            │
            ├── 1. systemctl status kubelet
            │       ├── Active: active (running) → kubelet OK
            │       └── Active: inactive/failed → systemctl start kubelet
            │
            ├── 2. crictl ps -a | grep kube
            │       ├── All containers Running → Network issue?
            │       ├── Some containers Exited → Check exit codes
            │       └── No containers → Manifests missing?
            │
            ├── 3. crictl logs <container-id>
            │       └── Read error messages → Fix the manifest
            │
            ├── 4. ls /etc/kubernetes/manifests/
            │       ├── All 4 .yaml files present → Check content
            │       └── Missing files! → Restore from backup
            │
            └── 5. journalctl -u kubelet --no-pager | tail -50
                    └── kubelet errors reveal the root cause
💡 CKA Pro Tip: Know Your Fallback Tools

When kubectl is down, your lifeline tools are: crictl ps -a (list all containers), crictl logs (read container logs), journalctl -u kubelet (kubelet logs), and direct file inspection of /etc/kubernetes/manifests/. Practice these until they're muscle memory — the CKA will break the API server and expect you to fix it without kubectl.

12.5 Troubleshooting Control Plane Failures

🎯 CKA EXAM TOPIC

The CKA frequently introduces intentional control plane failures. Here are the most common scenarios and systematic fixes.

Common CKA Control Plane Failure Scenarios

Scenario Symptom Root Cause Fix
Manifest typo Component CrashLoopBackOff Invalid flag or wrong image tag Fix the YAML in /etc/kubernetes/manifests/
Wrong cert path "unable to load certificate" Flag points to non-existent cert file Verify file exists, fix path
Manifest renamed Component missing from kubectl get pods File renamed to .bak or .disabled Rename back to .yaml
kubelet stopped ALL static pods down, kubectl dead systemctl status kubelet = inactive systemctl start kubelet
Disk full etcd crash, cluster unresponsive df -h shows 100% on /var Clear space, especially old logs/images
Port conflict Component won't start, "bind: address in use" Another process using the same port lsof -i :6443, kill conflict

Systematic Troubleshooting Flowchart

1
Identify the Broken Component

kubectl get pods -n kube-system or crictl ps -a | grep kube. Which component is CrashLoopBackOff or missing?

2
Read the Logs

kubectl -n kube-system logs <pod> --previous or crictl logs <container-id>. The error message tells you exactly what's wrong.

3
Inspect the Manifest

cat /etc/kubernetes/manifests/<component>.yaml. Look for typos in flags, wrong image tags, incorrect cert paths.

4
Fix and Wait

Edit the manifest with vim. Save. Wait 30-60 seconds for kubelet to detect the change and restart the pod.

5
Verify Recovery

kubectl get pods -n kube-system — component should be Running with READY 1/1. Check RESTARTS count to confirm restart.

📖 CKA Manifest Editing Checklist

When editing a control plane manifest on the exam, verify these before saving:

  • ✅ YAML indentation is correct (spaces, not tabs)
  • ✅ Flag format is --flag=value (with double dash and equals)
  • ✅ Each flag is a separate list item under command:
  • ✅ Image tag matches the expected Kubernetes version
  • ✅ Certificate paths point to files that actually exist in /etc/kubernetes/pki/
  • ✅ Volume mounts match the paths referenced by flags

⚠️ CKA Gotcha: Control Plane Management Traps

  • Static pods auto-restart — Edit a manifest in /etc/kubernetes/manifests/ and kubelet restarts the pod automatically. No kubectl apply needed. But if you make a syntax error, the component goes DOWN
  • API server down = kubectl dead — If you break kube-apiserver.yaml, you can't use kubectl. Use crictl ps and crictl logs to debug instead
  • Scheduler and Controller Manager — Both connect to the API server. If they crash, pods won't be scheduled or self-heal, but existing pods keep running
  • Static pod names include node suffix — A static pod manifest named myapp.yaml appears as myapp-<nodename> in kubectl get pods. Don't confuse it with a normal pod
  • kubelet config for static pods — The static pod path is configured in kubelet: staticPodPath in /var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml. The exam may change it

CKA Speed Tip — Control plane health check in 10 seconds:
kubectl get pods -n kube-system — all 4 components should be Running (1/1)
kubectl get cs — (deprecated but quick) scheduler, controller-manager, etcd status
If a component is missing, check: ls /etc/kubernetes/manifests/ + crictl ps -a

⚡ Control plane inspection commands: kubectl -n kube-system logs kube-apiserver-<node> — API server logs
kubectl -n kube-system logs kube-scheduler-<node> — scheduler logs
crictl ps -a | grep kube — check containers when kubectl is down
crictl logs <container-id> — read logs without kubectl
systemctl restart kubelet — force kubelet to re-read manifests
📝 Chapter 12 Practice Drill

Timed challenge (target: 5 minutes):

  • 1. Create a static pod: place a pod manifest in /etc/kubernetes/manifests/test-static.yaml
  • 2. Verify it appears: kubectl get pods -A | grep test-static
  • 3. Break the API server: add an invalid flag to /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml
  • 4. Observe: kubectl stops working. Use crictl ps -a to see the crash-looping container
  • 5. Fix the manifest → API server auto-recovers within 30 seconds
  • 6. Clean up: remove test-static.yaml from manifests dir

Key insight: When kubectl is dead, crictl + journalctl + direct manifest editing are your lifeline.

📝 Chapter 12 — CKA Practice Questions

Q1
What are static pods? Where are their manifests stored, and how does the kubelet know where to look?
Show Answer
Answer
# Static pods are managed directly by kubelet — NOT by the API server
# They exist only on the node where their manifest file lives

# Default manifest directory:
/etc/kubernetes/manifests/

# Control plane static pods:
ls /etc/kubernetes/manifests/
# etcd.yaml
# kube-apiserver.yaml
# kube-controller-manager.yaml
# kube-scheduler.yaml

# Finding the static pod path:
# Option 1: Check kubelet config
cat /var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml | grep staticPodPath
# staticPodPath: /etc/kubernetes/manifests

# Option 2: Check kubelet process args
ps aux | grep kubelet | grep static
# or: systemctl cat kubelet | grep config

# Create a static pod:
cat <<EOF > /etc/kubernetes/manifests/my-static.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: my-static
spec:
  containers:
  - name: web
    image: nginx
EOF
# kubelet automatically creates the pod
Explanation

Static pods are pods managed by the kubelet daemon on a specific node, without the API server observing them. The kubelet watches the staticPodPath directory and creates/deletes pods when files appear/disappear. On the CKA, you may need to: (1) create a static pod by placing a YAML in the manifests directory, (2) find the manifest path if it's been changed from default, or (3) identify which pods are static pods. Static pods have the node name appended: my-static-controlplane. They can't be deleted with kubectl delete — you must remove the manifest file.

Q2
Create a static pod named test-web running nginx:1.25 on the control plane node. How do you verify it's running and how do you delete it?
Show Answer
Answer
# Step 1: Create the manifest file
cat <<EOF > /etc/kubernetes/manifests/test-web.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: test-web
  labels:
    app: test-web
spec:
  containers:
  - name: nginx
    image: nginx:1.25
    ports:
    - containerPort: 80
EOF

# Step 2: Verify (kubelet creates it within seconds)
kubectl get pods -A | grep test-web
# NAMESPACE   NAME                      READY   STATUS    RESTARTS
# default     test-web-controlplane     1/1     Running   0

# Note the "-controlplane" suffix — that's the node name

# Step 3: Delete the static pod
# ❌ This does NOT work permanently:
kubectl delete pod test-web-controlplane
# kubelet will immediately recreate it!

# ✅ This works:
rm /etc/kubernetes/manifests/test-web.yaml
# kubelet detects file removal and deletes the pod
Explanation

Static pods cannot be controlled through the API server — kubectl delete removes the mirror pod, but kubelet immediately recreates it from the manifest file. To truly delete a static pod, you must remove or move the YAML file from the staticPodPath. On the CKA, if asked to "create a static pod on node01," you must SSH to node01 first, find its staticPodPath (may differ from default), and place the manifest there. The pod name in kubectl get pods always has -<nodename> appended.

Q3
The kube-scheduler is not working — new pods are stuck in Pending with "no nodes are available" even though nodes are Ready. How do you investigate?
Show Answer
Answer
# Step 1: Check if scheduler pod is running
kubectl -n kube-system get pods | grep scheduler
# If not listed or CrashLoopBackOff → scheduler is broke

# Step 2: Check scheduler logs
kubectl -n kube-system logs kube-scheduler-controlplane

# Step 3: Check scheduler manifest
cat /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-scheduler.yaml
# Look for: wrong image, typo in flags, wrong cert path

# Step 4: Common scheduler issues:
# a) Manifest file deleted or corrupted
ls -la /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-scheduler.yaml

# b) Scheduler can't reach API server
# Check --kubeconfig flag points to valid config

# c) Wrong port or address
# Default: --bind-address=0.0.0.0 --secure-port=10259

# Step 5: If scheduler manifest is missing, recreate:
# Check another working cluster or use kubeadm defaults
# Or restore from etcd backup
Explanation

The scheduler's job is to assign pods to nodes. If it's down, pods get created (they exist in etcd) but stay Pending because no node is assigned. The CKA frequently tests this: they'll break the scheduler manifest and ask you to fix it. Diagnosis: kubectl get pods -n kube-system to see if the scheduler pod exists. If it does exist but is crashing, check its logs. If it doesn't exist, check the manifest directory. Common gotcha: the scheduler manifest might be renamed (e.g., kube-scheduler.yaml.bak) — kubelet ignores non-.yaml files.

Q4
How do you identify which pods in the kube-system namespace are static pods vs regular pods?
Show Answer
Answer
# Method 1: Static pods have the node name as suffix
kubectl get pods -n kube-system
# etcd-controlplane                  ← static (has node name)
# kube-apiserver-controlplane        ← static
# kube-scheduler-controlplane        ← static
# kube-controller-manager-controlplane ← static
# coredns-5d78c9869d-abc12           ← regular (has ReplicaSet hash)
# kube-proxy-4x8n2                   ← regular (part of DaemonSet)

# Method 2: Check ownerReferences
kubectl get pod etcd-controlplane -n kube-system \
  -o jsonpath='{.metadata.ownerReferences[0].kind}'
# Node   ← Static pods are owned by the Node object

kubectl get pod coredns-5d78c9869d-abc12 -n kube-system \
  -o jsonpath='{.metadata.ownerReferences[0].kind}'
# ReplicaSet   ← Regular pod

# Method 3: Check annotations
kubectl get pod etcd-controlplane -n kube-system \
  -o jsonpath='{.metadata.annotations.kubernetes\.io/config\.source}'
# file   ← Created from a file (static pod)
Explanation

Static pods have three distinguishing features: (1) their name ends with -<nodename>, (2) their ownerReferences shows kind Node instead of ReplicaSet/DaemonSet, (3) they have the annotation kubernetes.io/config.source: file. On the CKA, the quickest visual check is the name suffix — all four control plane components (apiserver, etcd, scheduler, controller-manager) are static pods with the node name appended. CoreDNS and kube-proxy are regular pods managed by Deployments/DaemonSets.

Q5
You need to add a custom flag --profiling=false to the kube-controller-manager. Walk through the complete process.
Show Answer
Answer
# Step 1: Find the manifest
sudo vim /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-controller-manager.yaml

# Step 2: Add the flag to the command section
spec:
  containers:
  - command:
    - kube-controller-manager
    - --bind-address=127.0.0.1
    - --cluster-signing-cert-file=/etc/kubernetes/pki/ca.crt
    - --profiling=false              # ← Add this line
    # ... existing flags ...

# Step 3: Save and exit (:wq)
# kubelet detects the change and restarts the pod

# Step 4: Verify the pod restarted
kubectl -n kube-system get pod kube-controller-manager-controlplane
# RESTARTS count should increment

# Step 5: Verify the flag is applied
kubectl -n kube-system get pod kube-controller-manager-controlplane \
  -o jsonpath='{.spec.containers[0].command}' | tr ',' '\n' | grep profiling
# --profiling=false
Explanation

Modifying control plane components on the CKA always follows this pattern: edit the static pod manifest → save → wait for restart → verify. The YAML structure has the component flags as a list under spec.containers[0].command. Common mistakes: (1) adding the flag at the wrong indentation level, (2) using = vs space syntax incorrectly (use --flag=value), (3) forgetting that changes take 30-60 seconds to apply. If the pod doesn't restart, check crictl ps -a — a YAML error will prevent the pod from starting.

Q6
All four control plane components suddenly stop working. You can SSH to the control plane node but kubectl returns "connection refused." What is your debugging checklist?
Show Answer
Answer
# Without kubectl, use system-level tools:

# 1. Check kubelet (manages all static pods)
sudo systemctl status kubelet
sudo journalctl -u kubelet --no-pager -n 30

# 2. Check container runtime
sudo systemctl status containerd
sudo crictl ps -a | grep kube

# 3. Check if manifests exist
ls -la /etc/kubernetes/manifests/
# Should have: etcd.yaml, kube-apiserver.yaml,
#   kube-controller-manager.yaml, kube-scheduler.yaml

# 4. Check individual containers
sudo crictl ps -a
# Look for Exited containers and their exit codes

# 5. Read container logs
sudo crictl logs <apiserver-container-id>
sudo crictl logs <etcd-container-id>

# 6. Check system resources  
df -h           # Disk full?
free -h         # Memory exhaustion?
top             # CPU overload?

# 7. Common root causes:
# - Disk full: /var/lib/etcd grows indefinitely
# - Kubelet stopped: systemctl start kubelet
# - Manifests deleted: restore from backup
# - Cert expired: check /etc/kubernetes/pki/
Explanation

When all control plane components are down, the problem is usually kubelet, containerd, or a system-level issue (disk, memory). Start with kubelet — it manages all static pods. If kubelet is running but containers aren't, check crictl ps -a for container status and logs. If kubelet is down, journalctl -u kubelet shows why. Disk full (/var/lib/etcd growth) is a common cause in long-running clusters. On the CKA, this is the ultimate troubleshooting scenario — practice the kubectl-less debugging flow until it's second nature.

Q7
What is the kubelet's staticPodPath? How do you find it if it's been changed from the default, and how do you change it?
Show Answer
Answer
# Default staticPodPath
/etc/kubernetes/manifests

# Method 1: Check kubelet config file
cat /var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml | grep staticPodPath
# staticPodPath: /etc/kubernetes/manifests

# Method 2: Check kubelet process arguments
ps aux | grep kubelet | grep -oP 'config=\S+'
# Shows the kubelet config file path
# Then read that config file

# Method 3: Check systemd unit file
systemctl cat kubelet
# Shows ExecStart command and --config flag

# To change the static pod path:
# 1. Edit /var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml
#    staticPodPath: /opt/my-static-pods

# 2. Restart kubelet
sudo systemctl restart kubelet

# 3. Place manifests in the new path
sudo mv /etc/kubernetes/manifests/*.yaml /opt/my-static-pods/
Explanation

The CKA sometimes changes the staticPodPath to test if you know how to find it. Don't assume /etc/kubernetes/manifests/ — always verify. The path is in the kubelet config file, typically at /var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml. The kubelet config location itself is passed via --config flag in the kubelet systemd unit. If asked to "create a static pod on node01," first SSH to node01, find its kubelet config, read the staticPodPath, then place your manifest there.

Q8
What is the difference between the kube-controller-manager and the kube-scheduler? What happens if each one fails individually?
Show Answer
Answer
Aspect kube-scheduler kube-controller-manager
Job Assign pods to nodes Run reconciliation loops
If down New pods stay Pending No self-healing, no scaling
Running pods Unaffected Unaffected (until they crash)
Controllers N/A ReplicaSet, Namespace, Node, Job, SA, Endpoint, etc.
Explanation

The scheduler only handles initial pod placement — after a pod is assigned to a node, the scheduler's job is done. The controller-manager runs dozens of control loops: the ReplicaSet controller ensures desired replica count, the Node controller marks unreachable nodes, the Job controller manages batch jobs, etc. If the scheduler fails, existing pods are fine but new pods wait forever. If the controller-manager fails, the cluster can't self-heal — crashed pods aren't replaced, scaling doesn't work, and terminated namespaces aren't cleaned up.

Q9
How do you check the health of all control plane components? Show both kubectl and non-kubectl methods.
Show Answer
Answer
# Method 1: kubectl — check pods in kube-system
kubectl get pods -n kube-system
# All should show Running, READY 1/1

# Method 2: componentstatuses (deprecated but quick)
kubectl get componentstatuses  # or: kubectl get cs
# NAME                 STATUS    MESSAGE   ERROR
# scheduler            Healthy   ok
# controller-manager   Healthy   ok
# etcd-0               Healthy   ok

# Method 3: Health check endpoints
curl -k https://localhost:6443/healthz   # API server
curl -k https://localhost:10259/healthz  # Scheduler
curl -k https://localhost:10257/healthz  # Controller Manager

# Method 4: Without kubectl (when API server is down)
crictl ps | grep kube
# Shows running control plane containers

systemctl status kubelet
# kubelet manages all static pods

journalctl -u kubelet --no-pager -n 20
# kubelet logs show component status
Explanation

On the CKA, kubectl get pods -n kube-system is the fastest health check. Each control plane component exposes a /healthz endpoint for liveness checks. kubectl get cs is deprecated since v1.19 but still works and gives a quick overview. Know the port numbers: API server (6443), scheduler (10259), controller-manager (10257), etcd (2379). When kubectl is unavailable, fall back to crictl ps and journalctl. A healthy cluster has all four static pods running with 0 recent restarts.

Q10
You notice that the kube-controller-manager keeps restarting (high RESTARTS count). How do you investigate the cause?
Show Answer
Answer
# Step 1: Check current status and restart count
kubectl -n kube-system get pod kube-controller-manager-controlplane
# READY   STATUS             RESTARTS
# 0/1     CrashLoopBackOff   15

# Step 2: Check events
kubectl -n kube-system describe pod kube-controller-manager-controlplane
# Look at Events section for error messages

# Step 3: Check logs (current attempt)
kubectl -n kube-system logs kube-controller-manager-controlplane

# Step 4: Check logs from previous crash
kubectl -n kube-system logs kube-controller-manager-controlplane --previous

# Step 5: Common causes:
# a) Wrong certificate path in manifest
#    Error: "unable to load client certificate"
#    Fix: check --kubeconfig and cert file paths

# b) Can't reach API server
#    Error: "dial tcp 127.0.0.1:6443: connection refused"
#    Fix: check if API server is running first

# c) Invalid flag in manifest
#    Error: "unknown flag: --typo-flag"
#    Fix: remove or correct the flag

# Step 6: Fix the manifest
sudo vim /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-controller-manager.yaml
# Wait 30-60s for recovery
Explanation

CrashLoopBackOff on a control plane component means it starts, fails, and kubelet keeps restarting it. The --previous flag shows logs from the last crashed instance — crucial because the current instance might have started too recently to show useful logs. On the CKA, control plane troubleshooting follows a consistent pattern: get pods → describe pod → logs --previous → identify error → fix manifest → wait for recovery. The most common CKA trick: they modify a manifest with a typo or wrong path and ask you to fix it.

Q11
You need to enable the PodSecurity admission plugin and configure API audit logging on the kube-apiserver. Walk through the complete process including all required volume mounts.
Show Answer
Answer
# Step 1: Backup the manifest
cp /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml \
   /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml.bak

# Step 2: Create the audit policy file
mkdir -p /etc/kubernetes/audit
cat <<EOF > /etc/kubernetes/audit/audit-policy.yaml
apiVersion: audit.k8s.io/v1
kind: Policy
rules:
- level: Metadata
  resources:
  - group: ""
    resources: ["secrets", "configmaps"]
- level: RequestResponse
  resources:
  - group: ""
    resources: ["pods"]
- level: None
  resources:
  - group: ""
    resources: ["events"]
EOF

# Step 3: Edit the API server manifest
sudo vim /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml

# Add these flags under spec.containers[0].command:
    - --enable-admission-plugins=NodeRestriction,PodSecurity
    - --audit-log-path=/var/log/kubernetes/audit.log
    - --audit-policy-file=/etc/kubernetes/audit/audit-policy.yaml
    - --audit-log-maxage=30
    - --audit-log-maxbackup=10
    - --audit-log-maxsize=100

# Add volume mounts:
    volumeMounts:
    - mountPath: /etc/kubernetes/audit
      name: audit-policy
      readOnly: true
    - mountPath: /var/log/kubernetes
      name: audit-log

# Add corresponding volumes:
  volumes:
  - hostPath:
      path: /etc/kubernetes/audit
      type: DirectoryOrCreate
    name: audit-policy
  - hostPath:
      path: /var/log/kubernetes
      type: DirectoryOrCreate
    name: audit-log

# Step 4: Wait 30-60s, then verify
kubectl -n kube-system get pod kube-apiserver-controlplane
# Should be Running with incremented RESTARTS

# Step 5: Verify admission plugin
kubectl -n kube-system get pod kube-apiserver-controlplane \
  -o jsonpath='{.spec.containers[0].command}' | tr ',' '\n' \
  | grep admission
# --enable-admission-plugins=NodeRestriction,PodSecurity

# Step 6: Verify audit log is being written
ls -la /var/log/kubernetes/audit.log
Explanation

API server configuration on the CKA requires modifying three sections of the manifest: (1) command flags — add the new flags to the container command list, (2) volumeMounts — make host paths available inside the container, (3) volumes — define the hostPath sources.
