The most common mistake is adding flags that reference files inside the container (like --audit-policy-file) without adding the corresponding volume mount — the API server crashes because it can't find the file. Always add new volume mounts and volumes together with any flag that references a file path. For admission plugins, you append to the existing --enable-admission-plugins comma-separated list rather than adding a second copy of the flag.

Q12
Create a static pod named nginx-node01 running nginx:alpine on node01 (not the control plane). The static pod path on node01 has been changed from the default. Show the complete process.
Show Answer
Answer
# Step 1: SSH to node01
ssh node01

# Step 2: Find the kubelet config file path
ps aux | grep kubelet | grep -oP 'config=\S+'
# --config=/var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml
# (or check: systemctl cat kubelet)

# Step 3: Read the config to find staticPodPath
cat /var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml | grep staticPodPath
# staticPodPath: /opt/static-pods    ← NOT the default!

# Step 4: Create the static pod manifest in the correct path
cat <<EOF > /opt/static-pods/nginx-node01.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: nginx-node01
  labels:
    app: nginx-static
spec:
  containers:
  - name: nginx
    image: nginx:alpine
    ports:
    - containerPort: 80
EOF

# Step 5: Exit back to control plane
exit

# Step 6: Verify from control plane (wait a few seconds)
kubectl get pods -A | grep nginx-node01
# default   nginx-node01-node01   1/1   Running   0
#                       ↑ node name appended

# Alternative: if kubectl shows the pod but it's not starting,
# SSH back and check kubelet logs:
ssh node01
journalctl -u kubelet --no-pager -n 20 | grep nginx
Explanation

This is a classic CKA exam scenario. The key steps most candidates miss: (1) SSH to the target node first — you can't create a static pod remotely, (2) find the actual staticPodPath — never assume /etc/kubernetes/manifests/ because the exam often changes it, (3) check kubelet config, not the kubelet process args — the path is in the config file referenced by --config. The pod name in kubectl get pods will be nginx-node01-node01 (manifest name + node name suffix). If the pod doesn't appear within 10 seconds, SSH back and check journalctl -u kubelet for YAML parsing errors.

🟡 Part 4: Workloads & Scheduling Chapters 13-16

Chapter 13: Deployments, ReplicaSets, and Scaling

Deployments are the heart of Kubernetes workload management. In this chapter you'll master the complete lifecycle — creating, scaling, updating, and rolling back applications. We'll trace how a Deployment creates a ReplicaSet, which in turn creates Pods, and how the Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA) adds elastic scaling. Every example uses our anihpj/jobpost Django app so you see real-world patterns, not toy demos.

🟡 Intermediate ⏱️ ~1.5 hours 🚀 Workloads 📝 CKA EXAM

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ Create Deployments imperatively and declaratively — know the full YAML anatomy
  • ✅ Perform rolling updates and instant rollbacks using kubectl rollout
  • ✅ Understand how Deployments → ReplicaSets → Pods chain together under the hood
  • ✅ Scale manually with kubectl scale and automatically with HPA
  • ✅ Choose the right deployment strategy — RollingUpdate vs Recreate
  • ✅ Control update pace with maxSurge and maxUnavailable
🎓 CKA Relevance
Workloads & Scheduling 15% Troubleshooting 30%
Deployments Are CKA Exam Bread & Butter

Nearly every CKA exam has at least 2-3 tasks involving Deployments. You must be fast at:

  • kubectl create deployment NAME --image=IMG --replicas=N — imperative creation
  • kubectl set image deployment/NAME container=IMG:TAG — trigger rolling update
  • kubectl rollout undo deployment/NAME --to-revision=N — rollback
  • kubectl scale deployment/NAME --replicas=N — manual scaling
  • kubectl rollout status deployment/NAME — verify update progress

13.1 Deployments — Complete Lifecycle

A Deployment is Kubernetes' recommended way to manage stateless applications. It provides declarative updates, self-healing, scaling, and version history — all in one resource.

📖 Key Concept: Declarative vs Imperative

Kubernetes supports both styles. Imperative: kubectl create deployment anihpj --image=anihpj:v1. Declarative: kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml. On the CKA, use imperative commands to generate YAML fast, then edit if needed.

Deployment
🎛️ Manages ReplicaSets
📦 Creates Pods via RS
🔄 Rolling Updates
⏪ Rollback History
📈 Scale In/Out
✅ Self-Healing

a) Creating a Deployment

Imperative — Fast Exam Creation 
# Create deployment (CKA exam speed)
kubectl create deployment anihpj --image=anihpj:v1 --replicas=3

# Generate YAML without creating (save to file, then edit)
kubectl create deployment anihpj --image=anihpj:v1 --replicas=3 \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml > anihpj-deploy.yaml

# Verify
kubectl get deployments
kubectl describe deployment anihpj
anihpj-deployment.yaml 
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    project: jobpost
spec:
  replicas: 3                    # Desired number of pods
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj                # Must match template labels
  strategy:
    type: RollingUpdate
    rollingUpdate:
      maxSurge: 1                # 1 extra pod during update
      maxUnavailable: 0          # Never fewer than 3 running
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj              # Must match selector
        version: v1
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: django
        image: anihpj:v1
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8000
        resources:
          requests:
            cpu: "250m"
            memory: "256Mi"
          limits:
            cpu: "500m"
            memory: "512Mi"
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /health
            port: 8000
          initialDelaySeconds: 5
          periodSeconds: 10

Deployment YAML Anatomy

🔢 replicas
Desired pod count — the controller constantly ensures this number is running
🏷️ selector.matchLabels
How the Deployment finds its pods — must match template labels exactly
🔄 strategy
RollingUpdate (default) or Recreate — controls how pods are replaced
📦 template
The Pod spec — this is a "Pod blueprint" stamped out for each replica
📈 maxSurge
How many extra pods beyond replicas are allowed during an update (number or %)
📉 maxUnavailable
How many pods can be unavailable during an update (number or %). 0 = zero downtime.

b) Scaling

Scaling changes the spec.replicas field. The Deployment's ReplicaSet controller immediately creates or deletes pods to match.

Manual Scaling Commands 
# Scale up — handle more traffic
kubectl scale deployment anihpj --replicas=5

# Scale down — save resources
kubectl scale deployment anihpj --replicas=2

# Verify scaling
kubectl get deployment anihpj
# NAME      READY   UP-TO-DATE   AVAILABLE   AGE
# anihpj   5/5     5            5           10m

# Watch pods appear/disappear in real-time
kubectl get pods -w -l app=anihpj
Manual Scaling — Pods Appear Instantly
  kubectl scale deployment anihpj --replicas=5

  BEFORE (3 replicas):             AFTER (5 replicas):
  ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐    ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐
  │anihpj-1│ │anihpj-2│ │anihpj-3│    │anihpj-1│ │anihpj-2│ │anihpj-3│ │anihpj-4│ │anihpj-5│
  │  📦 v1 │ │  📦 v1 │ │  📦 v1 │    │  📦 v1 │ │  📦 v1 │ │  📦 v1 │ │ 🆕 v1  │ │ 🆕 v1  │
  └────────┘ └────────┘ └────────┘    └────────┘ └────────┘ └────────┘ └────────┘ └────────┘
                                                                          ↑ NEW       ↑ NEW

c) Rolling Updates

Rolling updates replace pods one at a time, ensuring your application stays available throughout the upgrade. This is the default strategy and is heavily tested on the CKA.

Triggering a Rolling Update 
# Update image — triggers rolling update
kubectl set image deployment/anihpj django=anihpj:v2

# Watch rollout progress (CKA exam: verify your work!)
kubectl rollout status deployment/anihpj
# Waiting for deployment "anihpj" rollout to finish:
#   1 out of 3 new replicas have been updated...
#   2 out of 3 new replicas have been updated...
#   deployment "anihpj" successfully rolled out

# Check rollout history
kubectl rollout history deployment/anihpj
# REVISION  CHANGE-CAUSE
# 1         <none>
# 2         <none>

# Add change cause for audit trail
kubectl annotate deployment/anihpj \
  kubernetes.io/change-cause="Update to v2 — new job search feature"
Rolling Update: anihpj v1 → v2 (Zero Downtime)
  maxSurge: 1       (1 extra pod allowed during update)
  maxUnavailable: 0 (never fewer than 3 running)

  Time 0:  [📦v1] [📦v1] [📦v1]              ← All running v1
           ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  Time 1:  [📦v1] [📦v1] [📦v1] [🆕v2]       ← v2 pod starting (surge=1)
           ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  Time 2:  [📦v1] [📦v1] [🗑️  ] [✅v2]       ← v2 ready → old v1 terminating
           ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  Time 3:  [📦v1] [📦v1] [✅v2] [🆕v2]       ← Next v2 pod starting
           ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  Time 4:  [📦v1] [🗑️  ] [✅v2] [✅v2]       ← Second v1 terminating
           ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  Time 5:  [📦v1] [✅v2] [✅v2] [🆕v2]       ← Last v2 starting
           ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  Time 6:  [✅v2] [✅v2] [✅v2]              ← All v2 ✅ Zero downtime! 🎉

  Key insight: At NO point were there fewer than 3 healthy pods serving traffic!

d) Rollbacks

If v2 is broken, you can instantly rollback. Kubernetes keeps a revision history of ReplicaSets.

⚠️ CKA Exam Alert: Rollback Commands

Rollback questions are extremely common. Know these cold:

  • kubectl rollout undo deployment/NAME — go back one revision
  • kubectl rollout undo deployment/NAME --to-revision=N — go to specific revision
  • kubectl rollout history deployment/NAME — see all revisions
Rollback Commands 
# Undo last update (go back to previous revision)
kubectl rollout undo deployment/anihpj

# Go to a specific revision
kubectl rollout undo deployment/anihpj --to-revision=2

# Check what happened
kubectl rollout history deployment/anihpj --revision=2

# Verify rollback succeeded
kubectl rollout status deployment/anihpj
kubectl get pods -l app=anihpj -o jsonpath='{.items[*].spec.containers[*].image}'
# anihpj:v1 anihpj:v1 anihpj:v1  ← back to v1!
Rollback Flow: v2 Broken → Instant Recovery to v1
  Deployment: anihpj
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                                                          │
  │  Revision 1 (v1) ─── Revision 2 (v2) ─── Revision 3    │
  │  ┌──────────┐        ┌──────────┐        ┌──────────┐   │
  │  │ReplicaSet│        │ReplicaSet│        │ReplicaSet│   │
  │  │ (scaled  │  ──→   │ (scaled  │  ──→   │ = rev 1  │   │
  │  │  to 0)   │        │  to 0)   │  undo  │ (active) │   │
  │  └──────────┘        └──────────┘        └──────────┘   │
  │                        ↑ 💀 broken          ↑ ✅ fixed  │
  │                                                          │
  │  kubectl rollout undo deployment/anihpj                  │
  │  → K8s scales old RS back up, scales broken RS down     │
  │  → Pods running v1 again within seconds! 🎉             │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
1
Create

kubectl create deployment or kubectl apply -f

2
Verify

kubectl get deploy,rs,pods

3
Scale

kubectl scale deploy --replicas=N

4
Update

kubectl set image deploy/NAME

5
Monitor

kubectl rollout status

6
Rollback

kubectl rollout undo if needed

e) Pause & Resume — Batch Multiple Changes

Sometimes you need to make multiple changes (image + resources + env vars) in one rollout instead of triggering separate updates for each. Pause the deployment, make all changes, then resume to trigger a single rollout.

Pause & Resume Workflow 
# Pause — no changes will trigger a rollout
kubectl rollout pause deployment/anihpj

# Make multiple changes (none trigger rollout yet)
kubectl set image deployment/anihpj django=anihpj:v3
kubectl set resources deployment/anihpj -c django \
  --requests=cpu=500m,memory=512Mi
kubectl set env deployment/anihpj DEBUG=false

# Resume — all changes deploy in ONE rollout
kubectl rollout resume deployment/anihpj

# Verify single rollout applied all changes
kubectl rollout status deployment/anihpj
Pause/Resume vs Normal — Rollout Count
  WITHOUT Pause/Resume:              WITH Pause/Resume:

  Change image → Rollout #1  🔄      kubectl rollout pause
  Change CPU   → Rollout #2  🔄      Change image  ┐
  Change env   → Rollout #3  🔄      Change CPU    ├─ no rollouts
                                      Change env    ┘
  3 separate rollouts!               kubectl rollout resume
  3 ReplicaSets created!              → Rollout #1 only  🔄
  ⚠️ Wasteful & slower               ✅ Single clean rollout!

13.2 ReplicaSets — Under the Hood

A ReplicaSet ensures a specified number of pod replicas are running at any time. You rarely create ReplicaSets directly — Deployments create and manage them for you. But understanding them is essential for troubleshooting.

💡 Why You Need to Know This

When kubectl get pods shows a pod stuck in ImagePullBackOff, you need to trace: Pod → ReplicaSet → Deployment to find the root cause. The CKA tests this troubleshooting chain.

The Deployment → ReplicaSet → Pod Chain
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  DEPLOYMENT: anihpj                                              │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
  │  │ You manage this layer                                       │ │
  │  │ "I want 3 pods running anihpj:v1"                          │ │
  │  └──────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────┘ │
  │                             │ creates & manages                 │
  │                             ▼                                    │
  │  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
  │  │  REPLICASET: anihpj-7d9f8b6c5d                           │   │
  │  │  Kubernetes manages this layer automatically              │   │
  │  │  selector: matchLabels: { app: anihpj }                  │   │
  │  │  replicas: 3                                              │   │
  │  └───────┬──────────────────┬──────────────────┬────────────┘   │
  │          │ owns             │ owns             │ owns           │
  │          ▼                  ▼                  ▼                │
  │  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐          │
  │  │ POD          │  │ POD          │  │ POD          │          │
  │  │ anihpj-..abc │  │ anihpj-..def │  │ anihpj-..ghi │          │
  │  │ 📦 anihpj:v1│  │ 📦 anihpj:v1│  │ 📦 anihpj:v1│          │
  │  │ labels:      │  │ labels:      │  │ labels:      │          │
  │  │  app: anihpj │  │  app: anihpj │  │  app: anihpj │          │
  │  └──────────────┘  └──────────────┘  └──────────────┘          │
  │                                                                  │
  │  Delete a pod? → ReplicaSet auto-creates a replacement! 🔄     │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
replicaset-example.yaml (for understanding — rarely created directly) 
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: ReplicaSet
metadata:
  name: anihpj-replicaset
  labels:
    app: anihpj
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:                 # Which pods this RS owns
      app: anihpj
    # Advanced: matchExpressions
    # matchExpressions:
    #   - key: app
    #     operator: In           # In, NotIn, Exists, DoesNotExist
    #     values: [anihpj, anihpj-staging]
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: django
        image: anihpj:v1
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8000
🏷️
matchLabels

Simple equality: app: anihpj. All labels must match. Most common approach.

🔍
matchExpressions

Operators: In, NotIn, Exists, DoesNotExist. For complex multi-value selectors.

Inspecting the Chain 
# See all three layers at once
kubectl get deploy,rs,pods -l app=anihpj

# NAME                     READY   UP-TO-DATE   AVAILABLE   AGE
# deployment.apps/anihpj   3/3     3            3           5m
#
# NAME                                DESIRED   CURRENT   READY   AGE
# replicaset.apps/anihpj-7d9f8b6c5d  3         3         3       5m
#
# NAME                          READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE
# pod/anihpj-7d9f8b6c5d-abc12  1/1     Running   0          5m
# pod/anihpj-7d9f8b6c5d-def34  1/1     Running   0          5m
# pod/anihpj-7d9f8b6c5d-ghi56  1/1     Running   0          5m

# See owner references (pod → RS → Deployment)
kubectl get pod anihpj-7d9f8b6c5d-abc12 -o jsonpath='{.metadata.ownerReferences[0].name}'
# anihpj-7d9f8b6c5d  ← owned by the ReplicaSet
⚠️ Never Delete ReplicaSets Directly

If you delete a ReplicaSet that belongs to a Deployment, the Deployment controller will immediately recreate it. To scale down, always modify the Deployment. Delete the Deployment to clean up all RS and Pods together.

💡 revisionHistoryLimit — Control Revision History

By default, Kubernetes keeps 10 old ReplicaSets (revision history) for rollback. Each old RS consumes etcd storage. You can control this with spec.revisionHistoryLimit:

  • revisionHistoryLimit: 5 — keep last 5 revisions (good for production)
  • revisionHistoryLimit: 0 — keep none (⚠️ no rollback possible!)
  • revisionHistoryLimit: 10 — default

13.3 Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA)

The HPA automatically scales your Deployment based on observed CPU, memory, or custom metrics. When traffic spikes, HPA adds pods. When traffic drops, it removes them. This is critical for production and tested on the CKA.

📋 Prerequisite: Metrics Server

HPA requires the Metrics Server to be installed in the cluster. Without it, HPA has no data to make scaling decisions. Verify: kubectl top nodes — if this works, Metrics Server is running.

HPA — Imperative (CKA Exam Speed) 
# Create HPA bound to deployment
kubectl autoscale deployment anihpj --min=3 --max=10 --cpu-percent=70

# Check HPA status
kubectl get hpa
# NAME     REFERENCE           TARGETS   MINPODS   MAXPODS   REPLICAS   AGE
# anihpj  Deployment/anihpj   45%/70%   3         10        3          2m

# Detailed view
kubectl describe hpa anihpj
anihpj-hpa.yaml 
apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: anihpj-hpa
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: anihpj
  minReplicas: 3
  maxReplicas: 10
  metrics:
  - type: Resource
    resource:
      name: cpu
      target:
        type: Utilization
        averageUtilization: 70    # Scale when avg CPU > 70%
  - type: Resource
    resource:
      name: memory
      target:
        type: Utilization
        averageUtilization: 80    # Also watch memory
  behavior:
    scaleUp:
      stabilizationWindowSeconds: 30   # React quickly to spikes
    scaleDown:
      stabilizationWindowSeconds: 300  # Wait 5 min before scaling down
HPA Scaling Flow — Automatic Response to Load
  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  HPA: Horizontal Pod Autoscaler for anihpj                      │
  │                                                                  │
  │  📊 Metrics Server reports: CPU = 85%  (above target of 70%)   │
  │       │                                                          │
  │       ▼                                                          │
  │  🤖 HPA evaluates every 15 seconds:                             │
  │     desiredReplicas = ceil(currentReplicas × (85% / 70%))       │
  │     desiredReplicas = ceil(3 × 1.21) = 4                        │
  │       │                                                          │
  │       ▼                                                          │
  │  📈 HPA updates: deployment.spec.replicas = 4                   │
  │       │                                                          │
  │       ▼                                                          │
  │  📦📦📦📦  4 pods running → CPU drops to 60%                    │
  │                                                                  │
  │  ... traffic keeps climbing ...                                  │
  │                                                                  │
  │  📊 CPU = 90%  →  HPA: replicas = 6                             │
  │  📦📦📦📦📦📦  6 pods running → CPU stabilizes at 55%           │
  │                                                                  │
  │  ... traffic decreases (night time) ...                          │
  │                                                                  │
  │  📊 CPU = 25%  →  HPA waits 5 min (stabilization window)       │
  │               →  then scales down to minReplicas = 3            │
  │  📦📦📦  3 pods — cost efficient! 💰                             │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
⬆️
Scale Up (Fast)

Default stabilization: 0 seconds. HPA reacts immediately to load spikes to protect users.

⬇️
Scale Down (Slow)

Default stabilization: 300 seconds. Avoids flapping — waits 5 min to confirm load is truly gone.

💡 CKA Tip: HPA Requires Resource Requests

HPA can only measure utilization as a percentage if resources.requests is set on the container. Without requests, HPA cannot calculate CPU percentage and will report <unknown>/70%. Always set resource requests on Deployments that use HPA.

13.4 Deployment Strategies

Kubernetes supports two built-in deployment strategies. Choosing the right one depends on your application's ability to handle multiple versions running simultaneously.

Strategy How It Works Downtime When to Use anihpj Example
RollingUpdate Replace pods gradually, one (or few) at a time ❌ None Default — use for most updates New Django feature release
Recreate Kill ALL old pods first, then start ALL new pods ✅ Yes — brief outage Breaking changes, DB schema migrations, incompatible API versions Major Django version upgrade with DB migration
RollingUpdate vs Recreate — Visual Comparison
  ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  ROLLINGUPDATE STRATEGY (default)          RECREATE STRATEGY
  ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

  T0: [v1] [v1] [v1]                       [v1] [v1] [v1]
       │                                         │
  T1: [v1] [v1] [v1] [v2] ← surge              [💀] [💀] [💀] ← all killed
       │                                         │
  T2: [v1] [v1] [v2]      ← replace 1           [  ] [  ] [  ] ← downtime! ⚠️
       │                                         │
  T3: [v1] [v2] [v2]      ← replace 2           [v2] [v2] [v2] ← all new
       │
  T4: [v2] [v2] [v2]      ← complete ✅

  Availability:                             Availability:
  ██████████████████ 100%                   ████░░░░████ ~70%
  Never less than minAvailable              Brief outage window

  ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

RollingUpdate — Tuning Parameters

strategy-examples.yaml 
# Conservative — zero downtime, slow rollout
strategy:
  type: RollingUpdate
  rollingUpdate:
    maxSurge: 1          # Only 1 extra pod at a time
    maxUnavailable: 0    # Never fewer than desired replicas

# Aggressive — faster rollout, allows brief capacity dip
strategy:
  type: RollingUpdate
  rollingUpdate:
    maxSurge: "50%"      # Up to 50% extra pods
    maxUnavailable: "25%" # Allow 25% to be unavailable

# Recreate — kill all, then create all
strategy:
  type: Recreate         # No rollingUpdate params needed
RollingUpdate — Safety ★★★★★
RollingUpdate — Speed ★★★☆☆
Recreate — Safety ★★☆☆☆
Recreate — Speed ★★★★★
💡 CKA Exam Strategy

On the exam, the default RollingUpdate is almost always what you want. Only use Recreate if the question explicitly says "all pods must be running the same version" or mentions database migrations that can't handle mixed versions.

📖 Beyond Built-in: Blue-Green & Canary Deployments

Kubernetes only has RollingUpdate and Recreate built in. Two other common strategies require manual setup or tools like Argo Rollouts / Istio:

  • Blue-Green — Run two full Deployments (blue=current, green=new). Switch the Service selector from blue to green once verified. Instant rollback by switching back.
  • Canary — Deploy new version alongside old with fewer replicas (e.g., 1 of 10). Route a small percentage of traffic to canary. If healthy, gradually increase. Popular with service meshes.
Blue-Green vs Canary — Not Built-in but Important to Know
  BLUE-GREEN:                           CANARY:

  ┌─────── Service ──────┐              ┌─────── Service ──────┐
  │  selector: ver=blue  │              │  selector: app=web   │
  └──────────┬───────────┘              └──────────┬───────────┘
             │                                     │
    ┌────────┴────────┐                   ┌────────┴────────┐
    ▼                 ▼                   ▼                 ▼
  Blue Deploy      Green Deploy        v1 Deploy (9)    v2 Deploy (1)
  (current ✅)     (new, testing)      (90% traffic)    (10% canary)
  [v1][v1][v1]     [v2][v2][v2]        [v1]×9           [v2]×1

  Switch: change   Promote: scale      Promote: scale v2 up,
  selector to      green, delete       scale v1 down gradually
  ver=green        blue

🔑 Chapter 13 Quick Reference

Action Command
Create deploymentkubectl create deployment NAME --image=IMG --replicas=N
Scalekubectl scale deployment NAME --replicas=N
Update imagekubectl set image deployment/NAME container=IMG:TAG
Check rolloutkubectl rollout status deployment/NAME
View historykubectl rollout history deployment/NAME
Undo lastkubectl rollout undo deployment/NAME
Go to revisionkubectl rollout undo deployment/NAME --to-revision=N
Autoscalekubectl autoscale deployment NAME --min=M --max=X --cpu-percent=P
Pause rolloutkubectl rollout pause deployment/NAME
Resume rolloutkubectl rollout resume deployment/NAME

⚠️ CKA Gotcha: Deployment & Rollout Traps

  • Rolling update is the default — If the question doesn't specify a strategy, it's RollingUpdate. Only use Recreate when explicitly asked (kills all pods first)
  • maxUnavailable + maxSurge — Defaults are 25% each. The exam may ask you to set specific values. Remember: maxUnavailable: 0 + maxSurge: 1 = zero-downtime
  • Rollback requires history — kubectl rollout undo only works if there's a previous revision. Add --record (deprecated) or use --to-revision=N for precision
  • kubectl set image container name — You must specify the container name: kubectl set image deployment/web nginx=nginx:1.25. Get it from kubectl get deploy web -o jsonpath='{.spec.template.spec.containers[*].name}'

CKA Speed Tip — Deployment lifecycle in 60 seconds:
kubectl create deploy web --image=nginx:1.24 --replicas=3
kubectl set image deploy/web nginx=nginx:1.25
kubectl rollout status deploy/web
kubectl rollout history deploy/web
kubectl rollout undo deploy/web — instant rollback

📝 Chapter 13 Practice Drill

Timed challenge (target: 4 minutes):

  • 1. Create deployment webapp with nginx:1.23, 4 replicas
  • 2. Update to nginx:1.24, watch rollout: kubectl rollout status deploy/webapp
  • 3. Update to nginx:BROKEN (bad image) — observe the stuck rollout
  • 4. Rollback: kubectl rollout undo deploy/webapp
  • 5. Verify image is nginx:1.24 again

📝 Chapter 13 — CKA Practice Questions

Q1
You need to update a Deployment from nginx:1.24 to nginx:1.25 and ensure zero downtime. Show the command and explain what happens under the hood.
Show Answer
Answer
# Update the image
kubectl set image deployment/webapp nginx=nginx:1.25

# Watch the rollout in real-time
kubectl rollout status deployment/webapp
# Waiting for deployment "webapp" rollout to finish:
#   1 out of 3 new replicas have been updated...
#   2 out of 3 new replicas have been updated...
#   deployment "webapp" successfully rolled out

# What happens under the hood:
# 1. New ReplicaSet (RS-v2) created with image nginx:1.25
# 2. RS-v2 scaled up by maxSurge (default 25%)
# 3. RS-v1 scaled down by the same amount
# 4. Repeat until RS-v2 has all replicas, RS-v1 has 0

# Check both ReplicaSets:
kubectl get rs
# webapp-6d87c8b9f5   3   3   3   2m  (new - nginx:1.25)
# webapp-7f4d8c9a2b   0   0   0   1h  (old - nginx:1.24)
Explanation

The RollingUpdate strategy (default) ensures zero downtime by gradually replacing old pods with new ones. maxSurge controls how many extra pods can exist during the update (default 25%), and maxUnavailable controls how many pods can be down (default 25%). The old ReplicaSet is kept at 0 replicas for rollback. On the CKA, kubectl set image is the fastest way to trigger an update. Always verify with kubectl rollout status — if the new image is broken, the rollout stalls.

Q2
A rolling update is stuck — only 1 of 3 pods updated successfully. The new pod keeps crashing. How do you rollback and what commands show the rollout history?
Show Answer
Answer
# Check rollout status (will show it's stuck)
kubectl rollout status deployment/webapp
# Waiting for deployment "webapp" rollout to finish:
#   1 out of 3 new replicas have been updated...

# View rollout history
kubectl rollout history deployment/webapp
# REVISION  CHANGE-CAUSE
# 1         <none>
# 2         <none>
# 3         <none>

# See details of a specific revision
kubectl rollout history deployment/webapp --revision=2

# Rollback to the previous version
kubectl rollout undo deployment/webapp

# Or rollback to a specific revision
kubectl rollout undo deployment/webapp --to-revision=1

# Verify the rollback
kubectl rollout status deployment/webapp
kubectl get deployment webapp -o jsonpath='{.spec.template.spec.containers[0].image}'
# nginx:1.24 (back to previous version)
Explanation

kubectl rollout undo reverses the last rollout by scaling up the previous ReplicaSet and scaling down the current one. It's essentially a new rollout in reverse. --to-revision lets you go back to any historical version. Kubernetes keeps old ReplicaSets (controlled by spec.revisionHistoryLimit, default 10). On the CKA, speed matters: if an update breaks, don't waste time debugging — undo first, investigate later. To make history more useful, record changes: kubectl set image deploy/webapp nginx=nginx:1.25 --record.

Q3
What is the difference between a Deployment's strategy.type: RollingUpdate and strategy.type: Recreate? When would you use each?
Show Answer
Answer
Aspect RollingUpdate Recreate
Downtime Zero Yes — all old pods killed first
Old + New versions running Briefly, yes Never — only one version at a time
Use case Stateless apps, web servers Schema migrations, single-writer DBs 
# Recreate strategy in YAML:
spec:
  strategy:
    type: Recreate    # Kill all old, then start new
Explanation

Use Recreate when you cannot have two versions running simultaneously — for example, an app that locks a database or writes to a shared volume. RollingUpdate is the default and is correct for 95% of scenarios. On the CKA, if a question mentions that two versions of the app must NOT run at the same time, the answer is Recreate. If it mentions zero downtime, the answer is RollingUpdate.

Q4
How do you scale a Deployment to 5 replicas? Show three different methods. What happens to existing traffic during scaling?
Show Answer
Answer
# Method 1: kubectl scale (imperative, fastest)
kubectl scale deployment webapp --replicas=5

# Method 2: kubectl edit (interactive)
kubectl edit deployment webapp
# Change spec.replicas to 5

# Method 3: kubectl patch (scriptable)
kubectl patch deployment webapp -p '{"spec":{"replicas":5}}'

# Method 4: kubectl apply (declarative)
# Edit the YAML file and apply
kubectl apply -f webapp-deployment.yaml

# During scale UP: existing traffic is unaffected
# New pods start and become Ready → added to Service endpoints
# During scale DOWN: pods are terminated gracefully
# terminationGracePeriodSeconds (default 30s) allows cleanup

# Verify:
kubectl get deployment webapp
# READY  UP-TO-DATE  AVAILABLE
# 5/5    5           5
Explanation

kubectl scale is the fastest for the CKA exam. Scaling up adds pods to the ReplicaSet — they start, become Ready, and are added to the Service's Endpoints list (traffic begins flowing). Scaling down selects pods to terminate — they receive SIGTERM, have terminationGracePeriodSeconds to shut down cleanly, then are removed from Endpoints (traffic stops). The key insight: the Service only routes to pods that pass their readinessProbe, so scaling is traffic-safe by design.

Q5
Explain the relationship between Deployment → ReplicaSet → Pod. What naming convention is used and how do you trace ownership?
Show Answer
Answer
# Naming chain:
# Deployment: webapp
# ReplicaSet: webapp-6d87c8b9f5     (deploy-name + pod-template-hash)
# Pod:        webapp-6d87c8b9f5-x7k  (rs-name + random-suffix)

# Trace ownership: Pod → ReplicaSet → Deployment
kubectl get pod webapp-6d87c8b9f5-x7k -o jsonpath='{.metadata.ownerReferences[0].name}'
# webapp-6d87c8b9f5  (owned by this ReplicaSet)

kubectl get rs webapp-6d87c8b9f5 -o jsonpath='{.metadata.ownerReferences[0].name}'
# webapp  (owned by this Deployment)

# Visual hierarchy:
# Deployment (webapp)
#   ├── ReplicaSet (webapp-6d87c8b9f5) ← current (3 replicas)
#   └── ReplicaSet (webapp-7f4d8c9a2b) ← old (0 replicas, for rollback)
#         ├── Pod (webapp-6d87c8b9f5-x7k)
#         ├── Pod (webapp-6d87c8b9f5-m9p)
#         └── Pod (webapp-6d87c8b9f5-q2j)
Explanation

Each Deployment creates a ReplicaSet for each unique pod template (spec change). Each ReplicaSet creates the desired number of Pods. The pod-template-hash label ensures each ReplicaSet is unique to a specific template version. On the CKA, understanding this chain helps with: (1) debugging — if pods aren't being created, check the ReplicaSet, then the Deployment, (2) rollbacks — each version has its own ReplicaSet, (3) identifying orphaned ReplicaSets — old RS with 0 replicas are normal.

Q6
What is the Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA)? Create one that scales a Deployment between 2 and 10 replicas based on 50% CPU utilization.
Show Answer
Answer
# Prerequisites: metrics-server must be installed
kubectl top pods  # If this works, metrics-server is running

# Create HPA imperatively (fastest on CKA)
kubectl autoscale deployment webapp \
  --min=2 --max=10 --cpu-percent=50

# Verify
kubectl get hpa
# NAME    REFERENCE          TARGETS   MINPODS  MAXPODS  REPLICAS
# webapp  Deployment/webapp  30%/50%   2        10       3

# YAML equivalent:
apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: webapp-hpa
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: webapp
  minReplicas: 2
  maxReplicas: 10
  metrics:
  - type: Resource
    resource:
      name: cpu
      target:
        type: Utilization
        averageUtilization: 50
Explanation

HPA automatically adjusts the Deployment's replicas based on observed metrics. It checks metrics every 15 seconds (default). Important: the Deployment's pods MUST have resources.requests.cpu set — HPA calculates utilization as actual / requested. Without requests, HPA can't compute the percentage. On the CKA, kubectl autoscale is the imperative command. HPA won't scale below minReplicas or above maxReplicas. Metrics-server must be running for HPA to work — if kubectl top pods fails, install/check metrics-server first.

Q7
What is maxSurge and maxUnavailable in a Deployment's rolling update strategy? If you have 4 replicas with maxSurge: 25% and maxUnavailable: 25%, how many pods exist during the update?
Show Answer
Answer
# With 4 replicas, maxSurge: 25%, maxUnavailable: 25%:
# maxSurge: 25% of 4 = 1 → max 5 pods total during update
# maxUnavailable: 25% of 4 = 1 → min 3 pods available

# During rolling update:
# Step 1: Create 1 new pod (5 total: 4 old + 1 new)
# Step 2: When new pod Ready → kill 1 old (4 total: 3 old + 1 new)
# Step 3: Create 1 new pod (5 total: 3 old + 2 new)
# Step 4: When new pod Ready → kill 1 old (4 total: 2 old + 2 new)
# ... continues until all 4 are new

# YAML:
spec:
  strategy:
    type: RollingUpdate
    rollingUpdate:
      maxSurge: 1          # Can also use absolute number
      maxUnavailable: 1    # Can also use absolute number
Explanation

maxSurge = how many MORE pods than desired can exist during update. maxUnavailable = how many FEWER pods than desired can be unavailable. Both can be absolute numbers or percentages. Higher maxSurge = faster updates but more resource usage. Higher maxUnavailable = faster updates but less capacity during rollout. Setting maxUnavailable: 0 ensures full capacity throughout (but requires at least maxSurge: 1). On the CKA, know that percentages are rounded up for maxSurge and rounded down for maxUnavailable.

Q8
How do you pause and resume a Deployment rollout? Why would you want to pause one?
Show Answer
Answer
# Pause the rollout
kubectl rollout pause deployment/webapp
# No new ReplicaSets are created, no scaling happens

# Make multiple changes while paused
kubectl set image deployment/webapp nginx=nginx:1.25
kubectl set resources deployment/webapp -c=nginx \
  --requests=cpu=200m,memory=256Mi
kubectl patch deployment webapp -p '{"spec":{"template":{"metadata":{"labels":{"version":"v2"}}}}}'

# Resume — ALL changes applied as ONE rollout
kubectl rollout resume deployment/webapp

# This creates a single new ReplicaSet with all changes
# Instead of 3 separate rollouts (one per change)
Explanation

Pausing a Deployment allows you to make multiple changes that get applied as a single rollout instead of triggering separate rollouts for each change. Without pausing, changing the image triggers rollout #1, then changing resources triggers rollout #2 — wasteful and slow. On the CKA, this is tested less frequently but shows advanced understanding. After resuming, verify with kubectl rollout status. Note: a paused Deployment doesn't scale — kubectl scale also waits until you resume.

Q9
Create a Deployment with nginx:1.25, 3 replicas, and set revisionHistoryLimit to 5. What does this setting control?
Show Answer
Answer
# Generate and modify:
kubectl create deployment webapp --image=nginx:1.25 --replicas=3 \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml > webapp.yaml

# Add revisionHistoryLimit:
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: webapp
spec:
  replicas: 3
  revisionHistoryLimit: 5    # ← Keep only 5 old ReplicaSets
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: webapp
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: webapp
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: nginx
        image: nginx:1.25

kubectl apply -f webapp.yaml

# After 7 rollouts:
kubectl get rs
# Only 5 old ReplicaSets kept (+ 1 current = 6 total)
# Older ones are garbage-collected
Explanation

revisionHistoryLimit controls how many old ReplicaSets are kept for rollback purposes. Default is 10. Setting it too low means you can't roll back to older versions. Setting it to 0 means no rollback at all (old RS are immediately deleted). On the CKA, this field shows understanding of the Deployment lifecycle. Each old ReplicaSet costs a small amount of API server/etcd storage. In production, 5-10 is typical.

Q10
You need to update a Deployment and record the change cause. Then view the history to see what changed in each revision. Show the complete workflow.
Show Answer
Answer
# Update with annotation for change tracking
kubectl set image deployment/webapp nginx=nginx:1.25
kubectl annotate deployment/webapp \
  kubernetes.io/change-cause="Updated nginx to 1.25 for security patch"

# View history with change causes
kubectl rollout history deployment/webapp
# REVISION  CHANGE-CAUSE
# 1         Initial deployment with nginx:1.24
# 2         Updated nginx to 1.25 for security patch

# See exactly what changed in revision 2
kubectl rollout history deployment/webapp --revision=2
# Shows the full pod template for that revision

# Rollback with awareness
kubectl rollout undo deployment/webapp --to-revision=1
kubectl annotate deployment/webapp \
  kubernetes.io/change-cause="Rolled back to nginx:1.24 due to bug"
Explanation

The kubernetes.io/change-cause annotation populates the CHANGE-CAUSE column in rollout history. Without it, the column shows <none>. The old --record flag was deprecated — use annotations instead. On the CKA, this shows you understand audit trails for deployments. kubectl rollout history --revision=N shows the complete pod template spec for that revision, letting you compare what changed between versions without needing to inspect ReplicaSets directly.

Chapter 14: Scheduling and Node Management

The Kubernetes scheduler decides where every pod runs. As a CKA administrator, you must master how to influence scheduling decisions — from simple node selectors to complex affinity rules, taints, tolerations, and node maintenance procedures. This chapter is one of the most heavily tested topics on the CKA exam.

🟡 Intermediate ⏱️ ~2 hours 📍 Scheduling 📝 CKA EXAM

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ How the Kubernetes scheduler makes placement decisions (filtering → scoring → binding)
  • ✅ Use nodeSelector for simple node placement
  • ✅ Configure node affinity (required vs preferred) for flexible scheduling
  • ✅ Use pod affinity/anti-affinity for co-location and spreading
  • ✅ Apply taints and tolerations to control which pods can run on which nodes
  • ✅ Perform safe node maintenance with cordon, drain, uncordon
  • ✅ Spread pods evenly with topology spread constraints
  • ✅ Use PriorityClasses for pod preemption
🎓 CKA Relevance
Workloads & Scheduling 15% Cluster Architecture 25%
Scheduling Is a CKA Exam Favourite

Expect at least 2-3 scheduling questions. The most common patterns:

  • Taints & Tolerations — taint a node, create a pod with/without toleration, verify placement
  • Node Affinity — label a node, create a pod with nodeAffinity targeting that label
  • Node Maintenance — cordon → drain → perform work → uncordon
  • Static Pods — create a manifest in /etc/kubernetes/manifests/

14.1 How the Scheduler Works

The kube-scheduler runs as a control plane component. When a new pod is created without a nodeName, it enters the scheduling queue. The scheduler then executes a multi-phase pipeline to find the best node.

1
Filtering

Which nodes CAN run this pod? Checks: sufficient resources, node taints, node selectors, affinity rules, port availability.

2
Scoring

Which passing node is the BEST fit? Scores based on: resource balance, affinity preference, spread, image locality.

3
Binding

The highest-scoring node wins. Scheduler sets pod.spec.nodeName and the kubelet on that node starts the container.

Scheduler Decision Flowchart — anihpj Pod Placement
  NEW POD: anihpj (requests: 256Mi memory, 250m CPU)
       │
       ▼
  ════════════════ PHASE 1: FILTERING ════════════════
  "Which nodes CAN run this pod?"

  Node 1 (512Mi free, 500m CPU free)   → ✅ PASSES — has enough resources
  Node 2 (128Mi free, 100m CPU free)   → ❌ FAILS  — insufficient memory
  Node 3 (1Gi free, 1000m CPU free)    → ✅ PASSES — has enough resources
  Node 4 (taint: gpu=true:NoSchedule)  → ❌ FAILS  — no matching toleration
       │
       ▼ (only passing nodes continue)
  ════════════════ PHASE 2: SCORING ═════════════════
  "Which passing node is the BEST fit?"

  Node 1: Score 65  — good fit, resources balanced
  Node 3: Score 82  — better fit, more headroom,
                      topology spread bonus, affinity match
       │
       ▼
  ════════════════ PHASE 3: BINDING ═════════════════

  🏆 Winner: Node 3 (highest score: 82)
  → pod.spec.nodeName = "node-3"
  → kubelet on Node 3 pulls image → starts container
  → Pod status: Running ✅
🎯
Resource Fit

Does the node have enough CPU & memory to satisfy the pod's requests?

🏷️
Label Match

Does the node have the labels required by nodeSelector or nodeAffinity?

🚫
Taint Check

Does the pod tolerate the node's taints? No toleration = rejected.

📊
Spread Score

How well does this placement spread pods across nodes/zones?

Static Pods — Bypass the Scheduler

Static pods are managed directly by the kubelet on a specific node, not by the API server. They're used for control plane components (etcd, apiserver, scheduler itself). The CKA may ask you to create one.

Creating a Static Pod 
# Static pods live in the kubelet's manifest directory
# Default: /etc/kubernetes/manifests/

# Find the staticPodPath on any node
ssh worker-1 "cat /var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml | grep staticPodPath"
# staticPodPath: /etc/kubernetes/manifests

# Create a static pod (just place a YAML file there)
ssh worker-1 "cat > /etc/kubernetes/manifests/static-nginx.yaml" <<'EOF'
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: static-nginx
spec:
  containers:
  - name: nginx
    image: nginx:1.25
    ports:
    - containerPort: 80
EOF

# kubelet auto-starts it! Check from control plane:
kubectl get pods
# NAME                       READY   STATUS    AGE
# static-nginx-worker-1      1/1     Running   10s
#                  ^^^^^^^^^ node name appended!

# Delete: remove the file from /etc/kubernetes/manifests/
# kubectl delete won't work — kubelet will recreate it!
⚠️ CKA Exam: Static Pod Key Facts
  • Static pod names always end with -<nodename>
  • They can only be created/deleted by adding/removing YAML from the manifest directory
  • kubectl delete on a static pod will NOT permanently remove it — kubelet recreates it
  • Control plane components (apiserver, etcd, scheduler) are static pods on the control plane node

14.2 Node Selectors — Simple Placement

The simplest way to constrain a pod to specific nodes. Label a node, then reference that label in the pod spec. It's a hard requirement — the pod won't schedule if no node matches.

Label a Node, Then Select It 
# Step 1: Label the node
kubectl label node worker-1 disk=ssd

# Verify label was applied
kubectl get node worker-1 --show-labels | grep disk

# Step 2: Create pod with nodeSelector (see YAML below)
kubectl apply -f anihpj-ssd-pod.yaml

# Verify pod landed on the right node
kubectl get pod anihpj-ssd -o wide
# NAME         READY   STATUS    NODE       
# anihpj-ssd   1/1     Running   worker-1   ← correct!

# Clean up: remove label
kubectl label node worker-1 disk-
anihpj-ssd-pod.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-ssd
spec:
  nodeSelector:
    disk: ssd                    # Only schedule on nodes with disk=ssd
  containers:
  - name: django
    image: anihpj:v1
    ports:
    - containerPort: 8000
💡 nodeSelector vs nodeAffinity

nodeSelector is simple but limited — it only supports exact equality matching. For "prefer but don't require" or "match any of these values" logic, use Node Affinity (see 14.3).

14.3 Node Affinity — Advanced Placement

Node affinity is the more powerful version of nodeSelector. It supports operators (In, NotIn, Exists, Gt, Lt) and offers two types: hard rules (required) and soft rules (preferred).

Type Behavior When Pod Can't Match Use Case
requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution Hard rule — MUST match Pod stays Pending forever GPU workloads, compliance zones
preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution Soft rule — TRY to match Scheduled anywhere available Prefer SSD, prefer same zone
anihpj-node-affinity.yaml 
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
    spec:
      affinity:
        nodeAffinity:
          # HARD: Must be on an SSD node
          requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
            nodeSelectorTerms:
            - matchExpressions:
              - key: disk
                operator: In
                values:
                - ssd
                - nvme
          # SOFT: Prefer us-east zone (but ok if not available)
          preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
          - weight: 80           # 1-100, higher = stronger preference
            preference:
              matchExpressions:
              - key: topology.kubernetes.io/zone
                operator: In
                values:
                - us-east-1a
                - us-east-1b
      containers:
      - name: django
        image: anihpj:v1
Node Affinity Decision Matrix
  Pod: anihpj (requires: disk ∈ {ssd, nvme}, prefers: zone ∈ {us-east-1a})

  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  Node          disk    zone          Required  Preferred   │
  │  ──────────    ────    ──────────    ────────  ─────────   │
  │  worker-1      ssd     us-east-1a   ✅ PASS   ✅ Match    │ ← Best (score 82)
  │  worker-2      ssd     us-west-1a   ✅ PASS   ❌ No match │ ← OK (score 65)
  │  worker-3      hdd     us-east-1a   ❌ FAIL   —           │ ← Rejected
  │  worker-4      nvme    us-east-1b   ✅ PASS   ✅ Match    │ ← Good (score 78)
  │  worker-5      —       us-east-1a   ❌ FAIL   —           │ ← Rejected
  │                                                             │
  │  Result: Pod scheduled on worker-1 (highest combined score)│
  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
⚠️ "IgnoredDuringExecution" — What It Means

If you remove a node's label after a pod is already scheduled there, the pod keeps running. The affinity rule is only checked at scheduling time, not continuously. A future Kubernetes version may add RequiredDuringExecution to also evict running pods.

14.4 Pod Affinity and Anti-Affinity

While Node Affinity places pods relative to nodes, Pod Affinity/Anti-Affinity places pods relative to other pods. This is essential for performance (co-locate) and high availability (spread out).

🤝
Pod Affinity

"Place me near this pod." Put anihpj Django on the same node/zone as its Redis cache for low latency.

↔️
Pod Anti-Affinity

"Keep me away from that pod." Spread anihpj replicas across different nodes so one node failure doesn't take all of them down.

anihpj-pod-antiaffinity.yaml — HA Deployment 
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
    spec:
      affinity:
        # Anti-Affinity: Spread replicas across nodes
        podAntiAffinity:
          requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
          - labelSelector:
              matchExpressions:
              - key: app
                operator: In
                values:
                - anihpj
            topologyKey: kubernetes.io/hostname  # "different nodes"

        # Affinity: Place near Redis cache
        podAffinity:
          preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
          - weight: 100
            podAffinityTerm:
              labelSelector:
                matchExpressions:
                - key: app
                  operator: In
                  values:
                  - anihpj-redis
              topologyKey: topology.kubernetes.io/zone  # "same zone"
      containers:
      - name: django
        image: anihpj:v1
Pod Anti-Affinity — anihpj Replicas Spread Across Nodes
  Deployment: anihpj (replicas: 3)
  podAntiAffinity: kubernetes.io/hostname (no 2 on same node)

  ┌────────────────┐  ┌────────────────┐  ┌────────────────┐
  │   worker-1     │  │   worker-2     │  │   worker-3     │
  │ ┌────────────┐ │  │ ┌────────────┐ │  │ ┌────────────┐ │
  │ │ anihpj-abc │ │  │ │ anihpj-def │ │  │ │ anihpj-ghi │ │
  │ │   📦 v1    │ │  │ │   📦 v1    │ │  │ │   📦 v1    │ │
  │ └────────────┘ │  │ └────────────┘ │  │ └────────────┘ │
  │                │  │                │  │                │
  │ ┌────────────┐ │  │                │  │                │
  │ │ redis-xyz  │ │  │                │  │                │
  │ │   🔴 cache │ │  │                │  │                │
  │ └────────────┘ │  │                │  │                │
  └────────────────┘  └────────────────┘  └────────────────┘

  ✅ Each node has exactly 1 anihpj pod — survives any single node failure!
  ✅ anihpj-abc co-located with redis (podAffinity) for low latency

14.5 Taints and Tolerations

Taints are the opposite of affinity — taints repel pods from a node, while tolerations let specific pods override that repulsion. This is a CKA must-know concept and appears on almost every exam.

📖 The Analogy

Think of a node's taint as a "No Entry" sign. A toleration is the "VIP pass" that lets a pod in despite the sign. Without the pass, the pod is rejected.

Effect Behavior Running Pods Use Case
NoSchedule New pods WITHOUT tolerance are rejected Existing pods stay Dedicated GPU nodes, team isolation
PreferNoSchedule Try NOT to schedule here, but allow if no other option Existing pods stay Soft preference — avoid but don't block
NoExecute Reject new pods AND evict running pods without tolerance Evicted! Node going into maintenance, toxic node
Taint & Toleration Commands 
# Add a taint to a node
kubectl taint node worker-1 gpu=true:NoSchedule

# Verify taint
kubectl describe node worker-1 | grep -A5 Taints
# Taints:  gpu=true:NoSchedule

# Remove a taint (add dash at the end)
kubectl taint node worker-1 gpu=true:NoSchedule-

# See control plane taints (this is why your pods don't run on master!)
kubectl describe node control-plane | grep Taints
# Taints:  node-role.kubernetes.io/control-plane:NoSchedule
pod-with-toleration.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: ml-training-job
spec:
  tolerations:
  - key: "gpu"
    operator: "Equal"        # Match exact value
    value: "true"
    effect: "NoSchedule"
  # Alternative: tolerate ANY value for the key
  # - key: "gpu"
  #   operator: "Exists"    # Don't care about value
  #   effect: "NoSchedule"
  containers:
  - name: trainer
    image: ml-trainer:v1
    resources:
      limits:
        nvidia.com/gpu: 1
Taints & Tolerations — Access Control
  ┌────────────────── Node: gpu-node ──────────────────┐
  │ Taint: gpu=true:NoSchedule   🚫                    │
  │                                                     │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
  │  │  anihpj pod (no toleration)                  │   │
  │  │  → ❌ REJECTED — "You don't have a VIP pass" │   │
  │  └─────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
  │                                                     │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
  │  │  ml-job pod (toleration: gpu=true)           │   │
  │  │  → ✅ SCHEDULED — "VIP pass accepted!"      │   │
  │  └─────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  ┌────────────── Control Plane Node ──────────────────┐
  │ Taint: node-role.kubernetes.io/control-plane:      │
  │        NoSchedule                                   │
  │                                                     │
  │  That's why your pods DON'T run on control plane!  │
  │  Only system pods (with tolerations) run here:     │
  │  ├── kube-apiserver                                 │
  │  ├── kube-scheduler                                 │
  │  ├── kube-controller-manager                        │
  │  └── etcd                                           │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
⚠️ CKA Trap: Taint Removal Syntax

To remove a taint, append a - (minus sign) at the end. Many candidates forget this on the exam:

  • Add: kubectl taint node worker-1 key=value:NoSchedule
  • Remove: kubectl taint node worker-1 key=value:NoSchedule-

14.6 Node Management for Admins

Cluster administrators regularly need to perform node maintenance — OS upgrades, kernel patches, hardware replacement. Kubernetes provides three commands for safe node management. This is a very common CKA exam scenario.

1
Cordon

Mark node as unschedulable. Existing pods stay, but no new pods can be scheduled here.

2
Drain

Safely evict all pods to other nodes. Respects PDB, grace periods, and pod lifecycle.

3
Maintenance

Perform the actual work — OS upgrade, kernel patch, reboot, hardware swap.

4
Uncordon

Mark node as schedulable again. New pods can now be placed here.

Node Maintenance Workflow 
# Step 1: Cordon — stop new pods from scheduling
kubectl cordon worker-1
# node/worker-1 cordoned

# Verify: node shows SchedulingDisabled
kubectl get nodes
# NAME        STATUS                     ROLES    AGE
# worker-1    Ready,SchedulingDisabled   worker   30d

# Step 2: Drain — safely evict all pods
kubectl drain worker-1 --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data
# evicting pod default/anihpj-7d9f8b6c5d-abc12
# evicting pod default/anihpj-7d9f8b6c5d-def34
# node/worker-1 drained

# Step 3: Perform maintenance (OS upgrade, reboot, etc.)
# ... work happens here ...

# Step 4: Uncordon — allow pods again
kubectl uncordon worker-1
# node/worker-1 uncordoned

# Verify: node is Ready again (no SchedulingDisabled)
kubectl get nodes
# NAME        STATUS   ROLES    AGE
# worker-1    Ready    worker   30d
Node Maintenance Flow — Safe Pod Migration
  ┌─────── worker-1 (BEFORE) ───────┐     ┌─────── worker-2 ────────┐
  │                                  │     │                          │
  │  📦 anihpj-abc    📦 redis-xyz  │     │  📦 anihpj-ghi          │
  │  📦 anihpj-def                  │     │                          │
  │                                  │     │                          │
  └──────────────────────────────────┘     └──────────────────────────┘

  Step 1: kubectl cordon worker-1
  ┌─── worker-1 (🚫 SchedulingDisabled) ──┐
  │  📦 anihpj-abc  📦 redis-xyz  (stay)  │  ← existing pods remain
  │  📦 anihpj-def                (stay)  │  ← new pods blocked
  └────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Step 2: kubectl drain worker-1 --ignore-daemonsets
  ┌─── worker-1 (EMPTY) ────┐     ┌─────── worker-2 ────────────┐
  │                          │     │  📦 anihpj-ghi (existing)   │
  │  (all pods evicted)      │     │  📦 anihpj-abc (migrated)   │
  │                          │     │  📦 anihpj-def (migrated)   │
  │  🔧 Maintenance time!   │     │  📦 redis-xyz  (migrated)   │
  └──────────────────────────┘     └──────────────────────────────┘

  Step 3-4: Maintenance → uncordon
  ┌─── worker-1 (✅ Ready) ──┐
  │  Now accepts new pods!    │
  └───────────────────────────┘
🚨 Drain Gotchas
  • --ignore-daemonsets is required if DaemonSet pods exist (they can't be evicted)
  • --delete-emptydir-data is needed if pods use emptyDir volumes
  • --force only if there are standalone pods (not managed by RS/Deployment) — data will be lost!
  • Without these flags, drain will refuse and show an error — know which flag to add!

14.7 Topology Spread Constraints

Topology spread constraints give you fine-grained control over how pods are distributed across topology domains (zones, nodes, regions). Unlike pod anti-affinity which is "avoid same node", topology spread says "distribute evenly".

anihpj-topology-spread.yaml 
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj
spec:
  replicas: 6
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
    spec:
      topologySpreadConstraints:
      - maxSkew: 1                           # Max diff in pod count between zones
        topologyKey: topology.kubernetes.io/zone  # Spread across zones
        whenUnsatisfiable: DoNotSchedule     # Hard constraint
        labelSelector:
          matchLabels:
            app: anihpj
      - maxSkew: 1
        topologyKey: kubernetes.io/hostname   # Also spread across nodes
        whenUnsatisfiable: ScheduleAnyway    # Soft constraint
        labelSelector:
          matchLabels:
            app: anihpj
      containers:
      - name: django
        image: anihpj:v1
Topology Spread — Even Distribution Across Zones
  6 replicas, maxSkew: 1, topologyKey: zone

  ┌───── us-east-1a ──────┐  ┌───── us-east-1b ──────┐  ┌───── us-east-1c ──────┐
  │  📦 anihpj-1           │  │  📦 anihpj-3           │  │  📦 anihpj-5           │
  │  📦 anihpj-2           │  │  📦 anihpj-4           │  │  📦 anihpj-6           │
  │  Count: 2              │  │  Count: 2              │  │  Count: 2              │
  └────────────────────────┘  └────────────────────────┘  └────────────────────────┘

  maxSkew = max(count) - min(count) = 2 - 2 = 0  ✅ (within maxSkew of 1)

  ❌ Without topology spread (all could land on one zone):
  ┌───── us-east-1a ──────┐  ┌───── us-east-1b ──────┐  ┌───── us-east-1c ──────┐
  │  📦📦📦📦📦📦           │  │  (empty)               │  │  (empty)               │
  │  Count: 6   ← BAD!    │  │  Count: 0              │  │  Count: 0              │
  └────────────────────────┘  └────────────────────────┘  └────────────────────────┘
  Zone failure = total outage! 💀

Topology Spread Parameters

📏 maxSkew
Maximum allowed difference in pod counts between topology domains. Lower = more even.
🗺️ topologyKey
Node label to group by: kubernetes.io/hostname (node), topology.kubernetes.io/zone (zone)
🚫 whenUnsatisfiable
DoNotSchedule (hard — pod stays Pending) or ScheduleAnyway (soft — best effort)

14.8 Priority and Preemption

PriorityClasses let you assign importance levels to pods. When the cluster is full, a high-priority pod can preempt (evict) a lower-priority pod to make room.

priority-classes.yaml 
# High priority — critical production workloads
apiVersion: scheduling.k8s.io/v1
kind: PriorityClass
metadata:
  name: high-priority
value: 1000000
globalDefault: false
preemptionPolicy: PreemptLowerPriority
description: "Critical production workloads like anihpj"
---
# Low priority — batch jobs, can be evicted
apiVersion: scheduling.k8s.io/v1
kind: PriorityClass
metadata:
  name: low-priority
value: 100
globalDefault: false
preemptionPolicy: Never          # Don't preempt others
description: "Batch jobs and background tasks"
---
# Usage in Deployment
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
    spec:
      priorityClassName: high-priority   # ← assign priority
      containers:
      - name: django
        image: anihpj:v1
Preemption — High Priority Pod Evicts Low Priority
  Cluster is FULL — no free capacity

  ┌──── worker-1 ─────┐  ┌──── worker-2 ─────┐
  │ 📦 batch-job-1     │  │ 📦 batch-job-3     │
  │ (low-priority:100) │  │ (low-priority:100) │
  │ 📦 batch-job-2     │  │ 📦 batch-job-4     │
  │ (low-priority:100) │  │ (low-priority:100) │
  └────────────────────┘  └────────────────────┘

  NEW POD: anihpj (high-priority: 1000000) needs scheduling!

  Scheduler: "No room, but anihpj has higher priority..."
  → PREEMPTS batch-job-1 on worker-1

  ┌──── worker-1 ─────┐  ┌──── worker-2 ─────┐
  │ 🌟 anihpj          │  │ 📦 batch-job-3     │
  │ (high-priority!)   │  │ (low-priority:100) │
  │ 📦 batch-job-2     │  │ 📦 batch-job-4     │
  │ (low-priority:100) │  │ (low-priority:100) │
  └────────────────────┘  └────────────────────┘

  batch-job-1 was evicted to make room for anihpj  ⚡
💡 When to Use PriorityClasses

Use high priority for production-critical workloads (web servers, APIs). Use low priority for batch jobs, CI/CD runners, dev environments. This ensures production always gets resources first.

🧩 Scheduling Mechanisms Comparison — When to Use What

Mechanism Controls Direction Best For
nodeSelector Pod → Node 🟢 Attract (hard) Simple label matching
Node Affinity Pod → Node 🟢 Attract (hard/soft) Complex rules, preferred placement
Pod Affinity Pod → Pod 🟢 Attract (co-locate) Low latency (app near cache)
Pod Anti-Affinity Pod → Pod 🔴 Repel (spread) HA (replicas on different nodes)
Taints/Tolerations Node → Pod 🔴 Repel + allow Dedicated nodes, maintenance
Topology Spread Pod distribution ⚖️ Even spread Balance across zones/nodes
Static Pods Fixed to node 📌 Pinned Control plane components
PriorityClass Pod importance ⬆️ Preempt lower Critical vs batch workloads

14.9 Practice Exercises

These exercises mirror actual CKA exam questions. Practice each one until you can complete it in under 5 minutes.

🏋️
Exercise 14.1: Taints & Tolerations

1. Taint node01 with env=prod:NoSchedule
2. Create an nginx pod — verify it does NOT schedule on node01
3. Add a toleration — verify it now schedules there
4. Remove the taint

🏋️
Exercise 14.2: Node Affinity

1. Label node01 with disktype=ssd
2. Create pod with required nodeAffinity for ssd nodes
3. Create pod with preferred nodeAffinity for ssd
4. Verify with kubectl get pods -o wide

🏋️
Exercise 14.3: Pod Anti-Affinity

1. Create anihpj deployment (replicas: 3)
2. Add podAntiAffinity — no 2 pods on same node
3. Verify: kubectl get pods -o wide (all on different nodes)
4. Scale to 4 — what happens if only 3 nodes?

🏋️
Exercise 14.4: Node Maintenance

1. Cordon worker-1
2. Deploy nginx (replicas: 3) — verify none land on worker-1
3. Drain worker-1 safely
4. Uncordon and verify

Exercise 14.1 — Solution (try it yourself first!) 
# 1. Taint the node
kubectl taint node node01 env=prod:NoSchedule

# 2. Create nginx pod (no toleration)
kubectl run nginx-test --image=nginx
kubectl get pod nginx-test -o wide
# → Should be on a different node (not node01)

# 3. Create pod WITH toleration
cat <<'EOF' | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: nginx-tolerant
spec:
  tolerations:
  - key: "env"
    operator: "Equal"
    value: "prod"
    effect: "NoSchedule"
  containers:
  - name: nginx
    image: nginx
EOF
kubectl get pod nginx-tolerant -o wide
# → Can schedule on node01

# 4. Remove taint
kubectl taint node node01 env=prod:NoSchedule-

# Clean up
kubectl delete pod nginx-test nginx-tolerant

🔑 Chapter 14 Quick Reference

Action Command
Label a nodekubectl label node NAME key=value
Remove labelkubectl label node NAME key-
Add taintkubectl taint node NAME key=val:Effect
Remove taintkubectl taint node NAME key=val:Effect-
Cordon nodekubectl cordon NAME
Drain nodekubectl drain NAME --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data
Uncordon nodekubectl uncordon NAME
Check node taintskubectl describe node NAME | grep Taints
Show node labelskubectl get nodes --show-labels
Check pod placementkubectl get pods -o wide

⚠️ CKA Gotcha: Scheduling & Node Management Traps

  • nodeSelector vs nodeAffinity — nodeSelector is simple key=value matching. nodeAffinity supports In, NotIn, Exists, DoesNotExist. The exam tests both
  • Taint effect matters — NoSchedule = new pods blocked. NoExecute = existing pods EVICTED. PreferNoSchedule = soft preference. Mixing them up = wrong answer
  • kubectl drain flags — Without --ignore-daemonsets it fails on DaemonSet pods. Without --delete-emptydir-data it fails on emptyDir volumes. Always use both
  • Cordon ≠ Drain — cordon only prevents NEW pods. drain evicts existing pods AND cordons. After maintenance: uncordon
  • Toleration must EXACTLY match taint — key, value, AND effect must match. A toleration with the wrong effect won't work

CKA Speed Tip — Node maintenance in 30 seconds:
kubectl drain node01 --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data
… perform maintenance (upgrade, reboot) …
kubectl uncordon node01
Done. Pods will be rescheduled back automatically.

📝 Chapter 14 Practice Drill

Timed challenge (target: 6 minutes):

  • 1. Label node node01 with disk=ssd
  • 2. Create a pod with nodeSelector: disk: ssd — verify it lands on node01
  • 3. Taint node01: kubectl taint node node01 maintenance=true:NoSchedule
  • 4. Create another pod WITHOUT toleration — it should go to a different node
  • 5. Drain node01, uncordon, verify pods reschedule

📝 Chapter 14 — CKA Practice Questions

Q1
Schedule a pod named gpu-pod to run ONLY on nodes labeled hardware=gpu. Show the imperative generation and the relevant YAML fields.
Show Answer
Answer
# First, label the target node
kubectl label node worker-1 hardware=gpu

# Generate pod YAML (add nodeSelector manually)
kubectl run gpu-pod --image=nvidia/cuda:12.0-base \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml > gpu-pod.yaml

# Add nodeSelector to spec:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: gpu-pod
spec:
  nodeSelector:         # ← Simple key-value matching
    hardware: gpu
  containers:
  - name: gpu-pod
    image: nvidia/cuda:12.0-base

kubectl apply -f gpu-pod.yaml

# Verify placement
kubectl get pod gpu-pod -o wide
# NAME      READY  STATUS   NODE      
# gpu-pod   1/1    Running  worker-1
Explanation

nodeSelector is the simplest way to constrain pods to specific nodes. It uses exact label matching — the node MUST have all specified labels. If no node matches, the pod stays Pending. On the CKA, nodeSelector is the most commonly tested scheduling mechanism because it's fast to configure. Remember: the label must exist on the node BEFORE the pod is created (or the pod stays Pending). Use kubectl get nodes --show-labels to verify.

Q2
A node named node01 has a taint env=production:NoSchedule. Write a pod YAML that can be scheduled on this node. What happens if you use NoExecute instead?
Show Answer
Answer
# Apply the taint
kubectl taint node node01 env=production:NoSchedule

# Pod YAML with matching toleration:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: prod-pod
spec:
  tolerations:
  - key: "env"
    operator: "Equal"
    value: "production"
    effect: "NoSchedule"
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx

# Taint effects comparison:
# NoSchedule:    Won't schedule new pods without toleration
#                Existing pods stay
# PreferNoSchedule: Try to avoid, but allow if no other option
# NoExecute:     Won't schedule new pods AND evicts existing
#                pods without toleration

# NoExecute with tolerationSeconds:
  tolerations:
  - key: "env"
    operator: "Equal"
    value: "production"
    effect: "NoExecute"
    tolerationSeconds: 3600   # Stay for 1 hour, then evicted
Explanation

NoSchedule only prevents NEW pods from being scheduled. NoExecute is more aggressive — it also EVICTS pods that are already running on the node unless they have a matching toleration. tolerationSeconds allows a pod to remain for a limited time before eviction. On the CKA, the taint effect is critical: NoSchedule for maintenance windows (existing pods stay), NoExecute for draining workloads. The operator: "Equal" requires exact key+value match; operator: "Exists" matches any value for that key.

Q3
You need to drain a node for maintenance. What sequence of commands should you use, and what safety flags are important?
Show Answer
Answer
# Step 1: Cordon the node (mark unschedulable)
kubectl cordon node01
# node/node01 cordoned

# Step 2: Drain the node (evict all pods)
kubectl drain node01 --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data
# Evicts all pods, ignoring DaemonSet-managed pods

# Important flags:
# --ignore-daemonsets    Required! DaemonSet pods can't be evicted
# --delete-emptydir-data  Required if pods use emptyDir volumes
# --force                 Required for pods not managed by a controller
#                         (standalone pods will be LOST forever!)
# --grace-period=30       Override default termination grace period

# Verify node is drained
kubectl get nodes
# NAME     STATUS                     ROLES
# node01   Ready,SchedulingDisabled   worker

kubectl get pods -o wide | grep node01
# (no pods, except DaemonSet ones)

# Step 3: Perform maintenance...

# Step 4: Uncordon when done
kubectl uncordon node01
# node/node01 uncordoned (scheduling re-enabled)
Explanation

kubectl drain = cordon + evict all pods. Without --ignore-daemonsets, it fails because DaemonSet pods are supposed to run on every node. Without --delete-emptydir-data, it fails for pods using emptyDir (data will be lost). --force deletes standalone pods (not managed by a controller) — these pods are gone permanently since no controller recreates them. On the CKA, always include --ignore-daemonsets. If the drain refuses, read the error — it tells you exactly which flag to add.

Q4
What is the difference between nodeSelector and nodeAffinity? When must you use nodeAffinity instead?
Show Answer
Answer
Feature nodeSelector nodeAffinity
Operators Exact match only In, NotIn, Exists, DoesNotExist, Gt, Lt
Required vs Preferred Always required Both options available
Complexity Simple key:value Complex expressions 
# nodeAffinity — required (hard rule)
spec:
  affinity:
    nodeAffinity:
      requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
        nodeSelectorTerms:
        - matchExpressions:
          - key: disktype
            operator: In
            values: ["ssd", "nvme"]    # Match either value

# nodeAffinity — preferred (soft rule)
      preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
      - weight: 80
        preference:
          matchExpressions:
          - key: zone
            operator: In
            values: ["us-east-1a"]
Explanation

Use nodeAffinity when you need: (1) multiple possible values (operator: In), (2) negation (NotIn), (3) soft preferences (preferred vs required), or (4) complex expressions. nodeSelector only supports exact single-value matching. On the CKA, nodeAffinity is harder to type from memory — bookmark the docs page. requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution means "enforce at schedule time, don't evict if label changes later." The word required = hard constraint; preferred = best-effort.

Q5
Schedule a pod on a specific node named controlplane without using labels or taints. What mechanism allows this?
Show Answer
Answer
# Use nodeName to bypass the scheduler entirely
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: debug-pod
spec:
  nodeName: controlplane    # ← Direct node assignment
  containers:
  - name: debug
    image: busybox
    command: ["sleep", "3600"]

# This pod is NOT scheduled by kube-scheduler
# It's bound directly to the named node
# If controlplane doesn't exist → pod stays Pending forever

# Verify:
kubectl get pod debug-pod -o wide
# NAME       READY  STATUS   NODE
# debug-pod  1/1    Running  controlplane
Explanation

nodeName bypasses the scheduler completely — the pod is directly assigned to the specified node. This ignores all taints, tolerations, nodeSelector, and affinity rules. It's useful for debugging or when the scheduler is down. On the CKA, nodeName appears in questions about "static scheduling" or "scheduling without the scheduler." Drawbacks: if the node goes down, the pod is NOT rescheduled (no controller watching). Use only for temporary debugging, not production workloads.

Q6
A pod is stuck in Pending state. kubectl describe pod shows: "0/3 nodes are available: 1 node(s) had taint {node-role.kubernetes.io/control-plane:}, 2 node(s) didn't match Pod's node affinity/selector." Diagnose and fix.
Show Answer
Answer
# Diagnosis:
# - 1 node: tainted as control-plane (expected)
# - 2 nodes: don't match the pod's nodeSelector or affinity

# Step 1: Check what the pod requires
kubectl get pod pending-pod -o jsonpath='{.spec.nodeSelector}'
# {"gpu":"true"}

# Step 2: Check available node labels
kubectl get nodes --show-labels | grep gpu
# (no output — no node has gpu=true label!)

# Fix Option 1: Label a worker node
kubectl label node worker-1 gpu=true

# Fix Option 2: Remove the nodeSelector from the pod
# (Delete and recreate without nodeSelector)
kubectl get pod pending-pod -o yaml > pod.yaml
# Edit pod.yaml: remove nodeSelector section
kubectl delete pod pending-pod
kubectl apply -f pod.yaml

# Verify
kubectl get pod pending-pod
# STATUS: Running
Explanation

The scheduler message tells you exactly why no node is suitable. On the CKA, this is a common troubleshooting scenario. The diagnostic flow: (1) Check Events in kubectl describe pod for the scheduler message, (2) Extract the pod's scheduling constraints (nodeSelector, affinity, tolerations), (3) Compare against available nodes and their labels/taints. Remember: control-plane nodes have a default taint — pods won't schedule there unless they have the matching toleration.

Q7
How do you remove a taint from a node? Show the command for removing env=production:NoSchedule from node01.
Show Answer
Answer
# Remove a specific taint (note the trailing minus -)
kubectl taint node node01 env=production:NoSchedule-
# node/node01 untainted

# Remove taint by key only (removes all effects for that key)
kubectl taint node node01 env-
# Removes env:NoSchedule, env:NoExecute, etc.

# Verify taint is removed
kubectl describe node node01 | grep -i taint
# Taints: <none>

# Common mistake — forgetting the minus sign:
kubectl taint node node01 env=production:NoSchedule
# This ADDS the taint instead of removing it!
Explanation

The trailing - (minus sign) at the end of the taint specification removes it. This is the same pattern as kubectl label node node01 key- for removing labels. On the CKA, forgetting the - is a common time-wasting error that ADDS the taint instead. If you specify key=value:effect, it removes that exact taint. If you specify just key-, it removes all taints with that key regardless of value or effect.

Q8
What is podAffinity vs podAntiAffinity? Give a scenario where you'd use each on the CKA exam.
Show Answer
Answer
# podAffinity: Schedule NEAR pods with specific labels
# Use case: web pod should be on same node as cache pod
spec:
  affinity:
    podAffinity:
      requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
      - labelSelector:
          matchExpressions:
          - key: app
            operator: In
            values: ["redis-cache"]
        topologyKey: kubernetes.io/hostname

# podAntiAffinity: Schedule AWAY from pods with specific labels
# Use case: spread replicas across different nodes
spec:
  affinity:
    podAntiAffinity:
      requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
      - labelSelector:
          matchExpressions:
          - key: app
            operator: In
            values: ["webapp"]
        topologyKey: kubernetes.io/hostname
# Each webapp replica lands on a different node
Explanation

podAffinity = co-locate pods (low latency between services). podAntiAffinity = spread pods apart (high availability). topologyKey defines the "zone" — kubernetes.io/hostname means per-node, topology.kubernetes.io/zone means per-AZ. On the CKA, anti-affinity is more commonly tested — especially "ensure no two replicas of the same app run on the same node." If you have 3 replicas and only 2 nodes with required anti-affinity, the 3rd pod stays Pending.

Q9
How do you check which taints are applied to all nodes in the cluster? And how do you find which pods have tolerations?
Show Answer
Answer
# View taints on all nodes
kubectl get nodes -o custom-columns=\
NAME:.metadata.name,\
TAINTS:.spec.taints

# Or with describe
kubectl describe nodes | grep -A5 "Taints:"
# Taints: node-role.kubernetes.io/control-plane:NoSchedule
# Taints: env=production:NoSchedule

# JSON path for specific node
kubectl get node node01 -o jsonpath='{.spec.taints[*]}'

# Find pods with tolerations
kubectl get pods -A -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.metadata.name}{"\t"}{.spec.tolerations}{"\n"}{end}'

# Or describe a specific pod
kubectl describe pod my-pod | grep -A10 "Tolerations:"
# Tolerations: node.kubernetes.io/not-ready:NoExecute op=Exists for 300s
#              node.kubernetes.io/unreachable:NoExecute op=Exists for 300s
Explanation

Every pod automatically gets two default tolerations: node.kubernetes.io/not-ready:NoExecute and node.kubernetes.io/unreachable:NoExecute with 300s tolerance — this is why pods stay for 5 minutes after a node becomes unreachable. On the CKA, quickly checking taints with custom-columns is efficient. The control-plane taint node-role.kubernetes.io/control-plane:NoSchedule prevents workload pods from scheduling on the control plane — this is normal and expected. DaemonSet pods (like kube-proxy) tolerate this automatically.

Q10
A DaemonSet pod is not running on node02 but runs on all other nodes. What are the possible causes and how do you investigate?
Show Answer
Answer
# Step 1: Check if node02 is cordoned
