kubectl get node node02
# STATUS: Ready,SchedulingDisabled ← cordoned!

# Step 2: Check taints on node02
kubectl describe node node02 | grep Taints
# Taints: special=true:NoSchedule

# Step 3: Check DaemonSet tolerations
kubectl get ds monitoring-agent -o jsonpath='{.spec.template.spec.tolerations}'
# Missing toleration for special=true:NoSchedule!

# Fix Option 1: Add toleration to DaemonSet
kubectl edit ds monitoring-agent
# Add toleration for the taint

# Fix Option 2: Remove the taint
kubectl taint node node02 special-

# Step 4: Check node conditions
kubectl describe node node02 | grep -A5 Conditions
# MemoryPressure: True ← node under memory pressure
# DiskPressure: True   ← node under disk pressure
# These auto-add taints that evict pods!

# Step 5: Check DaemonSet's nodeSelector
kubectl get ds monitoring-agent -o jsonpath='{.spec.template.spec.nodeSelector}'
# {"os":"linux"} — does node02 have this label?
Explanation

DaemonSets are designed to run on every node, but they still respect taints and nodeSelectors. Common causes for missing DaemonSet pods: (1) node is cordoned (scheduling disabled), (2) taint without matching toleration, (3) nodeSelector mismatch, (4) node conditions like MemoryPressure auto-add taints. On the CKA, DaemonSet scheduling issues are a classic troubleshooting question. Note: DaemonSets automatically tolerate node.kubernetes.io/unschedulable:NoSchedule (cordon), but if you manually add a custom taint, the DaemonSet must explicitly tolerate it.

Q11
Create a Deployment with topologySpreadConstraints that spreads pods evenly across nodes with a maximum skew of 1. What happens when the constraint can't be satisfied?
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj-web
spec:
  replicas: 6
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
    spec:
      topologySpreadConstraints:
      - maxSkew: 1                           # Max difference between zones
        topologyKey: kubernetes.io/hostname   # Spread across nodes
        whenUnsatisfiable: DoNotSchedule      # Block if can't spread
        labelSelector:
          matchLabels:
            app: anihpj
      containers:
      - name: web
        image: anihpj:v1

# With 3 nodes and 6 replicas:
#   node1: 2 pods, node2: 2 pods, node3: 2 pods ✅
#   node1: 3 pods, node2: 2 pods, node3: 1 pod  ❌ skew=2

# whenUnsatisfiable options:
# DoNotSchedule  → Pod stays Pending (strict)
# ScheduleAnyway → Schedule anyway, best-effort (soft)

# Check distribution:
kubectl get pods -l app=anihpj -o wide \
  --sort-by='.spec.nodeName'
# Shows which pod is on which node
Explanation

topologySpreadConstraints provide fine-grained control over pod distribution across failure domains (nodes, zones, regions). maxSkew defines the maximum allowed difference in pod count between topology domains. topologyKey determines what defines a domain — kubernetes.io/hostname for nodes, topology.kubernetes.io/zone for zones. whenUnsatisfiable: DoNotSchedule makes pods Pending if they'd violate the constraint — like a hard anti-affinity but more flexible. On the CKA, topology spread is the modern replacement for podAntiAffinity when you need even distribution with more control.

Q12
What is a PriorityClass? Create a high-priority class and show how a high-priority pod can preempt a lower-priority one.
Show Answer
Answer
# Step 1: Create PriorityClasses
apiVersion: scheduling.k8s.io/v1
kind: PriorityClass
metadata:
  name: high-priority
value: 1000000
globalDefault: false
preemptionPolicy: PreemptLowerPriority
description: "Critical production workloads"
---
apiVersion: scheduling.k8s.io/v1
kind: PriorityClass
metadata:
  name: low-priority
value: 100
globalDefault: false
preemptionPolicy: Never     # ← Won't evict others
description: "Background batch jobs"

# Step 2: Use in a Pod spec
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: critical-app
spec:
  priorityClassName: high-priority   # ← Reference by name
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: anihpj:v1
    resources:
      requests:
        cpu: "2"
        memory: "4Gi"

# What happens during preemption:
# 1. Cluster is full — no node has space for critical-app
# 2. Scheduler finds low-priority pods on a node
# 3. Scheduler evicts low-priority pods to make room
# 4. critical-app gets scheduled

# Check PriorityClasses:
kubectl get priorityclasses
# NAME            VALUE      GLOBAL-DEFAULT
# high-priority   1000000    false
# low-priority    100        false
# system-*        very high  (built-in for kube-system)
Explanation

PriorityClasses assign scheduling priority to pods. When the cluster is full, the scheduler evicts lower-priority pods to make room for higher-priority ones. value is an integer (higher = more important). preemptionPolicy: PreemptLowerPriority (default) allows evicting others; Never means the pod waits without evicting. Kubernetes has built-in system priority classes (e.g., system-cluster-critical value 2000000000) for control plane components. On the CKA, you may need to create a PriorityClass and assign it to a pod. Remember: PriorityClass is cluster-scoped (no namespace), and the API group is scheduling.k8s.io/v1.

Chapter 15: Resource Management

Every container in Kubernetes can request a minimum amount of CPU and memory, and be limited to a maximum. Understanding requests, limits, QoS classes, LimitRanges, and ResourceQuotas is critical for cluster stability — and prominently tested on the CKA exam. A misconfigured resource setting can crash an entire node.

🟡 Intermediate ⏱️ ~1 hour 📊 Resources 📝 CKA EXAM

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ Set CPU and memory requests (scheduling guarantee) and limits (hard ceiling)
  • ✅ Understand QoS classes — Guaranteed, Burstable, BestEffort
  • ✅ Configure LimitRange for namespace-wide default limits
  • ✅ Configure ResourceQuota to cap total namespace consumption
  • ✅ Know what happens when CPU is exceeded (throttle) vs memory (OOMKilled)
🎓 CKA Relevance
Workloads & Scheduling 15% Cluster Architecture 25%
Resource Questions on the CKA

You'll likely be asked to:

  • Create pods with specific resource requests and limits
  • Create LimitRange or ResourceQuota for a namespace
  • Identify why a pod is OOMKilled or stuck in Pending
  • Know: kubectl top pods and kubectl top nodes

15.1 Resource Requests and Limits

Every container can specify requests (minimum guaranteed) and limits (maximum allowed). The scheduler uses requests to decide where to place pods. The kubelet enforces limits at runtime.

📋
Requests

"I need at least this much." Used by the scheduler for placement. The node must have this amount free or the pod won't be scheduled.

🚧
Limits

"Don't use more than this." Enforced by the kubelet. CPU exceeds limit → throttled. Memory exceeds limit → OOMKilled.

anihpj-resources.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj
spec:
  containers:
  - name: django
    image: anihpj:v1
    resources:
      requests:                  # Scheduling guarantee
        cpu: "250m"              # 0.25 CPU cores (250 millicores)
        memory: "256Mi"          # 256 MiB RAM
      limits:                    # Hard ceiling
        cpu: "500m"              # 0.5 CPU cores max
        memory: "512Mi"          # 512 MiB RAM max

CPU & Memory Units

⚙️ CPU: 1000m = 1 core
100m = 0.1 core, 250m = 0.25 core, 1 = 1 full core, 2 = 2 cores
💾 Memory: Mi, Gi
128Mi = 128 MiB, 1Gi = 1 GiB. Use Mi/Gi (binary) not MB/GB (decimal).
⏳ CPU exceeded
Throttled — process slows down but keeps running. Not killed.
💀 Memory exceeded
OOMKilled — container is killed immediately. Restarts per restartPolicy.

⏳ CPU Exceeded → Throttled

  • Container is NOT killed
  • Process slows down, capped at the limit
  • No restarts, no OOM events
  • Hard to detect — app just becomes slow
  • Diagnose: kubectl top pod shows CPU pinned at limit
  • Fix: Increase limits.cpu or optimize app

💀 Memory Exceeded → OOMKilled

  • Container is killed immediately (SIGKILL)
  • Exit code 137 in pod description
  • Pod restarts → often leads to CrashLoopBackOff
  • Easy to detect: status shows OOMKilled
  • Diagnose: kubectl describe pod → Last State → OOMKilled
  • Fix: Increase limits.memory or fix memory leak
  Node Capacity: 4 CPU, 8Gi Memory
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                                                                  │
  │  ┌──────────── anihpj container ─────────────┐                  │
  │  │                                            │                  │
  │  │  CPU:    [====request 250m====|...limit 500m....]             │
  │  │          ├─── guaranteed ────┤├── can burst ──┤               │
  │  │                                            │                  │
  │  │  Memory: [====request 256Mi===|...limit 512Mi...]             │
  │  │          ├─── guaranteed ────┤├── can use ───┤                │
  │  │                                   ↑ exceed = OOMKill 💀      │
  │  └────────────────────────────────────────────┘                  │
  │                                                                  │
  │  What happens:                                                   │
  │  ├── Actual CPU  = 300m  → ✅ Within limit, no throttle         │
  │  ├── Actual CPU  = 600m  → ⏳ Throttled to 500m                 │
  │  ├── Actual Mem  = 400Mi → ✅ Within limit, fine                │
  │  └── Actual Mem  = 600Mi → 💀 OOMKilled! Container restarts    │
  │                                                                  │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
🚨 Never Skip Resource Settings in Production
  • No requests: Scheduler doesn't know what the pod needs → random placement → resource contention
  • No limits: Pod can consume ALL available node memory → OOMs other pods → cascading failure
  • Always set both on every production container!
Monitoring Resource Usage 
# Check actual usage vs requests/limits
kubectl top pods
# NAME      CPU(cores)   MEMORY(bytes)
# anihpj   120m         180Mi

kubectl top nodes
# NAME       CPU(cores)   CPU%   MEMORY(bytes)   MEMORY%
# worker-1   850m         42%    3200Mi           40%

# Find pods with no resource limits (dangerous!)
kubectl get pods -A -o json | jq '.items[] | 
  select(.spec.containers[].resources.limits == null) | 
  .metadata.name'

Troubleshooting OOMKilled Pods

One of the most common issues on the CKA and in production. Here's a systematic approach:

1
Find OOMKilled Pods

kubectl get pods -A | grep OOMKilled or grep CrashLoopBackOff (often caused by repeated OOM)

2
Confirm OOM Reason

kubectl describe pod NAME → look for Last State: Terminated, Reason: OOMKilled, Exit Code: 137

3
Check Current Limits

kubectl get pod NAME -o jsonpath='{.spec.containers[0].resources}' → see current memory limit

4
Check Actual Usage

kubectl top pod NAME → if usage is close to limit, the limit is too low

5
Fix & Verify

Increase memory limit: kubectl set resources deployment/NAME --limits=memory=512Mi. Watch pod restart successfully.

OOMKilled Diagnosis Steps 
# Step 1: Find OOMKilled pods
kubectl get pods -A | grep OOMKilled
kubectl get pods -A | grep CrashLoopBackOff   # often caused by OOM

# Step 2: Check the pod's last state
kubectl describe pod anihpj-abc12 | grep -A5 "Last State"
#   Last State:  Terminated
#     Reason:    OOMKilled
#     Exit Code: 137       ← 137 = SIGKILL (memory limit hit)

# Step 3: Check current resource config
kubectl get pod anihpj-abc12 -o jsonpath='{.spec.containers[0].resources}'
# {"limits":{"memory":"256Mi"},"requests":{"memory":"128Mi"}}

# Step 4: Check actual usage (if pod is running)
kubectl top pod anihpj-abc12
# CPU: 100m    MEMORY: 245Mi   ← close to 256Mi limit!

# Step 5: Fix — increase memory limit
kubectl set resources deployment/anihpj -c django \
  --limits=memory=512Mi --requests=memory=256Mi
OOMKill Decision Tree — Is It the Container or the Node?
  Pod terminated with Exit Code 137?
       │
       ├── YES → OOMKilled!
       │        │
       │   Was the pod using more memory
       │   than its spec.resources.limits.memory?
       │        │
       │   ┌────┴────┐
       │   YES        NO
       │   │          │
       │   Container  Node-level OOM
       │   limit hit  (node ran out of memory)
       │   │          │
       │   Fix:       Fix: add more nodes,
       │   Increase   reduce other workloads,
       │   memory     or fix memory leaks
       │   limits
       │
       └── Exit Code 1 = app error (not OOM)

15.2 QoS Classes — Quality of Service

Kubernetes automatically assigns a QoS class to every pod based on its resource configuration. This determines the eviction priority when a node runs out of resources.

QoS Class Criteria Eviction Priority anihpj Use Case
Guaranteed Every container has requests == limits (both CPU & memory set and equal) Last to be evicted (safest) 🛡️ ✅ Production anihpj
Burstable At least one container has requests < limits (or only requests set) Evicted after BestEffort ⚠️ ⚠️ Dev/staging anihpj
BestEffort No requests or limits set on any container First to be evicted (riskiest) 💀 ❌ Never in production
QoS Eviction Order — Node Under Memory Pressure
  Node memory: 95% used — kubelet starts evicting pods!

  Eviction Order:
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                                                   │
  │  💀 FIRST: BestEffort pods                       │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
  │  │ Pod: test-pod (no requests, no limits)      │ │
  │  │ → EVICTED immediately                       │ │
  │  └─────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
  │          │                                        │
  │          ▼ (still need more memory?)              │
  │  ⚠️ SECOND: Burstable pods                      │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
  │  │ Pod: dev-anihpj (requests < limits)         │ │
  │  │ → EVICTED if using more than requests       │ │
  │  └─────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
  │          │                                        │
  │          ▼ (extreme pressure only)                │
  │  🛡️ LAST: Guaranteed pods                       │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
  │  │ Pod: prod-anihpj (requests == limits)       │ │
  │  │ → Only evicted as absolute last resort      │ │
  │  └─────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
QoS Examples 
# Guaranteed QoS — requests EQUAL limits
resources:
  requests:
    cpu: "500m"
    memory: "512Mi"
  limits:
    cpu: "500m"          # same as request
    memory: "512Mi"      # same as request
# → QoS: Guaranteed ✅

---
# Burstable QoS — requests LESS THAN limits
resources:
  requests:
    cpu: "250m"
    memory: "256Mi"
  limits:
    cpu: "500m"          # more than request
    memory: "512Mi"      # more than request
# → QoS: Burstable ⚠️

---
# BestEffort QoS — NO resources at all
# (just don't include the resources section)
# → QoS: BestEffort 💀
Check Pod QoS Class 
# Check QoS class of a pod
kubectl get pod anihpj -o jsonpath='{.status.qosClass}'
# Guaranteed

# Check QoS of all pods
kubectl get pods -o custom-columns='NAME:.metadata.name,QOS:.status.qosClass'
# NAME              QOS
# anihpj-abc12      Guaranteed
# dev-app-xyz       Burstable
# test-pod          BestEffort
💡 Production QoS Strategy for anihpj

For your production stack, aim for these QoS classes:

  • Guaranteed: anihpj Django (production), PostgreSQL — set requests == limits
  • Burstable: anihpj staging, Redis cache — allow bursting for efficiency
  • BestEffort: Only for throwaway test pods in dev. Never in production!

Pro tip: Use Guaranteed for anything facing users. Use Burstable for internal services that benefit from occasional burst capacity.

15.3 LimitRange — Namespace Default Limits

A LimitRange sets default resource requests and limits for containers in a namespace. It also enforces min/max boundaries. This prevents pods from running without any resource configuration.

limitrange.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: LimitRange
metadata:
  name: anihpj-limits
  namespace: anihpj-dev
spec:
  limits:
  - type: Container
    default:                     # Default LIMITS (if not specified)
      cpu: "500m"
      memory: "512Mi"
    defaultRequest:              # Default REQUESTS (if not specified)
      cpu: "250m"
      memory: "256Mi"
    max:                         # Maximum allowed
      cpu: "2"
      memory: "2Gi"
    min:                         # Minimum allowed
      cpu: "50m"
      memory: "32Mi"
  - type: Pod
    max:
      cpu: "4"
      memory: "4Gi"
LimitRange in Action 
# Create namespace and LimitRange
kubectl create namespace anihpj-dev
kubectl apply -f limitrange.yaml

# Create pod WITHOUT specifying resources
kubectl run test-pod --image=nginx -n anihpj-dev

# Check — LimitRange injected default values!
kubectl get pod test-pod -n anihpj-dev -o jsonpath='{.spec.containers[0].resources}'
# {"limits":{"cpu":"500m","memory":"512Mi"},"requests":{"cpu":"250m","memory":"256Mi"}}

# Try to create pod exceeding max
# → Error: maximum cpu usage per Container is 2, but limit is 4
LimitRange — Automatic Defaults
  Namespace: anihpj-dev (with LimitRange)

  Pod created WITHOUT resources:        Pod received DEFAULTS:
  ┌──────────────────────────┐         ┌──────────────────────────────┐
  │ spec:                    │   →     │ spec:                        │
  │   containers:            │   →     │   containers:                │
  │   - name: nginx          │   →     │   - name: nginx              │
  │     image: nginx         │   →     │     image: nginx             │
  │     # no resources!      │   →     │     resources:               │
  │                          │   →     │       requests:              │
  │                          │   →     │         cpu: "250m"    ← injected! │
  │                          │   →     │         memory: "256Mi" ← injected!│
  │                          │   →     │       limits:               │
  │                          │   →     │         cpu: "500m"    ← injected! │
  │                          │   →     │         memory: "512Mi" ← injected!│
  └──────────────────────────┘         └──────────────────────────────┘

15.4 ResourceQuota — Namespace Capacity Control

A ResourceQuota limits the total resources consumed across all pods in a namespace. This prevents one team from using all cluster resources and is a key tool for multi-tenant clusters.

resourcequota.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: anihpj-quota
  namespace: anihpj-dev
spec:
  hard:
    # Compute resources
    requests.cpu: "4"            # Total CPU requests across all pods
    requests.memory: "8Gi"       # Total memory requests
    limits.cpu: "8"              # Total CPU limits
    limits.memory: "16Gi"        # Total memory limits

    # Object count limits
    pods: "10"                   # Max 10 pods in this namespace
    services: "5"                # Max 5 services
    persistentvolumeclaims: "5"  # Max 5 PVCs
    configmaps: "20"
    secrets: "20"
ResourceQuota Commands 
# Apply quota
kubectl apply -f resourcequota.yaml

# Check quota usage
kubectl get resourcequota -n anihpj-dev
# NAME           AGE   REQUEST                                              LIMIT
# anihpj-quota   5m    requests.cpu: 750m/4, requests.memory: 768Mi/8Gi    limits.cpu: 1500m/8, limits.memory: 1536Mi/16Gi

# Detailed view
kubectl describe resourcequota anihpj-quota -n anihpj-dev
# Name:                   anihpj-quota
# Namespace:              anihpj-dev
# Resource                Used    Hard
# --------                ----    ----
# limits.cpu              1500m   8
# limits.memory           1536Mi  16Gi
# pods                    3       10
# requests.cpu            750m    4
# requests.memory         768Mi   8Gi
Resource Control Hierarchy — 3 Levels
  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  LEVEL 1: ResourceQuota (namespace total budget)               │
  │  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
  │  │ "anihpj-dev can use max 4 CPUs, 8Gi memory total"       │  │
  │  │  Used: 750m / 4 CPU    Used: 768Mi / 8Gi memory         │  │
  │  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
  │       │                                                        │
  │       ▼                                                        │
  │  LEVEL 2: LimitRange (per-container defaults & bounds)       │
  │  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
  │  │ "Each container: default 250m req, 500m limit"           │  │
  │  │ "Min: 50m CPU, Max: 2 CPU per container"                 │  │
  │  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
  │       │                                                        │
  │       ▼                                                        │
  │  LEVEL 3: Pod spec (individual container settings)            │
  │  ┌────────────┐  ┌────────────┐  ┌────────────┐              │
  │  │ anihpj-1   │  │ anihpj-2   │  │ anihpj-3   │              │
  │  │ req: 250m  │  │ req: 250m  │  │ req: 250m  │              │
  │  │ lim: 500m  │  │ lim: 500m  │  │ lim: 500m  │              │
  │  └────────────┘  └────────────┘  └────────────┘              │
  │  Total: 750m request, 1500m limit (within quota ✅)           │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
⚠️ ResourceQuota Requires Resource Specs

When a ResourceQuota is active in a namespace, every pod must specify resource requests and limits. Without them, the pod creation will be rejected. Use a LimitRange to auto-inject defaults so developers don't have to specify them manually.

🔥

Scenario: "Pod Creation Rejected — Forbidden"

CKA EXAM

Symptom: kubectl run test --image=nginx -n dev → Error: forbidden: failed quota: dev-quota: must specify limits.cpu,limits.memory

Step 1 — Check quota

kubectl describe resourcequota -n dev → quota exists and enforces limits. No LimitRange present to auto-inject defaults.

Step 2 — Two fixes

Option A: Add resources to pod spec. Option B: Create a LimitRange with default and defaultRequest so future pods get automatic defaults.

Step 3 — Verify

Re-run kubectl run test --image=nginx -n dev → pod created. kubectl describe resourcequota -n dev → Used values increased.

Lesson: When ResourceQuota exists, always pair it with a LimitRange. This is a classic CKA trap — the exam expects you to know both work together.

🔑 Chapter 15 Quick Reference

Action Command
Check pod resource usagekubectl top pods
Check node resource usagekubectl top nodes
View pod QoS classkubectl get pod NAME -o jsonpath='{.status.qosClass}'
View LimitRangekubectl describe limitrange -n NAMESPACE
View ResourceQuotakubectl describe resourcequota -n NAMESPACE
Check OOMKilled podskubectl get pods | grep OOMKilled
View container resourceskubectl get pod NAME -o jsonpath='{.spec.containers[*].resources}'

⚠️ CKA Gotcha: Resource Management Traps

  • Requests vs Limits — requests = guaranteed minimum (used for scheduling). limits = maximum allowed (enforced at runtime). Scheduler uses requests, NOT limits
  • CPU units — 1 = 1 vCPU. 500m = 0.5 CPU. 100m = 0.1 CPU. The exam loves asking you to convert between these
  • Memory units — Mi (mebibytes) ≠ M (megabytes). Use Mi for consistency. 128Mi = 134MB
  • QoS classes — Guaranteed (requests=limits for all containers), Burstable (some requests set), BestEffort (no requests/limits). OOM killer targets BestEffort first
  • LimitRange applies to NEW pods only — Existing pods aren't affected. ResourceQuota blocks creation when the namespace total is exceeded

CKA Speed Tip: Find which pods are hogging resources:
kubectl top pods -A --sort-by=cpu — highest CPU consumers
kubectl top pods -A --sort-by=memory — highest memory consumers
kubectl describe node <name> | grep -A5 "Allocated resources" — node-level usage summary

⚡ Resource management commands: kubectl set resources deployment/web -c=nginx --requests=cpu=100m,memory=128Mi --limits=cpu=500m,memory=256Mi
kubectl create quota my-quota --hard=pods=10,requests.cpu=4,requests.memory=8Gi -n dev
kubectl describe resourcequota -n dev — see used vs hard limits

📝 Chapter 15 — CKA Practice Questions

Q1
Set a container's CPU request to 250m and memory limit to 512Mi. What do m and Mi mean, and what happens if the container exceeds its memory limit?
Show Answer
Answer
# Pod with resource requests and limits:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: resource-pod
spec:
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx
    resources:
      requests:
        cpu: "250m"        # 250 millicores = 0.25 CPU core
        memory: "256Mi"    # 256 Mebibytes
      limits:
        cpu: "500m"        # 500 millicores = 0.5 CPU core
        memory: "512Mi"    # 512 Mebibytes

# CPU units:
# 1 CPU = 1000m (millicores)
# 250m = 0.25 = 25% of one core
# 100m = 0.1 = 10% of one core

# Memory units:
# Mi = Mebibytes (1024 * 1024 bytes) — binary
# M  = Megabytes (1000 * 1000 bytes) — decimal
# Gi = Gibibytes, G = Gigabytes

# What happens when limits are exceeded:
# CPU:    Container is THROTTLED (slowed down, NOT killed)
# Memory: Container is OOM-KILLED (terminated, CrashLoopBackOff)
Explanation

CPU is compressible — when a container hits its CPU limit, it's throttled (slowed) but continues running. Memory is incompressible — when a container exceeds its memory limit, the kernel OOM-killer terminates it. The pod restarts and eventually enters CrashLoopBackOff if it keeps exceeding the limit. On the CKA, always use Mi (mebibytes) not M (megabytes) — they're subtly different. 250m CPU = quarter core. The requests field determines scheduling (scheduler finds a node with enough free resources), while limits enforce runtime caps.

Q2
Explain the three QoS (Quality of Service) classes in Kubernetes. How is the QoS class determined, and which class gets OOM-killed first?
Show Answer
Answer
QoS Class Condition OOM Kill Priority
Guaranteed All containers have requests = limits (both CPU & memory) Last to be killed (highest priority)
Burstable At least one container has a request or limit set Killed after BestEffort
BestEffort No requests or limits set on any container First to be killed (lowest priority) 
# Check a pod's QoS class
kubectl get pod my-pod -o jsonpath='{.status.qosClass}'
# Guaranteed

# Guaranteed example — requests MUST equal limits:
    resources:
      requests:
        cpu: "500m"
        memory: "256Mi"
      limits:
        cpu: "500m"       # Same as request
        memory: "256Mi"   # Same as request
Explanation

QoS classes determine which pods are killed first when a node runs out of memory. BestEffort pods are evicted first (no resource guarantees), Burstable pods are next, and Guaranteed pods are last. On the CKA, if a question asks "how to ensure a critical pod is the last to be evicted," the answer is QoS class Guaranteed (set requests = limits for ALL containers in the pod). The QoS class is automatically determined by Kubernetes — you can't set it manually.

Q3
Create a LimitRange in namespace dev that sets default CPU request to 100m, default CPU limit to 500m, and prevents any container from requesting more than 2 CPU.
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: v1
kind: LimitRange
metadata:
  name: cpu-limits
  namespace: dev
spec:
  limits:
  - type: Container
    default:          # Default LIMITS (applied if not specified)
      cpu: "500m"
    defaultRequest:   # Default REQUESTS (applied if not specified)
      cpu: "100m"
    max:              # Maximum allowed
      cpu: "2"
    min:              # Minimum allowed
      cpu: "50m"

kubectl apply -f limitrange.yaml

# Verify
kubectl describe limitrange cpu-limits -n dev
# Type      Resource  Min   Max   Default  Default Request
# Container cpu       50m   2     500m     100m

# Now any pod in namespace 'dev' without CPU specs
# automatically gets: requests.cpu=100m, limits.cpu=500m
# Any pod requesting more than 2 CPU is REJECTED
Explanation

LimitRange operates at the namespace level and applies to INDIVIDUAL pods/containers. default sets the default limit, defaultRequest sets the default request. max and min enforce hard boundaries — pods that violate them are rejected at admission. On the CKA, LimitRange prevents developers from deploying pods without resource constraints. Confusable with ResourceQuota — LimitRange = per-pod/container defaults and constraints, ResourceQuota = total namespace-wide limits.

Q4
Create a ResourceQuota in namespace production that limits the namespace to maximum 10 pods, 4 CPU cores total, and 8Gi total memory for requests.
Show Answer
Answer
# Imperative (fastest on CKA):
kubectl create quota prod-quota \
  --hard=pods=10,requests.cpu=4,requests.memory=8Gi \
  -n production

# Equivalent YAML:
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: prod-quota
  namespace: production
spec:
  hard:
    pods: "10"
    requests.cpu: "4"
    requests.memory: "8Gi"
    limits.cpu: "8"
    limits.memory: "16Gi"

# Check current usage vs limits
kubectl describe resourcequota prod-quota -n production
# Resource          Used   Hard
# --------          ----   ----
# limits.cpu        2500m  8
# limits.memory     4Gi    16Gi
# pods              6      10
# requests.cpu      1200m  4
# requests.memory   3Gi    8Gi
Explanation

ResourceQuota limits the TOTAL resources consumed by ALL pods in a namespace. Once a quota is set, every new pod MUST specify resources.requests — otherwise creation fails. This is why LimitRange and ResourceQuota are often used together: LimitRange provides defaults so pods without explicit resources still work under the quota. On the CKA, kubectl create quota is the fastest imperative command. kubectl describe resourcequota shows the Used vs Hard columns — essential for troubleshooting "forbidden: exceeded quota" errors.

Q5
A pod is being OOM-killed repeatedly. kubectl describe pod shows Last State: Terminated — Reason: OOMKilled. How do you diagnose and fix this?
Show Answer
Answer
# Step 1: Check current memory limits
kubectl get pod oom-pod -o jsonpath=\
'{.spec.containers[0].resources.limits.memory}'
# 128Mi  ← Too small!

# Step 2: Check actual memory usage (if pod is running)
kubectl top pod oom-pod
# NAME      CPU(cores)  MEMORY(bytes)
# oom-pod   50m         245Mi   ← Using 245Mi but limit is 128Mi!

# Step 3: Increase the memory limit
# For a Deployment:
kubectl set resources deployment/webapp -c=app \
  --limits=memory=512Mi --requests=memory=256Mi

# For a standalone pod (must delete and recreate):
kubectl get pod oom-pod -o yaml > pod.yaml
# Edit pod.yaml: change limits.memory to 512Mi
kubectl delete pod oom-pod
kubectl apply -f pod.yaml

# Step 4: Verify pod is stable
kubectl get pod oom-pod -w
# Watch for a few minutes — no more restarts
Explanation

OOMKilled means the container exceeded its memory limit and was killed by the Linux kernel's Out-Of-Memory killer. The fix is almost always: increase limits.memory. But first check kubectl top pod to see actual usage — if a pod is using gigabytes of memory, increasing the limit is just a band-aid; the app may have a memory leak. On the CKA, OOMKilled troubleshooting is: (1) describe pod → see OOMKilled in Last State, (2) top pod → see actual usage, (3) increase limit or fix the application. For Deployments, kubectl set resources triggers a new rollout.

Q6
What is the difference between requests and limits? What happens if you set limits but not requests?
Show Answer
Answer
# requests = GUARANTEED minimum resources
# limits   = MAXIMUM allowed resources

# Scheduling: scheduler uses REQUESTS to find a node
# A node with 2 CPU and 4Gi can fit:
#   - Pod A: requests.cpu=500m, requests.memory=1Gi → 1.5 CPU, 3Gi left
#   - Pod B: requests.cpu=1,    requests.memory=2Gi → 0.5 CPU, 1Gi left
#   - Pod C: requests.cpu=1 → DOESN'T FIT (only 0.5 CPU left)

# What if you set limits but NOT requests?
    resources:
      limits:
        cpu: "500m"
        memory: "256Mi"
    # requests will be automatically set EQUAL to limits!
    # This creates a Guaranteed QoS pod

# What if you set requests but NOT limits?
    resources:
      requests:
        cpu: "100m"
        memory: "128Mi"
    # No upper bound — container can use all available resources
    # This creates a Burstable QoS pod
Explanation

requests determine scheduling — the scheduler ensures the node has enough free (unrequested) resources. limits enforce runtime caps. When you set limits without requests, Kubernetes automatically sets requests = limits (creating a Guaranteed pod). This is important because: (1) the pod gets exactly what it needs, no more, (2) it's the last to be OOM-killed. On the CKA, understanding this automatic behavior prevents confusion about QoS classes. The total requests on a node can never exceed node capacity, but total limits CAN (overcommit).

Q7
A pod creation fails with: "Error from server (Forbidden): pods "test" is forbidden: exceeded quota: dev-quota, requested: requests.cpu=500m, used: requests.cpu=3800m, limited: requests.cpu=4". What's wrong and how do you fix it?
Show Answer
Answer
# The quota math:
# Quota limit:    requests.cpu = 4 (4000m)
# Currently used: requests.cpu = 3800m
# Requesting:     requests.cpu = 500m
# Total would be: 3800m + 500m = 4300m > 4000m limit → DENIED

# Fix Option 1: Reduce the new pod's CPU request
    resources:
      requests:
        cpu: "200m"   # 3800m + 200m = 4000m ← fits exactly

# Fix Option 2: Delete unused pods to free quota
kubectl get pods -n dev
# Find idle/unused pods and delete them
kubectl delete pod idle-pod -n dev

# Fix Option 3: Increase the quota (if authorized)
kubectl edit resourcequota dev-quota -n dev
# Change hard.requests.cpu to "8"

# Check current quota usage
kubectl describe resourcequota dev-quota -n dev
# Resource       Used    Hard
# requests.cpu   3800m   4
Explanation

The error message is very clear: used (3800m) + requested (500m) = 4300m exceeds the hard limit (4000m). On the CKA, quota errors are a common troubleshooting scenario. Read the error carefully — it tells you the exact numbers. Fix by: (1) reducing the pod's request, (2) freeing up quota by deleting other pods, or (3) increasing the quota. kubectl describe resourcequota is your diagnostic tool. Important: quotas track REQUESTS, not actual usage — even if pods are idle, their reserved quota counts against the limit.

Q8
How do you use kubectl top to check resource consumption? What prerequisite must be installed?
Show Answer
Answer
# Prerequisite: metrics-server must be installed
# Check if metrics-server is running:
kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep metrics-server

# Check node resource usage
kubectl top nodes
# NAME           CPU(cores)  CPU%  MEMORY(bytes)  MEMORY%
# controlplane   450m        22%   1200Mi         31%
# worker-1       280m        14%   890Mi          23%

# Check pod resource usage (current namespace)
kubectl top pods
# NAME        CPU(cores)  MEMORY(bytes)
# webapp-1    120m        256Mi
# redis       80m         128Mi

# All namespaces
kubectl top pods -A

# Sort by CPU (find resource hogs)
kubectl top pods --sort-by=cpu

# Sort by memory
kubectl top pods --sort-by=memory

# Specific pod containers
kubectl top pod webapp-1 --containers
# POD       NAME    CPU(cores)  MEMORY(bytes)
# webapp-1  nginx   100m        200Mi
# webapp-1  sidecar 20m         56Mi
Explanation

kubectl top requires metrics-server to be installed and running in the cluster. Without it, you get "error: Metrics API not available." On the CKA exam environment, metrics-server is usually pre-installed. kubectl top shows ACTUAL current usage (not requests/limits). Use --sort-by=cpu or --sort-by=memory to quickly find the heaviest consumers. The --containers flag breaks down usage per container in a pod — useful when a sidecar might be the memory hog, not the main container.

Q9
What is the kubectl set resources command? Show how to update a Deployment's container resources without editing YAML.
Show Answer
Answer
# Set both requests and limits for a specific container
kubectl set resources deployment/webapp -c=nginx \
  --requests=cpu=200m,memory=256Mi \
  --limits=cpu=500m,memory=512Mi

# Set only limits (requests auto-set to same value)
kubectl set resources deployment/webapp -c=nginx \
  --limits=cpu=1,memory=1Gi

# Remove all resource constraints
kubectl set resources deployment/webapp -c=nginx \
  --limits=cpu=0,memory=0 --requests=cpu=0,memory=0

# This triggers a new rollout (new ReplicaSet)
kubectl rollout status deployment/webapp

# Verify the change
kubectl get deployment webapp -o jsonpath=\
'{.spec.template.spec.containers[0].resources}'
# {"limits":{"cpu":"500m","memory":"512Mi"},
#  "requests":{"cpu":"200m","memory":"256Mi"}}
Explanation

kubectl set resources is the imperative way to modify resource requests/limits without editing YAML. It's much faster than kubectl edit on the CKA exam. The -c flag specifies which container (required for multi-container pods). This command modifies the Deployment's pod template, triggering a rolling update. It's equivalent to editing the YAML and applying it. Combine with rollout pause/resume if you need to make multiple changes in one rollout.

Q10
A namespace has a ResourceQuota but no LimitRange. You try to create a pod without specifying resources and get: "Error: failed quota: must specify requests.cpu, requests.memory". Why?
Show Answer
Answer
# The problem:
# When a ResourceQuota exists that tracks cpu/memory,
# EVERY pod must specify requests for those resources.
# Without a LimitRange providing defaults, quota can't
# calculate how much to charge for the pod → rejected.

# Fix Option 1: Add resources to the pod spec
spec:
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx
    resources:
      requests:
        cpu: "100m"
        memory: "128Mi"

# Fix Option 2: Create a LimitRange for defaults
apiVersion: v1
kind: LimitRange
metadata:
  name: default-limits
  namespace: dev
spec:
  limits:
  - type: Container
    defaultRequest:
      cpu: "100m"
      memory: "128Mi"
    default:
      cpu: "500m"
      memory: "256Mi"

# Now pods without explicit resources get the defaults
# and quota can track them properly
Explanation

When a ResourceQuota tracks CPU/memory, Kubernetes MUST know how much each pod "costs" to charge against the quota. Without explicit requests on the pod or a LimitRange providing defaults, the admission controller can't determine the cost → rejects the pod. This is why best practice is: always pair ResourceQuota with a LimitRange in the same namespace. On the CKA, this is a common "gotcha" — the error message tells you to specify resources, but creating a LimitRange with defaults is the scalable fix.

Chapter 16: Jobs, CronJobs, DaemonSets, and StatefulSets

Deployments aren't the only workload type. Kubernetes provides specialized controllers for run-to-completion tasks (Jobs), scheduled tasks (CronJobs), one-pod-per-node daemons (DaemonSets), and stateful applications (StatefulSets). Mastering when to use each is critical for the CKA exam.

🟡 Intermediate ⏱️ ~1.5 hours ⚙️ Workload Types 📝 CKA EXAM

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ Create Jobs for one-off tasks (database migrations, batch processing)
  • ✅ Schedule recurring tasks with CronJobs (backups, reports)
  • ✅ Deploy DaemonSets for per-node system services (logging, monitoring)
  • ✅ Understand StatefulSets for databases and stateful applications
  • ✅ Choose the right workload type for any scenario
🎓 CKA Relevance
Workloads & Scheduling 15% Troubleshooting 30%
Workload Type Questions on the CKA

The CKA often asks you to create Jobs and CronJobs. Know these imperative shortcuts:

  • kubectl create job NAME --image=IMG -- COMMAND
  • kubectl create cronjob NAME --image=IMG --schedule="*/5 * * * *" -- COMMAND
  • DaemonSets require YAML (no imperative shortcut)

16.1 Jobs — Run to Completion

A Job creates one or more pods and ensures they run to successful completion. Unlike Deployments (which run forever), Jobs stop once the task is done. Perfect for batch processing, database migrations, and one-off scripts.

Imperative Job Creation 
# Create a simple job
kubectl create job db-migrate --image=anihpj:v1 -- python manage.py migrate

# Generate YAML first (exam tip: always verify before applying)
kubectl create job db-migrate --image=anihpj:v1 \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml -- python manage.py migrate > job.yaml

# Check job status
kubectl get jobs
# NAME         COMPLETIONS   DURATION   AGE
# db-migrate   1/1           12s        30s

# View job pods
kubectl get pods --selector=job-name=db-migrate
# NAME               READY   STATUS      RESTARTS   AGE
# db-migrate-abc12   0/1     Completed   0          45s

# Check logs
kubectl logs job/db-migrate
anihpj-migrate-job.yaml 
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: Job
metadata:
  name: anihpj-db-migrate
spec:
  completions: 1              # How many pods must complete successfully
  parallelism: 1              # How many pods run simultaneously
  backoffLimit: 3             # Retry up to 3 times before marking failed
  activeDeadlineSeconds: 300  # Timeout: kill after 5 minutes
  ttlSecondsAfterFinished: 60 # Auto-delete finished job after 60s
  template:
    spec:
      restartPolicy: Never    # Jobs: Never or OnFailure (NOT Always!)
      containers:
      - name: migrate
        image: anihpj:v1
        command: ["python", "manage.py", "migrate"]
        env:
        - name: DATABASE_URL
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: anihpj-db-secret
              key: url

Job Configuration Fields

✅ completions
How many pods must succeed. Default: 1. Set to N for batch: process N items.
⚡ parallelism
How many pods run at once. Default: 1. Increase for faster batch processing.
🔄 backoffLimit
Retry count before job is marked Failed. Default: 6. Set lower for fast failure.
⏱️ activeDeadlineSeconds
Hard timeout. Job is killed if it runs longer, regardless of retries.
🗑️ ttlSecondsAfterFinished
Auto-cleanup. Completed/failed job is deleted after this many seconds.
🔁 restartPolicy
Never (creates new pod on failure) or OnFailure (restarts container in same pod). Never Always!
Job Lifecycle — Run, Complete, Stop
  Job: anihpj-db-migrate (completions: 1, backoffLimit: 3)

  Attempt 1:
  ┌──────────────┐
  │ Pod: migrate │ → Runs python manage.py migrate
  │   🔄 Running │ → ...
  │   ✅ Success │ → Exit code 0
  └──────────────┘
  Job Status: Complete ✅  (1/1 completions)

  ─── What if it fails? ───

  Attempt 1:
  ┌──────────────┐
  │ Pod: migrate │ → DB connection refused
  │   ❌ Failed  │ → Exit code 1
  └──────────────┘
  Attempt 2:  (backoff: 10s wait)
  ┌──────────────┐
  │ Pod: migrate │ → DB now available
  │   ✅ Success │ → Exit code 0
  └──────────────┘
  Job Status: Complete ✅  (1/1 completions, 1 retry)
⚠️ CKA Trap: restartPolicy

Jobs CANNOT use restartPolicy: Always (that's for Deployments). If you forget this, the Job will be rejected. Use Never or OnFailure.

Batch Processing with Parallelism

Jobs can process multiple items in parallel. Set completions for how many must finish and parallelism for how many run at once.

batch-processing-job.yaml 
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: Job
metadata:
  name: anihpj-image-processor
spec:
  completions: 10       # 10 total items to process
  parallelism: 3        # Run 3 pods at a time
  backoffLimit: 5
  template:
    spec:
      restartPolicy: Never
      containers:
      - name: processor
        image: anihpj:v1
        command: ["python", "manage.py", "process_images"]
Batch Job: 10 Completions, 3 Parallel
  Job: anihpj-image-processor (completions: 10, parallelism: 3)

  Wave 1: [📦 Pod-1] [📦 Pod-2] [📦 Pod-3]   ← 3 running
               ✅         ✅         ✅
  Wave 2: [📦 Pod-4] [📦 Pod-5] [📦 Pod-6]   ← 3 running
               ✅         ✅         ✅
  Wave 3: [📦 Pod-7] [📦 Pod-8] [📦 Pod-9]   ← 3 running
               ✅         ✅         ✅
  Wave 4: [📦 Pod-10]                        ← 1 running (last item)
               ✅
  Job Status: Complete ✅  (10/10 completions)

  Total time: ~4 waves instead of 10 sequential runs!
  Parallelism gives ~3x speedup ⚡

16.2 CronJobs — Scheduled Tasks

CronJobs are like Unix cron but in Kubernetes. They create Jobs on a schedule — perfect for backups, reports, cleanup tasks, and periodic data processing.

Imperative CronJob Creation 
# Create a CronJob — daily backup at 2 AM
kubectl create cronjob anihpj-backup \
  --image=anihpj:v1 \
  --schedule="0 2 * * *" \
  -- python manage.py dumpdata --output=/backup/db.json

# Check CronJob
kubectl get cronjobs
# NAME             SCHEDULE      SUSPEND   ACTIVE   LAST SCHEDULE   AGE
# anihpj-backup    0 2 * * *     False     0        30s             5m

# View recently created Jobs
kubectl get jobs --sort-by=.metadata.creationTimestamp
anihpj-backup-cronjob.yaml 
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: anihpj-backup
spec:
  schedule: "0 2 * * *"          # Daily at 2:00 AM
  concurrencyPolicy: Forbid       # Don't start new if previous still running
  successfulJobsHistoryLimit: 3   # Keep last 3 successful job records
  failedJobsHistoryLimit: 1       # Keep last 1 failed job record
  startingDeadlineSeconds: 200    # If job can't start in 200s, skip this run
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      backoffLimit: 2
      template:
        spec:
          restartPolicy: OnFailure
          containers:
          - name: backup
            image: anihpj:v1
            command:
            - /bin/sh
            - -c
            - "python manage.py dumpdata | gzip > /backup/$(date +%Y%m%d).json.gz"
            volumeMounts:
            - name: backup-volume
              mountPath: /backup
          volumes:
          - name: backup-volume
            persistentVolumeClaim:
              claimName: backup-pvc
Schedule Meaning Use Case
*/5 * * * *Every 5 minutesHealth checks, cache refresh
0 2 * * *Daily at 2:00 AMDatabase backup
0 0 * * 0Every Sunday at midnightWeekly report generation
0 */6 * * *Every 6 hoursData sync, cleanup
30 8 1 * *1st of each month at 8:30 AMMonthly invoice generation
Cron Expression Format
  ┌───────────── minute (0-59)
  │ ┌───────────── hour (0-23)
  │ │ ┌───────────── day of month (1-31)
  │ │ │ ┌───────────── month (1-12)
  │ │ │ │ ┌───────────── day of week (0-6, Sun=0)
  │ │ │ │ │
  * * * * *

  Examples:
  0 2 * * *     = "At 02:00 every day"
  */5 * * * *   = "Every 5 minutes"
  0 0 * * 0     = "At midnight every Sunday"
  30 8 1 * *    = "At 08:30 on the 1st of every month"
🔒
concurrencyPolicy: Forbid

Don't start new job if previous is still running. Prevents overlap. Safest for DB backups.

🔄
concurrencyPolicy: Replace

Kill running job and start a new one. Good for "latest data only" scenarios.

📖 Third Option: concurrencyPolicy: Allow (default)

Allow lets multiple jobs run simultaneously. If CronJob fires every 5 minutes but a job takes 7 minutes, you'll have overlapping runs. This is fine for independent tasks (e.g., sending reports) but dangerous for database operations where concurrent writes could corrupt data. Always set Forbid for DB backups.

16.3 DaemonSets — One Pod Per Node

A DaemonSet ensures that exactly one copy of a pod runs on every node (or every selected node). When a new node joins the cluster, the DaemonSet automatically schedules a pod there. When a node is removed, the pod is garbage-collected.

📖 Common DaemonSet Use Cases
  • 📋 Log collectors — Fluentd, Filebeat, Promtail
  • 📊 Monitoring agents — Node Exporter, Datadog Agent
  • 🌐 Network plugins — Calico, Cilium, Flannel (CNI)
  • 💾 Storage daemons — Ceph, GlusterFS
  • 🔐 Security agents — Falco, Twistlock
fluentd-daemonset.yaml 
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: DaemonSet
metadata:
  name: fluentd
  namespace: kube-system
  labels:
    app: fluentd
spec:
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: fluentd
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: fluentd
    spec:
      tolerations:
      # Tolerate control plane taint to run on ALL nodes
      - key: node-role.kubernetes.io/control-plane
        operator: Exists
        effect: NoSchedule
      containers:
      - name: fluentd
        image: fluentd:v1.16
        resources:
          requests:
            cpu: "100m"
            memory: "200Mi"
          limits:
            cpu: "200m"
            memory: "400Mi"
        volumeMounts:
        - name: varlog
          mountPath: /var/log
        - name: varlibdockercontainers
          mountPath: /var/lib/docker/containers
          readOnly: true
      volumes:
      - name: varlog
        hostPath:
          path: /var/log
      - name: varlibdockercontainers
        hostPath:
          path: /var/lib/docker/containers
DaemonSet — Automatic Pod Per Node
  DaemonSet: "fluentd"   (replicas: N/A — auto-managed)

  ┌──────────────────┐  ┌──────────────────┐  ┌──────────────────┐
  │    worker-1      │  │    worker-2      │  │    worker-3      │
  │  ┌────────────┐  │  │  ┌────────────┐  │  │  ┌────────────┐  │
  │  │  fluentd   │  │  │  │  fluentd   │  │  │  │  fluentd   │  │
  │  │  📋 logs   │  │  │  │  📋 logs   │  │  │  │  📋 logs   │  │
  │  └────────────┘  │  │  └────────────┘  │  │  └────────────┘  │
  │  + anihpj pods   │  │  + anihpj pods   │  │  + anihpj pods   │
  └──────────────────┘  └──────────────────┘  └──────────────────┘

  ┌──────────────────┐
  │    worker-4      │  ← NEW node joins cluster!
  │  ┌────────────┐  │
  │  │  fluentd   │  │  DaemonSet controller AUTOMATICALLY
  │  │  📋 logs   │  │  schedules a pod here! 🔄
  │  └────────────┘  │
  └──────────────────┘

  ✅ Every node always has exactly 1 fluentd pod
  ✅ New nodes get a pod automatically
  ✅ Removed nodes — pod is cleaned up automatically
DaemonSet Commands 
# View DaemonSets
kubectl get daemonsets -A
# NAMESPACE     NAME         DESIRED   CURRENT   READY   UP-TO-DATE   NODE SELECTOR   AGE
# kube-system   fluentd      4         4         4       4            <none>          10d
# kube-system   kube-proxy   4         4         4       4            <none>          30d

# DaemonSet on specific nodes only (using nodeSelector)
# Add nodeSelector to template.spec to limit which nodes get the pod

# Check which nodes have DaemonSet pods
kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l app=fluentd -o wide
💡 CKA Tip: No Imperative Command for DaemonSets

Unlike Deployments and Jobs, there's no kubectl create daemonset command. You must write YAML. Quick trick: generate a Deployment YAML, then change kind: Deployment to kind: DaemonSet and remove the replicas field.

DaemonSet Update Strategy

DaemonSets support two update strategies, similar to Deployments:

Strategy Behavior Use Case
RollingUpdate (default) Pods updated node by node — old pod killed, new pod started on each node sequentially Most updates — zero gap per node
OnDelete Pods only updated when you manually delete them — full control over rollout pace Critical system daemons where you want to control timing (e.g., CNI plugin)
DaemonSet Update Strategy YAML 
# In the DaemonSet spec:
spec:
  updateStrategy:
    type: RollingUpdate        # or OnDelete
    rollingUpdate:
      maxUnavailable: 1        # Update 1 node at a time (default)
      # maxSurge: 0            # Not supported for DaemonSets

16.4 StatefulSets — Stateful Applications

StatefulSets are for applications that need stable, unique identity and persistent storage. Unlike Deployments (random pod names, shared storage), StatefulSets provide predictable pod names, ordered deployment, and per-pod persistent volumes.

Feature Deployment StatefulSet
Pod Names Random: app-x7k2z, app-m9p4q Ordered: app-0, app-1, app-2
DNS Identity Only via Service (shared) Each pod gets own DNS: app-0.svc.ns
Storage Shared or none Per-pod PVC (survives pod restarts)
Creation Order All at once (parallel) Sequential: 0 → 1 → 2 (ordered)
Deletion Order Random Reverse: 2 → 1 → 0 (ordered)
Use Case Stateless apps (anihpj Django) Databases, Kafka, Redis Cluster, ZooKeeper
postgresql-statefulset.yaml — anihpj Database 
# Headless Service (required for StatefulSet DNS)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: postgres-headless
spec:
  clusterIP: None               # Headless — no cluster IP
  selector:
    app: postgres
  ports:
  - port: 5432
    name: postgres
---
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: StatefulSet
metadata:
  name: postgres
spec:
  serviceName: "postgres-headless"  # Required: links to headless service
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: postgres
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: postgres
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: postgres
        image: postgres:16
        ports:
        - containerPort: 5432
        env:
        - name: POSTGRES_DB
          value: "anihpj"
        - name: PGDATA
          value: "/var/lib/postgresql/data/pgdata"
        volumeMounts:
        - name: data
          mountPath: /var/lib/postgresql/data
        resources:
          requests:
            cpu: "500m"
            memory: "1Gi"
          limits:
            cpu: "1"
            memory: "2Gi"
  volumeClaimTemplates:            # Per-pod PVC template
  - metadata:
      name: data
    spec:
      accessModes: ["ReadWriteOnce"]
      storageClassName: standard
      resources:
        requests:
          storage: 10Gi
StatefulSet — Ordered Pods with Stable Identity & Per-Pod Storage
  StatefulSet: "postgres"    replicas: 3
  serviceName: "postgres-headless"

  Deployment Pods:             StatefulSet Pods:
  (random names)               (ordered, predictable)
  ┌──────────────┐             ┌──────────────┐
  │ app-x7k2z    │             │ postgres-0   │ ← Created FIRST (primary)
  ├──────────────┤             │  DNS: postgres-0.postgres-headless
  │ app-m9p4q    │             ├──────────────┤
  ├──────────────┤             │ postgres-1   │ ← Created SECOND (must wait for 0)
  │ app-j3r8w    │             │  DNS: postgres-1.postgres-headless
  └──────────────┘             ├──────────────┤
                               │ postgres-2   │ ← Created THIRD
                               │  DNS: postgres-2.postgres-headless
                               └──────────────┘

  Per-Pod Persistent Volumes (survive pod restarts!):
  ┌──────────┐     ┌──────────┐     ┌──────────┐
  │postgres-0│     │postgres-1│     │postgres-2│
  │┌────────┐│     │┌────────┐│     │┌────────┐│
  ││data-   ││     ││data-   ││     ││data-   ││
  ││postgres││     ││postgres││     ││postgres││
  ││-0      ││     ││-1      ││     ││-2      ││
  ││ 10Gi   ││     ││ 10Gi   ││     ││ 10Gi   ││
  │└───┬────┘│     │└───┬────┘│     │└───┬────┘│
  └────┼─────┘     └────┼─────┘     └────┼─────┘
       ▼                ▼                ▼
  ┌─────────┐     ┌─────────┐     ┌─────────┐
  │  PV-0   │     │  PV-1   │     │  PV-2   │
  │ (disk)  │     │ (disk)  │     │ (disk)  │
  └─────────┘     └─────────┘     └─────────┘

  Key guarantees:
  ✅ Stable network identity  — postgres-0.postgres-headless.default.svc
  ✅ Ordered deployment       — 0 → 1 → 2 (each must be Ready before next)
  ✅ Ordered deletion         — 2 → 1 → 0 (reverse order)
  ✅ Stable storage           — PVC survives pod restart (data preserved!)
⚠️ StatefulSet Requires a Headless Service

You must create a headless Service (clusterIP: None) for the StatefulSet. The serviceName field in the StatefulSet spec must match the Service name. This enables DNS records for each pod like pod-name.service-name.namespace.svc.cluster.local.

🚨 StatefulSet Deletion — PVCs Are NOT Deleted!

When you delete a StatefulSet (even with --cascade=foreground), the PersistentVolumeClaims are preserved. This is by design to prevent data loss. You must manually delete them:

  • kubectl delete statefulset postgres → Pods deleted, PVCs remain ✅
  • kubectl get pvc → data-postgres-0, data-postgres-1, data-postgres-2 still exist
  • kubectl delete pvc data-postgres-0 data-postgres-1 data-postgres-2 → Manually clean up

This is intentional — recreating the StatefulSet will automatically reattach to existing PVCs, preserving all data!

StatefulSet Scaling — Ordered Operations 
# Scale up — pods created in order (3, 4, 5...)
kubectl scale statefulset postgres --replicas=5
# postgres-3 created (waits until Ready)
# postgres-4 created (waits until Ready)

# Scale down — pods deleted in REVERSE order (4, 3)
kubectl scale statefulset postgres --replicas=3
# postgres-4 terminated first
# postgres-3 terminated second
# postgres-0, 1, 2 untouched ✅

# PVCs from scaled-down pods are PRESERVED
kubectl get pvc
# data-postgres-3 and data-postgres-4 still exist
# Re-scaling up will reattach to them!

Choosing the Right Workload Type

🚀
Deployment

Stateless apps that run forever.
anihpj Django web server, nginx, API services.

🗄️
StatefulSet

Apps needing stable identity & storage.
PostgreSQL, MySQL, Kafka, Redis Cluster.

📋
DaemonSet

One pod on every node.
Log collectors, monitoring agents, CNI plugins.

✅
Job / CronJob

Run-to-completion / scheduled tasks.
DB migrations, backups, batch processing.

Decision Tree: Which Workload Type Do I Need?
                        ┌──────────────────┐
                        │  New Workload?   │
                        └────────┬─────────┘
                                 │
                    ┌────────────┴────────────┐
                    │                         │
              Runs forever?              Runs once/scheduled?
                    │                         │
              ┌─────┴─────┐             ┌─────┴─────┐
              │           │             │           │
        All nodes?   Some pods?    On schedule?   One-time?
              │           │             │           │
         DaemonSet  ┌─────┴─────┐   CronJob      Job
                    │           │
              Needs stable   Stateless?
              identity/PVC?     │
                    │       Deployment
              StatefulSet

  ╔════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
  ║  anihpj Stack Mapping:                                ║
  ║                                                        ║
  ║  📦 anihpj Django      → Deployment (stateless)       ║
  ║  🗄️ PostgreSQL         → StatefulSet (needs storage)  ║
  ║  📋 Fluentd logging    → DaemonSet (every node)       ║
  ║  🔄 DB migration       → Job (run once)               ║
  ║  💾 Nightly backup     → CronJob (scheduled)          ║
  ╚════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝

🔑 Chapter 16 Quick Reference

Action Command
Create Jobkubectl create job NAME --image=IMG -- CMD
Create CronJobkubectl create cronjob NAME --image=IMG --schedule="EXPR" -- CMD
View Jobskubectl get jobs
View CronJobskubectl get cronjobs
View DaemonSetskubectl get daemonsets -A
View StatefulSetskubectl get statefulsets
Job logskubectl logs job/NAME
Delete completed jobskubectl delete jobs --field-selector status.successful=1
Suspend CronJobkubectl patch cronjob NAME -p '{"spec":{"suspend":true}}'
Trigger CronJob nowkubectl create job NAME-manual --from=cronjob/NAME

⚠️ CKA Gotcha: Jobs, CronJobs, DaemonSets & StatefulSets Traps

  • Job backoffLimit — Default is 6 retries. If your job fails 6 times, it stops. Set backoffLimit: 0 for no retries, or increase for flaky tasks
  • CronJob schedule syntax — */5 * * * * = every 5 minutes. The exam expects you to write cron expressions. Fields: minute, hour, day-of-month, month, day-of-week
  • DaemonSet skips control plane — By default, DaemonSets don't run on control plane nodes (they have a NoSchedule taint). Add a toleration if needed
  • StatefulSet pods are ordered — web-0 must be Running before web-1 starts. Deletion is reverse order. This matters for databases
  • StatefulSet needs a headless Service — clusterIP: None. Each pod gets a stable DNS: web-0.headless-svc.namespace.svc.cluster.local

CKA Speed Tip — Create workloads imperatively:
kubectl create job my-job --image=busybox -- echo "hello"
kubectl create cronjob my-cron --image=busybox --schedule="*/5 * * * *" -- echo "tick"
For DaemonSets and StatefulSets, generate a Deployment YAML and change kind: manually — no imperative command exists.

📝 Chapter 16 Practice Drill

Timed challenge (target: 5 minutes):

  • 1. Create a Job that runs busybox and executes echo CKA-PASS, completions=3, parallelism=2
  • 2. Create a CronJob that runs the same command every 2 minutes
  • 3. Manually trigger the CronJob: kubectl create job manual-run --from=cronjob/<name>
  • 4. Check Job status: kubectl get jobs — verify 3/3 completions
  • 5. Suspend the CronJob: kubectl patch cronjob <name> -p '{"spec":{"suspend":true}}'

📝 Chapter 16 — CKA Practice Questions

Q1
Create a Job named batch-job that runs busybox, executes echo "Processing complete", and requires 5 successful completions with a parallelism of 2. Show the imperative and YAML approach.
Show Answer
Answer
# Imperative: generate base YAML
kubectl create job batch-job --image=busybox \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml -- /bin/sh -c "echo Processing complete" \
  > batch-job.yaml

# Edit batch-job.yaml to add completions and parallelism:
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: Job
metadata:
  name: batch-job
spec:
  completions: 5       # Total successful runs needed
  parallelism: 2       # Run 2 pods at a time
  backoffLimit: 4      # Max retries before marking failed
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: batch-job
        image: busybox
        command: ["/bin/sh", "-c", "echo Processing complete"]
      restartPolicy: Never  # Jobs require Never or OnFailure

kubectl apply -f batch-job.yaml

# Monitor progress
kubectl get jobs batch-job -w
# NAME       COMPLETIONS   DURATION
# batch-job  0/5           5s
# batch-job  2/5           12s
# batch-job  4/5           20s
# batch-job  5/5           28s
Explanation

Jobs run pods to completion. completions = how many TOTAL successful runs, parallelism = how many pods run simultaneously. With completions=5 and parallelism=2, Kubernetes runs 2 pods at a time, waits for them to succeed, then starts the next batch, until 5 total succeed. restartPolicy MUST be Never or OnFailure (never Always — that's for Deployments). backoffLimit controls how many failures before the Job gives up. On the CKA, you'll likely need to add completions/parallelism to a generated YAML since the imperative command doesn't support them directly.

Q2
Create a CronJob that runs curl http://health-check:8080/ping every 5 minutes. Set it to keep 3 successful and 1 failed history.
Show Answer
Answer
# Imperative:
kubectl create cronjob health-check \
  --image=curlimages/curl \
  --schedule="*/5 * * * *" \
  -- curl -s http://health-check:8080/ping

# Full YAML with history settings:
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: health-check
spec:
  schedule: "*/5 * * * *"     # Every 5 minutes
  successfulJobsHistoryLimit: 3  # Keep 3 successful
  failedJobsHistoryLimit: 1      # Keep 1 failed
  concurrencyPolicy: Forbid      # Don't overlap runs
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          containers:
          - name: health-check
            image: curlimages/curl
            command: ["curl", "-s", "http://health-check:8080/ping"]
          restartPolicy: OnFailure

# Cron schedule reference:
# ┌───────── minute (0-59)
# │ ┌─────── hour (0-23)
# │ │ ┌───── day of month (1-31)
# │ │ │ ┌─── month (1-12)
# │ │ │ │ ┌─ day of week (0-6, Sun=0)
# * * * * *
Explanation

CronJobs create Jobs on a schedule (like Unix cron). successfulJobsHistoryLimit and failedJobsHistoryLimit control how many old Job objects are kept (default: 3 successful, 1 failed). concurrencyPolicy can be: Allow (default, overlapping ok), Forbid (skip if previous still running), or Replace (kill previous, start new). On the CKA, kubectl create cronjob handles the basics imperatively, but you'll need to edit the YAML for history limits and concurrency policy. The cron schedule format is the same as standard Unix cron — memorize the 5-field order.

Q3
How do you manually trigger a one-time run from an existing CronJob? Show the command and explain what happens.
Show Answer
Answer
# Create a Job from an existing CronJob
kubectl create job manual-health-check \
  --from=cronjob/health-check

# This creates a Job using the CronJob's jobTemplate
# The Job runs immediately, regardless of schedule

# Monitor the triggered job
kubectl get jobs
# NAME                    COMPLETIONS  DURATION
# manual-health-check     1/1          5s

# Check the logs
kubectl logs job/manual-health-check
# OK

# The manually created job is independent:
# - Not counted against history limits
# - Not affected by concurrencyPolicy
# - Must be deleted manually if needed
Explanation

kubectl create job --from=cronjob/NAME creates a one-off Job using the CronJob's template. This is essential for testing CronJobs without waiting for the next scheduled run. On the CKA, this command appears in scenarios like "verify the CronJob works before waiting." The --from flag copies the jobTemplate spec from the CronJob. The resulting Job is a standalone object — it won't appear in the CronJob's history, and the CronJob's concurrencyPolicy won't block it.

Q4
What is the difference between a DaemonSet and a Deployment? When must you use a DaemonSet?
Show Answer
Answer
Aspect Deployment DaemonSet
Pod placement Scheduler decides which nodes Exactly ONE pod per node
Scaling Manual or HPA (replicas count) Auto (scales with cluster size)
Use case Stateless apps, web servers Node-level agents, log collectors
Examples nginx, app servers fluentd, kube-proxy, node-exporter 
# DaemonSet YAML:
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: DaemonSet
metadata:
  name: log-collector
spec:
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: log-collector
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: log-collector
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: fluentd
        image: fluentd:latest
        volumeMounts:
        - name: varlog
          mountPath: /var/log
      volumes:
      - name: varlog
        hostPath:
          path: /var/log
Explanation

DaemonSets guarantee exactly ONE pod per node (or per matching node with nodeSelector). When a new node is added to the cluster, the DaemonSet automatically adds a pod to it. When a node is removed, the pod is garbage-collected. Use DaemonSets for: log collection (fluentd, filebeat), monitoring agents (node-exporter, datadog), network plugins (calico, weave). On the CKA, DaemonSets don't have a replicas field — the replica count equals the number of eligible nodes. There's no kubectl create daemonset — you must write YAML (tip: generate a Deployment YAML and change kind: DaemonSet, remove replicas).

Q5
What is a StatefulSet and how does it differ from a Deployment? What are its three guarantees?
Show Answer
Answer
# StatefulSet's 3 guarantees:
# 1. Stable network identity → web-0, web-1, web-2 (not random)
# 2. Ordered deployment      → web-0 before web-1 before web-2
# 3. Persistent storage      → Each pod gets its own PVC

# StatefulSet requires a headless Service:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: web-headless
spec:
  clusterIP: None       # ← Headless (no cluster IP)
  selector:
    app: web
  ports:
  - port: 80
---
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: StatefulSet
metadata:
  name: web
spec:
  serviceName: "web-headless"    # ← Required
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: web
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: web
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: nginx
        image: nginx
  volumeClaimTemplates:     # ← Each pod gets its own PVC
  - metadata:
      name: data
    spec:
      accessModes: ["ReadWriteOnce"]
      resources:
        requests:
          storage: 1Gi

# DNS entries created:
# web-0.web-headless.default.svc.cluster.local
# web-1.web-headless.default.svc.cluster.local
# web-2.web-headless.default.svc.cluster.local
Explanation

StatefulSets are for stateful applications like databases (MySQL, PostgreSQL, Redis cluster, Kafka). Pods get stable hostnames (web-0, web-1, web-2 — not random hashes), ordered startup/shutdown, and each pod gets its own PersistentVolumeClaim. A headless Service (clusterIP: None) is required to provide individual DNS names. On the CKA, know that: (1) deleting a StatefulSet does NOT delete the PVCs (data is preserved), (2) scaling down goes in REVERSE order (web-2 first, then web-1), (3) serviceName must match the headless Service name. StatefulSets are less commonly tested than Deployments but appear in advanced scenarios.

Q6
A Job has backoffLimit: 4 and activeDeadlineSeconds: 120. Explain what each does and what happens when either limit is reached.
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: Job
metadata:
  name: deadline-job
spec:
  backoffLimit: 4              # Max 4 retries
  activeDeadlineSeconds: 120   # Max 2 minutes total
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: worker
        image: busybox
        command: ["sh", "-c", "exit 1"]   # Always fails
      restartPolicy: Never

# Scenario 1: backoffLimit reached first
# Pod fails → retry after ~10s → fails → retry after ~20s →
# fails → retry after ~40s → fails (4th failure)
# Job status: Failed, reason: BackoffLimitExceeded

# Scenario 2: activeDeadlineSeconds reached first
# Pod is still running at 120s → KILLED
# Job status: Failed, reason: DeadlineExceeded
# ALL running pods for this Job are terminated

# Check why a Job failed:
kubectl describe job deadline-job | grep -A3 "Conditions"
# Type    Status  Reason
# Failed  True    BackoffLimitExceeded
Explanation

backoffLimit limits the number of retry attempts (default: 6). Failed pods are retried with exponential backoff (10s, 20s, 40s, ..., capped at 6 minutes). activeDeadlineSeconds is a hard wall clock deadline — if the Job isn't complete by then, ALL pods are killed regardless of progress. On the CKA, activeDeadlineSeconds is critical for preventing runaway Jobs. backoffLimit: 0 means no retries — fail immediately. These two limits work independently: whichever is reached first causes the Job to fail.

Q7
How do you suspend and resume a CronJob? What happens to already-running Jobs when you suspend?
Show Answer
Answer
# Suspend a CronJob (prevent future scheduled runs)
kubectl patch cronjob health-check \
  -p '{"spec":{"suspend":true}}'

# Or use kubectl edit
kubectl edit cronjob health-check
# Set spec.suspend: true

# Check suspension status
kubectl get cronjob health-check
# NAME           SCHEDULE      SUSPEND  ACTIVE  LAST SCHEDULE
# health-check   */5 * * * *   True     0       2m

# Resume
kubectl patch cronjob health-check \
  -p '{"spec":{"suspend":false}}'

# Important: suspending does NOT kill running Jobs!
# Already-running Jobs continue to completion
# Only NEW scheduled runs are blocked

# To also stop running Jobs:
kubectl delete job <running-job-name>
Explanation

spec.suspend: true prevents the CronJob from creating new Jobs at the scheduled time. Already-running Jobs continue — suspension only affects FUTURE runs. To completely stop everything, you need to also delete the active Jobs. On the CKA, suspending CronJobs is tested in maintenance scenarios: "pause this scheduled task while we perform an upgrade." The SUSPEND column in kubectl get cronjob quickly shows whether it's suspended. Missed schedules are not retroactively created when you resume — they're simply skipped.

Q8
Create a DaemonSet that runs a node-exporter container on ALL nodes including the control plane. What toleration is needed?
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: DaemonSet
metadata:
  name: node-exporter
spec:
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: node-exporter
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: node-exporter
    spec:
      tolerations:
      # Tolerate control-plane taint to run on master
      - key: node-role.kubernetes.io/control-plane
        operator: Exists
        effect: NoSchedule
      # Or tolerate ALL taints:
      # - operator: Exists
      containers:
      - name: node-exporter
        image: prom/node-exporter:latest
        ports:
        - containerPort: 9100
          hostPort: 9100    # ← Expose on node's port

# Verify it runs on ALL nodes:
kubectl get pods -l app=node-exporter -o wide
# NAME                 READY  NODE
# node-exporter-abc    1/1    controlplane
# node-exporter-def    1/1    worker-1
# node-exporter-ghi    1/1    worker-2
Explanation

Control-plane nodes have the taint node-role.kubernetes.io/control-plane:NoSchedule. Without a matching toleration, the DaemonSet pod won't run on control-plane nodes. operator: Exists (without specifying a value) matches the taint regardless of its value. Use hostPort with DaemonSets to expose the pod on the node's IP — useful for monitoring agents. On the CKA, the "run on ALL nodes including master" phrasing signals that you need the control-plane toleration. Tip: generate a Deployment YAML, change kind to DaemonSet, remove replicas, and add the toleration.

Q9
What restartPolicy settings are valid for Jobs? What happens if you use restartPolicy: Always?
Show Answer
Answer
# Valid restartPolicy for Jobs:
# - Never:     Don't restart failed containers, create new pods
# - OnFailure: Restart failed containers in the SAME pod

# Invalid for Jobs:
# - Always:    REJECTED by the API server
#   Error: spec.template.spec.restartPolicy: Unsupported value:
#   "Always": supported values: "OnFailure", "Never"

# Behavior comparison:
# restartPolicy: Never
#   → Failed container? Create a NEW pod
#   → Each attempt = new pod (can see all pods with kubectl get pods)
#   → Useful for debugging (logs preserved in each pod)

# restartPolicy: OnFailure
#   → Failed container? Restart in SAME pod
#   → Pod's restart count increments
#   → Uses less resources (no new pod overhead)
#   → kubectl get pods shows restart count increasing

# For CronJobs, the same rules apply (CronJob creates Jobs)
Explanation

Jobs MUST use Never or OnFailure. Always would create an infinite loop — the container completes, restarts, completes, restarts... which defeats the purpose of a Job (run to completion). With Never, each failure creates a new pod (up to backoffLimit), which is useful for debugging because each pod's logs are preserved. With OnFailure, the container restarts in-place, using fewer resources. On the CKA, if you generate a pod YAML and convert it to a Job, remember to change restartPolicy from Always to Never — it's a common mistake that causes a validation error.

Q10
List all Jobs created by a CronJob, find failed ones, and check their logs. Show the complete troubleshooting flow.
Show Answer
Answer
# Step 1: List all Jobs owned by the CronJob
kubectl get jobs --selector=job-name
# Or by owner reference:
kubectl get jobs | grep health-check
# NAME                        COMPLETIONS  DURATION  AGE
# health-check-28412345       1/1          5s        10m
# health-check-28412350       1/1          4s        5m
# health-check-28412355       0/1          2m        2m  ← FAILED?

# Step 2: Check the failed Job
kubectl describe job health-check-28412355
# Events:
#   Warning BackoffLimitExceeded ... Job has reached the specified backoff limit

# Step 3: Find the pods created by the Job
kubectl get pods --selector=job-name=health-check-28412355
# NAME                              READY  STATUS
# health-check-28412355-abc         0/1    Error
# health-check-28412355-def         0/1    Error

# Step 4: Check logs of the failed pod
kubectl logs health-check-28412355-abc
# curl: (7) Failed to connect to health-check port 8080: Connection refused

# Step 5: Check events
kubectl get events --field-selector involvedObject.name=health-check-28412355 \
  --sort-by='.lastTimestamp'
Explanation

CronJobs create Jobs with predictable names: cronjob-name-timestamp. Each Job creates pods. The debugging chain is: CronJob → Job → Pod → Logs. On the CKA, troubleshooting failed CronJobs follows this exact flow. Use kubectl get jobs to find failed Jobs (COMPLETIONS shows 0/1), kubectl describe job to see why, and kubectl logs on the pod for application errors. The successfulJobsHistoryLimit and failedJobsHistoryLimit control how many old Jobs are kept — if you can't find old Jobs, the limit may have cleaned them up.

🟡 Part 5: Services & Networking Chapters 17-20

Chapter 17: Kubernetes Networking Fundamentals

Networking is the backbone of Kubernetes. Every pod, service, and DNS lookup depends on a correctly configured network. In this chapter you'll master the 4 fundamental networking rules, understand how CNI plugins implement the network, trace packets between pods on the same and different nodes, explore how kube-proxy routes service traffic, and build a troubleshooting toolkit for common network issues — all using our anihpj/jobpost Django stack as a real-world example.

🟡 Intermediate ⏱️ ~2 hours 🌐 Networking 📝 CKA EXAM

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ The 4 fundamental Kubernetes networking rules — why NAT-free networking matters
  • ✅ How CNI plugins (Calico, Cilium, Flannel) implement the network layer
  • ✅ Pod-to-Pod communication — same-node vs cross-node packet paths
  • ✅ How kube-proxy translates Service IPs into pod endpoints using iptables/IPVS
  • ✅ Network troubleshooting commands and strategies for the CKA exam
🎓 CKA Relevance
Services & Networking 20% Troubleshooting 30%
Networking Is 20% of the CKA Exam

Networking questions on the CKA range from creating Services and Ingress to troubleshooting DNS and pod connectivity. Key exam skills:

  • Know which CNI plugin is installed: ls /etc/cni/net.d/
  • Check kube-proxy mode: kubectl logs -n kube-system kube-proxy-xxxxx
  • Test connectivity: kubectl exec -it pod -- curl http://service-name
  • Debug DNS: kubectl exec -it pod -- nslookup service-name

17.1 The Kubernetes Networking Model

Kubernetes enforces a flat, NAT-free networking model. This is fundamentally different from Docker's default bridge networking, and understanding these rules is critical for every CKA topic that follows.

1️⃣
Every Pod Gets a Unique IP

No two pods share an IP address. Containers within the same pod share the pod's IP and communicate via localhost.

2️⃣
Pod-to-Pod Without NAT

Any pod can reach any other pod directly using its IP — even across nodes. No network address translation needed.

3️⃣
Node-to-Pod Without NAT

Nodes can communicate directly with any pod IP in the cluster without address translation.

4️⃣
Pod Sees Its Own Real IP

Inside a pod, the IP it sees for itself is the same IP that every other pod and node sees. No surprises.

The 4 Fundamental Kubernetes Networking Rules
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  KUBERNETES FLAT NETWORKING MODEL                                        │
  │                                                                          │
  │  Rule 1: Every Pod gets a unique IP                                     │
  │  ┌────────────────┐  ┌────────────────┐  ┌────────────────┐            │
  │  │  anihpj-abc    │  │  postgres-0    │  │  redis-xyz     │            │
  │  │  10.244.1.5    │  │  10.244.2.8    │  │  10.244.3.2    │            │
  │  │  (Node 1)      │  │  (Node 2)      │  │  (Node 1)      │            │
  │  └────────────────┘  └────────────────┘  └────────────────┘            │
  │                                                                          │
  │  Rule 2: Pod-to-Pod — direct, no NAT                                    │
  │  anihpj (10.244.1.5) ────────────────────→ postgres (10.244.2.8) ✅    │
  │                       no translation needed!                             │
  │                                                                          │
  │  Rule 3: Node-to-Pod — direct, no NAT                                  │
  │  Node 1 (192.168.1.10) ──────────────────→ postgres (10.244.2.8) ✅    │
