  │                          on Node 2, still direct!                        │
  │                                                                          │
  │  Rule 4: Pod sees its own real IP                                       │
  │  Inside anihpj:     hostname -i  →  10.244.1.5                         │
  │  From postgres:     ping anihpj  →  10.244.1.5   ✅ Same IP!          │
  │                                                                          │
  │  ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════     │
  │  CONTRAST: Docker default networking (WITHOUT Kubernetes)                │
  │  Container A → NAT → Host IP → NAT → Container B   ❌ Complex!        │
  │  Container sees 172.17.0.2, outside sees host IP    ❌ Different!      │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
📖 Why This Model Matters

The flat networking model means you can take any application that communicates over TCP/IP and run it in Kubernetes without modifying the code. Your anihpj Django app connects to postgres:5432 exactly the same way whether running locally or in a Kubernetes cluster. No port mapping gymnastics, no NAT tables to debug.

Containers Sharing a Pod Network Namespace
  ┌──────────────────────── Pod: anihpj-abc ─────────────────────────┐
  │                   IP: 10.244.1.5                                  │
  │                                                                    │
  │  ┌────────────────┐  ┌────────────────┐  ┌───────────────────┐   │
  │  │  Container 1   │  │  Container 2   │  │  Container 3      │   │
  │  │  django        │  │  nginx sidecar │  │  log-collector    │   │
  │  │  :8000         │  │  :80           │  │  (no port)        │   │
  │  └───────┬────────┘  └───────┬────────┘  └────────┬──────────┘   │
  │          │                   │                     │              │
  │  ════════╧═══════════════════╧═════════════════════╧═══════════  │
  │                     Shared Network Namespace                      │
  │                     (same IP, same localhost)                      │
  │                                                                    │
  │  nginx → curl http://localhost:8000  ✅  (reaches django)        │
  │  django → curl http://localhost:80   ✅  (reaches nginx)         │
  │                                                                    │
  │  ⚠️  Port conflicts! Two containers can't both use :8000        │
  │                                                                    │
  │  eth0 ──── 10.244.1.5 ──── connected to node bridge             │
  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
💡 Multi-Container Pod Networking

All containers in a pod share the same network namespace — same IP, same port space, same localhost. This is why sidecar containers (like nginx reverse proxies or log collectors) can communicate with the main container via localhost. But beware: two containers can't listen on the same port.

IP Address Ranges in Kubernetes

Kubernetes Network CIDRs

🖥️ Node Network
Physical/VM network: 192.168.1.0/24 — each node gets an IP from your infrastructure
📦 Pod Network (CIDR)
10.244.0.0/16 (default for Flannel) or 192.168.0.0/16 (Calico). Each node gets a /24 subnet.
🔗 Service Network
10.96.0.0/12 (default). Virtual IPs — no real interface, handled by kube-proxy iptables rules.
Inspecting Cluster Network CIDRs 
# Pod CIDR — check kubeadm config or CNI
kubectl cluster-info dump | grep -m 1 cluster-cidr
# --cluster-cidr=10.244.0.0/16

# Service CIDR
kubectl cluster-info dump | grep -m 1 service-cluster-ip-range
# --service-cluster-ip-range=10.96.0.0/12

# Each node's pod subnet
kubectl get nodes -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.metadata.name}{"\t"}{.spec.podCIDR}{"\n"}{end}'
# worker-1    10.244.1.0/24
# worker-2    10.244.2.0/24
# worker-3    10.244.3.0/24

# Pod IPs — verify flat networking
kubectl get pods -o wide
# NAME           READY   IP            NODE
# anihpj-abc     1/1     10.244.1.5    worker-1
# postgres-0     1/1     10.244.2.8    worker-2

17.2 CNI Plugins — How Networking Is Implemented

The Container Network Interface (CNI) is a specification that defines how networking is set up for containers. Kubernetes itself does NOT implement networking — it delegates to a CNI plugin. As a CKA admin, you must know how to identify, install, and troubleshoot the CNI.

CNI Plugin — The Missing Piece
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  KUBERNETES says: "I need networking. Here's my spec."   │
  │                                                          │
  │  ┌──────────┐     ┌────────────────────────────────┐    │
  │  │ kubelet  │ ──→ │  CNI Plugin (your choice)       │    │
  │  │          │     │                                  │    │
  │  │ "Create  │     │  1. Assign pod IP               │    │
  │  │  network │     │  2. Set up veth pair             │    │
  │  │  for new │     │  3. Configure bridge/routing     │    │
  │  │  pod"    │     │  4. Enable cross-node traffic    │    │
  │  └──────────┘     └────────────────────────────────┘    │
  │                                                          │
  │  Without CNI → pods have NO networking → cluster broken  │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
CNI Plugin Networking Mode Network Policy Performance Best For
Calico BGP (Layer 3) / VXLAN overlay ✅ Full support ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Production, CKA labs, enterprise
Cilium eBPF (kernel-level) ✅ Advanced (L7) ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ High-performance, observability
Flannel VXLAN overlay (Layer 2) ❌ None ⭐⭐⭐☆☆ Simple clusters, learning
Weave Net Mesh overlay ✅ Basic ⭐⭐⭐☆☆ Easy setup, small clusters
Canal Flannel networking + Calico policies ✅ Full support ⭐⭐⭐⭐☆ Flannel users needing NetworkPolicy
Identifying and Inspecting the CNI Plugin 
# Which CNI is installed? Check the config directory
ls /etc/cni/net.d/
# 10-calico.conflist    ← Calico is installed

# Check CNI pods running
kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep -E "calico|cilium|flannel|weave"
# calico-node-abc12    1/1    Running
# calico-node-def34    1/1    Running

# View CNI binary directory
ls /opt/cni/bin/
# calico  calico-ipam  bandwidth  bridge  host-local  loopback  portmap

# Check pod CIDR assigned to each node
kubectl get nodes -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.metadata.name}: {.spec.podCIDR}{"\n"}{end}'

# If CNI pods are crashing — cluster networking is broken!
kubectl describe pod calico-node-abc12 -n kube-system
⚠️ CKA Exam: CNI Must Be Installed

After kubeadm init, nodes show NotReady until you install a CNI. The exam may ask you to install one:

  • Calico: kubectl apply -f https://docs.projectcalico.org/manifests/calico.yaml
  • Flannel: kubectl apply -f https://github.com/flannel-io/flannel/releases/latest/download/kube-flannel.yml
  • If nodes are NotReady — first check if CNI pods exist in kube-system
CNI Plugin Lifecycle — What Happens When a Pod Is Created
  kubectl run anihpj --image=anihpj:latest
         │
         ▼
  ┌────────────────────┐
  │  1. API Server      │  Stores pod spec in etcd
  └────────┬───────────┘
           ▼
  ┌────────────────────┐
  │  2. Scheduler       │  Assigns pod to worker-1
  └────────┬───────────┘
           ▼
  ┌────────────────────┐
  │  3. kubelet         │  On worker-1, sees new pod assignment
  │     (worker-1)      │
  └────────┬───────────┘
           ▼
  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  4. kubelet calls CNI plugin:                       │
  │     /opt/cni/bin/calico ADD                         │
  │                                                     │
  │     CNI plugin performs:                             │
  │     ┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
  │     │ a. Create network namespace for pod       │   │
  │     │ b. Create veth pair (pod eth0 ↔ host)    │   │
  │     │ c. Assign IP: 10.244.1.5  (from IPAM)   │   │
  │     │ d. Set up routing to/from pod            │   │
  │     │ e. Connect to bridge (cbr0/cni0)         │   │
  │     │ f. Apply any NetworkPolicy rules         │   │
  │     └──────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
  │                                                     │
  │     Returns: { "ips": [{"address": "10.244.1.5"}] } │
  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
           ▼
  ┌────────────────────┐
  │  5. kubelet starts  │  Container runtime starts containers
  │     containers      │  Pod is now RUNNING with networking ✅
  └────────────────────┘

17.3 Pod-to-Pod Communication

Understanding how packets travel between pods is essential for troubleshooting. The path differs depending on whether pods are on the same node or different nodes.

Key Network Primitives — Building Blocks

🔌 veth pair
A virtual Ethernet cable with two ends: one inside the pod (eth0), one on the host (connected to the bridge). Like a network patch cable between two switches.
🌉 Linux bridge (cbr0/cni0)
A virtual layer-2 switch that connects all pod veth endpoints on a single node. Forwards frames between pods.
📝 Network namespace
An isolated network stack (interfaces, routes, iptables). Each pod gets its own. Containers within a pod share one.
📦 VXLAN tunnel
Overlay encapsulation: the pod packet is wrapped in an outer UDP packet to cross the physical network between nodes.
Same-Node Communication — Via Linux Bridge
  ┌──────────────────────── Node 1 ────────────────────────────┐
  │                                                             │
  │  ┌─────────────────┐          ┌─────────────────┐          │
  │  │ anihpj-abc      │          │ redis-xyz        │          │
  │  │ 10.244.1.5      │          │ 10.244.1.12      │          │
  │  │ eth0 ──────┐    │          │    ┌────── eth0  │          │
  │  └────────────┼────┘          └────┼─────────────┘          │
  │               │  veth pair         │  veth pair              │
  │          ┌────▼────────────────────▼────┐                   │
  │          │       cbr0 / cni0 (bridge)   │                   │
  │          │       10.244.1.1             │                   │
  │          └──────────────────────────────┘                   │
  │                                                             │
  │  Packet path: anihpj eth0 → veth → bridge → veth → redis  │
  │  ✅ Fast — stays entirely within the node kernel           │
  │  ✅ No encapsulation overhead                               │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Cross-Node Communication — Via Overlay Network (VXLAN/BGP)
  ┌────────── Node 1 ──────────┐         ┌────────── Node 2 ──────────┐
  │                              │         │                              │
  │ ┌─────────────────┐         │         │         ┌─────────────────┐ │
  │ │ anihpj-abc      │         │         │         │ postgres-0      │ │
  │ │ 10.244.1.5      │         │         │         │ 10.244.2.8      │ │
  │ │ eth0            │         │         │         │        eth0     │ │
  │ └──────┬──────────┘         │         │         └────────▲────────┘ │
  │        │ veth                │         │                  │ veth     │
  │ ┌──────▼──────┐             │         │         ┌────────┴────┐    │
  │ │ cbr0 bridge │             │         │         │ cbr0 bridge │    │
  │ │ 10.244.1.1  │             │         │         │ 10.244.2.1  │    │
  │ └──────┬──────┘             │         │         └────────▲────┘    │
  │        │ routing             │         │                  │         │
  │ ┌──────▼──────┐             │         │         ┌────────┴────┐    │
  │ │  eth0 (NIC) │═════════════╪═════════╪═════════│  eth0 (NIC) │    │
  │ │ 192.168.1.10│   physical  │         │         │ 192.168.1.11│    │
  │ └─────────────┘   network   │         │         └─────────────┘    │
  └──────────────────────────────┘         └──────────────────────────────┘

  Packet journey:
  1. anihpj (10.244.1.5) → sends to postgres (10.244.2.8)
  2. Pod eth0 → veth → cbr0 bridge → node routing table
  3. CNI plugin encapsulates packet (VXLAN header or BGP route)
  4. Node 1 eth0 ═══ physical network ═══ Node 2 eth0
  5. Node 2 decapsulates → cbr0 → veth → postgres eth0
  6. postgres receives packet from 10.244.1.5 ✅
🌐
Overlay (VXLAN)

Pod packets wrapped in outer UDP packet. Works anywhere (cloud, bare metal). Slight overhead. Used by: Flannel, Calico (VXLAN mode), Cilium.

⚡
Routing (BGP)

Node routing tables updated to forward pod traffic directly. No encapsulation overhead. Requires L3 network. Used by: Calico (BGP mode).

Testing Pod-to-Pod Connectivity 
# Get pod IPs
kubectl get pods -o wide
# NAME           IP            NODE
# anihpj-abc     10.244.1.5    worker-1
# postgres-0     10.244.2.8    worker-2

# Test direct pod-to-pod (cross-node)
kubectl exec anihpj-abc -- curl -s --max-time 3 http://10.244.2.8:5432
# OR for TCP check
kubectl exec anihpj-abc -- nc -zv 10.244.2.8 5432
# 10.244.2.8 (10.244.2.8:5432) open

# Test same-node connectivity
kubectl exec anihpj-abc -- ping -c 2 10.244.1.12
# PING 10.244.1.12: 64 bytes, time=0.1ms  ← very fast (same node)

17.4 kube-proxy — Service Networking

kube-proxy runs on every node as a DaemonSet. Its job is to implement Service routing — when a pod sends traffic to a Service ClusterIP, kube-proxy's rules redirect that traffic to one of the Service's backend pods.

kube-proxy — How Service ClusterIP Routing Works
  anihpj pod calls: http://anihpj-svc:80   (ClusterIP: 10.96.45.2)

  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  Step 1: Pod sends packet to 10.96.45.2:80 (Service ClusterIP)  │
  │                                                                  │
  │  Step 2: Packet hits iptables/IPVS rules (set by kube-proxy)   │
  │  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
  │  │  kube-proxy rules on this node:                          │   │
  │  │                                                          │   │
  │  │  10.96.45.2:80 → DNAT to one of:                        │   │
  │  │    ├── 10.244.1.5:8000  (anihpj-abc on worker-1) 33%   │   │
  │  │    ├── 10.244.2.3:8000  (anihpj-def on worker-2) 33%   │   │
  │  │    └── 10.244.3.7:8000  (anihpj-ghi on worker-3) 33%   │   │
  │  │                                                          │   │
  │  │  Random selection (iptables) or round-robin (IPVS)      │   │
  │  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
  │                                                                  │
  │  Step 3: Packet destination rewritten to 10.244.2.3:8000        │
  │          → Normal pod-to-pod routing delivers it                │
  │                                                                  │
  │  Step 4: Response comes back, source NAT reverses               │
  │          Pod sees response from 10.96.45.2 (Service IP)         │
  │                                                                  │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐                    │
  │  │  Key insight: 10.96.45.2 is VIRTUAL     │                    │
  │  │  No network interface has this IP!       │                    │
  │  │  It only exists in iptables/IPVS rules  │                    │
  │  └─────────────────────────────────────────┘                    │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
kube-proxy Mode How It Works Performance When to Use
iptables Creates iptables NAT rules for each Service/Endpoint. Random backend selection. Good for < 1000 Services Default — most clusters
IPVS Uses Linux kernel IPVS module. Hash-based load balancing (round-robin, least connections, etc.). Excellent for 1000+ Services Large-scale production
nftables Uses nftables (successor to iptables). More efficient rule processing. Better than iptables Newer kernels (K8s 1.29+)
Inspecting kube-proxy 
# Check kube-proxy pods
kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-proxy
# NAME              READY   STATUS    NODE
# kube-proxy-abc12  1/1     Running   worker-1
# kube-proxy-def34  1/1     Running   worker-2

# Check kube-proxy mode
kubectl logs -n kube-system kube-proxy-abc12 | grep "Using"
# I0401 Using iptables proxier    ← iptables mode

# View kube-proxy config
kubectl get configmap kube-proxy -n kube-system -o yaml | grep mode
# mode: ""   ← empty means iptables (default)

# View actual iptables rules for a Service (on a node)
# ssh worker-1
iptables -t nat -L KUBE-SERVICES | grep anihpj-svc
# KUBE-SVC-xxx  tcp  --  anywhere  10.96.45.2  tcp dpt:http
💡 CKA Tip: kube-proxy Crash = Services Stop Working

If kube-proxy pods are down, Services still resolve via DNS but traffic can't be routed to pods. Symptoms: DNS lookup works but curl http://service:port hangs. Always check kube-proxy if Service connectivity fails but DNS is fine.

kube-proxy Mode Decision Flow
                     ┌─────────────────────────┐
                     │  How many Services in    │
                     │  your cluster?            │
                     └────────┬────────────────┘
                              │
               ┌──────────────┼──────────────┐
               │              │              │
          < 500         500 – 5000       > 5000
               │              │              │
               ▼              ▼              ▼
     ┌─────────────┐  ┌────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐
     │  iptables   │  │  Need LB   │  │    IPVS      │
     │  (default)  │  │  algos?    │  │  (required)  │
     │  ✅ Simple  │  └─────┬──────┘  │  ✅ O(1)     │
     │  ✅ Works   │    yes │ no      │  ✅ Scalable  │
     │  everywhere │        │         └──────────────┘
     └─────────────┘   ┌────┴────┐
                        │  IPVS  │
                        │ ✅ RR  │
                        │ ✅ LC  │   RR  = Round Robin
                        │ ✅ WRR │   LC  = Least Connections
                        │ ✅ SH  │   WRR = Weighted Round Robin
                        └────────┘   SH  = Source Hashing

  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  Running K8s 1.29+ on modern kernel?                 │
  │  Consider nftables mode — better rule processing    │
  │  Set: mode: "nftables" in kube-proxy ConfigMap      │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Switching kube-proxy to IPVS Mode 
# Step 1: Edit the kube-proxy ConfigMap
kubectl edit configmap kube-proxy -n kube-system
# Change: mode: ""  →  mode: "ipvs"

# Step 2: Restart kube-proxy DaemonSet to pick up the change
kubectl rollout restart daemonset kube-proxy -n kube-system

# Step 3: Verify IPVS mode is active
kubectl logs -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-proxy | grep "Using"
# I0401 Using ipvs proxier    ← IPVS mode!

# Step 4: View IPVS rules (on a node via ssh)
ipvsadm -Ln
# TCP  10.96.45.2:80 rr         ← round-robin load balancing
#   -> 10.244.1.5:8000  Masq  1  0  0
#   -> 10.244.2.3:8000  Masq  1  0  0

17.5 Network Troubleshooting for Admins

The CKA exam frequently tests your ability to diagnose and fix networking issues. Here's your systematic troubleshooting playbook.

1
Check CNI

Are CNI pods running? kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep calico

2
Check kube-proxy

Is kube-proxy running? kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep proxy

3
Test Pod IP

Can you reach the pod directly? kubectl exec -- curl POD_IP:PORT

4
Test Service IP

Can you reach the Service? kubectl exec -- curl SVC_IP:PORT

5
Test DNS

Does name resolution work? kubectl exec -- nslookup svc-name

Symptom Likely Cause Diagnostic Command Fix
Nodes NotReady CNI not installed or crashing kubectl get pods -n kube-system Install/fix CNI plugin
Pod can't reach pod on other node CNI overlay broken, firewall blocking kubectl exec -- ping <pod-ip> Check CNI pods, node firewall rules
DNS works but Service unreachable kube-proxy down or misconfigured kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-proxy Restart kube-proxy, check ConfigMap
Service returns no endpoints Selector doesn't match pod labels kubectl get endpoints svc-name Fix selector labels to match pods
nslookup fails CoreDNS pods down kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns Check CoreDNS pods and logs
Traffic blocked between pods NetworkPolicy denying traffic kubectl get networkpolicies -A Review/update NetworkPolicy rules
Network Troubleshooting Toolkit 
# 1. Quick connectivity test from a debug pod
kubectl run nettest --image=nicolaka/netshoot --rm -it -- bash

# Inside netshoot pod:
curl -s http://anihpj-svc:80          # Test Service by name
curl -s http://10.96.45.2:80          # Test Service by IP
ping -c 2 10.244.2.8                  # Test pod IP directly
nslookup anihpj-svc                   # Test DNS resolution
nslookup anihpj-svc.default.svc.cluster.local  # Full FQDN
traceroute 10.244.2.8                 # Trace packet path

# 2. Check endpoints (are pods registered with the Service?)
kubectl get endpoints anihpj-svc
# NAME          ENDPOINTS                                    AGE
# anihpj-svc   10.244.1.5:8000,10.244.2.3:8000,10.244.3.7:8000   5m
# If ENDPOINTS is empty → selector doesn't match any pods!

# 3. Verify Service selector matches pod labels
kubectl get svc anihpj-svc -o jsonpath='{.spec.selector}'
# {"app":"anihpj"}
kubectl get pods -l app=anihpj
# Should show your pods — if empty, labels don't match!

# 4. Check if NetworkPolicies are blocking traffic
kubectl get networkpolicies -A
kubectl describe networkpolicy -n anihpj-prod

🔑 Chapter 17 Quick Reference

Action Command
Check CNI pluginls /etc/cni/net.d/
Check CNI podskubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep calico
Check kube-proxykubectl get pods -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-proxy
View pod IPskubectl get pods -o wide
View pod CIDRskubectl get nodes -o jsonpath='{.items[*].spec.podCIDR}'
Test pod connectivitykubectl exec POD -- curl -s http://IP:PORT
Test DNSkubectl exec POD -- nslookup SVC-NAME
Check endpointskubectl get endpoints SVC-NAME
Debug networkingkubectl run nettest --image=nicolaka/netshoot --rm -it -- bash
Check NetworkPolicieskubectl get networkpolicies -A
🧪 CKA Practice Scenario: Network Debugging

Situation: A junior admin reports that the anihpj Django app can't connect to the postgres database. Pods are Running, but curl http://postgres-svc:5432 from the anihpj pod times out.

Step-by-Step Diagnosis 
# Step 1: Can DNS resolve the service name?
kubectl exec anihpj-pod -- nslookup postgres-svc
# ✅ Returns 10.96.88.5 — DNS is fine

# Step 2: Can we reach the pod directly by IP?
kubectl get pods -o wide | grep postgres
# postgres-0   10.244.2.8   worker-2
kubectl exec anihpj-pod -- nc -zv 10.244.2.8 5432
# ✅ open — pod-to-pod networking works

# Step 3: Can we reach the SERVICE IP?
kubectl exec anihpj-pod -- nc -zv 10.96.88.5 5432
# ❌ TIMEOUT — Service routing is broken!

# Step 4: Check kube-proxy
kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-proxy
# kube-proxy-abc12  0/1  CrashLoopBackOff  ← FOUND IT!

# Step 5: Check kube-proxy logs
kubectl logs kube-proxy-abc12 -n kube-system
# "failed to read config: invalid mode 'ipvss'"
# Typo in config! mode should be "ipvs" not "ipvss"

# Fix: Edit the kube-proxy ConfigMap
kubectl edit configmap kube-proxy -n kube-system
# Fix the mode value, then restart kube-proxy pods
kubectl rollout restart daemonset kube-proxy -n kube-system

⚠️ CKA Gotcha: Networking Fundamentals Traps

  • 4 networking rules — Every pod gets its own IP. Pods can communicate across nodes without NAT. Agents on a node can talk to all pods on that node. The CKA tests that you understand this flat network model
  • CNI plugin must be installed — Without it, nodes stay NotReady and pods stay Pending. The exam tells you which CNI to install (usually Calico or Flannel)
  • kube-proxy modes — iptables (default) vs IPVS. kube-proxy creates rules that route Service VIPs to pod IPs. If kube-proxy is down, services stop working but direct pod-to-pod still works
  • Pod CIDR vs Service CIDR — These are different ranges. Pod IPs come from --pod-network-cidr. Service IPs come from --service-cluster-ip-range. Don't confuse them

CKA Speed Tip — Network debugging toolkit:
kubectl run nettest --image=nicolaka/netshoot --rm -it -- bash — launch a debug pod with all network tools
kubectl get pods -o wide — see pod IPs and node placement
kubectl exec <pod> -- curl -s <service-ip>:<port> — test connectivity
kubectl exec <pod> -- nslookup <service-name> — test DNS resolution

⚡ Network inspection commands: kubectl get pods -A -o wide | grep kube-proxy — verify kube-proxy running on all nodes
kubectl logs -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-proxy — kube-proxy logs
kubectl get configmap kube-proxy -n kube-system -o yaml | grep mode — check proxy mode
ip route / iptables -t nat -L — node-level routing (from inside the node)

📝 Chapter 17 — CKA Practice Questions

Q1
What are the 4 fundamental Kubernetes networking rules? Why are they important for the CKA exam?
Show Answer
Answer
# The 4 Kubernetes Networking Rules:

# 1. Every pod gets its own unique IP address
#    → No port conflicts between pods
#    → Pods are treated like VMs from a networking perspective

# 2. Pods on ANY node can communicate with ALL other pods
#    WITHOUT NAT (Network Address Translation)
#    → Flat network — no routing hacks needed

# 3. Agents on a node (kubelet, kube-proxy) can communicate
#    with all pods on that node
#    → Node components can health-check and manage pods

# 4. Pods in the host network of a node can communicate
#    with all pods on ALL nodes without NAT
#    → hostNetwork:true pods participate in the flat network

# Verify rule #1: Each pod has unique IP
kubectl get pods -o wide
# NAME       READY  STATUS   IP            NODE
# webapp-1   1/1    Running  10.244.1.5    worker-1
# webapp-2   1/1    Running  10.244.2.8    worker-2 (different subnet!)

# Verify rule #2: Cross-node pod communication
kubectl exec webapp-1 -- curl -s 10.244.2.8:8080
# Response from webapp-2 (no NAT needed)
Explanation

These 4 rules define the Kubernetes network model. Every CNI plugin (Calico, Flannel, Weave, Cilium) MUST implement these rules. The flat network model means any pod can reach any other pod by IP — no NAT, no port mapping. This is fundamentally different from Docker's default bridge networking. On the CKA, understanding this model helps you: (1) debug connectivity issues (if pod-to-pod fails, the CNI is broken), (2) understand why Services exist (pods have dynamic IPs), (3) know that NetworkPolicies are the ONLY way to restrict traffic (everything is allowed by default).

Q2
A newly joined node shows NotReady status. kubectl describe node shows "runtime network not ready: NetworkReady=false reason:NetworkPluginNotReady". What's wrong?
Show Answer
Answer
# Diagnosis: CNI plugin is NOT installed on this node

# Step 1: Check if CNI is installed cluster-wide
kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep -E "calico|flannel|weave|cilium"
# (no output — CNI not installed!)

# Step 2: Install the CNI plugin
# Calico:
kubectl apply -f https://docs.projectcalico.org/manifests/calico.yaml

# Flannel:
kubectl apply -f https://github.com/flannel-io/flannel/releases/latest/download/kube-flannel.yml

# Step 3: Wait and verify
kubectl get nodes -w
# NAME      STATUS   ROLES       AGE
# worker-1  Ready    worker      5m   ← Now Ready!

# Step 4: Verify CNI pods are running
kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l k8s-app=calico-node
# calico-node-abc   1/1   Running   worker-1
# calico-node-def   1/1   Running   worker-2
Explanation

The CNI (Container Network Interface) plugin is responsible for setting up pod networking. Without it, the kubelet can't configure network namespaces for pods, so the node reports NotReady. On the CKA, this is a CLASSIC troubleshooting question. After kubeadm init or kubeadm join, the CNI is NOT automatically installed — you must apply it manually. The exam question will tell you which CNI to use and may provide the URL. Check the kubeadm init output notes or the question instructions.

Q3
What is kube-proxy and what modes can it operate in? How do you check which mode is being used?
Show Answer
Answer
# kube-proxy modes:
# 1. iptables (default) — uses iptables rules for Service routing
# 2. IPVS — uses Linux IPVS for load balancing (better performance)
# 3. userspace — legacy, rarely used (proxies in userspace)

# Check current mode:
kubectl get configmap kube-proxy -n kube-system -o yaml | grep mode
# mode: ""          ← empty string = iptables (default)
# mode: "ipvs"      ← IPVS mode
# mode: "iptables"  ← explicit iptables

# Or check kube-proxy logs:
kubectl logs -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-proxy | grep "Using"
# "Using iptables proxier"

# Verify kube-proxy is running on all nodes:
kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-proxy -o wide
# kube-proxy-abc   1/1   Running   controlplane
# kube-proxy-def   1/1   Running   worker-1
# kube-proxy-ghi   1/1   Running   worker-2

# kube-proxy runs as a DaemonSet:
kubectl get ds -n kube-system kube-proxy
Explanation

kube-proxy runs on every node as a DaemonSet and maintains network rules that allow Service traffic to reach pods. In iptables mode, it creates nat table rules. In IPVS mode, it uses Linux Virtual Server for more efficient load balancing. On the CKA, know that: (1) kube-proxy handles Service → Pod routing (not pod-to-pod, which is CNI), (2) if kube-proxy is down, Services break but direct pod-to-pod communication still works, (3) the mode is configured in the kube-proxy ConfigMap in kube-system.

Q4
What is the Pod CIDR and Service CIDR? How do you find these ranges in an existing cluster?
Show Answer
Answer
# Pod CIDR: IP range for pod IPs
# Service CIDR: IP range for Service ClusterIPs

# Find Pod CIDR:
kubectl cluster-info dump | grep -m1 cluster-cidr
# --cluster-cidr=10.244.0.0/16

# Or from the controller manager:
kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l component=kube-controller-manager \
  -o jsonpath='{.items[0].spec.containers[0].command}' | tr ',' '\n' | grep cluster-cidr
# --cluster-cidr=10.244.0.0/16

# Find Service CIDR:
kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l component=kube-apiserver \
  -o jsonpath='{.items[0].spec.containers[0].command}' | tr ',' '\n' | grep service-cluster
# --service-cluster-ip-range=10.96.0.0/12

# Per-node Pod CIDR:
kubectl get nodes -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.metadata.name}{"\t"}{.spec.podCIDR}{"\n"}{end}'
# controlplane    10.244.0.0/24
# worker-1        10.244.1.0/24
# worker-2        10.244.2.0/24
Explanation

Pod CIDR is set during kubeadm init --pod-network-cidr=10.244.0.0/16 and split into per-node subnets by the controller-manager. Service CIDR is set via --service-cluster-ip-range on the API server. These ranges MUST NOT overlap with each other or with the host network. On the CKA, knowing where to find these values is essential for: (1) troubleshooting IP conflicts, (2) configuring CNI plugins, (3) understanding why a pod IP is 10.244.x.x but a Service IP is 10.96.x.x.

Q5
How do you launch a network troubleshooting pod with all common tools? Show how to test connectivity between pods.
Show Answer
Answer
# Launch a debug pod with network tools:
kubectl run nettest --image=nicolaka/netshoot \
  --rm -it --restart=Never -- bash

# Inside the debug pod:
# Test DNS resolution
nslookup kubernetes.default
# Server: 10.96.0.10 (kube-dns)
# Name: kubernetes.default.svc.cluster.local
# Address: 10.96.0.1

# Test connectivity to a service
curl -s http://web-svc:80
# (HTML response)

# Test pod-to-pod directly by IP
curl -s http://10.244.1.5:8080

# Check network path
traceroute 10.244.2.8

# Test TCP port connectivity
nc -zv web-svc 80
# Connection to web-svc 80 port [tcp/http] succeeded!

# Minimal alternative (no netshoot image):
kubectl run test --image=busybox --rm -it -- wget -qO- http://web-svc:80
# Or:
kubectl exec existing-pod -- curl -s http://web-svc:80
Explanation

The nicolaka/netshoot image includes curl, nslookup, dig, traceroute, nc (netcat), tcpdump, and more. On the CKA, you can also use busybox (has wget, nslookup) or the curlimages/curl image. The --rm -it --restart=Never flags ensure the pod is cleaned up after exit. Key debugging pattern: (1) Test DNS with nslookup, (2) Test HTTP with curl/wget, (3) Test TCP with nc -zv. If DNS fails, the issue is CoreDNS. If DNS works but curl fails, the issue is kube-proxy or NetworkPolicy.

Q6
What happens to pod networking when kube-proxy crashes on a node? Can pods still communicate?
Show Answer
Answer
# When kube-proxy is down on a node:

# STILL WORKS:
# ✅ Pod-to-pod direct communication (by pod IP)
kubectl exec webapp-1 -- curl http://10.244.2.8:8080  # Works!

# BROKEN:
# ❌ Service → Pod routing (ClusterIP, NodePort, LoadBalancer)
kubectl exec webapp-1 -- curl http://web-svc:80  # Times out!
# (iptables/IPVS rules aren't being updated)

# DNS MAY WORK:
# ✅ DNS resolution still works (CoreDNS is independent)
kubectl exec webapp-1 -- nslookup web-svc
# Returns the ClusterIP, but traffic to that IP won't route

# Verify kube-proxy status:
kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-proxy -o wide
# Check if the pod on the affected node is CrashLoopBackOff

# Fix:
kubectl delete pod -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-proxy \
  --field-selector spec.nodeName=worker-1
# DaemonSet recreates it
Explanation

kube-proxy only handles Service-to-Pod routing by maintaining iptables/IPVS rules. Pod-to-pod networking is handled by the CNI plugin. So when kube-proxy is down: Services break (ClusterIP doesn't route) but direct pod IP communication works fine. On the CKA, this distinction is critical for diagnosing network issues: "Service doesn't work but pods can ping each other" = kube-proxy problem. "No pod communication at all" = CNI problem. Deleting the kube-proxy pod lets the DaemonSet recreate it, which usually fixes the issue.

Q7
What is the difference between hostNetwork: true and a regular pod? When would you use it?
Show Answer
Answer
# Regular pod: gets its own network namespace with a pod IP
# hostNetwork pod: uses the node's network namespace directly

apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: host-network-pod
spec:
  hostNetwork: true    # ← Uses node's IP and ports
  containers:
  - name: nginx
    image: nginx
    ports:
    - containerPort: 80  # Actually binds to the NODE's port 80!

# Regular pod:
#   IP: 10.244.1.5 (pod network)
#   Can bind any port without conflict with other pods

# hostNetwork pod:
#   IP: 192.168.1.100 (same as node!)
#   Port conflicts with other host-network pods
#   Can access node-level network interfaces

# Use cases:
# - Control plane components (kube-apiserver, etcd)
# - CNI plugins (need host network access)
# - Network monitoring tools
# - kube-proxy itself uses hostNetwork
Explanation

hostNetwork: true makes the pod share the node's network stack — it sees the node's interfaces, uses the node's IP, and can bind to the node's ports. This is how control-plane components work: the API server binds to port 6443 on the node's IP. On the CKA, hostNetwork appears in questions about: (1) static pods (control-plane components), (2) why certain system pods have node IPs instead of pod IPs, (3) networking troubleshooting where kubectl get pods -o wide shows a node IP instead of the expected pod CIDR IP.

Q8
How do you check if a CNI plugin is installed and which one is being used?
Show Answer
Answer
# Method 1: Check running CNI pods
kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep -iE "calico|flannel|weave|cilium"
# calico-node-abc           1/1   Running   (Calico is installed)

# Method 2: Check CNI configuration on the node
ls /etc/cni/net.d/
# 10-calico.conflist    ← Calico config file

cat /etc/cni/net.d/10-calico.conflist | head -5
# {"name": "k8s-pod-network", "cniVersion": "0.3.1", ...}

# Method 3: Check CNI binaries
ls /opt/cni/bin/
# calico  calico-ipam  bandwidth  bridge  flannel ...

# Method 4: Check node annotations
kubectl get node worker-1 -o jsonpath='{.metadata.annotations}' | python3 -m json.tool | grep cni

# Method 5: Check kubelet config
ps aux | grep kubelet | grep cni
# --cni-bin-dir=/opt/cni/bin --cni-conf-dir=/etc/cni/net.d
Explanation

CNI plugins install themselves as DaemonSets (one pod per node) and place their config in /etc/cni/net.d/ and binaries in /opt/cni/bin/. The kubelet reads the CNI config to set up pod networking. On the CKA, the fastest way to identify the CNI is kubectl get pods -n kube-system — look for calico-node, flannel, weave-net, or cilium pods. If NO CNI pods exist and nodes are NotReady, you need to install one. The exam question will specify which CNI to use.

Q9
A pod can reach other pods on the SAME node but not pods on DIFFERENT nodes. What's likely wrong?
Show Answer
Answer
# Same-node works, cross-node fails → CNI overlay issue

# Diagnostic steps:
# 1. Verify the problem
kubectl exec pod-on-node1 -- ping 10.244.1.5  # Same node → works
kubectl exec pod-on-node1 -- ping 10.244.2.8  # Other node → fails

# 2. Check CNI pods on both nodes
kubectl get pods -n kube-system -o wide | grep calico
# calico-node on node1: Running
# calico-node on node2: CrashLoopBackOff!  ← Problem!

# 3. Check CNI pod logs
kubectl logs -n kube-system calico-node-xyz

# 4. Check node routes
# On node1:
ip route | grep 10.244
# 10.244.1.0/24 → local (works)
# 10.244.2.0/24 → via 192.168.1.102 (node2's IP)

# 5. Check if nodes can reach each other
# SSH to node1:
ping 192.168.1.102  # Can node1 reach node2?

# Common causes:
# - CNI pod crashed on one node
# - Firewall blocking VXLAN (port 4789) or IP-in-IP traffic
# - Missing routes between node subnets
Explanation

Same-node pod communication uses the local bridge (handled by the CNI on that node). Cross-node communication requires the CNI's overlay network (VXLAN, IP-in-IP, or BGP routing). If cross-node fails: (1) Check if the CNI DaemonSet pod is running on both nodes, (2) Check for firewall rules blocking overlay protocols (VXLAN uses UDP 4789, Calico IP-in-IP uses protocol 4), (3) Verify node-to-node connectivity. On the CKA, this is an advanced troubleshooting scenario — check CNI pods first, then logs.

Q10
How do you verify that the Kubernetes networking model is working correctly? Show a systematic verification approach.
Show Answer
Answer
# Systematic network verification (5 checks):

# 1. Node status (CNI installed?)
kubectl get nodes
# All nodes should be Ready

# 2. Pod-to-Pod same node
kubectl run test1 --image=busybox --restart=Never -- sleep 3600
kubectl run test2 --image=busybox --restart=Never -- sleep 3600
# Force same node with nodeName or check with -o wide
kubectl exec test1 -- wget -qO- http://$(kubectl get pod test2 -o jsonpath='{.status.podIP}')

# 3. Pod-to-Pod cross node
# Schedule pods on different nodes and test connectivity

# 4. Pod-to-Service (kube-proxy working?)
kubectl exec test1 -- wget -qO- http://kubernetes.default.svc:443 --no-check-certificate

# 5. DNS resolution (CoreDNS working?)
kubectl exec test1 -- nslookup kubernetes.default
# Should return 10.96.0.1

# Quick all-in-one test:
kubectl run netcheck --image=nicolaka/netshoot --rm -it -- sh -c \
  "echo '=== DNS ===' && nslookup kubernetes.default && \
   echo '=== SVC ===' && curl -sk https://kubernetes.default:443/healthz && \
   echo '=== PASS ==='"
Explanation

A systematic network check covers all layers: (1) Node-level (CNI installed, nodes Ready), (2) Pod-level (same-node communication), (3) Overlay (cross-node communication), (4) Service (kube-proxy routing), (5) DNS (CoreDNS resolution). On the CKA, if asked to "verify networking," start from the bottom up: nodes → pods → services → DNS. If step 1 fails, nothing else will work. If steps 1-3 work but step 4 fails, it's a kube-proxy issue. If steps 1-4 work but step 5 fails, it's CoreDNS.

Chapter 18: Services Deep Dive

Services are the stable networking abstraction that makes Kubernetes applications accessible. While pods come and go with dynamic IPs, a Service provides a fixed endpoint — a virtual IP and DNS name — that automatically routes traffic to healthy pods. In this chapter you'll master every Service type, understand what happens under the hood, and learn how pods discover each other using our anihpj/jobpost Django stack throughout.

🟡 Intermediate ⏱️ ~2 hours 🔗 Services 📝 CKA EXAM

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ How Services work under the hood — Endpoints, selectors, and kube-proxy integration
  • ✅ ClusterIP — internal-only access (the default and most common type)
  • ✅ NodePort — expose a Service on every node's IP at a static port
  • ✅ LoadBalancer — cloud-provider external load balancers
  • ✅ ExternalName — CNAME-based redirection to external services
  • ✅ Headless Services — direct pod DNS without proxy
  • ✅ Service Discovery — DNS-based and environment-variable methods
🎓 CKA Relevance
Services & Networking 20%
Services Are Heavily Tested on the CKA

Expect 2-3 questions creating/debugging Services. Must-know imperative commands:

  • kubectl expose deployment anihpj --port=80 --target-port=8000 --type=ClusterIP
  • kubectl expose deployment anihpj --port=80 --target-port=8000 --type=NodePort
  • kubectl get svc and kubectl get endpoints — always check both
  • Debug: kubectl describe svc anihpj-svc — verify selector, endpoints, ports

18.1 How Services Work — Under the Hood

A Service is an API object that defines a logical set of pods (via a label selector) and a policy for accessing them. Here's what happens when you create a Service:

Service Creation — The Full Chain
  kubectl apply -f anihpj-svc.yaml
           │
           ▼
  ┌─────────────────────┐
  │    API Server        │  Stores Service object in etcd
  └─────────┬───────────┘
            │
            ▼
  ┌─────────────────────┐
  │  Endpoint Controller │  Watches pods matching selector
  │                      │  Creates Endpoints object:
  │                      │  anihpj-svc → [10.244.1.5:8000,
  │                      │                10.244.2.3:8000]
  └─────────┬───────────┘
            │
            ▼
  ┌─────────────────────┐
  │    kube-proxy        │  Watches Service + Endpoints
  │    (on every node)   │  Creates iptables/IPVS rules:
  │                      │  10.96.45.2:80 → DNAT → pod IPs
  └─────────┬───────────┘
            │
            ▼
  ┌─────────────────────┐
  │      CoreDNS         │  Creates DNS record:
  │                      │  anihpj-svc.default.svc.cluster.local
  │                      │  → 10.96.45.2
  └─────────────────────┘

Anatomy of a Service Object

🏷️ spec.selector
Label selector — determines which pods receive traffic. app: anihpj
📡 spec.ports
port = Service port (what clients use), targetPort = pod port (where app listens)
🔢 spec.clusterIP
Virtual IP assigned from Service CIDR (10.96.0.0/12). Immutable once created.
📋 Endpoints
Auto-populated list of pod IPs matching the selector. Updated on pod add/remove.
🌐 spec.type
ClusterIP (default), NodePort, LoadBalancer, or ExternalName
📖 Key Concept: Port vs TargetPort

port: 80 is what clients connect to on the Service IP. targetPort: 8000 is the container port where your app actually listens. These can be different! Your anihpj Django app listens on 8000, but clients access http://anihpj-svc:80.

18.2 ClusterIP Services

ClusterIP is the default and most common Service type. It assigns a virtual IP only reachable from within the cluster — pods and nodes can access it, but external clients cannot.

✅
When to Use
  • Internal microservice communication
  • Database access (postgres-svc)
  • Cache layer (redis-svc)
  • Backend APIs not exposed externally
❌
Limitations
  • Not accessible from outside the cluster
  • Cannot be reached from your laptop
  • Need NodePort/LoadBalancer/Ingress for external access
anihpj-clusterip-svc.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: anihpj-svc
  namespace: default
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    tier: web
spec:
  type: ClusterIP          # Default — can be omitted
  selector:
    app: anihpj             # Must match pod labels!
  ports:
    - name: http
      protocol: TCP
      port: 80              # Service port (clients connect here)
      targetPort: 8000      # Container port (Django listens here)
    - name: admin
      protocol: TCP
      port: 8080            # Second port — Django admin
      targetPort: 8000
Imperative ClusterIP Creation & Verification 
# Create ClusterIP service (imperative — fast for CKA)
kubectl expose deployment anihpj --name=anihpj-svc \
  --port=80 --target-port=8000 --type=ClusterIP

# Verify Service created
kubectl get svc anihpj-svc
# NAME         TYPE        CLUSTER-IP    EXTERNAL-IP   PORT(S)   AGE
# anihpj-svc  ClusterIP   10.96.45.2    <none>        80/TCP    5s

# Check endpoints — are pods registered?
kubectl get endpoints anihpj-svc
# NAME         ENDPOINTS                        AGE
# anihpj-svc  10.244.1.5:8000,10.244.2.3:8000  5s

# Test from another pod
kubectl exec -it debug-pod -- curl http://anihpj-svc:80
# HTTP 200 — Django responds!
Feature Default Behavior Configuration When to Use
Session Affinity None — random pod each request spec.sessionAffinity: ClientIP Sticky sessions, WebSocket apps
Session Timeout 10800s (3 hours) spec.sessionAffinityConfig.clientIP.timeoutSeconds: 3600 Adjust affinity window
Multi-Port Single port Multiple entries in spec.ports[] (each needs a name) App + metrics, HTTP + HTTPS
Session Affinity — Sticky Sessions 
# All requests from the same client IP go to the same pod
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: anihpj-sticky
spec:
  type: ClusterIP
  sessionAffinity: ClientIP      # ← Enable sticky sessions
  sessionAffinityConfig:
    clientIP:
      timeoutSeconds: 3600       # 1 hour affinity window
  selector:
    app: anihpj
  ports:
    - port: 80
      targetPort: 8000

18.3 NodePort Services

NodePort exposes the Service on a static port on every node's IP in the cluster. External clients can access the Service via <NodeIP>:<NodePort>.

NodePort — How External Traffic Reaches Pods
  External client: http://192.168.1.10:30080

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                                                                 │
  │  External Request → ANY node at port 30080                     │
  │                                                                 │
  │  ┌── Node 1 ──────────────┐    ┌── Node 2 ──────────────┐     │
  │  │  192.168.1.10:30080    │    │  192.168.1.11:30080    │     │
  │  │         │              │    │         │              │     │
  │  │         ▼ kube-proxy   │    │         ▼ kube-proxy   │     │
  │  │  ┌─────────────────┐   │    │  ┌─────────────────┐   │     │
  │  │  │ iptables/IPVS   │   │    │  │ iptables/IPVS   │   │     │
  │  │  │ DNAT to pod     │   │    │  │ DNAT to pod     │   │     │
  │  │  └────────┬────────┘   │    │  └────────┬────────┘   │     │
  │  │           │            │    │           │            │     │
  │  │  ┌────────▼─────────┐  │    │  ┌────────▼─────────┐  │     │
  │  │  │ anihpj-pod-abc   │  │    │  │ anihpj-pod-def   │  │     │
  │  │  │ 10.244.1.5:8000  │  │    │  │ 10.244.2.3:8000  │  │     │
  │  │  └──────────────────┘  │    │  └──────────────────┘  │     │
  │  └────────────────────────┘    └────────────────────────┘     │
  │                                                                 │
  │  Note: Request to Node 1:30080 can be routed to pod on Node 2  │
  │  NodePort range: 30000 - 32767 (configurable in API server)    │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
anihpj-nodeport-svc.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: anihpj-nodeport
spec:
  type: NodePort
  selector:
    app: anihpj
  ports:
    - name: http
      protocol: TCP
      port: 80              # ClusterIP port (internal)
      targetPort: 8000      # Container port
      nodePort: 30080       # External port on every node
                            # Range: 30000-32767
                            # Omit to auto-assign
Working with NodePort Services 
# Create NodePort (imperative)
kubectl expose deployment anihpj --name=anihpj-nodeport \
  --port=80 --target-port=8000 --type=NodePort

# Service gets BOTH a ClusterIP AND a NodePort
kubectl get svc anihpj-nodeport
# NAME              TYPE       CLUSTER-IP    PORT(S)        AGE
# anihpj-nodeport  NodePort   10.96.80.12   80:31245/TCP   5s
#                                            ↑    ↑
#                                            |    └ NodePort (auto-assigned)
#                                            └ ClusterIP port

# Access from outside the cluster
curl http://192.168.1.10:31245     # via Node 1
curl http://192.168.1.11:31245     # via Node 2 — same result!
⚠️ NodePort Limitations
  • Only 1 Service per port (limited range 30000-32767)
  • No TLS termination — clients use HTTP
  • Clients must know node IPs — no load balancing across nodes
  • Not suitable for production — use LoadBalancer or Ingress instead
externalTrafficPolicy Behavior Client Source IP Trade-off
Cluster (default) Traffic can route to pods on any node — extra hop if pod is on a different node ❌ SNAT'd — original IP lost Better load distribution, but loses client IP
Local Traffic only routes to pods on the same node it arrived on ✅ Preserved — see real client IP May have uneven load if pods aren't on every node
Preserving Client Source IP 
# Important for logging, rate limiting, geo-routing
spec:
  type: NodePort
  externalTrafficPolicy: Local    # ← Preserves client IP
  selector:
    app: anihpj
  ports:
    - port: 80
      targetPort: 8000
      nodePort: 30080

18.4 LoadBalancer Services

LoadBalancer is the standard way to expose Services to the internet in cloud environments (AWS, GCP, Azure). It provisions an actual external load balancer that distributes traffic across your nodes.

LoadBalancer Service — Cloud Integration
  Internet user: http://203.0.113.50:80

  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                                                                  │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐                    │
  │  │  Cloud Load Balancer (AWS ELB / GCP LB) │                    │
  │  │  External IP: 203.0.113.50              │                    │
  │  │  Provisioned automatically by K8s       │                    │
  │  └────────────┬────────────────┬───────────┘                    │
  │               │                │                                 │
  │     ┌─────────▼────┐    ┌─────▼────────┐                       │
  │     │ Node 1:30080 │    │ Node 2:30080 │   (NodePort used      │
  │     │ (kube-proxy) │    │ (kube-proxy) │    under the hood)    │
  │     └──────┬───────┘    └────┬─────────┘                       │
  │            │                 │                                   │
  │     ┌──────▼──────────┐  ┌──▼──────────────┐                   │
  │     │ anihpj pod-abc  │  │ anihpj pod-def  │                   │
  │     │ 10.244.1.5:8000 │  │ 10.244.2.3:8000 │                   │
  │     └─────────────────┘  └─────────────────┘                   │
  │                                                                  │
  │  LoadBalancer = ClusterIP + NodePort + External LB              │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
anihpj-loadbalancer-svc.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: anihpj-lb
  annotations:
    # Cloud-specific annotations
    service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-type: nlb   # AWS NLB
spec:
  type: LoadBalancer
  selector:
    app: anihpj
  ports:
    - name: http
      protocol: TCP
      port: 80
      targetPort: 8000
  # Optional: restrict external access by IP
  loadBalancerSourceRanges:
    - 10.0.0.0/8
    - 203.0.113.0/24
Working with LoadBalancer Services 
# Create LoadBalancer service
kubectl apply -f anihpj-loadbalancer-svc.yaml

# Check — EXTERNAL-IP may show <pending> initially
kubectl get svc anihpj-lb -w
# NAME        TYPE           CLUSTER-IP    EXTERNAL-IP     PORT(S)        AGE
# anihpj-lb  LoadBalancer   10.96.90.1    <pending>       80:31890/TCP   5s
# anihpj-lb  LoadBalancer   10.96.90.1    203.0.113.50    80:31890/TCP   30s

# Access from the internet
curl http://203.0.113.50:80
# Django anihpj responds!

# On bare metal without cloud LB — use MetalLB
# EXTERNAL-IP stays <pending> forever without a LB controller
💡 Bare Metal: Use MetalLB

On-premises clusters have no cloud LB provider. MetalLB fills this gap by assigning real external IPs from a configured pool. Often used in CKA lab environments and homelab setups.

EndpointSlices — The Modern Endpoints

📋 Endpoints (legacy)
Single object listing ALL pod IPs for a Service. Becomes huge in large clusters — every pod change updates the entire object.
📦 EndpointSlices (v1)
Splits endpoints into chunks of ~100 pods each. Only the affected slice is updated — much more efficient at scale.
🔍 View them
kubectl get endpointslices -l kubernetes.io/service-name=anihpj-svc
✅ Default since
Kubernetes 1.21+. Automatically created for every Service. Endpoints still exist for backward compatibility.

18.5 ExternalName Services

ExternalName is a special Service type that maps a Service name to an external DNS name via a CNAME record. No proxying, no ClusterIP — just DNS redirection.

🔀
How It Works

When a pod resolves external-db, CoreDNS returns a CNAME pointing to the external hostname. Traffic goes directly to the external endpoint — kube-proxy is not involved.

📋
Use Cases
  • Point to an external database (RDS, Cloud SQL)
  • Migrate from external to internal service gradually
  • Abstract external dependencies behind K8s DNS
external-db-svc.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: external-db
  namespace: default
spec:
  type: ExternalName
  externalName: mydb.example.com    # External DNS name
  # No selector!
  # No ClusterIP!
  # No ports!
Using ExternalName Services 
# From inside a pod:
nslookup external-db
# external-db.default.svc.cluster.local → CNAME → mydb.example.com

# Django settings.py can just use:
# DATABASES = { 'HOST': 'external-db' }
# If you later migrate to an internal postgres pod, just
# delete the ExternalName service and create a ClusterIP service
# with the same name — zero code changes!

18.6 Headless Services

A Headless Service is a ClusterIP Service with clusterIP: None. Instead of a single virtual IP, DNS resolution returns the individual pod IPs directly. This is essential for StatefulSets where clients need to reach specific pods.

Headless vs Normal Service — DNS Comparison
  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                                                                    │
  │  NORMAL Service (ClusterIP: 10.96.45.2)                          │
  │  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
  │  │  nslookup anihpj-svc                                     │    │
  │  │  → 10.96.45.2  (single ClusterIP — kube-proxy routes)   │    │
  │  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
  │                                                                    │
  │  HEADLESS Service (clusterIP: None)                               │
  │  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
  │  │  nslookup postgres-headless                               │    │
  │  │  → 10.244.1.15  (postgres-0)                             │    │
  │  │  → 10.244.2.18  (postgres-1)                             │    │
  │  │  → 10.244.3.22  (postgres-2)                             │    │
  │  │                                                           │    │
  │  │  PLUS individual pod DNS:                                 │    │
  │  │  postgres-0.postgres-headless.default.svc.cluster.local  │    │
  │  │  → 10.244.1.15                                           │    │
  │  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
  │                                                                    │
  │  Use headless when you need to reach SPECIFIC pods               │
  │  (StatefulSets, database replicas, leader election)              │
  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
postgres-headless-svc.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: postgres-headless
spec:
  clusterIP: None            # ← This makes it headless!
  selector:
    app: postgres
  ports:
    - name: postgres
      port: 5432
      targetPort: 5432
---
# Used with StatefulSet
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: StatefulSet
metadata:
  name: postgres
spec:
  serviceName: postgres-headless    # ← Must reference headless service
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: postgres
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: postgres
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: postgres
          image: postgres:15
          ports:
            - containerPort: 5432
Using Headless Service DNS 
# DNS resolves to ALL pod IPs
kubectl exec debug-pod -- nslookup postgres-headless
# Name: postgres-headless.default.svc.cluster.local
# Address: 10.244.1.15
# Address: 10.244.2.18
# Address: 10.244.3.22

# Access specific pod by ordinal name
kubectl exec debug-pod -- nslookup postgres-0.postgres-headless
# Address: 10.244.1.15    ← always the primary!

# Django connects to primary:
# DATABASES = { 'HOST': 'postgres-0.postgres-headless' }

18.7 Service Discovery — How Pods Find Services

Kubernetes provides two built-in methods for pods to discover Services. DNS is the primary method used in practice.

Method How It Works Pros Cons
DNS (Primary) CoreDNS creates records: svc-name.namespace.svc.cluster.local Always current, discoverable by name, works across namespaces Requires CoreDNS running
Environment Variables kubelet injects SVC_NAME_SERVICE_HOST and SVC_NAME_SERVICE_PORT into pods Works without DNS, simple Only services created BEFORE the pod; ordering-dependent
Service Discovery in Action 
# Method 1: DNS — the recommended way
kubectl exec anihpj-pod -- nslookup anihpj-svc
# Name:   anihpj-svc.default.svc.cluster.local
# Address: 10.96.45.2

# Short names work within the same namespace:
kubectl exec anihpj-pod -- curl http://anihpj-svc:80       # ✅ same namespace
kubectl exec anihpj-pod -- curl http://anihpj-svc.prod:80  # ✅ "prod" namespace
kubectl exec anihpj-pod -- curl http://anihpj-svc.prod.svc.cluster.local:80  # ✅ FQDN

# Method 2: Environment variables
kubectl exec anihpj-pod -- env | grep ANIHPJ_SVC
# ANIHPJ_SVC_SERVICE_HOST=10.96.45.2
# ANIHPJ_SVC_SERVICE_PORT=80
# ANIHPJ_SVC_SERVICE_PORT_HTTP=80
DNS Name Resolution Hierarchy
  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  DNS Resolution for Services                                     │
  │                                                                   │
  │  Short name:     anihpj-svc                                      │
  │                  ↓ (search domain appended)                      │
  │  Within NS:      anihpj-svc.default                              │
  │                  ↓                                                │
  │  Cluster FQDN:   anihpj-svc.default.svc.cluster.local           │
  │                  ↓                                                │
  │  Resolves to:    10.96.45.2 (ClusterIP)                          │
  │                                                                   │
  │  ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════          │
  │                                                                   │
  │  Cross-namespace: anihpj-svc.prod                                │
  │                   ↓                                               │
  │  Cluster FQDN:    anihpj-svc.prod.svc.cluster.local             │
  │                   ↓                                               │
  │  Resolves to:     10.96.88.5 (prod namespace Service)            │
  │                                                                   │
  │  ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════          │
  │                                                                   │
  │  Pod DNS resolv.conf (auto-configured):                          │
  │  nameserver 10.96.0.10  (CoreDNS ClusterIP)                     │
  │  search default.svc.cluster.local svc.cluster.local cluster.local│
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

🔑 Chapter 18 Quick Reference

Service Type ClusterIP External Access Use Case
ClusterIP✅ Virtual IP❌ Internal onlyPod-to-pod communication
NodePort✅ Virtual IP✅ via NodeIP:PortDev/test, simple external access
LoadBalancer✅ Virtual IP✅ via cloud LBProduction external access
ExternalName❌ NoneN/A (CNAME only)External service aliasing
Headless❌ None❌ Internal onlyStatefulSets, direct pod access
🧪 CKA Practice Scenario: Service Debugging

Situation: You've deployed the anihpj app and created a Service, but kubectl get endpoints anihpj-svc shows <none>. The app pods are Running.

Debug: Empty Endpoints 
# Step 1: Check what the Service selector is looking for
kubectl get svc anihpj-svc -o jsonpath='{.spec.selector}'
# {"app":"anihpj-web"}     ← The selector value

# Step 2: Check what labels the pods actually have
kubectl get pods --show-labels | grep anihpj
# anihpj-abc  Running  app=anihpj,tier=web
#                      ↑ Label is "anihpj" not "anihpj-web"!

# Step 3: Fix — either update the Service selector or the pod labels
# Option A: Patch the Service selector
kubectl patch svc anihpj-svc -p '{"spec":{"selector":{"app":"anihpj"}}}'

# Step 4: Verify endpoints are now populated
kubectl get endpoints anihpj-svc
# ENDPOINTS: 10.244.1.5:8000,10.244.2.3:8000  ✅ Fixed!

⚠️ CKA Gotcha: Services Deep Dive Traps

  • ClusterIP vs NodePort vs LoadBalancer — ClusterIP = internal only. NodePort = external via <NodeIP>:30000-32767. LoadBalancer = cloud only (won't work on kubeadm clusters without MetalLB)
  • Empty endpoints = selector mismatch — If kubectl get endpoints <svc> shows no IPs, the Service selector doesn't match any pod labels. This is the #1 service debugging scenario on the exam
  • targetPort vs port — port is what clients connect to (the Service port). targetPort is where traffic goes (the container port). If your app listens on 8000, targetPort: 8000
  • Headless Service — clusterIP: None — no virtual IP, DNS returns individual pod IPs. Required for StatefulSets. The exam tests this
  • ExternalName Service — Returns a CNAME record. No proxying. Used for external database references. Rarely tested but know it exists

CKA Speed Tip — Service troubleshooting flowchart:
1. kubectl get svc — does the service exist?
2. kubectl get endpoints <svc> — are there backend IPs?
3. If no endpoints: compare kubectl get svc <svc> -o yaml | grep selector vs kubectl get pods --show-labels
4. If endpoints exist but no traffic: check kube-proxy logs and NetworkPolicies

⚡ Service commands: kubectl expose deployment web --port=80 --target-port=8080 --type=NodePort
kubectl expose pod mypod --port=80 --name=mypod-svc --type=ClusterIP
kubectl get svc -o wide — see selectors and ports
kubectl get endpoints — verify backend pod IPs

📝 Chapter 18 — CKA Practice Questions

Q1
Create a ClusterIP Service that exposes a Deployment webapp on port 80, targeting container port 8080. Show the imperative and YAML approach.
Show Answer
Answer
# Imperative (fastest on CKA):
kubectl expose deployment webapp \
  --port=80 --target-port=8080 \
  --type=ClusterIP --name=webapp-svc

# YAML equivalent:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: webapp-svc
spec:
  type: ClusterIP        # Default — internal only
  selector:
    app: webapp           # Must match Deployment pod labels!
  ports:
  - port: 80             # Service port (clients connect here)
    targetPort: 8080     # Container port (traffic forwarded here)
    protocol: TCP

# Verify:
kubectl get svc webapp-svc
# NAME        TYPE       CLUSTER-IP    PORT(S)
# webapp-svc  ClusterIP  10.96.45.23   80/TCP

kubectl get endpoints webapp-svc
# NAME        ENDPOINTS
# webapp-svc  10.244.1.5:8080,10.244.2.8:8080,10.244.1.9:8080
Explanation

kubectl expose creates a Service using the Deployment's pod labels as the selector. port is what clients use (Service's listening port), targetPort is where traffic is forwarded (container's port). If targetPort is omitted, it defaults to the same value as port. On the CKA, always check kubectl get endpoints after creating a Service — if it shows no endpoints, the selector doesn't match any pod labels. This is the #1 Service debugging scenario.

Q2
What are the 4 Service types in Kubernetes? Explain each with a use case.
Show Answer
Answer
Type Access Use Case
ClusterIP Internal only (default) Backend services, databases
NodePort External via <NodeIP>:30000-32767 Dev/test, bare-metal access
LoadBalancer Cloud load balancer + external IP Production cloud deployments
ExternalName CNAME DNS redirect External service aliases 
# NodePort example:
kubectl expose deployment webapp --type=NodePort \
  --port=80 --target-port=8080
# Access: curl http://<node-ip>:<assigned-nodeport>

# LoadBalancer (cloud only):
kubectl expose deployment webapp --type=LoadBalancer \
  --port=80 --target-port=8080
# Access: curl http://<external-ip>:80

# ExternalName:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: external-db
spec:
  type: ExternalName
  externalName: db.example.com  # CNAME record
Explanation

Each type builds on the previous: NodePort includes a ClusterIP, LoadBalancer includes a NodePort + ClusterIP. ExternalName is different — it just returns a CNAME record (no proxying, no ClusterIP). On the CKA, LoadBalancer won't get an external IP on kubeadm clusters (stays <pending>) — only works on cloud providers or with MetalLB. NodePort range is always 30000-32767. Know these boundaries and which type to use in each scenario.

Q3
A Service has no endpoints. kubectl get endpoints webapp-svc shows <none>. How do you diagnose and fix this?
Show Answer
Answer
# Step 1: Check the Service selector
kubectl get svc webapp-svc -o jsonpath='{.spec.selector}'
# {"app":"webapp"}

# Step 2: Check pod labels
kubectl get pods --show-labels
# NAME       READY  LABELS
# web-abc    1/1    app=web         ← Mismatch! "web" ≠ "webapp"

# Step 3: Fix — either change the Service selector or pod labels

# Option A: Fix the Service selector
kubectl patch svc webapp-svc -p '{"spec":{"selector":{"app":"web"}}}'

# Option B: Fix the pod labels
kubectl label pod web-abc app=webapp --overwrite

# Step 4: Verify endpoints appear
kubectl get endpoints webapp-svc
# NAME        ENDPOINTS
# webapp-svc  10.244.1.5:8080

# Common causes of empty endpoints:
# 1. Selector doesn't match pod labels (most common!)
# 2. No pods exist with those labels
# 3. Pods exist but aren't Ready (failed readinessProbe)
# 4. Pods are in a different namespace than the Service
Explanation

Empty endpoints = the Service selector doesn't match any pod labels. This is the #1 Service debugging scenario on the CKA exam. The fix is always: compare service.spec.selector with pod.metadata.labels. They must match exactly (key AND value). Also check: pods must be Ready (passing readinessProbe) AND in the same namespace. A pod that exists but fails its readiness probe won't appear in endpoints either. On the CKA, this 3-step pattern solves most Service issues: (1) get svc selector, (2) get pod labels, (3) compare.

Q4
What is a headless Service? How is it different from a regular ClusterIP Service and when do you need one?
Show Answer
Answer
# Headless Service: clusterIP: None
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: db-headless
spec:
  clusterIP: None        # ← No virtual IP assigned
  selector:
    app: postgres
  ports:
  - port: 5432

# Regular ClusterIP:
# DNS lookup → returns the Service's virtual IP (10.96.x.x)
# nslookup db-svc → 10.96.0.50

# Headless Service:
# DNS lookup → returns individual pod IPs directly
# nslookup db-headless → 10.244.1.5, 10.244.2.8
# (one IP per pod matching the selector)

# Use cases for headless Services:
# 1. StatefulSets (each pod needs its own DNS name)
#    web-0.db-headless.default.svc.cluster.local
#    web-1.db-headless.default.svc.cluster.local
# 2. Client-side load balancing (app picks which pod)
# 3. Service discovery without proxying
Explanation

A headless Service has no ClusterIP — DNS returns pod IPs directly instead of a single virtual IP. This is required for StatefulSets because each pod needs its own stable DNS name (e.g., postgres-0.db-headless.ns.svc.cluster.local). Without a headless Service, StatefulSets can't provide stable network identities. On the CKA, the key signals for headless: (1) question mentions StatefulSet, (2) "individual pod DNS names needed," (3) "client-side load balancing." Set clusterIP: None — that's the only difference from a normal ClusterIP Service.

Q5
Expose a pod named nginx-pod as a NodePort Service on port 30080. Show the command and verify external access.
Show Answer
Answer
# Create NodePort with specific port
kubectl expose pod nginx-pod --port=80 --type=NodePort \
  --name=nginx-nodeport

# The nodePort is randomly assigned (30000-32767)
# To set a specific nodePort, use YAML:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: nginx-nodeport
spec:
  type: NodePort
  selector:
    run: nginx-pod    # Match the pod's labels
  ports:
  - port: 80
    targetPort: 80
    nodePort: 30080   # ← Specific port

kubectl apply -f nodeport-svc.yaml

# Verify:
kubectl get svc nginx-nodeport
# NAME             TYPE      CLUSTER-IP   PORT(S)       
# nginx-nodeport   NodePort  10.96.15.8   80:30080/TCP

# Test access from ANY node's IP:
curl http://<node-ip>:30080
# Welcome to nginx!

# Works on ALL nodes, even nodes where nginx-pod isn't running
# kube-proxy routes the traffic to the correct node
Explanation

NodePort exposes the Service on a static port on every node. You can access it via <any-node-IP>:<nodePort>. kube-proxy ensures traffic reaches the correct pod regardless of which node you hit. On the CKA, use kubectl expose --type=NodePort for quick creation, but you'll need YAML to specify a custom nodePort. The nodePort field must be in the 30000-32767 range. Note: NodePort also creates a ClusterIP — so the Service is accessible both internally (via ClusterIP) and externally (via NodePort).

Q6
What happens to Service endpoints when a pod fails its readiness probe? How is this different from a liveness probe failure?
Show Answer
Answer
# Readiness probe failure:
# → Pod is REMOVED from Service endpoints
# → No traffic is routed to it
# → Pod keeps running (not restarted)
# → When probe passes again, pod is re-added to endpoints

# Liveness probe failure:
# → Pod is RESTARTED (killed and recreated)
# → During restart, it's also removed from endpoints
# → After restart, if readiness passes, re-added to endpoints

# Combined behavior:
spec:
  containers:
  - name: app
    readinessProbe:     # Controls endpoint membership
      httpGet:
        path: /ready
        port: 8080
      periodSeconds: 5
    livenessProbe:      # Controls pod lifecycle
      httpGet:
        path: /healthz
        port: 8080
      periodSeconds: 10

# Watch endpoints change in real-time:
kubectl get endpoints webapp-svc -w
# ENDPOINTS
# 10.244.1.5:8080,10.244.2.8:8080  (2 healthy pods)
# 10.244.1.5:8080                   (pod 2 failed readiness)
# 10.244.1.5:8080,10.244.2.8:8080  (pod 2 recovered)
Explanation

Readiness probes control traffic routing: failing = removed from endpoints (no traffic). Liveness probes control pod lifecycle: failing = pod restarted. A pod can fail readiness but pass liveness — it stays running but receives no traffic (useful for warm-up periods). On the CKA, if a Service intermittently doesn't reach a pod, check its readiness probe. If a pod keeps restarting, check its liveness probe. Best practice: use BOTH — readiness for traffic gating, liveness for crash recovery.

Q7
How do you change an existing Service from ClusterIP to NodePort without deleting it?
Show Answer
Answer
# Method 1: kubectl edit
kubectl edit svc webapp-svc
# Change spec.type from ClusterIP to NodePort
# Save — a nodePort is auto-assigned

# Method 2: kubectl patch
kubectl patch svc webapp-svc \
  -p '{"spec":{"type":"NodePort"}}'

# Method 3: With specific nodePort
kubectl patch svc webapp-svc \
  -p '{"spec":{"type":"NodePort","ports":[{"port":80,"nodePort":30080}]}}'

# Verify the change:
kubectl get svc webapp-svc
# TYPE      CLUSTER-IP   PORT(S)
# NodePort  10.96.45.23  80:30080/TCP

# Note: Going FROM NodePort TO ClusterIP requires removing nodePort:
kubectl patch svc webapp-svc \
  --type='json' \
  -p='[{"op":"remove","path":"/spec/ports/0/nodePort"},
       {"op":"replace","path":"/spec/type","value":"ClusterIP"}]'
Explanation

You CAN change Service types in-place without deleting. Going ClusterIP → NodePort is straightforward. Going NodePort → ClusterIP requires removing the nodePort field first. Going to/from LoadBalancer depends on the cloud provider. On the CKA, kubectl patch is faster than kubectl edit for type changes. The ClusterIP is preserved during the change, so internal consumers aren't affected. Only the external access method changes.

Q8
How does Kubernetes discover Services? What are the two discovery mechanisms?
Show Answer
Answer
# Mechanism 1: Environment Variables (legacy)
# Every pod gets env vars for each Service in the SAME namespace
kubectl exec webapp-pod -- env | grep -i redis
# REDIS_SVC_SERVICE_HOST=10.96.78.12
# REDIS_SVC_SERVICE_PORT=6379
# REDIS_SVC_PORT=tcp://10.96.78.12:6379

# Limitation: Service must exist BEFORE the pod starts!
# Pods don't get env vars for Services created after them

# Mechanism 2: DNS (preferred, always works)
kubectl exec webapp-pod -- nslookup redis-svc
# Server: 10.96.0.10 (CoreDNS)
# Name: redis-svc.default.svc.cluster.local
# Address: 10.96.78.12

# DNS formats:
# Same namespace:     redis-svc
# Cross namespace:    redis-svc.other-ns
# Full FQDN:          redis-svc.other-ns.svc.cluster.local

# DNS always works regardless of creation order
# This is why DNS is the recommended discovery method
Explanation

Kubernetes provides two discovery mechanisms: environment variables (injected at pod start) and DNS (via CoreDNS, always available). DNS is preferred because: (1) it works regardless of creation order, (2) it works cross-namespace, (3) it's dynamic (reflects Service changes). Environment variables only contain Services that existed when the pod started. On the CKA, always use DNS names (redis-svc) instead of ClusterIPs or environment variables. For cross-namespace access, use svc-name.namespace.

Q9
Create a Service that exposes multiple ports (HTTP 80 and HTTPS 443) for a single Deployment.
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: webapp-multi-port
spec:
  selector:
    app: webapp
  ports:
  - name: http          # Name is REQUIRED for multi-port
    port: 80
    targetPort: 8080
    protocol: TCP
  - name: https         # Name is REQUIRED for multi-port
    port: 443
    targetPort: 8443
    protocol: TCP

# CRITICAL: When a Service has multiple ports,
# each port MUST have a unique "name" field!
# Without names, the API rejects the Service

# Verify:
kubectl get svc webapp-multi-port
# PORT(S)
# 80/TCP,443/TCP

# Test each port:
kubectl exec test -- curl http://webapp-multi-port:80
kubectl exec test -- curl -k https://webapp-multi-port:443
Explanation

Multi-port Services are common in production — web servers often serve both HTTP and HTTPS. The key CKA gotcha: when defining multiple ports, each MUST have a unique name field — otherwise the API server rejects the definition. Single-port Services don't require a name (it's optional). On the CKA, if you're asked to expose both ports, remember: (1) add name to each port, (2) ensure targetPort matches the container's actual listening ports, (3) names must be lowercase alphanumeric with hyphens.

Q10
What is sessionAffinity in a Service? How do you configure it so that all requests from the same client go to the same pod?
Show Answer
Answer
# Default: No session affinity (round-robin)
# Requests from same client may hit different pods

# Enable session affinity (sticky sessions):
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: webapp-svc
spec:
  selector:
    app: webapp
  sessionAffinity: ClientIP      # ← Sticky by client IP
  sessionAffinityConfig:
    clientIP:
      timeoutSeconds: 10800      # ← Sticky for 3 hours (default)
  ports:
  - port: 80
    targetPort: 8080

# Or via kubectl patch:
kubectl patch svc webapp-svc \
  -p '{"spec":{"sessionAffinity":"ClientIP"}}'

# Verify:
kubectl describe svc webapp-svc | grep -i session
# Session Affinity: ClientIP

# Now all requests from the same source IP
# go to the same backend pod for 3 hours
Explanation

sessionAffinity: ClientIP makes the Service route all requests from the same client IP to the same backend pod. This is useful for applications with server-side sessions (though stateless apps are preferred). The only option Kubernetes supports is ClientIP (no cookie-based affinity at the Service level — use Ingress for that). On the CKA, session affinity is rarely tested directly, but understanding it helps debug "inconsistent responses" where different pods have different state.

Q11
Create an ExternalName Service that maps external-db to db.prod.example.com. How does it differ from other Service types? When would you use it?
Show Answer
Answer
# Create ExternalName Service
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: external-db
  namespace: default
spec:
  type: ExternalName
  externalName: db.prod.example.com   # ← FQDN, not an IP!

# Or imperatively:
kubectl create service externalname external-db \
  --external-name=db.prod.example.com

# How it works:
# Pod does DNS lookup for "external-db" or
#   "external-db.default.svc.cluster.local"
# CoreDNS returns a CNAME record → db.prod.example.com
# No proxy, no ClusterIP, no Endpoints — just DNS aliasing

# Verify:
kubectl get svc external-db
# NAME          TYPE           CLUSTER-IP   EXTERNAL-IP             PORT(S)
# external-db   ExternalName   <none>       db.prod.example.com     <none>

# Key differences from other Service types:
# ❌ No ClusterIP assigned
# ❌ No Endpoints object created
# ❌ No label selector — doesn't match any pods
# ❌ Cannot use with IP addresses (must be DNS name)
# ✅ Works purely at DNS level (CNAME redirect)

# Use case: point to external databases, APIs, or
# services in other namespaces without hardcoding URLs

# Cross-namespace alias example:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: postgres
  namespace: dev
spec:
  type: ExternalName
  externalName: postgres-svc.production.svc.cluster.local
Explanation

ExternalName is the simplest Service type — it creates a DNS CNAME alias with no proxying. When a pod resolves external-db, CoreDNS returns a CNAME pointing to the external FQDN. No ClusterIP, no Endpoints, no kube-proxy rules. It's used to reference external services (databases, APIs) or services in other namespaces by a local name. Two key limitations: (1) it only works with DNS names, not IP addresses, (2) it doesn't support ports — the client must know the target port. On the CKA, know that ExternalName is the only Service type that creates no Endpoints and has no ClusterIP.

Chapter 19: DNS and CoreDNS

DNS is the service discovery backbone of Kubernetes. Every time your anihpj pod connects to postgres-svc, a DNS query resolves that name to a ClusterIP. CoreDNS is the cluster DNS server that handles all of this — and knowing how to configure and troubleshoot it is a core CKA skill.

🟡 Intermediate ⏱️ ~1 hour 🔍 DNS 📝 CKA EXAM

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ How Kubernetes DNS works end-to-end — from pod query to CoreDNS response
  • ✅ CoreDNS architecture, Corefile configuration, and plugins
  • ✅ Every type of DNS record Kubernetes creates (A, AAAA, SRV, CNAME)
  • ✅ Systematic DNS troubleshooting — the #1 exam debugging skill
🎓 CKA Relevance
Services & Networking 20% Troubleshooting 30%
