DNS Questions Appear on Nearly Every CKA Exam

CoreDNS troubleshooting is one of the most tested topics. Memorize these:

  • CoreDNS pods: kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns
  • CoreDNS ConfigMap: kubectl get configmap coredns -n kube-system -o yaml
  • Test DNS: kubectl exec POD -- nslookup SERVICE_NAME
  • Full FQDN format: svc-name.namespace.svc.cluster.local

19.1 How Kubernetes DNS Works

Every pod in Kubernetes is automatically configured to use the cluster DNS server (CoreDNS). Here's the complete resolution flow:

DNS Resolution Flow — End to End
  anihpj pod runs: curl http://postgres-svc:5432

  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                                                                      │
  │  Step 1: Pod checks /etc/resolv.conf (auto-configured by kubelet)   │
  │  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
  │  │  nameserver 10.96.0.10                                        │ │
  │  │  search default.svc.cluster.local svc.cluster.local           │ │
  │  │         cluster.local                                          │ │
  │  │  ndots:5                                                       │ │
  │  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
  │                                                                      │
  │  Step 2: "postgres-svc" has fewer than 5 dots → search path used   │
  │          → Queries: postgres-svc.default.svc.cluster.local          │
  │                                                                      │
  │  Step 3: Query sent to 10.96.0.10 (CoreDNS ClusterIP)             │
  │  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────┐                    │
  │  │  CoreDNS (kube-system namespace)           │                    │
  │  │  Pod: coredns-abc-123                      │                    │
  │  │  ClusterIP: 10.96.0.10 (kube-dns Service) │                    │
  │  │                                            │                    │
  │  │  Looks up Service "postgres-svc" in        │                    │
  │  │  namespace "default"                        │                    │
  │  │  → Found!  ClusterIP: 10.96.88.5          │                    │
  │  └──────────────────┬─────────────────────────┘                    │
  │                     │                                                │
  │  Step 4: Returns A record: 10.96.88.5                              │
  │                                                                      │
  │  Step 5: Pod connects to 10.96.88.5:5432                           │
  │          → kube-proxy DNAT → actual postgres pod IP                │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Pod's /etc/resolv.conf — Explained

🔢 nameserver
10.96.0.10 — The ClusterIP of the kube-dns Service in kube-system (points to CoreDNS pods)
🔍 search domains
default.svc.cluster.local — Allows short names like postgres-svc to resolve within the namespace
🔢 ndots:5
If the name has fewer than 5 dots, append search domains first. postgres-svc (0 dots) → search path used. api.example.com (2 dots) → also search path first!
📖 ndots:5 Performance Consideration

The default ndots:5 means external domains like api.example.com generate 4-5 DNS queries before the real one succeeds. For performance-sensitive apps, you can override this in the pod spec with dnsConfig: { options: [{ name: ndots, value: "2" }] }.

DNS Resolution Decision Path

1
Pod Makes DNS Query

App calls postgres-svc → reads /etc/resolv.conf → name has 0 dots (< ndots:5)

▼
2
Search Path Applied

Tries: postgres-svc.default.svc.cluster.local → postgres-svc.svc.cluster.local → postgres-svc.cluster.local

▼
3
CoreDNS Lookup

CoreDNS checks its in-memory cache → watches K8s API for Services/Endpoints → finds match → returns ClusterIP

▼
4
Connection Made

Pod connects to ClusterIP → kube-proxy DNAT rules → routed to actual pod IP → connection established

dnsPolicy resolv.conf Source Can Resolve Services? When Used
ClusterFirst (default) CoreDNS (10.96.0.10)
+ search domains		 ✅ Yes Standard pods — always use this
Default Node's /etc/resolv.conf ❌ No — can't see Services Rarely used; hostNetwork pods
ClusterFirstWithHostNet CoreDNS (10.96.0.10) ✅ Yes Pods with hostNetwork: true that need Service DNS
None Must supply dnsConfig manually Depends on config Custom DNS (e.g., external resolver only)
Custom DNS Configuration in Pod Spec 
# Override DNS settings for a specific pod
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-custom-dns
spec:
  dnsPolicy: None           # ← Take full control of DNS
  dnsConfig:
    nameservers:
      - 10.96.0.10           # CoreDNS
      - 8.8.8.8              # Google DNS as fallback
    searches:
      - default.svc.cluster.local
      - svc.cluster.local
    options:
      - name: ndots
        value: "2"           # ← Fewer search attempts for external domains
  containers:
    - name: anihpj
      image: anihpj:latest

19.2 CoreDNS Configuration

CoreDNS is deployed as a Deployment in the kube-system namespace, typically with 2 replicas for high availability. Its configuration lives in a ConfigMap called coredns.

CoreDNS Architecture in Kubernetes
  ┌──────────────────── kube-system namespace ────────────────────┐
  │                                                                │
  │  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
  │  │  Deployment: coredns (replicas: 2)                       │ │
  │  │  ┌─────────────────┐   ┌─────────────────┐              │ │
  │  │  │ coredns-abc-111 │   │ coredns-abc-222 │              │ │
  │  │  │ (Pod 1)         │   │ (Pod 2)         │              │ │
  │  │  └────────┬────────┘   └────────┬────────┘              │ │
  │  └───────────┼─────────────────────┼────────────────────────┘ │
  │              │                     │                           │
  │  ┌───────────▼─────────────────────▼────────────────────────┐ │
  │  │  Service: kube-dns (ClusterIP: 10.96.0.10)              │ │
  │  │  Ports: 53/UDP, 53/TCP, 9153/TCP (metrics)              │ │
  │  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
  │                                                                │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
  │  │  ConfigMap: coredns                                      │  │
  │  │  Contains: Corefile (plugin configuration)               │  │
  │  │  Mounted at: /etc/coredns/Corefile                       │  │
  │  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
The Default Corefile — Annotated 
# kubectl get configmap coredns -n kube-system -o yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: coredns
  namespace: kube-system
data:
  Corefile: |
    .:53 {                          # Listen on port 53 for all zones
        errors                       # Log errors to stdout
        health {                     # Health check endpoint on :8080
            lameduck 5s              # Wait 5s before shutting down
        }
        ready                        # Readiness probe on :8181
        kubernetes cluster.local in-addr.arpa ip6.arpa {
            pods insecure            # Enable pod DNS records
            fallthrough in-addr.arpa ip6.arpa
            ttl 30                   # Cache TTL: 30 seconds
        }
        prometheus :9153             # Metrics endpoint for monitoring
        forward . /etc/resolv.conf {  # Forward external queries to
            max_concurrent 1000       # upstream DNS (node's resolv.conf)
        }
        cache 30                     # Cache all responses for 30s
        loop                         # Detect and stop DNS loops
        reload                       # Auto-reload Corefile on change
        loadbalance                  # Round-robin A/AAAA records
    }
Common CoreDNS Customizations 
# Add a custom DNS entry (stub domain)
# Edit the ConfigMap to forward *.example.com to a specific DNS server
kubectl edit configmap coredns -n kube-system

# Add this block inside the Corefile:
#   example.com:53 {
#       forward . 10.0.0.1
#   }

# Add a static host entry (hosts plugin)
# Add inside the main server block, before kubernetes:
#   hosts {
#       10.10.10.50 mydb.internal
#       fallthrough
#   }

# After editing — CoreDNS auto-reloads (reload plugin)
# Verify: check CoreDNS logs
kubectl logs -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns --tail=20
⚠️ CKA Exam: Never Delete the CoreDNS ConfigMap

If you're asked to modify DNS configuration, use kubectl edit configmap coredns -n kube-system. The reload plugin means changes take effect automatically within 30 seconds. Do NOT restart CoreDNS pods unless explicitly required — they auto-reload.

CoreDNS Plugin Purpose Key Config
kubernetes Resolves Service and Pod DNS names from the K8s API kubernetes cluster.local
forward Forwards non-cluster queries to upstream DNS servers forward . /etc/resolv.conf or forward . 8.8.8.8
cache Caches DNS responses to reduce API server load cache 30 (seconds)
loop Detects DNS forwarding loops and stops CoreDNS loop (no arguments)
reload Auto-reloads Corefile when ConfigMap changes reload (default: every 30s)
hosts Serves static DNS entries (like /etc/hosts) hosts { 10.0.0.1 mydb.local }
errors Logs DNS errors to stdout (visible via kubectl logs) errors
health HTTP health check endpoint on port 8080 health { lameduck 5s }
ready Readiness probe endpoint on port 8181 ready
prometheus Exposes metrics for monitoring on port 9153 prometheus :9153

19.3 DNS Records in Kubernetes

Kubernetes automatically creates DNS records for Services and (optionally) Pods. Understanding these record formats is essential for cross-namespace communication.

Record Type DNS Name Format Resolves To Example
A Record (Service) svc-name.namespace.svc.cluster.local ClusterIP anihpj-svc.default.svc.cluster.local → 10.96.45.2
A Record (Headless) svc-name.namespace.svc.cluster.local All Pod IPs postgres-headless.default.svc.cluster.local → 10.244.1.15, 10.244.2.18
A Record (Pod via Headless) pod-name.svc-name.namespace.svc.cluster.local Specific Pod IP postgres-0.postgres-headless.default.svc.cluster.local → 10.244.1.15
SRV Record _port-name._protocol.svc-name.namespace.svc.cluster.local Port + hostname _http._tcp.anihpj-svc.default.svc.cluster.local → 0 10 80 ...
CNAME (ExternalName) svc-name.namespace.svc.cluster.local External hostname external-db.default.svc.cluster.local → mydb.example.com
A Record (Pod direct) pod-ip-dashed.namespace.pod.cluster.local Pod IP 10-244-1-5.default.pod.cluster.local → 10.244.1.5
Exploring All DNS Record Types 
# Launch a DNS debug pod
kubectl run dnstest --image=busybox:1.36 --rm -it -- sh

# A record — Service ClusterIP
nslookup anihpj-svc.default.svc.cluster.local
# Address: 10.96.45.2

# A records — Headless Service (returns all pod IPs)
nslookup postgres-headless.default.svc.cluster.local
# Address: 10.244.1.15
# Address: 10.244.2.18

# A record — specific pod via headless service
nslookup postgres-0.postgres-headless.default.svc.cluster.local
# Address: 10.244.1.15

# SRV record — discover port and protocol
nslookup -type=SRV _http._tcp.anihpj-svc.default.svc.cluster.local
# service = 0 10 80 anihpj-svc.default.svc.cluster.local

# Pod DNS (IP dashes)
nslookup 10-244-1-5.default.pod.cluster.local
# Address: 10.244.1.5

# Cross-namespace resolution
nslookup anihpj-svc.prod.svc.cluster.local
# Address: 10.96.88.5  (Service in "prod" namespace)
💡 Short Names vs FQDN

Within the same namespace, use short names: anihpj-svc or anihpj-svc:80. Across namespaces, include the namespace: anihpj-svc.prod. Full FQDNs are rarely needed in practice but may appear on the CKA exam.

📛 Short Names (Same Namespace)

  • anihpj-svc → resolves using search path
  • anihpj-svc:80 → with port
  • Queries: anihpj-svc.default.svc.cluster.local
  • Pro: Simple, clean code
  • Con: Won't work cross-namespace
  • Use for: Most app-to-service calls

🌐 FQDN (Cross-Namespace)

  • anihpj-svc.prod.svc.cluster.local
  • db-svc.database.svc.cluster.local
  • Bypasses search path → single DNS query
  • Pro: Works from any namespace, slightly faster
  • Con: Verbose, hardcoded namespace
  • Use for: Cross-namespace calls, external DNS
🔤
A Records (Address)

Map service/pod names to IP addresses. ClusterIP services get one A record. Headless services return all pod IPs. Most common record type in K8s.

📡
SRV Records

Include port number + hostname. Format: _port._protocol.svc.ns.svc.cluster.local. Used to discover both port and address of a service.

🔗
CNAME Records

Created by ExternalName services. Maps a K8s service name to an external hostname. No proxying — just DNS aliasing.

🏷️
Pod DNS Records

Pod IPs get dashed-IP format: 10-244-1-5.ns.pod.cluster.local. Via headless services: pod-name.svc-name.ns.svc.cluster.local.

19.4 Troubleshooting DNS Issues

DNS failures are one of the most common Kubernetes problems — and one of the most tested on the CKA. Here's a systematic approach to diagnose and fix DNS issues.

1
Is CoreDNS Running?

kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns — both pods should be Running

2
Is the kube-dns Service Up?

kubectl get svc kube-dns -n kube-system — should have ClusterIP 10.96.0.10

3
Can Pods Reach CoreDNS?

kubectl exec POD -- nslookup kubernetes.default — tests basic DNS connectivity

4
Check CoreDNS Logs

kubectl logs -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns — look for errors

5
Check Corefile Config

kubectl get cm coredns -n kube-system -o yaml — verify configuration is correct

DNS Symptom Likely Cause Diagnostic Fix
nslookup times out CoreDNS pods not running kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns Check CoreDNS deployment, restart pods
SERVFAIL response Corefile misconfiguration kubectl logs -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns Fix Corefile syntax in ConfigMap
NXDOMAIN — name not found Service doesn't exist or wrong namespace kubectl get svc -A | grep svc-name Create the Service or use correct namespace prefix
External domains fail forward plugin pointing to wrong upstream Check forward . /etc/resolv.conf in Corefile Fix upstream DNS (e.g., forward . 8.8.8.8)
DNS works in some pods, not others Pod's dnsPolicy is set to Default kubectl get pod POD -o yaml | grep dnsPolicy Change to dnsPolicy: ClusterFirst (the default)
DNS loop detected Node's /etc/resolv.conf points to a local DNS that forwards back to CoreDNS CoreDNS logs: "Loop ... detected" Change forward . /etc/resolv.conf to forward . 8.8.8.8 8.8.4.4
DNS Troubleshooting Playbook 
# Step 1: Check CoreDNS pods
kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns
# NAME                     READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE
# coredns-abc-111          1/1     Running   0          5d
# coredns-abc-222          1/1     Running   0          5d

# Step 2: Check kube-dns Service
kubectl get svc kube-dns -n kube-system
# NAME       TYPE        CLUSTER-IP   PORT(S)
# kube-dns   ClusterIP   10.96.0.10   53/UDP,53/TCP,9153/TCP

# Step 3: Test DNS from a debug pod
kubectl run dnsdiag --image=busybox:1.36 --rm -it -- sh
nslookup kubernetes.default     # Should resolve to API server ClusterIP
nslookup anihpj-svc             # Should resolve to Service ClusterIP
nslookup google.com             # Should resolve (tests external forwarding)

# Step 4: Check CoreDNS logs for errors
kubectl logs -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns --tail=50
# Look for: "SERVFAIL", "i/o timeout", "Loop detected"

# Step 5: Verify pod resolv.conf
kubectl exec anihpj-pod -- cat /etc/resolv.conf
# nameserver 10.96.0.10
# search default.svc.cluster.local svc.cluster.local cluster.local

# Step 6: Check the Corefile
kubectl get configmap coredns -n kube-system -o yaml

🔑 Chapter 19 Quick Reference

Action Command
Check CoreDNS podskubectl get pods -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns
Check kube-dns Servicekubectl get svc kube-dns -n kube-system
Test DNS resolutionkubectl exec POD -- nslookup SERVICE
View CoreDNS configkubectl get cm coredns -n kube-system -o yaml
Edit CoreDNS configkubectl edit cm coredns -n kube-system
CoreDNS logskubectl logs -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns
Pod DNS configkubectl exec POD -- cat /etc/resolv.conf
Service FQDN formatsvc-name.namespace.svc.cluster.local
Pod FQDN (headless)pod-name.svc-name.namespace.svc.cluster.local
🧪 CKA Practice Scenario: DNS Broken After Cluster Upgrade

Situation: After upgrading to K8s 1.29, pods report nslookup: can't resolve 'anihpj-svc'. The CoreDNS pods are stuck in CrashLoopBackOff.

Step-by-Step DNS Recovery 
# Step 1: Confirm CoreDNS is crashing
kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns
# coredns-abc-111  0/1  CrashLoopBackOff  5 (2m ago)
# coredns-abc-222  0/1  CrashLoopBackOff  5 (2m ago)

# Step 2: Check logs for the error
kubectl logs -n kube-system coredns-abc-111
# "Loop (127.0.0.1:43522 -> :53) detected for zone '.'"
# ← DNS forwarding loop!

# Step 3: The cause — node's /etc/resolv.conf points to
# 127.0.0.53 (systemd-resolved), which forwards back to CoreDNS
# This creates a loop!

# Step 4: Fix the Corefile — replace /etc/resolv.conf with
# explicit upstream DNS servers
kubectl edit configmap coredns -n kube-system
# Change:  forward . /etc/resolv.conf
# To:      forward . 8.8.8.8 8.8.4.4

# Step 5: Wait for auto-reload (30s) or delete pods to force restart
kubectl delete pods -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns

# Step 6: Verify DNS is working again
kubectl run dnscheck --image=busybox --rm -it -- nslookup anihpj-svc
# Address: 10.96.45.2  ✅ Fixed!

⚠️ CKA Gotcha: DNS & CoreDNS Traps

  • DNS format matters — <svc>.<namespace>.svc.cluster.local for services. <pod-ip-dashed>.<namespace>.pod.cluster.local for pods. The exam tests exact FQDN knowledge
  • CoreDNS is a Deployment, not DaemonSet — Typically 2 replicas. Its ConfigMap is named coredns. The Service is kube-dns (not coredns!) in kube-system
  • DNS loop — If the node's /etc/resolv.conf points to 127.0.0.53 (systemd-resolved), CoreDNS's forward . /etc/resolv.conf creates a loop. Fix: use explicit upstream 8.8.8.8
  • ndots: 5 default — Pods first try appending search domains. nslookup postgres actually tries 4-5 FQDNs before the bare name. This slows DNS. For external names, use trailing dot: google.com.

CKA Speed Tip — DNS debugging in 30 seconds:
kubectl run dnstest --image=busybox:1.36 --rm -it -- nslookup kubernetes.default
If this fails, DNS is broken. Check:
kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns — CoreDNS pods running?
kubectl get svc -n kube-system kube-dns — kube-dns service exists?
kubectl logs -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns — error in CoreDNS?

📝 Chapter 19 Practice Drill

Timed challenge (target: 5 minutes):

  • 1. Create a service web-svc in namespace prod
  • 2. From a test pod, resolve: nslookup web-svc.prod.svc.cluster.local
  • 3. Verify short name works within same namespace: nslookup web-svc
  • 4. Edit CoreDNS Corefile to add a custom rewrite rule
  • 5. Verify CoreDNS config reload: kubectl logs -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns | grep reload

📝 Chapter 19 — CKA Practice Questions

Q1
What is the full DNS format for a Service in Kubernetes? Show the FQDN for a Service named web-svc in namespace production.
Show Answer
Answer
# Service DNS format:
# <service-name>.<namespace>.svc.cluster.local

# Full FQDN for web-svc in production:
# web-svc.production.svc.cluster.local

# DNS resolution from different contexts:
# Same namespace (production):
nslookup web-svc                    # Works!
nslookup web-svc.production         # Works!
nslookup web-svc.production.svc     # Works!
nslookup web-svc.production.svc.cluster.local  # Full FQDN

# Different namespace (from default):
nslookup web-svc                    # FAILS (wrong ns)
nslookup web-svc.production         # Works!
nslookup web-svc.production.svc.cluster.local  # Works!

# Pod DNS format (different!):
# <pod-ip-dashed>.<namespace>.pod.cluster.local
# Pod IP 10.244.1.5 → 10-244-1-5.production.pod.cluster.local

# StatefulSet pod DNS:
# <pod-name>.<headless-svc>.<namespace>.svc.cluster.local
# web-0.web-headless.production.svc.cluster.local
Explanation

Kubernetes DNS has strict formats: Services use svc-name.ns.svc.cluster.local, pods use ip-dashed.ns.pod.cluster.local, StatefulSet pods use pod-name.headless-svc.ns.svc.cluster.local. Short names like web-svc only work within the SAME namespace because of the search domain in /etc/resolv.conf. For cross-namespace access, you MUST use at least svc-name.namespace. On the CKA, DNS FQDN questions are common — memorize the format.

Q2
CoreDNS is not working — all pods fail DNS resolution. Show the complete troubleshooting flow.
Show Answer
Answer
# Step 1: Test DNS from a pod
kubectl run dnstest --image=busybox:1.36 --rm -it -- nslookup kubernetes.default
# ;; connection timed out; no servers could be reached

# Step 2: Check CoreDNS pods
kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns
# NAME                      READY  STATUS
# coredns-5d78c9869d-abc    0/1    CrashLoopBackOff  ← Problem!

# Step 3: Check CoreDNS logs
kubectl logs -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns
# "Loop detected" ← DNS forwarding loop!

# Step 4: Check kube-dns Service
kubectl get svc -n kube-system kube-dns
# NAME       TYPE       CLUSTER-IP   PORT(S)
# kube-dns   ClusterIP  10.96.0.10   53/UDP,53/TCP

# Step 5: Check CoreDNS ConfigMap
kubectl get configmap coredns -n kube-system -o yaml
# Look for: forward . /etc/resolv.conf
# If node's resolv.conf points to 127.0.0.53 → loop!

# Fix: Change forward to explicit upstream DNS
kubectl edit configmap coredns -n kube-system
# Change: forward . /etc/resolv.conf
# To:     forward . 8.8.8.8 8.8.4.4

# Restart CoreDNS to apply
kubectl rollout restart deployment coredns -n kube-system
Explanation

The DNS loop is a classic CKA troubleshooting scenario. On many Linux systems, /etc/resolv.conf points to 127.0.0.53 (systemd-resolved), which forwards to... CoreDNS, creating a loop. Fix by pointing to external DNS (8.8.8.8). The troubleshooting flow: (1) test DNS from a pod, (2) check CoreDNS pods in kube-system, (3) check logs for errors, (4) check the coredns ConfigMap. Remember: the Service is named kube-dns (not coredns) but the pods are coredns-*.

Q3
What is the CoreDNS Corefile and how do you modify it? Show how to add a custom DNS record.
Show Answer
Answer
# The Corefile is stored in the coredns ConfigMap:
kubectl get configmap coredns -n kube-system -o yaml

# Default Corefile:
# .:53 {
#     errors
#     health { lazystart }
#     ready
#     kubernetes cluster.local in-addr.arpa ip6.arpa {
#         pods insecure
#         fallthrough in-addr.arpa ip6.arpa
#     }
#     prometheus :9153
#     forward . /etc/resolv.conf {
#         max_concurrent 1000
#     }
#     cache 30
#     loop
#     reload
#     loadbalance
# }

# Add a custom DNS rewrite rule:
kubectl edit configmap coredns -n kube-system
# Add inside the .:53 block:
#     rewrite name db.myapp.local postgres-svc.production.svc.cluster.local

# Add a custom hosts entry:
# Add inside the .:53 block:
#     hosts {
#         192.168.1.100 legacy-server.company.com
#         fallthrough
#     }

# CoreDNS auto-reloads (reload plugin)
# Check reload:
kubectl logs -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns | grep reload
Explanation

The Corefile configures CoreDNS behavior. Key plugins: kubernetes (resolves cluster DNS), forward (upstream DNS for external names), cache (caching), loop (loop detection), reload (hot-reload on ConfigMap changes). On the CKA, you may be asked to: (1) modify the forward directive, (2) add custom rewrites, (3) fix a broken Corefile. The reload plugin means CoreDNS picks up ConfigMap changes automatically (within ~30 seconds) without pod restart. However, kubectl rollout restart deployment coredns -n kube-system forces immediate reload.

Q4
How do you check what DNS configuration a pod is using? What does ndots: 5 mean and why does it matter?
Show Answer
Answer
# Check a pod's DNS config:
kubectl exec my-pod -- cat /etc/resolv.conf
# nameserver 10.96.0.10
# search default.svc.cluster.local svc.cluster.local cluster.local
# options ndots:5

# ndots:5 means:
# If a name has FEWER than 5 dots, append search domains first
# "google.com" has 1 dot (< 5), so DNS tries:
#   1. google.com.default.svc.cluster.local → NXDOMAIN
#   2. google.com.svc.cluster.local → NXDOMAIN
#   3. google.com.cluster.local → NXDOMAIN
#   4. google.com → RESOLVED! (4 queries for 1 lookup!)

# Optimization: add trailing dot for external names
# "google.com." → treated as FQDN, only 1 query
kubectl exec my-pod -- nslookup google.com.

# Override DNS config in pod spec:
spec:
  dnsPolicy: "None"
  dnsConfig:
    nameservers:
    - 8.8.8.8
    searches:
    - default.svc.cluster.local
    options:
    - name: ndots
      value: "2"            # ← Reduce DNS queries
Explanation

ndots:5 is the Kubernetes default — any name with fewer than 5 dots tries all search domains first. This is why looking up google.com generates 4+ DNS queries before the final resolution. For performance-critical apps, lower ndots or use FQDNs (trailing dot). On the CKA, dnsPolicy options are: ClusterFirst (default, use CoreDNS), Default (use node's DNS), None (custom DNS config). Know how to read /etc/resolv.conf inside a pod and understand why DNS might be slow (ndots causing extra queries).

Q5
From namespace frontend, how do you connect to a Service named api-svc in namespace backend? What DNS name do you use?
Show Answer
Answer
# Cross-namespace DNS access:
# Short form (usually enough):
curl http://api-svc.backend:8080

# Full FQDN:
curl http://api-svc.backend.svc.cluster.local:8080

# Why "api-svc" alone doesn't work:
# The pod's /etc/resolv.conf has:
# search frontend.svc.cluster.local svc.cluster.local cluster.local
# So "api-svc" resolves to:
#   api-svc.frontend.svc.cluster.local → NOT what we want!

# You MUST include the namespace:
kubectl exec frontend-pod -n frontend -- \
  curl -s http://api-svc.backend:8080
# Response from backend API

# Test DNS resolution:
kubectl exec frontend-pod -n frontend -- \
  nslookup api-svc.backend
# Name: api-svc.backend.svc.cluster.local
# Address: 10.96.78.90
Explanation

Cross-namespace Service access requires including the target namespace in the DNS name: svc-name.namespace. This is because the search domain in a pod's /etc/resolv.conf only includes its own namespace. Using just api-svc would resolve to api-svc.frontend.svc.cluster.local (wrong namespace). On the CKA, cross-namespace communication is commonly tested — remember to include the namespace. Note: there are no network restrictions by default between namespaces — you need NetworkPolicies to restrict cross-namespace traffic.

Q6
What is the kube-dns Service and how does it relate to CoreDNS? Why the naming confusion?
Show Answer
Answer
# CoreDNS components:
kubectl get all -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns

# Deployment: coredns (runs CoreDNS pods)
# Pods: coredns-5d78c9869d-abc, coredns-5d78c9869d-def (2 replicas)
# Service: kube-dns (ClusterIP: 10.96.0.10) ← Named "kube-dns"!

# Why "kube-dns" not "coredns"?
# kube-dns was the original DNS server (before v1.12)
# CoreDNS replaced it, but the Service kept the name
# for backward compatibility

# All pods' /etc/resolv.conf points to:
# nameserver 10.96.0.10  ← kube-dns Service IP
# This IP is configured in kubelet:
#   --cluster-dns=10.96.0.10

# Key facts:
# - Deployment name: coredns
# - Service name: kube-dns (confusing but intentional)
# - ConfigMap name: coredns
# - Label: k8s-app=kube-dns (on everything)
Explanation

This naming mismatch is a classic CKA trap. The Deployment is coredns, the ConfigMap is coredns, but the Service is kube-dns. All share the label k8s-app=kube-dns. The Service IP (usually 10.96.0.10) is hardcoded in kubelet's --cluster-dns flag and injected into every pod's /etc/resolv.conf. On the CKA, if you're asked to check DNS, remember: kubectl get svc kube-dns -n kube-system (not coredns). CoreDNS runs as a Deployment (typically 2 replicas for HA), NOT as a DaemonSet.

Q7
How do you set a pod's dnsPolicy? What are the available policies and when do you use each?
Show Answer
Answer
Policy Behavior Use Case
ClusterFirst Use CoreDNS, fall back to node DNS (default) Normal pods
ClusterFirstWithHostNet ClusterFirst for hostNetwork pods hostNetwork: true pods needing cluster DNS
Default Use node's /etc/resolv.conf Pods needing host DNS only
None Custom — must set dnsConfig Full DNS control 
# hostNetwork pod that needs cluster DNS:
spec:
  hostNetwork: true
  dnsPolicy: ClusterFirstWithHostNet  # ← Required!
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx
Explanation

The default ClusterFirst policy sends all DNS queries to CoreDNS first. For hostNetwork pods, the default would use the node's DNS — so you need ClusterFirstWithHostNet explicitly to use CoreDNS. None requires dnsConfig to be set manually. On the CKA, ClusterFirstWithHostNet appears when a hostNetwork: true pod can't resolve Service names — that's the fix. Confusing name: Default is NOT the default policy; ClusterFirst is.

Q8
How do you quickly test if DNS is working in a cluster? What is the fastest one-liner?
Show Answer
Answer
# Fastest one-liner:
kubectl run dnscheck --image=busybox:1.36 --rm -it --restart=Never \
  -- nslookup kubernetes.default
# Server:    10.96.0.10
# Address 1: 10.96.0.10 kube-dns.kube-system.svc.cluster.local
# Name:      kubernetes.default
# Address 1: 10.96.0.1  ← API server's Service IP

# If DNS works, kubernetes.default always resolves
# It's the built-in kubernetes API Service

# If DNS fails, deeper checks:
# 1. Is CoreDNS running?
kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns

# 2. Is kube-dns Service accessible?
kubectl get svc kube-dns -n kube-system
# ClusterIP: 10.96.0.10, Ports: 53/UDP, 53/TCP

# 3. Can we reach CoreDNS directly?
kubectl run dnstest --image=busybox:1.36 --rm -it --restart=Never \
  -- nslookup kubernetes.default 10.96.0.10

# 4. Is the pod's resolv.conf correct?
kubectl exec my-pod -- cat /etc/resolv.conf
Explanation

kubernetes.default is the built-in Service that points to the API server. It always exists in every cluster. If nslookup kubernetes.default works, DNS is functioning. If it fails, CoreDNS is broken. On the CKA, this one-liner is your go-to DNS test. Use busybox:1.36 (not latest — some versions have broken nslookup). The --rm -it --restart=Never flags create a temporary pod that's cleaned up after use.

Q9
How does DNS work for a headless Service backing a StatefulSet? Show the DNS records created.
Show Answer
Answer
# Setup:
# StatefulSet: web (3 replicas)
# Headless Service: web-headless (clusterIP: None)
# Namespace: default

# DNS records created:

# Service DNS (returns ALL pod IPs):
nslookup web-headless.default.svc.cluster.local
# Address: 10.244.1.5   (web-0)
# Address: 10.244.2.8   (web-1)
# Address: 10.244.1.12  (web-2)

# Individual pod DNS:
nslookup web-0.web-headless.default.svc.cluster.local
# Address: 10.244.1.5   (only web-0)

nslookup web-1.web-headless.default.svc.cluster.local
# Address: 10.244.2.8   (only web-1)

nslookup web-2.web-headless.default.svc.cluster.local
# Address: 10.244.1.12  (only web-2)

# Format: <pod-name>.<headless-svc>.<namespace>.svc.cluster.local
# This is why StatefulSets REQUIRE a headless Service:
# It creates per-pod DNS entries!
Explanation

Headless Services for StatefulSets create two types of DNS entries: (1) the Service DNS returns all pod IPs (for discovery), (2) individual pod DNS records (pod-name.svc-name.ns.svc.cluster.local) for direct pod access. This is how database replicas find each other — web-0 can always reach web-1 by DNS name, even if its IP changes. On the CKA, knowing this format is essential for StatefulSet questions. The Service name in the DNS must match the StatefulSet's spec.serviceName field.

Q10
CoreDNS pods keep crashing with CrashLoopBackOff. The logs show "plugin/loop: Loop ... detected". How do you fix this?
Show Answer
Answer
# Problem: DNS forwarding loop
# CoreDNS forwards to /etc/resolv.conf
# Node's resolv.conf points to 127.0.0.53 (systemd-resolved)
# systemd-resolved forwards to... CoreDNS → infinite loop!

# Fix 1: Edit CoreDNS ConfigMap (recommended for CKA)
kubectl edit configmap coredns -n kube-system

# Change:
#   forward . /etc/resolv.conf
# To:
#   forward . 8.8.8.8 8.8.4.4

# Restart CoreDNS pods to pick up change immediately:
kubectl rollout restart deployment coredns -n kube-system

# Fix 2: Disable loop detection (NOT recommended)
# Remove the "loop" line from Corefile

# Fix 3: Fix node's resolv.conf (node-level fix)
# Edit /etc/resolv.conf on each node:
# Change nameserver 127.0.0.53 to nameserver 8.8.8.8

# Verify fix:
kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns
# Both coredns pods: Running

kubectl run test --image=busybox:1.36 --rm -it -- nslookup google.com
# Name: google.com
# Address: 142.250.x.x ← Working!
Explanation

The DNS loop is the most common CoreDNS failure on kubeadm clusters. On systems with systemd-resolved, /etc/resolv.conf points to 127.0.0.53 — a local stub resolver that may forward back to CoreDNS, creating an infinite loop. The loop plugin detects this and crashes CoreDNS intentionally (better to crash than infinite loop). On the CKA, the fix is always: change the forward directive in the coredns ConfigMap to explicit upstream servers (8.8.8.8). Edit the ConfigMap, restart the Deployment, verify with nslookup.

Chapter 20: Ingress and Network Policies

While Services handle internal routing, Ingress manages external HTTP/HTTPS traffic with path-based routing, TLS termination, and virtual hosts. Network Policies act as a firewall for pod-to-pod traffic — controlling which pods can talk to each other. Together, these are the most heavily tested networking topics on the CKA exam.

🟡 Intermediate ⏱️ ~2 hours 🛡️ Network Security 📝 CKA EXAM

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ Ingress — HTTP/HTTPS routing with path and host rules
  • ✅ Setting up an Ingress Controller (NGINX) in a cluster
  • ✅ Network Policies — the pod-level firewall for zero-trust networking
  • ✅ Policy types: Ingress (incoming), Egress (outgoing), and combined
  • ✅ Real-world YAML patterns for common Network Policy scenarios
  • ✅ Testing and verifying Network Policy enforcement
🎓 CKA Relevance
Services & Networking 20%
Ingress + NetworkPolicy = High-Value CKA Points

These are among the highest-weighted questions on the CKA. Tips:

  • Know the Ingress YAML by heart — the exam won't have auto-complete for all fields
  • NetworkPolicy: remember that empty podSelector {} selects ALL pods, and an empty ingress: [{}] allows ALL traffic
  • If asked to "deny all traffic" — apply a NetworkPolicy with podSelector: {} and policyTypes: [Ingress, Egress] with empty rules
  • An Ingress Controller must be running for Ingress resources to work

20.1 Ingress — HTTP/HTTPS Routing

An Ingress is an API object that manages external HTTP/HTTPS access to Services. It provides path-based routing, host-based routing, and TLS termination — all features that NodePort/LoadBalancer Services lack.

Ingress — How External HTTP Traffic Is Routed
  Internet user: https://anihpj.example.com/api/jobs/
                 https://anihpj.example.com/admin/

  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                                                                  │
  │  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐       │
  │  │  Ingress Controller (NGINX / Traefik / HAProxy)      │       │
  │  │  Deployed as a pod, exposed via LoadBalancer/NodePort │       │
  │  │  Reads Ingress rules from API server                 │       │
  │  └───────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────┘       │
  │                      │                                           │
  │          ┌───────────┼────────────┐                             │
  │          │           │            │                              │
  │   path: /api/*  path: /admin/*  path: /static/*                │
  │          │           │            │                              │
  │   ┌──────▼─────┐ ┌──▼────────┐ ┌─▼───────────┐                │
  │   │ anihpj-svc │ │ admin-svc │ │  static-svc │                │
  │   │ port: 80   │ │ port: 80  │ │  port: 80   │                │
  │   └──────┬─────┘ └──┬────────┘ └─┬───────────┘                │
  │          │           │            │                              │
  │   ┌──────▼─────┐ ┌──▼────────┐ ┌─▼───────────┐                │
  │   │ anihpj     │ │ anihpj    │ │  nginx      │                │
  │   │ pods       │ │ pods      │ │  pods       │                │
  │   └────────────┘ └───────────┘ └─────────────┘                │
  │                                                                  │
  │  Benefits over NodePort/LoadBalancer:                           │
  │  ✅ Single external IP for multiple services                   │
  │  ✅ Path-based and host-based routing                          │
  │  ✅ TLS termination at the edge                                │
  │  ✅ Centralized SSL certificate management                     │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
anihpj-ingress.yaml 
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: anihpj-ingress
  namespace: default
  annotations:
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/rewrite-target: /
spec:
  ingressClassName: nginx         # Which Ingress Controller to use
  tls:                             # TLS termination
    - hosts:
        - anihpj.example.com
      secretName: anihpj-tls       # TLS cert stored in a Secret
  rules:
    - host: anihpj.example.com     # Host-based routing
      http:
        paths:
          - path: /api              # Path-based routing
            pathType: Prefix        # Prefix or Exact
            backend:
              service:
                name: anihpj-svc   # Route to this Service
                port:
                  number: 80
          - path: /admin
            pathType: Prefix
            backend:
              service:
                name: anihpj-svc
                port:
                  number: 80
    - host: docs.example.com       # Different host → different Service
      http:
        paths:
          - path: /
            pathType: Prefix
            backend:
              service:
                name: docs-svc
                port:
                  number: 80
pathType Match Behavior Example
Prefix Matches URL path prefix by / segments /api matches /api, /api/, /api/jobs
Exact Exact URL path match (case-sensitive) /api matches only /api, NOT /api/
ImplementationSpecific Depends on the IngressClass / controller Behavior varies — avoid on exam
Working with Ingress Resources 
# Apply the Ingress
kubectl apply -f anihpj-ingress.yaml

# Check Ingress — look for ADDRESS (external IP)
kubectl get ingress
# NAME             CLASS   HOSTS                  ADDRESS        PORTS     AGE
# anihpj-ingress  nginx   anihpj.example.com     203.0.113.50   80, 443   5s

# Describe for detailed routing info
kubectl describe ingress anihpj-ingress
# Rules:
#   Host                  Path  Backends
#   ----                  ----  --------
#   anihpj.example.com
#                         /api    anihpj-svc:80 (10.244.1.5:8000,10.244.2.3:8000)
#                         /admin  anihpj-svc:80 (10.244.1.5:8000,10.244.2.3:8000)

# Create TLS Secret for HTTPS
kubectl create secret tls anihpj-tls \
  --cert=tls.crt --key=tls.key -n default
TLS Termination at Ingress — HTTPS Flow
  Browser: https://anihpj.example.com/api/jobs/

  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                                                                  │
  │  ┌──────────┐     HTTPS (443)      ┌──────────────────────┐    │
  │  │  Client   │ ═══════════════════▶ │  Ingress Controller  │    │
  │  │ (Browser) │    TLS encrypted     │  (NGINX pod)         │    │
  │  └──────────┘                       │                      │    │
  │                                     │  1. TLS handshake    │    │
  │                                     │  2. Reads TLS cert   │    │
  │                                     │     from Secret      │    │
  │                                     │     "anihpj-tls"     │    │
  │                                     │  3. Decrypts HTTPS   │    │
  │                                     │  4. Reads path /api  │    │
  │                                     │  5. Routes to        │    │
  │                                     │     anihpj-svc:80    │    │
  │                                     └──────────┬───────────┘    │
  │                                                │                 │
  │                                     HTTP (80) — unencrypted     │
  │                                                │                 │
  │                                     ┌──────────▼───────────┐    │
  │                                     │  anihpj pod          │    │
  │                                     │  :8000 (Django)      │    │
  │                                     └──────────────────────┘    │
  │                                                                  │
  │  ⚡ TLS terminated at Ingress — backend pods use plain HTTP    │
  │  🔐 Certificate stored as K8s Secret (type: kubernetes.io/tls) │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
💡 Default Backend & 404 Handling

If no Ingress rule matches the request path, the controller returns a 404. You can configure a default backend in the Ingress spec to serve a custom error page: spec.defaultBackend.service.name: default-http-backend.

20.2 Ingress Controller Setup

An Ingress resource is just configuration — it does nothing without an Ingress Controller. The controller is the actual reverse proxy (NGINX, Traefik, HAProxy) that reads Ingress rules and routes traffic.

📦
Ingress Resource

The rules — path routing, TLS config, host rules. Just a YAML API object. Does nothing by itself.

⚙️
Ingress Controller

The implementation — NGINX/Traefik pod that reads rules and configures the reverse proxy. Must be installed separately.

Ingress Controller Provider Features Best For
NGINX Ingress Kubernetes community Path/host routing, TLS, rate limiting, auth CKA exam, most clusters
Traefik Traefik Labs Auto-discovery, Let's Encrypt, middleware Dynamic environments
HAProxy HAProxy Technologies High performance, TCP/HTTP load balancing High-traffic production
AWS ALB AWS AWS Application Load Balancer integration EKS clusters
Installing NGINX Ingress Controller 
# Install NGINX Ingress Controller (bare metal / kubeadm)
kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kubernetes/ingress-nginx/controller-v1.10.0/deploy/static/provider/baremetal/deploy.yaml

# Verify the controller is running
kubectl get pods -n ingress-nginx
# NAME                                       READY   STATUS
# ingress-nginx-controller-abc12-xyz         1/1     Running

# Check the IngressClass
kubectl get ingressclass
# NAME    CONTROLLER                           PARAMETERS   AGE
# nginx   k8s.io/ingress-nginx                 <none>       30s

# Verify the controller Service
kubectl get svc -n ingress-nginx
# NAME                        TYPE       EXTERNAL-IP   PORT(S)
# ingress-nginx-controller    NodePort   <none>        80:31080/TCP,443:31443/TCP
⚠️ CKA Exam: Ingress Controller Must Be Running

If the exam asks you to create an Ingress resource and it's not working, first check if an Ingress Controller is installed: kubectl get pods -n ingress-nginx. An Ingress resource without a controller does nothing — the ADDRESS field will be empty.

20.3 Network Policies — Firewall for Pods

By default, Kubernetes allows all pods to communicate with all other pods — no restrictions. Network Policies let you define fine-grained rules controlling which pods can send traffic to which other pods, implementing a zero-trust network.

Default Behavior vs Network Policy Applied
  ┌──────────────────────── DEFAULT (No NetworkPolicy) ──────────────┐
  │                                                                   │
  │  anihpj ─────────→ postgres  ✅                                  │
  │  anihpj ─────────→ redis     ✅                                  │
  │  random-pod ─────→ postgres  ✅  ← anyone can access DB!        │
  │  random-pod ─────→ anihpj   ✅  ← no isolation at all          │
  │                                                                   │
  │  EVERYTHING is allowed. Any pod talks to any pod.                │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  ┌──────────────── WITH NetworkPolicy on postgres ──────────────────┐
  │                                                                   │
  │  NetworkPolicy: "only pods with app=anihpj can reach postgres"  │
  │                                                                   │
  │  anihpj ─────────→ postgres  ✅  (label matches)                │
  │  random-pod ─────→ postgres  ❌  BLOCKED                        │
  │  redis ──────────→ postgres  ❌  BLOCKED                        │
  │                                                                   │
  │  anihpj ─────────→ redis     ✅  (no policy on redis)           │
  │  random-pod ─────→ anihpj   ✅  (no policy on anihpj)          │
  │                                                                   │
  │  ⚠️ Policy only affects the TARGET pod (podSelector)            │
  │  ⚠️ Pods with NO policy → still fully open                     │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
🛡️
Key Concepts
  • Policies are additive — multiple policies are OR'd together
  • Applying ANY policy to a pod makes it deny by default
  • No policy = all traffic allowed (default open)
  • Requires CNI with NetworkPolicy support (Calico, Cilium)
⚠️
Critical Requirement

Flannel does NOT support NetworkPolicies. If you apply a NetworkPolicy with Flannel, it's silently ignored. The CKA exam uses Calico or Cilium — both support NetworkPolicy.

20.4 Network Policy Types

Network Policies have two directions: Ingress (incoming traffic TO the pods) and Egress (outgoing traffic FROM the pods). You can control both independently.

Policy Type Direction Controls Example
Ingress Incoming → to selected pods Who can SEND traffic to these pods Only anihpj pods can reach postgres on port 5432
Egress Outgoing ← from selected pods Where these pods can SEND traffic anihpj pods can only reach postgres and DNS
Both Bidirectional Controls both directions Fully locked-down pods — explicit allow required

Network Policy Selectors — Who Can Talk?

📦 podSelector
Match pods by labels in the same namespace. matchLabels: { app: anihpj }
📁 namespaceSelector
Match all pods in namespaces with specific labels. matchLabels: { env: prod }
🌐 ipBlock
Match traffic from/to specific IP ranges. cidr: 10.0.0.0/8, except: [10.0.1.0/24]

20.5 Network Policy YAML Patterns

Here are the most common Network Policy patterns you'll encounter on the CKA exam, using our anihpj stack as context.

Pattern 1: Deny All Ingress (Default Deny)

deny-all-ingress.yaml 
# Deny ALL incoming traffic to ALL pods in "default" namespace
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: deny-all-ingress
  namespace: default
spec:
  podSelector: {}          # Empty = selects ALL pods in namespace
  policyTypes:
    - Ingress              # Only control incoming traffic
  # ingress: []            # Omitted = no traffic allowed in
                           # (there are no "allow" rules)

Pattern 2: Allow Specific Pod Access

allow-anihpj-to-postgres.yaml 
# Only anihpj pods can reach postgres on port 5432
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-anihpj-to-postgres
  namespace: default
spec:
  podSelector:              # Target: postgres pods
    matchLabels:
      app: postgres
  policyTypes:
    - Ingress
  ingress:
    - from:                  # Allow FROM:
        - podSelector:       # Pods with these labels
            matchLabels:
              app: anihpj
      ports:                 # On these ports only
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 5432

Pattern 3: Allow from Specific Namespace

allow-from-monitoring-ns.yaml 
# Allow monitoring namespace to scrape all pods in default namespace
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-monitoring
  namespace: default
spec:
  podSelector: {}           # All pods in default
  policyTypes:
    - Ingress
  ingress:
    - from:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              name: monitoring    # Namespace must have this label!

Pattern 4: Egress — Restrict Outgoing Traffic

anihpj-egress-policy.yaml 
# anihpj pods can only reach postgres and DNS
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: anihpj-egress
  namespace: default
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
  policyTypes:
    - Egress
  egress:
    - to:                    # Allow TO postgres
        - podSelector:
            matchLabels:
              app: postgres
      ports:
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 5432
    - to: []                 # Allow DNS (required!)
      ports:
        - protocol: UDP
          port: 53
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 53

Pattern 5: Deny All Ingress + Egress (Total Lockdown)

deny-all-traffic.yaml 
# Complete isolation — no traffic in or out
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: deny-all
  namespace: default
spec:
  podSelector: {}           # All pods
  policyTypes:
    - Ingress
    - Egress
  # No ingress or egress rules = deny everything
⚠️ CKA Gotcha: AND vs OR Logic in Network Policies

Within a single from block, multiple selectors are ANDed. Separate - from: entries are ORed.

AND vs OR — Critical Difference 
# AND — pod must match BOTH selectors
ingress:
  - from:
      - namespaceSelector:          # AND
          matchLabels: { env: prod }
        podSelector:                # AND (same list item!)
          matchLabels: { app: anihpj }

# OR — pod matches EITHER selector
ingress:
  - from:
      - namespaceSelector:          # OR
          matchLabels: { env: prod }
      - podSelector:                # OR (separate list item!)
          matchLabels: { app: anihpj }

Pattern 6: Allow from Specific IP Range (ipBlock)

allow-from-office-ips.yaml 
# Allow only office network to reach the anihpj admin panel
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-office-only
  namespace: default
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
  policyTypes:
    - Ingress
  ingress:
    - from:
        - ipBlock:
            cidr: 10.0.0.0/8        # Allow entire 10.x.x.x range
            except:
              - 10.0.99.0/24         # Except the guest WiFi subnet
        - podSelector:
            matchLabels:
              app: nginx-ingress     # Also allow Ingress controller
      ports:
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 8000

Pattern 7: Full Production Lockdown (Ingress + Egress)

anihpj-full-lockdown.yaml 
# anihpj pods: accept from Ingress only, talk only to postgres + DNS
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: anihpj-lockdown
  namespace: default
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
  policyTypes:
    - Ingress
    - Egress
  ingress:
    - from:                         # Only accept from Ingress controller
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              name: ingress-nginx
          podSelector:
            matchLabels:
              app.kubernetes.io/name: ingress-nginx
      ports:
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 8000
  egress:
    - to:                           # Can reach postgres
        - podSelector:
            matchLabels:
              app: postgres
      ports:
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 5432
    - to: []                        # Can reach DNS (required!)
      ports:
        - protocol: UDP
          port: 53
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 53

20.6 Testing Network Policies

Always verify your Network Policies work as expected. Here's a systematic testing approach using our anihpj stack.

Testing Network Policy Enforcement 
# Step 1: Apply the "allow-anihpj-to-postgres" policy
kubectl apply -f allow-anihpj-to-postgres.yaml

# Step 2: Verify policy is created
kubectl get networkpolicies
# NAME                        POD-SELECTOR     AGE
# allow-anihpj-to-postgres   app=postgres     5s

# Step 3: Describe the policy for details
kubectl describe networkpolicy allow-anihpj-to-postgres
# Allowing ingress traffic:
#   To Port: 5432/TCP
#   From:
#     PodSelector: app=anihpj

# Step 4: Test ALLOWED traffic (anihpj → postgres)
kubectl exec anihpj-pod -- nc -zv postgres-svc 5432
# postgres-svc (10.96.88.5:5432) open   ✅ ALLOWED

# Step 5: Test BLOCKED traffic (random pod → postgres)
kubectl run test-blocked --image=busybox --rm -it -- nc -zv postgres-svc 5432
# → hangs/timeout  ❌ BLOCKED!   (This pod has no "app: anihpj" label)

# Step 6: Test that non-policy pods are still open
kubectl exec anihpj-pod -- curl -s http://redis-svc:6379
# ✅ Works — redis has no NetworkPolicy, so it's still fully open
1
List Policies

kubectl get networkpolicies -A

2
Check Target Pods

kubectl describe netpol POLICY — verify podSelector matches

3
Test Allowed Path

kubectl exec ALLOWED-POD -- nc -zv TARGET PORT

4
Test Blocked Path

kubectl run test -- nc -zv TARGET PORT — should timeout

5
Verify CNI Support

If nothing is blocked — CNI may not support policies (Flannel)

🔑 Chapter 20 Quick Reference

Action Command / YAML
List Ingress resourceskubectl get ingress -A
Describe Ingress routingkubectl describe ingress NAME
Check Ingress Controllerkubectl get pods -n ingress-nginx
Create TLS Secretkubectl create secret tls NAME --cert=FILE --key=FILE
List NetworkPolicieskubectl get networkpolicies -A
Describe policy detailskubectl describe netpol NAME
Deny all ingresspodSelector: {} / policyTypes: [Ingress]
Allow specific podfrom: [podSelector: {matchLabels: {app: X}}]
Allow DNS in egressto: [] / ports: [{port: 53, protocol: UDP}]
Test connectivitykubectl exec POD -- nc -zv TARGET PORT
🧪 CKA Practice Scenario: Network Policy Challenge

Task: In the anihpj-prod namespace, create a NetworkPolicy so that only the anihpj web pods can access the postgres pods on port 5432. All other ingress traffic to postgres must be denied. Pods must still be able to resolve DNS.

Solution 
# Step 1: Create the NetworkPolicy YAML
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: postgres-allow-anihpj
  namespace: anihpj-prod
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: postgres           # Target: postgres pods
  policyTypes:
    - Ingress                 # Control incoming traffic
  ingress:
    - from:
        - podSelector:
            matchLabels:
              app: anihpj     # Only anihpj pods can connect
      ports:
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 5432          # Only on postgres port
EOF

# Step 2: Verify the policy
kubectl get netpol -n anihpj-prod
# NAME                    POD-SELECTOR    AGE
# postgres-allow-anihpj  app=postgres    5s

# Step 3: Test — anihpj CAN reach postgres 
kubectl exec -n anihpj-prod anihpj-pod -- nc -zv postgres-svc 5432
# open  ✅

# Step 4: Test — random pod CANNOT reach postgres
kubectl run -n anihpj-prod test --image=busybox --rm -it -- \
  nc -w 3 -zv postgres-svc 5432
# timeout  ❌ BLOCKED — policy works!
Part 5 Complete Network Architecture — anihpj Stack
  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                    INTERNET                                        │
  │                       │                                             │
  │              ┌────────▼────────┐                                   │
  │              │  Cloud LB /     │  TLS Termination                  │
  │              │  Ingress (443)  │  anihpj.example.com               │
  │              └────────┬────────┘                                   │
  │                  Ingress Rules:                                    │
  │                  /api  → anihpj-svc:80                            │
  │                  /admin → anihpj-svc:80                           │
  │                       │                                             │
  │   ┌───────────────────▼──────────────────────────────────────┐    │
  │   │              anihpj-svc (ClusterIP: 10.96.45.2:80)      │    │
  │   │              selector: app=anihpj                         │    │
  │   └────────┬────────────────────┬────────────────┬───────────┘    │
  │            │                    │                │                  │
  │   ┌────────▼──────┐  ┌─────────▼─────┐  ┌──────▼─────────┐      │
  │   │ anihpj-abc    │  │ anihpj-def    │  │ anihpj-ghi    │      │
  │   │ 10.244.1.5    │  │ 10.244.2.3    │  │ 10.244.3.7    │      │
  │   │ :8000 Django  │  │ :8000 Django  │  │ :8000 Django  │      │
  │   └───────┬───────┘  └───────┬───────┘  └──────┬────────┘      │
  │           │ NetworkPolicy     │                  │                  │
  │           │ allows :5432      │                  │                  │
  │   ┌───────▼──────────────────────────────────────▼──────────┐    │
  │   │        postgres-headless (clusterIP: None)              │    │
  │   │        selector: app=postgres                            │    │
  │   └────────┬────────────────────┬───────────────────────────┘    │
  │   ┌────────▼──────┐   ┌────────▼──────┐                         │
  │   │ postgres-0    │   │ postgres-1    │   StatefulSet           │
  │   │ 10.244.1.15   │   │ 10.244.2.18   │   + PVC per replica    │
  │   │ PRIMARY       │   │ REPLICA       │                         │
  │   └───────────────┘   └───────────────┘                         │
  │                                                                    │
  │   DNS: anihpj-svc.default.svc.cluster.local → 10.96.45.2        │
  │   DNS: postgres-0.postgres-headless.default... → 10.244.1.15     │
  │   CNI: Calico (VXLAN) │ kube-proxy: iptables                     │
  │   CoreDNS: 10.96.0.10 (kube-dns Service)                        │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

⚠️ CKA Gotcha: Ingress & Network Policy Traps

  • Ingress requires a controller — Creating an Ingress resource does nothing without an Ingress Controller (nginx, traefik). The exam cluster has one pre-installed — check with kubectl get pods -n ingress-nginx
  • Ingress pathType is REQUIRED — Must be Prefix, Exact, or ImplementationSpecific. Missing it = validation error. Prefix matches /app AND /app/sub
  • NetworkPolicy default behavior — By default, ALL traffic is allowed. Once you apply ANY NetworkPolicy that selects a pod, all non-matching traffic to that pod is DENIED. This catches people off guard
  • NetworkPolicy is namespace-scoped — A policy in prod doesn't affect dev. To allow cross-namespace traffic, use namespaceSelector in the ingress/egress rules
  • Egress policies block DNS too — If you create an egress policy, you must explicitly allow DNS (port 53 UDP to kube-system) or all name resolution breaks

CKA Speed Tip — NetworkPolicy deny-all then allow pattern:
Step 1: Create a deny-all ingress policy for the namespace
Step 2: Create specific allow policies for needed traffic
This is the most common exam pattern. Memorize the deny-all YAML:

⚡ Ingress & NetworkPolicy commands: kubectl create ingress myingress --rule="host.com/path=svc:port" --class=nginx
kubectl get ingress — check ADDRESS column (should have an IP)
kubectl describe ingress <name> — see rules and backend services
kubectl get networkpolicy -A — list all network policies
kubectl describe networkpolicy <name> -n <ns> — see allowed traffic rules
📝 Chapter 20 Practice Drill

Timed challenge (target: 8 minutes):

  • 1. Create a deny-all NetworkPolicy for namespace secure
  • 2. Create an allow policy: only pods with label role=frontend can reach role=backend on port 8080
  • 3. Verify: exec into a frontend pod and curl backend-svc:8080 — should work
  • 4. Exec into an unlabeled pod and curl — should be blocked
  • 5. Create an Ingress routing /api to backend-svc:8080 and / to frontend-svc:80

Key insight: NetworkPolicy + Ingress = 20% of CKA networking domain.

📝 Chapter 20 — CKA Practice Questions

Q1
Create a deny-all NetworkPolicy that blocks ALL ingress traffic to pods in namespace secure.
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: deny-all-ingress
  namespace: secure
spec:
  podSelector: {}        # ← Empty = ALL pods in namespace
  policyTypes:
  - Ingress              # ← Only block ingress (not egress)
  # No ingress rules → all ingress denied!

# Apply:
kubectl apply -f deny-all.yaml

# Verify — nothing can reach pods in "secure" ns:
kubectl exec -n default test-pod -- curl -s --max-time 3 \
  http://web-svc.secure:80
# curl: (28) Connection timed out ← Blocked!
Explanation

podSelector: {} selects ALL pods in the namespace. Listing Ingress in policyTypes without any ingress: rules means zero ingress is allowed — effectively a deny-all. This is the most common CKA NetworkPolicy question. Without this policy, all pods accept traffic from anywhere (Kubernetes is open by default). Once ANY NetworkPolicy selects a pod, that pod switches to "default deny" for the listed policyTypes.

Q2
Allow pods with label role=frontend to access pods with label role=backend on port 8080 in the same namespace. Write the NetworkPolicy.
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-frontend-to-backend
  namespace: production
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      role: backend          # ← Applies to backend pods
  policyTypes:
  - Ingress
  ingress:
  - from:
    - podSelector:
        matchLabels:
          role: frontend     # ← Allow from frontend pods
    ports:
    - protocol: TCP
      port: 8080             # ← Only on port 8080

# Test:
# From frontend pod → backend: ALLOWED
kubectl exec frontend-pod -- curl -s backend-svc:8080 ✓

# From random pod → backend: DENIED  
kubectl exec other-pod -- curl -s backend-svc:8080 ✗
Explanation

The podSelector (target) defines WHICH pods the policy applies to (backend). The ingress.from.podSelector (source) defines WHO can send traffic (frontend). The ports restrict to specific ports. On the CKA, pay attention to: (1) which pods the policy selects (target), (2) which pods are allowed (source), (3) the port. A common mistake is confusing target vs source selectors.

Q3
Create an Ingress resource that routes /api to api-svc on port 8080 and / to web-svc on port 80. Use pathType Prefix.
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: app-ingress
  namespace: default
  annotations:
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/rewrite-target: /
spec:
  ingressClassName: nginx      # ← Required since K8s 1.18+
  rules:
  - http:
      paths:
      - path: /api
        pathType: Prefix       # ← Matches /api, /api/v1, /api/users
        backend:
          service:
            name: api-svc
            port:
              number: 8080
      - path: /
        pathType: Prefix       # ← Matches everything else
        backend:
          service:
            name: web-svc
            port:
              number: 80

# Apply and verify:
kubectl apply -f ingress.yaml
kubectl get ingress app-ingress
# NAME          CLASS   HOSTS  ADDRESS        PORTS  AGE
# app-ingress   nginx   *      192.168.1.50   80     10s
Explanation

Ingress provides HTTP/HTTPS routing to Services. Key fields: ingressClassName selects which controller handles it, pathType defines matching (Prefix = prefix match, Exact = exact match, ImplementationSpecific = controller decides). Order matters — more specific paths should come first (/api before /). On the CKA, always include ingressClassName — it's required. An Ingress without a deployed Ingress Controller does nothing.

Q4
How do you verify that an Ingress Controller is installed and running? What happens if you create an Ingress without a controller?
Show Answer
Answer
# Check for Ingress Controller pods:
kubectl get pods -n ingress-nginx
# NAME                                       READY  STATUS   AGE
# ingress-nginx-controller-6b94c75599-abc    1/1    Running  5d

# Check IngressClass:
kubectl get ingressclass
# NAME    CONTROLLER                           PARAMETERS  AGE
# nginx   k8s.io/ingress-nginx                 <none>      5d

# Check if Ingress has an ADDRESS:
kubectl get ingress
# NAME      CLASS   HOSTS  ADDRESS         PORTS  AGE
# my-ing    nginx   *      192.168.1.50    80     1h  ← Controller assigned
# bad-ing   nginx   *      <none>          80     1h  ← No controller!

# Without a controller:
# - Ingress resource is created (kubectl get ingress works)
# - ADDRESS remains <none> forever
# - No traffic routing occurs
# - No errors — it just silently does nothing!
Explanation

An Ingress resource is just a config object — it needs an Ingress Controller (like NGINX) to actually route traffic. On the CKA, the controller is usually pre-installed. Check with kubectl get pods -n ingress-nginx and kubectl get ingressclass. If the Ingress shows no ADDRESS, the controller isn't processing it (wrong ingressClassName or no controller). Common CKA trap: creating a valid Ingress but routing doesn't work because the Service name or port is wrong.

Q5
Write a NetworkPolicy that allows pods in namespace web to access pods in namespace db on port 5432. Use namespaceSelector.
Show Answer
Answer
# First, label the source namespace:
kubectl label namespace web purpose=web-tier

# NetworkPolicy in the db namespace:
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-web-to-db
  namespace: db              # ← Applied in TARGET namespace
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: postgres          # ← Protect postgres pods
  policyTypes:
  - Ingress
  ingress:
  - from:
    - namespaceSelector:
        matchLabels:
          purpose: web-tier  # ← Allow from "web" namespace
    ports:
    - protocol: TCP
      port: 5432

# Important: namespaceSelector uses NAMESPACE labels
# You must label the namespace first!
kubectl get namespaces --show-labels | grep web
Explanation

Cross-namespace NetworkPolicies use namespaceSelector which matches on namespace labels (not names). You must label the source namespace first (kubectl label ns web purpose=web-tier). The policy goes in the TARGET namespace (where the pods being protected live). On the CKA, forgetting to label the namespace is a very common mistake. A namespaceSelector without a podSelector allows ALL pods from that namespace. Combine both to restrict to specific pods in specific namespaces.

Q6
What is the difference between pathType: Prefix, pathType: Exact, and pathType: ImplementationSpecific in an Ingress?
Show Answer
Answer
pathType Matches Example
Prefix URL prefix (path segment level) /api → matches /api, /api/, /api/v1
Exact Exact URL match (case-sensitive) /api → only /api (NOT /api/)
ImplementationSpecific Depends on IngressClass Controller-specific behavior 
# Prefix matching rules:
# path: /api
# /api     → MATCH ✓
# /api/    → MATCH ✓
# /api/v1  → MATCH ✓
# /apis    → NO MATCH ✗ (not a segment boundary)

# Exam tip: pathType is REQUIRED since K8s 1.22
# Omitting it causes validation error
Explanation

Prefix matching works on path segments — /api matches /api/v1 but NOT /apis. Exact matches the exact path only. ImplementationSpecific defers to the controller. On the CKA, almost always use Prefix. Know that pathType is mandatory (since v1.22) — omitting it fails validation. A trailing slash matters for Exact but not for Prefix.

Q7
Write a NetworkPolicy that allows egress ONLY to DNS (port 53) and blocks all other outgoing traffic.
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-dns-only
  namespace: restricted
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: locked-down
  policyTypes:
  - Egress                  # ← Control outgoing traffic
  egress:
  - ports:
    - protocol: UDP
      port: 53              # ← DNS UDP
    - protocol: TCP
      port: 53              # ← DNS TCP (for large responses)
    # No "to:" selector = allow DNS to ANY destination
    # (CoreDNS, external DNS servers, etc.)

# Result:
# ✓ DNS queries work (nslookup succeeds)
# ✗ curl google.com → blocked (port 80/443 not allowed)
# ✗ Any non-DNS traffic → blocked

# Common mistake: forgetting TCP/53
# Some DNS queries use TCP (large responses, zone transfers)
Explanation

Egress NetworkPolicies control outgoing traffic. Blocking all egress except DNS (port 53) is a common CKA scenario. Without the DNS exception, pods can't resolve ANY names (even internal Service names). Always include both UDP/53 and TCP/53. On the CKA, if a question says "block all egress but allow the pod to resolve DNS," this is the answer. Omitting the to: field means DNS is allowed to any destination (needed because CoreDNS pod IPs change).

Q8
An Ingress is created but traffic returns 404. The Service and pods are running. How do you troubleshoot?
Show Answer
Answer
# Step 1: Check Ingress resource
kubectl describe ingress my-ingress
# Look for:
#   Default backend: <default>
#   Rules:
#     Host  Path  Backend
#     *     /app  app-svc:8080 (10.244.1.5:8080, 10.244.2.8:8080)
#                              ↑ Endpoints listed = good
#                 app-svc:8080 ()  ← Empty = broken!

# Step 2: Verify Service has endpoints
kubectl get endpoints app-svc
# NAME      ENDPOINTS
# app-svc   10.244.1.5:8080,10.244.2.8:8080  ← Good
# app-svc   <none>  ← Labels don't match!

# Step 3: Check Service selector matches pod labels
kubectl get svc app-svc -o yaml | grep -A2 selector
kubectl get pods --show-labels | grep app

# Step 4: Check Service port matches Ingress backend port
kubectl get svc app-svc
# PORT(S): 80/TCP  ← Ingress says 8080, Service is 80!

# Step 5: Check Ingress Controller logs
kubectl logs -n ingress-nginx -l app.kubernetes.io/name=ingress-nginx

# Common causes:
# 1. Service port mismatch (Ingress port ≠ Service port)
# 2. Service selector doesn't match pod labels
# 3. Wrong namespace
# 4. Wrong pathType causing no match
Explanation

The 404 on Ingress troubleshooting flow: (1) kubectl describe ingress to check rules and endpoints, (2) verify Service has endpoints, (3) match Service selector to pod labels, (4) verify port numbers match across Ingress → Service → Pod. On the CKA, the most common issues are: port mismatch (Ingress backend port must match Service port, not container port), wrong Service name, or labels not matching. Always use describe first — it shows the endpoint IPs right in the Ingress output.

Q9
How do you combine podSelector AND namespaceSelector in the same NetworkPolicy rule? What's the difference between putting them in the same item vs separate items?
Show Answer
Answer
# SAME item = AND (both must match):
ingress:
- from:
  - namespaceSelector:        # ← Single rule
      matchLabels:
        env: production
    podSelector:              # ← AND condition
      matchLabels:
        role: frontend
  # Allows: frontend pods IN production namespace

# SEPARATE items = OR (either matches):
ingress:
- from:
  - namespaceSelector:        # ← Rule 1
      matchLabels:
        env: production
  - podSelector:              # ← Rule 2 (separate item)
      matchLabels:
        role: frontend
  # Allows: ANY pod from production namespace
  #     OR: frontend pods from ANY namespace
  # ⚠️ Much more permissive!

# The difference is the YAML array dash "-":
# No dash before podSelector = AND (combined)
# Dash before podSelector = OR (separate rule)

# CKA TRAP: One small dash changes everything!
Explanation

This is the #1 CKA NetworkPolicy trap. A single from: item with both selectors means AND (namespace AND pod labels). Two separate from: items means OR (either namespace OR pod labels). The difference is a single YAML dash (-). The OR version is MUCH more permissive — it allows any pod from the namespace, plus any matching pod from any namespace. On the CKA, read the YAML carefully: are the selectors in the same array item (AND) or different items (OR)?

Q10
How do you add TLS termination to an Ingress? Show the complete steps from creating the TLS secret to the Ingress spec.
Show Answer
Answer
# Step 1: Create TLS secret from cert files:
kubectl create secret tls my-tls-secret \
  --cert=tls.crt \
  --key=tls.key \
  -n default

# Step 2: Create Ingress with TLS:
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: secure-ingress
spec:
  ingressClassName: nginx
  tls:
  - hosts:
    - myapp.example.com      # ← Must match rules host
    secretName: my-tls-secret # ← TLS secret name
  rules:
  - host: myapp.example.com  # ← Must match tls host
    http:
      paths:
      - path: /
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service:
            name: web-svc
            port:
              number: 80

# Verify:
kubectl describe ingress secure-ingress
# TLS:
#   my-tls-secret terminates myapp.example.com

# Test:
curl -k https://myapp.example.com
# -k to skip cert verification in testing
Explanation

TLS in Ingress has 3 components: (1) a TLS Secret of type kubernetes.io/tls with tls.crt and tls.key, (2) the spec.tls section listing hosts and the secret name, (3) the rules.host must match the TLS host. On the CKA, use kubectl create secret tls (not generic!) — it validates the cert format. The Ingress Controller handles TLS termination: HTTPS outside, HTTP between controller and backend Service. Common mistake: host in tls doesn't match host in rules.

🟡 Part 6: Storage Chapters 21-23

Chapter 21: Volumes and Persistent Storage

Containers are ephemeral by design — when a container dies, everything inside it vanishes. For our anihpj/jobpost Django app, that means uploaded resumes, profile photos, and the entire SQLite database would be wiped on every restart. Volumes solve this problem by providing storage that outlives containers. In this chapter you'll understand why volumes exist, explore the different volume types Kubernetes offers, and master emptyDir and hostPath — the two simplest volume types that form the foundation for everything in Chapters 22 and 23.

🟡 Intermediate ⏱️ ~1 hour 💾 Volumes 📝 CKA EXAM

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ Why container storage is ephemeral and the real-world problems this causes
  • ✅ The 7 most important volume types in Kubernetes and when to use each
  • ✅ How emptyDir enables sidecar container patterns with shared temp storage
  • ✅ How hostPath maps node directories into pods — and why it's dangerous in production
  • ✅ Volume lifecycle — what survives container restarts vs pod deletions
🎓 CKA Relevance
Storage 10% Workloads & Scheduling 15%
Storage Is 10% of the CKA Exam

CKA storage questions typically ask you to create PVs, PVCs, and mount them into pods. But understanding basic volume types is the prerequisite. Key skills for this chapter:

  • Know the difference between emptyDir (pod-scoped) and hostPath (node-scoped)
  • Understand volume lifecycle: emptyDir survives container restarts but NOT pod deletions
  • Be able to write a pod spec with volumes: and volumeMounts: from scratch
  • Know why hostPath is NOT recommended in production (node affinity, security)

Volume Imperative Commands

ActionCommand
Create pod with emptyDir (dry-run)kubectl run mypod --image=nginx --dry-run=client -o yaml > pod.yaml then add volumes manually
Inspect pod volumeskubectl describe pod <name> | grep -A10 Volumes
Check files in mounted volumekubectl exec <pod> -- ls -la /path/to/mount
Check disk usagekubectl exec <pod> -- df -h /path/to/mount
View mount events/errorskubectl describe pod <name> | grep -A5 Events
Watch pod statuskubectl get pod <name> -w

Pod with Volume — Exam Skeleton

Memorize this structure — you'll write it from scratch on the CKA exam.

apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: my-pod
spec:
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx
    volumeMounts:                    # ◀── WHERE to mount
    - name: my-vol
      mountPath: /data               # ◀── path inside container
      readOnly: false                 # ◀── optional
  volumes:                           # ◀── WHAT to mount
  - name: my-vol                     # ◀── must match volumeMounts.name
    emptyDir: {}                     # ◀── volume type
    # OR: hostPath: { path: /host/path, type: Directory }
    # OR: persistentVolumeClaim: { claimName: my-pvc }
    # OR: configMap: { name: my-cm }
    # OR: secret: { secretName: my-secret }

Volume Gotchas

  • ❌ emptyDir persists across pod restarts — it persists across container restarts, NOT pod deletions
  • ✅ emptyDir survives container crashes but dies with the pod
  • ❌ hostPath works the same on any node — it reads that specific node's filesystem. Different node = different data
  • ✅ hostPath is node-specific — pod rescheduling changes the data
  • volumes[] and volumeMounts[] names must match exactly — typo = mount failure
  • emptyDir: {medium: Memory} counts against the container's memory limit, not disk
  • Forgetting readOnly: true on hostPath mounts is a security risk

Find Volume Fields Fast

kubectl explain pod.spec.volumes              # all volume types
kubectl explain pod.spec.volumes.emptyDir      # emptyDir options
kubectl explain pod.spec.volumes.hostPath      # hostPath options
kubectl explain pod.spec.containers.volumeMounts  # mount options
  • pod.spec.volumes[].emptyDir.medium — "" (disk) or "Memory" (tmpfs)
  • pod.spec.volumes[].emptyDir.sizeLimit — max size before eviction
  • pod.spec.volumes[].hostPath.path — required, path on node
  • pod.spec.volumes[].hostPath.type — validation type (Directory, File, etc.)
  • pod.spec.containers[].volumeMounts[].subPath — mount single file without hiding dir
7 Key Volume Types
2 CKA Must-Know (emptyDir, hostPath)
Pod emptyDir Lifecycle
⚠️ hostPath — Security Risk

🗺️ Volume Types — When to Use What

Pod Storage Need
📂 emptyDir Temp scratch space, sidecar sharing
🖥️ hostPath Node logs, etcd certs, dev/testing
💾 PVC Databases, uploads, persistent data
⚙️ configMap Config files mounted into containers
🔐 secret TLS certs, passwords as files

21.1 The Problem: Container Storage is Ephemeral

Every container starts with a fresh, writable filesystem layer provided by its image. When the container stops or crashes, that layer is destroyed. Any files created, modified, or downloaded during runtime are gone forever. This is by design — containers are disposable — but it creates a massive problem for stateful applications.

The Data Loss Problem — Without Volumes
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  WITHOUT VOLUMES — DATA IS LOST ON EVERY POD RESTART                    │
  │                                                                          │
  │  Timeline:  t=0              t=5min             t=5min+1sec              │
  │             Pod starts       Pod crashes         New pod starts          │
  │                                                                          │
  │  ┌────────────────┐    ┌────────────────┐    ┌────────────────┐         │
  │  │ 📦 anihpj Pod  │    │ 📦 anihpj Pod  │    │ 📦 anihpj Pod  │         │
  │  │                │    │                │    │   (new)         │         │
  │  │ 📄 db.sqlite3  │    │ 📄 db.sqlite3  │    │                │         │
  │  │ 📄 resume.pdf  │    │ 📄 resume.pdf  │    │ 📄 (empty!)    │  💀     │
  │  │ 📄 photo.jpg   │    │ 📄 photo.jpg   │    │                │         │
