Note that WaitForFirstConsumer is NOT a bug — the PVC intentionally stays Pending until a pod is created, so the PV can be provisioned on the same node as the pod. Always start with kubectl describe pvc.

Q4
What happens to a PV when its PVC is deleted? Explain all reclaim policies.
Show Answer
Answer
Policy PV Status After PVC Delete Data Next Steps
Retain Released Preserved Manual cleanup, edit PV to remove claimRef
Delete PV deleted Deleted Nothing — PV and data are gone
Recycle Available Scrubbed (rm -rf) Deprecated — don't use 
# To reuse a Retained PV after PVC deletion:
# 1. PV shows "Released" — can't bind a new PVC yet
kubectl get pv pv-data
# STATUS: Released

# 2. Remove the old claimRef:
kubectl patch pv pv-data -p '{"spec":{"claimRef":null}}'
# OR:
kubectl edit pv pv-data
# Delete the entire claimRef section

# 3. PV returns to Available:
kubectl get pv pv-data
# STATUS: Available ← Ready for new PVC

# Change reclaim policy on existing PV:
kubectl patch pv pv-data -p \
  '{"spec":{"persistentVolumeReclaimPolicy":"Retain"}}'
Explanation

Reclaim policy controls PV fate after PVC deletion. Retain is safest — data preserved, PV goes to Released state (NOT Available). To reuse a Released PV, you must remove the claimRef. Delete removes both PV and backing storage (default for dynamic provisioning — dangerous!). On the CKA, changing policy from Delete to Retain with kubectl patch is a common task. Know the Released → Available transition requires manual intervention.

Q5
Create a StorageClass with volumeBindingMode: WaitForFirstConsumer. Why is this important?
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: storage.k8s.io/v1
kind: StorageClass
metadata:
  name: local-storage
provisioner: kubernetes.io/no-provisioner  # ← Manual provisioning
volumeBindingMode: WaitForFirstConsumer    # ← KEY!
reclaimPolicy: Retain

# What it does:
# Immediate (default):
#   PVC → PV binding happens immediately
#   PV might be on node-1
#   Pod gets scheduled to node-2
#   → Pod stuck Pending! PV is on wrong node!

# WaitForFirstConsumer:
#   PVC → Stays Pending (normal!)
#   Pod created → scheduler picks node-2
#   PV binding happens on node-2
#   → Pod starts successfully!

# Verify:
kubectl get sc local-storage
# NAME            PROVISIONER                    RECLAIMPOLICY  VOLUMEBINDINGMODE     
# local-storage   kubernetes.io/no-provisioner   Retain         WaitForFirstConsumer

# PVC with this StorageClass:
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: local-pvc
spec:
  storageClassName: local-storage
  accessModes: ["ReadWriteOnce"]
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 5Gi
# This PVC stays Pending until a pod uses it!
Explanation

WaitForFirstConsumer delays PV binding until a pod using the PVC is scheduled. This is critical for local storage (hostPath, local PVs) to ensure the PV is on the same node as the pod. Without it, the PV could bind to a node where no pod runs. On the CKA, if you see a PVC stuck in Pending with WaitForFirstConsumer and NO pod using it — that's expected behavior, not an error. Create the pod and it will bind. kubernetes.io/no-provisioner means admins must manually create PVs.

Q6
What is the difference between storageClassName: "" and omitting storageClassName in a PVC?
Show Answer
Answer
# Scenario: Cluster has a default StorageClass "standard"

# Option 1: Omit storageClassName
spec:
  accessModes: ["ReadWriteOnce"]
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 5Gi
  # storageClassName: ← not specified
# Result: Uses DEFAULT StorageClass ("standard")
# → Dynamic provisioning creates PV automatically

# Option 2: storageClassName: ""
spec:
  accessModes: ["ReadWriteOnce"]
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 5Gi
  storageClassName: ""     # ← Explicitly empty
# Result: NO StorageClass used
# → Only binds to PVs that also have NO class
# → No dynamic provisioning

# Option 3: storageClassName: "fast"
spec:
  storageClassName: "fast"
# Result: Only binds to PVs with class "fast"
# → Or dynamic provisioning via "fast" StorageClass

# Check default StorageClass:
kubectl get sc
# NAME                  PROVISIONER          AGE
# standard (default)    rancher.io/local     5d
#          ↑ "(default)" annotation
Explanation

This is one of the most subtle CKA traps. Omitting storageClassName uses the cluster's default StorageClass (dynamic provisioning). Setting storageClassName: "" explicitly means "no class" — only matches PVs with no class. On the CKA, if you want static binding to a specific PV without dynamic provisioning, use storageClassName: "". To check the default: kubectl get sc — look for (default) annotation.

Q7
Expand a PVC from 5Gi to 10Gi. What conditions must be met?
Show Answer
Answer
# Requirements for PVC expansion:
# 1. StorageClass must have allowVolumeExpansion: true
kubectl get sc standard -o yaml | grep allowVolumeExpansion
# allowVolumeExpansion: true  ← Required!

# 2. Simply edit the PVC size:
kubectl edit pvc my-pvc
# Change: storage: 5Gi → storage: 10Gi

# Or use patch:
kubectl patch pvc my-pvc -p \
  '{"spec":{"resources":{"requests":{"storage":"10Gi"}}}}'

# 3. Check status:
kubectl get pvc my-pvc
# CAPACITY: 10Gi ← Expanded!

# For some volume types, expansion requires pod restart:
kubectl describe pvc my-pvc
# Conditions:
#   Type: FileSystemResizePending
#   Message: Waiting for user to restart the pod

# Restart the pod:
kubectl delete pod my-pod
# Deployment recreates pod → filesystem expands

# ⚠️ Cannot SHRINK a PVC! Only expansion is supported.
Explanation

PVC expansion requires allowVolumeExpansion: true on the StorageClass. Just edit the PVC spec to request more storage. Some provisioners expand online (no pod restart needed), others need pod restart (check for FileSystemResizePending condition). On the CKA, this is a straightforward task but watch for: (1) StorageClass doesn't allow expansion (verify first), (2) can never shrink — only grow, (3) may need to restart the pod for filesystem-level resize.

Q8
How do you sort PersistentVolumes by capacity and find all PVCs across all namespaces?
Show Answer
Answer
# Sort PVs by capacity:
kubectl get pv --sort-by=.spec.capacity.storage
# NAME     CAPACITY  ACCESS  RECLAIM  STATUS     CLASS     AGE
# pv-sm    1Gi       RWO     Retain   Available  local     5m
# pv-md    5Gi       RWO     Delete   Bound      standard  10m
# pv-lg    20Gi      RWX     Retain   Available  fast      2m

# All PVCs in all namespaces:
kubectl get pvc -A
# NAMESPACE  NAME       STATUS  VOLUME  CAPACITY  ACCESS  AGE
# default    data-pvc   Bound   pv-md   5Gi       RWO     10m
# prod       db-pvc     Bound   pv-lg   20Gi      RWX     5m
# dev        cache-pvc  Pending -       -         -       2m

# Custom columns for overview:
kubectl get pv -o custom-columns=\
NAME:.metadata.name,\
SIZE:.spec.capacity.storage,\
STATUS:.status.phase,\
CLAIM:.spec.claimRef.name,\
CLASS:.spec.storageClassName

# Find which PV is bound to which PVC:
kubectl get pv -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.metadata.name}{"\t"}{.spec.claimRef.namespace}/{.spec.claimRef.name}{"\n"}{end}'
Explanation

PVs are cluster-scoped (no -n flag needed), PVCs are namespaced (-A to see all). --sort-by uses JSONPath for sorting. On the CKA, you may be asked to find a specific PV/PVC relationship — use the claimRef field on a PV to see which PVC it's bound to. Custom columns are faster than -o wide for specific information. Remember: kubectl get pv (no namespace), kubectl get pvc -n <ns> (needs namespace).

Q9
What is the difference between ReadWriteOnce, ReadOnlyMany, ReadWriteMany, and ReadWriteOncePod?
Show Answer
Answer
Mode Short Description Use Case
ReadWriteOnce RWO Read-write by ONE node Single database pod
ReadOnlyMany ROX Read-only by many nodes Shared config/static assets
ReadWriteMany RWX Read-write by many nodes Shared uploads (NFS,EFS)
ReadWriteOncePod RWOP Read-write by ONE pod Exclusive pod access (1.22+) 
# CKA gotcha:
# RWO = "one NODE" not "one pod"!
# Multiple pods on the SAME node can share an RWO volume
# RWOP = "one POD" — exclusive access guarantee

# hostPath supports: RWO only
# NFS supports: RWO, ROX, RWX
# AWS EBS supports: RWO only
# AWS EFS supports: RWX
Explanation

The biggest trap: ReadWriteOnce means one NODE, not one pod. Multiple pods on the same node CAN mount the same RWO volume simultaneously. For true single-pod exclusivity, use ReadWriteOncePod (K8s 1.22+). On the CKA, access mode mismatches are a top reason for PVC binding failure. If a PVC requests RWX but only RWO PVs exist, it stays Pending. Not all storage backends support all modes — hostPath only supports RWO.

Q10
A PV shows status Released after its PVC was deleted. How do you make it Available again for a new PVC?
Show Answer
Answer
# Check current state:
kubectl get pv pv-data
# NAME     CAPACITY  ACCESS  RECLAIM  STATUS    CLAIM               AGE
# pv-data  10Gi      RWO     Retain   Released  default/old-pvc     1h

# The PV has a claimRef to the old PVC — must remove it:
kubectl edit pv pv-data
# Delete the entire claimRef section:
# spec:
#   claimRef:          ← DELETE these 4 lines
#     apiVersion: v1
#     kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
#     name: old-pvc
#     namespace: default
#     uid: abc-123

# Or use patch:
kubectl patch pv pv-data --type json \
  -p '[{"op": "remove", "path": "/spec/claimRef"}]'

# Verify:
kubectl get pv pv-data
# STATUS: Available ← Ready for new PVC!

# ⚠️ The data from the old PVC is still on disk!
# You may want to clean it before reuse:
# SSH to node → rm -rf /mnt/data/*
Explanation

When a PVC is deleted and the PV has Retain policy, the PV goes to Released (not Available). The claimRef still references the old PVC, preventing new bindings. Remove it to return to Available. On the CKA, this is a multi-step task: (1) identify the Released PV, (2) remove claimRef, (3) verify Available status. Note that old data remains — the admin must decide whether to clean it. This is why Retain requires manual intervention by design.

Q11
Write a StatefulSet with volumeClaimTemplates for a PostgreSQL database. How do volumeClaimTemplates differ from regular PVCs? What happens to the volumes when pods are deleted?
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: StatefulSet
metadata:
  name: postgres
spec:
  serviceName: postgres-headless
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: postgres
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: postgres
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: postgres
        image: postgres:15
        ports:
        - containerPort: 5432
        volumeMounts:
        - name: pgdata
          mountPath: /var/lib/postgresql/data
  volumeClaimTemplates:         # ← Not under template!
  - metadata:
      name: pgdata               # Matches volumeMount name
    spec:
      accessModes: ["ReadWriteOnce"]
      storageClassName: standard
      resources:
        requests:
          storage: 10Gi

# What gets created:
# postgres-0 → PVC: pgdata-postgres-0 → PV (auto-provisioned)
# postgres-1 → PVC: pgdata-postgres-1 → PV (auto-provisioned)
# postgres-2 → PVC: pgdata-postgres-2 → PV (auto-provisioned)

# Key differences from regular PVCs:
# 1. Each pod gets its OWN unique PVC (not shared)
# 2. PVC name = <template-name>-<statefulset-name>-<ordinal>
# 3. PVCs are NOT deleted when pods or StatefulSet are deleted
# 4. When pod restarts, it reattaches to the SAME PVC

# Verify:
kubectl get pvc
# pgdata-postgres-0   Bound   pv-abc   10Gi   RWO    standard
# pgdata-postgres-1   Bound   pv-def   10Gi   RWO    standard
# pgdata-postgres-2   Bound   pv-ghi   10Gi   RWO    standard

# ⚠️ Deleting StatefulSet does NOT delete PVCs:
kubectl delete sts postgres
kubectl get pvc
# All PVCs still exist! Data is preserved.
# Manual cleanup: kubectl delete pvc pgdata-postgres-{0,1,2}
Explanation

volumeClaimTemplates are unique to StatefulSets and create a dedicated PVC per pod replica. Unlike Deployments where all pods share the same PVC (or have no persistent storage), StatefulSets guarantee that pod postgres-0 always gets pgdata-postgres-0. PVCs survive pod restarts, rescheduling, and even StatefulSet deletion — this is by design to prevent accidental data loss. On the CKA, remember: volumeClaimTemplates is a top-level field of StatefulSet spec (same level as template), NOT nested inside the pod template. The naming convention <vct-name>-<sts-name>-<ordinal> is deterministic.

Chapter 23: ConfigMaps and Secrets

Hard-coding configuration into container images is an anti-pattern. Every time you change a setting, you'd need to rebuild and redeploy the image. ConfigMaps and Secrets solve this by externalizing configuration — injecting settings into pods at runtime via environment variables or mounted files. For our anihpj/jobpost Django app, this means database URLs, Django settings, and credentials all live outside the image, making it portable across dev, staging, and production environments.

🟡 Intermediate ⏱️ ~1 hour 🔐 Config 📝 CKA EXAM

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ How to create ConfigMaps from literals, files, and env-files
  • ✅ How to inject ConfigMap data as environment variables and mounted files
  • ✅ How Secrets differ from ConfigMaps — base64 encoding, types, and security caveats
  • ✅ Best practices for managing configuration in production clusters
  • ✅ Immutable ConfigMaps and Secrets for performance and safety
🎓 CKA Relevance
Storage 10% Workloads & Scheduling 15%
ConfigMaps & Secrets Appear in Multiple CKA Domains

These topics cross storage and workload domains. You'll be asked to create them imperatively and mount them into pods. Master these commands:

  • kubectl create configmap myconfig --from-literal=key=value
  • kubectl create secret generic mysecret --from-literal=password=abc123
  • kubectl get configmap myconfig -o yaml — inspect contents
  • kubectl create configmap myconfig --from-file=config.properties
  • Know both injection methods: envFrom (env vars) and volumeMounts (files)

ConfigMap & Secret Imperative Commands

ActionCommand
CM from literalkubectl create configmap myconfig --from-literal=key=value
CM from filekubectl create configmap myconfig --from-file=app.conf
CM from env-filekubectl create configmap myconfig --from-env-file=.env
Secret generic literalkubectl create secret generic mysecret --from-literal=pass=abc123
Secret from filekubectl create secret generic mysecret --from-file=cert.pem
Secret TLSkubectl create secret tls tls-secret --cert=cert.crt --key=key.pem
Inspect CMkubectl get cm myconfig -o yaml
Decode Secretkubectl get secret mysecret -o jsonpath='{.data.pass}' | base64 -d

ConfigMap + Secret + Pod Injection — Exam Skeleton

You MUST know both injection methods. Env-vars for single values, volume mounts for whole files.

# ConfigMap
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: app-config
data:
  APP_ENV: production              # ◀── key-value pair
  config.properties: |             # ◀── multi-line file content
    server.port=8080
    log.level=INFO
---
# Secret
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: app-secret
type: Opaque
data:
  DB_PASS: cGFzc3dvcmQxMjM=       # ◀── base64 encoded
stringData:
  API_KEY: my-plain-text-key       # ◀── auto-encoded at creation
---
# Pod — env var injection
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: app-pod
spec:
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx
    envFrom:                       # ◀── inject ALL keys as env vars
    - configMapRef:
        name: app-config
    env:
    - name: DB_PASSWORD            # ◀── inject SINGLE key
      valueFrom:
        secretKeyRef:
          name: app-secret
          key: DB_PASS
    volumeMounts:                  # ◀── inject as FILES
    - name: config-vol
      mountPath: /etc/config
      readOnly: true
    - name: secret-vol
      mountPath: /etc/secrets
      readOnly: true
  volumes:
  - name: config-vol
    configMap:
      name: app-config
  - name: secret-vol
    secret:
      secretName: app-secret

ConfigMap & Secret Exam Traps

  • ❌ Secrets are encrypted by default in etcd
  • ✅ Secrets are only base64 encoded — enable EncryptionConfiguration for real encryption
  • ❌ ConfigMap changes auto-update environment variables
  • ✅ Env vars are set at Pod start. Only volume-mounted CMs auto-update (with delay)
  • The ConfigMap/Secret must exist BEFORE the Pod — otherwise the Pod stays Pending (unless marked optional: true)
  • data: values are base64 in Secrets, but stringData: accepts plain text (auto-encoded)
  • Secret type Opaque is the default — kubernetes.io/tls requires both tls.crt and tls.key
  • Max ConfigMap/Secret size: 1 MiB

Find ConfigMap & Secret YAML Fields Fast

kubectl explain configmap.data                 # key-value data
kubectl explain secret.data                    # base64-encoded data
kubectl explain secret.stringData              # plain text (auto-encoded)
kubectl explain secret.type                    # Opaque, tls, etc.
kubectl explain pod.spec.containers.envFrom    # bulk injection
kubectl explain pod.spec.containers.env.valueFrom  # single key injection
kubectl explain pod.spec.volumes.configMap     # CM volume mount
kubectl explain pod.spec.volumes.secret        # Secret volume mount

✅ DO

  • Use stringData for plain-text Secret creation
  • Mount Secrets as readOnly volumes
  • Use envFrom to inject all CM keys at once
  • Use --from-literal for quick exam answers
  • Check Secret data with base64 -d

❌ DON'T

  • Store sensitive data in ConfigMaps
  • Assume Secrets are encrypted at rest
  • Forget readOnly: true on Secret mounts
  • Expect env var updates without Pod restart
  • Exceed the 1 MiB size limit
2 Config Types (CM + Secret)
2 Injection Methods (env / vol)
1 MiB Max Size Per Object
base64 Secret Encoding (NOT encryption!)

23.1 ConfigMaps — Non-Sensitive Configuration

A ConfigMap stores key-value pairs of non-sensitive configuration data. It decouples application configuration from the container image, so the same image can run with different settings in different environments.

ConfigMap — Same Image, Different Config Per Environment
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  ONE IMAGE — THREE ENVIRONMENTS — THREE CONFIGMAPS                       │
  │                                                                          │
  │  ┌─────────────────┐                                                    │
  │  │  anihpj:latest   │  ◀── same image everywhere                        │
  │  └────────┬────────┘                                                    │
  │           │                                                              │
  │     ┌─────┼──────────────────┐                                          │
  │     │     │                  │                                          │
  │     ▼     ▼                  ▼                                          │
  │  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐                      │
  │  │ DEV      │  │ STAGING      │  │ PRODUCTION   │                      │
  │  │          │  │              │  │              │                      │
  │  │ ConfigMap│  │ ConfigMap    │  │ ConfigMap    │                      │
  │  │ DEBUG=   │  │ DEBUG=       │  │ DEBUG=       │                      │
  │  │  True    │  │  True        │  │  False       │                      │
  │  │ DB_HOST= │  │ DB_HOST=     │  │ DB_HOST=     │                      │
  │  │  localhost│  │  staging-db  │  │  prod-db     │                      │
  │  │ REPLICAS=│  │ REPLICAS=    │  │ REPLICAS=    │                      │
  │  │  1       │  │  2           │  │  5           │                      │
  │  │ LOG=     │  │ LOG=         │  │ LOG=         │                      │
  │  │  verbose │  │  info        │  │  warning     │                      │
  │  └──────────┘  └──────────────┘  └──────────────┘                      │
  │                                                                          │
  │  No image rebuild needed! Just change the ConfigMap and restart pods.   │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
📝
Create from Literals

kubectl create configmap anihpj-config --from-literal=DEBUG=False --from-literal=DB_HOST=postgres — quick key-value pairs, great for simple settings.

📄
Create from File

kubectl create configmap nginx-conf --from-file=nginx.conf — entire file becomes a key (filename) with file contents as value. Perfect for config files.

📋
Create from Env File

kubectl create configmap app-env --from-env-file=.env — reads a .env file and creates one key per line. Great for migrating from Docker Compose.

📜
Create from YAML

kubectl apply -f configmap.yaml — full control over keys and values using declarative YAML. Best for version-controlled configs.

anihpj-configmap.yaml 
# ConfigMap for anihpj Django settings
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: anihpj-config
  namespace: anihpj-production
data:
  # Simple key-value pairs → injected as env vars
  DEBUG: "False"
  ALLOWED_HOSTS: "*.anihpj.com,localhost"
  DB_HOST: "postgres.anihpj-production.svc.cluster.local"
  DB_PORT: "5432"
  DB_NAME: "anihpj_db"
  STATIC_URL: "/static/"
  MEDIA_URL: "/media/"

  # Multi-line value → best mounted as a file
  settings.ini: |
    [database]
    engine = django.db.backends.postgresql
    host = postgres
    port = 5432
    name = anihpj_db

    [cache]
    backend = django.core.cache.backends.redis.RedisCache
    location = redis://redis:6379/0

    [email]
    backend = django.core.mail.backends.smtp.EmailBackend
    host = smtp.anihpj.com
    port = 587
Two Ways to Inject ConfigMap Data into Pods
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  METHOD 1: Environment Variables        METHOD 2: Mounted Files          │
  │  (envFrom / env)                        (volumeMounts)                   │
  │                                                                          │
  │  ConfigMap: anihpj-config               ConfigMap: anihpj-config         │
  │  ┌─────────────────────┐                ┌─────────────────────┐          │
  │  │ DEBUG=False          │                │ settings.ini: |     │          │
  │  │ DB_HOST=postgres     │                │   [database]        │          │
  │  │ DB_PORT=5432         │                │   engine=postgresql │          │
  │  └─────────┬───────────┘                └─────────┬───────────┘          │
  │            │                                      │                      │
  │            ▼                                      ▼                      │
  │  Pod sees env vars:                     Pod sees files:                  │
  │  $ echo $DEBUG                          $ ls /etc/config/                │
  │  False                                  settings.ini                     │
  │  $ echo $DB_HOST                        $ cat /etc/config/settings.ini   │
  │  postgres                               [database]                       │
  │                                         engine=postgresql                │
  │  ✅ Simple, fast access                 ✅ Complex configs, auto-update  │
  │  ❌ Requires pod restart to update      ✅ Updates without pod restart   │
  │  ❌ All keys become env vars            ✅ Selective file mounting       │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
deployment-with-configmap.yaml 
# anihpj Deployment using ConfigMap — both env vars AND mounted files
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj-web
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: django
        image: anihpj:latest
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8000

        # ── METHOD 1: All keys as env vars ──
        envFrom:
        - configMapRef:
            name: anihpj-config       # ◀── all keys become env vars
                                       #     DEBUG, DB_HOST, DB_PORT, etc.

        # ── METHOD 1b: Selective env vars ──
        env:
        - name: DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE
          value: anihpj.settings       # ◀── hardcoded value
        - name: DATABASE_HOST           # ◀── rename the key
          valueFrom:
            configMapKeyRef:
              name: anihpj-config
              key: DB_HOST              # ◀── specific key from ConfigMap

        # ── METHOD 2: Mount as files ──
        volumeMounts:
        - name: config-volume
          mountPath: /etc/anihpj/config  # ◀── files appear here
          readOnly: true

      volumes:
      - name: config-volume
        configMap:
          name: anihpj-config
          items:                         # ◀── selective: only mount these keys
          - key: settings.ini
            path: settings.ini           # ◀── /etc/anihpj/config/settings.ini
📖 ConfigMap Updates and Pod Behavior

When you update a ConfigMap, volume-mounted files update automatically (within ~60 seconds, or on kubelet sync period). However, environment variables do NOT update — the pod must be restarted. This is a key distinction for the CKA exam. If you need live config reloads, use volume mounts.

23.2 Secrets — Sensitive Configuration

CKA EXAM TOPIC SECURITY

Secrets are the companion to ConfigMaps — they store sensitive data like passwords, API keys, and TLS certificates. While structurally similar to ConfigMaps, Secrets have additional protections and CKA exam-specific nuances you must understand.

`n
📄 ConfigMap
  • Purpose: Non-sensitive config
  • Storage: Plain text in etcd
  • Data field: data: (plain text)
  • Example: DB_HOST, LOG_LEVEL
  • Safe to log: ✅ Yes
`n
🔒 Secret
  • Purpose: Sensitive data
  • Storage: Base64 in etcd (not encrypted by default!)
  • Data field: data: (base64) or stringData: (plain)
  • Example: DB_PASSWORD, SECRET_KEY
  • Safe to log: ❌ Never
⚠️ Base64 is NOT Encryption!

Kubernetes Secrets are base64 encoded, NOT encrypted by default. Anyone with kubectl get secret -o yaml access can decode them instantly. Base64 is just encoding — like translating English to pig Latin. It provides zero security on its own.

🔍 Base64 Encoding — What Actually Happens

  Creating a Secret:                         Reading a Secret:

  "postgres123"                              "cG9zdGdyZXMxMjM="
       │                                          │
       ▼                                          ▼
  ┌─────────────┐                          ┌─────────────┐
  │  base64     │                          │  base64     │
  │  encode     │                          │  decode     │
  └──────┬──────┘                          └──────┬──────┘
         ▼                                        ▼
  "cG9zdGdyZXMxMjM="                      "postgres123"
         │                                        │
         ▼                                        ▼
  ┌──────────────────┐                    ┌────────────────┐
  │  Stored in etcd  │                    │  Anyone with   │
  │  as base64       │                    │  RBAC access   │
  │                  │                    │  can read it!  │
  └──────────────────┘                    └────────────────┘

  $ echo -n "postgres123" | base64         $ echo "cG9zdGdyZXMxMjM=" | base64 -d
  cG9zdGdyZXMxMjM=                         postgres123

📋 Kubernetes Secret Types

Type Purpose Required Keys CKA Exam
Opaque Generic key-value (default) Any ⭐ Most common
kubernetes.io/tls TLS certificate + key tls.crt, tls.key ⭐ Ingress TLS
kubernetes.io/dockerconfigjson Private registry credentials .dockerconfigjson Sometimes tested
kubernetes.io/basic-auth Basic authentication username, password Rare
kubernetes.io/ssh-auth SSH private key ssh-privatekey Rare
kubernetes.io/service-account-token ServiceAccount token (legacy) Auto-generated Know it exists

🛠️ Creating Secrets — Imperative Commands (CKA Speed)

On the CKA exam, imperative commands save precious time. Master these:

bash — imperative Secret creation 
# 1. From literal values (most common on CKA)
kubectl create secret generic anihpj-secrets \
  --from-literal=SECRET_KEY=django-insecure-k8s-prod-key-2024 \
  --from-literal=DB_PASSWORD=postgres123 \
  --from-literal=DB_USER=anihpj_admin

# 2. From a file (e.g., TLS cert)
kubectl create secret tls anihpj-tls \
  --cert=./tls.crt \
  --key=./tls.key

# 3. From a docker registry (private images)
kubectl create secret docker-registry regcred \
  --docker-server=registry.example.com \
  --docker-username=admin \
  --docker-password=secret123

# 4. Generate YAML without applying (--dry-run trick)
kubectl create secret generic my-secret \
  --from-literal=password=supersecret \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml > secret.yaml

# Verify (notice values are hidden by default)
kubectl get secret anihpj-secrets -o yaml
# data:
#   SECRET_KEY: ZGphbmdvLWluc2VjdXJlLWs4cy1wcm9kLWtleS0yMDI0
#   DB_PASSWORD: cG9zdGdyZXMxMjM=
anihpj-secrets.yaml — declarative Secret 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: anihpj-secrets
  namespace: anihpj
type: Opaque
# Option A: base64-encoded (required for `data:`)
data:
  SECRET_KEY: ZGphbmdvLWluc2VjdXJlLWs4cy1wcm9kLWtleS0yMDI0   # ◀── base64
  DB_PASSWORD: cG9zdGdyZXMxMjM=                                  # ◀── base64
  DB_USER: YW5paHBqX2FkbWlu                                      # ◀── base64

# Option B: plain text (Kubernetes encodes it for you)
# stringData:                   # ◀── use this to avoid manual base64
#   SECRET_KEY: django-insecure-k8s-prod-key-2024
#   DB_PASSWORD: postgres123
#   DB_USER: anihpj_admin
💡 stringData vs data

Use stringData: when writing YAML by hand — it accepts plain text and Kubernetes base64-encodes it automatically. Use data: when you already have base64-encoded values (e.g., from kubectl get secret -o yaml). On the CKA exam, stringData: saves time and prevents encoding mistakes.

📦 Injecting Secrets into Pods

🔌 Two Injection Methods — Same as ConfigMaps

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                        anihpj Pod                               │
  │                                                                 │
  │  Method 1: Environment Variables          Method 2: Volume      │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────┐    ┌──────────────────────┐   │
  │  │  env:                       │    │  /etc/anihpj/secrets/│   │
  │  │    SECRET_KEY=django-ins... │    │  ├── SECRET_KEY      │   │
  │  │    DB_PASSWORD=postgres123  │    │  ├── DB_PASSWORD     │   │
  │  │    DB_USER=anihpj_admin     │    │  └── DB_USER         │   │
  │  │                             │    │                      │   │
  │  │  ✅ Easy to use in code    │    │  ✅ Auto-updates     │   │
  │  │  ❌ Visible in env dump    │    │  ✅ One file per key │   │
  │  │  ❌ No auto-update         │    │  ❌ Read file in code│   │
  │  └─────────────────────────────┘    └──────────────────────┘   │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
anihpj-deployment-with-secrets.yaml 
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj-web
  namespace: anihpj
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj-web
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj-web
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: django
        image: anihpj/web:latest
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8000

        # ── Method 1: Inject ALL Secret keys as env vars ──
        envFrom:
        - secretRef:
            name: anihpj-secrets        # ◀── all keys become env vars

        # ── Method 2: Inject specific keys as env vars ──
        env:
        - name: DATABASE_URL
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: anihpj-secrets
              key: DB_PASSWORD           # ◀── just one key

        # ── Method 3: Mount as files ──
        volumeMounts:
        - name: secret-volume
          mountPath: /etc/anihpj/secrets
          readOnly: true                 # ◀── ALWAYS set readOnly for secrets

      volumes:
      - name: secret-volume
        secret:
          secretName: anihpj-secrets
          defaultMode: 0400              # ◀── restrict file permissions (owner read only)
🔐 Secret Volume Best Practices

Always set readOnly: true on secret volume mounts and use restrictive defaultMode: 0400 (owner read-only). This prevents containers from accidentally modifying secret files and follows the principle of least privilege. For the CKA exam, remember: secret volume type (not configMap), and secretName (not name) in the volume spec.

🔍 Verifying Secrets (CKA Exam Commands)

bash — Secret verification commands 
# List secrets in namespace
kubectl get secrets -n anihpj

# View secret (values are hidden)
kubectl describe secret anihpj-secrets -n anihpj

# View base64-encoded values
kubectl get secret anihpj-secrets -n anihpj -o yaml

# Decode a specific value
kubectl get secret anihpj-secrets -n anihpj \
  -o jsonpath='{.data.DB_PASSWORD}' | base64 -d
# Output: postgres123

# Verify secret is mounted in pod
kubectl exec -it deploy/anihpj-web -n anihpj -- ls /etc/anihpj/secrets/
# Output: DB_PASSWORD  DB_USER  SECRET_KEY

kubectl exec -it deploy/anihpj-web -n anihpj -- cat /etc/anihpj/secrets/DB_PASSWORD
# Output: postgres123  (automatically decoded when mounted!)
📖 Secrets Are Decoded When Mounted

When Secrets are injected as environment variables or mounted as volume files, Kubernetes automatically decodes the base64. Your application receives plain text — no decoding needed in your code. The base64 encoding only exists in etcd and the API response.

23.3 ConfigMap and Secret Best Practices

BEST PRACTICES 📝 CKA EXAM

Real-world Kubernetes security depends on following strict practices around configuration management. These rules apply equally to the CKA exam and production clusters.

`n
✅ DO — Best Practices
  • ✅ Use ConfigMaps for non-sensitive config (DB_HOST, LOG_LEVEL, feature flags)
  • ✅ Use Secrets for sensitive data (passwords, API keys, TLS certs)
  • ✅ Use envFrom for bulk injection — fewer lines, fewer mistakes
  • ✅ Use volume mounts when config is a complete file (nginx.conf, settings.ini)
  • ✅ Enable encryption at rest for Secrets (EncryptionConfiguration)
  • ✅ Use RBAC to restrict who can get/list Secrets
  • ✅ Set readOnly: true on Secret volume mounts
  • ✅ Use stringData: in YAML for readability
  • ✅ Use immutable: true for configs that should never change
`n
❌ DON'T — Common Mistakes
  • ❌ Never commit Secrets to Git — use sealed-secrets or external vaults
  • ❌ Never hardcode secrets in Dockerfiles — they persist in image layers
  • ❌ Never log secret values — use placeholder text in logs
  • ❌ Never use ConfigMaps for passwords — they have no access restrictions
  • ❌ Never share Secrets across namespaces without good reason
  • ❌ Never give broad Secret access — get secrets * is dangerous
  • ❌ Never skip RBAC for Secret access — default allows too much
  • ❌ Never assume base64 = encrypted — it's just encoding
  • ❌ Never store large files in Secrets — 1MB limit per Secret

🔐 Secret Security Layers — Defense in Depth

  Layer 1: RBAC (WHO can access)
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  Role: secret-reader                                         │
  │    rules:                                                    │
  │    - apiGroups: [""]                                         │
  │      resources: ["secrets"]                                  │
  │      verbs: ["get"]          ◀── only get, not list/watch   │
  │      resourceNames: ["anihpj-secrets"]  ◀── specific secret │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Layer 2: Encryption at Rest (HOW it's stored in etcd)
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  Without encryption:  Secret → base64 → etcd (readable!)    │
  │  With encryption:     Secret → base64 → AES-CBC → etcd ✅  │
  │                                                              │
  │  Configure via: /etc/kubernetes/enc/enc.yaml                 │
  │  API server flag: --encryption-provider-config               │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Layer 3: Network (HOW it's transmitted)
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  API server ←──TLS──→ kubelet ←──tmpfs──→ Pod              │
  │  Secrets mounted as tmpfs (memory) — never written to disk  │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

🏗️ anihpj — Complete Configuration Architecture

Putting it all together — how the anihpj Django project separates configuration from code:

📐 anihpj Configuration Flow

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                    anihpj Namespace                                 │
  │                                                                     │
  │  ConfigMap: anihpj-config          Secret: anihpj-secrets          │
  │  ┌──────────────────────┐          ┌──────────────────────┐        │
  │  │ DB_HOST=postgres-svc │          │ SECRET_KEY=django... │        │
  │  │ DB_NAME=anihpj_db    │          │ DB_PASSWORD=post...  │        │
  │  │ DB_PORT=5432          │          │ DB_USER=anihpj_admin│        │
  │  │ DEBUG=False            │          │                      │        │
  │  │ ALLOWED_HOSTS=*       │          │  (base64 encoded)    │        │
  │  │ settings.ini (file)   │          └──────────┬───────────┘        │
  │  └──────────┬───────────┘                      │                    │
  │             │                                  │                    │
  │             ▼                                  ▼                    │
  │  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐      │
  │  │               anihpj-web Deployment                      │      │
  │  │  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │      │
  │  │  │  django container                                   │  │      │
  │  │  │                                                     │  │      │
  │  │  │  ENV:  DB_HOST, DB_NAME, DB_PORT  ◀── configMapRef │  │      │
  │  │  │        SECRET_KEY, DB_PASSWORD    ◀── secretRef    │  │      │
  │  │  │                                                     │  │      │
  │  │  │  FILES: /etc/anihpj/config/settings.ini ◀── CM vol │  │      │
  │  │  │         /etc/anihpj/secrets/       ◀── Secret vol  │  │      │
  │  │  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │      │
  │  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘      │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
💡 CKA Exam Quick Reference

On the exam, you'll typically need to: (1) create a ConfigMap or Secret from literals, (2) mount it in a Pod/Deployment as env vars or volume, and (3) verify the values are accessible inside the container. Practice the full flow: kubectl create → kubectl apply deployment → kubectl exec -- printenv.

🔐 Enabling Encryption at Rest for Secrets (CKA Advanced)

By default, Secrets are stored unencrypted in etcd. To encrypt them, you configure the API server with an EncryptionConfiguration file. This is a key topic for cluster hardening.

/etc/kubernetes/enc/enc.yaml — Encryption at Rest 
# Encryption configuration for Secrets at rest in etcd
apiVersion: apiserver.config.k8s.io/v1
kind: EncryptionConfiguration
resources:
  - resources:
    - secrets                             # ◀── only encrypt Secrets
    providers:
    - aescbc:                             # ◀── AES-CBC encryption
        keys:
        - name: key1
          secret: dGhpcyBpcyBhIDMyIGJ5dGUga2V5  # ◀── base64 of a 32-byte key
    - identity: {}                        # ◀── fallback: read unencrypted Secrets

# API server flag:
# --encryption-provider-config=/etc/kubernetes/enc/enc.yaml

# After enabling, re-encrypt existing Secrets:
# kubectl get secrets -A -o json | kubectl replace -f -

📋 ConfigMap vs Secret — Complete Comparison

Feature ConfigMap Secret
Purpose Non-sensitive config Sensitive data
Data encoding Plain text Base64 (data:) or plain (stringData:)
etcd storage Plain text Base64 (optionally encrypted at rest)
Size limit 1 MiB per ConfigMap 1 MiB per Secret
RBAC Standard resource rules Should have restricted access rules
Volume type configMap secret
Ref in pod spec configMapRef / configMapKeyRef secretRef / secretKeyRef
Volume field configMap.name secret.secretName
tmpfs mount No (on-disk) Yes (in-memory by default) — never on disk
kubectl describe Shows values Shows sizes only (hides values)
Immutable support ✅ immutable: true ✅ immutable: true

23.4 Immutable ConfigMaps and Secrets

CONCEPT PERFORMANCE

Kubernetes v1.21+ supports marking ConfigMaps and Secrets as immutable — once created, they cannot be modified. This provides safety and performance benefits for production clusters.

`n
🛡️ Safety Benefits
  • Prevents accidental changes — can't kubectl edit or kubectl apply updates
  • Protects production — config drift becomes impossible
  • Audit trail — changes require delete + recreate (visible in audit logs)
  • GitOps friendly — ensures deployed config matches Git source
`n
⚡ Performance Benefits
  • Kubelet optimization — stops watching immutable objects for changes
  • Reduced API server load — fewer watch connections per node
  • Scales better — significant at 1000+ ConfigMaps/Secrets
  • Faster sync — kubelet skips polling cycles for immutable objects
immutable-configmap.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: anihpj-config-v2          # ◀── version in name (required for updates)
  namespace: anihpj
immutable: true                     # ◀── THE KEY FIELD — cannot be changed once set
data:
  DB_HOST: "postgres-svc"
  DB_NAME: "anihpj_db"
  DB_PORT: "5432"
  DEBUG: "False"
  ALLOWED_HOSTS: "*"
immutable-secret.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: anihpj-secrets-v2         # ◀── version in name
  namespace: anihpj
type: Opaque
immutable: true                    # ◀── same field works for Secrets too
stringData:
  SECRET_KEY: django-insecure-k8s-prod-key-2024
  DB_PASSWORD: postgres123
  DB_USER: anihpj_admin

🚫 What Happens When You Try to Modify an Immutable Object

  $ kubectl edit configmap anihpj-config-v2
  error: configmaps "anihpj-config-v2" is immutable

  $ kubectl apply -f updated-config.yaml
  The ConfigMap "anihpj-config-v2" is invalid: data: Forbidden:
  field is immutable when `immutable` is set

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  Update Strategy for Immutable Configs:                     │
  │                                                             │
  │  1. Create NEW ConfigMap with new version name              │
  │     anihpj-config-v2 → anihpj-config-v3                   │
  │                                                             │
  │  2. Update Deployment to reference new name                 │
  │     configMapRef:                                           │
  │       name: anihpj-config-v3   ◀── point to new version   │
  │                                                             │
  │  3. Rolling update deploys pods with new config             │
  │                                                             │
  │  4. Delete old ConfigMap when no pods reference it          │
  │     kubectl delete configmap anihpj-config-v2              │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
💡 Immutability Rule of Thumb

Use immutable: true for production configs that are versioned via CI/CD. Skip it for development environments where you frequently tweak settings. On the CKA exam, know that the immutable field exists and that the only way to "update" an immutable ConfigMap is to delete it and create a new one — which requires restarting pods that reference it.

🏋️ CKA Practice Scenario — ConfigMaps and Secrets

Task: Create a namespace webapp. Create a ConfigMap named app-config with keys APP_COLOR=blue and APP_MODE=prod. Create a Secret named app-secret with key DB_PASS=supersecret. Create a Pod named webapp-pod using image nginx that loads the ConfigMap as environment variables and mounts the Secret at /etc/secrets. Verify the values are accessible.

Solution:

bash — CKA scenario solution 
# Step 1: Create namespace
kubectl create namespace webapp

# Step 2: Create ConfigMap
kubectl create configmap app-config \
  --from-literal=APP_COLOR=blue \
  --from-literal=APP_MODE=prod \
  -n webapp

# Step 3: Create Secret
kubectl create secret generic app-secret \
  --from-literal=DB_PASS=supersecret \
  -n webapp

# Step 4: Create Pod with both ConfigMap and Secret
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: webapp-pod
  namespace: webapp
spec:
  containers:
  - name: nginx
    image: nginx
    envFrom:
    - configMapRef:
        name: app-config         # ◀── all CM keys as env vars
    volumeMounts:
    - name: secret-vol
      mountPath: /etc/secrets
      readOnly: true
  volumes:
  - name: secret-vol
    secret:
      secretName: app-secret     # ◀── mount Secret as files
EOF

# Step 5: Verify
kubectl exec webapp-pod -n webapp -- printenv APP_COLOR
# Output: blue

kubectl exec webapp-pod -n webapp -- cat /etc/secrets/DB_PASS
# Output: supersecret

📋 Part 6 Summary — Storage (CKA 10%)

🗺️ Part 6 Complete Knowledge Map

  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  PART 6: STORAGE — COMPLETE KNOWLEDGE MAP                                │
  │                                                                          │
  │  Ch 21: VOLUMES                                                          │
  │  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
  │  │  emptyDir ──── Temp, pod-scoped. Sidecar pattern, scratch space │    │
  │  │  hostPath ──── Node filesystem. Logs, etcd certs. ⚠️ Dangerous │    │
  │  │  PVC ───────── Persistent. Databases, uploads. ⭐ CKA critical │    │
  │  │  configMap ─── Config files as volumes                          │    │
  │  │  secret ────── Sensitive files as tmpfs volumes                 │    │
  │  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
  │                                                                          │
  │  Ch 22: PV / PVC / STORAGECLASS                                         │
  │  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
  │  │  Admin creates PV ─────→ Dev creates PVC ────→ Pod mounts PVC   │    │
  │  │       (capacity,              (request,             (volumes:   │    │
  │  │        accessModes,            storageClass)         claimName) │    │
  │  │        reclaimPolicy)                                           │    │
  │  │                                                                  │    │
  │  │  StorageClass ── Dynamic provisioning. Auto-creates PV on PVC   │    │
  │  │  Statuses: Available → Bound → Released → (Retain/Delete)       │    │
  │  │  Access Modes: RWO (1 node), ROX (many read), RWX (many r/w)   │    │
  │  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
  │                                                                          │
  │  Ch 23: CONFIGMAPS & SECRETS                                           │
  │  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
  │  │  ConfigMap ── Non-sensitive config. envFrom or volume mount      │    │
  │  │  Secret ───── Sensitive. Base64 encoded (NOT encrypted by default)   │
  │  │                                                                  │    │
  │  │  Inject via: envFrom (all keys) │ env (selective) │ volume (files)   │
  │  │  Volume-mounted configs AUTO-UPDATE. Env vars DO NOT.           │    │
  │  │  immutable: true ── prevents changes, improves kubelet perf     │    │
  │  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
  │                                                                          │
  │  🎯 CKA MUST-REMEMBER COMMANDS:                                        │
  │  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
  │  │  kubectl create configmap myconfig --from-literal=key=value     │    │
  │  │  kubectl create secret generic mysecret --from-literal=pw=abc   │    │
  │  │  kubectl get pv,pvc                                              │    │
  │  │  kubectl get sc                    (StorageClasses)              │    │
  │  │  kubectl describe pvc <name>       (debug Pending PVCs)        │    │
  │  │  kubectl get secret <name> -o jsonpath='{.data.key}' | base64 -d   │
  │  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

⚠️ CKA Gotcha: ConfigMap & Secret Traps

  • Secrets are NOT encrypted by default — They are only base64-encoded (not encryption!). Anyone with API access can decode them. Enable encryption at rest via EncryptionConfiguration on the API server. The CKA tests this knowledge
  • envFrom vs env — envFrom imports ALL keys from a ConfigMap/Secret as env vars. env with valueFrom imports one specific key. If the exam says "inject the key DB_HOST from configmap app-config", use env + valueFrom
  • Env vars do NOT auto-update — If you change a ConfigMap, pods using envFrom won't see the change until restarted. Volume-mounted ConfigMaps DO auto-update (within ~60s). This is a classic trick question
  • immutable: true — Once set, the ConfigMap/Secret cannot be changed. This improves kubelet performance (stops watching for changes). You must delete and recreate to modify
  • Secret types matter — generic (Opaque) for arbitrary data, docker-registry for image pull credentials, tls for TLS cert/key pairs. The exam may specify which type to create

CKA Speed Tip — ConfigMap/Secret creation cheat sheet:
kubectl create configmap myconfig --from-literal=key1=val1 --from-literal=key2=val2
kubectl create configmap myconfig --from-file=config.properties
kubectl create secret generic mysecret --from-literal=password=S3cret!
kubectl create secret tls mytls --cert=tls.crt --key=tls.key
Decode a secret: kubectl get secret mysecret -o jsonpath='{.data.password}' | base64 -d

⚡ ConfigMap & Secret commands: kubectl get configmaps / kubectl get secrets
kubectl describe configmap <name> — see all key-value pairs
kubectl create configmap <name> --from-env-file=.env — import from .env file
kubectl get secret <name> -o yaml — see base64 values
kubectl create secret docker-registry regcred --docker-server=<url> --docker-username=<user> --docker-password=<pw>
📝 Chapter 23 Practice Drill

Timed challenge (target: 6 minutes):

  • 1. Create a ConfigMap app-config with keys: DB_HOST=postgres, DB_PORT=5432
  • 2. Create a Secret app-secret with key: DB_PASSWORD=MyP@ssw0rd
  • 3. Create a pod that loads ALL ConfigMap keys via envFrom
  • 4. In the same pod, inject only DB_PASSWORD from the Secret via env + valueFrom
  • 5. Exec in and verify: echo $DB_HOST $DB_PORT $DB_PASSWORD
  • 6. Mount the ConfigMap as a volume at /etc/config and verify files exist

📝 Chapter 23 — CKA Practice Questions

Q1
Create a ConfigMap named app-config with keys DB_HOST=postgres and DB_PORT=5432 using an imperative command.
Show Answer
Answer
# Imperative creation (fastest for CKA):
kubectl create configmap app-config \
  --from-literal=DB_HOST=postgres \
  --from-literal=DB_PORT=5432

# Verify:
kubectl get configmap app-config -o yaml
# apiVersion: v1
# kind: ConfigMap
# metadata:
#   name: app-config
# data:
#   DB_HOST: postgres
#   DB_PORT: "5432"     ← Note: stored as STRING

# Other creation methods:
# From file:
kubectl create configmap app-config --from-file=config.properties

# From env file:
kubectl create configmap app-config --from-env-file=.env

# From directory (each file becomes a key):
kubectl create configmap app-config --from-file=./configs/

# Dry-run to generate YAML:
kubectl create configmap app-config \
  --from-literal=DB_HOST=postgres \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml > configmap.yaml
Explanation

ConfigMaps store non-sensitive configuration data as key-value pairs. On the CKA, --from-literal is the fastest for simple key-value pairs. --from-file uses the filename as the key and file content as the value. --from-env-file reads KEY=VALUE lines from a .env file. Always use --dry-run=client -o yaml to generate YAML that you can modify. All values are stored as strings — even numbers like "5432".

Q2
Create a Secret named db-creds with username=admin and password=S3cret!. How do you decode the values?
Show Answer
Answer
# Create:
kubectl create secret generic db-creds \
  --from-literal=username=admin \
  --from-literal=password='S3cret!'

# View (values are base64-encoded):
kubectl get secret db-creds -o yaml
# data:
#   password: UzNjcmV0IQ==
#   username: YWRtaW4=

# Decode a specific value:
kubectl get secret db-creds \
  -o jsonpath='{.data.password}' | base64 -d
# S3cret!

# Decode all values:
kubectl get secret db-creds -o go-template='
{{range $k, $v := .data}}
{{$k}}: {{$v | base64decode}}
{{end}}'
# username: admin
# password: S3cret!

# ⚠️ base64 is NOT encryption!
# Anyone with API access can decode secrets
# Enable EncryptionConfiguration for at-rest encryption
Explanation

Secrets are base64-encoded, NOT encrypted. On the CKA, decoding secrets with jsonpath + base64 -d is a commonly tested skill. The imperative command auto-encodes values to base64. If creating via YAML, you must base64-encode values yourself (echo -n "admin" | base64) or use stringData (plain text, auto-encoded on apply). Secret types: generic (Opaque), docker-registry (image pull), tls (cert/key).

Q3
Create a pod that injects ALL keys from a ConfigMap as environment variables using envFrom.
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: env-pod
spec:
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx
    envFrom:                       # ← Import ALL keys
    - configMapRef:
        name: app-config           # ← All keys become env vars
    - secretRef:
        name: db-creds             # ← All secret keys too

# Verify:
kubectl exec env-pod -- env | grep -E 'DB_|username|password'
# DB_HOST=postgres
# DB_PORT=5432
# username=admin
# password=S3cret!

# vs. importing a SINGLE key:
env:
- name: DATABASE_HOST              # ← Custom env var name
  valueFrom:
    configMapKeyRef:
      name: app-config
      key: DB_HOST                 # ← Specific key
- name: DB_PASS
  valueFrom:
    secretKeyRef:
      name: db-creds
      key: password
Explanation

envFrom imports ALL keys from a ConfigMap/Secret as environment variables. env with valueFrom imports ONE specific key with a custom name. On the CKA, know both patterns: (1) "inject all config" → use envFrom, (2) "inject specific key as DATABASE_URL" → use env + valueFrom. Gotcha: if two sources have the same key name, the last one in the list wins. Invalid env var names (keys with dashes) are silently skipped with envFrom.

Q4
Mount a ConfigMap as a volume. What happens when you update the ConfigMap?
Show Answer
Answer
# Mount ConfigMap as volume:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: config-vol-pod
spec:
  volumes:
  - name: config-vol
    configMap:
      name: app-config
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx
    volumeMounts:
    - name: config-vol
      mountPath: /etc/config

# Each ConfigMap key becomes a file:
kubectl exec config-vol-pod -- ls /etc/config
# DB_HOST  DB_PORT

kubectl exec config-vol-pod -- cat /etc/config/DB_HOST
# postgres

# Update the ConfigMap:
kubectl edit configmap app-config
# Change DB_HOST to "mysql"

# After ~60 seconds (kubelet sync period):
kubectl exec config-vol-pod -- cat /etc/config/DB_HOST
# mysql  ← Auto-updated!

# ⚠️ BUT env vars do NOT auto-update!
# envFrom values only change on pod restart

# ⚠️ subPath mounts do NOT auto-update either!
# volumeMounts with subPath are frozen at pod creation
Explanation

Volume-mounted ConfigMaps auto-update within ~60 seconds (kubelet sync period). Environment variables do NOT auto-update. subPath mounts do NOT auto-update. On the CKA, this is a classic trick question: "How do you make your app pick up ConfigMap changes without restart?" Answer: mount as a volume (not env vars). Some apps also need to watch for file changes or receive SIGHUP to reload configuration. The auto-update uses symlinks internally — /etc/config/DB_HOST is actually a symlink to a timestamped directory.

Q5
What is stringData in a Secret YAML and how is it different from data?
Show Answer
Answer
# Using 'data' — values must be base64-encoded:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: my-secret
type: Opaque
data:
  username: YWRtaW4=          # echo -n "admin" | base64
  password: UzNjcmV0IQ==     # echo -n "S3cret!" | base64

# Using 'stringData' — plain text (auto-encoded):
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: my-secret
type: Opaque
stringData:                   # ← Plain text!
  username: admin
  password: S3cret!

# Both produce the SAME Secret!
# stringData is write-only — after apply, API returns 'data' (base64)

# Mix both:
data:
  existing-key: YWJj
stringData:
  new-key: plain text value   # ← Easier for new keys

# ⚠️ stringData doesn't appear in kubectl get -o yaml
# After creation, everything is stored under 'data' as base64
Explanation

stringData is a convenience field that accepts plain text — Kubernetes auto-encodes to base64 on creation. data requires pre-encoded base64 values. On the CKA, stringData saves time when writing YAML manually (no need to run echo -n | base64). After creation, kubectl get secret -o yaml only shows data (base64). If both data and stringData have the same key, stringData wins. Use stringData to avoid base64 encoding mistakes.

Q6
Create a pod that mounts a Secret as a volume with specific file permissions (0400).
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: secret-vol-pod
spec:
  volumes:
  - name: secret-vol
    secret:
      secretName: db-creds
      defaultMode: 0400           # ← Octal: owner read-only
      # Per-key permissions:
      items:
      - key: password
        path: db-password         # ← Custom filename
        mode: 0400                # ← Per-file mode
      - key: username
        path: db-username
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx
    volumeMounts:
    - name: secret-vol
      mountPath: /etc/secrets
      readOnly: true              # ← Extra safety

# Verify permissions:
kubectl exec secret-vol-pod -- ls -la /etc/secrets/
# -r--------  1 root root  7 Jan 1 00:00 db-password
# -r--------  1 root root  5 Jan 1 00:00 db-username

kubectl exec secret-vol-pod -- cat /etc/secrets/db-password
# S3cret!
Explanation

defaultMode sets file permissions for all keys (octal format: 0644 = rw-r--r--, 0400 = r--------). Per-key mode overrides defaultMode for individual files. On the CKA, Secret volumes default to 0644 — which might be too permissive for passwords. Set 0400 for sensitive data. Adding readOnly: true on the volumeMount adds an extra layer. The items field controls which keys to mount and their filenames, similar to ConfigMap volumes.

Q7
How do you make a ConfigMap immutable? What are the benefits?
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: prod-config
data:
  DB_HOST: prod-db.example.com
  DB_PORT: "5432"
immutable: true                   # ← Cannot be modified after apply!

# Trying to edit:
kubectl edit configmap prod-config
# error: configmaps "prod-config" is immutable

# To change: must delete and recreate
kubectl delete configmap prod-config
kubectl create configmap prod-config \
  --from-literal=DB_HOST=new-db.example.com

# Benefits of immutable:
# 1. Protection against accidental changes
# 2. Performance: kubelet stops watching for updates
#    (reduces API server load significantly)
# 3. Clear intent: "this config MUST NOT change"

# Works for Secrets too:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: prod-secret
immutable: true
stringData:
  API_KEY: my-key-that-never-changes
Explanation

immutable: true prevents any modification. The main benefit is performance: kubelet stops watching for changes, reducing API server load (significant with many ConfigMaps). Once set, you CANNOT unset it — you must delete and recreate. On the CKA, this might appear as: "Ensure this ConfigMap cannot be accidentally modified." Apply immutable: true. Works for both ConfigMaps and Secrets. Note: pods mounting an immutable ConfigMap must be restarted after recreating the ConfigMap.

Q8
What is the docker-registry secret type and how do you use it to pull images from a private registry?
Show Answer
Answer
# Create docker-registry secret:
kubectl create secret docker-registry regcred \
  --docker-server=registry.example.com \
  --docker-username=myuser \
  --docker-password=mypass \
  --docker-email=user@example.com

# Use in a pod:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: private-app
spec:
  imagePullSecrets:               # ← Pod-level field
  - name: regcred                 # ← Reference the secret
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: registry.example.com/myapp:v1

# Or attach to a ServiceAccount (all pods inherit):
kubectl patch serviceaccount default \
  -p '{"imagePullSecrets": [{"name": "regcred"}]}'

# Verify the secret content:
kubectl get secret regcred -o jsonpath='{.data.\.dockerconfigjson}' \
  | base64 -d | python -m json.tool
# {
#   "auths": {
#     "registry.example.com": {
#       "username": "myuser",
#       "password": "mypass",
#       "auth": "bXl1c2VyOm15cGFzcw=="
#     }
#   }
# }
Explanation

The docker-registry secret type stores Docker credentials in .dockerconfigjson format. Use it with imagePullSecrets in the pod spec or attach to a ServiceAccount for automatic injection. On the CKA, if a pod fails with ImagePullBackOff from a private registry, create a docker-registry secret and reference it. Attaching to the default ServiceAccount affects all pods in the namespace that don't specify a different SA.

Q9
A pod can't access its ConfigMap values. The ConfigMap exists and the pod is running. How do you troubleshoot?
Show Answer
Answer
# Check 1: Is the ConfigMap in the same namespace?
kubectl get configmap app-config -n production
# Error: not found ← ConfigMap is in wrong namespace!

# Check 2: Pod spec references correct name?
kubectl get pod my-pod -o yaml | grep -A5 configMapRef
#   configMapRef:
#     name: app-configg  ← Typo! Extra 'g'

# Check 3: env vs envFrom?
kubectl exec my-pod -- env | grep DB_HOST
# (empty) ← Env var not set!

# Possible causes:
# - envFrom only imports keys that are valid env var names
#   Key "my-config.key" with dot → SKIPPED silently!
# - env with key that doesn't exist → pod won't start
#   (pod stays ContainerCreating with error event)

# Check 4: Volume mount present?
kubectl describe pod my-pod | grep -A5 Mounts
kubectl exec my-pod -- ls /etc/config/
# ls: /etc/config/: No such file or directory ← Mount missing!

# Check 5: ConfigMap has the right keys?
kubectl describe configmap app-config
# Data
# ====
# db_host:   ← lowercase!
# Pod expects DB_HOST ← case mismatch!
Explanation

ConfigMap troubleshooting checklist: (1) same namespace? (2) name spelled correctly? (3) key names valid for env vars? (4) volume mount correct? (5) key case match? On the CKA, the most common issues are namespace mismatch (ConfigMap in different ns than pod) and typos in names/keys. If using envFrom, keys with special characters are silently skipped. If using env + valueFrom with a non-existent key, the pod fails to start — check Events with describe.

Q10
How do you create a Secret from a certificate file? Show creating a TLS Secret and verifying it.
Show Answer
Answer
# Create TLS Secret from cert and key files:
kubectl create secret tls app-tls \
  --cert=server.crt \
  --key=server.key

# Verify:
kubectl get secret app-tls -o yaml
# type: kubernetes.io/tls
# data:
#   tls.crt: LS0tLS1CRUd...   ← base64 of cert
#   tls.key: LS0tLS1CRUd...   ← base64 of key

# Decode and inspect the certificate:
kubectl get secret app-tls \
  -o jsonpath='{.data.tls\.crt}' | base64 -d | openssl x509 -text -noout
# Subject: CN = myapp.example.com
# Issuer: ...
# Validity: ...

# Use in an Ingress:
spec:
  tls:
  - hosts:
    - myapp.example.com
    secretName: app-tls      # ← Reference TLS secret

# Create generic secret from file:
kubectl create secret generic ssh-key \
  --from-file=id_rsa=/path/to/private-key
# Key = "id_rsa", value = file contents
Explanation

kubectl create secret tls validates that the cert and key are valid PEM format. The Secret type is kubernetes.io/tls with required keys tls.crt and tls.key. On the CKA, TLS secrets are used with Ingress TLS termination and are covered in the security domain. Use --from-file on generic secrets for arbitrary files (SSH keys, config files). The filename becomes the key unless you use --from-file=custom-key=/path.

🟡 Part 7: Security Chapters 24-27

Chapter 24: RBAC — Role-Based Access Control

In a production Kubernetes cluster, you don't want every developer deleting nodes or every CI pipeline creating ClusterRoleBindings. RBAC (Role-Based Access Control) is Kubernetes' built-in authorization system that controls WHO can perform WHAT actions on WHICH resources. For our anihpj/jobpost Django app, RBAC lets us give developers permission to deploy pods while preventing them from modifying secrets or disrupting the cluster. This chapter covers all four RBAC objects, how to create and test them, and real-world scenarios you'll face on the CKA exam.

🟡 Intermediate ⏱️ ~2 hours 🔒 RBAC 📝 CKA EXAM

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ Why RBAC exists and what happens without it — the blast radius of unrestricted access
  • ✅ The four RBAC objects: Role, ClusterRole, RoleBinding, ClusterRoleBinding
  • ✅ How to create Roles with specific verbs, resources, and API groups
  • ✅ How to bind Roles to users, groups, and ServiceAccounts
  • ✅ Testing permissions with kubectl auth can-i and the --as flag
  • ✅ Building a complete RBAC setup for the anihpj project with dev, QA, and ops roles
🎓 CKA Relevance
Cluster Architecture, Installation & Configuration 25%
RBAC Is Heavily Tested on the CKA

Expect 2-3 questions creating Roles/Bindings and debugging access issues. Key exam skills:

  • Create Role and RoleBinding imperatively with kubectl create role and kubectl create rolebinding
  • Know the difference between Role (namespaced) and ClusterRole (cluster-wide)
  • Use kubectl auth can-i with --as to verify permissions
  • Understand that a ClusterRole can be bound at namespace level with a RoleBinding
  • Write RBAC YAML from scratch — practice until it's muscle memory

RBAC Imperative Commands

ActionCommand
Create Rolekubectl create role pod-reader --verb=get,list,watch --resource=pods -n anihpj
Create ClusterRolekubectl create clusterrole node-viewer --verb=get,list --resource=nodes
Create RoleBindingkubectl create rolebinding dev-bind --role=pod-reader --user=jane -n anihpj
Create ClusterRoleBindingkubectl create clusterrolebinding admin-bind --clusterrole=cluster-admin --user=admin
Bind to ServiceAccountkubectl create rolebinding sa-bind --role=pod-reader --serviceaccount=anihpj:default -n anihpj
Test permissionskubectl auth can-i list pods --as=jane -n anihpj
List all permissionskubectl auth can-i --list --as=jane -n anihpj
View roleskubectl get roles,rolebindings -n anihpj

Role + RoleBinding — Exam Skeleton

This is the most common RBAC pattern on the CKA. Memorize it.

# 1. Role — WHAT permissions (namespaced)
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: pod-reader                   # ◀── role name
  namespace: anihpj                  # ◀── MUST specify namespace
rules:
- apiGroups: [""]                    # ◀── "" = core API group
  resources: ["pods"]                # ◀── resource type
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]    # ◀── allowed actions
- apiGroups: ["apps"]                # ◀── apps API group
  resources: ["deployments"]
  verbs: ["get", "list"]
---
# 2. RoleBinding — WHO gets the permissions
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: read-pods-binding
  namespace: anihpj                  # ◀── same namespace as Role
subjects:
- kind: User                        # ◀── User, Group, or ServiceAccount
  name: jane                        # ◀── the WHO
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
roleRef:
  kind: Role                        # ◀── Role or ClusterRole
  name: pod-reader                   # ◀── must match Role name
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io

RBAC Exam Traps

  • ❌ ClusterRoleBinding can reference a Role
  • ✅ ClusterRoleBinding can ONLY reference a ClusterRole — never a Role
  • ❌ A RoleBinding with a ClusterRole gives cluster-wide access
  • ✅ RoleBinding + ClusterRole = ClusterRole permissions scoped to THAT namespace only
  • apiGroups: [""] means core API — pods, services, configmaps. Apps group needs ["apps"]
  • Verbs are: get, list, watch, create, update, patch, delete, deletecollection
  • ServiceAccount subject format: system:serviceaccount:<namespace>:<name>
  • kubectl auth can-i returns "yes"/"no" — use --list to see ALL permissions

Find RBAC YAML Fields Fast

kubectl explain role.rules                     # permission rules
kubectl explain role.rules.apiGroups           # API group filter
kubectl explain role.rules.resources           # resource types
kubectl explain role.rules.verbs               # allowed actions
kubectl explain rolebinding.subjects           # who gets access
kubectl explain rolebinding.roleRef            # which role to bind
👤User / SA
→
🔐Authentication
→
✅RBAC Check
→
🛡️Admission
→
💾etcd
4 RBAC Objects
8 Verbs (get, list...delete)
25% CKA Exam Domain Weight
3 Subject Types (User/Group/SA)

24.1 What is RBAC and Why It Matters

RBAC answers one question: "Is this user allowed to do this action on this resource?" Without RBAC, anyone who can reach the Kubernetes API server can create, delete, or modify anything — pods, secrets, nodes, the entire cluster.

Without RBAC vs With RBAC — The Security Gate
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                                                                              │
  │  ❌ WITHOUT RBAC (or permissive RBAC)         ✅ WITH PROPER RBAC            │
  │                                                                              │
  │  ┌──────────────┐                          ┌──────────────┐                 │
  │  │ 👨‍💻 Developer  │──┐                      │ 👨‍💻 Developer  │──┐              │
  │  └──────────────┘  │                      └──────────────┘  │              │
  │  ┌──────────────┐  │   ┌──────────┐       ┌──────────────┐  │  ┌────────┐ │
  │  │ 🤖 CI/CD Bot  │──┼──▶│ API      │       │ 🤖 CI/CD Bot  │──┼─▶│ RBAC   │ │
  │  └──────────────┘  │   │ Server   │       └──────────────┘  │  │ Gate   │ │
  │  ┌──────────────┐  │   │          │       ┌──────────────┐  │  │        │ │
  │  │ 🧪 Intern    │──┘   │ FULL     │       │ 🧪 Intern    │──┘  │ ✅ pods │ │
  │  └──────────────┘      │ ACCESS   │       └──────────────┘     │ ❌ nodes│ │
  │                         │ TO       │                            │ ❌ sec. │ │
  │  Everyone can:         │ EVERY-   │       Only allowed          │        │ │
  │  • Delete nodes 💀    │ THING    │       actions pass →       └───┬────┘ │
  │  • Read secrets 🔓    │          │                               │      │
  │  • Modify RBAC 😱     │          │                          ┌────▼─────┐ │
  │  • Break cluster 💣   └──────────┘                          │ API      │ │
  │                                                              │ Server   │ │
  │                                                              └──────────┘ │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
⚠️ Real-World Disaster Without RBAC

In 2018, Tesla's Kubernetes dashboard was left open without RBAC. Attackers accessed the cluster, found AWS credentials in environment variables, and used Tesla's infrastructure for cryptocurrency mining. RBAC would have prevented this entirely.

🔑 The Three Questions RBAC Answers

Every API request is checked against these three dimensions:

👤
WHO?

Subject — User, Group, or ServiceAccount making the request

⚡
WHAT?

Verb — The action: get, list, create, update, delete, watch, patch

📦
WHICH?

Resource — pods, services, secrets, nodes, deployments, etc.

check-rbac-enabled.sh 
# Verify RBAC is enabled on your cluster
kubectl api-versions | grep rbac
# Output: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1

# Check API server authorization mode
kubectl describe pod kube-apiserver-controlplane -n kube-system | grep authorization-mode
# Output: --authorization-mode=Node,RBAC

24.2 The Four RBAC Objects

Kubernetes RBAC uses exactly four objects that work in pairs: two define permissions (Role, ClusterRole) and two assign permissions (RoleBinding, ClusterRoleBinding).

The Four RBAC Objects — How They Connect
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                          RBAC OBJECT MAP                                     │
  │                                                                              │
  │         DEFINE PERMISSIONS              ASSIGN PERMISSIONS                   │
  │         (What can be done?)            (Who gets them?)                      │
  │                                                                              │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────┐     ┌─────────────────────────────┐            │
  │  │   📋 Role               │     │   🔗 RoleBinding             │            │
  │  │   Scope: ONE namespace  │◀────│   Scope: ONE namespace      │            │
  │  │                         │     │                             │            │
  │  │   "pods: get,list"      │     │   "jane → pod-reader"      │            │
  │  │   in namespace: anihpj  │     │   in namespace: anihpj     │            │
  │  └─────────────────────────┘     └──────────────┬──────────────┘            │
  │                                                  │                          │
  │                                        subjects: │                          │
  │                                    ┌─────────────┼─────────────┐            │
  │                                    │             │             │            │
  │                                 ┌──▼──┐    ┌────▼───┐   ┌────▼────┐       │
  │                                 │User │    │ Group  │   │Service  │       │
  │                                 │jane │    │dev-team│   │Account  │       │
  │                                 └─────┘    └────────┘   └─────────┘       │
  │                                                                              │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────┐     ┌─────────────────────────────┐            │
  │  │   📋 ClusterRole        │     │   🔗 ClusterRoleBinding      │            │
  │  │   Scope: ENTIRE cluster │◀────│   Scope: ENTIRE cluster     │            │
  │  │                         │     │                             │            │
  │  │   "nodes: get,list"     │     │   "ops-admin → node-viewer"│            │
  │  │   cluster-wide          │     │   cluster-wide              │            │
  │  └─────────────────────────┘     └─────────────────────────────┘            │
  │                                                                              │
  │  💡 TRICK: A RoleBinding CAN reference a ClusterRole!                       │
  │     This gives ClusterRole permissions scoped to ONE namespace.              │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Object Scope Purpose Can Reference
Role Namespace Define permissions within a single namespace —
ClusterRole Cluster Define permissions cluster-wide (nodes, PVs, namespaces) —
RoleBinding Namespace Grant Role OR ClusterRole to subjects in a namespace Role or ClusterRole
ClusterRoleBinding Cluster Grant ClusterRole to subjects cluster-wide ClusterRole only
RBAC Decision
👤 Subject User, Group, or ServiceAccount
⚡ Verb get, list, create, delete...
📦 Resource pods, services, secrets, nodes
🏷️ API Group "" (core), apps, rbac, networking
🌐 Namespace Scoped or cluster-wide
💡 When to Use ClusterRole + RoleBinding

Create ONE ClusterRole (e.g., "pod-reader") and bind it in multiple namespaces with separate RoleBindings. This avoids duplicating Role definitions in every namespace. The ClusterRole is reused, but access is scoped per namespace.

24.3 Creating Roles and ClusterRoles

A Role is a set of rules. Each rule specifies an API group, a list of resources, and the verbs (actions) allowed on them.

📝 The Three Parts of Every RBAC Rule

apiGroups → resources → verbs — "In this API group, on these resources, allow these actions"

API Group Value in YAML Resources
Core""pods, services, configmaps, secrets, PVCs, nodes, namespaces
Apps"apps"deployments, replicasets, statefulsets, daemonsets
Batch"batch"jobs, cronjobs
Networking"networking.k8s.io"networkpolicies, ingresses
RBAC"rbac.authorization.k8s.io"roles, rolebindings, clusterroles, clusterrolebindings
Storage"storage.k8s.io"storageclasses, csinodes
role-pod-reader.yaml 
# Role: Read-only access to pods in anihpj namespace
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: pod-reader
  namespace: anihpj
rules:
- apiGroups: [""]              # core API group
  resources: ["pods", "pods/log"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["services"]
  verbs: ["get", "list"]
clusterrole-node-viewer.yaml 
# ClusterRole: View nodes cluster-wide (no namespace)
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRole
metadata:
  name: node-viewer              # no namespace field!
rules:
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["nodes"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["persistentvolumes"]   # PVs are cluster-scoped
  verbs: ["get", "list"]
imperative-role-creation.sh 
# Imperative — faster on the exam!
kubectl create role pod-reader \
  --verb=get,list,watch \
  --resource=pods,pods/log \
  -n anihpj

kubectl create clusterrole node-viewer \
  --verb=get,list,watch \
  --resource=nodes

# Generate YAML to inspect before applying
kubectl create role deploy-manager \
  --verb=get,list,create,update,delete \
  --resource=deployments.apps \
  -n anihpj \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml
📌 Resource Sub-resources

Some resources have sub-resources like pods/log, pods/exec, pods/portforward. These must be listed separately in the resources array. Getting pod logs requires both pods and pods/log permissions.

24.4 Creating RoleBindings and ClusterRoleBindings

A Binding connects a subject (who) to a role (what permissions). The binding itself determines the scope.

Binding Combinations — All 4 Valid Configurations
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                    ALL VALID BINDING COMBINATIONS                             │
  │                                                                              │
  │  ┌─────────────────┐    ┌──────────────────┐    ┌─────────────────────┐     │
  │  │ 1️⃣ Role         │───▶│ RoleBinding       │───▶│ Access in ONE       │     │
  │  │   (namespaced)  │    │ (namespaced)      │    │ namespace           │     │
  │  └─────────────────┘    └──────────────────┘    └─────────────────────┘     │
  │                                                                              │
  │  ┌─────────────────┐    ┌──────────────────┐    ┌─────────────────────┐     │
  │  │ 2️⃣ ClusterRole  │───▶│ ClusterRoleBinding│───▶│ Access EVERYWHERE   │     │
  │  │   (cluster)     │    │ (cluster)         │    │ (all namespaces)    │     │
  │  └─────────────────┘    └──────────────────┘    └─────────────────────┘     │
