  │                                                                              │
  │  ┌─────────────────┐    ┌──────────────────┐    ┌─────────────────────┐     │
  │  │ 3️⃣ ClusterRole  │───▶│ RoleBinding       │───▶│ ClusterRole scoped  │     │
  │  │   (cluster)     │    │ (namespaced)      │    │ to ONE namespace!   │     │
  │  └─────────────────┘    └──────────────────┘    └─────────────────────┘     │
  │                                                  ☝️ Reuse ClusterRole       │
  │                                                     across namespaces       │
  │                                                                              │
  │  ┌─────────────────┐    ┌──────────────────┐                                │
  │  │ 4️⃣ Role         │ ✘  │ ClusterRoleBinding│    ❌ INVALID! Never works    │
  │  │   (namespaced)  │    │ (cluster)         │                                │
  │  └─────────────────┘    └──────────────────┘                                │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
rolebinding-user.yaml 
# Bind "pod-reader" role to user "jane" in anihpj namespace
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: jane-pod-reader
  namespace: anihpj
subjects:
- kind: User
  name: jane
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
roleRef:
  kind: Role
  name: pod-reader
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
clusterrolebinding-admin.yaml 
# Give "ops-admin" cluster-wide node viewing access
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRoleBinding
metadata:
  name: ops-node-viewer
subjects:
- kind: User
  name: ops-admin
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
roleRef:
  kind: ClusterRole          # ClusterRoleBinding MUST ref ClusterRole
  name: node-viewer
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
imperative-bindings.sh 
# Imperative RoleBinding — FASTEST for exam
kubectl create rolebinding jane-pod-reader \
  --role=pod-reader \
  --user=jane \
  -n anihpj

# ClusterRoleBinding
kubectl create clusterrolebinding ops-node-viewer \
  --clusterrole=node-viewer \
  --user=ops-admin

# Bind ClusterRole to namespace (combination #3)
kubectl create rolebinding dev-pod-reader \
  --clusterrole=pod-reader-cluster \
  --group=dev-team \
  -n anihpj

# Bind to ServiceAccount
kubectl create rolebinding sa-deployer \
  --role=deploy-manager \
  --serviceaccount=anihpj:anihpj-deployer \
  -n anihpj
⚠️ RoleBindings Are Immutable in roleRef

You cannot change the roleRef field after creating a RoleBinding. To change which Role is referenced, you must delete and recreate the binding. You CAN add/remove subjects though.

24.5 Testing RBAC

The kubectl auth can-i command is your best friend for RBAC verification. On the CKA exam, always verify after creating roles and bindings.

testing-rbac.sh 
# Test specific permission
kubectl auth can-i list pods --as=jane -n anihpj
# yes ✅

kubectl auth can-i delete pods --as=jane -n anihpj
# no ❌ (jane only has get,list,watch)

kubectl auth can-i create deployments --as=jane -n anihpj
# no ❌

# Test cluster-scoped resources
kubectl auth can-i list nodes --as=ops-admin
# yes ✅

kubectl auth can-i delete nodes --as=ops-admin
# no ❌ (only get,list,watch)

# List ALL permissions for a user in a namespace
kubectl auth can-i --list --as=jane -n anihpj
# Resources       Non-Resource URLs   Resource Names   Verbs
# pods             []                  []               [get list watch]
# pods/log         []                  []               [get list watch]
# services         []                  []               [get list]

# Test as a ServiceAccount
kubectl auth can-i list pods \
  --as=system:serviceaccount:anihpj:anihpj-deployer \
  -n anihpj

# SelfSubjectAccessReview — check YOUR OWN permissions
kubectl auth can-i create pods -n anihpj
# yes (checks your current kubeconfig identity)
RBAC Testing Decision Tree
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                   RBAC TESTING WORKFLOW                          │
  │                                                                  │
  │     Step 1: Create Role/ClusterRole                             │
  │         │                                                        │
  │         ▼                                                        │
  │     Step 2: Create Binding                                      │
  │         │                                                        │
  │         ▼                                                        │
  │     Step 3: Test with can-i                                     │
  │         │                                                        │
  │    ┌────┴────┐                                                  │
  │    │         │                                                  │
  │  "yes" ✅  "no" ❌                                              │
  │    │         │                                                  │
  │    ▼         ▼                                                  │
  │  DONE    Debug:                                                 │
  │          ├── kubectl get rolebinding -n <ns> -o yaml           │
  │          ├── Check: namespace matches?                          │
  │          ├── Check: role name matches?                          │
  │          ├── Check: subject name correct?                       │
  │          ├── Check: apiGroup correct?                           │
  │          └── Check: verb and resource correct?                  │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
💡 CKA Speed Tip: Use --list for Debugging

kubectl auth can-i --list --as=jane -n anihpj shows ALL permissions at once. Much faster than testing individual verbs when debugging why something is denied.

24.6 RBAC for anihpj — Practical Example

Let's build a complete RBAC setup for the anihpj job board application with three team roles: developers (full deploy access), QA team (read-only), and monitoring (read across all namespaces).

anihpj RBAC Architecture — Three Teams, Three Access Levels
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                    anihpj RBAC SETUP                                          │
  │                                                                              │
  │  ┌──────────────────┐                                                       │
  │  │ 👨‍💻 DEVELOPERS     │    Role: anihpj-developer                            │
  │  │ (group: dev-team)│───▶ pods: get,list,watch,create,update,delete         │
  │  │                  │    deployments: get,list,create,update                 │
  │  │ Full deploy      │    services: get,list,create                          │
  │  │ access in anihpj │    configmaps: get,list                               │
  │  └──────────────────┘    Scope: namespace "anihpj" only                     │
  │                                                                              │
  │  ┌──────────────────┐                                                       │
  │  │ 🧪 QA TEAM        │    Role: anihpj-qa-viewer                             │
  │  │ (group: qa-team) │───▶ pods: get,list,watch                              │
  │  │                  │    pods/log: get                                       │
  │  │ Read-only in     │    deployments: get,list                              │
  │  │ anihpj           │    services: get,list                                 │
  │  └──────────────────┘    Scope: namespace "anihpj" only                     │
  │                                                                              │
  │  ┌──────────────────┐                                                       │
  │  │ 📊 MONITORING     │    ClusterRole: monitoring-reader                     │
  │  │ (user: monitor)  │───▶ pods: get,list,watch (ALL namespaces)             │
  │  │                  │    nodes: get,list                                     │
  │  │ Read access      │    events: get,list                                   │
  │  │ across cluster   │    Scope: cluster-wide (ClusterRoleBinding)           │
  │  └──────────────────┘                                                       │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
anihpj-rbac-complete.yaml 
# ── 1. Developer Role ──
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: anihpj-developer
  namespace: anihpj
rules:
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["pods", "pods/log", "pods/exec", "services", "configmaps"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch", "create", "update", "delete"]
- apiGroups: ["apps"]
  resources: ["deployments", "replicasets"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "create", "update", "patch"]
---
# ── 2. Developer RoleBinding ──
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: dev-team-binding
  namespace: anihpj
subjects:
- kind: Group
  name: dev-team
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
roleRef:
  kind: Role
  name: anihpj-developer
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
---
# ── 3. QA Read-Only Role ──
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: anihpj-qa-viewer
  namespace: anihpj
rules:
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["pods", "pods/log", "services", "events"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
- apiGroups: ["apps"]
  resources: ["deployments"]
  verbs: ["get", "list"]
---
# ── 4. QA RoleBinding ──
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: qa-team-binding
  namespace: anihpj
subjects:
- kind: Group
  name: qa-team
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
roleRef:
  kind: Role
  name: anihpj-qa-viewer
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
---
# ── 5. Monitoring ClusterRole ──
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRole
metadata:
  name: monitoring-reader
rules:
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["pods", "nodes", "services", "events"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
- apiGroups: ["apps"]
  resources: ["deployments", "replicasets"]
  verbs: ["get", "list"]
---
# ── 6. Monitoring ClusterRoleBinding ──
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRoleBinding
metadata:
  name: monitoring-binding
subjects:
- kind: User
  name: monitor
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
roleRef:
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: monitoring-reader
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
verify-anihpj-rbac.sh 
# Apply all RBAC resources
kubectl apply -f anihpj-rbac-complete.yaml

# ── Test developer access ──
kubectl auth can-i create pods --as-group=dev-team --as=dev-user -n anihpj
# yes ✅
kubectl auth can-i delete nodes --as-group=dev-team --as=dev-user
# no ❌ (no cluster access)

# ── Test QA access ──
kubectl auth can-i list pods --as-group=qa-team --as=qa-user -n anihpj
# yes ✅
kubectl auth can-i delete pods --as-group=qa-team --as=qa-user -n anihpj
# no ❌ (read-only)

# ── Test monitoring access ──
kubectl auth can-i list pods --as=monitor -n kube-system
# yes ✅ (all namespaces)
kubectl auth can-i delete pods --as=monitor -n anihpj
# no ❌ (read-only)

✅ RBAC Best Practices

  • Use Groups over individual Users
  • Apply least privilege — only verbs actually needed
  • Use ClusterRole + RoleBinding for reusable rules
  • Always can-i test after creating bindings
  • Name roles descriptively: anihpj-developer

❌ RBAC Anti-Patterns

  • Binding cluster-admin to regular users
  • Using * wildcard verbs and resources
  • Not testing after creating bindings
  • One huge Role instead of multiple specific ones
  • Forgetting namespace on RoleBindings

RBAC Verification Checklist

  • kubectl auth can-i VERB RESOURCE --as=USER -n NS → verify access granted/denied
  • kubectl get roles -n NS → confirm role exists with correct name
  • kubectl get rolebindings -n NS → confirm binding exists
  • kubectl describe rolebinding NAME -n NS → verify subject and roleRef match
  • kubectl auth can-i --list --as=USER -n NS → list ALL permissions for user
  • kubectl get clusterroles | grep NAME → confirm ClusterRole exists
  • kubectl describe clusterrolebinding NAME → verify cluster-wide binding

24.7 Practice Exercises

Practice these scenarios until you can complete each one in under 5 minutes — that's CKA exam speed.

⏱️ Target: 4 minutes
Exercise 24.1 — Create Role and RoleBinding

Create RBAC resources so user jane can read pods in a specific namespace.

  1. Create namespace exam-practice
  2. Create Role pod-reader allowing get, list, watch on pods
  3. Create RoleBinding binding pod-reader to user jane
  4. Test: kubectl auth can-i list pods --as=jane -n exam-practice → should return yes
  5. Test: kubectl auth can-i delete pods --as=jane -n exam-practice → should return no
💡 Hint: Use imperative commands — kubectl create role and kubectl create rolebinding are fastest.
📝 View Solution 
# Step 1: Create namespace
kubectl create namespace exam-practice

# Step 2: Create Role
kubectl create role pod-reader \
  --verb=get,list,watch \
  --resource=pods \
  -n exam-practice

# Step 3: Create RoleBinding
kubectl create rolebinding pod-reader-binding \
  --role=pod-reader \
  --user=jane \
  -n exam-practice

# Step 4: Verify
kubectl auth can-i list pods --as=jane -n exam-practice
# yes
kubectl auth can-i delete pods --as=jane -n exam-practice
# no
⏱️ Target: 3 minutes
Exercise 24.2 — ClusterRole for Node Viewer

Create a ClusterRole and ClusterRoleBinding so user ops-admin can view nodes cluster-wide.

  1. Create ClusterRole node-viewer allowing get, list on nodes
  2. Create ClusterRoleBinding for user ops-admin
  3. Verify: kubectl auth can-i list nodes --as=ops-admin → yes
  4. Verify: kubectl auth can-i delete nodes --as=ops-admin → no
💡 Hint: ClusterRole + ClusterRoleBinding = cluster-wide access (no namespace flag).
📝 View Solution 
# Step 1: Create ClusterRole
kubectl create clusterrole node-viewer \
  --verb=get,list \
  --resource=nodes

# Step 2: Create ClusterRoleBinding
kubectl create clusterrolebinding node-viewer-binding \
  --clusterrole=node-viewer \
  --user=ops-admin

# Step 3: Verify
kubectl auth can-i list nodes --as=ops-admin
# yes
kubectl auth can-i delete nodes --as=ops-admin
# no
⏱️ Target: 5 minutes
Exercise 24.3 — ServiceAccount RBAC

Grant the anihpj-deployer ServiceAccount permissions to manage deployments and services in the anihpj namespace.

  1. Create ServiceAccount anihpj-deployer in namespace anihpj
  2. Create Role allowing create, get, list, update on deployments and services
  3. Bind ServiceAccount to the Role
  4. Test: kubectl auth can-i create deployments --as=system:serviceaccount:anihpj:anihpj-deployer -n anihpj
💡 Hint: The --as impersonation for ServiceAccounts uses the format system:serviceaccount:NAMESPACE:SA_NAME.
📝 View Solution 
# Step 1: Create ServiceAccount
kubectl create serviceaccount anihpj-deployer -n anihpj

# Step 2: Create Role with multiple resources
kubectl create role anihpj-deploy-role \
  --verb=create,get,list,update \
  --resource=deployments,services \
  -n anihpj

# Step 3: Bind SA to Role
kubectl create rolebinding anihpj-deploy-binding \
  --role=anihpj-deploy-role \
  --serviceaccount=anihpj:anihpj-deployer \
  -n anihpj

# Step 4: Verify
kubectl auth can-i create deployments \
  --as=system:serviceaccount:anihpj:anihpj-deployer \
  -n anihpj
# yes
kubectl auth can-i delete pods \
  --as=system:serviceaccount:anihpj:anihpj-deployer \
  -n anihpj
# no — only deployments and services are allowed
⏱️ Target: 5 minutes
Exercise 24.4 — Debug RBAC Issues

Intentionally create a broken Role, identify the issue, and fix it.

  1. Create a Role with the wrong verb (e.g., getlist instead of get, list)
  2. Bind it and test — observe the failure
  3. Use kubectl auth can-i --list to find what the user CAN do
  4. Fix the Role and verify access is restored
💡 Hint: Use kubectl edit role to fix the verbs. Each verb must be a separate list item, not concatenated.
📝 View Solution 
# Step 1: Create broken Role (wrong verb)
kubectl create role broken-role \
  --verb=getlist \
  --resource=pods \
  -n exam-practice

kubectl create rolebinding broken-binding \
  --role=broken-role \
  --user=testuser \
  -n exam-practice

# Step 2: Test — fails because "getlist" isn't a valid verb
kubectl auth can-i get pods --as=testuser -n exam-practice
# no

# Step 3: Debug — see what user CAN do
kubectl auth can-i --list --as=testuser -n exam-practice
# Shows "getlist" verb — not "get" or "list"

# Step 4: Fix the Role
kubectl edit role broken-role -n exam-practice
# Change verbs from ["getlist"] to ["get", "list"]
# Or delete and recreate:
kubectl delete role broken-role -n exam-practice
kubectl create role broken-role \
  --verb=get,list \
  --resource=pods \
  -n exam-practice

# Step 5: Verify — now works
kubectl auth can-i get pods --as=testuser -n exam-practice
# yes
⏱️ Target: 5 Minutes Per Exercise

On the CKA exam, RBAC questions must be fast. Use imperative commands (kubectl create role, kubectl create rolebinding) not YAML files. Only fall back to YAML for complex multi-rule roles.

⚠️ CKA Gotcha: RBAC Traps

  • Role vs ClusterRole — Role is namespace-scoped (pods, services). ClusterRole is cluster-wide (nodes, PVs, namespaces). If the exam says "allow listing nodes", you MUST use ClusterRole + ClusterRoleBinding
  • RoleBinding can reference a ClusterRole — This is powerful and confusing. A RoleBinding in namespace prod pointing to a ClusterRole limits those permissions to prod only. The exam tests this
  • Verbs are comma-separated strings, not arrays in CLI — --verb=get,list,watch NOT --verb=[get,list,watch]. In YAML they ARE arrays: ["get", "list"]
  • kubectl auth can-i is your verification tool — Always verify after creating RBAC: kubectl auth can-i get pods --as=jane -n dev. The --as flag impersonates a user
  • API groups matter — Core resources (pods, services) use apiGroups: [""]. Deployments use apiGroups: ["apps"]. Forgetting the API group = silent permission denial

CKA Speed Tip — RBAC in 4 imperative commands:
kubectl create role pod-reader --verb=get,list,watch --resource=pods -n dev
kubectl create rolebinding pod-reader-binding --role=pod-reader --user=jane -n dev
kubectl create clusterrole node-reader --verb=get,list --resource=nodes
kubectl create clusterrolebinding node-reader-binding --clusterrole=node-reader --user=jane
Never write RBAC YAML from scratch on the exam unless forced to.

⚡ RBAC commands: kubectl auth can-i '*' '*' --as=system:serviceaccount:ns:sa-name — check SA permissions
kubectl get roles,rolebindings -n <ns> — list namespace RBAC
kubectl get clusterroles,clusterrolebindings — list cluster RBAC
kubectl describe rolebinding <name> -n <ns> — see subjects and role refs
kubectl auth can-i --list --as=jane -n dev — list ALL permissions for a user

📝 Chapter 24 — CKA Practice Questions

Q1
Create a Role named pod-reader in namespace dev that allows get, list, and watch on pods. Then bind it to user jane.
Show Answer
Answer
# Create Role:
kubectl create role pod-reader \
  --verb=get,list,watch \
  --resource=pods \
  -n dev

# Create RoleBinding:
kubectl create rolebinding pod-reader-binding \
  --role=pod-reader \
  --user=jane \
  -n dev

# Verify:
kubectl auth can-i get pods --as=jane -n dev
# yes

kubectl auth can-i delete pods --as=jane -n dev
# no ← Only get, list, watch!

kubectl auth can-i get pods --as=jane -n prod
# no ← Role only in dev namespace!

# View created objects:
kubectl describe role pod-reader -n dev
kubectl describe rolebinding pod-reader-binding -n dev
Explanation

Roles define WHAT actions on WHICH resources. RoleBindings connect WHO (user/group/SA) to a Role. Both are namespace-scoped. On the CKA, use imperative commands — never write RBAC YAML from scratch unless forced. The --verb flag accepts comma-separated verbs. Verify with kubectl auth can-i. Common verbs: get, list, watch, create, update, patch, delete, deletecollection.

Q2
What is the difference between Role/RoleBinding and ClusterRole/ClusterRoleBinding? When must you use ClusterRole?
Show Answer
Answer
Aspect Role + RoleBinding ClusterRole + ClusterRoleBinding
Scope Single namespace Cluster-wide
Resources Namespaced (pods, svc, cm) All + non-namespaced (nodes, PVs, ns)
Use case Dev permissions in one ns Cluster admin, node ops
Combo Role → RoleBinding only ClusterRole → RoleBinding OR ClusterRoleBinding 
# MUST use ClusterRole for:
# - Nodes (non-namespaced)
# - PersistentVolumes (non-namespaced)
# - Namespaces (non-namespaced)
# - CSRs, ClusterRoles, ClusterRoleBindings

# Trick: ClusterRole + RoleBinding = scoped power!
# Grant ClusterRole permissions in ONE namespace:
kubectl create rolebinding scoped-admin \
  --clusterrole=admin \
  --user=jane \
  -n dev    # ← jane is admin ONLY in dev
Explanation

ClusterRoles are needed for non-namespaced resources (nodes, PVs, namespaces) and cross-namespace access. The power combo: ClusterRole + RoleBinding limits ClusterRole permissions to a single namespace. On the CKA, if you see "allow user to list nodes" → ClusterRole + ClusterRoleBinding (nodes are cluster-scoped). If "allow user to list pods in namespace dev" → Role + RoleBinding (or ClusterRole + RoleBinding in dev).

Q3
How do you check what permissions a user has? List all permissions for user dev-user in namespace production.
Show Answer
Answer
# Check specific permission:
kubectl auth can-i get pods --as=dev-user -n production
# yes

kubectl auth can-i delete deployments --as=dev-user -n production
# no

# List ALL permissions:
kubectl auth can-i --list --as=dev-user -n production
# Resources          Non-Resource URLs    Verbs
# pods               []                   [get list watch]
# services           []                   [get list]
# configmaps         []                   [get]
# deployments.apps   []                   [get list watch create update]

# For a ServiceAccount:
kubectl auth can-i get pods \
  --as=system:serviceaccount:production:deploy-sa -n production
# yes

# Check if you (current user) can do something:
kubectl auth can-i create clusterroles
# no  (unless you're cluster-admin)

# Wildcard check:
kubectl auth can-i '*' '*' --as=admin-user
# yes ← Full admin!
Explanation

kubectl auth can-i is the essential RBAC verification tool. Use --as to impersonate users, --as=system:serviceaccount:NS:NAME for ServiceAccounts. --list shows ALL permissions in a namespace. On the CKA, ALWAYS verify after creating RBAC objects — auth can-i confirms your configuration works. This is also how you debug "permission denied" errors: check what the user/SA can actually do.

Q4
What API group do you use for Deployments? For Pods? How does this affect RBAC rules?
Show Answer
Answer
# Core API resources (apiGroups: [""]):
# pods, services, configmaps, secrets, PVCs, endpoints, nodes

# apps group (apiGroups: ["apps"]):
# deployments, replicasets, daemonsets, statefulsets

# networking.k8s.io:
# networkpolicies, ingresses

# rbac.authorization.k8s.io:
# roles, rolebindings, clusterroles, clusterrolebindings

# Quick lookup:
kubectl api-resources | grep -i deployment
# NAME          SHORTNAMES  APIVERSION  NAMESPACED  KIND
# deployments   deploy      apps/v1     true        Deployment
#                           ↑ apps group!

# RBAC rule for Deployments:
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: deploy-manager
  namespace: dev
rules:
- apiGroups: ["apps"]        # ← NOT empty string!
  resources: ["deployments"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "create", "update", "delete"]
- apiGroups: [""]            # ← Core API (pods)
  resources: ["pods"]
  verbs: ["get", "list"]

# ⚠️ Forgetting apiGroups or using wrong group = silent denial!
Explanation

API groups are critical in RBAC rules. Core resources (pods, services) use empty string "". Deployments use "apps". NetworkPolicies use "networking.k8s.io". Use kubectl api-resources to find the right group. On the CKA, wrong API group = the role won't grant access (silently fails). Most common mistake: using apiGroups: [""] for Deployments when it should be apiGroups: ["apps"].

Q5
Create a ClusterRole that allows reading nodes and PersistentVolumes. Bind it to user infra-admin.
Show Answer
Answer
# Create ClusterRole:
kubectl create clusterrole infra-reader \
  --verb=get,list,watch \
  --resource=nodes,persistentvolumes

# Create ClusterRoleBinding:
kubectl create clusterrolebinding infra-reader-binding \
  --clusterrole=infra-reader \
  --user=infra-admin

# Verify:
kubectl auth can-i list nodes --as=infra-admin
# yes

kubectl auth can-i list pv --as=infra-admin
# yes

kubectl auth can-i delete nodes --as=infra-admin
# no ← Only read verbs!

kubectl auth can-i list pods --as=infra-admin
# no ← Only nodes and PVs!

# Multiple resources in one rule:
# The CLI creates:
# rules:
# - apiGroups: [""]
#   resources: ["nodes", "persistentvolumes"]
#   verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
Explanation

Nodes and PVs are cluster-scoped resources — they require ClusterRole + ClusterRoleBinding. A regular Role can't grant access to cluster-scoped resources. On the CKA, the imperative command handles multiple resources with comma separation. Both nodes and PVs are in the core API group (""). Verify with auth can-i — check both positive (what's allowed) and negative (what's denied) cases.

Q6
A user runs kubectl get pods -n production and gets Forbidden. How do you troubleshoot?
Show Answer
Answer
# Step 1: Check what the user CAN do:
kubectl auth can-i --list --as=jane -n production
# If empty → no permissions at all

# Step 2: Check RoleBindings in the namespace:
kubectl get rolebindings -n production -o wide
# NAME           ROLE                 SUBJECTS
# dev-binding    Role/dev-role        User:jane  ← Found!

# Step 3: Check the Role:
kubectl describe role dev-role -n production
# Rules:
#   Resources: [deployments]
#   Verbs: [get list]
# ← pods not included!

# Step 4: Fix — add pods to the role:
kubectl edit role dev-role -n production
# Add:
# - apiGroups: [""]
#   resources: ["pods"]
#   verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]

# Or create a new role and binding:
kubectl create role pod-reader \
  --verb=get,list,watch --resource=pods -n production
kubectl create rolebinding pod-reader-jane \
  --role=pod-reader --user=jane -n production

# Step 5: Verify fix:
kubectl auth can-i get pods --as=jane -n production
# yes
Explanation

RBAC troubleshooting flow: (1) check current permissions with auth can-i --list, (2) find RoleBindings with -o wide to see subjects, (3) describe the Role to see rules, (4) fix the gap. On the CKA, common issues: wrong namespace, missing verb (has get but not list), wrong API group, or no binding at all. The -o wide flag on rolebindings shows the subject (user/group/SA) — essential for debugging.

Q7
How do you grant a ClusterRole's permissions in only ONE namespace? What is this pattern called?
Show Answer
Answer
# Pattern: ClusterRole + RoleBinding (scoped power)
# Grant built-in "edit" ClusterRole in just "dev" namespace:
kubectl create rolebinding dev-editor \
  --clusterrole=edit \
  --user=jane \
  -n dev

# jane now has edit permissions in dev ONLY:
kubectl auth can-i create deployments --as=jane -n dev
# yes

kubectl auth can-i create deployments --as=jane -n production
# no ← Not in other namespaces!

# Built-in ClusterRoles you can reuse:
# cluster-admin   → full access to everything
# admin           → full namespace access (except quotas/ns)
# edit            → read-write (no RBAC or quotas)
# view            → read-only

# This pattern is called "role aggregation" or "scoped ClusterRole"
# It avoids creating duplicate Roles in every namespace
Explanation

A RoleBinding can reference a ClusterRole — the ClusterRole's permissions are then scoped to the RoleBinding's namespace. This is efficient: define once as ClusterRole, bind in specific namespaces with RoleBindings. On the CKA, use the built-in ClusterRoles (view, edit, admin) with namespace-scoped RoleBindings. Note: a RoleBinding can NEVER reference a regular Role from another namespace — it can only reference a ClusterRole or a Role in the same namespace.

Q8
Write an RBAC rule that allows accessing the logs subresource of pods. What is a subresource?
Show Answer
Answer
# Subresources are actions on a resource's child endpoints:
# pods/log        → kubectl logs
# pods/exec       → kubectl exec
# pods/portforward → kubectl port-forward
# pods/status     → pod status updates
# deployments/scale → kubectl scale

# Role allowing logs and exec:
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: pod-debugger
  namespace: dev
rules:
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["pods"]
  verbs: ["get", "list"]         # ← Read pods
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["pods/log"]        # ← Log subresource
  verbs: ["get"]
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["pods/exec"]       # ← Exec subresource
  verbs: ["create"]              # ← exec uses create verb!

# Imperative:
kubectl create role pod-debugger \
  --verb=get,list --resource=pods \
  --verb=get --resource=pods/log \
  -n dev

# ⚠️ exec uses verb "create" not "get"!
# It creates an exec session
Explanation

Subresources extend resources with specific operations: pods/log, pods/exec, pods/portforward, deployments/scale. On the CKA, a common task: "allow a user to view logs but not exec into pods." Grant pods/log with get verb, but NOT pods/exec. Critical gotcha: kubectl exec uses the create verb (not get), because it creates a new exec session. Granting permission on pods does NOT automatically grant subresource access.

Q9
How do you grant permissions only on specific named resources (e.g., only pod important-pod)?
Show Answer
Answer
# Use resourceNames to restrict to specific objects:
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: specific-pod-reader
  namespace: production
rules:
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["pods"]
  resourceNames: ["important-pod", "critical-pod"]  # ← Only these!
  verbs: ["get", "watch", "delete"]

# Result:
kubectl auth can-i get pod/important-pod \
  --as=jane -n production
# yes

kubectl auth can-i get pod/other-pod \
  --as=jane -n production
# no ← Not in resourceNames!

# ⚠️ resourceNames doesn't work with "list":
# You can't "list" specific resources
# "list" returns ALL resources — it's always all-or-nothing
# Use get for specific names, list for all

# Also works with secrets:
rules:
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["secrets"]
  resourceNames: ["db-password"]
  verbs: ["get"]
Explanation

resourceNames narrows access to specific named objects. Useful for secrets (only allow reading one specific secret). On the CKA, this is used in least-privilege scenarios: "grant access to secret db-creds only." Key limitation: list and create verbs cannot use resourceNames (list returns everything, create doesn't have a name yet). Only get, update, patch, delete, and watch work with resourceNames.

Q10
How do you quickly find which RoleBindings/ClusterRoleBindings apply to a specific user or ServiceAccount?
Show Answer
Answer
# Check namespace RoleBindings:
kubectl get rolebindings -n dev -o wide
# NAME              ROLE                 SUBJECTS
# pod-reader-rb     Role/pod-reader      User:jane
# deploy-editor     ClusterRole/edit     Group:developers

# Check ClusterRoleBindings:
kubectl get clusterrolebindings -o wide | grep jane
# node-reader-crb   ClusterRole/node-reader   User:jane

# Search ALL namespaces:
kubectl get rolebindings -A -o wide | grep jane
# dev        pod-reader-rb    Role/pod-reader    User:jane
# staging    deploy-rb        Role/deploy-mgr    User:jane

# For ServiceAccounts:
kubectl get rolebindings -A -o wide | grep deploy-sa
kubectl get clusterrolebindings -o wide | grep deploy-sa

# Describe for full details:
kubectl describe rolebinding pod-reader-rb -n dev
# Role:
#   Kind: Role
#   Name: pod-reader
# Subjects:
#   Kind   Name   Namespace
#   ----   ----   ---------
#   User   jane

# All permissions summary:
kubectl auth can-i --list --as=jane -n dev
Explanation

Finding all RBAC bindings for a user: (1) kubectl get rolebindings -A -o wide | grep <user> for namespace bindings, (2) kubectl get clusterrolebindings -o wide | grep <user> for cluster bindings. The -o wide flag shows the SUBJECTS column, making it greppable. On the CKA, when debugging permissions, check BOTH RoleBindings (per namespace) and ClusterRoleBindings (global). auth can-i --list gives the final computed permissions after all bindings are evaluated.

Q11
Set up a multi-role RBAC system: a dev user who can manage pods/deployments in the dev namespace, a qa user who can only view resources in all namespaces, and an ops user with full cluster admin. Show all the Roles, ClusterRoles, and Bindings.
Show Answer
Answer
# 1. DEV user — manage pods/deployments in "dev" namespace only
kubectl create role dev-role -n dev \
  --verb=get,list,watch,create,update,delete \
  --resource=pods,deployments,services

kubectl create rolebinding dev-binding -n dev \
  --role=dev-role --user=dev

# 2. QA user — read-only across ALL namespaces
# Use built-in ClusterRole "view"
kubectl create clusterrolebinding qa-binding \
  --clusterrole=view --user=qa

# 3. OPS user — full cluster admin
# Use built-in ClusterRole "cluster-admin"
kubectl create clusterrolebinding ops-binding \
  --clusterrole=cluster-admin --user=ops

# Verify each user's permissions:
kubectl auth can-i create deployments -n dev --as=dev
# yes
kubectl auth can-i create deployments -n production --as=dev
# no ← dev only has access to "dev" namespace

kubectl auth can-i get pods -n kube-system --as=qa
# yes ← "view" ClusterRole works in all namespaces
kubectl auth can-i delete pods -n dev --as=qa
# no ← view is read-only

kubectl auth can-i '*' '*' --as=ops
# yes ← cluster-admin can do everything

# List all bindings for verification:
kubectl get rolebindings -n dev -o wide
kubectl get clusterrolebindings -o wide | grep -E 'qa|ops'
Explanation

This pattern demonstrates the three common RBAC tiers: (1) Namespace-scoped via Role + RoleBinding — limits access to one namespace, (2) Cluster-wide read-only via built-in view ClusterRole + ClusterRoleBinding, (3) Full admin via built-in cluster-admin ClusterRole. Kubernetes ships with four default ClusterRoles: view (read-only), edit (read+write, no RBAC), admin (namespace admin including RBAC), cluster-admin (everything). On the CKA, always prefer imperative commands for speed: kubectl create role, kubectl create rolebinding, kubectl create clusterrolebinding. Use --as= to impersonate and verify.

Chapter 25: Service Accounts and API Access

Humans use kubectl with certificates to talk to the API server, but what about pods? When your anihpj/jobpost Django application needs to read ConfigMaps, list pods, or interact with the Kubernetes API, it does so through a Service Account. Every pod in Kubernetes automatically gets a Service Account, but the default one has minimal permissions. In this chapter, you'll learn how to create dedicated Service Accounts, control their permissions with RBAC, manage token projection, and follow security best practices.

🟡 Intermediate ⏱️ ~1 hour 👤 Service Accounts 📝 CKA EXAM

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ The difference between User Accounts (humans) and Service Accounts (pods)
  • ✅ How the default Service Account works and why it's insufficient for production
  • ✅ Creating and assigning Service Accounts to pods
  • ✅ Token projection — how modern K8s handles SA tokens securely
  • ✅ Binding RBAC roles to Service Accounts for precise pod-level permissions
🎓 CKA Relevance
Cluster Architecture, Installation & Configuration 25%
Service Accounts on the CKA

Expect questions that combine SA creation with RBAC binding. Key skills:

  • kubectl create serviceaccount <name> -n <ns> — know this by heart
  • Assign a SA to a Pod via spec.serviceAccountName
  • Disable auto-mount with automountServiceAccountToken: false
  • The SA subject format: system:serviceaccount:<namespace>:<name>

Service Account Imperative Commands

ActionCommand
Create SAkubectl create serviceaccount anihpj-sa -n anihpj
List SAskubectl get serviceaccounts -n anihpj
Describe SAkubectl describe sa anihpj-sa -n anihpj
Create tokenkubectl create token anihpj-sa -n anihpj
Create token (1hr)kubectl create token anihpj-sa --duration=3600s -n anihpj
Bind SA to Rolekubectl create rolebinding sa-bind --role=pod-reader --serviceaccount=anihpj:anihpj-sa -n anihpj
Test SA permissionskubectl auth can-i list pods --as=system:serviceaccount:anihpj:anihpj-sa -n anihpj

ServiceAccount + Pod — Exam Skeleton

Know how to create a SA, assign it to a pod, and disable auto-mount.

# ServiceAccount
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: anihpj-sa
  namespace: anihpj
automountServiceAccountToken: true   # ◀── default; set false to disable
---
# Pod using the ServiceAccount
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-web
  namespace: anihpj
spec:
  serviceAccountName: anihpj-sa      # ◀── assign SA to pod
  automountServiceAccountToken: true  # ◀── pod-level override
  containers:
  - name: web
    image: anihpj:latest
    # Token is auto-mounted at:
    # /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/token
    # /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/ca.crt
    # /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/namespace

Service Account Gotchas

  • ❌ The default SA has full access to the namespace
  • ✅ The default SA has almost NO permissions — create specific SAs with explicit RBAC
  • ❌ SA tokens never expire
  • ✅ Since K8s 1.22, projected tokens are time-limited and audience-bound. Legacy tokens are deprecated
  • automountServiceAccountToken: false on the pod overrides the SA-level setting
  • SA is namespaced — you cannot use a SA from namespace A in namespace B
  • Deleting a SA doesn't delete its RoleBindings — orphaned bindings remain

Find Service Account Fields Fast

kubectl explain serviceaccount                         # SA spec
kubectl explain pod.spec.serviceAccountName            # assign SA to pod
kubectl explain pod.spec.automountServiceAccountToken  # disable token mount
2 Account Types (User vs SA)
1 Default SA per Namespace
3 Auto-Mounted Files
1h Default Token TTL

25.1 User Accounts vs Service Accounts

Kubernetes has two types of identities, but only one is managed by the API server. Understanding this distinction is critical for RBAC and the CKA exam.

Aspect User Account (Human) Service Account (Pod)
Used byHumans (kubectl, dashboard)Pods / Applications
Created byExternal system (LDAP, OIDC, certs)Kubernetes API (kubectl create sa)
ScopeCluster-wide (no namespace)Namespaced
CredentialsX.509 certificates / OIDC tokensAuto-mounted projected tokens
Managed by K8s❌ No — external to cluster✅ Yes — API objects
kubectl create❌ Not possible✅ kubectl create sa
RBAC subject kindUser or GroupServiceAccount
User Account vs Service Account — Who Talks to the API Server?
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                                                                              │
  │    HUMAN (User Account)                    POD (Service Account)             │
  │                                                                              │
  │    ┌──────────────┐                       ┌──────────────────────┐           │
  │    │  👨‍💻 kubectl   │                       │  📦 anihpj-web pod   │           │
  │    │              │                       │                      │           │
  │    │  ~/.kube/    │                       │  /var/run/secrets/   │           │
  │    │  config      │                       │  kubernetes.io/      │           │
  │    │              │                       │  serviceaccount/     │           │
  │    │  🔑 x509 cert│                       │  ├── token  🎫      │           │
  │    │  or OIDC     │                       │  ├── ca.crt 📜      │           │
  │    │  token       │                       │  └── namespace 📝   │           │
  │    └──────┬───────┘                       └──────────┬───────────┘           │
  │           │                                          │                       │
  │           │     HTTPS + Certificate/Token             │     HTTPS + SA Token │
  │           │                                          │                       │
  │           └──────────────┬───────────────────────────┘                       │
  │                          ▼                                                   │
  │                 ┌────────────────┐                                           │
  │                 │  🏛️ API Server  │                                           │
  │                 │                │                                           │
  │                 │  1. Auth-N ────│── Verify identity                         │
  │                 │  2. Auth-Z ────│── Check RBAC                              │
  │                 │  3. Admit  ────│── Apply policies                          │
  │                 │  4. Persist ───│── Write to etcd                           │
  │                 └────────────────┘                                           │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
📌 Key Insight

Kubernetes does NOT manage User Accounts — there's no kubectl create user command. Users are created externally (certificates, LDAP, OIDC). But Service Accounts ARE Kubernetes objects that you create, list, and delete with kubectl.

25.2 Default Service Account

Every namespace automatically gets a default Service Account. If you don't specify a serviceAccountName in your pod spec, it uses this one automatically.

inspect-default-sa.sh 
# Every namespace has a "default" SA
kubectl get sa -n anihpj
# NAME      SECRETS   AGE
# default   0         30d

# See what the default SA can do (spoiler: almost nothing)
kubectl auth can-i --list \
  --as=system:serviceaccount:anihpj:default \
  -n anihpj

# Check which SA a running pod uses
kubectl get pod anihpj-web -n anihpj -o jsonpath='{.spec.serviceAccountName}'
# default

# See the mounted token inside a pod
kubectl exec anihpj-web -n anihpj -- \
  cat /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/token
⚠️ Default SA is Not Safe for Production

The default SA has a token auto-mounted into every pod. Even though it has minimal permissions, if your pod is compromised, an attacker gets that token. Best practice: create specific SAs per application and disable auto-mount where not needed.

3 Auto-Mounted Files in Every Pod

🎫 token
/var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/token — JWT token for authenticating to the API server. Auto-refreshed by kubelet before expiry (~1 hour).
📜 ca.crt
/var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/ca.crt — Cluster CA certificate. Used to verify the API server's TLS certificate when making HTTPS calls.
📝 namespace
/var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/namespace — Plain text file containing the pod's namespace name. Used by SDK clients to discover their namespace.
✅
When to Use Default SA

Simple pods that never call the Kubernetes API. Disable the token with automountServiceAccountToken: false.

🔧
When to Create Custom SA

Any pod that needs API access: reading ConfigMaps, listing pods, managing deployments, or running as a controller.

25.3 Creating and Using Service Accounts

Creating a dedicated SA for your anihpj application is straightforward — create it, then reference it in the pod spec.

1
Create the SA

kubectl create sa anihpj-sa -n anihpj

2
Create RBAC Role

Define what permissions the SA needs

3
Bind SA to Role

kubectl create rolebinding sa-bind --role=... --serviceaccount=anihpj:anihpj-sa

4
Assign to Pod

Set spec.serviceAccountName: anihpj-sa

5
Verify

kubectl auth can-i --list --as=system:serviceaccount:anihpj:anihpj-sa

anihpj-serviceaccount-setup.yaml 
# 1. ServiceAccount
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: anihpj-sa
  namespace: anihpj
---
# 2. Role — what anihpj needs
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: anihpj-app-role
  namespace: anihpj
rules:
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["configmaps", "secrets"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]          # read config at startup
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["pods"]
  verbs: ["get", "list"]                   # health-check readiness
---
# 3. RoleBinding
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: anihpj-sa-binding
  namespace: anihpj
subjects:
- kind: ServiceAccount
  name: anihpj-sa
  namespace: anihpj
roleRef:
  kind: Role
  name: anihpj-app-role
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
---
# 4. Pod using the ServiceAccount
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-web
  namespace: anihpj
spec:
  serviceAccountName: anihpj-sa            # ◀── use our custom SA
  containers:
  - name: web
    image: anihpj:latest
    ports:
    - containerPort: 8000
disable-auto-mount.yaml 
# Pod that does NOT need API access — disable token mount
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-worker
  namespace: anihpj
spec:
  serviceAccountName: anihpj-sa
  automountServiceAccountToken: false      # ◀── no token mounted!
  containers:
  - name: worker
    image: anihpj:latest
    command: ["python", "manage.py", "process_jobs"]

25.4 Token Projection and Token Request API

Before Kubernetes 1.22, SA tokens were stored as Secrets and never expired. Modern K8s uses projected tokens — they're time-limited, audience-bound, and automatically refreshed by the kubelet.

Legacy Tokens vs Projected Tokens — Security Evolution
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                                                                              │
  │  ❌ LEGACY (pre-1.22)                  ✅ PROJECTED (1.22+)                  │
  │                                                                              │
  │  ┌────────────────────┐               ┌────────────────────────┐            │
  │  │  Secret object     │               │  TokenRequest API      │            │
  │  │  ├── token: xyz... │               │  ├── audience: api     │            │
  │  │  │                 │               │  ├── expiry: 1 hour    │            │
  │  │  │  ⚠️ NEVER       │               │  ├── auto-refresh: ✅  │            │
  │  │  │    EXPIRES!     │               │  │                      │            │
  │  │  │                 │               │  │  🔒 Time-limited    │            │
  │  │  │  ⚠️ NO audience│               │  │  🔒 Audience-bound  │            │
  │  │  │    binding      │               │  │  🔒 Auto-rotated    │            │
  │  │  │                 │               │  │                      │            │
  │  │  │  Stored in      │               │  │  Generated on       │            │
  │  │  │  etcd forever   │               │  │  demand by kubelet  │            │
  │  └────────────────────┘               └────────────────────────┘            │
  │                                                                              │
  │  If token is stolen →                 If token is stolen →                   │
  │  UNLIMITED access 💀                  Expires in ≤1 hour 🛡️                 │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
projected-token-volume.yaml 
# Pod with explicit projected token volume (advanced scenario)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-api-client
  namespace: anihpj
spec:
  serviceAccountName: anihpj-sa
  containers:
  - name: api-client
    image: anihpj:latest
    volumeMounts:
    - name: token-vol
      mountPath: /var/run/secrets/tokens
      readOnly: true
  volumes:
  - name: token-vol
    projected:
      sources:
      - serviceAccountToken:
          path: api-token               # file name
          expirationSeconds: 3600       # 1 hour
          audience: api                 # intended audience
create-token-cli.sh 
# Create a short-lived token from CLI (TokenRequest API)
kubectl create token anihpj-sa -n anihpj --duration=3600s

# Create a token for a specific audience
kubectl create token anihpj-sa -n anihpj --audience=https://my-api.example.com

# Decode and inspect a token (JWT)
TOKEN=$(kubectl create token anihpj-sa -n anihpj)
echo $TOKEN | cut -d. -f2 | base64 -d 2>/dev/null | jq .
# Shows: iss, sub, aud, exp, iat, kubernetes.io fields
📌 CKA Exam Note

The CKA rarely tests projected token details, but you must know that kubectl create token is the modern way to generate tokens, and that auto-mounted tokens now expire. Legacy Secret-based tokens are deprecated.

Projected Token Lifecycle

1
Pod Scheduled

Kubelet calls TokenRequest API → gets a time-limited JWT token for the pod's ServiceAccount

▼
2
Token Mounted

Token written to /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/token as a projected volume

▼
3
App Uses Token

Application reads token → sends in Authorization: Bearer <token> header to API server

▼
4
Auto-Refresh

Before token expires (~80% of TTL), kubelet automatically requests a new token and updates the mounted file

25.5 Binding RBAC to Service Accounts

The real power comes when you combine Service Accounts with RBAC — giving each application precisely the permissions it needs, nothing more.

🔑 SA Subject Format in RBAC

When using --as flag or in RoleBinding subjects, the format is:
system:serviceaccount:<namespace>:<sa-name>

anihpj SA + RBAC — Least Privilege in Action
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │             anihpj SERVICE ACCOUNT RBAC ARCHITECTURE                         │
  │                                                                              │
  │  ┌──────────────────┐    ┌────────────────┐    ┌──────────────────────┐     │
  │  │ 📦 anihpj-web    │    │ 🔗 RoleBinding  │    │ 📋 Role              │     │
  │  │                  │    │                │    │ anihpj-app-role      │     │
  │  │ SA: anihpj-sa    │───▶│ anihpj-sa ───▶│───▶│ configmaps: get,list │     │
  │  │                  │    │ Role           │    │ secrets: get         │     │
  │  │ Can read config  │    │                │    │ pods: get,list       │     │
  │  │ ✅ configmaps    │    └────────────────┘    └──────────────────────┘     │
  │  │ ✅ secrets       │                                                       │
  │  │ ❌ delete pods   │                                                       │
  │  │ ❌ create deploy │                                                       │
  │  └──────────────────┘                                                       │
  │                                                                              │
  │  ┌──────────────────┐    ┌────────────────┐    ┌──────────────────────┐     │
  │  │ 📦 anihpj-worker │    │ No binding     │    │ No permissions       │     │
  │  │                  │    │                │    │                      │     │
  │  │ SA: anihpj-sa    │    │ automount:     │    │ Token not mounted    │     │
  │  │ automount: false │    │ false          │    │ Can't call API       │     │
  │  │                  │    │                │    │ 🔒 Maximum security  │     │
  │  │ ❌ everything    │    └────────────────┘    └──────────────────────┘     │
  │  └──────────────────┘                                                       │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
bind-sa-complete.sh 
# Complete SA + RBAC setup (imperative — exam speed!)

# 1. Create ServiceAccount
kubectl create sa anihpj-sa -n anihpj

# 2. Create Role
kubectl create role anihpj-app-role \
  --verb=get,list,watch \
  --resource=configmaps,secrets,pods \
  -n anihpj

# 3. Bind SA to Role
kubectl create rolebinding anihpj-sa-binding \
  --role=anihpj-app-role \
  --serviceaccount=anihpj:anihpj-sa \
  -n anihpj

# 4. Verify permissions
kubectl auth can-i get configmaps \
  --as=system:serviceaccount:anihpj:anihpj-sa \
  -n anihpj
# yes ✅

kubectl auth can-i delete pods \
  --as=system:serviceaccount:anihpj:anihpj-sa \
  -n anihpj
# no ❌ (least privilege working!)

# 5. List all SA permissions
kubectl auth can-i --list \
  --as=system:serviceaccount:anihpj:anihpj-sa \
  -n anihpj

✅ SA Best Practices

  • One SA per application/workload
  • automountServiceAccountToken: false if no API access needed
  • Bind with specific Role, not cluster-admin
  • Use projected tokens (K8s 1.22+)
  • Always verify with can-i

❌ SA Anti-Patterns

  • Using default SA for everything
  • Binding cluster-admin to a SA
  • Leaving auto-mount on when not needed
  • Sharing SAs across unrelated apps
  • Using legacy Secret-based tokens

Service Account Verification Checklist

  • kubectl get sa -n NS → confirm SA exists
  • kubectl describe sa SA_NAME -n NS → check token and secrets
  • kubectl auth can-i VERB RESOURCE --as=system:serviceaccount:NS:SA -n NS → verify RBAC
  • kubectl exec POD -- ls /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/ → check token mount
  • kubectl exec POD -- cat /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/token → inspect mounted token
  • kubectl create token SA_NAME -n NS → verify TokenRequest API works

🏋️ Chapter 25 Practice Exercises

Complete each exercise in under 5 minutes to build CKA-level speed.

⏱️ Target: 5 minutes
Exercise 25.1 — Create SA with RBAC

Set up a ServiceAccount with read-only ConfigMap access in a dedicated namespace.

  1. Create namespace sa-practice
  2. Create SA app-sa in that namespace
  3. Create Role configmap-reader allowing get, list on configmaps
  4. Bind app-sa to the role
  5. Verify: kubectl auth can-i list configmaps --as=system:serviceaccount:sa-practice:app-sa -n sa-practice → yes
💡 Hint: Use --serviceaccount=NS:SA in rolebinding command, not --user.
📝 View Solution 
# Step 1: Create namespace
kubectl create namespace sa-practice

# Step 2: Create ServiceAccount
kubectl create sa app-sa -n sa-practice

# Step 3: Create Role
kubectl create role configmap-reader \
  --verb=get,list \
  --resource=configmaps \
  -n sa-practice

# Step 4: Bind SA to Role
kubectl create rolebinding app-sa-cm-binding \
  --role=configmap-reader \
  --serviceaccount=sa-practice:app-sa \
  -n sa-practice

# Step 5: Verify
kubectl auth can-i list configmaps \
  --as=system:serviceaccount:sa-practice:app-sa \
  -n sa-practice
# yes
⏱️ Target: 4 minutes
Exercise 25.2 — Disable Token Auto-Mount

Deploy a pod that does NOT mount a ServiceAccount token (for pods that don't need API access).

  1. Create a pod no-api-pod using SA app-sa
  2. Set automountServiceAccountToken: false
  3. Exec into the pod and verify: ls /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/ → should fail (directory doesn't exist)
💡 Hint: automountServiceAccountToken goes at spec level, not container level.
📝 View Solution 
# Create the pod with no token mount
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: no-api-pod
  namespace: sa-practice
spec:
  serviceAccountName: app-sa
  automountServiceAccountToken: false
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: busybox
    command: ["sleep", "3600"]
EOF

# Wait for pod to be ready
kubectl wait --for=condition=ready pod/no-api-pod -n sa-practice

# Verify no token is mounted
kubectl exec no-api-pod -n sa-practice -- \
  ls /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/
# ls: /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/: No such file or directory
⏱️ Target: 3 minutes
Exercise 25.3 — Create Short-Lived Token

Generate a time-limited token using the TokenRequest API and inspect its expiration.

  1. Create a token for app-sa valid for 10 minutes: kubectl create token app-sa -n sa-practice --duration=600s
  2. Decode the JWT payload: pipe through cut -d. -f2 | base64 -d
  3. Verify the exp field is ~10 minutes from iat
💡 Hint: JWT has 3 dot-separated parts (header.payload.signature). The payload (part 2) is base64-encoded JSON.
📝 View Solution 
# Step 1: Create a 10-minute token
TOKEN=$(kubectl create token app-sa -n sa-practice --duration=600s)

# Step 2: Decode the JWT payload (part 2)
echo $TOKEN | cut -d. -f2 | base64 -d 2>/dev/null | jq .
# Output includes:
# {
#   "iss": "https://kubernetes.default.svc",
#   "sub": "system:serviceaccount:sa-practice:app-sa",
#   "iat": 1700000000,      ← issued at
#   "exp": 1700000600,      ← expires (iat + 600s)
#   ...
# }

# Step 3: Verify expiration (exp - iat should be 600)
echo $TOKEN | cut -d. -f2 | base64 -d 2>/dev/null | \
  jq '{iat, exp, duration_seconds: (.exp - .iat)}'
# duration_seconds: 600

⚠️ CKA Gotcha: Service Account Traps

  • Default SA has no useful permissions — Every namespace has a default ServiceAccount, but it can't do much. The exam expects you to create dedicated SAs and bind RBAC roles to them
  • K8s 1.24+ changed token behavior — Tokens are no longer auto-mounted as persistent secrets. Use kubectl create token <sa> for time-limited tokens. The exam uses 1.29+, so use the new approach
  • automountServiceAccountToken: false — Set this on pods that don't need API access (most app pods). This is a security hardening best practice the exam may ask about
  • SA name in pod spec — Use serviceAccountName (NOT the deprecated serviceAccount field). The SA must exist before the pod is created or the pod stays Pending
  • Token audience and expiry — kubectl create token sa-name --duration=3600s --audience=https://myapi. Projected tokens have expiry, bound to specific audiences. Know this for security questions

CKA Speed Tip — Service Account workflow:
kubectl create sa my-sa -n prod
kubectl create role my-role --verb=get,list --resource=pods -n prod
kubectl create rolebinding my-rb --role=my-role --serviceaccount=prod:my-sa -n prod
Note the SA format in rolebinding: --serviceaccount=NAMESPACE:SA-NAME

⚡ Service Account commands: kubectl create sa <name> -n <ns> — create service account
kubectl create token <sa-name> -n <ns> — generate a bound token
kubectl get sa -n <ns> — list service accounts
kubectl auth can-i get pods --as=system:serviceaccount:<ns>:<sa> — test SA permissions
kubectl set sa deployment/<name> <sa-name> — assign SA to existing deployment
🔥

Scenario: "Pod Can't Read ConfigMaps — 403 Forbidden"

CKA EXAM

Symptom: anihpj pod logs show Error: configmaps "app-config" is forbidden: User "system:serviceaccount:anihpj:default" cannot get resource

Step 1 — Identify

The pod is using the default SA, which has no permissions. It needs a custom SA with RBAC.

Step 2 — Create SA + Role + Binding

kubectl create sa anihpj-sa -n anihpj
kubectl create role cm-reader --verb=get,list --resource=configmaps -n anihpj
kubectl create rolebinding cm-rb --role=cm-reader --serviceaccount=anihpj:anihpj-sa -n anihpj

Step 3 — Assign SA to Pod

kubectl set sa deployment/anihpj anihpj-sa -n anihpj — pod restarts with new SA, ConfigMap reads succeed.

Lesson: Never rely on the default SA for API access. Create dedicated SAs with least-privilege RBAC. The CKA tests this workflow frequently.

📝 Chapter 25 — CKA Practice Questions

Q1
Create a ServiceAccount named deploy-sa in namespace production. Then create a pod that uses this ServiceAccount.
Show Answer
Answer
# Create ServiceAccount:
kubectl create sa deploy-sa -n production

# Create pod using the SA:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: deploy-pod
  namespace: production
spec:
  serviceAccountName: deploy-sa    # ← Use custom SA
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx

# Or imperatively with dry-run and edit:
kubectl run deploy-pod --image=nginx -n production \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml > pod.yaml
# Add serviceAccountName: deploy-sa to spec
kubectl apply -f pod.yaml

# Verify:
kubectl get pod deploy-pod -n production \
  -o jsonpath='{.spec.serviceAccountName}'
# deploy-sa

# Check token is mounted:
kubectl exec deploy-pod -n production -- \
  ls /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/
# ca.crt  namespace  token
Explanation

ServiceAccounts provide pod identity for API access. Use serviceAccountName (not the deprecated serviceAccount). The SA must exist BEFORE the pod — otherwise the pod stays Pending. The token, CA cert, and namespace are auto-mounted at /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/. On the CKA, creating a SA, binding RBAC, and using it in a pod is a common multi-step task.

Q2
How do you prevent a pod from automatically mounting a ServiceAccount token?
Show Answer
Answer
# Method 1: At the pod level (overrides SA setting):
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: no-token-pod
spec:
  automountServiceAccountToken: false  # ← Pod level
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx

# Method 2: At the ServiceAccount level (affects all pods using it):
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: no-api-sa
  namespace: default
automountServiceAccountToken: false    # ← SA level

# Verify no token mounted:
kubectl exec no-token-pod -- \
  ls /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/ 2>&1
# ls: No such file or directory ← Token not mounted!

# When to use this:
# - App pods that never call the Kubernetes API
# - Security hardening (principle of least privilege)
# - Reducing attack surface if pod is compromised

# Pod-level overrides SA-level:
# SA: automount=false + Pod: automount=true → token IS mounted
# SA: automount=true + Pod: automount=false → token NOT mounted
Explanation

Set automountServiceAccountToken: false at pod or SA level. Pod-level overrides SA-level. On the CKA, this is a security hardening best practice — most application pods don't need API access. If a compromised pod has a token, an attacker could interact with the Kubernetes API. Disabling auto-mount reduces this risk. Set this on ALL pods that don't need API access.

Q3
Create a ServiceAccount, bind a Role to it, and verify it can list pods but not delete them.
Show Answer
Answer
# Step 1: Create SA
kubectl create sa app-sa -n dev

# Step 2: Create Role
kubectl create role pod-lister \
  --verb=get,list,watch \
  --resource=pods \
  -n dev

# Step 3: Bind Role to SA
# Note: SA format is namespace:name
kubectl create rolebinding app-sa-binding \
  --role=pod-lister \
  --serviceaccount=dev:app-sa \
  -n dev

# Step 4: Verify
kubectl auth can-i list pods \
  --as=system:serviceaccount:dev:app-sa -n dev
# yes

kubectl auth can-i delete pods \
  --as=system:serviceaccount:dev:app-sa -n dev
# no

kubectl auth can-i list pods \
  --as=system:serviceaccount:dev:app-sa -n production
# no ← Only in dev!

# Step 5: Create pod with this SA
kubectl run test-pod --image=nginx -n dev \
  --overrides='{"spec":{"serviceAccountName":"app-sa"}}'
Explanation

The full SA workflow: (1) create SA, (2) create Role, (3) bind with RoleBinding using --serviceaccount=NAMESPACE:NAME. For auth can-i, the SA format is system:serviceaccount:NAMESPACE:NAME. On the CKA, this three-step workflow is heavily tested. Common mistakes: forgetting the namespace prefix in --serviceaccount, or using the wrong format in --as. --overrides with kubectl run is the fastest way to set a SA imperatively.

Q4
How do you generate a short-lived token for a ServiceAccount? What changed in Kubernetes 1.24+?
Show Answer
Answer
# Generate a time-limited token (1.24+ way):
kubectl create token deploy-sa -n production
# eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIs...  ← JWT token (1 hour default)

# Custom duration:
kubectl create token deploy-sa -n production --duration=3600s
# Token valid for 1 hour

# Custom audience:
kubectl create token deploy-sa -n production \
  --audience=https://my-api.example.com

# Pre-1.24 (old way — still works but deprecated):
# Secrets with type kubernetes.io/service-account-token
# were auto-created for each SA. No longer auto-created!

# If you need a long-lived token (NOT recommended):
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: deploy-sa-token
  namespace: production
  annotations:
    kubernetes.io/service-account.name: deploy-sa
type: kubernetes.io/service-account-token
# K8s auto-populates the token data

kubectl get secret deploy-sa-token -n production \
  -o jsonpath='{.data.token}' | base64 -d
Explanation

Kubernetes 1.24+ removed auto-creation of Secret-based tokens. Now use kubectl create token for time-limited, projected tokens (more secure). On the CKA (K8s 1.29+), use kubectl create token — it generates a JWT with automatic expiration. For legacy compatibility, you can manually create a Secret with type kubernetes.io/service-account-token and annotate it with the SA name. But the exam prefers the new approach: short-lived, audience-bound tokens.

Q5
How do you assign a ServiceAccount to an existing Deployment without editing the YAML?
Show Answer
Answer
# Use kubectl set:
kubectl set sa deployment/web-app deploy-sa -n production
# deployment.apps/web-app serviceaccount updated

# This triggers a rolling update (new pods get the SA)

# Verify:
kubectl get deployment web-app -n production \
  -o jsonpath='{.spec.template.spec.serviceAccountName}'
# deploy-sa

# Alternative: kubectl patch
kubectl patch deployment web-app -n production \
  -p '{"spec":{"template":{"spec":{"serviceAccountName":"deploy-sa"}}}}'

# Check the new pods:
kubectl get pods -n production -l app=web-app
# NAME                      READY  STATUS   RESTARTS  AGE
# web-app-6f7b8c-new1       1/1    Running  0         10s ← New pod with SA

kubectl exec web-app-6f7b8c-new1 -n production -- \
  cat /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/token | cut -d. -f2 | base64 -d
# {"iss":"https://kubernetes.default.svc","sub":"system:serviceaccount:production:deploy-sa"...}
Explanation

kubectl set sa is the fastest way to assign a SA to a Deployment. It triggers a rolling update — old pods are replaced with new ones that use the new SA. On the CKA, this is faster than editing YAML. The SA must exist before assignment, or new pods will fail to start. You can verify by checking the token's sub (subject) field inside the pod.

Q6
What files are automatically mounted in a pod from its ServiceAccount? Where are they?
Show Answer
Answer
# Mounted at: /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/
kubectl exec my-pod -- ls -la \
  /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/
# lrwxrwxrwx 1  token     → projected token (JWT)
# lrwxrwxrwx 1  ca.crt    → cluster CA certificate
# lrwxrwxrwx 1  namespace → pod's namespace name

# token: JWT for API authentication
kubectl exec my-pod -- cat \
  /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/token
# eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIs...

# ca.crt: Used to verify API server's TLS certificate
kubectl exec my-pod -- cat \
  /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/ca.crt
# -----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----
# MIICyDCCAbC...

# namespace: The pod's namespace
kubectl exec my-pod -- cat \
  /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/namespace
# production

# Using these from inside a pod:
kubectl exec my-pod -- sh -c '
TOKEN=$(cat /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/token)
curl -s --cacert /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/ca.crt \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  https://kubernetes.default.svc/api/v1/namespaces/production/pods
'
Explanation

Three files are projected: token (JWT for authentication), ca.crt (CA to verify API server TLS), namespace (pod's namespace). In K8s 1.24+, the token is a *projected* volume with automatic rotation and expiry. On the CKA, knowing this path is important for: (1) debugging API access from pods, (2) security hardening (disable with automountServiceAccountToken: false), (3) understanding how pods authenticate to the API server.

Q7
How do you attach imagePullSecrets to a ServiceAccount so all pods using it can pull from a private registry?
Show Answer
Answer
# Step 1: Create the docker-registry secret:
kubectl create secret docker-registry regcred \
  --docker-server=registry.example.com \
  --docker-username=myuser \
  --docker-password=mypass \
  -n production

# Step 2: Attach to ServiceAccount:
kubectl patch sa deploy-sa -n production \
  -p '{"imagePullSecrets": [{"name": "regcred"}]}'

# Verify:
kubectl get sa deploy-sa -n production -o yaml
# imagePullSecrets:
# - name: regcred

# Now ALL pods using deploy-sa automatically inherit imagePullSecrets!
# No need to add imagePullSecrets to each pod spec

# Alternative: attach to default SA (affects ALL pods in ns):
kubectl patch sa default -n production \
  -p '{"imagePullSecrets": [{"name": "regcred"}]}'
Explanation

Attaching imagePullSecrets to a ServiceAccount means every pod using that SA inherits the credentials — no need to specify imagePullSecrets in each pod spec. On the CKA, patching the default SA in a namespace affects all pods in that namespace (quick fix for ImagePullBackOff on private images). This is more maintainable than adding secrets to every Deployment.

Q8
What is the difference between serviceAccountName and serviceAccount in a pod spec?
Show Answer
Answer
# ✓ Correct (use this):
spec:
  serviceAccountName: deploy-sa   # ← Current field

# ✗ Deprecated (do NOT use):
spec:
  serviceAccount: deploy-sa       # ← Deprecated since 1.16

# Both work, but:
# - serviceAccountName is the official field
# - serviceAccount is deprecated and may be removed
# - If both are set and different → error
# - CKA expects serviceAccountName

# Quick check which field a pod uses:
kubectl get pod my-pod -o jsonpath='{.spec.serviceAccountName}'
# deploy-sa

# Default SA:
# If no serviceAccountName is specified, pods use "default" SA
kubectl get pod unset-pod -o jsonpath='{.spec.serviceAccountName}'
# default
Explanation

Always use serviceAccountName — serviceAccount is deprecated. On the CKA, using the deprecated field may not cause errors, but it's bad practice. If you see both in a YAML and they differ, the API server rejects the pod. When no SA is specified, pods use the default ServiceAccount in their namespace.

Q9
A pod using a custom ServiceAccount can't call the Kubernetes API. How do you debug?
Show Answer
Answer
# Step 1: Check SA assignment
kubectl get pod api-pod -o jsonpath='{.spec.serviceAccountName}'
# deploy-sa ← Correct SA

# Step 2: Check token is mounted
kubectl exec api-pod -- ls /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/
# ca.crt  namespace  token  ← Token present

# Step 3: Check SA permissions
kubectl auth can-i list pods \
  --as=system:serviceaccount:default:deploy-sa
# no ← Missing RBAC!

# Step 4: Check for RoleBindings
kubectl get rolebindings -o wide | grep deploy-sa
# (empty) ← No binding!

# Step 5: Fix — create Role and RoleBinding
kubectl create role api-reader \
  --verb=get,list --resource=pods
kubectl create rolebinding api-rb \
  --role=api-reader \
  --serviceaccount=default:deploy-sa

# Step 6: Verify
kubectl auth can-i list pods \
  --as=system:serviceaccount:default:deploy-sa
# yes

# Step 7: Check automountServiceAccountToken
kubectl get pod api-pod -o yaml | grep automount
# automountServiceAccountToken: false  ← Token not mounted!
# Fix: set to true or remove the field
Explanation

SA API access debugging: (1) verify SA is assigned, (2) check token is mounted, (3) test permissions with auth can-i, (4) find/create RBAC bindings, (5) check automountServiceAccountToken. On the CKA, the most common issue is: SA exists and is assigned, but no Role/RoleBinding grants it permissions. The default SA has no useful permissions — you must always create RBAC explicitly.

Q10
What is a projected ServiceAccount token and how does it differ from legacy tokens?
Show Answer
Answer
Feature Legacy Token (pre-1.24) Projected Token (1.24+)
Storage Stored in Secret object Projected volume (not in Secret)
Expiry Never expires Expires (default 1h, auto-rotated)
Audience Any API server Bound to specific audience
Auto-created Yes (auto Secret per SA) No Secret created
Security Risky (leaked = permanent) Better (expires, rotates) 
# Projected token is auto-rotated by kubelet
# Check token expiry from inside pod:
kubectl exec my-pod -- cat \
  /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/token \
  | cut -d. -f2 | base64 -d 2>/dev/null | python3 -m json.tool
# "exp": 1735689600  ← Expiry timestamp
Explanation

Projected tokens (K8s 1.24+) are more secure: they expire, are bound to a specific audience, and are auto-rotated by the kubelet. Legacy tokens were stored as Secrets and never expired — a leaked token gave permanent access. On the CKA (K8s 1.29+), projected tokens are the default. If a question asks about token security, mention: time-limited, audience-bound, auto-rotated. For legacy apps needing long-lived tokens, manually create a Secret with the SA annotation.

Chapter 26: TLS Certificates and PKI

Every component in a Kubernetes cluster communicates over TLS-encrypted connections. The API server, etcd, kubelet, scheduler, and controller-manager all present certificates to prove their identity. For the CKA exam, you must understand where these certificates live, how to inspect them, how to approve Certificate Signing Requests (CSRs), check expiry, and renew certificates using kubeadm. This chapter covers the entire Kubernetes PKI (Public Key Infrastructure) from root CA to individual component certificates.

🟡 Intermediate ⏱️ ~1.5 hours 🔐 TLS/PKI 📝 CKA EXAM

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ The Kubernetes PKI architecture — what the CA signs and why
  • ✅ Certificate file locations under /etc/kubernetes/pki/
  • ✅ How to create and approve Certificate Signing Requests (CSRs)
  • ✅ Checking certificate expiry with kubeadm certs check-expiration
  • ✅ Renewing certificates with kubeadm certs renew
🎓 CKA Relevance
Cluster Architecture, Installation & Configuration 25% Troubleshooting 30%
TLS/Certificates on the CKA Exam

Certificate questions are frequently tested. You must be able to:

  • Generate a private key, create a CSR, submit it to Kubernetes, approve it, and extract the certificate
  • Know certificate file locations: /etc/kubernetes/pki/
  • Check certificate expiry with kubeadm certs check-expiration
  • Renew certificates with kubeadm certs renew all
  • Use openssl x509 to inspect certificate details

TLS Certificate Commands

ActionCommand
Generate private keyopenssl genrsa -out user.key 2048
Create CSRopenssl req -new -key user.key -subj "/CN=dev-user/O=dev-team" -out user.csr
Submit K8s CSRkubectl apply -f csr.yaml
Approve CSRkubectl certificate approve dev-user
Deny CSRkubectl certificate deny dev-user
Get signed certkubectl get csr dev-user -o jsonpath='{.status.certificate}' | base64 -d > user.crt
Check expirykubeadm certs check-expiration
Renew all certskubeadm certs renew all
Inspect certopenssl x509 -in /etc/kubernetes/pki/apiserver.crt -text -noout

CertificateSigningRequest — Exam Skeleton

CSR creation is a common CKA task. Know this pattern cold.

# Step 1: Generate key + CSR (on command line)
openssl genrsa -out dev-user.key 2048
openssl req -new -key dev-user.key \
  -subj "/CN=dev-user/O=dev-team" \
  -out dev-user.csr

# Step 2: Create Kubernetes CSR object
apiVersion: certificates.k8s.io/v1
kind: CertificateSigningRequest
metadata:
  name: dev-user                         # ◀── CSR name
spec:
  request: $(cat dev-user.csr | base64 | tr -d '\n')  # ◀── base64-encoded CSR
  signerName: kubernetes.io/kube-apiserver-client       # ◀── REQUIRED signer
  usages:
  - client auth                          # ◀── for user authentication
  expirationSeconds: 86400               # ◀── optional: 24 hours

# Step 3: Approve and extract
# kubectl certificate approve dev-user
# kubectl get csr dev-user -o jsonpath='{.status.certificate}' | base64 -d > dev-user.crt

Certificate Exam Traps

  • ❌ CSR request field takes the raw CSR file content
  • ✅ The request field requires base64-encoded CSR content
  • signerName is required since K8s 1.22 — use kubernetes.io/kube-apiserver-client for user certs
  • Certificates expire after 1 year by default — kubeadm certs check-expiration shows all dates
  • After renewing certs, you MUST restart control plane pods (they load certs at startup)
  • The CN (Common Name) in the CSR becomes the username in K8s. The O (Organization) becomes the group
  • /etc/kubernetes/pki/ is the default cert location — memorize this path

Find Certificate Fields Fast

kubectl explain csr.spec                       # CSR spec
kubectl explain csr.spec.request               # base64-encoded CSR
kubectl explain csr.spec.signerName            # required signer
kubectl explain csr.spec.usages                # client auth, server auth, etc.
kubectl explain csr.spec.expirationSeconds     # cert lifetime
6+ Component Certificates
1 yr Default Cert Lifetime
/etc/kubernetes/pki/ Default Cert Location
1 Root CA Signs Everything

26.1 Kubernetes PKI Overview

PKI (Public Key Infrastructure) is the system of certificates that secure all communication in a Kubernetes cluster. At the top is a Root Certificate Authority (CA) that signs certificates for every cluster component.

Kubernetes PKI Architecture — The Certificate Tree
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                      KUBERNETES PKI HIERARCHY                                │
  │                                                                              │
  │                    ┌──────────────────────┐                                  │
  │                    │   🏛️ ROOT CA          │                                  │
  │                    │   /etc/kubernetes/    │                                  │
  │                    │   pki/ca.crt          │                                  │
  │                    │       ca.key          │                                  │
  │                    └──────────┬───────────┘                                  │
  │                               │ signs                                        │
  │          ┌────────────────────┼────────────────────┐                         │
  │          │                    │                    │                         │
  │          ▼                    ▼                    ▼                         │
  │  ┌───────────────┐   ┌───────────────┐   ┌───────────────┐                 │
  │  │ 🌐 API Server │   │ 💾 etcd       │   │ 📡 kubelet    │                 │
  │  │               │   │               │   │               │                 │
  │  │ apiserver.crt │   │ server.crt   │   │ kubelet.crt   │                 │
  │  │ apiserver.key │   │ server.key   │   │ kubelet.key   │                 │
  │  │               │   │               │   │               │                 │
  │  │ Also needs:   │   │ peer.crt     │   │ Authenticates │                 │
  │  │ kubelet-client│   │ peer.key     │   │ to API server │                 │
  │  │ .crt/.key     │   │               │   └───────────────┘                 │
  │  └───────────────┘   └───────────────┘                                      │
  │          │                                                                   │
  │          │ also serves as client to:                                         │
  │          ▼                                                                   │
  │  ┌───────────────┐   ┌───────────────┐   ┌───────────────┐                 │
  │  │ 📋 scheduler  │   │ 🔄 controller │   │ 🌐 front-proxy│                 │
  │  │               │   │   manager     │   │               │                 │
  │  │ Uses kubeconf │   │ Uses kubeconf │   │ front-proxy-  │                 │
  │  │ with embedded │   │ with embedded │   │ ca.crt/key    │                 │
  │  │ client cert   │   │ client cert   │   │ (aggregation) │                 │
  │  └───────────────┘   └───────────────┘   └───────────────┘                 │
  │                                                                              │
  │  📁 All certificates live under: /etc/kubernetes/pki/                        │
  │  📁 etcd certs specifically: /etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/                       │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
🔒
Encryption

All component communication uses TLS — no plaintext data ever crosses the network.

🪪
Identity
