Certificates prove component identity. The API server verifies kubelet is who it claims to be.

🤝
Mutual TLS

Both sides present certificates. API server authenticates kubelet AND kubelet authenticates API server.

26.2 Certificate Locations and Purpose

Every certificate in the Kubernetes cluster has a specific purpose. For the CKA, you must know where they live and what they do.

File Used By Purpose
ca.crt / ca.keyCluster CARoot CA — signs all other certificates
apiserver.crt / .keyAPI ServerServe HTTPS on port 6443
apiserver-kubelet-client.crtAPI ServerAPI server authenticating to kubelets
apiserver-etcd-client.crtAPI ServerAPI server authenticating to etcd
etcd/server.crt / .keyetcdetcd serving TLS to API server
etcd/peer.crt / .keyetcdetcd-to-etcd peer communication (HA)
front-proxy-ca.crtAggregationAPI aggregation layer (metrics server)
sa.pub / sa.keySA tokensSigning/verifying ServiceAccount tokens
inspect-certificates.sh 
# List all certificate files
ls -la /etc/kubernetes/pki/
# ca.crt  ca.key  apiserver.crt  apiserver.key  ...
ls -la /etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/
# ca.crt  ca.key  server.crt  server.key  peer.crt  peer.key

# Inspect API server certificate details
openssl x509 -in /etc/kubernetes/pki/apiserver.crt -text -noout

# Key fields to check:
# - Issuer: kubernetes (the CA that signed it)
# - Subject: CN=kube-apiserver
# - Not After: expiry date
# - Subject Alternative Names (SANs): all valid hostnames/IPs

# Check who signed a certificate
openssl x509 -in /etc/kubernetes/pki/apiserver.crt -issuer -noout
# issuer=CN = kubernetes

# Verify cert is signed by the CA
openssl verify -CAfile /etc/kubernetes/pki/ca.crt \
  /etc/kubernetes/pki/apiserver.crt
# /etc/kubernetes/pki/apiserver.crt: OK
Certificate File Layout on Disk
  /etc/kubernetes/
  ├── pki/                                 ◀── Main PKI directory
  │   ├── ca.crt                           ◀── Root CA certificate
  │   ├── ca.key                           ◀── Root CA private key 🔒
  │   ├── apiserver.crt                    ◀── API server TLS cert
  │   ├── apiserver.key                    ◀── API server private key 🔒
  │   ├── apiserver-kubelet-client.crt     ◀── API→kubelet client cert
  │   ├── apiserver-kubelet-client.key     ◀── API→kubelet private key 🔒
  │   ├── apiserver-etcd-client.crt        ◀── API→etcd client cert
  │   ├── apiserver-etcd-client.key        ◀── API→etcd private key 🔒
  │   ├── front-proxy-ca.crt              ◀── Aggregation CA
  │   ├── front-proxy-ca.key              ◀── Aggregation CA key 🔒
  │   ├── front-proxy-client.crt          ◀── Aggregation client cert
  │   ├── front-proxy-client.key          ◀── Aggregation client key 🔒
  │   ├── sa.pub                           ◀── SA token verification key
  │   ├── sa.key                           ◀── SA token signing key 🔒
  │   └── etcd/                            ◀── etcd-specific certs
  │       ├── ca.crt                       ◀── etcd CA (may be separate)
  │       ├── ca.key
  │       ├── server.crt                   ◀── etcd server TLS
  │       ├── server.key
  │       ├── peer.crt                     ◀── etcd peer communication
  │       ├── peer.key
  │       ├── healthcheck-client.crt       ◀── etcd health checks
  │       └── healthcheck-client.key
  ├── admin.conf                           ◀── cluster-admin kubeconfig
  ├── controller-manager.conf              ◀── controller-manager kubeconfig
  ├── scheduler.conf                       ◀── scheduler kubeconfig
  └── kubelet.conf                         ◀── kubelet kubeconfig
⚠️ Private Keys Are Secrets

The .key files are private keys — anyone with access can impersonate that component. In production, restrict access to /etc/kubernetes/pki/ to root only. On the CKA exam, you'll SSH into the control plane node to read these files.

X.509 Certificate Fields — What to Read

👤 Subject (CN)
CN=kube-apiserver — The Common Name identifies the component. For user certs, CN becomes the username in Kubernetes RBAC.
👥 Subject (O)
O=system:masters — The Organization becomes the group. system:masters maps to cluster-admin.
🏛️ Issuer
CN=kubernetes — The CA that signed this certificate. All cluster certs should show the same issuer.
📅 Not After
Expiry date. Default 1 year for component certs. kubeadm certs check-expiration shows all expiry dates at once.
🌐 SANs
Subject Alternative Names — list of valid hostnames & IPs (kubernetes, kubernetes.default.svc, 10.96.0.1, control plane IP).

26.3 Certificate Signing Requests (CSR)

To add a new user to the cluster, you create a CSR and have the K8s CA sign it. This process is heavily tested on the CKA exam.

1
Generate Key

openssl genrsa -out user.key 2048

2
Create CSR

openssl req -new -key user.key -subj "/CN=dev-user/O=dev-team"

3
Submit to K8s

Create CertificateSigningRequest YAML with base64-encoded CSR

4
Approve

kubectl certificate approve dev-user

5
Extract Cert

Get the signed certificate from the CSR's .status.certificate

csr-complete-workflow.sh 
# ── COMPLETE CSR WORKFLOW — CKA EXAM PATTERN ──

# Step 1: Generate a private key
openssl genrsa -out dev-user.key 2048

# Step 2: Create a Certificate Signing Request
# CN = username, O = group (for RBAC)
openssl req -new -key dev-user.key \
  -subj "/CN=dev-user/O=dev-team" \
  -out dev-user.csr

# Step 3: Encode the CSR in base64 (single line, no wrapping)
CSR_BASE64=$(cat dev-user.csr | base64 | tr -d '\n')

# Step 4: Create the K8s CSR YAML
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: certificates.k8s.io/v1
kind: CertificateSigningRequest
metadata:
  name: dev-user
spec:
  request: ${CSR_BASE64}
  signerName: kubernetes.io/kube-apiserver-client
  usages:
  - client auth
EOF

# Step 5: Check CSR status
kubectl get csr
# NAME       AGE   SIGNERNAME                          CONDITION
# dev-user   5s    kubernetes.io/kube-apiserver-client  Pending

# Step 6: Approve the CSR
kubectl certificate approve dev-user

# Step 7: Extract the signed certificate
kubectl get csr dev-user -o jsonpath='{.status.certificate}' \
  | base64 -d > dev-user.crt

# Step 8: Verify the certificate
openssl x509 -in dev-user.crt -text -noout
# Subject: CN=dev-user, O=dev-team

# Step 9: Add user to kubeconfig
kubectl config set-credentials dev-user \
  --client-certificate=dev-user.crt \
  --client-key=dev-user.key

kubectl config set-context dev-user@kubernetes \
  --cluster=kubernetes \
  --user=dev-user \
  --namespace=anihpj
💡 CKA Shortcut: CSR Encoding

Use cat dev-user.csr | base64 | tr -d '\n' to get the single-line base64 string for the CSR YAML. The tr -d '\n' removes line breaks that would break the YAML.

CSR Approval Flow — From Key to Kubeconfig

1
Generate Private Key

openssl genrsa -out user.key 2048 — creates RSA private key (stays with the user, never shared)

▼
2
Create CSR File

openssl req -new -key user.key -subj "/CN=user/O=group" — CN becomes username, O becomes group in RBAC

▼
3
Submit to Kubernetes

Base64-encode the CSR → create CertificateSigningRequest YAML → kubectl apply. Status: Pending

▼
4
Admin Approves

kubectl certificate approve user — K8s CA signs the cert. Status: Approved, Issued

▼
5
Extract & Use

Extract signed cert from .status.certificate → add to kubeconfig with kubectl config set-credentials

26.4 Checking Certificate Expiry

Kubernetes certificates expire after 1 year by default. If they expire, cluster components stop communicating and the cluster goes down. Monitoring expiry is critical.

check-expiry.sh 
# ── METHOD 1: kubeadm (EASIEST — use on CKA exam) ──
kubeadm certs check-expiration
# CERTIFICATE                EXPIRES                  RESIDUAL TIME
# admin.conf                 Apr 01, 2027 00:00 UTC   364d
# apiserver                  Apr 01, 2027 00:00 UTC   364d
# apiserver-etcd-client      Apr 01, 2027 00:00 UTC   364d
# apiserver-kubelet-client   Apr 01, 2027 00:00 UTC   364d
# controller-manager.conf    Apr 01, 2027 00:00 UTC   364d
# etcd-healthcheck-client    Apr 01, 2027 00:00 UTC   364d
# etcd-peer                  Apr 01, 2027 00:00 UTC   364d
# etcd-server                Apr 01, 2027 00:00 UTC   364d
# front-proxy-client         Apr 01, 2027 00:00 UTC   364d
# scheduler.conf             Apr 01, 2027 00:00 UTC   364d

# ── METHOD 2: openssl (manual — for specific cert) ──
openssl x509 -in /etc/kubernetes/pki/apiserver.crt \
  -noout -enddate
# notAfter=Apr  1 00:00:00 2027 GMT

# ── METHOD 3: Check specific fields ──
openssl x509 -in /etc/kubernetes/pki/apiserver.crt \
  -noout -subject -issuer -dates
# subject=CN = kube-apiserver
# issuer=CN = kubernetes
# notBefore=Apr  1 00:00:00 2026 GMT
# notAfter=Apr  1 00:00:00 2027 GMT
🚨 Expired Certificates = Cluster Down

If the API server certificate expires, kubectl stops working entirely. If etcd certificates expire, the API server can't read/write state. Always check expiry at least monthly in production. On the CKA exam, kubeadm certs check-expiration is the answer.

26.5 Renewing Certificates

Certificate renewal is a critical cluster maintenance task and a common CKA exam topic.

renew-certificates.sh 
# ── Renew ALL certificates at once ──
kubeadm certs renew all
# [renew] Reading configuration from the cluster...
# certificate embedded in admin.conf renewed
# certificate apiserver renewed
# certificate apiserver-kubelet-client renewed
# ...

# ── Renew a SPECIFIC certificate ──
kubeadm certs renew apiserver
kubeadm certs renew apiserver-kubelet-client
kubeadm certs renew etcd-server

# ── CRITICAL: Restart control plane after renewal ──
# Control plane pods load certs at startup — they need restart!

# Option A: If using static pods (kubeadm default)
# Move and restore manifests to trigger restart:
cd /etc/kubernetes/manifests/
mv kube-apiserver.yaml /tmp/
# Wait for pod to stop...
mv /tmp/kube-apiserver.yaml .
# Repeat for controller-manager, scheduler, etcd

# Option B: Force restart via crictl
crictl pods --name kube-apiserver -q | xargs crictl stopp
crictl pods --name kube-controller-manager -q | xargs crictl stopp
crictl pods --name kube-scheduler -q | xargs crictl stopp

# ── Verify renewal worked ──
kubeadm certs check-expiration
# All dates should now show ~1 year from today
Certificate Renewal Workflow
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │              CERTIFICATE RENEWAL PROCEDURE                       │
  │                                                                  │
  │  ┌─────────────────┐                                            │
  │  │ 1. Check Expiry │─── kubeadm certs check-expiration          │
  │  └────────┬────────┘                                            │
  │           │                                                      │
  │           ▼                                                      │
  │  ┌─────────────────┐                                            │
  │  │ 2. Renew Certs  │─── kubeadm certs renew all                │
  │  └────────┬────────┘                                            │
  │           │                                                      │
  │           ▼                                                      │
  │  ┌─────────────────┐                                            │
  │  │ 3. Restart      │─── Move/restore manifests or crictl       │
  │  │    Components   │    (API server, controller, scheduler)     │
  │  └────────┬────────┘                                            │
  │           │                                                      │
  │           ▼                                                      │
  │  ┌─────────────────┐                                            │
  │  │ 4. Verify       │─── kubeadm certs check-expiration          │
  │  │    New Dates    │    (should show ~1 year from now)          │
  │  └────────┬────────┘                                            │
  │           │                                                      │
  │           ▼                                                      │
  │  ┌─────────────────┐                                            │
  │  │ 5. Update       │─── Copy new admin.conf to ~/.kube/config  │
  │  │    kubeconfig   │    cp /etc/kubernetes/admin.conf           │
  │  └─────────────────┘    ~/.kube/config                          │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
⚠️ Don't Forget kubeconfig!

After renewing certs, the kubeconfig files (admin.conf, etc.) also get updated. Copy the new admin.conf to ~/.kube/config or kubectl will stop working with "certificate expired" errors!

💡 Automatic Renewal

kubeadm upgrade automatically renews all certificates during a cluster upgrade. If you upgrade regularly (recommended), you may never need manual renewal.

Certificate Troubleshooting — Common Errors & Fixes
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │            CERTIFICATE TROUBLESHOOTING QUICK REFERENCE                       │
  │                                                                              │
  │  ❌ ERROR: "x509: certificate has expired or is not yet valid"              │
  │  ✅ FIX:   kubeadm certs renew all → restart control plane pods            │
  │                                                                              │
  │  ❌ ERROR: "unable to connect to the server: x509: certificate             │
  │            signed by unknown authority"                                      │
  │  ✅ FIX:   Copy updated admin.conf: cp /etc/kubernetes/admin.conf           │
  │            ~/.kube/config                                                    │
  │                                                                              │
  │  ❌ ERROR: "error: You must be logged in to the server (Unauthorized)"      │
  │  ✅ FIX:   Check certificate CN matches expected user. Verify CSR was       │
  │            approved. Check RBAC bindings.                                    │
  │                                                                              │
  │  ❌ ERROR: API server won't start after cert renewal                        │
  │  ✅ FIX:   Check /var/log/containers/ for kube-apiserver logs.              │
  │            Verify cert files exist in /etc/kubernetes/pki/.                  │
  │            Check file permissions (certs should be 644, keys 600).          │
  │                                                                              │
  │  ❌ ERROR: "tls: bad certificate" on etcd                                   │
  │  ✅ FIX:   Renew etcd-specific certs: kubeadm certs renew etcd-server      │
  │            kubeadm certs renew etcd-peer                                    │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

✅ Certificate DO

  • Check expiry monthly: kubeadm certs check-expiration
  • Always restart control plane after renewal
  • Update ~/.kube/config after renewing admin.conf
  • Use kubeadm upgrade — it renews certs automatically
  • Set correct CN/O in CSRs (CN=username, O=group)

❌ Certificate DON'T

  • Ignore certificate expiry warnings
  • Share private keys (.key files) across teams
  • Skip the signerName field on CSR objects (required since 1.22)
  • Forget to base64-encode the CSR request field
  • Assume certs renew themselves — only kubeadm upgrade auto-renews

TLS Certificate Verification Checklist

  • openssl x509 -in CERT -text -noout | grep -A1 Subject: → verify CN and O fields
  • openssl x509 -in CERT -text -noout | grep "Not After" → check expiry date
  • openssl verify -CAfile /etc/kubernetes/pki/ca.crt CERT → confirm signed by cluster CA
  • kubeadm certs check-expiration → view all certificate expiry dates at once
  • kubectl get csr → check pending/approved CSR objects
  • kubectl describe csr NAME → verify signerName, status, and conditions

🏋️ Chapter 26 Practice Exercises

These exercises simulate real CKA certificate questions.

⏱️ Target: 8 minutes
Exercise 26.1 — Create User Certificate via CSR

Generate a private key, create a CSR, submit it to Kubernetes, approve it, and extract the signed certificate.

  1. Generate key: openssl genrsa -out exam-user.key 2048
  2. Create CSR: openssl req -new -key exam-user.key -subj "/CN=exam-user/O=exam-team"
  3. Submit K8s CSR object with signerName: kubernetes.io/kube-apiserver-client
  4. Approve: kubectl certificate approve exam-user
  5. Extract signed cert and verify CN with openssl x509 -text
💡 Hint: The CSR request field must be base64-encoded with no line wrapping — use base64 -w 0.
📝 View Solution 
# Step 1: Generate private key
openssl genrsa -out exam-user.key 2048

# Step 2: Create CSR (PEM format)
openssl req -new -key exam-user.key \
  -subj "/CN=exam-user/O=exam-team" \
  -out exam-user.csr

# Step 3: Base64-encode the CSR (no line wrapping!)
CSR_B64=$(cat exam-user.csr | base64 -w 0)

# Step 4: Create K8s CSR object
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: certificates.k8s.io/v1
kind: CertificateSigningRequest
metadata:
  name: exam-user
spec:
  request: ${CSR_B64}
  signerName: kubernetes.io/kube-apiserver-client
  usages:
  - client auth
EOF

# Step 5: Approve the CSR
kubectl certificate approve exam-user

# Step 6: Extract the signed certificate
kubectl get csr exam-user -o jsonpath='{.status.certificate}' \
  | base64 -d > exam-user.crt

# Step 7: Verify the certificate
openssl x509 -in exam-user.crt -text -noout | grep Subject
# Subject: O = exam-team, CN = exam-user
⏱️ Target: 3 minutes
Exercise 26.2 — Inspect API Server Certificate

Examine the API server's TLS certificate to identify key fields tested on the CKA exam.

  1. SSH to the control plane node
  2. Run: openssl x509 -in /etc/kubernetes/pki/apiserver.crt -text -noout
  3. Identify: Subject (CN), Issuer, expiry date, and Subject Alternative Names (SANs)
  4. Verify it was signed by the cluster CA: openssl verify -CAfile /etc/kubernetes/pki/ca.crt /etc/kubernetes/pki/apiserver.crt
💡 Hint: SANs are in the X509v3 Subject Alternative Name section of the certificate output.
📝 View Solution 
# SSH to control plane
ssh controlplane

# Inspect the API server certificate
openssl x509 -in /etc/kubernetes/pki/apiserver.crt -text -noout

# Key fields to identify:
# Subject: CN = kube-apiserver
# Issuer:  CN = kubernetes   (signed by cluster CA)
# Not After: (expiry date — typically 1 year)
# X509v3 Subject Alternative Name:
#   DNS:kubernetes, DNS:kubernetes.default,
#   DNS:kubernetes.default.svc,
#   DNS:kubernetes.default.svc.cluster.local,
#   IP Address:10.96.0.1, IP Address:192.168.x.x

# Verify CA signature
openssl verify -CAfile /etc/kubernetes/pki/ca.crt \
  /etc/kubernetes/pki/apiserver.crt
# apiserver.crt: OK
⏱️ Target: 5 minutes
Exercise 26.3 — Check and Renew Certificates

Check certificate expiry dates and renew the API server certificate.

  1. Check all certificate expiry dates: kubeadm certs check-expiration
  2. Renew the API server certificate only: kubeadm certs renew apiserver
  3. Restart the API server (move/restore static pod manifest)
  4. Verify the new expiry date matches today + 1 year
💡 Hint: Moving the manifest out of /etc/kubernetes/manifests/ stops the pod. Moving it back triggers a restart with the new cert.
📝 View Solution 
# Step 1: Check all certificate expiry dates
kubeadm certs check-expiration
# CERTIFICATE                EXPIRES                  RESIDUAL TIME
# admin.conf                 Jan 01, 2026 00:00 UTC   364d
# apiserver                  Jan 01, 2026 00:00 UTC   364d
# apiserver-kubelet-client   Jan 01, 2026 00:00 UTC   364d
# ...

# Step 2: Renew API server certificate only
kubeadm certs renew apiserver

# Step 3: Restart API server (static pod)
mv /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml /tmp/
# Wait for pod to stop (~10 seconds)
sleep 10
mv /tmp/kube-apiserver.yaml /etc/kubernetes/manifests/
# Wait for restart
sleep 30

# Step 4: Verify new expiry
openssl x509 -in /etc/kubernetes/pki/apiserver.crt -text -noout \
  | grep "Not After"
# Not After : (should be ~1 year from today)

# Verify API server is running
kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep apiserver

⚠️ CKA Gotcha: TLS & Certificate Traps

  • Certificate locations — All certs live under /etc/kubernetes/pki/. API server: apiserver.crt, apiserver-key.crt. CA: ca.crt, ca.key. etcd has its own directory: /etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/
  • CSR approval — kubectl certificate approve <csr-name>. The exam may ask you to create a CSR for a new user and approve it. Don't forget: the CSR object needs the right signerName (kubernetes.io/kube-apiserver-client)
  • Certificate expiry — kubeadm certs check-expiration shows all cert expiry dates. Certs default to 1 year. CA cert is 10 years. If a cert expires, that component stops working
  • After renewing certs, restart the component — For static pods (apiserver, controller-manager, scheduler): move the manifest out of /etc/kubernetes/manifests/, wait, move it back. This is the tested pattern
  • openssl x509 -in cert.crt -text -noout — Memorize this for inspecting any cert. Look at Subject, Issuer, Not After, and SANs (Subject Alternative Names)

CKA Speed Tip — Certificate inspection one-liners:
openssl x509 -in /etc/kubernetes/pki/apiserver.crt -text -noout | grep -A2 "Validity" — check expiry
openssl x509 -in /etc/kubernetes/pki/apiserver.crt -text -noout | grep -A1 "Alternative" — check SANs
kubeadm certs check-expiration — all certs at once
kubeadm certs renew all — renew everything (then restart control plane)

⚡ TLS/PKI commands: kubeadm certs check-expiration — show all cert expiry dates
kubeadm certs renew apiserver — renew a specific cert
kubectl get csr — list pending certificate signing requests
kubectl certificate approve <csr-name> — approve a CSR
kubectl certificate deny <csr-name> — deny a CSR
📝 Chapter 26 Practice Drill

Timed challenge (target: 8 minutes):

  • 1. Check all certificate expiration dates: kubeadm certs check-expiration
  • 2. Inspect the API server cert's SANs: openssl x509 -in /etc/kubernetes/pki/apiserver.crt -text -noout | grep -A1 "Alternative"
  • 3. Generate a private key and CSR for user "devuser": openssl genrsa -out devuser.key 2048 then openssl req -new -key devuser.key -subj "/CN=devuser/O=dev" -out devuser.csr
  • 4. Create and approve a Kubernetes CSR object for devuser
  • 5. Extract the signed certificate and create a kubeconfig for devuser
🔥

Scenario: "API Server Unreachable — Expired Certificates"

CKA EXAM

Symptom: kubectl get nodes returns Unable to connect to the server: x509: certificate has expired. The cluster appears completely down.

Step 1 — SSH to control plane

Can't use kubectl (API down). SSH directly: ssh controlplane. Check cert expiry: sudo kubeadm certs check-expiration — shows expired dates.

Step 2 — Renew certificates

sudo kubeadm certs renew all — regenerates all component certificates signed by the CA.

Step 3 — Restart control plane

Static pods don't auto-reload certs. Move manifests: sudo mv /etc/kubernetes/manifests/*.yaml /tmp/, wait 10s, move back. Or: sudo systemctl restart kubelet.

Step 4 — Update kubeconfig

sudo cp /etc/kubernetes/admin.conf ~/.kube/config — the admin.conf was also renewed. Now kubectl get nodes works again.

Lesson: Cert expiry is a silent killer — the cluster works fine until the exact moment certs expire, then everything breaks at once. Monitor with kubeadm certs check-expiration proactively.

📝 Chapter 26 — CKA Practice Questions

Q1
Where are the Kubernetes PKI certificates stored on a kubeadm cluster? List the key files and their purpose.
Show Answer
Answer
# Default PKI directory:
/etc/kubernetes/pki/

# Key files:
ls -la /etc/kubernetes/pki/
# ca.crt              ← Cluster CA certificate
# ca.key              ← Cluster CA private key (PROTECT THIS!)
# apiserver.crt       ← API server TLS certificate
# apiserver.key       ← API server TLS private key
# apiserver-kubelet-client.crt  ← API→kubelet auth
# apiserver-kubelet-client.key
# apiserver-etcd-client.crt     ← API→etcd auth
# apiserver-etcd-client.key
# front-proxy-ca.crt  ← Front proxy CA
# front-proxy-ca.key
# front-proxy-client.crt
# front-proxy-client.key
# sa.key              ← SA signing key
# sa.pub              ← SA verification key

# etcd certificates:
ls /etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/
# ca.crt, ca.key, server.crt, server.key,
# peer.crt, peer.key, healthcheck-client.crt/key

# Kubeconfig files (contain embedded certs):
ls /etc/kubernetes/
# admin.conf, controller-manager.conf,
# scheduler.conf, kubelet.conf
Explanation

All kubeadm PKI files live in /etc/kubernetes/pki/. The CA cert/key pair is the root of trust — ca.key must be protected. On the CKA, you need to know this path for: checking certificate expiry, renewing certificates, creating CSRs signed by the cluster CA, and troubleshooting TLS errors. The etcd subdirectory has separate CA and certificates for etcd mTLS.

Q2
How do you check when Kubernetes certificates expire using kubeadm?
Show Answer
Answer
# Check ALL certificate expirations:
kubeadm certs check-expiration
# CERTIFICATE                EXPIRES                  RESIDUAL TIME
# admin.conf                 Dec 15, 2025 10:30 UTC   364d
# apiserver                  Dec 15, 2025 10:30 UTC   364d
# apiserver-etcd-client      Dec 15, 2025 10:30 UTC   364d
# apiserver-kubelet-client   Dec 15, 2025 10:30 UTC   364d
# controller-manager.conf    Dec 15, 2025 10:30 UTC   364d
# etcd-healthcheck-client    Dec 15, 2025 10:30 UTC   364d
# etcd-peer                  Dec 15, 2025 10:30 UTC   364d
# etcd-server                Dec 15, 2025 10:30 UTC   364d
# front-proxy-client         Dec 15, 2025 10:30 UTC   364d
# scheduler.conf             Dec 15, 2025 10:30 UTC   364d

# Check a specific cert with openssl:
openssl x509 -in /etc/kubernetes/pki/apiserver.crt \
  -noout -enddate
# notAfter=Dec 15 10:30:00 2025 GMT

# Check with days remaining:
openssl x509 -in /etc/kubernetes/pki/apiserver.crt \
  -noout -enddate -checkend 2592000
# Certificate will not expire  ← (within 30 days)
Explanation

kubeadm certs check-expiration shows all cert expiry dates at once — this is the fastest CKA method. Kubeadm certs are valid for 1 year by default. The CA cert is valid for 10 years. openssl x509 -enddate checks individual certs. -checkend SECONDS returns exit code 1 if cert expires within that time — useful in scripts. On the CKA, if you see API server TLS errors, cert expiry is the first thing to check.

Q3
How do you inspect the SANs (Subject Alternative Names) on the API server certificate?
Show Answer
Answer
# View full certificate details:
openssl x509 -in /etc/kubernetes/pki/apiserver.crt \
  -noout -text | grep -A1 "Subject Alternative Name"
# X509v3 Subject Alternative Name:
#   DNS:controlplane, DNS:kubernetes, DNS:kubernetes.default,
#   DNS:kubernetes.default.svc,
#   DNS:kubernetes.default.svc.cluster.local,
#   IP Address:10.96.0.1, IP Address:192.168.1.10

# Just extract SANs cleanly:
openssl x509 -in /etc/kubernetes/pki/apiserver.crt \
  -noout -ext subjectAltName

# Check Subject (CN):
openssl x509 -in /etc/kubernetes/pki/apiserver.crt \
  -noout -subject
# subject= /CN=kube-apiserver

# Check Issuer (who signed it):
openssl x509 -in /etc/kubernetes/pki/apiserver.crt \
  -noout -issuer
# issuer= /CN=kubernetes
Explanation

SANs define which hostnames/IPs the cert is valid for. The API server cert must include: kubernetes, kubernetes.default, kubernetes.default.svc, kubernetes.default.svc.cluster.local, the ClusterIP of the kubernetes service (usually 10.96.0.1), and the node's IP. On the CKA, if you add a load balancer or new DNS name, you must regenerate the API server cert with updated SANs using --apiserver-cert-extra-sans.

Q4
Create a CertificateSigningRequest (CSR) for a new user devuser in Kubernetes and approve it.
Show Answer
Answer
# Step 1: Generate private key
openssl genrsa -out devuser.key 2048

# Step 2: Create CSR (CN=username, O=group)
openssl req -new -key devuser.key \
  -subj "/CN=devuser/O=developers" -out devuser.csr

# Step 3: Base64 encode the CSR
cat devuser.csr | base64 | tr -d '\n'

# Step 4: Create K8s CSR object
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: certificates.k8s.io/v1
kind: CertificateSigningRequest
metadata:
  name: devuser-csr
spec:
  request: $(cat devuser.csr | base64 | tr -d '\n')
  signerName: kubernetes.io/kube-apiserver-client
  expirationSeconds: 86400    # 24 hours
  usages:
  - client auth
EOF

# Step 5: Approve the CSR
kubectl certificate approve devuser-csr

# Step 6: Extract the signed certificate
kubectl get csr devuser-csr \
  -o jsonpath='{.status.certificate}' | base64 -d > devuser.crt

# Step 7: Verify
openssl x509 -in devuser.crt -noout -subject
# subject= /CN=devuser/O=developers
Explanation

The CSR workflow is a HIGH-WEIGHT CKA task. Key details: (1) CN becomes the username, O becomes the group, (2) signerName must be kubernetes.io/kube-apiserver-client for user certs, (3) usages must include client auth, (4) the request field must be base64-encoded with no newlines (tr -d '\n'). After approval, extract the cert from .status.certificate. Common mistake: forgetting to base64-encode the CSR or using the wrong signerName.

Q5
How do you renew all kubeadm certificates? What happens to control plane components?
Show Answer
Answer
# Renew ALL certificates at once:
sudo kubeadm certs renew all
# [renew] Reading configuration from the cluster...
# certificate embedded in admin.conf renewed
# certificate front-proxy-client renewed
# certificate apiserver renewed
# ... all renewed

# Renew specific certificate:
sudo kubeadm certs renew apiserver
sudo kubeadm certs renew apiserver-kubelet-client

# CRITICAL: Restart control plane after renewal!
# kubeadm static pods auto-restart on cert change?
# NO — you must restart them:

# Method 1: Move and restore manifests
cd /etc/kubernetes/manifests/
sudo mv kube-apiserver.yaml /tmp/
# Wait ~20s for kubelet to stop the pod
sudo mv /tmp/kube-apiserver.yaml .
# Repeat for controller-manager and scheduler

# Method 2: Restart kubelet (easier)
sudo systemctl restart kubelet

# Method 3: If using crictl
sudo crictl pods --name kube-apiserver -q | xargs sudo crictl stopp

# Verify new certs:
kubeadm certs check-expiration
# ← Should show dates ~1 year from now

# Also update admin kubeconfig:
sudo cp /etc/kubernetes/admin.conf ~/.kube/config
sudo chown $(id -u):$(id -g) ~/.kube/config
Explanation

kubeadm certs renew all renews every certificate. CRITICAL: after renewal, restart the control plane components so they pick up new certs. Static pods read certs from disk — if you restart kubelet, it detects the manifest changes and recreates the pods. Don't forget to update ~/.kube/config (copy the new admin.conf). On the CKA, cert renewal + restart + kubeconfig update is a common multi-step question. Certs renewed via kubeadm inherit the same 1-year validity.

Q6
Create a kubeconfig file for user devuser using a signed certificate.
Show Answer
Answer
# Assuming you have: devuser.key, devuser.crt, ca.crt

# Step 1: Set cluster
kubectl config set-cluster my-cluster \
  --certificate-authority=/etc/kubernetes/pki/ca.crt \
  --server=https://192.168.1.10:6443 \
  --embed-certs=true \
  --kubeconfig=devuser.kubeconfig

# Step 2: Set credentials
kubectl config set-credentials devuser \
  --client-certificate=devuser.crt \
  --client-key=devuser.key \
  --embed-certs=true \
  --kubeconfig=devuser.kubeconfig

# Step 3: Set context
kubectl config set-context devuser-context \
  --cluster=my-cluster \
  --user=devuser \
  --namespace=dev \
  --kubeconfig=devuser.kubeconfig

# Step 4: Use context
kubectl config use-context devuser-context \
  --kubeconfig=devuser.kubeconfig

# Step 5: Test
kubectl get pods \
  --kubeconfig=devuser.kubeconfig -n dev
# Error (Forbidden) ← Need RBAC!
# This is expected — cert gives identity, RBAC gives permissions
Explanation

Kubeconfig creation is a 4-step process: set-cluster, set-credentials, set-context, use-context. --embed-certs=true embeds certs directly in the kubeconfig (portable). On the CKA, the order matters: cluster → credentials → context → use-context. After creating the kubeconfig, the user still needs RBAC (Role+RoleBinding) to do anything. Without RBAC, they authenticate successfully but all requests return Forbidden.

Q7
The API server won't start and you see TLS certificate errors in the kubelet logs. How do you troubleshoot?
Show Answer
Answer
# Step 1: Check kubelet logs for API server errors
journalctl -u kubelet -l --no-pager | grep -i "tls\|cert\|x509" | tail -20
# "x509: certificate has expired or is not yet valid"
# "tls: private key does not match public key"

# Step 2: Check cert expiry
openssl x509 -in /etc/kubernetes/pki/apiserver.crt \
  -noout -enddate -checkend 0
# Certificate has expired ← Problem found!

# Step 3: Check cert-key match
openssl x509 -in /etc/kubernetes/pki/apiserver.crt \
  -noout -modulus | md5sum
openssl rsa -in /etc/kubernetes/pki/apiserver.key \
  -noout -modulus | md5sum
# If hashes differ → cert/key mismatch!

# Step 4: Check static pod manifest references correct files
cat /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml | grep -E "cert|key"
#   --tls-cert-file=/etc/kubernetes/pki/apiserver.crt
#   --tls-private-key-file=/etc/kubernetes/pki/apiserver.key

# Step 5: Fix expired certs
sudo kubeadm certs renew apiserver
sudo kubeadm certs renew apiserver-kubelet-client

# Step 6: Restart
sudo systemctl restart kubelet

# Step 7: Verify API is back
kubectl get nodes
Explanation

TLS troubleshooting: check kubelet logs for x509 errors, verify cert expiry, confirm cert/key match (modulus comparison), verify manifest paths. Common causes: (1) expired certs — renew with kubeadm, (2) cert/key mismatch — regenerate, (3) wrong file paths in manifests — fix the static pod YAML. On the CKA, journalctl -u kubelet is your first stop when the API server is down.

Q8
What is the difference between the cluster CA certificate and component certificates?
Show Answer
Answer
Certificate Type Purpose Validity
ca.crt/key Root CA Signs all other certs 10 years
apiserver.crt Server cert API server TLS 1 year
apiserver-kubelet-client Client cert API→kubelet auth 1 year
apiserver-etcd-client Client cert API→etcd auth 1 year
front-proxy-* Proxy CA+cert Aggregation layer 1 year
etcd/ca.crt etcd CA etcd internal TLS 10 years 
# Verify cert is signed by cluster CA:
openssl verify -CAfile /etc/kubernetes/pki/ca.crt \
  /etc/kubernetes/pki/apiserver.crt
# apiserver.crt: OK
Explanation

The cluster CA (ca.crt/ca.key) is the trust root — it signs all component certs. CAs last 10 years; component certs last 1 year. There are TWO CAs: the cluster CA (for API server, kubelets) and the etcd CA (for etcd peers/clients). On the CKA, openssl verify checks that a cert was signed by a specific CA. Client certs authenticate components to each other (mutual TLS). If any component cert doesn't chain to the correct CA, communication fails.

Q9
How do you deny a CertificateSigningRequest and list all CSRs?
Show Answer
Answer
# List all CSRs:
kubectl get csr
# NAME          AGE   SIGNERNAME                         REQUESTOR       CONDITION
# devuser-csr   2m    kubernetes.io/kube-apiserver-client admin           Approved,Issued
# baduser-csr   1m    kubernetes.io/kube-apiserver-client admin           Pending

# Deny a CSR:
kubectl certificate deny baduser-csr
# certificatesigningrequest.certificates.k8s.io/baduser-csr denied

# Delete a CSR:
kubectl delete csr baduser-csr

# View CSR details:
kubectl get csr devuser-csr -o yaml
# status:
#   conditions:
#   - type: Approved
#     reason: KubectlApprove
#     message: "This CSR was approved by kubectl certificate approve."

# Describe for more info:
kubectl describe csr devuser-csr
# Requesting User:  kubernetes-admin
# Signer:          kubernetes.io/kube-apiserver-client
# Status:          Approved,Issued
Explanation

CSR management: kubectl certificate approve|deny to handle requests. CSRs have three possible states: Pending, Approved, Denied. On the CKA, you'll see Pending CSRs (e.g., from node bootstrapping or user cert requests). kubectl get csr lists all with status. Key: CSRs don't auto-delete — clean up with kubectl delete csr. The CONDITION column shows the current state. Node bootstrap CSRs use kubernetes.io/kubelet-serving as signerName.

Q10
How do you add extra SANs to the API server certificate for a new load balancer IP 10.0.0.100?
Show Answer
Answer
# Step 1: Delete old API server cert
sudo rm /etc/kubernetes/pki/apiserver.crt
sudo rm /etc/kubernetes/pki/apiserver.key

# Step 2: Regenerate with extra SAN
sudo kubeadm init phase certs apiserver \
  --apiserver-cert-extra-sans=10.0.0.100,lb.example.com

# OR: Edit kubeadm config first, then regenerate:
kubectl -n kube-system get cm kubeadm-config -o yaml
# Add to ClusterConfiguration:
#   apiServer:
#     certSANs:
#     - 10.0.0.100
#     - lb.example.com

# Step 3: Restart API server
sudo crictl pods --name kube-apiserver -q | xargs sudo crictl stopp
# OR
sudo systemctl restart kubelet

# Step 4: Verify new SANs
openssl x509 -in /etc/kubernetes/pki/apiserver.crt \
  -noout -text | grep -A2 "Subject Alternative"
# DNS:kubernetes, DNS:lb.example.com, ...
# IP:10.96.0.1, IP:192.168.1.10, IP:10.0.0.100 ← New!

# CAUTION: Never delete ca.crt/ca.key!
# Only apiserver.crt/key are safe to regenerate
Explanation

To add SANs: (1) delete old API server cert/key, (2) regenerate with kubeadm init phase certs apiserver --apiserver-cert-extra-sans=..., (3) restart API server. On the CKA, this is needed when adding a load balancer or additional access point. NEVER delete ca.crt/ca.key — only the API server cert is safe to regenerate. The kubeadm config in the kube-system/kubeadm-config ConfigMap stores the cluster configuration including extra SANs.

Chapter 27: Pod Security and Admission Control

Kubernetes provides multiple layers of pod-level security. Pod Security Standards (PSS) define three security profiles (Privileged, Baseline, Restricted). Pod Security Admission (PSA) enforces these standards at the namespace level using labels. Security Contexts let you set user IDs, capabilities, and read-only filesystems per pod/container. Admission Controllers intercept API requests to validate and mutate objects before they hit etcd. Mastering these is essential for CKA and real-world cluster hardening.

🟡 Intermediate ⏱️ ~1 hour 🛡️ Pod Security 📝 CKA EXAM

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ Pod Security Standards — Privileged, Baseline, and Restricted profiles
  • ✅ Pod Security Admission — enforce/audit/warn modes via namespace labels
  • ✅ Security Contexts — runAsNonRoot, capabilities, readOnlyRootFilesystem
  • ✅ Admission Controllers — built-in and webhook-based controllers
🎓 CKA Relevance
Cluster Architecture, Installation & Configuration 25% Workloads & Scheduling 15%
Pod Security on the CKA Exam

Expect questions that require you to:

  • Apply PSA labels to namespaces to enforce security standards
  • Configure SecurityContext fields (runAsNonRoot, runAsUser, capabilities)
  • Know which admission controllers are enabled by default
  • Troubleshoot pods rejected by admission policies

Pod Security Commands

ActionCommand
Label namespace (enforce restricted)kubectl label ns prod pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce=restricted
Label namespace (warn baseline)kubectl label ns dev pod-security.kubernetes.io/warn=baseline
Label namespace (audit baseline)kubectl label ns staging pod-security.kubernetes.io/audit=baseline
Check namespace labelskubectl get ns prod --show-labels
Remove PSA labelkubectl label ns prod pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce-
Dry-run pod against PSAkubectl apply --dry-run=server -f pod.yaml
List enabled admission controllerskube-apiserver -h | grep enable-admission-plugins

SecurityContext — Exam Skeleton

Know both pod-level and container-level SecurityContext fields.

apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: secure-pod
spec:
  securityContext:                    # ◀── POD-level (applies to all containers)
    runAsUser: 1000                   # ◀── UID for all containers
    runAsGroup: 3000                  # ◀── GID for all containers
    fsGroup: 2000                     # ◀── supplemental group for volume mounts
    runAsNonRoot: true                # ◀── REJECT if image runs as root
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx
    securityContext:                  # ◀── CONTAINER-level (overrides pod-level)
      allowPrivilegeEscalation: false # ◀── block setuid binaries
      readOnlyRootFilesystem: true    # ◀── immutable container filesystem
      capabilities:
        drop: ["ALL"]                 # ◀── drop ALL Linux capabilities
        add: ["NET_BIND_SERVICE"]     # ◀── add back only what's needed

Pod Security Exam Traps

  • ❌ Container SecurityContext inherits all pod-level settings
  • ✅ Container SecurityContext overrides pod-level for overlapping fields
  • PSA labels use the format pod-security.kubernetes.io/MODE=LEVEL — three modes (enforce/warn/audit) × three levels (privileged/baseline/restricted)
  • PSA enforce mode rejects pods. warn shows a warning but allows. audit logs only
  • runAsNonRoot: true checks the image USER directive — if the image has no USER set, the pod is rejected even if the process would run as non-root
  • readOnlyRootFilesystem blocks writes to the container filesystem — use emptyDir volumes for writable directories
  • capabilities.drop: ["ALL"] then add specific ones = principle of least privilege
  • The restricted PSS requires: runAsNonRoot, no privilege escalation, drop ALL capabilities, seccomp profile — missing ANY of these causes rejection

Find Security Fields Fast

kubectl explain pod.spec.securityContext             # pod-level security
kubectl explain pod.spec.containers.securityContext  # container-level security
kubectl explain pod.spec.securityContext.runAsUser    # UID field
kubectl explain pod.spec.containers.securityContext.capabilities  # Linux caps
3 Security Levels (PSS)
3 Enforcement Modes
9 PSA Label Combinations
30+ Built-in Admission Controllers

27.1 Pod Security Standards (PSS)

Pod Security Standards define three progressively restrictive security profiles that cover the most common pod security needs.

Three Pod Security Standards — Increasing Restriction
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │          POD SECURITY STANDARDS (PSS) — THREE LEVELS                    │
  │                                                                         │
  │  ┌─────────────────┐   ┌─────────────────┐   ┌─────────────────┐       │
  │  │   🔓 PRIVILEGED │   │   🔒 BASELINE   │   │   🔐 RESTRICTED │       │
  │  │                 │   │                 │   │                 │       │
  │  │ • No restrict.  │   │ • Blocks known  │   │ • Strictest     │       │
  │  │ • Full access   │   │   escalations   │   │ • Hardened pods │       │
  │  │ • Host PID/Net  │   │ • No hostPID    │   │ • Non-root only │       │
  │  │ • Privileged    │   │ • No hostNet    │   │ • Drop ALL caps │       │
  │  │   containers OK │   │ • No privileged │   │ • No privilege  │       │
  │  │                 │   │   containers    │   │   escalation    │       │
  │  │ Use: system     │   │ • No hostPath   │   │ • Read-only FS  │       │
  │  │ components,     │   │                 │   │ • Seccomp       │       │
  │  │ kube-system     │   │ Use: general    │   │                 │       │
  │  │                 │   │ workloads       │   │ Use: sensitive  │       │
  │  │                 │   │                 │   │ workloads       │       │
  │  │  ◀─── OPEN ────│── ├── MODERATE ────│── ├── STRICT ──▶   │       │
  │  └─────────────────┘   └─────────────────┘   └─────────────────┘       │
  │                                                                         │
  │  💡 Choose based on workload needs:                                     │
  │     kube-system → Privileged | apps → Baseline | PII/finance → Restric │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Check Privileged Baseline Restricted
Privileged containers✅ Allowed❌ Blocked❌ Blocked
Host namespaces (PID/IPC/Net)✅ Allowed❌ Blocked❌ Blocked
hostPath volumes✅ Allowed❌ Blocked❌ Blocked
Host ports✅ AllowedRestricted❌ Blocked
runAsNonRootNot requiredNot required✅ Required
Privilege escalation✅ Allowed✅ Allowed❌ Must be false
CapabilitiesAnyRestricted setDrop ALL, add specific only
Seccomp profileNot requiredNot required✅ Required (RuntimeDefault/Localhost)
📝 PSS vs PodSecurityPolicy

PodSecurityPolicy (PSP) was deprecated in K8s 1.21 and removed in 1.25. Pod Security Standards + Pod Security Admission (PSA) replaced it. The CKA tests PSS/PSA, NOT PSP.

PSA Admission Decision Flow

1
Pod Created / Updated

kubectl apply -f pod.yaml → API server receives the request

▼
2
Check Namespace Labels

PSA reads pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce, /warn, /audit labels on the target namespace

▼
3
Evaluate Against Standard

Compares pod spec fields (securityContext, capabilities, hostNetwork, etc.) against the configured PSS level

▼
4
Apply Mode Action

enforce → REJECT pod | warn → allow + show warning | audit → allow + log to audit

27.2 Pod Security Admission (PSA)

Pod Security Admission is the built-in admission controller that enforces Pod Security Standards. It's configured entirely through namespace labels — no YAML files needed.

PSA Enforcement via Namespace Labels
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │           POD SECURITY ADMISSION — HOW IT WORKS                         │
  │                                                                         │
  │   kubectl apply -f pod.yaml                                             │
  │          │                                                              │
  │          ▼                                                              │
  │   ┌──────────────┐                                                      │
  │   │  API Server   │                                                      │
  │   └──────┬───────┘                                                      │
  │          │ intercepts                                                    │
  │          ▼                                                              │
  │   ┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐                          │
  │   │  PSA Admission Controller                 │                          │
  │   │                                           │                          │
  │   │  Checks namespace labels:                 │                          │
  │   │  pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce=X      │                          │
  │   │  pod-security.kubernetes.io/warn=X         │                          │
  │   │  pod-security.kubernetes.io/audit=X        │                          │
  │   └──────┬───────────┬──────────┬────────────┘                          │
  │          │           │          │                                        │
  │      ENFORCE       WARN      AUDIT                                      │
  │          │           │          │                                        │
  │     ❌ REJECT   ⚠️ Warning   📝 Log                                    │
  │     the pod     but ALLOW    but ALLOW                                  │
  │                                                                         │
  │   💡 You can combine modes: enforce=baseline + warn=restricted          │
  │      → Blocks baseline violations, warns about restricted violations    │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
psa-namespace-labels.sh 
# ── Apply PSA labels to namespaces ──

# Production: enforce restricted (reject non-compliant pods)
kubectl label ns production \
  pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce=restricted \
  pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce-version=latest

# Staging: enforce baseline, warn about restricted violations
kubectl label ns staging \
  pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce=baseline \
  pod-security.kubernetes.io/warn=restricted

# Development: warn only (don't block anything)
kubectl label ns development \
  pod-security.kubernetes.io/warn=baseline \
  pod-security.kubernetes.io/audit=baseline

# Verify labels
kubectl get ns production --show-labels
# NAME         STATUS   AGE   LABELS
# production   Active   5d    pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce=restricted,...

# ── Test: What happens when you deploy a non-compliant pod? ──

# This pod runs as root — violates "restricted"
kubectl run test --image=nginx -n production
# Error: pods "test" is forbidden: violates PodSecurity "restricted:latest"

# Same pod in dev namespace — just a warning
kubectl run test --image=nginx -n development
# Warning: would violate PodSecurity "baseline:latest"
# pod/test created  ← allowed!
❌
enforce

Rejects pods that violate the standard. Use in production to prevent insecure workloads from deploying.

⚠️
warn

Warns the user but allows the pod. Perfect for migration — see what would break before enforcing.

📝
audit

Logs the violation to the audit log silently. Pods still run. Use for monitoring compliance.

27.3 Security Contexts

SecurityContext lets you set fine-grained security settings at the pod and container level. These are the fields that PSA actually checks against the Pod Security Standards.

Field Level What It Does
runAsUserPod / ContainerSet the UID the process runs as
runAsGroupPod / ContainerSet the primary GID
runAsNonRootPod / ContainerReject if image would run as root (UID 0)
fsGroupPod onlySupplemental group for mounted volumes
readOnlyRootFilesystemContainer onlyMake container filesystem read-only
allowPrivilegeEscalationContainer onlyBlock setuid/setgid binaries
capabilities.dropContainer onlyRemove Linux capabilities
capabilities.addContainer onlyAdd back specific capabilities
privilegedContainer onlyFull host access (avoid!)
seccompProfilePod / ContainerSystem call filtering profile
restricted-compliant-pod.yaml 
# This pod passes the "restricted" PSS — use this as template
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: secure-app
  namespace: production     # namespace with enforce=restricted
spec:
  securityContext:
    runAsNonRoot: true      # ◀── Required by restricted
    runAsUser: 1000
    runAsGroup: 3000
    fsGroup: 2000
    seccompProfile:         # ◀── Required by restricted
      type: RuntimeDefault
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: gcr.io/distroless/static:nonroot
    securityContext:
      allowPrivilegeEscalation: false  # ◀── Required by restricted
      readOnlyRootFilesystem: true     # ◀── Best practice
      capabilities:
        drop: ["ALL"]                  # ◀── Required by restricted
    resources:
      limits:
        memory: "128Mi"
        cpu: "250m"
    volumeMounts:
    - name: tmp
      mountPath: /tmp       # ◀── writable dir via emptyDir
  volumes:
  - name: tmp
    emptyDir: {}            # use emptyDir for writable paths
Pod-Level vs Container-Level SecurityContext
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │   POD-LEVEL securityContext          applies to ALL containers  │
  │   ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐     │
  │   │  runAsUser: 1000                                       │     │
  │   │  runAsGroup: 3000                                      │     │
  │   │  fsGroup: 2000                                         │     │
  │   │  runAsNonRoot: true                                    │     │
  │   │  seccompProfile: RuntimeDefault                        │     │
  │   └───────────┬───────────────────────┬────────────────────┘     │
  │               │                       │                          │
  │               ▼                       ▼                          │
  │   ┌───────────────────┐   ┌───────────────────┐                  │
  │   │ CONTAINER-LEVEL   │   │ CONTAINER-LEVEL   │                  │
  │   │ (overrides pod)   │   │ (inherits pod)    │                  │
  │   │                   │   │                   │                  │
  │   │ runAsUser: 2000   │   │ (uses 1000 from   │                  │
  │   │ ← overrides 1000  │   │  pod-level)       │                  │
  │   │                   │   │                   │                  │
  │   │ capabilities:     │   │ capabilities:     │                  │
  │   │   drop: ["ALL"]   │   │   drop: ["ALL"]   │                  │
  │   │                   │   │                   │                  │
  │   │ allowPrivilegeEsc │   │ readOnlyRootFS:   │                  │
  │   │ : false           │   │ true              │                  │
  │   └───────────────────┘   └───────────────────┘                  │
  │     container: app          container: sidecar                   │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
💡 CKA Pattern: Restricted-Compliant Pod

To make ANY pod pass the "restricted" standard, add these 4 things: runAsNonRoot: true, allowPrivilegeEscalation: false, capabilities.drop: ["ALL"], and seccompProfile.type: RuntimeDefault. Missing any one ➔ rejected.

27.4 Admission Controllers

Admission Controllers are plugins that intercept API requests after authentication and authorization but before the object is persisted to etcd. They can validate, mutate, or reject requests.

Request Flow Through Admission Controllers
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                API REQUEST LIFECYCLE                                          │
  │                                                                              │
  │   kubectl apply ──▶ API Server                                               │
  │                        │                                                     │
  │                   ┌────▼────┐                                                │
  │                   │  AuthN  │ ── Who are you? (certs, tokens)                │
  │                   └────┬────┘                                                │
  │                   ┌────▼────┐                                                │
  │                   │  AuthZ  │ ── Are you allowed? (RBAC)                     │
  │                   └────┬────┘                                                │
  │                   ┌────▼──────────────────────────────────┐                  │
  │                   │  ADMISSION CONTROLLERS                │                  │
  │                   │                                       │                  │
  │                   │  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐  │                  │
  │                   │  │  MUTATING    │  │  VALIDATING  │  │                  │
  │                   │  │              │  │              │  │                  │
  │                   │  │ • Defaults   │  │ • Policy     │  │                  │
  │                   │  │ • Inject     │  │ • Rules      │  │                  │
  │                   │  │   sidecars   │  │ • Quotas     │  │                  │
  │                   │  │ • Labels     │  │ • PSA        │  │                  │
  │                   │  │ • Webhooks   │  │ • Webhooks   │  │                  │
  │                   │  └──────┬───────┘  └──────┬───────┘  │                  │
  │                   │         │ modify           │ check    │                  │
  │                   └─────────┼──────────────────┼──────────┘                  │
  │                             │                  │                             │
  │                             └────────┬─────────┘                             │
  │                                      │ if ALL pass                           │
  │                                 ┌────▼────┐                                  │
  │                                 │  etcd   │ ── Object persisted              │
  │                                 └─────────┘                                  │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Admission Controller Type Purpose
NamespaceLifecycleValidatingPrevents creating resources in terminating/non-existent namespaces
LimitRangerMutatingApplies default resource limits from LimitRange objects
ResourceQuotaValidatingEnforces resource quotas per namespace
ServiceAccountMutatingAuto-mounts SA tokens onto pods
PodSecurityValidatingEnforces Pod Security Standards (PSA)
DefaultStorageClassMutatingAssigns default StorageClass to PVCs without one
MutatingAdmissionWebhookMutatingCalls external webhooks to modify objects
ValidatingAdmissionWebhookValidatingCalls external webhooks to validate objects
admission-controllers.sh 
# ── Check which admission controllers are enabled ──
# Look at API server manifest on control plane node
cat /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml \
  | grep enable-admission-plugins
# --enable-admission-plugins=NodeRestriction,...

# ── Enable/disable admission controllers ──
# Edit the API server static pod manifest:
vi /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml
# Add to the command args:
# --enable-admission-plugins=NodeRestriction,PodSecurity,ResourceQuota
# --disable-admission-plugins=DefaultStorageClass

# ── After editing, API server auto-restarts (static pod) ──

# ── List default enabled controllers ──
kube-apiserver -h | grep enable-admission-plugins
# Default: CertificateApproval,CertificateSigning,
# CertificateSubjectRestriction,DefaultIngressClass,
# DefaultStorageClass,DefaultTolerationSeconds,LimitRanger,
# MutatingAdmissionWebhook,NamespaceLifecycle,PodSecurity,
# ResourceQuota,ServiceAccount,StorageObjectInUseProtection,
# ValidatingAdmissionPolicy,ValidatingAdmissionWebhook
⚠️ CKA: Editing API Server Manifest

On the exam, you may need to enable an admission controller by editing /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml. After saving, the kubelet auto-restarts the API server pod. Wait ~30 seconds and verify with kubectl get pods -n kube-system. If the API server doesn't come back, check your YAML syntax.

✅ DO
  • Use PSA labels for namespace-level enforcement
  • Start with warn mode before switching to enforce
  • Set runAsNonRoot: true for all application pods
  • Drop ALL capabilities, add only what's needed
  • Use readOnlyRootFilesystem + emptyDir for writable paths
❌ DON'T
  • Run containers as root without a compelling reason
  • Use privileged: true for application workloads
  • Skip seccomp profiles in restricted namespaces
  • Forget to restart API server after editing admission plugins
  • Confuse PodSecurityPolicy (removed) with Pod Security Standards
Security Context Quick Reference — CKA Cheat Sheet
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │        SECURITY CONTEXT CHEAT SHEET — WHAT TO SET WHERE                      │
  │                                                                              │
  │  ┌──────────────────────────────────┐  ┌──────────────────────────────────┐ │
  │  │  POD-LEVEL                       │  │  CONTAINER-LEVEL                 │ │
  │  │  spec.securityContext            │  │  spec.containers[].securityCtx   │ │
  │  │                                  │  │                                  │ │
  │  │  ✅ runAsUser: 1000              │  │  ✅ allowPrivilegeEscalation     │ │
  │  │  ✅ runAsGroup: 3000             │  │  ✅ readOnlyRootFilesystem       │ │
  │  │  ✅ runAsNonRoot: true           │  │  ✅ capabilities.drop/add       │ │
  │  │  ✅ fsGroup: 2000                │  │  ✅ privileged (avoid!)         │ │
  │  │  ✅ seccompProfile               │  │  ✅ runAsUser (overrides pod)   │ │
  │  │  ✅ supplementalGroups           │  │  ✅ runAsNonRoot (overrides)    │ │
  │  │                                  │  │  ✅ seccompProfile (overrides)  │ │
  │  │  ❌ NOT available:               │  │                                  │ │
  │  │     capabilities                 │  │  ❌ NOT available:               │ │
  │  │     readOnlyRootFilesystem       │  │     fsGroup                      │ │
  │  │     allowPrivilegeEscalation     │  │     supplementalGroups           │ │
  │  └──────────────────────────────────┘  └──────────────────────────────────┘ │
  │                                                                              │
  │  💡 Rule: Container-level OVERRIDES pod-level for shared fields             │
  │     Pod sets runAsUser: 1000, Container sets runAsUser: 2000                │
  │     → Container runs as UID 2000                                            │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Pod Security Verification Checklist

  • kubectl get ns NAME --show-labels → verify PSA labels are applied
  • kubectl label ns NAME pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce=restricted --dry-run=server → preview label effect
  • kubectl apply --dry-run=server -f pod.yaml → test pod against PSA before deploying
  • kubectl exec POD -- id → confirm runAsUser/runAsGroup
  • kubectl exec POD -- touch /test → verify readOnlyRootFilesystem (should fail)
  • kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep apiserver → verify apiserver restarted after admission controller changes
  • cat /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml | grep enable-admission → check enabled admission controllers

🏋️ Chapter 27 Practice Exercises

These exercises cover the most common CKA pod security patterns.

⏱️ Target: 4 minutes
Exercise 27.1 — Apply PSA to a Namespace

Configure Pod Security Admission to enforce baseline and warn for restricted on a namespace.

  1. Create namespace secure-ns
  2. Label it to enforce the baseline standard and warn for restricted
  3. Try deploying a pod with hostNetwork: true → should be rejected
  4. Deploy a normal nginx pod → should succeed with a warning about restricted
💡 Hint: PSA labels follow the format pod-security.kubernetes.io/MODE=LEVEL. You can set multiple modes on one namespace.
📝 View Solution 
# Step 1: Create namespace
kubectl create namespace secure-ns

# Step 2: Apply PSA labels
kubectl label ns secure-ns \
  pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce=baseline \
  pod-security.kubernetes.io/warn=restricted

# Step 3: Try deploying a pod that violates baseline
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: bad-pod
  namespace: secure-ns
spec:
  hostNetwork: true    # violates baseline!
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx
EOF
# Error: pods "bad-pod" is forbidden: violates PodSecurity "baseline:latest"

# Step 4: Deploy a normal pod (passes baseline, warns restricted)
kubectl run nginx --image=nginx -n secure-ns
# Warning: would violate PodSecurity "restricted:latest": ...
# pod/nginx created  ← allowed!
⏱️ Target: 6 minutes
Exercise 27.2 — Create Restricted-Compliant Pod

Create a pod that passes the strictest PSS level — restricted.

  1. Label namespace secure-ns to enforce restricted
  2. Create a pod with all required fields: runAsNonRoot, allowPrivilegeEscalation: false, capabilities.drop: ["ALL"], seccompProfile.type: RuntimeDefault
  3. Use a non-root image (e.g., gcr.io/distroless/static:nonroot)
  4. Verify the pod runs successfully in the restricted namespace
💡 Hint: The restricted standard requires ALL 4 of these: runAsNonRoot, no privilege escalation, drop ALL capabilities, and a seccomp profile. Missing any one causes rejection.
📝 View Solution 
# Step 1: Upgrade PSA to enforce restricted
kubectl label ns secure-ns \
  pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce=restricted \
  --overwrite

# Step 2: Create restricted-compliant pod
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: secure-pod
  namespace: secure-ns
spec:
  securityContext:
    runAsNonRoot: true
    runAsUser: 1000
    seccompProfile:
      type: RuntimeDefault
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: gcr.io/distroless/static:nonroot
    securityContext:
      allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
      readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
      capabilities:
        drop: ["ALL"]
    resources:
      limits:
        memory: "64Mi"
        cpu: "100m"
EOF

# Step 3: Verify pod is running
kubectl get pod secure-pod -n secure-ns
# NAME         READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE
# secure-pod   1/1     Running   0          5s
⏱️ Target: 5 minutes
Exercise 27.3 — SecurityContext Fields

Practice setting security context fields and verifying their effects inside the container.

  1. Create a pod with runAsUser: 1000 and readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
  2. Exec into the pod: kubectl exec -it pod-name -- id → should show uid=1000
  3. Try writing a file: touch /test → should fail (read-only filesystem)
  4. Mount an emptyDir at /tmp and verify writing works there
💡 Hint: readOnlyRootFilesystem is a container-level field. Use emptyDir volumes for any writable paths (logs, temp files, caches).
📝 View Solution 
# Create pod with security context + writable emptyDir
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: sec-ctx-pod
spec:
  securityContext:
    runAsUser: 1000
    runAsGroup: 3000
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: busybox
    command: ["sleep", "3600"]
    securityContext:
      readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
    volumeMounts:
    - name: tmp
      mountPath: /tmp
  volumes:
  - name: tmp
    emptyDir: {}
EOF

# Wait for pod
kubectl wait --for=condition=ready pod/sec-ctx-pod

# Verify UID
kubectl exec sec-ctx-pod -- id
# uid=1000 gid=3000

# Try writing to root filesystem (fails)
kubectl exec sec-ctx-pod -- touch /test
# touch: /test: Read-only file system

# Write to emptyDir (succeeds)
kubectl exec sec-ctx-pod -- touch /tmp/test
kubectl exec sec-ctx-pod -- ls /tmp/test
# /tmp/test  ← success!
⏱️ Target: 5 minutes
Exercise 27.4 — Enable Admission Controller

Edit the API server manifest to enable an additional admission controller plugin.

  1. SSH to control plane and check current admission plugins: cat /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml | grep enable-admission
  2. Add NodeRestriction to the list (if not present)
  3. Save the file — kubelet auto-restarts the API server
  4. Wait 30 seconds and verify: kubectl get pods -n kube-system
💡 Hint: Static pod manifests auto-restart when saved. If API server doesn't come back, you made a YAML syntax error — check with crictl ps and crictl logs.
📝 View Solution 
# Step 1: SSH to control plane
ssh controlplane

# Step 2: Check current admission plugins
cat /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml \
  | grep enable-admission-plugins
# --enable-admission-plugins=NodeRestriction

# Step 3: Edit the manifest
vi /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml
# Find the --enable-admission-plugins line and add to the list:
# --enable-admission-plugins=NodeRestriction,PodSecurity,ResourceQuota
# IMPORTANT: comma-separated, no spaces!

# Step 4: Wait for API server to restart (auto by kubelet)
sleep 30

# Step 5: Verify API server is running
kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep apiserver
# kube-apiserver-controlplane   1/1   Running   0   25s

# Step 6: Verify the admission plugin is active
kubectl get pod kube-apiserver-controlplane \
  -n kube-system -o yaml | grep enable-admission
# --enable-admission-plugins=NodeRestriction,PodSecurity,ResourceQuota
⏱️ CKA Speed Tip: PSA Labels

The fastest way to apply PSA is a single kubectl label command. No YAML needed!
kubectl label ns my-ns pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce=restricted pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce-version=latest

⚠️ CKA Gotcha: Pod Security & Admission Control Traps

  • 3 PSA modes — enforce (reject violations), warn (allow but show warning), audit (log only). The exam usually asks for enforce. Apply via namespace labels, not cluster config
  • 3 PSS levels — privileged (no restrictions), baseline (blocks known escalations), restricted (hardened). Restricted requires runAsNonRoot: true, drop ALL capabilities, and seccompProfile
  • SecurityContext at pod vs container level — spec.securityContext sets defaults for ALL containers. spec.containers[].securityContext overrides per container. Container-level wins
  • runAsNonRoot: true fails if image uses root — The container must have a non-root USER in Dockerfile, or set runAsUser: 1000 explicitly. Otherwise the pod is rejected at startup
  • Admission controllers are API server flags — --enable-admission-plugins=NodeRestriction,PodSecurity. To add one, edit /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml. The API server auto-restarts

CKA Speed Tip — SecurityContext skeleton (memorize):
securityContext:
  runAsUser: 1000
  runAsNonRoot: true
  readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
  allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
  capabilities: { drop: ["ALL"] }
This covers 90% of SecurityContext questions on the exam.

⚡ Pod Security commands: kubectl label ns <ns> pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce=restricted — apply PSA
kubectl label ns <ns> pod-security.kubernetes.io/warn=baseline — warning mode
kubectl get ns <ns> --show-labels — verify PSA labels
kubectl get pod <name> -o jsonpath='{.spec.securityContext}' — pod security context
kubectl get pod <name> -o jsonpath='{.spec.containers[0].securityContext}' — container security context
📝 Chapter 27 Practice Drill

Timed challenge (target: 7 minutes):

  • 1. Create namespace hardened with PSA enforce=restricted
  • 2. Try to deploy a pod with default settings — it should be REJECTED
  • 3. Fix the pod: add runAsNonRoot: true, runAsUser: 1000, seccompProfile: {type: RuntimeDefault}, drop ALL capabilities
  • 4. Deploy the fixed pod — should succeed
  • 5. Verify the API server has PodSecurity admission plugin enabled
🔥

Scenario: "Pod Rejected — Violates Restricted Policy"

CKA EXAM

Symptom: kubectl apply -f app.yaml -n production → Error: pods "app" is forbidden: violates PodSecurity "restricted:latest": allowPrivilegeEscalation != false, unrestricted capabilities, seccompProfile

Step 1 — Read the error

The error lists exactly which checks failed: allowPrivilegeEscalation, capabilities, and seccompProfile. Fix each one.

Step 2 — Add required fields

In each container's securityContext: set allowPrivilegeEscalation: false, capabilities: {drop: ["ALL"]}. In pod-level: seccompProfile: {type: RuntimeDefault}, runAsNonRoot: true.

Step 3 — Re-apply and verify

kubectl apply -f app.yaml -n production → pod created successfully. kubectl get pods -n production → Running.

Lesson: PSA error messages are incredibly helpful — they list every failing check. Read them carefully and fix each violation. The restricted standard needs ALL four: runAsNonRoot + no privilege escalation + drop ALL caps + seccomp profile.

📝 Chapter 27 — CKA Practice Questions

Q1
Create a pod that runs as a non-root user with UID 1000, drops all capabilities, and has a read-only root filesystem.
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: secure-pod
spec:
  securityContext:
    runAsNonRoot: true           # ← Reject if image runs as root
    runAsUser: 1000              # ← Run all containers as UID 1000
    runAsGroup: 3000             # ← Primary group
    fsGroup: 2000                # ← Volume group
    seccompProfile:
      type: RuntimeDefault       # ← Enable default seccomp
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx
    securityContext:
      allowPrivilegeEscalation: false  # ← No setuid
      readOnlyRootFilesystem: true     # ← Immutable root fs
      capabilities:
        drop:
        - ALL                          # ← Drop ALL Linux caps
    volumeMounts:
    - name: tmp
      mountPath: /tmp                  # ← Writable tmp
    - name: cache
      mountPath: /var/cache/nginx      # ← nginx needs this
    - name: run
      mountPath: /var/run              # ← nginx PID file
  volumes:
  - name: tmp
    emptyDir: {}
  - name: cache
    emptyDir: {}
  - name: run
    emptyDir: {}

# Apply and verify:
kubectl apply -f secure-pod.yaml
kubectl exec secure-pod -- id
# uid=1000 gid=3000 groups=2000
kubectl exec secure-pod -- touch /etc/test 2>&1
# touch: /etc/test: Read-only file system ✓
Explanation

Pod-level securityContext sets defaults for all containers; container-level overrides specific settings. readOnlyRootFilesystem: true makes the root filesystem immutable — you must provide emptyDir volumes for any writable paths (nginx needs /tmp, /var/cache/nginx, /var/run). drop ALL capabilities removes all Linux capabilities. allowPrivilegeEscalation: false prevents setuid binaries from gaining elevated privileges. On the CKA, this is the "restricted" PSS-compliant pod pattern.

Q2
What are the three Pod Security Standards (PSS) levels? Describe each.
Show Answer
Answer
Standard Purpose Key Restrictions Use Case
Privileged Unrestricted None — anything goes System-level (kube-system)
Baseline Prevent known escalations No privileged, no hostNetwork/PID/IPC, no hostPath, restricted ports Most workloads
Restricted Hardened best practices All baseline + must run as non-root, drop ALL caps, read-only root fs, seccomp required Security-sensitive apps 
# Apply via namespace labels:
kubectl label ns production \
  pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce=baseline \
  pod-security.kubernetes.io/warn=restricted \
  pod-security.kubernetes.io/audit=restricted

# enforce → REJECT violating pods
# warn    → ALLOW but warn the user
# audit   → ALLOW but log to audit log
Explanation

Three levels: Privileged (no restrictions), Baseline (prevents known exploits), Restricted (hardened). Applied via namespace labels with three modes: enforce (reject), warn (allow + warning), audit (allow + log). On the CKA, the typical pattern is: enforce baseline + warn restricted. This catches most security issues while giving you visibility into what would break under restricted mode. kube-system usually stays at Privileged because system components need elevated access.

Q3
Label a namespace to enforce the restricted Pod Security Standard. Then try to create a default pod — what happens?
Show Answer
Answer
# Create and label namespace:
kubectl create ns secure-ns
kubectl label ns secure-ns \
  pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce=restricted \
  pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce-version=latest

# Try a default pod (no security context):
kubectl run test --image=nginx -n secure-ns
# Error from server (Forbidden): pods "test" is forbidden:
# violates PodSecurity "restricted:latest":
# allowPrivilegeEscalation != false
# unrestricted capabilities
# runAsNonRoot != true
# seccompProfile not set

# Fix with compliant pod:
kubectl run test --image=nginx -n secure-ns \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml > test.yaml
# Edit test.yaml to add:
# spec:
#   securityContext:
#     runAsNonRoot: true
#     seccompProfile:
#       type: RuntimeDefault
#   containers:
#   - securityContext:
#       allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
#       capabilities:
#         drop: ["ALL"]
kubectl apply -f test.yaml

# Verify labels:
kubectl get ns secure-ns --show-labels
# pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce=restricted
Explanation

When enforce=restricted is set, the API server rejects any pod that doesn't meet ALL restricted requirements. The error message lists exactly which fields are missing — use it as a checklist. On the CKA, you'll need to: (1) label the namespace, (2) create a compliant pod with all required fields. The version label (enforce-version=latest) pins to the current K8s version's definition of "restricted."

Q4
What is the difference between pod-level and container-level securityContext?
Show Answer
Answer
Field Pod Level Container Level
runAsUser ✅ All containers ✅ This container (overrides pod)
runAsNonRoot ✅ ✅ (overrides)
fsGroup ✅ Pod only ❌ Not available
capabilities ❌ Not available ✅ Container only
readOnlyRootFilesystem ❌ Not available ✅ Container only
allowPrivilegeEscalation ❌ Not available ✅ Container only
seccompProfile ✅ All containers ✅ (overrides) 
# Container-level OVERRIDES pod-level:
spec:
  securityContext:
    runAsUser: 1000      # ← Default for all containers
  containers:
  - name: special
    securityContext:
      runAsUser: 2000    # ← This container = UID 2000
  - name: normal
    # No override → Uses UID 1000 from pod level
Explanation

Pod-level securityContext sets defaults; container-level overrides for that specific container. Some fields are ONLY pod-level (fsGroup) and some are ONLY container-level (capabilities, readOnlyRootFilesystem, allowPrivilegeEscalation). On the CKA, a common gotcha: you set runAsUser at pod level but one container needs a different user — use container-level override. Also: capabilities can only be set per container, not pod-wide.

Q5
Add the NET_ADMIN capability to a container while dropping all others. When would you need this?
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: net-admin-pod
spec:
  containers:
  - name: network-tool
    image: nicolaka/netshoot
    securityContext:
      capabilities:
        drop:
        - ALL               # ← First drop ALL
        add:
        - NET_ADMIN          # ← Then add only what's needed
    command: ["sleep", "3600"]

# Verify capabilities:
kubectl exec net-admin-pod -- cat /proc/1/status | grep -i cap
# CapPrm: 00000000000010000  ← Only NET_ADMIN

# Common capabilities:
# NET_ADMIN    → Modify network config (iptables, routes)
# NET_RAW      → Use raw sockets (ping)
# SYS_TIME     → Set system clock
# SYS_PTRACE   → Trace processes (debugging)
# CHOWN        → Change file ownership
# DAC_OVERRIDE → Bypass file permission checks

# NOTE: In restricted PSS, you can ONLY add NET_BIND_SERVICE
# NET_ADMIN requires baseline or privileged PSS
Explanation

Best practice: drop ALL then add only what's needed. NET_ADMIN allows modifying network configuration (iptables rules, routing tables, interfaces) — needed by network plugins like Calico/Cilium. On the CKA, the pattern is always drop first, add selectively. Under the restricted PSS, only NET_BIND_SERVICE can be added. If you need NET_ADMIN, the namespace must use baseline or privileged enforcement.

Q6
What is a seccomp profile and how do you apply the default runtime seccomp profile to a pod?
Show Answer
Answer
# seccomp = Secure Computing mode
# Filters which syscalls a container can make

# Three types:
# RuntimeDefault → Container runtime's default profile
# Localhost      → Custom profile from node filesystem
# Unconfined     → No filtering (default if not set)

# Apply RuntimeDefault (REQUIRED for restricted PSS):
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: seccomp-pod
spec:
  securityContext:
    seccompProfile:
      type: RuntimeDefault    # ← Pod level (all containers)
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx
    securityContext:
      seccompProfile:
        type: RuntimeDefault  # ← Container level (overrides)

# Custom profile (Localhost):
spec:
  securityContext:
    seccompProfile:
      type: Localhost
      localhostProfile: profiles/my-profile.json
# Profile must exist at:
# /var/lib/kubelet/seccomp/profiles/my-profile.json

# Verify:
kubectl get pod seccomp-pod -o jsonpath=\
'{.spec.securityContext.seccompProfile}'
# {"type":"RuntimeDefault"}
Explanation

Seccomp filters syscalls — RuntimeDefault blocks dangerous ones like reboot, mount, ptrace. The restricted PSS REQUIRES seccompProfile: RuntimeDefault (or Localhost). On the CKA, always set this in pods targeting restricted namespaces. Without it, seccomp is Unconfined (no filtering). Custom profiles live at /var/lib/kubelet/seccomp/ on the node. Pod-level seccomp applies to all containers; container-level overrides.

Q7
A pod fails to deploy with violates PodSecurity "restricted:latest". How do you fix it?
Show Answer
Answer
# Typical error message lists ALL violations:
# pods "my-pod" is forbidden: violates PodSecurity "restricted:latest":
# allowPrivilegeEscalation != false (container "app")
# unrestricted capabilities (container "app")
# runAsNonRoot != true
# seccompProfile (pod or containers[*] must set)

# Fix — add ALL required fields:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: my-pod
spec:
  securityContext:
    runAsNonRoot: true               # ← Fix 1
    runAsUser: 1000                   # ← Ensure non-root UID
