    seccompProfile:
      type: RuntimeDefault           # ← Fix 4
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx
    securityContext:
      allowPrivilegeEscalation: false # ← Fix 2
      capabilities:
        drop:
        - ALL                         # ← Fix 3

# Quick checklist for restricted compliance:
# ✅ runAsNonRoot: true (pod or container)
# ✅ seccompProfile: RuntimeDefault (pod or container)
# ✅ allowPrivilegeEscalation: false (each container)
# ✅ capabilities.drop: ["ALL"] (each container)
# ✅ No privileged: true
# ✅ No hostNetwork/hostPID/hostIPC
# ✅ No hostPath volumes
Explanation

The error message is your checklist — it lists EVERY violation. Fix them all. On the CKA, the four most common missing fields for restricted compliance are: (1) runAsNonRoot: true, (2) allowPrivilegeEscalation: false, (3) capabilities.drop: ["ALL"], (4) seccompProfile: RuntimeDefault. Apply these to every container. The restricted PSS also prohibits hostPath, hostNetwork, hostPID, hostIPC, and privileged mode.

Q8
What are Kubernetes admission controllers? How do you check which ones are enabled?
Show Answer
Answer
# Admission controllers intercept API requests
# AFTER authentication & authorization, BEFORE persistence

# Two types:
# Mutating  → Can MODIFY the request (e.g., add defaults)
# Validating → Can only ACCEPT or REJECT

# Check enabled admission plugins:
cat /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml | \
  grep enable-admission
# --enable-admission-plugins=NodeRestriction,PodSecurity

# OR: From running process
ps aux | grep kube-apiserver | grep admission

# Key admission controllers for CKA:
# NodeRestriction  → Limits what kubelets can modify
# PodSecurity      → Enforces PSS (replaced PSP)
# LimitRanger      → Applies default resource limits
# ResourceQuota    → Enforces namespace quotas
# NamespaceLifecycle → Prevents ops in terminating ns
# ServiceAccount   → Auto-mounts SA tokens

# Enable an additional controller:
# Edit /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml:
# --enable-admission-plugins=NodeRestriction,PodSecurity,LimitRanger
# API server auto-restarts (static pod)

# Disable a controller:
# --disable-admission-plugins=DefaultTolerationSeconds
Explanation

Admission controllers are plugins in the API server pipeline: authn → authz → admission → etcd. They can mutate (add defaults, inject sidecars) or validate (reject invalid resources). On the CKA: (1) find enabled controllers in /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml, (2) add new ones by editing the --enable-admission-plugins flag. The API server static pod auto-restarts when its manifest changes. Key controllers to know: NodeRestriction, PodSecurity, LimitRanger, ResourceQuota.

Q9
How do you use the warn and audit PSA modes to test policies before enforcing?
Show Answer
Answer
# Gradual rollout strategy:
# Step 1: Start with warn+audit (no enforcement):
kubectl label ns production \
  pod-security.kubernetes.io/warn=restricted \
  pod-security.kubernetes.io/audit=restricted
# Existing pods keep running
# New non-compliant pods show warnings but ARE CREATED

# Step 2: Deploy pods and watch for warnings:
kubectl run test --image=nginx -n production
# Warning: would violate PodSecurity "restricted:latest":
# allowPrivilegeEscalation != false, ...
# pod/test created  ← Pod is STILL created!

# Step 3: Check audit logs:
# /var/log/kubernetes/audit/audit.log
# Look for: "pod-security.kubernetes.io/audit-violations"

# Step 4: After fixing all workloads, enforce:
kubectl label ns production \
  pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce=restricted \
  --overwrite

# Dry-run: Check existing pods against a policy:
kubectl label ns production \
  pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce=restricted \
  --dry-run=server --overwrite
# Warning: existing pods in namespace "production"
# that violate: pod1, pod2
Explanation

Use warn + audit to discover violations WITHOUT breaking workloads, then switch to enforce once compliant. warn shows messages to the user; audit logs to the audit log. On the CKA, you might be asked to: (1) label a namespace with warn mode, (2) identify violating pods, (3) fix them, (4) enable enforce. The --dry-run=server trick checks existing pods against a policy before applying it — very useful for CKA scenarios.

Q10
A container needs to run as root UID 0 but you want to prevent privilege escalation. Is this possible?
Show Answer
Answer
# Yes — you can run as root but block escalation:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: root-limited
spec:
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: ubuntu
    command: ["sleep", "3600"]
    securityContext:
      runAsUser: 0                     # ← Root UID
      allowPrivilegeEscalation: false  # ← No setuid/setgid
      capabilities:
        drop:
        - ALL                          # ← No extra capabilities
      readOnlyRootFilesystem: true     # ← Can't modify fs

# This is ALLOWED under baseline PSS (not restricted!)
# Restricted requires runAsNonRoot: true

# The security context means:
# ✓ Process runs as root
# ✗ Cannot gain additional privileges via setuid
# ✗ Has NO Linux capabilities
# ✗ Cannot write to filesystem

# When is this needed?
# Legacy apps that check UID=0 but don't need real root power
# Containers with ENTRYPOINT that checks whoami

# WARNING: This VIOLATES restricted PSS
# restricted requires: runAsNonRoot: true
# So this only works in baseline or privileged namespaces

# Better approach if possible:
# Use a non-root image: runAsUser: 1000
Explanation

Yes — runAsUser: 0 + allowPrivilegeEscalation: false + capabilities.drop: ALL gives a "root in name only" container. The process has UID 0 but no capabilities and can't escalate. This is allowed under baseline PSS but NOT restricted (which requires runAsNonRoot: true). On the CKA, understand the distinction: restricted PSS = must be non-root, baseline PSS = root is OK if you block escalation. The ideal solution is always to run as non-root, but legacy apps sometimes require UID 0.

🔴 Part 8: Cluster Maintenance Chapters 28-30

Chapter 28: Cluster Upgrades with kubeadm

Kubernetes releases a new minor version roughly every 4 months. As a cluster administrator, you must upgrade clusters regularly to stay within the supported version window, receive security patches, and access new features. For your anihpj/jobpost production cluster, a botched upgrade means downtime for your job board. The CKA exam consistently tests the upgrade procedure — expect to perform a full control plane and worker node upgrade hands-on. This chapter gives you a battle-tested, step-by-step workflow.

🔴 Advanced ⏱️ ~2 hours ⬆️ Upgrades 📝 CKA EXAM

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ Kubernetes release cycle — minor vs patch versions, support window
  • ✅ Component version skew policy — what can differ, what can't
  • ✅ Upgrade strategy — control plane first, then workers
  • ✅ Hands-on control plane upgrade — kubeadm, kubelet, kubectl
  • ✅ Hands-on worker node upgrade — drain, upgrade, uncordon
🎓 CKA Relevance
Cluster Architecture, Installation & Configuration 25%
Cluster Upgrades on the CKA Exam

Expect a question like: "Upgrade the control plane node from v1.30.0 to v1.31.0 using kubeadm." You must:

  • Know the exact sequence: upgrade kubeadm → plan → apply → drain → upgrade kubelet/kubectl → uncordon
  • Use apt-mark unhold/hold to manage package versions
  • Remember systemctl daemon-reload && systemctl restart kubelet after upgrading kubelet
  • Never skip minor versions — 1.30 → 1.31 is OK, 1.30 → 1.32 is NOT

Cluster Upgrade Commands

ActionCommand
Check current versionkubectl get nodes
Unhold kubeadmapt-mark unhold kubeadm
Install specific kubeadmapt-get install -y kubeadm=1.31.0-1.1
Hold kubeadmapt-mark hold kubeadm
Check upgrade plankubeadm upgrade plan
Apply upgrade (control plane)kubeadm upgrade apply v1.31.0
Upgrade worker node configkubeadm upgrade node
Drain nodekubectl drain NODE --ignore-daemonsets
Upgrade kubelet + kubectlapt-get install -y kubelet=1.31.0-1.1 kubectl=1.31.0-1.1
Restart kubeletsystemctl daemon-reload && systemctl restart kubelet
Uncordon nodekubectl uncordon NODE

Upgrade Exam Traps

  • ❌ Skip from 1.30 to 1.32 directly
  • ✅ Upgrade one minor version at a time: 1.30 → 1.31 → 1.32
  • ❌ Upgrade workers before control plane
  • ✅ Always upgrade control plane FIRST, then workers
  • ❌ Forget systemctl daemon-reload after upgrading kubelet
  • ✅ Always run systemctl daemon-reload && systemctl restart kubelet
  • On workers use kubeadm upgrade node, NOT kubeadm upgrade apply
  • --ignore-daemonsets is REQUIRED on drain — DaemonSet pods can't be evicted
  • Check the package version format — some providers use =1.31.0-1.1 not =1.31.0-00

Find Upgrade Info Fast

kubectl get nodes                          # current versions
kubeadm version                             # kubeadm version
kubelet --version                           # kubelet version
kubeadm upgrade plan                        # available upgrades
apt-cache madison kubeadm                   # available package versions
~4 mo Minor Release Cycle
3 Supported Minor Versions
±1 Version Skew (kubelet)
7 Steps per Upgrade

28.1 Kubernetes Release Cycle

Understanding the release cycle helps you plan when and how to upgrade your cluster. Kubernetes follows a predictable release pattern.

Kubernetes Version Numbering
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                    KUBERNETES VERSION FORMAT                                  │
  │                                                                              │
  │                         v1.31.2                                              │
  │                          │  │  │                                             │
  │                          │  │  └── PATCH version (bug fixes, security)       │
  │                          │  └──── MINOR version (new features)               │
  │                          └────── MAJOR version (always 1)                    │
  │                                                                              │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐         │
  │  │  RELEASE TIMELINE — Kubernetes Support Window                   │         │
  │  │                                                                 │         │
  │  │  Time ──────────────────────────────────────────────▶           │         │
  │  │                                                                 │         │
  │  │  v1.29  ████████████████░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░              │         │
  │  │         ◀── supported ─▶ ◀── end of life ──────▶               │         │
  │  │                                                                 │         │
  │  │  v1.30  ░░░░████████████████░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░              │         │
  │  │              ◀── supported ─▶                                   │         │
  │  │                                                                 │         │
  │  │  v1.31  ░░░░░░░░████████████████░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░              │         │
  │  │                  ◀── supported ─▶  ◀── current                  │         │
  │  │                                                                 │         │
  │  │  v1.32  ░░░░░░░░░░░░████████████████                           │         │
  │  │                      ◀── latest ───▶                            │         │
  │  │                                                                 │         │
  │  │  📌 Only the latest 3 minor versions receive patches           │         │
  │  │  📌 Each minor version supported for ~14 months                │         │
  │  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘         │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Aspect Minor Release Patch Release
FrequencyEvery ~4 monthsMonthly (or as needed)
What changesNew features, API changesBug fixes, security patches
Example1.30 → 1.311.31.0 → 1.31.1
Can you skip?❌ No — upgrade one at a time✅ Yes — jump from .0 to .3
Downtime riskHigher — requires drain/restartLower — kubelet restart only
Planning neededRead release notes, test firstApply when available
Component Version Skew Policy
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │              COMPONENT VERSION SKEW RULES                                    │
  │                                                                              │
  │   The API server is the "anchor" — everything else is relative to it        │
  │                                                                              │
  │   ┌─────────────────┐                                                       │
  │   │  kube-apiserver  │  ◀── THIS sets the version baseline                  │
  │   │    v1.31         │                                                       │
  │   └────────┬────────┘                                                       │
  │            │                                                                 │
  │   ┌────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────┐                  │
  │   │        │  ALLOWED VERSION RANGES                      │                  │
  │   │        │                                              │                  │
  │   │  kubelet:         v1.30 or v1.31  (N-1 to N)         │                  │
  │   │  kube-proxy:      v1.30 or v1.31  (N-1 to N)         │                  │
  │   │  controller-mgr:  v1.31 only      (must match)       │                  │
  │   │  scheduler:       v1.31 only      (must match)       │                  │
  │   │  kubectl:         v1.30 to v1.32  (N-1 to N+1)      │                  │
  │   │                                                       │                  │
  │   │  ⚠️ kubelet CANNOT be newer than API server!          │                  │
  │   │  ⚠️ This is why you upgrade control plane FIRST!      │                  │
  │   └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘                  │
  │                                                                              │
  │   EXAMPLE: API server at v1.31                                               │
  │   ┌──────────┬───────────────┬──────────────┐                               │
  │   │Component │  OK Versions  │  NOT OK      │                               │
  │   ├──────────┼───────────────┼──────────────┤                               │
  │   │kubelet   │ 1.30, 1.31    │ 1.29, 1.32   │                               │
  │   │kubectl   │ 1.30,1.31,1.32│ 1.29         │                               │
  │   │scheduler │ 1.31          │ 1.30, 1.32   │                               │
  │   └──────────┴───────────────┴──────────────┘                               │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
⚠️ Why Control Plane First?

kubelet can be one minor version behind the API server, but never ahead. If you upgrade workers first, the kubelet would be v1.31 while the API server is still v1.30 — this violates the skew policy and causes unpredictable behavior. Always: control plane → workers.

28.2 Upgrade Strategy

A cluster upgrade follows a strict sequence. Think of it as a conveyor belt — each step must complete before the next begins.

Complete Cluster Upgrade Workflow — 10 Steps
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │         CLUSTER UPGRADE — COMPLETE WORKFLOW                                  │
  │                                                                              │
  │  ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════              │
  │  PHASE 1: CONTROL PLANE UPGRADE                                              │
  │  ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════              │
  │                                                                              │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────┐                                    │
  │  │ STEP 1: Upgrade kubeadm             │                                    │
  │  │ apt-mark unhold kubeadm             │                                    │
  │  │ apt-get install kubeadm=1.31.0-1.1  │                                    │
  │  │ apt-mark hold kubeadm               │                                    │
  │  └──────────────┬──────────────────────┘                                    │
  │                 ▼                                                            │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────┐                                    │
  │  │ STEP 2: Check upgrade plan          │                                    │
  │  │ kubeadm upgrade plan                │                                    │
  │  │ (shows available target versions)   │                                    │
  │  └──────────────┬──────────────────────┘                                    │
  │                 ▼                                                            │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────┐                                    │
  │  │ STEP 3: Apply upgrade               │  ◀── This upgrades API server,    │
  │  │ kubeadm upgrade apply v1.31.0       │      controller-mgr, scheduler,   │
  │  │                                     │      kube-proxy, CoreDNS, etcd     │
  │  └──────────────┬──────────────────────┘                                    │
  │                 ▼                                                            │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────┐                                    │
  │  │ STEP 4: Drain control plane node    │                                    │
  │  │ kubectl drain controlplane          │                                    │
  │  │   --ignore-daemonsets               │                                    │
  │  └──────────────┬──────────────────────┘                                    │
  │                 ▼                                                            │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────┐                                    │
  │  │ STEP 5: Upgrade kubelet + kubectl   │                                    │
  │  │ apt-get install kubelet=1.31.0-1.1  │                                    │
  │  │                kubectl=1.31.0-1.1   │                                    │
  │  └──────────────┬──────────────────────┘                                    │
  │                 ▼                                                            │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────┐                                    │
  │  │ STEP 6: Restart kubelet             │                                    │
  │  │ systemctl daemon-reload             │                                    │
  │  │ systemctl restart kubelet           │                                    │
  │  └──────────────┬──────────────────────┘                                    │
  │                 ▼                                                            │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────┐                                    │
  │  │ STEP 7: Uncordon control plane      │                                    │
  │  │ kubectl uncordon controlplane       │                                    │
  │  └─────────────────────────────────────┘                                    │
  │                                                                              │
  │  ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════              │
  │  PHASE 2: WORKER NODE UPGRADE (repeat for EACH worker)                       │
  │  ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════              │
  │                                                                              │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────┐                                    │
  │  │ STEP 8: Drain worker                │  ◀── Run from control plane       │
  │  │ kubectl drain worker01              │                                    │
  │  │   --ignore-daemonsets               │                                    │
  │  └──────────────┬──────────────────────┘                                    │
  │                 ▼                                                            │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────┐                                    │
  │  │ STEP 9: SSH to worker + upgrade     │  ◀── Run ON the worker node       │
  │  │ apt-get install kubeadm=1.31.0-1.1  │                                    │
  │  │ kubeadm upgrade node                │  ◀── NOT "upgrade apply"!         │
  │  │ apt-get install kubelet=1.31.0-1.1  │                                    │
  │  │ systemctl daemon-reload             │                                    │
  │  │ systemctl restart kubelet           │                                    │
  │  └──────────────┬──────────────────────┘                                    │
  │                 ▼                                                            │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────┐                                    │
  │  │ STEP 10: Uncordon worker            │  ◀── Run from control plane       │
  │  │ kubectl uncordon worker01           │                                    │
  │  │ kubectl get nodes (verify Ready)    │                                    │
  │  └─────────────────────────────────────┘                                    │
  │                                                                              │
  │  💡 Repeat Steps 8-10 for worker02, worker03, etc.                          │
  │  💡 Upgrade one worker at a time to maintain app availability!              │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
🔧
apt-mark hold

Prevents apt from auto-upgrading kubeadm, kubelet, or kubectl during apt-get upgrade. Always hold after installing.

📋
kubeadm upgrade plan

Shows available upgrade targets, pre-flight checks, and component versions. Run this BEFORE applying to verify everything is ready.

🔄
daemon-reload

Tells systemd to re-read unit files. Required after upgrading kubelet binary — without it, systemd runs the OLD version.

✅ Upgrade Best Practices

  • Always backup etcd BEFORE upgrading (Chapter 29)
  • Read release notes for breaking changes
  • Test upgrade in a staging cluster first
  • Upgrade one minor version at a time
  • Upgrade workers one at a time for availability
  • Verify kubectl get nodes shows correct version after each node

❌ Upgrade Anti-Patterns

  • Skipping minor versions (1.30 → 1.32)
  • Upgrading all workers simultaneously
  • Forgetting to drain before upgrading
  • Not running kubeadm upgrade plan first
  • Skipping systemctl daemon-reload
  • Not holding packages after install
📌 anihpj Upgrade Scenario

Your anihpj job board runs 3 replicas across 2 worker nodes. When you drain worker01, Kubernetes evicts the anihpj pods and the Deployment controller reschedules them on worker02. With a PodDisruptionBudget (Chapter 30) of minAvailable: 2, the drain ensures at least 2 replicas stay running throughout the upgrade.

✅ Pre-Upgrade Checklist — Before You Touch Anything
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                  PRE-UPGRADE CHECKLIST (CKA & Production)                │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                          │
│  □ 1. CHECK current versions                                             │
│       kubectl get nodes                                                  │
│       kubeadm version                                                    │
│       kubelet --version                                                  │
│                                                                          │
│  □ 2. BACKUP etcd (see Chapter 29)                                       │
│       ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot save /opt/pre-upgrade-backup.db \   │
│         --endpoints=... --cacert=... --cert=... --key=...                │
│                                                                          │
│  □ 3. READ the release notes                                             │
│       Check for removed APIs, deprecated features, breaking changes      │
│                                                                          │
│  □ 4. VERIFY cluster health                                              │
│       kubectl get cs            (component status — deprecated but useful)│
│       kubectl get nodes         (all nodes Ready?)                       │
│       kubectl get pods -A       (any pods in CrashLoop or Pending?)      │
│                                                                          │
│  □ 5. CHECK available versions                                           │
│       apt-cache madison kubeadm (see available versions in repo)         │
│       kubeadm upgrade plan      (see upgrade targets)                    │
│                                                                          │
│  □ 6. CONFIRM you're upgrading ONE minor version                         │
│       Current: v1.30.x → Target: v1.31.x ✅                             │
│       Current: v1.30.x → Target: v1.32.x ❌ (skip not allowed!)        │
│                                                                          │
│  □ 7. VERIFY sufficient capacity                                         │
│       Can remaining nodes handle ALL pods while one is being upgraded?   │
│       kubectl top nodes (check CPU/memory headroom)                      │
│                                                                          │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

📋 Upgrade Order — What Gets Upgraded When

Step Component Where Command
1 kubeadm Control plane apt-get install kubeadm=1.31.0-1.1
2 Control plane components Control plane kubeadm upgrade apply v1.31.0
3 kubelet + kubectl Control plane apt-get install kubelet=... kubectl=...
4 kubeadm Each worker (one at a time) apt-get install kubeadm=1.31.0-1.1
5 Node config Each worker kubeadm upgrade node
6 kubelet + kubectl Each worker apt-get install kubelet=... kubectl=...

28.3 Hands-On: Upgrading the Control Plane

🎯 CKA EXAM TOPIC

This is the exact workflow you'll perform on the CKA exam. Every command below maps directly to what the exam environment expects. Practice this sequence until it becomes muscle memory.

🔧 Control Plane Upgrade — Complete Command Flow
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                  CONTROL PLANE NODE UPGRADE PROCEDURE                    │
│                  (SSH into control-plane node first)                      │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                          │
│  PHASE 1 ─ UPGRADE kubeadm                                              │
│  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│  │ Step 1: Unhold kubeadm package                                    │   │
│  │ $ apt-mark unhold kubeadm                                         │   │
│  │                                                                    │   │
│  │ Step 2: Install target kubeadm version                            │   │
│  │ $ apt-get update && apt-get install -y kubeadm=1.31.0-1.1         │   │
│  │                                                                    │   │
│  │ Step 3: Hold kubeadm to prevent auto-updates                      │   │
│  │ $ apt-mark hold kubeadm                                           │   │
│  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│                               │                                          │
│                               ▼                                          │
│  PHASE 2 ─ PLAN & APPLY                                                 │
│  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│  │ Step 4: Verify upgrade plan (shows available versions)            │   │
│  │ $ kubeadm upgrade plan                                            │   │
│  │                                                                    │   │
│  │ Step 5: Apply the upgrade to control plane                        │   │
│  │ $ kubeadm upgrade apply v1.31.0                                   │   │
│  │         ▲                                                          │   │
│  │         └─ ONLY on first control-plane node!                      │   │
│  │            Additional control planes use: kubeadm upgrade node    │   │
│  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│                               │                                          │
│                               ▼                                          │
│  PHASE 3 ─ DRAIN & UPGRADE kubelet                                      │
│  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│  │ Step 6: Drain the control plane node                              │   │
│  │ $ kubectl drain controlplane --ignore-daemonsets                   │   │
│  │                                                                    │   │
│  │ Step 7: Upgrade kubelet & kubectl                                 │   │
│  │ $ apt-mark unhold kubelet kubectl                                 │   │
│  │ $ apt-get update && apt-get install -y \                          │   │
│  │       kubelet=1.31.0-1.1 kubectl=1.31.0-1.1                      │   │
│  │ $ apt-mark hold kubelet kubectl                                   │   │
│  │                                                                    │   │
│  │ Step 8: Restart kubelet                                           │   │
│  │ $ systemctl daemon-reload                                         │   │
│  │ $ systemctl restart kubelet                                       │   │
│  │         ▲                                                          │   │
│  │         └─ ⚠️ BOTH commands required! daemon-reload first!       │   │
│  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│                               │                                          │
│                               ▼                                          │
│  PHASE 4 ─ VERIFY & UNCORDON                                            │
│  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│  │ Step 9: Mark node schedulable again                               │   │
│  │ $ kubectl uncordon controlplane                                   │   │
│  │                                                                    │   │
│  │ Step 10: Verify upgrade                                           │   │
│  │ $ kubectl get nodes                                               │   │
│  │ NAME           STATUS   ROLES           VERSION                   │   │
│  │ controlplane   Ready    control-plane   v1.31.0  ← ✅ Updated   │   │
│  │ worker01       Ready    <none>          v1.30.0  ← Still old    │   │
│  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│                                                                          │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Step 1–3: Upgrade kubeadm Package

control-plane-node (SSH) 
# Step 1: Unhold kubeadm so we can upgrade it
apt-mark unhold kubeadm

# Step 2: Update repositories and install target version
apt-get update && apt-get install -y kubeadm=1.31.0-1.1

# Step 3: Hold kubeadm again to prevent accidental updates
apt-mark hold kubeadm

# Verify kubeadm version
kubeadm version

Step 4–5: Plan and Apply the Upgrade

control-plane-node (SSH) 
# Step 4: Check what upgrade is available
kubeadm upgrade plan
# Shows: Components that will be upgraded
#        Available versions
#        Any manual actions needed

# Step 5: Apply the upgrade (FIRST control-plane only!)
kubeadm upgrade apply v1.31.0
# ⚠️ For additional control-plane nodes, use:
#    kubeadm upgrade node
⚠️ apply vs node — Critical Distinction

kubeadm upgrade apply v1.31.0 — Used on the first control-plane node. Downloads new component images and upgrades the cluster configuration.

kubeadm upgrade node — Used on additional control-plane nodes AND all worker nodes. Only updates local kubelet config.

Step 6–8: Drain, Upgrade kubelet, Restart

control-plane-node (SSH) 
# Step 6: Drain the node (evict workloads, mark unschedulable)
kubectl drain controlplane --ignore-daemonsets

# Step 7: Upgrade kubelet and kubectl
apt-mark unhold kubelet kubectl
apt-get update && apt-get install -y kubelet=1.31.0-1.1 kubectl=1.31.0-1.1
apt-mark hold kubelet kubectl

# Step 8: Reload systemd config and restart kubelet
systemctl daemon-reload
systemctl restart kubelet

⚠️ Exam Trap: Forgetting daemon-reload

❌ Wrong✅ Correct
systemctl restart kubelet (only) systemctl daemon-reload && systemctl restart kubelet

After upgrading the kubelet binary, systemd still has the old unit file cached. Without daemon-reload, the restart may use the old configuration or fail silently.

Step 9–10: Uncordon and Verify

control-plane-node (SSH) 
# Step 9: Make node schedulable again
kubectl uncordon controlplane

# Step 10: Verify upgrade succeeded
kubectl get nodes
# Expected output:
# NAME           STATUS   ROLES           AGE   VERSION
# controlplane   Ready    control-plane   45d   v1.31.0
# worker01       Ready    <none>          45d   v1.30.0
💡 Complete Control Plane Upgrade — Copy-Paste Block

Run these commands in order on the control plane node. Replace version numbers with your target:

full-control-plane-upgrade.sh 
#!/bin/bash
# === COMPLETE CONTROL PLANE UPGRADE (v1.30 → v1.31) ===
TARGET=1.31.0-1.1
TARGET_SHORT=v1.31.0

# 1. Upgrade kubeadm
apt-mark unhold kubeadm
apt-get update && apt-get install -y kubeadm=${TARGET}
apt-mark hold kubeadm

# 2. Plan and apply
kubeadm upgrade plan
kubeadm upgrade apply ${TARGET_SHORT}

# 3. Drain, upgrade kubelet, restart
kubectl drain controlplane --ignore-daemonsets
apt-mark unhold kubelet kubectl
apt-get update && apt-get install -y kubelet=${TARGET} kubectl=${TARGET}
apt-mark hold kubelet kubectl
systemctl daemon-reload
systemctl restart kubelet

# 4. Uncordon and verify
kubectl uncordon controlplane
kubectl get nodes

28.4 Hands-On: Upgrading Worker Nodes

🎯 CKA EXAM TOPIC

After the control plane is upgraded, each worker node must be upgraded one at a time to maintain application availability. The process involves commands on two different nodes — pay close attention to where each command runs.

🔧 Worker Node Upgrade — Two-Node Command Flow
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              WORKER NODE UPGRADE — WHERE EACH COMMAND RUNS               │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                          │
│  🖥️ FROM CONTROL PLANE                  🖥️ SSH INTO WORKER NODE        │
│  ┌─────────────────────────┐            ┌─────────────────────────┐     │
│  │                         │            │                         │     │
│  │ Step 1: Drain worker    │            │                         │     │
│  │ $ kubectl drain \       │            │                         │     │
│  │   worker01 \            │            │                         │     │
│  │   --ignore-daemonsets \ │            │                         │     │
│  │   --delete-emptydir-data│            │                         │     │
│  │                         │───────────▶│ Step 2: SSH to worker   │     │
│  │                         │            │ $ ssh worker01          │     │
│  │                         │            │                         │     │
│  │                         │            │ Step 3: Upgrade kubeadm │     │
│  │                         │            │ $ apt-mark unhold \     │     │
│  │                         │            │   kubeadm               │     │
│  │                         │            │ $ apt-get update && \   │     │
│  │                         │            │   apt-get install -y \  │     │
│  │                         │            │   kubeadm=1.31.0-1.1    │     │
│  │                         │            │ $ apt-mark hold kubeadm │     │
│  │                         │            │                         │     │
│  │                         │            │ Step 4: Upgrade node    │     │
│  │                         │            │ $ kubeadm upgrade node  │     │
│  │                         │            │   ▲                     │     │
│  │                         │            │   └─ NOT "apply"!       │     │
│  │                         │            │                         │     │
│  │                         │            │ Step 5: Upgrade kubelet │     │
│  │                         │            │ $ apt-mark unhold \     │     │
│  │                         │            │   kubelet kubectl       │     │
│  │                         │            │ $ apt-get install -y \  │     │
│  │                         │            │   kubelet=1.31.0-1.1 \  │     │
│  │                         │            │   kubectl=1.31.0-1.1    │     │
│  │                         │            │ $ apt-mark hold \       │     │
│  │                         │            │   kubelet kubectl       │     │
│  │                         │            │                         │     │
│  │                         │            │ Step 6: Restart kubelet │     │
│  │                         │◀───────────│ $ systemctl daemon- \   │     │
│  │                         │            │   reload                │     │
│  │ Step 7: Uncordon        │            │ $ systemctl restart \   │     │
│  │ $ kubectl uncordon \    │            │   kubelet               │     │
│  │   worker01              │            │ $ exit                  │     │
│  │                         │            │                         │     │
│  └─────────────────────────┘            └─────────────────────────┘     │
│                                                                          │
│  Step 8: Verify     $ kubectl get nodes                                  │
│  NAME           STATUS   ROLES           VERSION                         │
│  controlplane   Ready    control-plane   v1.31.0  ✅                    │
│  worker01       Ready    <none>          v1.31.0  ✅                    │
│  worker02       Ready    <none>          v1.30.0  ← next               │
│                                                                          │
│  🔁 REPEAT Steps 1–8 for worker02, worker03, etc.                       │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

📊 Control Plane vs Worker Node Upgrade — Key Differences

Aspect Control Plane (First) Worker Node
kubeadm command kubeadm upgrade apply v1.31.0 kubeadm upgrade node
Drain from Same node (self-drain) Control plane (remote drain)
What it upgrades API server, controller-manager, scheduler, etcd kubelet config only
Order Must be upgraded FIRST After ALL control planes
Parallelism One at a time (HA: first with apply, rest with node) One at a time for availability
kubelet/kubectl Must upgrade + restart Must upgrade + restart

From Control Plane — Drain the Worker

controlplane (kubectl) 
# Drain the worker node from control plane
kubectl drain worker01 --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data

# Verify pods have been evicted
kubectl get pods -o wide | grep worker01
# Should show no pods (except DaemonSets)

On Worker Node — Upgrade Components

worker01 (SSH) 
# SSH into the worker node
ssh worker01

# Upgrade kubeadm
apt-mark unhold kubeadm
apt-get update && apt-get install -y kubeadm=1.31.0-1.1
apt-mark hold kubeadm

# Apply node upgrade (NOT "apply" — that's for first control-plane only!)
kubeadm upgrade node

# Upgrade kubelet and kubectl
apt-mark unhold kubelet kubectl
apt-get update && apt-get install -y kubelet=1.31.0-1.1 kubectl=1.31.0-1.1
apt-mark hold kubelet kubectl

# Restart kubelet (BOTH commands required!)
systemctl daemon-reload
systemctl restart kubelet

# Exit back to control plane
exit

Back on Control Plane — Uncordon and Verify

controlplane (kubectl) 
# Make worker schedulable again
kubectl uncordon worker01

# Verify ALL nodes are Ready and on correct version
kubectl get nodes
# NAME           STATUS   ROLES           AGE   VERSION
# controlplane   Ready    control-plane   45d   v1.31.0
# worker01       Ready    <none>          45d   v1.31.0  ← ✅
# worker02       Ready    <none>          45d   v1.30.0

# Repeat for worker02:
# kubectl drain worker02 --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data
# ssh worker02 → same upgrade steps → exit
# kubectl uncordon worker02
📌 anihpj Worker Upgrade — What Happens to Your Pods

When you drain worker01 where 2 of 3 anihpj replicas run:

  1. The 2 anihpj pods on worker01 receive SIGTERM and begin graceful shutdown
  2. The Deployment controller detects 2 missing replicas and creates replacements
  3. The scheduler places both new pods on worker02 (the only available node)
  4. Your PDB (minAvailable: 2) ensures at least 2 replicas stay running throughout
  5. After you uncordon worker01, the scheduler can use it again for future pod scheduling

⚠️ Exam Trap: apply vs node on Workers

❌ Wrong✅ Correct
kubeadm upgrade apply v1.31.0 (on worker) kubeadm upgrade node (on worker)

Using apply on a worker node will fail or cause unexpected behavior. Only the first control-plane node uses apply. All other nodes (additional control-planes AND workers) use kubeadm upgrade node.

🔬 Practice Drill 28.1 — Upgrade the Control Plane

⏱️ Target Time: 8 minutes
Scenario

Your cluster is running Kubernetes v1.30.0. You need to upgrade the control plane node named controlplane to v1.31.0. Complete the full upgrade process.

  1. Upgrade kubeadm to 1.31.0-1.1 on the control plane
  2. Run the upgrade plan and apply the upgrade
  3. Drain the control plane node
  4. Upgrade kubelet and kubectl to 1.31.0-1.1
  5. Restart kubelet and uncordon the node
  6. Verify the control plane shows v1.31.0
Hint: Remember the order — kubeadm first, then plan/apply, then drain, then kubelet/kubectl. Don't forget apt-mark hold after each install and daemon-reload before restart.
View Solution 
# On controlplane node:

# 1. Upgrade kubeadm
apt-mark unhold kubeadm
apt-get update && apt-get install -y kubeadm=1.31.0-1.1
apt-mark hold kubeadm

# 2. Plan and apply
kubeadm upgrade plan
kubeadm upgrade apply v1.31.0

# 3. Drain
kubectl drain controlplane --ignore-daemonsets

# 4. Upgrade kubelet and kubectl
apt-mark unhold kubelet kubectl
apt-get update && apt-get install -y kubelet=1.31.0-1.1 kubectl=1.31.0-1.1
apt-mark hold kubelet kubectl

# 5. Restart kubelet
systemctl daemon-reload
systemctl restart kubelet

# 6. Uncordon and verify
kubectl uncordon controlplane
kubectl get nodes
# controlplane should show v1.31.0

🔬 Practice Drill 28.2 — Upgrade a Worker Node

⏱️ Target Time: 6 minutes
Scenario

The control plane is already on v1.31.0. Now upgrade worker node worker01 to v1.31.0. Remember: commands run on two different nodes.

  1. Drain worker01 from the control plane
  2. SSH into worker01 and upgrade kubeadm
  3. Run kubeadm upgrade node (not apply!)
  4. Upgrade kubelet and kubectl
  5. Restart kubelet and exit back to control plane
  6. Uncordon worker01 and verify all nodes
Hint: On workers, use kubeadm upgrade node — NOT kubeadm upgrade apply. The drain and uncordon commands run from the control plane, not the worker.
View Solution 
# FROM CONTROL PLANE:
kubectl drain worker01 --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data

# SSH INTO WORKER01:
ssh worker01

# Upgrade kubeadm
apt-mark unhold kubeadm
apt-get update && apt-get install -y kubeadm=1.31.0-1.1
apt-mark hold kubeadm

# Upgrade node config (NOT apply!)
kubeadm upgrade node

# Upgrade kubelet and kubectl
apt-mark unhold kubelet kubectl
apt-get update && apt-get install -y kubelet=1.31.0-1.1 kubectl=1.31.0-1.1
apt-mark hold kubelet kubectl

# Restart kubelet
systemctl daemon-reload
systemctl restart kubelet
exit

# BACK ON CONTROL PLANE:
kubectl uncordon worker01
kubectl get nodes
# worker01 should show v1.31.0

🔬 Practice Drill 28.3 — Find the Correct Package Version

⏱️ Target Time: 2 minutes
Scenario

You need to upgrade your cluster but aren't sure what package versions are available. Find all available kubeadm versions for 1.31 in the apt repository.

  1. List available kubeadm versions using apt-cache
  2. Identify the correct version string to use with apt-get install
Hint: Use apt-cache madison to list available package versions from repositories.
View Solution 
# List all available versions of kubeadm
apt-cache madison kubeadm

# Example output:
#    kubeadm |  1.31.0-1.1 | https://pkgs.k8s.io/...
#    kubeadm |  1.30.4-1.1 | https://pkgs.k8s.io/...
#    kubeadm |  1.30.0-1.1 | https://pkgs.k8s.io/...

# The version string to use is the second column: 1.31.0-1.1
# Usage: apt-get install -y kubeadm=1.31.0-1.1

✅ Chapter 28 — Verification Checklist

  • kubectl get nodes — All nodes show expected version
  • kubeadm version — kubeadm matches target version
  • kubelet --version — kubelet matches target version
  • kubectl version --short — kubectl matches target version
  • kubectl get pods -n kube-system — All control plane pods running
  • kubectl get nodes -o wide — All nodes Ready, no SchedulingDisabled
  • apt-mark showhold — kubeadm, kubelet, kubectl are held

⚠️ CKA Gotcha: Cluster Upgrade Traps

  • Upgrade order is rigid — Always: kubeadm → control plane → kubelet+kubectl → worker nodes. Never upgrade workers before the control plane. Never skip minor versions (1.30→1.32 is NOT allowed, must go 1.30→1.31→1.32)
  • Drain before upgrading workers — kubectl drain <node> --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data. Forgetting --ignore-daemonsets causes the drain to fail. Forgetting to drain means workloads crash during kubelet restart
  • apt-mark hold after install — Always hold kubeadm, kubelet, kubectl after upgrading: apt-mark hold kubeadm kubelet kubectl. This prevents accidental upgrades via apt upgrade
  • Control plane static pods restart automatically — After kubeadm upgrade apply, the API server, controller-manager, and scheduler restart. There's a brief disruption — the exam expects you to know this
  • Version skew policy — kubelet can be up to 3 minor versions behind the API server. kubectl can be ±1 minor version. kube-proxy must match kubelet. The exam tests awareness of these constraints

CKA Speed Tip — Upgrade workflow memorization:
Control plane:
apt-get update && apt-get install -y kubeadm=1.31.0-1.1
kubeadm upgrade plan → kubeadm upgrade apply v1.31.0
apt-get install -y kubelet=1.31.0-1.1 kubectl=1.31.0-1.1
systemctl daemon-reload && systemctl restart kubelet
Worker:
kubectl drain <node> --ignore-daemonsets (from control plane)
SSH to worker → upgrade kubeadm → kubeadm upgrade node → upgrade kubelet → restart
kubectl uncordon <node> (from control plane)

⚡ Upgrade commands: kubeadm upgrade plan — shows available upgrade targets
kubeadm upgrade apply v1.31.0 — upgrade control plane (first node only)
kubeadm upgrade node — upgrade additional control planes and workers
kubectl get nodes — verify versions after upgrade
apt-cache madison kubeadm — find available package versions

📝 Chapter 28 — CKA Practice Questions

Q1
Upgrade the control plane node from Kubernetes 1.30.0 to 1.31.0. Show the exact commands.
Show Answer
Answer
# Step 1: Upgrade kubeadm
sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get install -y --allow-change-held-packages \
  kubeadm=1.31.0-1.1
sudo apt-mark hold kubeadm

# Step 2: Verify kubeadm version
kubeadm version
# kubeadm version: v1.31.0

# Step 3: Check upgrade plan
sudo kubeadm upgrade plan
# Components that must be upgraded:
# COMPONENT   CURRENT   TARGET
# kube-apiserver    v1.30.0   v1.31.0
# kube-controller-manager ...
# kube-scheduler ...
# Available versions: v1.31.0

# Step 4: Apply the upgrade
sudo kubeadm upgrade apply v1.31.0
# [upgrade] Successfully upgraded cluster to v1.31.0

# Step 5: Upgrade kubelet and kubectl
sudo apt-get install -y --allow-change-held-packages \
  kubelet=1.31.0-1.1 kubectl=1.31.0-1.1
sudo apt-mark hold kubelet kubectl

# Step 6: Restart kubelet
sudo systemctl daemon-reload
sudo systemctl restart kubelet

# Step 7: Verify
kubectl get nodes
# NAME           STATUS   ROLES          VERSION
# controlplane   Ready    control-plane  v1.31.0
Explanation

Upgrade order is CRITICAL: kubeadm first → kubeadm upgrade apply → kubelet + kubectl → restart. Always apt-mark hold after installing to prevent accidental upgrades. kubeadm upgrade plan shows what will be upgraded and if there are prerequisites. On the CKA, this exact sequence is tested — you must know it by heart. Static pods (API server, etcd, etc.) restart automatically during kubeadm upgrade apply.

Q2
Upgrade a worker node from 1.30.0 to 1.31.0. Include draining and uncordoning.
Show Answer
Answer
# FROM CONTROL PLANE:
# Step 1: Drain the worker node
kubectl drain worker-1 --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data
# node/worker-1 cordoned
# evicting pod default/web-app-xxxxx
# pod/web-app-xxxxx evicted

# ON WORKER NODE (ssh worker-1):
# Step 2: Upgrade kubeadm
sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get install -y --allow-change-held-packages \
  kubeadm=1.31.0-1.1
sudo apt-mark hold kubeadm

# Step 3: Upgrade node config
sudo kubeadm upgrade node
# [upgrade] Successfully upgraded node config

# Step 4: Upgrade kubelet and kubectl
sudo apt-get install -y --allow-change-held-packages \
  kubelet=1.31.0-1.1 kubectl=1.31.0-1.1
sudo apt-mark hold kubelet kubectl

# Step 5: Restart kubelet
sudo systemctl daemon-reload
sudo systemctl restart kubelet

# BACK ON CONTROL PLANE:
# Step 6: Uncordon the worker
kubectl uncordon worker-1
# node/worker-1 uncordoned

# Step 7: Verify
kubectl get nodes
# NAME           STATUS   ROLES          VERSION
# controlplane   Ready    control-plane  v1.31.0
# worker-1       Ready    <none>         v1.31.0
Explanation

Worker upgrade: drain (from control plane) → SSH to worker → upgrade kubeadm → kubeadm upgrade node (NOT apply) → upgrade kubelet/kubectl → restart → uncordon (from control plane). Key differences from control plane: workers use kubeadm upgrade node (not apply), and you MUST drain before upgrading. Forgetting to uncordon is a common CKA mistake — the node stays SchedulingDisabled.

Q3
What is the Kubernetes version skew policy? Which component version relationships must be maintained?
Show Answer
Answer
Component Relative to API Server Example (API=1.31)
kubelet Up to 3 minor behind 1.28 – 1.31
kube-proxy Same or 3 minor behind 1.28 – 1.31
kubectl ±1 minor version 1.30 – 1.32
controller-manager Same minor version 1.31 only
scheduler Same minor version 1.31 only 
# Key rules:
# 1. NEVER skip minor versions (1.30→1.32 = INVALID)
# 2. Upgrade control plane BEFORE workers
# 3. API server is always the newest component

# Check current versions:
kubectl get nodes -o wide
kubectl version --short
Explanation

The version skew policy ensures component compatibility. Key rules: kubelet can lag up to 3 minor versions behind the API server, kubectl is ±1, and control plane components must match. You cannot skip minor versions during upgrades (must upgrade sequentially). On the CKA, they may show you a cluster with mixed versions and ask if it's valid. The API server is always the "baseline" — everything is measured relative to it.

Q4
How do you find available Kubernetes package versions for upgrade?
Show Answer
Answer
# List all available kubeadm versions:
apt-cache madison kubeadm
#    kubeadm |  1.31.0-1.1 | https://pkgs.k8s.io ...
#    kubeadm |  1.30.2-1.1 | https://pkgs.k8s.io ...
#    kubeadm |  1.30.1-1.1 | https://pkgs.k8s.io ...
#    kubeadm |  1.30.0-1.1 | https://pkgs.k8s.io ...

# OR with apt-cache policy:
apt-cache policy kubeadm
# Shows installed version and available versions

# Check if kubeadm repo is configured:
cat /etc/apt/sources.list.d/kubernetes.list

# Important: You may need to change the repo
# for a different minor version:
# Edit /etc/apt/sources.list.d/kubernetes.list:
# deb https://pkgs.k8s.io/core:/stable:/v1.31/deb/ /

# Check current installed versions:
kubeadm version
kubelet --version
kubectl version --client

# Check held packages:
apt-mark showhold
# kubeadm kubelet kubectl
Explanation

apt-cache madison kubeadm lists all available versions — use this to find the exact version string for install commands. On the CKA, the version string format matters: 1.31.0-1.1 (not just 1.31.0). If upgrading to a new minor version, you may need to update the apt repository URL first. Always check apt-mark showhold to see what's being held back.

Q5
After running kubeadm upgrade apply, the API server doesn't come back up. How do you troubleshoot?
Show Answer
Answer
# Step 1: Check kubelet status
sudo systemctl status kubelet
# If inactive: sudo systemctl start kubelet

# Step 2: Check kubelet logs
sudo journalctl -u kubelet -l --no-pager | tail -50
# Look for: manifests error, cert error, port in use

# Step 3: Check static pod manifests
ls -la /etc/kubernetes/manifests/
# Should have: kube-apiserver.yaml, kube-controller-manager.yaml,
#              kube-scheduler.yaml, etcd.yaml

# Step 4: Check container runtime
sudo crictl ps -a | grep apiserver
# If exited: check logs
sudo crictl logs <container-id>

# Step 5: Common fix — daemon-reload
sudo systemctl daemon-reload
sudo systemctl restart kubelet

# Step 6: Check if kubelet was upgraded
kubelet --version
# If still old version → apt-get install kubelet=target

# Step 7: Port conflict check
sudo ss -tlnp | grep 6443
# If something else is on 6443, kill it

# Step 8: Wait and check
# Static pods take 30-60 seconds to restart
sleep 60
kubectl get nodes
Explanation

Post-upgrade API server failure: (1) check kubelet is running, (2) read kubelet logs, (3) verify manifests exist, (4) check container runtime with crictl, (5) ensure kubelet was also upgraded. Most common issue: forgetting systemctl daemon-reload after upgrading kubelet. On the CKA, give the API server 60 seconds to come up before panicking — static pods restart can be slow.

Q6
What is the difference between kubeadm upgrade apply and kubeadm upgrade node?
Show Answer
Answer
Command Used On What It Does
kubeadm upgrade apply v1.31.0 First control plane node Upgrades cluster components, updates ConfigMaps, re-generates static pod manifests
kubeadm upgrade node Additional control planes + ALL workers Updates local kubelet config and static pods (if any), does NOT upgrade cluster-level components 
# Summary:
# First control plane: kubeadm upgrade apply v1.31.0
# Second control plane: kubeadm upgrade node
# Third control plane: kubeadm upgrade node
# Worker-1: kubeadm upgrade node
# Worker-2: kubeadm upgrade node
# Worker-N: kubeadm upgrade node

# NEVER run "kubeadm upgrade apply" on workers!
# NEVER run "kubeadm upgrade apply" on non-first control planes!
Explanation

kubeadm upgrade apply is for the FIRST control plane only — it upgrades cluster-wide components (API server manifest, CoreDNS, kube-proxy, etc.). kubeadm upgrade node is for everything else (additional control planes and all workers). On the CKA, using the wrong command is a common mistake. apply requires a version argument; node doesn't (it reads the target version from the cluster).

Q7
Why do you need systemctl daemon-reload after upgrading kubelet?
Show Answer
Answer
# The kubelet package installs a systemd unit file:
# /usr/lib/systemd/system/kubelet.service

# The kubelet drop-in config:
# /usr/lib/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10-kubeadm.conf

# When the package is upgraded, these files may change
# systemd CACHES the unit files in memory
# daemon-reload tells systemd to re-read them

sudo systemctl daemon-reload    # ← Re-read unit files
sudo systemctl restart kubelet  # ← Restart with new binary

# Without daemon-reload:
# systemd may use OLD unit configuration
# kubelet may fail with config mismatch
# Or use deprecated flags from old service file

# Full upgrade sequence for kubelet:
sudo apt-get install -y kubelet=1.31.0-1.1
sudo apt-mark hold kubelet
sudo systemctl daemon-reload    # ← MUST do this
sudo systemctl restart kubelet  # ← MUST do this

# Verify:
kubelet --version
# Kubernetes v1.31.0
sudo systemctl status kubelet
# Active: active (running)
Explanation

daemon-reload tells systemd to re-read all unit files from disk. Without it, systemd uses cached (old) configuration, which can cause the kubelet to fail or use wrong settings. On the CKA, daemon-reload + restart kubelet is required after every kubelet binary upgrade. Forgetting this step is a classic exam mistake — the kubelet appears upgraded but behaves incorrectly.

Q8
How do you verify that a cluster upgrade was successful?
Show Answer
Answer
# Step 1: Check node versions
kubectl get nodes
# NAME           STATUS   ROLES          VERSION
# controlplane   Ready    control-plane  v1.31.0
# worker-1       Ready    <none>         v1.31.0
# All nodes should show new version

# Step 2: Check all nodes are Ready
kubectl get nodes
# All should be Ready, not NotReady

# Step 3: Check system pods
kubectl get pods -n kube-system
# All should be Running (not CrashLoopBackOff)
# Key pods: kube-apiserver, kube-controller-manager,
#           kube-scheduler, etcd, coredns, kube-proxy

# Step 4: Check component health
kubectl get componentstatuses  # (deprecated but still works)
# OR
kubectl get --raw /healthz
# ok

# Step 5: Verify workloads
kubectl get pods -A
# Check no unexpected restarts or pending pods

# Step 6: Check no nodes are cordoned
kubectl get nodes
# Should NOT see SchedulingDisabled
# If seen: kubectl uncordon <node>

# Step 7: Verify version details
kubectl version
# Client Version: v1.31.0
# Server Version: v1.31.0
Explanation

Post-upgrade verification checklist: (1) all nodes show new version, (2) all nodes are Ready, (3) system pods are Running, (4) cluster is healthy, (5) workloads are unaffected, (6) no nodes left cordoned. On the CKA, verifying after the upgrade is part of the task. The most common post-upgrade issue: worker nodes still cordoned (forgot uncordon) or kubelet not restarted.

Q9
What happens to running workloads during a control plane upgrade?
Show Answer
Answer
# During kubeadm upgrade apply:
# ✓ Running pods on worker nodes → UNAFFECTED
# ✓ Already-scheduled pods keep running
# ✗ API server briefly unavailable (seconds)
# ✗ Cannot create/delete/modify resources during restart
# ✗ kubectl commands may timeout temporarily

# What restarts:
# kube-apiserver         → brief downtime
# kube-controller-manager → auto-recovers
# kube-scheduler         → auto-recovers
# etcd                   → auto-recovers (if single node)

# What does NOT restart:
# kubelet (until you manually upgrade and restart it)
# kube-proxy (DaemonSet, updated via rolling update)
# CoreDNS (Deployment, updated via rolling update)

# Impact on workloads:
# - Existing pods: keep running (kubelet manages them)
# - New scheduling: paused during API server restart
# - Services/networking: unaffected (kube-proxy still runs)
# - DNS: unaffected (CoreDNS still runs)

# For zero-downtime upgrades:
# Use HA control plane (multiple API servers)
# Upgrade one control plane at a time
Explanation

Control plane upgrade causes brief API server downtime but does NOT affect running pods. Workers keep running independently. On the CKA, understand: existing pods survive, new scheduling pauses, kubectl commands fail briefly. For HA clusters, upgrade one control plane at a time for zero-downtime. The kubelet continues managing pods locally even without API server connectivity.

Q10
You need to upgrade a cluster but apt-cache madison kubeadm doesn't show the target version. What's wrong?
Show Answer
Answer
# The apt repository is versioned by minor release
# Each minor version has its own repo URL

# Check current repo:
cat /etc/apt/sources.list.d/kubernetes.list
# deb https://pkgs.k8s.io/core:/stable:/v1.30/deb/ /
#                                        ^^^^ Current minor

# Fix: Update to target minor version repo:
sudo vi /etc/apt/sources.list.d/kubernetes.list
# Change v1.30 → v1.31:
# deb https://pkgs.k8s.io/core:/stable:/v1.31/deb/ /

# OR use sed:
sudo sed -i 's/v1.30/v1.31/' \
  /etc/apt/sources.list.d/kubernetes.list

# Then update:
sudo apt-get update

# Now check again:
apt-cache madison kubeadm
# kubeadm | 1.31.0-1.1 | ... ← Now visible!

# Continue with normal upgrade:
sudo apt-get install -y kubeadm=1.31.0-1.1
Explanation

Since Kubernetes 1.28+, packages are hosted in version-specific repositories. Each minor version (1.30, 1.31) has its own repo URL. If madison doesn't show the target version, the repo needs updating. On the CKA, the exam environment may already have the correct repo configured, but if not, you need to know how to update it. Always apt-get update after changing the repo.

Chapter 29: etcd Backup and Restore

etcd is the single source of truth for your entire cluster. Every pod, service, secret, RBAC rule, and configuration lives in etcd. Losing etcd without a backup means losing your entire cluster state. This chapter covers the backup and restore procedures that are almost guaranteed to appear on the CKA exam.

🔴 Advanced ⏱️ ~1.5 hours 💾 etcd Backup 📝 CKA EXAM

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ Why etcd backup is critical — understand what's stored in etcd
  • ✅ Perform etcd snapshot backup — memorize the 4 critical flags
  • ✅ Restore from snapshot — full restore procedure with data-dir change
  • ✅ Automate backups — create CronJob for scheduled etcd snapshots
  • ✅ Find etcd certificates — locate cert paths from pod manifests

📋 CKA Exam Relevance

Domain: Cluster Architecture, Installation & Configuration — 25%

Likelihood: Almost guaranteed to appear. etcd backup/restore is one of the most frequently tested topics on the CKA exam. You MUST practice this until you can do it in under 8 minutes.

💡 CKA Exam Tip

  • The exam will tell you the backup path and may or may not give you cert paths
  • If cert paths aren't given, check the etcd pod: kubectl describe pod etcd-controlplane -n kube-system
  • Always set ETCDCTL_API=3 — version 2 uses different commands
  • After restore, you need to update the etcd manifest's --data-dir and hostPath

⌨️ etcd Command Reference

ActionCommand
Take snapshotETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot save <path>
Verify snapshotETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot status <path> --write-table
Restore snapshotETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot restore <path> --data-dir=<new-dir>
Check etcd healthETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl endpoint health
List etcd membersETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl member list
Find cert pathskubectl describe pod etcd-controlplane -n kube-system
Check etcd podkubectl get pods -n kube-system -l component=etcd
View etcd manifestcat /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml

⚠️ etcd Backup/Restore Exam Traps

❌ Wrong✅ Correct
Forgetting ETCDCTL_API=3Always prefix with ETCDCTL_API=3 or export ETCDCTL_API=3
Missing any of the 4 cert flagsInclude --endpoints, --cacert, --cert, --key
Restoring to the same --data-dirRestore to a NEW directory, then update etcd manifest
Using wrong cert pathsCheck kubectl describe pod etcd-* for actual paths
Not waiting for etcd pod restartAfter manifest change, wait for kubelet to restart etcd
Forgetting to update hostPath volumeBoth --data-dir arg AND hostPath must point to new dir

🚀 Quick Reference

  • ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot save /opt/backup.db --endpoints=... --cacert=... --cert=... --key=...
  • ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot status /opt/backup.db --write-table
  • ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot restore /opt/backup.db --data-dir=/var/lib/etcd-restored
  • kubectl describe pod etcd-controlplane -n kube-system | grep -A5 'Command\|--cert\|--key\|--cacert'
💾 4 Flags Required for Backup
📁 3 Cert Files ca.crt, server.crt, server.key
⏱️ < 8 min Target Time
🎯 ~100% Exam Likelihood

29.1 Why etcd Backup is Critical

etcd is a distributed key-value store that holds the entire state of your Kubernetes cluster. Without etcd, the API server has no data to serve — your cluster effectively doesn't exist.

💾 What etcd Contains — Your Entire Cluster State
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                        etcd KEY-VALUE STORE                              │
│                   (Single Source of Truth)                                │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                          │
│  📦 Workloads           🌐 Networking          🔐 Security              │
│  ┌─────────────────┐   ┌─────────────────┐   ┌─────────────────┐       │
│  │ All Pods         │   │ All Services     │   │ All Secrets      │       │
│  │ All Deployments  │   │ All Ingresses    │   │ All RBAC Rules   │       │
│  │ All StatefulSets │   │ All NetworkPols  │   │ All ServiceAccts │       │
│  │ All DaemonSets   │   │ All Endpoints    │   │ All CertSignReqs │       │
│  │ All Jobs/CronJobs│   │ DNS Records      │   │ All ClusterRoles │       │
│  └─────────────────┘   └─────────────────┘   └─────────────────┘       │
│                                                                          │
│  ⚙️ Configuration       📊 Storage            🏷️ Metadata              │
│  ┌─────────────────┐   ┌─────────────────┐   ┌─────────────────┐       │
│  │ All ConfigMaps   │   │ PersistentVolumes│   │ Namespaces       │       │
│  │ All ResourceQuota│   │ PV Claims       │   │ Labels/Annotns   │       │
│  │ All LimitRanges  │   │ StorageClasses   │   │ Finalizers       │       │
│  │ Custom Resources │   │ VolumeAttach     │   │ OwnerReferences  │       │
│  │ Admission Config │   │ CSI Drivers      │   │ Resource Versions│       │
│  └─────────────────┘   └─────────────────┘   └─────────────────┘       │
│                                                                          │
│  ⚠️  LOSE etcd = LOSE EVERYTHING ABOVE                                  │
│  ✅  BACKUP etcd = CAN RESTORE ENTIRE CLUSTER TO THAT POINT IN TIME    │
│                                                                          │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
🏗️ etcd in the Kubernetes Architecture
                    ┌─────────────────────────┐
                    │       kubectl            │
                    │   (User Commands)        │
                    └────────────┬────────────┘
                                 │ REST API
                                 ▼
                    ┌─────────────────────────┐
                    │      API Server          │
                    │  (kube-apiserver)         │
                    └────────────┬────────────┘
                                 │
                    ┌────────────┼────────────┐
                    │            │            │
                    ▼            ▼            ▼
              ┌──────────┐ ┌──────────┐ ┌──────────┐
              │Controller│ │Scheduler │ │  etcd    │ ◀── THE BRAIN
              │ Manager  │ │          │ │          │     All state
              └──────────┘ └──────────┘ │  ┌────┐  │     lives here
                                        │  │ DB │  │
                                        │  └────┘  │
                                        └──────────┘
                                             │
                              ┌──────────────┼──────────────┐
                              │              │              │
                        ┌──────────┐   ┌──────────┐   ┌──────────┐
                        │  Node 1  │   │  Node 2  │   │  Node 3  │
                        │ kubelet  │   │ kubelet  │   │ kubelet  │
                        └──────────┘   └──────────┘   └──────────┘
⚠️ Real-World Disaster Scenarios
  • Disk failure on the etcd node — all cluster state lost
  • Accidental deletion — someone deletes all namespaces
  • Botched upgrade — etcd data corrupted during cluster upgrade
  • Security breach — attacker modifies RBAC rules and secrets

In every case, a recent etcd snapshot lets you restore the cluster to a known-good state.

📌 anihpj — What's at Stake

Your anihpj job board has: 3 Deployment replicas, a Service, ConfigMaps with database URLs, Secrets with credentials, RBAC roles for the team, and NetworkPolicies. Without etcd backup, a disk failure means manually recreating ALL of this from scratch — assuming you even remember every configuration.

29.2 etcd Backup

🎯 CKA EXAM TOPIC

The backup uses etcdctl snapshot save and requires 4 critical flags for TLS authentication. Memorize these flags — you'll need them on the exam.

🔗
--endpoints

Where etcd listens for connections.
https://127.0.0.1:2379
From: --listen-client-urls in pod spec

🛡️
--cacert

CA certificate for TLS verification.
/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt
From: --trusted-ca-file in pod spec

📜
--cert

Server TLS certificate.
/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt
From: --cert-file in pod spec

🔑
--key

Server private key.
/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key
From: --key-file in pod spec

🔑 The 4 Critical Flags — MEMORIZE THESE
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              etcd BACKUP — THE 4 REQUIRED TLS FLAGS                      │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                          │
│  Flag              │ Purpose                  │ Default Path             │
│  ──────────────────┼──────────────────────────┼────────────────────────  │
│  --endpoints       │ Where etcd listens       │ https://127.0.0.1:2379  │
│  --cacert          │ CA certificate           │ /etc/kubernetes/pki/     │
│                    │                          │   etcd/ca.crt            │
│  --cert            │ Server certificate       │ /etc/kubernetes/pki/     │
│                    │                          │   etcd/server.crt        │
│  --key             │ Server private key       │ /etc/kubernetes/pki/     │
│                    │                          │   etcd/server.key        │
│                                                                          │
│  💡 TIP: Check actual paths from etcd pod:                               │
│  $ kubectl describe pod etcd-controlplane -n kube-system                │
│  Look under Command: for --cert-file, --key-file, --trusted-ca-file    │
│                                                                          │
│  Certificate Directory Structure:                                        │
│  /etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/                                               │
│  ├── ca.crt          ← --cacert (--trusted-ca-file in pod)              │
│  ├── ca.key                                                              │
│  ├── server.crt      ← --cert   (--cert-file in pod)                    │
│  ├── server.key      ← --key    (--key-file in pod)                     │
│  ├── peer.crt                                                            │
│  ├── peer.key                                                            │
│  ├── healthcheck-client.crt                                              │
│  └── healthcheck-client.key                                              │
│                                                                          │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Step 1: Find Certificate Paths

find-etcd-certs.sh 
# Method 1: From the etcd pod spec
kubectl describe pod etcd-controlplane -n kube-system

# Look for these lines in the Command section:
# --cert-file=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt
# --key-file=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key
# --trusted-ca-file=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt
# --listen-client-urls=https://127.0.0.1:2379,...

# Method 2: From the etcd static pod manifest
cat /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml | grep -E 'cert-file|key-file|trusted-ca|listen-client'

# Method 3: Quick grep for just the values
kubectl get pod etcd-controlplane -n kube-system -o yaml | grep -E 'cert|key|ca\.'

Step 2: Take the Snapshot

etcd-backup.sh 
# Take etcd snapshot backup
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot save /opt/etcd-backup.db \
  --endpoints=https://127.0.0.1:2379 \
  --cacert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt \
  --cert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt \
  --key=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key

# ✅ Output: Snapshot saved at /opt/etcd-backup.db

Step 3: Verify the Backup

verify-backup.sh 
# Verify the snapshot is valid
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot status /opt/etcd-backup.db --write-table

# Example output:
# +----------+----------+------------+------------+
# |   HASH   | REVISION | TOTAL KEYS | TOTAL SIZE |
# +----------+----------+------------+------------+
# | 5d16a713 |    38504 |       1234 |     2.1 MB |
# +----------+----------+------------+------------+

# Also check file exists and has reasonable size
ls -lh /opt/etcd-backup.db

📊 Mapping: etcd Pod Arguments → etcdctl Flags

etcd Pod Argument etcdctl Flag What It Is
--listen-client-urls --endpoints Where etcd accepts client connections
--trusted-ca-file --cacert CA certificate for verification
--cert-file --cert Server TLS certificate
--key-file --key Server TLS private key
💡 Exam Strategy: Build the Command Step by Step
  1. Start with: ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot save /path/backup.db
  2. Run: kubectl describe pod etcd-controlplane -n kube-system
  3. Find the 3 cert paths from the Command section
  4. Find the endpoint from --listen-client-urls
  5. Append all 4 flags to your command

Backup Procedure — Quick Process

1
Find Certs

kubectl describe pod etcd-* -n kube-system — Locate --trusted-ca-file, --cert-file, --key-file, and --listen-client-urls.

2
Take Snapshot

ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot save <path> with all 4 TLS flags. Snapshot is written to disk immediately.

3
Verify Backup

ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot status <path> --write-table — Confirm HASH, REVISION, TOTAL KEYS, and SIZE are valid.

29.3 etcd Restore

🎯 CKA EXAM TOPIC

Restoring from a snapshot is a multi-step process. You restore to a new data directory, then update the etcd static pod manifest to point to it. The kubelet detects the manifest change and restarts etcd with the restored data.

💾 Backup Command

ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot save /opt/backup.db

  • Needs 4 TLS flags (endpoints, cacert, cert, key)
  • Creates a single snapshot file
  • Non-disruptive (cluster keeps running)
  • No manifest changes needed
🔄 Restore Command

ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot restore /opt/backup.db --data-dir=<new>

  • No TLS flags needed (works locally)
  • Must restore to a NEW directory
  • Requires manifest changes (data-dir + hostPath)
  • Causes etcd pod restart (~30-60s downtime)
🔄 etcd Restore — Complete Flow
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    etcd SNAPSHOT RESTORE PROCEDURE                        │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                          │
│  Step 1: RESTORE snapshot to NEW directory                               │
│  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│  │ $ ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot restore /opt/etcd-backup.db \   │   │
│  │     --data-dir=/var/lib/etcd-restored                             │   │
│  │                                                                    │   │
│  │  /var/lib/etcd           /var/lib/etcd-restored                   │   │
│  │  ├── member/    (OLD)    ├── member/    (RESTORED)                │   │
│  │  └── ...                 └── ...                                   │   │
│  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│                               │                                          │
│                               ▼                                          │
│  Step 2: EDIT etcd static pod manifest                                   │
│  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│  │ $ vi /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml                          │   │
│  │                                                                    │   │
│  │ Change TWO things:                                                 │   │
│  │                                                                    │   │
│  │ a) Command argument:                                               │   │
│  │    --data-dir=/var/lib/etcd                                        │   │
│  │              ↓                                                     │   │
│  │    --data-dir=/var/lib/etcd-restored                               │   │
│  │                                                                    │   │
│  │ b) Volume hostPath:                                                │   │
│  │    hostPath:                                                       │   │
│  │      path: /var/lib/etcd                                           │   │
│  │              ↓                                                     │   │
│  │    hostPath:                                                       │   │
│  │      path: /var/lib/etcd-restored                                  │   │
│  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│                               │                                          │
│                               ▼                                          │
│  Step 3: WAIT for etcd pod to restart                                    │
│  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│  │ kubelet detects manifest change → restarts etcd pod               │   │
│  │ Wait 30-60 seconds, then verify:                                  │   │
│  │                                                                    │   │
│  │ $ kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l component=etcd               │   │
│  │ $ kubectl get all --all-namespaces                                │   │
│  │                                                                    │   │
│  │ ✅ All previous resources should be back!                         │   │
│  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│                                                                          │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Step 1: Restore the Snapshot

etcd-restore.sh 
# Restore snapshot to a NEW data directory
# ⚠️ Do NOT restore to /var/lib/etcd — use a NEW path!
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot restore /opt/etcd-backup.db \
  --data-dir=/var/lib/etcd-restored

# Verify the restored directory exists
ls -la /var/lib/etcd-restored/
# Should show: member/ directory

Step 2: Update the etcd Static Pod Manifest

/etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml 
# Edit the etcd manifest:
# $ vi /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml

# CHANGE 1: Update the --data-dir argument
spec:
  containers:
  - command:
    - etcd
    - --data-dir=/var/lib/etcd-restored     # ← Changed from /var/lib/etcd
    - --cert-file=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt
    # ... other flags unchanged ...

# CHANGE 2: Update the hostPath volume mount
  volumes:
  - hostPath:
      path: /var/lib/etcd-restored           # ← Changed from /var/lib/etcd
      type: DirectoryOrCreate
    name: etcd-data

Step 3: Wait and Verify

verify-restore.sh 
# Wait for etcd pod to restart (kubelet watches manifest dir)
# This can take 30-60 seconds

# Watch etcd pod come back
watch kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l component=etcd

# Once etcd is running, verify cluster state is restored
kubectl get all --all-namespaces
kubectl get nodes

# Verify the anihpj deployment is back
