kubectl get deployments -n default
# Should show your anihpj deployment with 3 replicas

⚠️ Exam Trap: The Two Changes You Must Make

ChangeWhereWhat to Change
1. Command arg spec.containers[0].command --data-dir=/var/lib/etcd → --data-dir=/var/lib/etcd-restored
2. Volume hostPath spec.volumes[0].hostPath.path /var/lib/etcd → /var/lib/etcd-restored

If you only change the --data-dir but not the hostPath, etcd will mount the old data directory into the container and ignore your restored data!

💡 Complete Restore — Copy-Paste Block

Full restore procedure in one block:

full-etcd-restore.sh 
#!/bin/bash
# === COMPLETE etcd RESTORE PROCEDURE ===

# 1. Restore snapshot to new directory
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot restore /opt/etcd-backup.db \
  --data-dir=/var/lib/etcd-restored

# 2. Update etcd manifest (both --data-dir and hostPath)
# Using sed to make both changes:
sed -i 's|path: /var/lib/etcd$|path: /var/lib/etcd-restored|' \
  /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml
sed -i 's|--data-dir=/var/lib/etcd$|--data-dir=/var/lib/etcd-restored|' \
  /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml

# 3. Wait for etcd to restart (kubelet detects manifest change)
sleep 30

# 4. Verify
kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l component=etcd
kubectl get all --all-namespaces

29.4 etcd Backup Automation

In production, you should automate etcd backups using a CronJob or external tooling. Here's a Kubernetes CronJob that takes automatic etcd snapshots on a schedule.

etcd-backup-cronjob.yaml 
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: etcd-backup
  namespace: kube-system
spec:
  schedule: "0 */6 * * *"          # Every 6 hours
  successfulJobsHistoryLimit: 3
  failedJobsHistoryLimit: 1
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          hostNetwork: true
          containers:
          - name: etcd-backup
            image: registry.k8s.io/etcd:3.5.12-0
            command:
            - /bin/sh
            - -c
            - |
              ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot save \
                /backup/etcd-snapshot-$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M%S).db \
                --endpoints=https://127.0.0.1:2379 \
                --cacert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt \
                --cert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt \
                --key=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key
            volumeMounts:
            - name: etcd-certs
              mountPath: /etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd
              readOnly: true
            - name: backup-dir
              mountPath: /backup
          volumes:
          - name: etcd-certs
            hostPath:
              path: /etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd
          - name: backup-dir
            hostPath:
              path: /opt/etcd-backups
              type: DirectoryOrCreate
          restartPolicy: OnFailure
          nodeSelector:
            node-role.kubernetes.io/control-plane: ""
          tolerations:
          - key: node-role.kubernetes.io/control-plane
            effect: NoSchedule

✅ etcd Backup Best Practices Checklist

PracticeWhy
✅ Backup before every cluster upgradeRollback point if upgrade fails
✅ Store backups externally (not on same node)Node failure won't destroy backups too
✅ Test restore procedure regularlyUntested backups are unreliable backups
✅ Document backup/restore steps for teamAnyone on-call should be able to restore
✅ Include timestamp in backup filenameIdentify which backup to restore from
✅ Verify backup with snapshot statusConfirm backup is not corrupted

🛠️ etcd Restore Troubleshooting — Common Failures

Problem Symptom Fix
etcd pod won't start after restore CrashLoopBackOff for etcd pod Check BOTH --data-dir and hostPath.path in manifest point to restored directory
Forgot to update hostPath etcd starts but uses old data (restore appears to fail) Edit /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml — change volumes[].hostPath.path too
Permission denied on restored dir permission denied in etcd logs chown -R root:root /var/lib/etcd-restored
kubectl not responding after restore All kubectl commands hang or timeout Wait 30-60s for kubelet to detect manifest change — use crictl ps | grep etcd instead
Wrong backup file used Cluster restored but resources are from wrong point in time Verify backup with etcdctl snapshot status before restoring — check revision count
etcd restore command fails Error: data-dir already exists Always restore to a NEW directory — never overwrite /var/lib/etcd directly
🎯 CKA Exam: etcd Backup/Restore Decision Flowchart
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                 etcd BACKUP/RESTORE — EXAM DECISION TREE                 │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                          │
│  Question says "backup etcd"?                                            │
│  ┌──── YES ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐      │
│  │ 1. Find cert paths:                                            │      │
│  │    kubectl describe pod etcd-controlplane -n kube-system       │      │
│  │    Look for: --trusted-ca-file, --cert-file, --key-file        │      │
│  │    Also check: --listen-client-urls for endpoint               │      │
│  │                                                                │      │
│  │ 2. Take snapshot:                                              │      │
│  │    ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot save /path/backup.db \       │      │
│  │      --endpoints=https://127.0.0.1:2379 \                     │      │
│  │      --cacert=<ca-path> --cert=<cert-path> --key=<key-path>   │      │
│  │                                                                │      │
│  │ 3. Verify:                                                     │      │
│  │    ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot status /path/backup.db       │      │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘      │
│                                                                          │
│  Question says "restore etcd"?                                           │
│  ┌──── YES ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐      │
│  │ 1. Restore to NEW directory:                                   │      │
│  │    ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot restore /path/backup.db \    │      │
│  │      --data-dir=/var/lib/etcd-restored                        │      │
│  │                                                                │      │
│  │ 2. Edit manifest (TWO changes!):                               │      │
│  │    vi /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml                      │      │
│  │    Change: --data-dir=/var/lib/etcd-restored                  │      │
│  │    Change: hostPath.path: /var/lib/etcd-restored              │      │
│  │                                                                │      │
│  │ 3. Wait and verify:                                            │      │
│  │    Watch: crictl ps | grep etcd (don't rely on kubectl yet)   │      │
│  │    Then: kubectl get all --all-namespaces                      │      │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘      │
│                                                                          │
│  ⚠️ REMEMBER: etcdctl needs NO certs for restore — only for backup!    │
│                                                                          │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
📦 What's Inside an etcd Backup — Everything Kubernetes Knows
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                  etcd BACKUP CONTAINS ALL CLUSTER STATE                  │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                          │
│  /registry/                                                              │
│  ├── deployments/                                                        │
│  │   ├── default/anihpj          ← Your Deployment specs                │
│  │   └── kube-system/coredns     ← System Deployments                  │
│  ├── services/                                                           │
│  │   ├── default/anihpj-svc      ← Your Service configs                │
│  │   └── default/kubernetes      ← Cluster API service                  │
│  ├── pods/                                                               │
│  │   └── (pod specs, not running state)                                 │
│  ├── configmaps/                                                         │
│  │   ├── default/anihpj-config   ← Your ConfigMaps                     │
│  │   └── kube-system/kubeadm-config                                     │
│  ├── secrets/                                                            │
│  │   ├── default/anihpj-secret   ← Your Secrets (encrypted)            │
│  │   └── (service account tokens, TLS certs)                            │
│  ├── namespaces/                  ← All namespace definitions           │
│  ├── roles/ & rolebindings/     ← All RBAC policies                    │
│  ├── persistentvolumes/           ← PV definitions (not data!)          │
│  ├── nodes/                       ← Node registrations                  │
│  └── ...every API object          ← CRDs, NetworkPolicies, etc.        │
│                                                                          │
│  ❌ NOT in etcd backup:                                                  │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
│  │ • Container images (stored in registry)                          │    │
│  │ • Persistent Volume DATA (stored on disk/cloud)                  │    │
│  │ • Container logs (stored on nodes)                               │    │
│  │ • Node OS state (packages, configs)                              │    │
│  │ • Running container state (only desired state is in etcd)        │    │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
│                                                                          │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

🔬 Practice Drill 29.1 — Backup etcd

⏱️ Target Time: 4 minutes
Scenario

Take a snapshot backup of etcd and save it to /opt/etcd-backup-drill.db. The etcd server is running on https://127.0.0.1:2379 with certificates in the default location under /etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/.

  1. Determine the correct certificate file paths from the etcd pod
  2. Take the snapshot using etcdctl with all 4 required flags
  3. Verify the snapshot is valid
Hint: Use kubectl describe pod etcd-controlplane -n kube-system to find cert paths. Remember to set ETCDCTL_API=3 and include --endpoints, --cacert, --cert, --key.
View Solution 
# Find certificate paths
kubectl describe pod etcd-controlplane -n kube-system | grep -E 'cert-file|key-file|trusted-ca'

# Take backup with all 4 flags
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot save /opt/etcd-backup-drill.db \
  --endpoints=https://127.0.0.1:2379 \
  --cacert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt \
  --cert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt \
  --key=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key

# Verify
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot status /opt/etcd-backup-drill.db --write-table

🔬 Practice Drill 29.2 — Restore etcd from Backup

⏱️ Target Time: 6 minutes
Scenario

A backup file exists at /opt/etcd-backup-drill.db. Some critical resources have been accidentally deleted. Restore the cluster from this backup.

  1. Restore the snapshot to a new data directory /var/lib/etcd-restored
  2. Update the etcd static pod manifest to use the new data directory
  3. Wait for the etcd pod to restart and verify the cluster is restored
Hint: You need to change TWO things in /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml: the --data-dir command argument AND the hostPath.path volume. Both must point to the new directory.
View Solution 
# 1. Restore snapshot to new directory
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot restore /opt/etcd-backup-drill.db \
  --data-dir=/var/lib/etcd-restored

# 2. Update etcd manifest — change BOTH --data-dir and hostPath
vi /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml
# Change: --data-dir=/var/lib/etcd → --data-dir=/var/lib/etcd-restored
# Change: hostPath.path: /var/lib/etcd → hostPath.path: /var/lib/etcd-restored

# 3. Wait for etcd pod to restart (30-60 seconds)
watch kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l component=etcd

# 4. Verify cluster state is restored
kubectl get all --all-namespaces
kubectl get nodes

🔬 Practice Drill 29.3 — Create an Automated Backup CronJob

⏱️ Target Time: 8 minutes
Scenario

Create a CronJob named etcd-backup in the kube-system namespace that takes an etcd snapshot every 6 hours. The backup should be saved to /opt/etcd-backups/ on the control plane node with a timestamp in the filename.

  1. Write a CronJob YAML with the correct schedule (0 */6 * * *)
  2. Mount etcd certificates and the backup directory as volumes
  3. Use the registry.k8s.io/etcd image with the snapshot save command
  4. Add nodeSelector and tolerations for the control plane
Hint: The CronJob needs hostNetwork: true to reach etcd on localhost, nodeSelector for control-plane, and tolerations for the control-plane taint. Mount /etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd as read-only for certs.
View Solution 
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: etcd-backup
  namespace: kube-system
spec:
  schedule: "0 */6 * * *"
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          hostNetwork: true
          containers:
          - name: backup
            image: registry.k8s.io/etcd:3.5.12-0
            command: ["/bin/sh", "-c"]
            args:
            - |
              ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot save \
                /backup/snapshot-$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M%S).db \
                --endpoints=https://127.0.0.1:2379 \
                --cacert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt \
                --cert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt \
                --key=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key
            volumeMounts:
            - name: certs
              mountPath: /etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd
              readOnly: true
            - name: backup
              mountPath: /backup
          volumes:
          - name: certs
            hostPath:
              path: /etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd
          - name: backup
            hostPath:
              path: /opt/etcd-backups
              type: DirectoryOrCreate
          restartPolicy: OnFailure
          nodeSelector:
            node-role.kubernetes.io/control-plane: ""
          tolerations:
          - key: node-role.kubernetes.io/control-plane
            effect: NoSchedule

✅ Chapter 29 — Verification Checklist

  • ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot status /opt/backup.db --write-table — Backup is valid (shows hash, revision, keys, size)
  • ls -lh /var/lib/etcd-restored/member/ — Restored data directory exists after restore
  • kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l component=etcd — etcd pod is Running after restore
  • kubectl get all --all-namespaces — All resources restored to backup state
  • grep data-dir /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml — Manifest points to correct data directory
  • crictl ps | grep etcd — etcd container is running (alternative to kubectl during restore)

⚠️ CKA Gotcha: etcd Backup & Restore Traps

  • 3 flags are REQUIRED for etcdctl snapshot save — --cacert, --cert, --key. Without all three, the command fails. Find them from: kubectl describe pod etcd-controlplane -n kube-system or from /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml
  • ETCDCTL_API=3 must be set — Either export it or prefix every command. Without it, etcdctl defaults to API v2 which uses different syntax and fails silently
  • Restore creates a NEW data directory — etcdctl snapshot restore uses --data-dir=/var/lib/etcd-restored. You must then update the etcd manifest's --data-dir AND the hostPath volume to point to the new directory
  • After restore, etcd restarts automatically — The kubelet watches /etc/kubernetes/manifests/. Changing etcd.yaml triggers a restart. Wait 30-60 seconds before verifying
  • Backup path matters — The exam specifies WHERE to save the backup (e.g., /opt/cluster-backup.db). Use the exact path given or you lose marks

CKA Speed Tip — etcd backup one-liner (memorize):
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot save /opt/backup.db \
  --cacert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt \
  --cert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt \
  --key=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key
Restore:
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot restore /opt/backup.db --data-dir=/var/lib/etcd-restored
Then edit /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml → change --data-dir and hostPath.path

⚡ etcd commands: ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot save <path> — take backup
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot status <path> --write-table — verify backup integrity
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot restore <path> --data-dir=<new-dir> — restore
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl member list — list etcd cluster members
kubectl describe pod etcd-controlplane -n kube-system | grep -A5 Command — find cert paths
📝 Chapter 29 Practice Drill

Timed challenge (target: 8 minutes):

  • 1. Find etcd cert paths from the etcd pod manifest
  • 2. Take a snapshot: save to /opt/etcd-backup.db
  • 3. Verify the backup: etcdctl snapshot status
  • 4. Create a test resource: kubectl create ns backup-test
  • 5. Restore the snapshot to /var/lib/etcd-restored
  • 6. Update etcd manifest to use the new data-dir
  • 7. Verify: kubectl get ns backup-test should return NotFound (pre-backup state)

📝 Chapter 29 — CKA Practice Questions

Q1
Take a snapshot of etcd and save it to /opt/etcd-backup.db. Show the complete command with all required flags.
Show Answer
Answer
# Find cert paths from etcd pod:
kubectl describe pod etcd-controlplane -n kube-system | \
  grep -E "cert|key|cacert"
# --cert-file=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt
# --key-file=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key
# --trusted-ca-file=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt

# OR from the manifest:
cat /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml | \
  grep -E "cert-file|key-file|trusted-ca"

# Take the snapshot:
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot save /opt/etcd-backup.db \
  --cacert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt \
  --cert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt \
  --key=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key

# Verify:
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot status /opt/etcd-backup.db \
  --write-table
# +--------+----------+------------+------------+
# |  HASH  | REVISION | TOTAL KEYS | TOTAL SIZE |
# +--------+----------+------------+------------+
# | abc123 |    14500 |       1200 |     4.1 MB |
# +--------+----------+------------+------------+

ls -la /opt/etcd-backup.db
# -rw------- 1 root root 4194304 ...
Explanation

Three flags are REQUIRED: --cacert, --cert, --key. Without all three, etcdctl fails with connection errors. Find the paths from the etcd pod manifest at /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml. Always set ETCDCTL_API=3 (v2 uses different syntax). On the CKA, use the EXACT path specified in the question for the backup file. --write-table gives a readable verification output.

Q2
Restore an etcd snapshot from /opt/etcd-backup.db. Show the full restore process including manifest updates.
Show Answer
Answer
# Step 1: Restore to NEW directory
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot restore /opt/etcd-backup.db \
  --data-dir=/var/lib/etcd-restored

# Step 2: Update etcd manifest
sudo vi /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml

# Change 1: --data-dir flag
# FROM: --data-dir=/var/lib/etcd
# TO:   --data-dir=/var/lib/etcd-restored

# Change 2: hostPath volume
# FROM:
#   - hostPath:
#       path: /var/lib/etcd
# TO:
#   - hostPath:
#       path: /var/lib/etcd-restored

# Step 3: Wait for etcd to restart (~30-60 seconds)
# kubelet detects manifest change and recreates the pod

# Step 4: Verify
kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep etcd
# etcd-controlplane  1/1  Running  0  30s

kubectl get nodes
kubectl get pods -A
# Cluster should be in the state from backup time

# COMMON MISTAKES:
# - Forgetting to update BOTH --data-dir AND hostPath
# - Using the old /var/lib/etcd path (data is stale)
# - Not waiting long enough for restart
Explanation

Restore creates a NEW data directory — never restore in-place to /var/lib/etcd. You MUST update TWO things in the etcd manifest: (1) the --data-dir command flag and (2) the hostPath.path volume. On the CKA, this is a HIGH-WEIGHT question. The kubelet auto-detects the manifest change and recreates the etcd pod. Wait 30-60 seconds before verifying. Resources created AFTER the backup are gone after restore.

Q3
Where do you find the etcd certificate file paths on a kubeadm cluster?
Show Answer
Answer
# Method 1: From etcd static pod manifest (FASTEST):
cat /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml | \
  grep -E "cert-file|key-file|trusted-ca"
# --cert-file=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt
# --key-file=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key
# --trusted-ca-file=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt
# --peer-cert-file=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/peer.crt
# --peer-key-file=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/peer.key
# --peer-trusted-ca-file=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt

# Method 2: From running pod:
kubectl describe pod etcd-controlplane -n kube-system | \
  grep -A5 Command

# Method 3: From process:
ps aux | grep etcd | grep -oP 'cert-file=\S+'

# Mapping to etcdctl flags:
# etcd flag:          → etcdctl flag:
# --cert-file         → --cert
# --key-file          → --key
# --trusted-ca-file   → --cacert

# Default paths (kubeadm):
# /etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt      → --cacert
# /etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt  → --cert
# /etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key  → --key
Explanation

The etcd manifest at /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml is the fastest source. Note: etcd uses different flag names than etcdctl — --cert-file (etcd) maps to --cert (etcdctl). On the CKA, they may NOT give you the cert paths — you must find them yourself. The manifest method works even when kubectl is unavailable (API server down). Default kubeadm paths are always under /etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/.

Q4
How do you verify that an etcd backup is valid and not corrupted?
Show Answer
Answer
# Method 1: snapshot status (recommended)
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot status /opt/etcd-backup.db \
  --write-table
# +--------+----------+------------+------------+
# |  HASH  | REVISION | TOTAL KEYS | TOTAL SIZE |
# +--------+----------+------------+------------+
# | 7c2...  |    15234 |       1456 |     5.2 MB |
# +--------+----------+------------+------------+
# ← Hash, revision, key count, size all shown

# If corrupted:
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot status /bad-backup.db
# Error: expected sha256 ...
# ← Hash mismatch = corrupted backup!

# Method 2: JSON output for scripting
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot status /opt/etcd-backup.db \
  --write-table=false
# Shows: hash, revision, totalKey, totalSize

# Method 3: File size check
ls -la /opt/etcd-backup.db
# Should be non-zero (typically several MB)

# Method 4: Dry-run restore
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot restore /opt/etcd-backup.db \
  --data-dir=/tmp/etcd-test
ls /tmp/etcd-test/
# member/  ← Restore succeeded
rm -rf /tmp/etcd-test  # Cleanup
Explanation

etcdctl snapshot status verifies the backup's integrity hash. If the hash doesn't match, the backup is corrupted. On the CKA, always verify after taking a backup — the exam may ask you to confirm the backup's validity. The --write-table flag gives human-readable output. A zero-size file or hash mismatch means you need to retake the backup.

Q5
The etcd pod is not starting after a restore. What should you check?
Show Answer
Answer
# Step 1: Check if manifest was updated correctly
cat /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml | grep data-dir
# --data-dir=/var/lib/etcd-restored ← Correct?

cat /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml | grep -A2 hostPath
# hostPath:
#   path: /var/lib/etcd-restored ← Must match --data-dir!

# Step 2: Check the restored directory exists
ls -la /var/lib/etcd-restored/
# member/ ← Should exist

# Check ownership (must be root or etcd user):
ls -la /var/lib/ | grep etcd
# drwx------ 3 root root etcd-restored

# Step 3: Check kubelet logs
journalctl -u kubelet -l | grep etcd | tail -20

# Step 4: Check container status
crictl ps -a | grep etcd
# If restarting: check crictl logs <id>

# Common issues after restore:
# 1. hostPath not updated → etcd starts with old data
# 2. --data-dir not updated → path mismatch, crash
# 3. Permission denied → chown the restored directory
# 4. Both changes not saved → yaml syntax error
# 5. Wrong restore path → directory doesn't exist

# Fix example:
sudo chown -R root:root /var/lib/etcd-restored
sudo chmod 700 /var/lib/etcd-restored
Explanation

Post-restore etcd failures: (1) --data-dir and hostPath mismatch — BOTH must point to the restored directory, (2) restored directory doesn't exist — re-run the restore, (3) permission issues — chown to root, (4) YAML syntax error in the manifest — validate the file. On the CKA, the most common mistake is updating only one of the two settings. Always verify both the command flag AND the volume path.

Q6
How do you check etcd cluster health and list members?
Show Answer
Answer
# List etcd members:
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl member list \
  --cacert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt \
  --cert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt \
  --key=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key \
  --write-out=table
# +--------+---------+-----------+-------------------+-------------------+
# |   ID   | STATUS  |   NAME    |    PEER ADDRS     |   CLIENT ADDRS    |
# +--------+---------+-----------+-------------------+-------------------+
# | abc123 | started | controlplane | https://10.0.0.1:2380 | https://10.0.0.1:2379 |
# +--------+---------+-----------+-------------------+-------------------+

# Check endpoint health:
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl endpoint health \
  --cacert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt \
  --cert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt \
  --key=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key
# https://127.0.0.1:2379 is healthy: committed proposal: took = 2ms

# Endpoint status (detailed):
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl endpoint status \
  --cacert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt \
  --cert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt \
  --key=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key \
  --write-out=table
# Shows: endpoint, ID, version, db size, is leader, raft term/index
Explanation

member list shows cluster membership, endpoint health checks if etcd is responding, endpoint status shows detailed info including leader status. All require the same 3 cert flags. On the CKA, these commands help verify etcd is healthy after a restore or during troubleshooting. In multi-node etcd clusters, prefix with --endpoints= to check specific members.

Q7
What is the difference between --endpoints and --advertise-client-urls in etcd?
Show Answer
Answer
# --endpoints (client-side, used with etcdctl):
# Tells etcdctl WHERE to connect
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl \
  --endpoints=https://10.0.0.1:2379 \
  snapshot save /opt/backup.db \
  --cacert=... --cert=... --key=...

# Default endpoint: https://127.0.0.1:2379
# For multi-node etcd:
# --endpoints=https://10.0.0.1:2379,https://10.0.0.2:2379

# --advertise-client-urls (server-side, etcd config):
# Tells etcd what URL to advertise to clients
# Found in: /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml
# --advertise-client-urls=https://10.0.0.1:2379

# --listen-client-urls (server-side):
# What addresses etcd actually listens on
# --listen-client-urls=https://127.0.0.1:2379,https://10.0.0.1:2379

# On the CKA exam:
# You usually DON'T need --endpoints (localhost works)
# The default https://127.0.0.1:2379 is fine when
# running etcdctl on the control plane node
Explanation

--endpoints is a CLIENT-side flag (used with etcdctl) specifying where to connect. --advertise-client-urls is a SERVER-side config (in etcd.yaml) specifying what URL to advertise. On the CKA, you usually run etcdctl on the control plane, so the default localhost endpoint works. You only need --endpoints if connecting remotely or to a specific member in a multi-node etcd cluster.

Q8
How do you find the etcd --data-dir path on a running cluster?
Show Answer
Answer
# Method 1: From static pod manifest (FASTEST)
grep data-dir /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml
# --data-dir=/var/lib/etcd

# Method 2: From running pod
kubectl describe pod etcd-controlplane -n kube-system | \
  grep data-dir
# --data-dir=/var/lib/etcd

# Method 3: From process
ps aux | grep etcd | grep -oP 'data-dir=\S+'
# data-dir=/var/lib/etcd

# Method 4: From etcd pod volumes
kubectl get pod etcd-controlplane -n kube-system \
  -o jsonpath='{.spec.volumes[?(@.name=="etcd-data")].hostPath.path}'
# /var/lib/etcd

# Check actual data directory size:
sudo du -sh /var/lib/etcd
# 48M /var/lib/etcd

# List contents:
sudo ls -la /var/lib/etcd/
# member/
#   snap/
#     db        ← The actual etcd database
#   wal/        ← Write-ahead log
Explanation

The --data-dir tells etcd where to store its data. Default is /var/lib/etcd. On the CKA, you need this path for restore operations — the restored data goes to a NEW directory, then you update the manifest to point there. The fastest method is grep data-dir /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml. The member/snap/db file is the actual BoltDB database.

Q9
What happens to the cluster state when you restore an older etcd backup?
Show Answer
Answer
# Scenario:
# 1. Take backup at 10:00 AM
# 2. At 10:30 AM: create ns "test-ns", deploy app
# 3. At 11:00 AM: restore 10:00 AM backup

# After restore:
kubectl get ns test-ns
# Error: namespaces "test-ns" not found ← GONE!

kubectl get deploy -n test-ns
# Error: namespace not found ← GONE!

# EVERYTHING created AFTER the backup is lost:
# ✗ Namespaces created after backup
# ✗ Pods, Deployments, Services created after backup
# ✗ ConfigMaps, Secrets created after backup
# ✗ RBAC changes made after backup
# ✗ CRDs and custom resources created after backup

# EVERYTHING from BEFORE the backup is restored:
# ✓ Namespaces that existed at backup time
# ✓ All resources from backup time
# ✓ Cluster configuration from backup time

# Key insight:
# etcd backup = COMPLETE cluster state snapshot
# Restore = TIME TRAVEL to that exact moment
# This is why regular backups are critical
Explanation

Restoring an etcd backup reverts the ENTIRE cluster state to the backup moment. Everything created after the backup is lost — pods, namespaces, secrets, RBAC rules, everything. On the CKA, they verify this by asking you to create a resource after backup, restore, then confirm the resource no longer exists. This is why etcd backups are critical — losing etcd without a backup means rebuilding the entire cluster from scratch.

Q10
Write a complete etcd backup and restore procedure as a scripted workflow.
Show Answer
Answer
#!/bin/bash
# === ETCD BACKUP AND RESTORE WORKFLOW ===

# --- Variables (adjust to your cluster) ---
CACERT=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt
CERT=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt
KEY=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key
BACKUP_PATH=/opt/etcd-backup.db
RESTORE_DIR=/var/lib/etcd-restored
MANIFEST=/etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml

# --- BACKUP ---
echo "Taking etcd backup..."
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot save $BACKUP_PATH \
  --cacert=$CACERT --cert=$CERT --key=$KEY

echo "Verifying backup..."
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot status $BACKUP_PATH \
  --write-table

# --- RESTORE ---
echo "Restoring from backup..."
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot restore $BACKUP_PATH \
  --data-dir=$RESTORE_DIR

echo "Updating etcd manifest..."
# Update --data-dir in command
sudo sed -i "s|--data-dir=/var/lib/etcd|--data-dir=$RESTORE_DIR|" \
  $MANIFEST

# Update hostPath volume
sudo sed -i "s|path: /var/lib/etcd$|path: $RESTORE_DIR|" \
  $MANIFEST

echo "Waiting for etcd to restart (60s)..."
sleep 60

echo "Verifying cluster..."
kubectl get nodes
kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep etcd
Explanation

This workflow captures the complete CKA backup/restore procedure: set variables → save snapshot → verify → restore to new dir → update manifest (both --data-dir and hostPath) → wait → verify. On the CKA, you won't write a script, but knowing this flow by heart is essential. The sed commands show what needs changing in the manifest. Practice this workflow until you can do it in under 8 minutes.

Q11
Create a CronJob that takes an automated etcd snapshot backup every 6 hours and stores it in /opt/etcd-backups/. Include the required certificates and retention of the last 7 backups.
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: etcd-backup
  namespace: kube-system
spec:
  schedule: "0 */6 * * *"          # Every 6 hours
  concurrencyPolicy: Forbid          # Don't overlap backups
  successfulJobsHistoryLimit: 7
  failedJobsHistoryLimit: 3
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          hostNetwork: true           # Access etcd on localhost
          restartPolicy: OnFailure
          containers:
          - name: etcd-backup
            image: registry.k8s.io/etcd:3.5.12-0
            command: ["/bin/sh", "-c"]
            args:
            - |
              BACKUP_FILE=/backups/etcd-$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M%S).db
              etcdctl snapshot save $BACKUP_FILE \
                --cacert=/certs/ca.crt \
                --cert=/certs/server.crt \
                --key=/certs/server.key
              etcdctl snapshot status $BACKUP_FILE --write-table
              # Rotate: keep last 7 backups
              ls -t /backups/etcd-*.db | tail -n +8 | xargs rm -f
              echo "Backup complete: $BACKUP_FILE"
            env:
            - name: ETCDCTL_API
              value: "3"
            volumeMounts:
            - name: etcd-certs
              mountPath: /certs
              readOnly: true
            - name: backup-dir
              mountPath: /backups
          volumes:
          - name: etcd-certs
            hostPath:
              path: /etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd
              type: Directory
          - name: backup-dir
            hostPath:
              path: /opt/etcd-backups
              type: DirectoryOrCreate
          nodeSelector:
            node-role.kubernetes.io/control-plane: ""
          tolerations:
          - key: node-role.kubernetes.io/control-plane
            effect: NoSchedule

# Verify the CronJob:
kubectl get cronjob etcd-backup -n kube-system
# NAME          SCHEDULE      SUSPEND  ACTIVE  LAST SCHEDULE
# etcd-backup   0 */6 * * *   False    0       <none>

# Trigger a manual run to test:
kubectl create job test-backup -n kube-system \
  --from=cronjob/etcd-backup

# Check the backup:
ls -la /opt/etcd-backups/
Explanation

Automated etcd backups require several pieces: (1) hostNetwork: true to reach etcd on localhost:2379, (2) etcd PKI certificates mounted from /etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd, (3) nodeSelector + tolerations to run on the control plane node (which has the taint node-role.kubernetes.io/control-plane:NoSchedule), (4) backup directory mounted as hostPath. The rotation logic (ls -t | tail -n +8 | xargs rm) keeps the 7 newest files. On the CKA, you're more likely to do a manual backup, but understanding this CronJob pattern demonstrates mastery of Jobs, scheduling, hostPath, tolerations, and nodeSelector — all CKA-tested topics combined.

Chapter 30: Node Maintenance and OS Upgrades

Safely removing nodes from service for maintenance, protecting applications with PodDisruptionBudgets, and performing OS upgrades without downtime. These are essential skills for any CKA candidate and day-to-day cluster operations.

🔴 Advanced ⏱️ ~1 hour 🔧 Maintenance 📝 CKA EXAM

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ Drain, cordon, uncordon — understand the difference and when to use each
  • ✅ PodDisruptionBudgets — protect application availability during voluntary disruptions
  • ✅ OS upgrades — safely perform kernel patches and OS updates on nodes
  • ✅ Pod eviction timeout — understand what happens when nodes go down unexpectedly

📋 CKA Exam Relevance

Domain: Cluster Architecture, Installation & Configuration — 25%

Node drain/cordon operations appear frequently. You need to drain nodes before upgrades (Ch28), and PDBs control how drain interacts with your deployments.

💡 CKA Exam Tip

  • The exam may ask you to drain a node — remember --ignore-daemonsets
  • If pods with local storage exist, add --delete-emptydir-data
  • Know how to create a PDB from a YAML manifest
  • Understand that drain = cordon + evict all pods

⌨️ Node Maintenance Command Reference

ActionCommand
Drain nodekubectl drain <node> --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data
Cordon nodekubectl cordon <node>
Uncordon nodekubectl uncordon <node>
Check node statuskubectl get nodes
Check PDBskubectl get pdb
Create PDBkubectl apply -f pdb.yaml
Describe PDBkubectl describe pdb <name>
Check pod placementkubectl get pods -o wide

⚠️ Node Maintenance Exam Traps

❌ Wrong✅ Correct
kubectl drain node01 (no flags)kubectl drain node01 --ignore-daemonsets
Forgetting to uncordon after maintenancekubectl uncordon node01 after work is done
Draining node with local storage pods (fails)Add --delete-emptydir-data flag
Thinking cordon evicts podsCordon only stops NEW scheduling; existing pods stay
PDB with both minAvailable AND maxUnavailableUse only ONE — they are mutually exclusive
🔧 3 Commands drain, cordon, uncordon
🛡️ PDB Disruption Protection
⏱️ 5 min Default Eviction Timeout
📝 High Exam Likelihood

30.1 Draining Nodes — Safe Pod Eviction

🎯 CKA EXAM TOPIC

Before maintenance, you must safely move all pods off a node. Kubernetes provides three commands for this: drain, cordon, and uncordon. Understanding when to use each is critical.

🚿
kubectl drain

Cordons the node AND evicts all pods (except DaemonSets). Use before maintenance. Almost always needs --ignore-daemonsets.

🚧
kubectl cordon

Marks node as SchedulingDisabled — no new pods scheduled, but existing pods keep running. Use to gradually move workloads.

✅
kubectl uncordon

Removes the SchedulingDisabled mark — node accepts new pods again. Always run after maintenance or pods won't come back.

🛡️
PodDisruptionBudget

Controls how many pods can be evicted at once. Drain respects PDBs — if evicting would violate minAvailable, drain blocks until safe.

📊 drain vs cordon vs uncordon

Command Marks Unschedulable? Evicts Existing Pods? Use Case
kubectl cordon <node> ✅ Yes ❌ No — pods stay Prevent new pods before planned maintenance
kubectl drain <node> ✅ Yes (implicit cordon) ✅ Yes — evicts all Remove ALL workloads before maintenance
kubectl uncordon <node> ❌ Removes mark N/A Make node schedulable again after maintenance
🔄 Node Maintenance Flow — drain → maintain → uncordon
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                     NODE MAINTENANCE LIFECYCLE                            │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                          │
│   NORMAL              CORDONED            DRAINED             READY      │
│  ┌──────────┐       ┌──────────┐       ┌──────────┐       ┌──────────┐  │
│  │  Node    │       │  Node    │       │  Node    │       │  Node    │  │
│  │  Ready   │──────▶│  Ready   │──────▶│  Ready   │──────▶│  Ready   │  │
│  │  Sched   │cordon │ SchedDis │ drain │ SchedDis │uncord │  Sched   │  │
│  │ 🟢🟢🟢🟢 │       │ 🟢🟢🟢🟢 │       │ ⬜⬜⬜⬜ │       │ 🟢🟢🟢🟢 │  │
│  └──────────┘       └──────────┘       └──────────┘       └──────────┘  │
│   Pods run           Pods STAY          Pods EVICTED       New pods      │
│   Accepts new        No new pods        to other nodes     can be        │
│   scheduling         scheduled          Empty node         scheduled     │
│                                          Do maintenance                  │
│                                                                          │
│  ⚡ drain = cordon (mark unschedulable) + evict all pods                │
│  ⚡ cordon = only mark unschedulable (pods keep running)                │
│  ⚡ uncordon = mark schedulable again                                   │
│                                                                          │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Drain Command — Common Flags

drain-commands.sh 
# Basic drain — will FAIL if DaemonSet pods exist
kubectl drain worker01

# With --ignore-daemonsets (almost always needed)
kubectl drain worker01 --ignore-daemonsets

# With local storage pods (emptyDir volumes)
kubectl drain worker01 --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data

# Force drain (delete pods not managed by a controller)
kubectl drain worker01 --ignore-daemonsets --force

# Drain with grace period
kubectl drain worker01 --ignore-daemonsets --grace-period=60

# Drain with timeout
kubectl drain worker01 --ignore-daemonsets --timeout=120s
📦 What Happens When You Drain a Node
  kubectl drain worker01 --ignore-daemonsets
           │
           ▼
  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │ 1. Mark worker01 as SchedulingDisabled (cordon)         │
  │ 2. For each non-DaemonSet pod on worker01:              │
  │    ├── Check PodDisruptionBudgets                        │
  │    ├── If PDB would be violated → WAIT (don't evict)    │
  │    ├── Send SIGTERM to pod (graceful shutdown)           │
  │    └── Wait for pod termination (gracePeriod)            │
  │ 3. Controller detects missing replicas                   │
  │ 4. Scheduler places new pods on OTHER nodes              │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
           │
           ▼
  ┌──────────────┐    ┌──────────────┐
  │  worker01    │    │  worker02    │
  │  (EMPTY)     │    │  🟢🟢🟢🟢🟢  │
  │  SchedDisabl │    │  All pods    │
  │  Ready for   │    │  rescheduled │
  │  maintenance │    │  here        │
  └──────────────┘    └──────────────┘
cordon-uncordon.sh 
# Cordon — mark unschedulable without evicting
kubectl cordon worker01
kubectl get nodes
# NAME       STATUS                     ROLES    VERSION
# worker01   Ready,SchedulingDisabled   <none>   v1.31.0

# Uncordon — make schedulable again
kubectl uncordon worker01
kubectl get nodes
# NAME       STATUS   ROLES    VERSION
# worker01   Ready    <none>   v1.31.0
📌 anihpj Drain Scenario

When you drain worker01 where your anihpj pods are running:

  1. anihpj pods on worker01 receive SIGTERM
  2. Django's graceful shutdown handler completes in-flight requests
  3. The Deployment controller creates replacement pods
  4. Scheduler places them on worker02
  5. The Service endpoints update — traffic flows to new pods
  6. Zero downtime if you have enough replicas and a PDB

🛠️ Drain Troubleshooting — When Drain Gets Stuck

Problem Symptom Solution
DaemonSet pods blocking cannot delete DaemonSet-managed Pods Add --ignore-daemonsets flag
Pods with emptyDir cannot delete Pods with local storage Add --delete-emptydir-data flag
Unreplicated pods cannot delete Pods not managed by... Add --force (pod will be lost!)
PDB blocking drain Drain hangs — waiting for eviction to be allowed Wait for pods to reschedule, or use --disable-eviction (bypasses PDB!)
Drain taking too long Pods not terminating within timeout Use --timeout=300s to set a deadline, or check terminationGracePeriodSeconds
Pod terminating but not dying Pod stuck in Terminating state Check for finalizers: kubectl get pod -o json | jq '.metadata.finalizers'
📋 What Gets Evicted vs What Stays During Drain
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                kubectl drain worker01 --ignore-daemonsets                 │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                          │
│  ❌ EVICTED (moved to other nodes):              ✅ STAYS (not touched): │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────┐            ┌────────────────────┐  │
│  │ Deployment-managed pods          │            │ DaemonSet pods     │  │
│  │ ReplicaSet-managed pods          │            │ (kube-proxy, etc.) │  │
│  │ StatefulSet-managed pods         │            │                    │  │
│  │ Job pods                         │            │ Static pods        │  │
│  │ Bare/naked pods (with --force)   │            │ (mirror pods)      │  │
│  └──────────────────────────────────┘            └────────────────────┘  │
│                                                                          │
│  ⚠️ SPECIAL CASES:                                                      │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
│  │ emptyDir pods → need --delete-emptydir-data (data will be lost) │    │
│  │ Standalone pods → need --force (no controller to recreate them) │    │
│  │ PDB-protected → drain waits for eviction API to allow removal   │    │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
│                                                                          │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

30.2 PodDisruptionBudgets (PDB)

🎯 CKA EXAM TOPIC

A PodDisruptionBudget controls how many pods can be unavailable during voluntary disruptions like drain, upgrades, and node scaling. It prevents drain from killing too many pods at once, ensuring your application stays available.

🛡️ PDB — Protecting Applications During Disruptions
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│          PodDisruptionBudget — How It Works                               │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                          │
│  Deployment: anihpj (3 replicas)                                         │
│  PDB: minAvailable: 2                                                    │
│                                                                          │
│  WITHOUT PDB:                        WITH PDB:                           │
│  ┌────────────────────────┐         ┌────────────────────────┐           │
│  │ kubectl drain worker01 │         │ kubectl drain worker01 │           │
│  │                        │         │                        │           │
│  │ Pod A ─── EVICTED ❌   │         │ Pod A ─── EVICTED ❌   │           │
│  │ Pod B ─── EVICTED ❌   │         │ Pod B ─── WAIT ⏸️     │           │
│  │ Pod C ─── running ✅   │         │          (PDB blocks   │           │
│  │                        │         │           until Pod A   │           │
│  │ ⚠️ Only 1 replica!    │         │           reschedules)  │           │
│  │ Possible downtime!     │         │ Pod B ─── EVICTED ❌   │           │
│  │                        │         │ Pod C ─── running ✅   │           │
│  │                        │         │ Pod A' ── running ✅   │           │
│  │                        │         │ ✅ Always ≥2 replicas  │           │
│  └────────────────────────┘         └────────────────────────┘           │
│                                                                          │
│  PDB guarantees minimum availability during voluntary disruptions        │
│                                                                          │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

📊 minAvailable vs maxUnavailable — Choose ONE

Field Meaning Example (3 replicas) Max Disrupted
minAvailable: 2 At least N pods must stay running 2 must run → only 1 can be disrupted 1 pod
minAvailable: "67%" Percentage must stay running 67% of 3 = 2 must run → 1 disrupted 1 pod
maxUnavailable: 1 At most N pods can be down Max 1 down → 2 must run 1 pod
maxUnavailable: "33%" Max percentage can be down 33% of 3 = 1 can be down 1 pod

PDB with minAvailable (anihpj Example)

anihpj-pdb-minavailable.yaml 
apiVersion: policy/v1
kind: PodDisruptionBudget
metadata:
  name: anihpj-pdb
  namespace: default
spec:
  minAvailable: 2              # At least 2 pods must always be running
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj              # Must match the Deployment's pod labels

PDB with maxUnavailable

anihpj-pdb-maxunavailable.yaml 
apiVersion: policy/v1
kind: PodDisruptionBudget
metadata:
  name: anihpj-pdb
  namespace: default
spec:
  maxUnavailable: 1            # At most 1 pod can be unavailable at a time
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
pdb-commands.sh 
# Create PDB
kubectl apply -f anihpj-pdb.yaml

# View PDBs
kubectl get pdb
# NAME         MIN AVAILABLE   MAX UNAVAILABLE   ALLOWED DISRUPTIONS   AGE
# anihpj-pdb   2               N/A               1                     5m

# Describe PDB for details
kubectl describe pdb anihpj-pdb

# Now drain will respect the PDB
kubectl drain worker01 --ignore-daemonsets
# If draining would violate PDB, it will WAIT until pods reschedule
⚠️ PDB Only Protects Against Voluntary Disruptions

PDBs protect against: kubectl drain, cluster autoscaler scale-down, rolling updates.

PDBs do NOT protect against: node crash, node out of memory (OOM), pod deletions with kubectl delete pod, hardware failure.

🔄 PDB + Drain — Eviction Sequence Timeline
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│     kubectl drain worker01 (Deployment: 3 replicas, PDB: minAvailable=2)│
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                          │
│  TIME ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────►     │
│                                                                          │
│  t=0s     Drain starts. Eviction API checks PDB:                        │
│           Running: [Pod-A, Pod-B, Pod-C] → 3 available                  │
│           PDB requires ≥2 → CAN evict 1                                 │
│           ┌───────────────────────────────┐                              │
│           │ Pod-A: EVICT ❌ → Terminating │                              │
│           │ Pod-B: running ✅             │                              │
│           │ Pod-C: running ✅             │                              │
│           └───────────────────────────────┘                              │
│                                                                          │
│  t=15s    Pod-A terminates. Deployment creates Pod-A'.                  │
│           Scheduler places Pod-A' on worker02.                          │
│           Running: [Pod-B, Pod-C] → only 2!                             │
│           PDB requires ≥2 → CANNOT evict more yet                       │
│           ┌───────────────────────────────┐                              │
│           │ Pod-A': Pending → Running     │                              │
│           │ Pod-B: running ✅ (blocked)   │                              │
│           │ Pod-C: running ✅             │                              │
│           └───────────────────────────────┘                              │
│                                                                          │
│  t=30s    Pod-A' is Running and Ready.                                  │
│           Running: [Pod-A', Pod-B, Pod-C] → 3 available                 │
│           PDB allows eviction again → evict Pod-B                       │
│           ┌───────────────────────────────┐                              │
│           │ Pod-A': running ✅            │                              │
│           │ Pod-B: EVICT ❌ → Terminating │                              │
│           │ Pod-C: running ✅             │                              │
│           └───────────────────────────────┘                              │
│                                                                          │
│  t=45s    Pod-B' scheduled on worker02. Drain complete.                 │
│           ✅ At NO point were there fewer than 2 running pods           │
│                                                                          │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

📊 Voluntary vs Involuntary Disruptions — Complete Reference

Disruption Type Examples PDB Honored? Graceful?
Voluntary kubectl drain ✅ Yes ✅ Yes
Voluntary Cluster autoscaler scale-down ✅ Yes ✅ Yes
Voluntary Deployment rolling update ✅ Yes ✅ Yes
Involuntary Node hardware failure / crash ❌ No ❌ No
Involuntary Node kernel panic ❌ No ❌ No
Involuntary OOM kill (out of memory) ❌ No ❌ No
Bypass kubectl delete pod ❌ No ✅ Yes
Bypass kubectl drain --disable-eviction ❌ No ✅ Yes

📋 PDB — YAML Skeleton (Exam Quick Reference)

pdb-skeleton.yaml 
apiVersion: policy/v1                    # ← Always policy/v1 (not policy/v1beta1)
kind: PodDisruptionBudget
metadata:
  name: <pdb-name>
  namespace: <namespace>
spec:
  # Choose ONE of these (not both):
  minAvailable: <number-or-percent>      # At least N pods must be running
  # maxUnavailable: <number-or-percent>  # At most N pods can be down
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      <label-key>: <label-value>          # Must match pod labels

30.3 OS Upgrades and Kernel Patches

When a node needs an OS upgrade or kernel patch, you follow the drain → upgrade → uncordon pattern. The key concern is the pod-eviction-timeout — if a node goes down without being drained first, Kubernetes waits 5 minutes before rescheduling pods.

🖥️ OS Upgrade Procedure — Step by Step
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    OS UPGRADE / KERNEL PATCH PROCEDURE                    │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                          │
│  Step 1: DRAIN the node                                                  │
│  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│  │ $ kubectl drain worker01 --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data│  │
│  │ Pods gracefully shut down and reschedule to other nodes           │   │
│  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│                               │                                          │
│                               ▼                                          │
│  Step 2: PERFORM the OS upgrade / kernel patch                           │
│  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│  │ (SSH into the node)                                               │   │
│  │ $ apt-get update && apt-get upgrade -y                            │   │
│  │ $ reboot     (if kernel patch requires reboot)                    │   │
│  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│                               │                                          │
│                               ▼                                          │
│  Step 3: VERIFY the node is healthy                                      │
│  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│  │ Wait for node to come back online after reboot                    │   │
│  │ $ systemctl status kubelet       (is kubelet running?)            │   │
│  │ $ kubectl get nodes              (is node Ready?)                 │   │
│  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│                               │                                          │
│                               ▼                                          │
│  Step 4: UNCORDON the node                                               │
│  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│  │ $ kubectl uncordon worker01                                       │   │
│  │ Node is now schedulable — new pods can be placed here             │   │
│  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│                                                                          │
│  ⏱️ pod-eviction-timeout = 5 minutes (default)                          │
│  If a node goes down WITHOUT drain, the controller-manager waits 5      │
│  minutes before marking pods as "Unknown" and rescheduling them.        │
│  Always DRAIN first to avoid this delay!                                 │
│                                                                          │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
os-upgrade-workflow.sh 
# === COMPLETE OS UPGRADE WORKFLOW ===

# FROM CONTROL PLANE:
# 1. Drain the node
kubectl drain worker01 --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data

# 2. SSH to worker and perform upgrade
ssh worker01

# ON WORKER NODE:
apt-get update && apt-get upgrade -y
# If kernel upgrade:
reboot

# Wait for node to come back...

# FROM CONTROL PLANE:
# 3. Verify node is healthy
kubectl get nodes
# worker01 should show Ready,SchedulingDisabled

# 4. Verify kubelet is running
ssh worker01 systemctl status kubelet

# 5. Make node schedulable again
kubectl uncordon worker01

# 6. Final verification
kubectl get nodes
# All nodes should show Ready (no SchedulingDisabled)

📊 Planned Drain vs Unplanned Node Failure

Scenario Pod Rescheduling Downtime
Planned: kubectl drain first Immediate — pods evicted and rescheduled instantly Minimal (graceful shutdown period only)
Unplanned: Node crashes After pod-eviction-timeout (default 5 minutes) 5+ minutes — pods stuck in Unknown state
Unplanned: Node power off After node becomes NotReady + eviction timeout 5+ minutes — no graceful shutdown
💡 Key Takeaway

Always kubectl drain before maintenance. It ensures pods are gracefully rescheduled immediately instead of waiting 5 minutes for the eviction timeout. Combined with a PDB, your application maintains availability throughout the process.

⏱️ Node Controller Timeline — Unplanned Node Failure
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                   WHAT HAPPENS WHEN A NODE GOES DOWN                     │
│                   (without kubectl drain)                                │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                          │
│  TIME ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────►     │
│                                                                          │
│  t=0s        Node stops sending heartbeats to API server                │
│              kubelet stops updating node lease object                    │
│              Status: Node still shows "Ready"                            │
│                                                                          │
│  t=40s       node-monitor-grace-period expires (default: 40s)           │
│              ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐            │
│              │ Node Controller marks node as "NotReady"     │            │
│              │ kubectl get nodes → STATUS: NotReady         │            │
│              │ Pods still show "Running" (not evicted yet)  │            │
│              └──────────────────────────────────────────────┘            │
│                                                                          │
│  t=5m 40s    pod-eviction-timeout expires (default: 5 minutes)          │
│              ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐            │
│              │ Node Controller adds NoExecute taint         │            │
│              │ Taint-based eviction kicks in                │            │
│              │ Pods get tolerationSeconds (default: 300s)   │            │
│              │ Pod status → "Unknown"                       │            │
│              └──────────────────────────────────────────────┘            │
│                                                                          │
│  t=10m 40s   tolerationSeconds expires for pods                         │
│              ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐            │
│              │ Pods finally evicted and rescheduled         │            │
│              │ Deployment controller creates replacements   │            │
│              │ ⚠️ Total downtime: ~10+ minutes!            │            │
│              └──────────────────────────────────────────────┘            │
│                                                                          │
│  Compare with kubectl drain: pods rescheduled in ~15-30 seconds! ✅     │
│                                                                          │
│  KEY PARAMETERS (kube-controller-manager):                               │
│  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│  │ --node-monitor-grace-period=40s    Time before marking NotReady  │   │
│  │ --pod-eviction-timeout=5m0s        Time before evicting pods     │   │
│  │ --node-monitor-period=5s           How often node status checked │   │
│  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│                                                                          │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
🔄 Multi-Node Rolling Maintenance Strategy (anihpj Production)
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│          ROLLING MAINTENANCE — 3 Worker Nodes, Zero Downtime             │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                          │
│  BEFORE: anihpj (3 replicas), PDB: minAvailable=2                       │
│  ┌────────────┐  ┌────────────┐  ┌────────────┐                        │
│  │  worker01   │  │  worker02   │  │  worker03   │                      │
│  │  anihpj-1 ✅│  │  anihpj-2 ✅│  │  anihpj-3 ✅│                     │
│  └────────────┘  └────────────┘  └────────────┘                        │
│                                                                          │
│  ROUND 1: Maintain worker01                                              │
│  ┌────────────┐  ┌────────────┐  ┌────────────┐                        │
│  │  worker01   │  │  worker02   │  │  worker03   │                      │
│  │  🔧 DRAIN  │  │  anihpj-2 ✅│  │  anihpj-3 ✅│                     │
│  │  upgrading  │  │  anihpj-1'✅│  │             │                      │
│  └────────────┘  └────────────┘  └────────────┘                        │
│  ✅ 3 pods running across 2 nodes — PDB satisfied                       │
│                                                                          │
│  ROUND 2: Uncordon worker01, drain worker02                              │
│  ┌────────────┐  ┌────────────┐  ┌────────────┐                        │
│  │  worker01   │  │  worker02   │  │  worker03   │                      │
│  │  anihpj-2'✅│  │  🔧 DRAIN  │  │  anihpj-3 ✅│                     │
│  │             │  │  upgrading  │  │  anihpj-1'✅│                      │
│  └────────────┘  └────────────┘  └────────────┘                        │
│  ✅ 3 pods running across 2 nodes — PDB satisfied                       │
│                                                                          │
│  ROUND 3: Uncordon worker02, drain worker03                              │
│  ┌────────────┐  ┌────────────┐  ┌────────────┐                        │
│  │  worker01   │  │  worker02   │  │  worker03   │                      │
│  │  anihpj-1 ✅│  │  anihpj-2 ✅│  │  🔧 DRAIN  │                     │
│  │             │  │  anihpj-3'✅│  │  upgrading  │                      │
│  └────────────┘  └────────────┘  └────────────┘                        │
│  ✅ 3 pods running across 2 nodes — PDB satisfied                       │
│                                                                          │
│  AFTER: All nodes upgraded, zero downtime achieved! 🎉                   │
│  ┌────────────┐  ┌────────────┐  ┌────────────┐                        │
│  │  worker01 ✅│  │  worker02 ✅│  │  worker03 ✅│                      │
│  │  upgraded   │  │  upgraded   │  │  upgraded   │                      │
│  └────────────┘  └────────────┘  └────────────┘                        │
│                                                                          │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
rolling-maintenance.sh 
# === MULTI-NODE ROLLING MAINTENANCE ===
# Ensure PDB is in place BEFORE starting

# Verify PDB
kubectl get pdb
# ALLOWED DISRUPTIONS should be ≥ 1

# Node list
NODES="worker01 worker02 worker03"

for NODE in $NODES; do
  echo "=== Maintaining $NODE ==="

  # 1. Drain
  kubectl drain $NODE --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data
  echo "  Drained $NODE — pods rescheduled"

  # 2. SSH and upgrade (customize per environment)
  ssh $NODE "apt-get update && apt-get upgrade -y && reboot"

  # 3. Wait for node to come back
  echo "  Waiting for $NODE to rejoin cluster..."
  until kubectl get node $NODE | grep -q " Ready"; do
    sleep 10
  done

  # 4. Uncordon
  kubectl uncordon $NODE
  echo "  $NODE back in service ✅"

  # 5. Brief pause for pods to stabilize
  sleep 30
done

echo "=== All nodes upgraded! ==="
kubectl get nodes

🚨 CKA Exam Gotchas — Node Maintenance

TrapWhat HappensCorrect Approach
Forgot to uncordon after maintenance Node stays SchedulingDisabled — new pods won't land on it Always kubectl uncordon <node> as your LAST step
Using kubectl delete node instead of drain Node removed from cluster — must rejoin with kubeadm Use kubectl drain for maintenance, never delete
Confused cordon vs drain Cordon only prevents NEW pods — existing pods stay running Drain = cordon + evict all pods. Use drain for maintenance
Draining control plane without thought If single control plane, you lose API server access! Control plane static pods can't be drained anyway — only user workloads

Node Maintenance Decision Tree

🤔Maintenance needed?
→
🛡️Create PDB first
→
🚿kubectl drain node
→
🔧Perform work
→
✅kubectl uncordon
🛠️ Scenario: kubectl drain Gets Stuck

You run kubectl drain worker01 --ignore-daemonsets and it hangs for minutes without completing. What's happening?

  • PDB violation: A PodDisruptionBudget prevents eviction — check kubectl get pdb
  • Pod with no controller: Standalone pods (not managed by Deployment/ReplicaSet) block drain — add --force
  • Local storage pods: Pods with emptyDir volumes block drain — add --delete-emptydir-data
  • Long graceful shutdown: Pod has a high terminationGracePeriodSeconds — add --grace-period=30

Quick fix: kubectl drain worker01 --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data --force --grace-period=30

🔬 Practice Drill 30.1 — Drain and Uncordon a Node

⏱️ Target Time: 3 minutes
Scenario

Node worker02 needs emergency maintenance. Safely remove all workloads from the node, then after the maintenance is complete, make it schedulable again.

  1. Drain worker02, ignoring DaemonSet-managed pods
  2. Verify no user pods are running on worker02
  3. After maintenance, uncordon the node
  4. Verify the node is Ready and schedulable
Hint: Use --ignore-daemonsets with drain. DaemonSet pods (like kube-proxy) cannot be evicted — the flag tells drain to skip them. If pods use emptyDir, add --delete-emptydir-data.
View Solution 
# 1. Drain the node
kubectl drain worker02 --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data

# 2. Verify no user pods on worker02
kubectl get pods -A -o wide | grep worker02
# Only DaemonSet pods should remain (kube-proxy, etc.)

# 3. Perform maintenance (OS upgrade, kernel patch, etc.)
# ...

# 4. Uncordon the node
kubectl uncordon worker02

# 5. Verify node is Ready and schedulable
kubectl get nodes
# worker02 should show Ready (no SchedulingDisabled)

🔬 Practice Drill 30.2 — Create a PodDisruptionBudget

⏱️ Target Time: 4 minutes
Scenario

Your anihpj deployment has 3 replicas with label app: anihpj. Create a PodDisruptionBudget named anihpj-pdb that ensures at least 2 pods are always available during voluntary disruptions.

  1. Create a PDB YAML with minAvailable: 2
  2. Apply the PDB to the cluster
  3. Verify the PDB shows correct allowed disruptions
  4. Test by draining a node — observe the PDB in action
Hint: The PDB's selector.matchLabels must match the pod labels from your Deployment. Use apiVersion: policy/v1 and choose either minAvailable or maxUnavailable — not both.
View Solution 
# anihpj-pdb.yaml
apiVersion: policy/v1
kind: PodDisruptionBudget
metadata:
  name: anihpj-pdb
  namespace: default
spec:
  minAvailable: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
# Apply
kubectl apply -f anihpj-pdb.yaml

# Verify
kubectl get pdb
# NAME         MIN AVAILABLE   MAX UNAVAILABLE   ALLOWED DISRUPTIONS   AGE
# anihpj-pdb   2               N/A               1                     10s

# Test — drain will respect PDB
kubectl drain worker01 --ignore-daemonsets
# Drain will evict pods ONE AT A TIME, waiting for each to reschedule
# before evicting the next, ensuring at least 2 are always running

🔬 Practice Drill 30.3 — Complete OS Upgrade Workflow

⏱️ Target Time: 5 minutes
Scenario

Worker node worker01 needs a kernel upgrade. Perform the complete maintenance workflow: drain, upgrade, verify, and restore service.

  1. Drain worker01
  2. SSH to worker01 and simulate an OS upgrade
  3. Verify kubelet is running and the node is healthy
  4. Uncordon and verify pods can be scheduled again
Hint: After a reboot, kubelet should start automatically (it's a systemd service). If it doesn't, check with systemctl status kubelet and start it with systemctl start kubelet.
View Solution 
# From control plane:
kubectl drain worker01 --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data

# SSH to worker:
ssh worker01
apt-get update && apt-get upgrade -y
# If kernel upgrade: reboot
# Wait for node to come back...

# From control plane — verify:
kubectl get nodes
# worker01 should show Ready,SchedulingDisabled

# Check kubelet
ssh worker01 systemctl status kubelet

# Uncordon
kubectl uncordon worker01

# Verify
kubectl get nodes
# All nodes Ready, no SchedulingDisabled

✅ Chapter 30 — Verification Checklist

  • kubectl get nodes — All nodes Ready, no SchedulingDisabled after maintenance
  • kubectl get pods -A -o wide — Pods distributed across available nodes
  • kubectl get pdb — PDBs show correct ALLOWED DISRUPTIONS count
  • kubectl describe pdb <name> — PDB selector matches target pods
  • kubectl drain <node> --dry-run=client — Preview what drain would do
  • systemctl status kubelet — kubelet running on nodes after reboot

⚠️ CKA Gotcha: Node Maintenance Traps

  • drain vs cordon — cordon only marks the node as unschedulable (existing pods stay). drain cordons AND evicts all pods. The exam says "remove all workloads from node" = drain, not cordon
  • Drain fails without flags — DaemonSet pods can't be evicted (use --ignore-daemonsets). Pods with emptyDir data (use --delete-emptydir-data). Unmanaged pods without a controller (use --force — but data is lost)
  • PDB blocks drain — If a PodDisruptionBudget would be violated, drain hangs. Check PDBs: kubectl get pdb. If ALLOWED DISRUPTIONS is 0, you must wait or scale up first
  • Don't forget to uncordon — After maintenance, kubectl uncordon <node>. Without this, the node stays SchedulingDisabled and no new pods land on it. The exam checks for this
  • PDB spec: minAvailable vs maxUnavailable — Use one OR the other, not both. minAvailable: 2 means at least 2 pods must stay up. maxUnavailable: 1 means at most 1 pod can be down

CKA Speed Tip — Node maintenance workflow:
kubectl drain <node> --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data
Perform maintenance (reboot, OS upgrade, etc.)
kubectl uncordon <node>
kubectl get nodes — verify Ready status
PDB creation: kubectl create pdb my-pdb --selector=app=web --min-available=2

⚡ Node maintenance commands: kubectl cordon <node> — mark unschedulable (no eviction)
kubectl drain <node> --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data — evict all pods
kubectl uncordon <node> — re-enable scheduling
kubectl get pdb -A — list all PodDisruptionBudgets
kubectl describe pdb <name> — check allowed disruptions and selector
📝 Chapter 30 Practice Drill

Timed challenge (target: 6 minutes):

  • 1. Create a deployment web with 3 replicas: kubectl create deploy web --image=nginx --replicas=3
  • 2. Create a PDB: kubectl create pdb web-pdb --selector=app=web --min-available=2
  • 3. Drain a worker node — observe that only 1 pod is evicted (PDB allows max 1 disruption)
  • 4. Verify: kubectl get pdb — ALLOWED DISRUPTIONS should be 0 during drain
  • 5. Uncordon the node and verify pods rebalance

📝 Chapter 30 — CKA Practice Questions

Q1
What is the difference between kubectl cordon and kubectl drain?
Show Answer
Answer
Command Marks Unschedulable Evicts Pods Use Case
cordon ✅ Yes ❌ No — existing pods stay Prevent new pods while existing finish
drain ✅ Yes (cordons first) ✅ Yes — evicts all pods Full node evacuation for maintenance 
# Cordon:
kubectl cordon worker-1
# node/worker-1 cordoned
kubectl get nodes
# worker-1  Ready,SchedulingDisabled  ← Exists pods stay

# Drain:
kubectl drain worker-1 --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data
# Cordons + evicts all non-DaemonSet pods

# Uncordon (same for both):
kubectl uncordon worker-1
# node/worker-1 uncordoned
Explanation

cordon = soft block (no new pods, existing stay). drain = hard block (cordons + evicts everything). On the CKA, "remove all workloads from a node" = drain. "Prevent new pods on a node" = cordon. Both show SchedulingDisabled in node status. uncordon reverses both. Drain includes cordon, so you only need uncordon after drain.

Q2
Drain a node safely. What flags do you need and why?
Show Answer
Answer
# Full drain command:
kubectl drain worker-1 \
  --ignore-daemonsets \
  --delete-emptydir-data \
  --force

# Flag breakdown:
# --ignore-daemonsets  → DaemonSet pods can't be evicted
#   (they're managed by the DaemonSet controller)
#   Without this: "cannot delete DaemonSet-managed Pods"

# --delete-emptydir-data → Pods with emptyDir volumes
#   (data in emptyDir is lost on eviction)
#   Without this: "cannot delete Pods with local storage"

# --force → Pods not managed by a controller
#   (standalone pods with no ReplicaSet/Deployment)
#   Without this: "cannot delete Pods not managed by..."
#   WARNING: forced pods are DELETED, not rescheduled!

# Best practice (CKA safe combo):
kubectl drain worker-1 --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data

# Timeout (if drain hangs):
kubectl drain worker-1 --ignore-daemonsets \
  --delete-emptydir-data --timeout=60s
Explanation

Without flags, drain fails on: (1) DaemonSet pods (--ignore-daemonsets), (2) pods with emptyDir (--delete-emptydir-data), (3) unmanaged pods (--force). On the CKA, --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data is the standard combo. Use --force only when told to — it permanently deletes standalone pods. DaemonSet pods are NOT evicted (they're recreated by the DS controller if the node becomes schedulable).

Q3
Create a PodDisruptionBudget that ensures at least 2 pods of a deployment stay available during disruptions.
Show Answer
Answer
# Imperative (fastest):
kubectl create pdb web-pdb \
  --selector=app=web \
  --min-available=2

# YAML (for reference):
apiVersion: policy/v1
kind: PodDisruptionBudget
metadata:
  name: web-pdb
spec:
  minAvailable: 2           # At least 2 pods must stay up
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: web

# Alternative: use maxUnavailable:
apiVersion: policy/v1
kind: PodDisruptionBudget
metadata:
  name: web-pdb
spec:
  maxUnavailable: 1         # At most 1 pod can be down
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: web

# Verify:
kubectl get pdb
# NAME      MIN AVAILABLE  MAX UNAVAILABLE  ALLOWED DISRUPTIONS  AGE
# web-pdb   2              N/A              1                    10s

# Test: drain a node with pods matching the selector
kubectl drain worker-1 --ignore-daemonsets
# If draining would violate PDB → drain HANGS until safe
Explanation

PDBs protect against voluntary disruptions (drain, upgrades). Use minAvailable OR maxUnavailable, not both. minAvailable: 2 means drain will only evict pods if at least 2 will remain. On the CKA, the imperative kubectl create pdb is fastest. The ALLOWED DISRUPTIONS column shows how many pods can currently be disrupted. If it's 0, drain will hang. PDBs only affect voluntary disruptions — node crashes still kill pods.

Q4
A kubectl drain command hangs and won't complete. What could be wrong?
Show Answer
Answer
# Cause 1: PDB blocks the drain
kubectl get pdb -A
# NAME      MIN AVAILABLE  ALLOWED DISRUPTIONS
# web-pdb   2              0  ← ZERO allowed!
# If deployment has exactly 2 replicas and minAvailable=2,
# NO pods can be evicted

# Fix: Scale up first
kubectl scale deploy web --replicas=3
# Now ALLOWED DISRUPTIONS becomes 1
# Drain can proceed (one at a time)

# Cause 2: Missing flags
kubectl drain worker-1
# Error: cannot delete DaemonSet-managed Pods
# Fix: --ignore-daemonsets
# Error: cannot delete Pods with local storage
# Fix: --delete-emptydir-data

# Cause 3: Pod termination takes too long
# Pods have long graceful shutdown (terminationGracePeriodSeconds)
# Fix: Add timeout
kubectl drain worker-1 --ignore-daemonsets --timeout=120s

# Cause 4: Unmanaged pods
# Standalone pods with no controller
kubectl drain worker-1 --ignore-daemonsets --force
# WARNING: these pods will be DELETED permanently

# Debug: See what's blocking
kubectl drain worker-1 --ignore-daemonsets --dry-run=client
# Shows what WOULD be evicted without doing it
Explanation

Drain hangs because: (1) PDB won't allow disruption (ALLOWED=0), (2) missing flags for DaemonSets/emptyDir, (3) pods taking too long to terminate, (4) unmanaged standalone pods. On the CKA, check PDBs first with kubectl get pdb. If ALLOWED DISRUPTIONS is 0, scale up the deployment or delete the PDB (if appropriate). Use --dry-run=client to preview what drain would do without executing it.

Q5
Perform a complete OS upgrade on a worker node without losing workloads.
Show Answer
Answer
# Step 1: Create PDB for critical workloads (optional)
kubectl create pdb web-pdb --selector=app=web --min-available=2

# Step 2: Drain the node (from control plane)
kubectl drain worker-1 \
  --ignore-daemonsets \
  --delete-emptydir-data
# All pods evicted and rescheduled to other nodes

# Step 3: SSH to the worker
ssh worker-1

# Step 4: Perform OS upgrade
sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get upgrade -y
# Or: sudo apt-get dist-upgrade -y

# Step 5: Reboot if kernel was updated
sudo reboot

# Step 6: Wait for node to come back online
# From control plane:
kubectl get nodes -w
# worker-1  NotReady → Ready (after reboot)

# Step 7: Uncordon the node
kubectl uncordon worker-1

# Step 8: Verify
kubectl get nodes
# worker-1  Ready  ← Schedulable again!

kubectl get pods -o wide
# Pods will gradually be scheduled back to worker-1
Explanation

OS upgrade workflow: drain → SSH → upgrade → reboot → uncordon. Draining first ensures workloads move to other nodes. PDBs protect against too many pods being evicted. On the CKA, this is a multi-step task: you must drain, perform maintenance, then uncordon. After uncordon, pods won't automatically move back — they stay on other nodes until rescheduled (by scale-up, rolling update, or pod restart).

Q6
What happens to pods when a node becomes NotReady for more than 5 minutes?
Show Answer
Answer
# Timeline when a node goes down:
# 0s:    Node stops sending heartbeats
# 40s:   Node status → Unknown (default node-monitor-grace-period)
# 5m:    Pods marked for eviction
#        (default pod-eviction-timeout, set on controller-manager)
# 5m+:   Pods rescheduled to healthy nodes

# Controller-manager settings:
cat /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-controller-manager.yaml
# --pod-eviction-timeout=5m0s          ← Default
# --node-monitor-grace-period=40s      ← Default
# --node-monitor-period=5s             ← How often to check

# Check node conditions:
kubectl describe node worker-1 | grep -A5 Conditions
# Ready    Unknown    ... kubelet stopped posting status

# Check pod status:
kubectl get pods -o wide | grep worker-1
