# web-xxx  1/1  Terminating  ← Being evicted
# After eviction, controller creates replacement on other nodes

# DaemonSet pods: NOT evicted (rescheduled when node returns)
# StatefulSet pods: Wait for node to return (no auto-reschedule)
Explanation

After ~5 minutes of NotReady, the controller-manager evicts pods from the node (configurable via pod-eviction-timeout). Deployment/ReplicaSet pods are rescheduled to healthy nodes. DaemonSet pods are skipped. StatefulSet pods are NOT auto-rescheduled (unique identity). On the CKA, know the timeline: 40s grace → status Unknown → 5m eviction timeout → pods rescheduled. This is why planned maintenance uses drain (immediate, controlled) instead of just powering off the node.

Q7
What is the difference between minAvailable and maxUnavailable in a PDB?
Show Answer
Answer
# minAvailable: MINIMUM pods that must stay running
# Scenario: 5 replicas, minAvailable: 3
# Allowed disruptions: 5 - 3 = 2 (can evict 2 at a time)

# maxUnavailable: MAXIMUM pods that can be down
# Scenario: 5 replicas, maxUnavailable: 2
# Allowed disruptions: 2 (can evict 2 at a time)

# They're equivalent in this case!

# Percentage support:
apiVersion: policy/v1
kind: PodDisruptionBudget
metadata:
  name: web-pdb
spec:
  minAvailable: "50%"       # At least 50% must stay up
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: web
# 10 replicas → at least 5 must stay up

# Edge cases:
# minAvailable: 0   → ALL pods can be disrupted (useless PDB)
# maxUnavailable: 0 → NO pods can be disrupted (drain always hangs!)
# minAvailable: 100% → Same as maxUnavailable: 0 (nothing can be evicted)

# You can ONLY use ONE — not both:
# ✗ minAvailable + maxUnavailable = ERROR
# ✓ minAvailable only
# ✓ maxUnavailable only
Explanation

Both control the same thing from different angles. minAvailable = floor ("at least N must run"), maxUnavailable = ceiling ("at most N can be down"). Can be integer or percentage. Use only ONE. On the CKA, maxUnavailable: 1 is often preferred — it's intuitive and works regardless of replica count. Setting maxUnavailable: 0 or minAvailable: 100% prevents all voluntary disruptions — drain will hang forever.

Q8
After uncordoning a node, pods don't move back to it. Is this expected?
Show Answer
Answer
# YES — this is expected behavior!

# After uncordon:
# ✓ Node becomes schedulable again
# ✓ NEW pods CAN be scheduled there
# ✗ Existing pods do NOT automatically move back

# Why? Kubernetes doesn't reschedule running pods.
# The scheduler only places NEW pods.

# To rebalance pods manually:
# Method 1: Delete pods (controller recreates them)
kubectl delete pod web-app-xxxxx
# ReplicaSet creates new pod → scheduler may pick the empty node

# Method 2: Rolling restart
kubectl rollout restart deployment/web-app
# All pods are recreated → rebalanced across nodes

# Method 3: Use Descheduler (not built-in)
# Third-party tool that automatically rebalances

# Verify pods are now on the uncordoned node:
kubectl get pods -o wide | grep worker-1
# web-app-new-xxx  1/1  Running  worker-1 ← Rebalanced!
Explanation

Kubernetes does NOT auto-rebalance after uncordon. Running pods stay where they are — only NEW pods consider the uncordoned node. On the CKA, this is expected behavior. To force rebalancing: kubectl rollout restart (graceful) or delete individual pods. The scheduler considers resource availability, so the "empty" node is likely to get new pods. The Descheduler is a community project — NOT on the CKA.

Q9
How do you remove a node from the cluster permanently?
Show Answer
Answer
# Step 1: Drain all workloads
kubectl drain worker-3 --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data

# Step 2: Delete the node object
kubectl delete node worker-3
# node "worker-3" deleted

# Step 3: On the worker node, reset kubeadm
ssh worker-3
sudo kubeadm reset -f
# Clears: kubelet config, PKI certs, etcd data

# Step 4: Clean up network (on the worker)
sudo iptables -F && sudo iptables -t nat -F
sudo ipvsadm --clear  # if using IPVS
sudo rm -rf /etc/cni/net.d

# Step 5: Verify from control plane
kubectl get nodes
# worker-3 should be GONE

# To re-join later:
# On control plane, generate new token:
kubeadm token create --print-join-command
# On worker: run the join command output
Explanation

Permanent removal: drain → delete node → kubeadm reset on worker → clean up networking. kubeadm reset removes all cluster configuration from the node. On the CKA, this is less common but may appear in cluster maintenance scenarios. After reset, the node can rejoin with a new kubeadm join command. Always drain first to gracefully move workloads.

Q10
How do you check which pods will be evicted by a drain without actually draining?
Show Answer
Answer
# Dry-run drain:
kubectl drain worker-1 \
  --ignore-daemonsets \
  --delete-emptydir-data \
  --dry-run=client
# node/worker-1 already cordoned (simulated)
# evicting pod default/web-app-xxxxx (simulated)
# evicting pod default/api-xxxxx (simulated)
# pod/web-app-xxxxx evicted (simulated)
# pod/api-xxxxx evicted (simulated)
# ← Shows ALL pods that WOULD be evicted

# List pods on a specific node:
kubectl get pods -A --field-selector spec.nodeName=worker-1
# Shows all pods currently on worker-1

# Filter out DaemonSet pods (they won't be evicted):
kubectl get pods -A --field-selector spec.nodeName=worker-1 \
  -o custom-columns=\
'NAMESPACE:.metadata.namespace,NAME:.metadata.name,OWNER:.metadata.ownerReferences[0].kind' | \
  grep -v DaemonSet

# Check PDB impact:
kubectl get pdb -A
# See ALLOWED DISRUPTIONS column
# If 0 for any PDB matching pods on this node → drain will hang
Explanation

--dry-run=client shows what drain WOULD do without executing. On the CKA, use this to verify before actual drain — especially when PDBs are involved. List pods on a node with --field-selector spec.nodeName=. DaemonSet pods are always skipped (with --ignore-daemonsets). Check kubectl get pdb to see if any PDB would block the drain.

🔴 Part 9: Troubleshooting Chapters 31-34

Chapter 31: Debugging Pods and Containers

Pod lifecycle, status codes, debugging toolkit, essential debug commands, and common troubleshooting scenarios. This is the heaviest CKA exam domain at 30% — master these skills.

🔴 Advanced ⏱️ ~2 hours 🔍 Debug 📝 CKA EXAM

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ Pod lifecycle phases — Pending, Running, Succeeded, Failed, Unknown
  • ✅ Container status codes and what each exit code means
  • ✅ The systematic debugging toolkit — describe, logs, exec, events
  • ✅ Ephemeral debug containers for distroless images
  • ✅ Real-world troubleshooting scenarios with anihpj Django app
🎓 CKA Relevance
Troubleshooting 30%
🎯 Troubleshooting is 30% of CKA — The Heaviest Domain

Every CKA exam includes multiple debugging tasks. You must have these commands in muscle memory:

  • kubectl describe pod <name> — always check Events at the bottom
  • kubectl logs <pod> --previous — see why a crashed container failed
  • kubectl exec -it <pod> -- /bin/sh — shell into a running container
  • kubectl get events --sort-by='.lastTimestamp' — cluster-wide event timeline

31.1 Pod Lifecycle and Status Codes

🎯 CKA EXAM TOPIC

Understanding pod phases and container states is the foundation of all Kubernetes troubleshooting. When something breaks, the status tells you where to look.

Pod Lifecycle — From Creation to Termination

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                        POD LIFECYCLE FLOW                                     │
│                                                                              │
│  kubectl apply ─▶ API Server ─▶ Scheduler ─▶ kubelet ─▶ Container Runtime   │
│                                                                              │
│  ┌─────────┐     ┌─────────┐     ┌─────────┐     ┌───────────┐             │
│  │ Pending │────▶│ Running │────▶│Succeeded│     │  Failed   │             │
│  │         │     │         │──┐  │ (exit 0)│     │(exit ≠ 0) │             │
│  └─────────┘     └─────────┘  │  └─────────┘     └───────────┘             │
│       │               │      │                        ▲                     │
│       │               │      └────────────────────────┘                     │
│       ▼               ▼                                                     │
│  ┌─────────┐     ┌──────────────┐                                          │
│  │ Unknown │     │CrashLoopBack │  ← Container keeps crashing              │
│  │(node    │     │    Off       │     and Kubernetes keeps restarting       │
│  │ lost)   │     │              │     it with exponential backoff           │
│  └─────────┘     └──────────────┘     (10s, 20s, 40s, ... 5min max)        │
│                                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Pod Phases

Phase Meaning What To Do
Pending Accepted by API server but not yet running — scheduling, image pull, or resource wait kubectl describe pod → check Events
Running At least one container is running, starting, or restarting Check if pod is Ready (readiness probe passing)
Succeeded All containers terminated with exit code 0 Normal for Jobs and CronJobs
Failed All containers terminated, at least one with non-zero exit kubectl logs <pod> --previous
Unknown Cannot determine pod state — node communication lost Check node status: kubectl get nodes

Container States — What Each Status Means

Status Common Cause Debug Command
Waiting: ContainerCreating Image being pulled or volume mounting kubectl describe pod → Events
Waiting: ErrImagePull Wrong image name, tag doesn't exist, or registry auth failed kubectl describe pod → check image field
Waiting: ImagePullBackOff Same as ErrImagePull but after repeated failures (with backoff) Fix image reference or add imagePullSecrets
Waiting: CrashLoopBackOff Container starts then crashes repeatedly kubectl logs <pod> --previous
Waiting: CreateContainerError SecurityContext issue, ConfigMap/Secret missing kubectl describe pod → Events
Running but not Ready Readiness probe failing — app not serving traffic kubectl describe pod → Conditions section
Terminated: OOMKilled Container exceeded memory limit (exit code 137) Increase resources.limits.memory
Terminated: Error Container exited with non-zero code (app crash) kubectl logs <pod> --previous
💡 Exit Code Quick Reference

Exit 0 = Success (normal completion). Exit 1 = Application error. Exit 137 = OOM Killed (128 + signal 9). Exit 139 = Segmentation fault. Exit 143 = Graceful termination (128 + signal 15 SIGTERM).

Restart Policies — How Kubernetes Handles Failures

Restart Policy Behavior Used By
Always (default) Restart container regardless of exit code — with exponential backoff (10s, 20s, 40s, ... 5min max) Deployments, StatefulSets, DaemonSets
OnFailure Restart only if exit code ≠ 0. Exit 0 = done. Jobs (retry on failure)
Never Never restart, regardless of exit code Jobs (one-shot), debug pods

Restart Backoff Timeline — CrashLoopBackOff Explained

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  restartPolicy: Always — Exponential Backoff                                 │
│                                                                              │
│  Time:  0s     10s     30s      70s       150s        310s       600s       │
│         │       │       │        │          │           │          │         │
│  Run:   ▓▓▓X   ▓▓X    ▓▓▓X    ▓▓X       ▓▓▓X       ▓▓X        ▓▓▓        │
│         crash   crash  crash   crash     crash      crash      success?    │
│                                                                              │
│  Wait:  ──10s── ─20s── ──40s── ───80s─── ──160s──── ──300s────             │
│                                                                              │
│  Status:  CrashLoopBackOff ────────────────────────────▶  Running           │
│                                                                              │
│  💡 Max backoff = 5 minutes (300s). Resets after pod runs for 10+ minutes.  │
│  💡 The STATUS alternates between "CrashLoopBackOff" and "Error".           │
│  💡 Restart count = shown in kubectl get pods RESTARTS column.              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

31.2 The Debugging Toolkit

🎯 CKA EXAM TOPIC

When a pod isn't working, follow this decision tree systematically. Never guess — always gather evidence first.

Systematic Debug Sequence

1
Get Status

kubectl get pods -o wide — What phase is the pod in? How many restarts? Which node?

2
Describe Pod

kubectl describe pod <name> — Check Events at the bottom for scheduling/pulling/startup errors.

3
Check Logs

kubectl logs <pod> --previous — For CrashLoopBackOff, --previous shows the last crash output.

4
Shell In

kubectl exec -it <pod> -- /bin/sh — If pod is Running, interactively inspect files, network, processes.

5
Check Events

kubectl get events --sort-by='.lastTimestamp' — Cluster-wide timeline showing all recent events.

Exit Code Quick Reference

✅
Exit 0 — Success

Container completed normally. Expected for Jobs/CronJobs. For long-running pods, this means the process quit unexpectedly.

❌
Exit 1 — App Error

Application crashed (unhandled exception, config error, missing dependency). Check kubectl logs --previous.

💀
Exit 137 — OOM Killed

Container exceeded memory limit (128 + SIGKILL=9). Fix: increase resources.limits.memory.

🛑
Exit 143 — SIGTERM

Graceful termination (128 + SIGTERM=15). Normal during pod deletion/rollout. Not an error.

🔍 Pod Debugging Decision Tree

┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                 POD NOT WORKING? Follow this tree:                         │
│                                                                            │
│  $ kubectl get pods -o wide                                                │
│       │                                                                    │
│       ├── STATUS: Pending?                                                 │
│       │    └── $ kubectl describe pod <name>                               │
│       │         ├── "Insufficient cpu/memory"                              │
│       │         │    → Scale cluster or adjust resource requests           │
│       │         ├── "no nodes match pod's node selector"                   │
│       │         │    → Check nodeSelector/nodeAffinity labels              │
│       │         ├── "node(s) had taint... pod didn't tolerate"             │
│       │         │    → Add toleration to pod spec OR remove taint          │
│       │         └── "persistentvolumeclaim not found"                      │
│       │              → Create the missing PVC or fix PVC name              │
│       │                                                                    │
│       ├── STATUS: CrashLoopBackOff?                                        │
│       │    └── $ kubectl logs <pod> --previous                             │
│       │         ├── Application error / stack trace                        │
│       │         │    → Fix app code or configuration                       │
│       │         ├── "connection refused" / "no such host"                  │
│       │         │    → Dependent service not ready (DB, Redis)             │
│       │         ├── Missing config/secret reference                        │
│       │         │    → Check ConfigMap/Secret exists & mounted correctly   │
│       │         └── Permission denied                                      │
│       │              → Check SecurityContext & file permissions            │
│       │                                                                    │
│       ├── STATUS: ImagePullBackOff?                                        │
│       │    └── $ kubectl describe pod <name>                               │
│       │         ├── Wrong image name/tag                                   │
│       │         │    → Fix spec.containers[].image                         │
│       │         └── Private registry auth failed                           │
│       │              → Add imagePullSecrets to pod spec                    │
│       │                                                                    │
│       └── STATUS: Running but not working?                                 │
│            ├── $ kubectl logs <pod>           ← application logs           │
│            ├── $ kubectl exec -it <pod> -- /bin/sh  ← shell in            │
│            ├── $ kubectl port-forward <pod> 8080:80 ← test locally        │
│            └── Check readiness/liveness probes in describe output          │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

⚠️ Common Debugging Mistakes

  • ✗ Wrong Immediately editing the pod YAML without checking logs first
  • ✓ Right Always run describe → logs → events before making changes
  • ✗ Wrong Using kubectl logs <pod> for a crashed container
  • ✓ Right Using kubectl logs <pod> --previous to see the last crashed container's output
  • ✗ Wrong Forgetting to check the Events section at the bottom of describe
  • ✓ Right Scroll to the very bottom — Events show scheduling/pulling/startup timeline

31.3 Essential Debug Commands

🎯 CKA EXAM TOPIC

These are the commands you'll use on every single troubleshooting task in the CKA exam. Memorize them.

🚀 The Debug Command Sequence

Follow this order — each step narrows down the problem:

Step Command What You Learn
1. Overview kubectl get pods -o wide Pod status, restarts, node placement, IP
2. Details kubectl describe pod <name> Config, conditions, events timeline
3. App logs kubectl logs <pod> Application stdout/stderr output
4. Prev logs kubectl logs <pod> --previous Output from last crashed container
5. Shell in kubectl exec -it <pod> -- /bin/sh Interactive debugging inside container
6. Events kubectl get events --sort-by='.lastTimestamp' Cluster-wide event timeline
7. Resources kubectl top pod <name> Current CPU/memory usage
Essential Debug Commands — Complete Reference 
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# 1. GET — Overview of pod status
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
kubectl get pods                           # All pods in current namespace
kubectl get pods -o wide                   # + Node, IP columns
kubectl get pods -A                        # All namespaces
kubectl get pods --show-labels             # See pod labels
kubectl get pods -w                        # Watch for changes in real-time

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# 2. DESCRIBE — Detailed pod information + Events
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
kubectl describe pod <name>                # Full details + Events at bottom
# KEY SECTIONS TO CHECK:
#   - Status / Conditions     → Is it ready? Why not?
#   - Containers → State      → Waiting/Running/Terminated + reason
#   - Events (bottom)         → Scheduling, pulling, startup timeline

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# 3. LOGS — Application output
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
kubectl logs <pod>                         # Current container stdout
kubectl logs <pod> --previous              # Previous crashed container ⭐
kubectl logs <pod> -f                      # Follow / stream live
kubectl logs <pod> --tail=100              # Last 100 lines only
kubectl logs <pod> --since=1h              # Last hour only
kubectl logs <pod> -c <container>          # Specific container (multi-container)
kubectl logs <pod> --all-containers        # All containers in the pod

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# 4. EXEC — Shell into a running container
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
kubectl exec -it <pod> -- /bin/sh          # Interactive shell (Alpine/BusyBox)
kubectl exec -it <pod> -- /bin/bash        # Bash shell (Ubuntu/Debian)
kubectl exec <pod> -- cat /etc/resolv.conf # Run single command
kubectl exec <pod> -- env                  # Check environment variables
kubectl exec <pod> -- wget -qO- http://svc # Test connectivity from inside

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# 5. EVENTS — Cluster-wide timeline
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
kubectl get events --sort-by='.metadata.creationTimestamp'
kubectl get events --field-selector reason=Failed
kubectl get events -n kube-system          # Control plane events

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# 6. RESOURCE USAGE — CPU and memory
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
kubectl top pods                           # CPU/memory per pod
kubectl top pods --sort-by=cpu             # Sort by CPU usage
kubectl top pods --sort-by=memory          # Sort by memory usage
kubectl top nodes                          # Node-level resource usage

✅ Verify Your Debug Skills

  • Can you get previous container logs? → kubectl logs <pod> --previous
  • Can you check events sorted by time? → kubectl get events --sort-by='.metadata.creationTimestamp'
  • Can you shell into a specific container? → kubectl exec -it <pod> -c <container> -- /bin/sh

Ephemeral Debug Containers — Debugging Distroless Images

💡 When kubectl exec Won't Work

Many production images are distroless — they don't include a shell (/bin/sh), curl, or any debug tools. Ephemeral containers let you inject a debug container into a running pod without restarting it.

Ephemeral Debug Containers 
# Attach a debug container to a running pod
kubectl debug -it <pod-name> --image=busybox:1.36 --target=<container>

# Debug with a full toolkit (nicolaka/netshoot has curl, dig, tcpdump, etc.)
kubectl debug -it <pod-name> --image=nicolaka/netshoot --target=app

# Create a copy of the pod with a debug container (doesn't affect original)
kubectl debug <pod-name> -it --image=busybox --copy-to=debug-pod

# Debug a node directly (creates a pod with host namespaces)
kubectl debug node/<node-name> -it --image=busybox

Ephemeral Containers vs kubectl exec

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                                                                              │
│  REGULAR CONTAINER (distroless)        EPHEMERAL DEBUG CONTAINER             │
│  ┌─────────────────────────┐          ┌─────────────────────────┐           │
│  │  Your App (Go binary)   │          │  Debug Tools            │           │
│  │                         │          │  • /bin/sh              │           │
│  │  ❌ No /bin/sh          │  ◄──────▶│  • curl, wget           │           │
│  │  ❌ No curl, dig        │  shares  │  • dig, nslookup        │           │
│  │  ❌ No package manager  │  process │  • tcpdump, strace      │           │
│  │                         │  space   │  • ps, netstat          │           │
│  └─────────────────────────┘          └─────────────────────────┘           │
│                                                                              │
│  💡 The ephemeral container shares the same PID namespace, network,         │
│     and IPC namespace — you can see the app's processes, files, and         │
│     network connections without modifying the original container.            │
│                                                                              │
│  ⚠️  Ephemeral containers CANNOT be removed once added — they stay          │
│     until the pod is deleted. They are never restarted.                      │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
✅ kubectl exec — Standard Debugging

kubectl exec -it <pod> -- /bin/sh

  • Works when container has a shell
  • Direct access to running process
  • Can modify files, check network
  • Best for: standard images (nginx, python, node)
🔍 kubectl debug — Advanced Debugging

kubectl debug -it <pod> --image=busybox

  • Works on distroless images (no shell)
  • Injects a sidecar with debug tools
  • Shares process/network namespace
  • Best for: Go binaries, scratch images, production pods

31.4 Debugging anihpj — Common Scenarios

Let's apply the debugging toolkit to real-world scenarios using our anihpj Django application. Each scenario follows the identify → gather → analyze → fix → verify flow.

Scenario 1: anihpj Pod in CrashLoopBackOff

🔴 CrashLoopBackOff — Database Connection Refused

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  STEP-BY-STEP WALKTHROUGH                                               │
│                                                                         │
│  1. IDENTIFY:                                                           │
│     $ kubectl get pods -n anihpj                                        │
│     NAME                  READY   STATUS             RESTARTS   AGE     │
│     anihpj-web-7d4f8c5    0/1     CrashLoopBackOff   5          10m     │
│     anihpj-db-0           1/1     Running            0          10m     │
│                                                                         │
│  2. GATHER — Check logs from the PREVIOUS crash:                        │
│     $ kubectl logs anihpj-web-7d4f8c5 -n anihpj --previous             │
│                                                                         │
│     django.db.utils.OperationalError:                                   │
│       could not connect to server: Connection refused               ◄── │
│       Is the server running on host "anihpj-db" and                     │
│       accepting TCP/IP connections on port 5432?                        │
│                                                                         │
│  3. ANALYZE:                                                            │
│     → The web pod starts before the DB pod is ready                     │
│     → Django tries to connect to PostgreSQL → fails → crashes           │
│     → Kubernetes restarts it → same thing → CrashLoopBackOff            │
│                                                                         │
│  4. FIX — Add an init container that waits for DB:                      │
│     initContainers:                                                     │
│     - name: wait-for-db                                                 │
│       image: busybox                                                    │
│       command: ['sh', '-c',                                             │
│         'until nc -z anihpj-db 5432; do sleep 2; done']                 │
│                                                                         │
│  5. VERIFY:                                                             │
│     $ kubectl get pods -n anihpj -w                                     │
│     anihpj-web-xxxx   0/1   Init:0/1   0   5s    ← init running        │
│     anihpj-web-xxxx   1/1   Running    0   15s   ← ✅ success          │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Scenario 2: anihpj Pod Stuck in Pending

🟡 Pending — Insufficient Memory

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  STEP-BY-STEP WALKTHROUGH                                               │
│                                                                         │
│  1. IDENTIFY:                                                           │
│     $ kubectl get pods -n anihpj                                        │
│     NAME                  READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE              │
│     anihpj-web-xxxx       0/1     Pending   0          5m               │
│                                                                         │
│  2. GATHER:                                                             │
│     $ kubectl describe pod anihpj-web-xxxx -n anihpj                    │
│     ...                                                                 │
│     Events:                                                             │
│       Warning  FailedScheduling  0/2 nodes available:                   │
│         2 Insufficient memory.  ◄── The clue is always in Events!       │
│                                                                         │
│  3. ANALYZE:                                                            │
│     → Pod requests 2Gi memory but no node has that much free            │
│     → Check current requests vs capacity:                               │
│       $ kubectl describe node worker01 | grep -A5 "Allocated"           │
│                                                                         │
│  4. FIX — Reduce the request to fit available resources:                │
│     resources:                                                          │
│       requests:                                                         │
│         memory: "512Mi"    ← was 2Gi, reduced to fit                    │
│         cpu: "250m"                                                     │
│                                                                         │
│  5. VERIFY:                                                             │
│     $ kubectl get pods -n anihpj                                        │
│     anihpj-web-xxxx   1/1   Running   0   30s   ← ✅ scheduled         │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Scenario 3: anihpj Running but 502 Errors

🟢 Running but Broken — Django Not Binding Correctly

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  STEP-BY-STEP WALKTHROUGH                                               │
│                                                                         │
│  1. IDENTIFY:                                                           │
│     $ kubectl get pods -n anihpj                                        │
│     anihpj-web-xxxx   1/1   Running   0   5m   ← Looks fine!           │
│                                                                         │
│     $ kubectl get svc -n anihpj                                         │
│     anihpj-svc   ClusterIP   10.96.45.12   80/TCP   ← Service exists   │
│                                                                         │
│     $ kubectl exec test-pod -- wget -qO- http://anihpj-svc.anihpj      │
│     → 502 Bad Gateway                                                   │
│                                                                         │
│  2. GATHER — Shell into the pod:                                        │
│     $ kubectl exec -it anihpj-web-xxxx -n anihpj -- /bin/bash           │
│     root@anihpj:/app$ python manage.py runserver                        │
│     → Listening on http://127.0.0.1:8000/   ◄── PROBLEM!               │
│     → Django binds to localhost only!                                    │
│                                                                         │
│  3. ANALYZE:                                                            │
│     → Django listens on 127.0.0.1 (localhost)                           │
│     → Service sends traffic to the container's pod IP                   │
│     → Connection refused because Django isn't listening on 0.0.0.0      │
│                                                                         │
│  4. FIX — Bind to all interfaces:                                       │
│     command: ["python", "manage.py", "runserver", "0.0.0.0:8000"]       │
│     Also add to settings.py:                                            │
│       ALLOWED_HOSTS = ['*']    # or specific hostnames                  │
│                                                                         │
│  5. VERIFY:                                                             │
│     $ kubectl exec test-pod -- wget -qO- http://anihpj-svc.anihpj      │
│     → 200 OK ✅                                                         │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

🏋️ Practice Drill: Debug These Pods

1
Create a pod with a wrong image name: kubectl run broken --image=nginxxx:latest

Debug: What status? What does describe show?

2
Create a pod that crashes immediately: kubectl run crasher --image=busybox -- /bin/sh -c "exit 1"

Debug: Use --previous flag. What exit code?

3
Create a pod with impossible resource request: kubectl run toobig --image=nginx --dry-run=client -o yaml | sed 's/resources: {}/resources:\n requests:\n memory: "100Gi"/' | kubectl apply -f -

Debug: Why is it Pending? Check describe Events.

⚠️ CKA Gotcha: Pod Debugging Traps

  • Pod status decode — ImagePullBackOff = wrong image name/tag or no registry access. CrashLoopBackOff = container starts then exits (check logs + exit code). Pending = scheduling issue (resources, taints, nodeSelector). Init:Error = init container failed
  • kubectl logs shows CURRENT container — If the container crashed and restarted, use kubectl logs <pod> --previous to see the LAST crash output. Without --previous you see the new (maybe empty) logs
  • Multi-container pods need -c — kubectl logs <pod> -c <container>. Without it, you get an error if there are multiple containers. Use kubectl describe pod to find container names
  • Exit codes matter — Exit 0 = success (Completed). Exit 1 = application error. Exit 137 = OOMKilled (needs more memory). Exit 139 = segfault. The exam expects you to interpret these
  • kubectl debug for distroless images — kubectl debug -it <pod> --image=busybox --target=<container> attaches a debug container sharing the process namespace. Use this when the pod has no shell

CKA Speed Tip — Pod debug flow (memorize):
1. kubectl get pod <name> -o wide — status + node
2. kubectl describe pod <name> — events at bottom tell the story
3. kubectl logs <name> [--previous] — app-level errors
4. kubectl exec -it <name> -- sh — get inside and investigate
5. kubectl get events --sort-by=.lastTimestamp — cluster-wide event timeline

⚡ Pod debugging commands: kubectl get pods -A --field-selector=status.phase!=Running — find non-running pods
kubectl logs <pod> --all-containers=true — logs from all containers
kubectl logs <pod> --since=5m — last 5 minutes only
kubectl top pod <name> — CPU/memory usage (needs metrics-server)
kubectl get pod <name> -o jsonpath='{.status.containerStatuses[0].state}' — detailed state

📝 Chapter 31 — CKA Practice Questions

Q1
A pod is in CrashLoopBackOff state. Walk through the debugging process.
Show Answer
Answer
# Step 1: Check pod status
kubectl get pod crash-pod
# NAME       READY  STATUS            RESTARTS  AGE
# crash-pod  0/1    CrashLoopBackOff  5         3m

# Step 2: Check PREVIOUS container logs
kubectl logs crash-pod --previous
# Error: connection refused to database at db:5432
# ← App started but couldn't connect to DB

# Step 3: Check current logs (may be empty if crash is instant)
kubectl logs crash-pod
# (empty — container crashed before logging)

# Step 4: Check describe for events and exit code
kubectl describe pod crash-pod
# Last State:  Terminated
#   Exit Code: 1          ← App error (non-zero exit)
#   Reason:    Error
# Events:
#   Back-off restarting failed container

# Step 5: Decode exit code
# 0   = Success (Completed)
# 1   = Generic app error
# 137 = OOMKilled (need more memory)
# 139 = Segfault
# 143 = SIGTERM (graceful termination)

# Step 6: Fix based on cause
# Exit 1 → Fix app config, env vars, dependencies
# Exit 137 → Increase memory limits
# Exit 139 → Fix application bug
Explanation

CrashLoopBackOff = container starts, crashes, Kubernetes restarts it with exponential backoff. Key: use --previous to see the LAST crash's logs (current container may not have logged anything yet). Exit codes tell the story: 1 = app error, 137 = OOM (out of memory), 139 = segfault. On the CKA, always check describe for events AND exit code, then logs with --previous.

Q2
A pod is stuck in Pending state. What are the possible causes and how do you diagnose?
Show Answer
Answer
# Step 1: Describe the pod — check Events
kubectl describe pod pending-pod | tail -20
# Events:
# Warning  FailedScheduling  ...

# Cause 1: Insufficient resources
# "0/3 nodes are available: 3 Insufficient cpu"
# Fix: Lower resource requests OR add nodes
kubectl get pod pending-pod -o yaml | grep -A5 resources

# Cause 2: NodeSelector doesn't match
# "0/3 nodes are available: 3 node(s) didn't match Pod's node selector"
kubectl get pod pending-pod -o yaml | grep -A3 nodeSelector
kubectl get nodes --show-labels | grep <label>
# Fix: Add label to node OR fix nodeSelector

# Cause 3: Taints with no toleration
# "0/3 nodes are available: 3 node(s) had taint {key: NoSchedule}"
kubectl describe nodes | grep Taints
# Fix: Add tolerations OR remove taint

# Cause 4: PVC not bound
# "persistentvolumeclaim "data-pvc" not found" OR PVC Pending
kubectl get pvc
# Fix: Create PV or fix StorageClass

# Cause 5: SA doesn't exist
# "serviceaccount "missing-sa" not found"
kubectl get sa -n <namespace>
# Fix: Create the ServiceAccount

# Quick diagnosis:
kubectl get events --field-selector type=Warning \
  --sort-by=.lastTimestamp | head -10
Explanation

Pending = scheduler can't place the pod. Five main causes: insufficient resources, nodeSelector mismatch, taints without toleration, unbound PVC, missing ServiceAccount. On the CKA, kubectl describe pod Events section ALWAYS explains why. The FailedScheduling message lists: how many nodes exist, why each was rejected. Read this message carefully — it's your diagnosis.

Q3
A pod shows ImagePullBackOff. How do you fix it?
Show Answer
Answer
# Step 1: Describe to find the exact error
kubectl describe pod img-pod | grep -A5 "Events"
# Warning  Failed  ...  Failed to pull image "ngnix:latest"
# Warning  Failed  ...  rpc error: pull access denied

# Diagnosis by error message:
# "manifest unknown"     → Wrong tag (e.g., ngnix instead of nginx)
# "pull access denied"   → Private registry, no credentials
# "no such host"         → Wrong registry URL
# "i/o timeout"          → Network issue

# Fix 1: Wrong image name/tag
kubectl set image pod/img-pod app=nginx:1.25
# OR
kubectl edit pod img-pod  # Fix image name

# Fix 2: Private registry credentials
kubectl create secret docker-registry regcred \
  --docker-server=registry.example.com \
  --docker-username=user \
  --docker-password=pass + then add imagePullSecrets

# Fix 3: Check if image exists
# From a node with network:
crictl pull nginx:1.25
# If this works, the image name is correct

# Quick fix: delete and recreate with correct image
kubectl delete pod img-pod
kubectl run img-pod --image=nginx:1.25
Explanation

ImagePullBackOff = can't download the container image. Three main causes: (1) typo in image name/tag (most common on CKA), (2) private registry without credentials, (3) network issues. On the CKA, the fix is usually a simple typo: ngnix → nginx, or wrong tag. kubectl set image is the fastest fix. Check the Events section for the exact error message — it tells you exactly what failed.

Q4
How do you use kubectl debug to troubleshoot a pod with a distroless image (no shell)?
Show Answer
Answer
# Problem: Pod has no shell (distroless/scratch image)
kubectl exec -it app-pod -- sh
# OCI runtime exec failed: exec failed: unable to start
# container process: exec: "sh": executable file not found

# Solution: Ephemeral debug container
kubectl debug -it app-pod \
  --image=busybox \
  --target=app-container
# Attaches a busybox container that shares:
# - Process namespace (can see app processes)
# - Network namespace (same IP, ports)

# Inside the debug container:
ps aux  # See app processes
netstat -tlnp  # See listening ports
wget -O- localhost:8080  # Test app endpoint
cat /proc/1/environ | tr '\0' '\n'  # See env vars

# Alternative: Copy pod for debugging
kubectl debug app-pod -it \
  --image=busybox \
  --copy-to=debug-pod \
  --share-processes
# Creates a copy of the pod with debug container added

# Debug a node:
kubectl debug node/worker-1 -it --image=ubuntu
# Creates a privileged pod on the node
# Root filesystem at /host
chroot /host  # Full node access
Explanation

kubectl debug adds an ephemeral container to a running pod without restarting it. Use --target= to share the process namespace with a specific container. On the CKA, this is the way to debug distroless images. The debug container sees the app's processes and network. kubectl debug node/ creates a privileged pod on a node for node-level debugging. The --copy-to option creates a copy, preserving the original pod.

Q5
How do you check why a container was OOMKilled and fix it?
Show Answer
Answer
# Identify OOMKilled:
kubectl describe pod oom-pod
# Containers:
#   app:
#     Last State:  Terminated
#       Reason:    OOMKilled    ← Out of Memory!
#       Exit Code: 137          ← 128 + 9 (SIGKILL)

# Check current memory usage:
kubectl top pod oom-pod
# NAME      CPU(cores)  MEMORY(bytes)
# oom-pod   50m         490Mi  ← Close to limit!

# Check memory limits:
kubectl get pod oom-pod -o yaml | grep -A3 limits
# limits:
#   memory: 512Mi  ← 490Mi usage / 512Mi limit = 95%!

# Fix: Increase memory limit
kubectl patch pod oom-pod -p \
  '{"spec":{"containers":[{"name":"app","resources":{"limits":{"memory":"1Gi"}}}]}}'
# Error: Pod spec changes not allowed (immutable field)

# Must delete and recreate:
kubectl get pod oom-pod -o yaml > oom-fix.yaml
# Edit: Change memory limit from 512Mi to 1Gi
vi oom-fix.yaml
kubectl delete pod oom-pod
kubectl apply -f oom-fix.yaml

# For Deployments (easier):
kubectl set resources deployment/web \
  --limits=memory=1Gi --requests=memory=512Mi
# Triggers rolling update with new limits
Explanation

OOMKilled = container exceeded its memory limit. Exit code 137 = 128 + 9 (SIGKILL). On the CKA: (1) identify via describe (Reason: OOMKilled), (2) check current usage with kubectl top, (3) compare to limits, (4) increase memory limit. Pod resource specs are immutable — you must delete and recreate. For Deployments, use kubectl set resources which triggers a rolling update. Common CKA trap: confusing memory requests (scheduling) with limits (enforcement).

Q6
How do you get logs from all containers in a multi-container pod?
Show Answer
Answer
# List containers in the pod:
kubectl get pod multi-pod -o jsonpath='{.spec.containers[*].name}'
# app sidecar

# Logs from specific container:
kubectl logs multi-pod -c app
kubectl logs multi-pod -c sidecar

# Logs from ALL containers:
kubectl logs multi-pod --all-containers=true

# Stream ALL containers:
kubectl logs multi-pod --all-containers=true -f

# Previous crash logs for specific container:
kubectl logs multi-pod -c app --previous

# Last N lines from all containers:
kubectl logs multi-pod --all-containers --tail=50

# Logs from all pods matching a label:
kubectl logs -l app=web --all-containers
# (max 5 pods by default)
kubectl logs -l app=web --all-containers --max-log-requests=20

# Init container logs:
kubectl logs multi-pod -c init-container-name

# Check which container is failing:
kubectl describe pod multi-pod | grep -A10 "Container.*:"
Explanation

Multi-container pods require -c to specify which container's logs to view. --all-containers=true shows logs from every container. On the CKA, if you see "a]]container name must be specified" error, the pod has multiple containers — use -c. Init container logs: same syntax with -c init-name. -l flag retrieves logs from multiple pods by label selector. Default limit is 5 concurrent pods.

Q7
A pod is running but the application inside isn't responding. How do you debug?
Show Answer
Answer
# Pod shows Running but app doesn't work:
kubectl get pod app-pod
# NAME      READY  STATUS   RESTARTS  AGE
# app-pod   1/1    Running  0         10m

# Step 1: Check app logs
kubectl logs app-pod
# [ERROR] Failed to bind to port 8080: address already in use
# ← App started but errored internally

# Step 2: Exec into the pod
kubectl exec -it app-pod -- sh
# Inside:
curl localhost:8080  # Test from inside
netstat -tlnp        # Check listening ports
ps aux               # Verify process is running

# Step 3: Check readiness probe
kubectl describe pod app-pod | grep -A5 Readiness
# Readiness probe failed: connection refused
# READY shows 0/1 → Service won't send traffic

# Step 4: Check liveness probe
kubectl describe pod app-pod | grep -A5 Liveness
# If liveness fails → pod gets restarted

# Step 5: Check environment variables
kubectl exec app-pod -- env | sort
# Missing DB_HOST? Wrong PORT?

# Step 6: Check if service endpoints are populated
kubectl get endpoints service-name
# If empty → selector doesn't match pod labels

# Fix common issues:
# Wrong port in probe → kubectl edit pod
# Missing env var → fix ConfigMap/Secret
# App crash silent → check app-level config
Explanation

Running ≠ working. A pod can be Running but the app inside may be broken. On the CKA: check logs first (app-level errors), then exec in to test connectivity, then check probes (readiness/liveness), then verify environment variables. If READY shows 0/1, the readiness probe is failing — the Service won't send traffic to this pod. Common CKA scenario: probe targets wrong port or path.

Q8
How do you find all non-running pods across all namespaces?
Show Answer
Answer
# Method 1: Field selector (fastest)
kubectl get pods -A --field-selector=status.phase!=Running
# NAMESPACE   NAME          READY  STATUS            RESTARTS
# default     broken-pod    0/1    CrashLoopBackOff  8
# kube-system missing-img   0/1    ImagePullBackOff  0
# prod        pending-pod   0/1    Pending           0

# Method 2: Include Succeeded (completed jobs)
kubectl get pods -A --field-selector=status.phase!=Running,status.phase!=Succeeded

# Method 3: Custom columns for more detail
kubectl get pods -A --field-selector=status.phase!=Running \
  -o custom-columns=\
'NS:.metadata.namespace,NAME:.metadata.name,STATUS:.status.phase,NODE:.spec.nodeName'

# Method 4: Sort by restart count (find crashers)
kubectl get pods -A --sort-by='.status.containerStatuses[0].restartCount'

# Method 5: Events for all failing pods
kubectl get events -A --field-selector type=Warning \
  --sort-by=.lastTimestamp | head -20

# Quick health check:
kubectl get pods -A | grep -v Running | grep -v Completed
Explanation

--field-selector=status.phase!=Running finds all non-running pods instantly. On the CKA, start troubleshooting by finding what's broken. -A for all namespaces. Sort by restart count to find pods that keep crashing. The events command with type=Warning shows what's going wrong cluster-wide. Quick grep: | grep -v Running | grep -v Completed filters to only problem pods.

Q9
How do you check why a container exited with a specific exit code?
Show Answer
Answer
Exit Code Meaning Common Cause Fix
0 Success Process completed normally Expected for Jobs/init containers
1 Generic error App crash, missing config Check logs
126 Cannot execute Permission denied Check command/entrypoint
127 Command not found Wrong command in spec Fix command/args
137 SIGKILL (128+9) OOMKilled or manual kill Increase memory limits
139 SIGSEGV (128+11) Segmentation fault Fix app bug
143 SIGTERM (128+15) Graceful termination Normal during rollouts 
# Get exit code:
kubectl describe pod crash-pod | grep "Exit Code"
#   Exit Code:    137

# OR via jsonpath:
kubectl get pod crash-pod -o jsonpath=\
'{.status.containerStatuses[0].lastState.terminated.exitCode}'
# 137
Explanation

Exit codes above 128 mean the process was killed by a signal (exit code = 128 + signal number). On the CKA: 137 = OOMKilled (SIGKILL), 143 = graceful shutdown (SIGTERM), 1 = app error. Use kubectl describe or jsonpath to get the exit code. Combined with --previous logs, the exit code tells you exactly what happened and guides the fix.

Q10
A pod's init container is failing. How do you diagnose and fix it?
Show Answer
Answer
# Identify: Pod status shows Init:Error or Init:CrashLoopBackOff
kubectl get pod init-pod
# NAME       READY  STATUS         RESTARTS  AGE
# init-pod   0/1    Init:Error     3         2m

# Step 1: Find init container name
kubectl describe pod init-pod | grep -A10 "Init Containers"
# Init Containers:
#   wait-for-db:
#     State:    Terminated
#     Reason:   Error
#     Exit Code: 1

# Step 2: Check init container logs
kubectl logs init-pod -c wait-for-db
# Connection refused: db-service:5432
# ← The database isn't ready yet

# Step 3: Common init container issues:
# - Waiting for a service that doesn't exist
# - Wrong service name or port
# - DNS not resolving (service in different namespace)
# - Missing ConfigMap/Secret referenced by init container

# Step 4: Fix
# Check if the dependency service exists:
kubectl get svc db-service
# If missing: create it
# If wrong namespace: use FQDN (db-service.other-ns.svc.cluster.local)

# Step 5: Verify init container succeeds
kubectl get pod init-pod -w
# Init:0/1 → PodInitializing → Running ✓

# Remember: Init containers run SEQUENTIALLY
# If init-1 fails, init-2 never starts
# Main containers don't start until ALL inits succeed
Explanation

Init containers run before main containers and must complete successfully. Status shows Init:Error or Init:CrashLoopBackOff. Use -c init-container-name to get init container logs. On the CKA, init containers typically wait for a dependency (database, config). If the dependency doesn't exist, the init container loops. Fix the dependency → init succeeds → main containers start. Init containers run sequentially — if one fails, the rest don't execute.

Chapter 32: Troubleshooting Nodes and Cluster Components

Node Not Ready diagnosis, kubelet troubleshooting, control plane component recovery, and networking issues. These skills are critical for the CKA exam.

🔴 Advanced ⏱️ ~2 hours 🔧 Troubleshoot 📝 CKA EXAM

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ Diagnosing and recovering Node NotReady conditions
  • ✅ kubelet troubleshooting — service status, logs, configuration
  • ✅ Control plane component recovery — API server, scheduler, controller-manager, etcd
  • ✅ Network troubleshooting — DNS, Services, kube-proxy, CNI plugins
🎓 CKA Relevance
Troubleshooting 30% Cluster Architecture 25%

32.1 Node Not Ready — Diagnosis

🎯 CKA EXAM TOPIC

A node in NotReady state means the kubelet has stopped reporting to the API server. Your job is to find out why and fix it.

Common NotReady Causes

🛑
kubelet Stopped

The most common cause. kubelet crashed or was stopped. Fix: systemctl start kubelet and systemctl enable kubelet.

🔌
CNI Plugin Missing

No network plugin installed or CNI binary missing. Pods can't get IPs. Fix: Install/reinstall the CNI plugin (Calico, Flannel, etc.).

💾
Swap Enabled

kubelet refuses to start if swap is on (default). Fix: swapoff -a and remove swap entry from /etc/fstab.

📦
containerd Down

Container runtime crashed. No containers can be created. Fix: systemctl restart containerd.

Systematic Node NotReady Fix

1
SSH to Node

ssh worker01 — You can't fix node issues remotely via kubectl. You must be on the node.

2
Check kubelet

systemctl status kubelet — Is it active? If dead: systemctl start kubelet.

3
Read Logs

journalctl -u kubelet --no-pager | tail -50 — Error messages reveal the root cause.

4
Fix & Restart

Fix the underlying issue (config, certs, swap, containerd) then systemctl restart kubelet.

5
Verify

kubectl get nodes — Wait for node to transition from NotReady to Ready (may take 30-60s).

🔍 Node Troubleshooting Flowchart

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  $ kubectl get nodes                                                         │
│  NAME           STATUS     ROLES           AGE   VERSION                     │
│  controlplane   Ready      control-plane   30d   v1.31.0                    │
│  worker01       NotReady   <none>          30d   v1.31.0  ◄── PROBLEM      │
│       │                                                                      │
│       ▼                                                                      │
│  $ kubectl describe node worker01                                            │
│       │                                                                      │
│       ├── Conditions Section:                                                │
│       │   ┌──────────────────┬────────┬──────────────────────────────┐       │
│       │   │ Condition        │ Status │ What It Means                │       │
│       │   ├──────────────────┼────────┼──────────────────────────────┤       │
│       │   │ Ready            │ False  │ kubelet not reporting        │       │
│       │   │ MemoryPressure   │ True   │ Node running out of RAM     │       │
│       │   │ DiskPressure     │ True   │ Node running out of disk    │       │
│       │   │ PIDPressure      │ True   │ Too many processes          │       │
│       │   │ NetworkUnavail.  │ True   │ CNI plugin not working      │       │
│       │   └──────────────────┴────────┴──────────────────────────────┘       │
│       │                                                                      │
│       ▼                                                                      │
│  SSH into the node: $ ssh worker01                                           │
│       │                                                                      │
│       ├── $ systemctl status kubelet                                         │
│       │    ├── active (running) → kubelet is up, check network              │
│       │    └── inactive (dead)  → kubelet crashed or was stopped            │
│       │                                                                      │
│       ├── $ journalctl -u kubelet --no-pager | tail -50                      │
│       │    └── Look for error messages about certificates, config, etc.     │
│       │                                                                      │
│       └── Common Fixes:                                                      │
│            ├── Kubelet stopped   → systemctl start kubelet                   │
│            ├── Kubelet disabled  → systemctl enable --now kubelet            │
│            ├── Container runtime → systemctl restart containerd              │
│            ├── Swap enabled      → swapoff -a                                │
│            └── Wrong config      → check /var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml       │
│                                                                              │
│  VERIFY: $ kubectl get nodes   → worker01 should show Ready                 │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
⚠️ Node Conditions vs Node Status

Status (Ready/NotReady) is the overall health. Conditions (MemoryPressure, DiskPressure, etc.) tell you why a node is unhealthy. A node is Ready only when ALL pressure conditions are False and kubelet is communicating with the API server.

Node Diagnosis Commands 
# Step 1: Get node status overview
kubectl get nodes -o wide

# Step 2: Describe the problematic node
kubectl describe node worker01

# Key areas to check in the output:
# - Conditions     → Ready, MemoryPressure, DiskPressure
# - Capacity       → Total CPU, memory, pods
# - Allocatable    → Available for pods
# - Allocated      → Currently used by pods
# - Events         → Recent node events

# Step 3: SSH to node and check kubelet
ssh worker01
systemctl status kubelet
journalctl -u kubelet --no-pager -l | tail -50

# Step 4: Check container runtime
systemctl status containerd
crictl ps -a                    # List all containers on the node

# Step 5: Check disk and memory
df -h                           # Disk usage
free -m                         # Memory usage
top                             # Process and CPU overview

32.2 Troubleshooting kubelet

🎯 CKA EXAM TOPIC

kubelet is the only component that runs as a systemd service (not a static pod). It's the node agent — if kubelet is down, the node goes NotReady.

kubelet — The Node Agent Architecture

┌──────────────────────────── Worker Node ─────────────────────────────────┐
│                                                                          │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐        │
│  │                    kubelet (systemd service)                 │        │
│  │                                                             │        │
│  │  📡 Reports to API Server every 10s (node heartbeat)        │        │
│  │  📋 Watches for pod assignments from Scheduler              │        │
│  │  🔄 Manages pod lifecycle via Container Runtime             │        │
│  │  💉 Runs health probes (liveness, readiness, startup)       │        │
│  │  📊 Collects metrics for Metrics Server                     │        │
│  │                                                             │        │
│  │  Config: /var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml                       │        │
│  │  Certs:  /var/lib/kubelet/pki/                              │        │
│  │  Logs:   journalctl -u kubelet                              │        │
│  └────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────┘        │
│                           │                                              │
│                           ▼                                              │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐                       │
│  │         Container Runtime (containerd)        │                       │
│  │                                              │                       │
│  │  ┌─────┐  ┌─────┐  ┌─────┐  ┌─────┐        │                       │
│  │  │Pod A│  │Pod B│  │Pod C│  │Pod D│        │                       │
│  │  └─────┘  └─────┘  └─────┘  └─────┘        │                       │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────┘                       │
│                                                                          │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Common kubelet Issues and Fixes

Problem Symptom Diagnosis Fix
kubelet stopped Node goes NotReady systemctl status kubelet → inactive systemctl start kubelet
kubelet disabled Doesn't start after reboot systemctl is-enabled kubelet → disabled systemctl enable kubelet
Wrong config kubelet crashes repeatedly journalctl -u kubelet → config errors Fix /var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml
Certificate expired "x509: certificate has expired" openssl x509 -in cert -noout -dates kubeadm certs renew all
Cannot reach API server "connection refused" in kubelet logs Check network, firewall, API server IP Fix network/firewall or API server address in kubelet config
Swap enabled kubelet refuses to start free -m shows swap swapoff -a and remove from /etc/fstab
kubelet Troubleshooting — Step by Step 
# ═══ On the problematic node (SSH in first) ═══

# 1. Check kubelet status
systemctl status kubelet
# Look for: active (running) vs inactive (dead) vs failed

# 2. Check kubelet logs for errors
journalctl -u kubelet --no-pager | tail -50
journalctl -u kubelet -f                    # Follow live

# 3. If kubelet is stopped, start it
systemctl start kubelet
systemctl enable kubelet                    # Persist across reboots

# 4. Check kubelet configuration
cat /var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml
# Verify: clusterDNS, clusterDomain, authentication/authorization

# 5. Check kubelet certificate
openssl x509 -in /var/lib/kubelet/pki/kubelet-client-current.pem \
  -noout -text | grep -A2 "Validity"

# 6. Restart kubelet after any config change
systemctl daemon-reload                     # If service file changed
systemctl restart kubelet

# 7. Verify from control plane
kubectl get nodes                           # Should show Ready

⚠️ kubelet Gotchas on the CKA Exam

  • ✗ Wrong Looking for kubelet in kubectl get pods -n kube-system — kubelet is NOT a pod
  • ✓ Right Check kubelet via systemctl status kubelet on the node itself
  • ✗ Wrong Forgetting systemctl daemon-reload after editing service files
  • ✓ Right Always run daemon-reload before restart if you modified a systemd unit
  • ✗ Wrong Restarting kubelet without checking WHY it crashed
  • ✓ Right Read journalctl -u kubelet first to understand the root cause

32.3 Troubleshooting Control Plane Components

🎯 CKA EXAM TOPIC

Control plane components run as static pods managed by kubelet. Their manifests live in /etc/kubernetes/manifests/. If a component is broken, check its manifest and logs.

Control Plane Components — Where They Live

┌──────────────────────── Control Plane Node ──────────────────────────────┐
│                                                                          │
│  /etc/kubernetes/manifests/           ← kubelet watches this directory   │
│  ├── kube-apiserver.yaml              ← API Server static pod           │
│  ├── kube-controller-manager.yaml     ← Controller Manager static pod   │
│  ├── kube-scheduler.yaml              ← Scheduler static pod            │
│  └── etcd.yaml                        ← etcd static pod                 │
│                                                                          │
│  How static pods work:                                                   │
│  ┌──────────┐    watches    ┌────────────────────┐                      │
│  │ kubelet  │ ──────────▶  │ /etc/kubernetes/   │                      │
│  │          │    dir for    │    manifests/      │                      │
│  │          │    changes    │                    │                      │
│  └────┬─────┘               └────────────────────┘                      │
│       │ creates/restarts                                                 │
│       ▼                                                                  │
│  ┌──────────┐ ┌──────────┐ ┌──────────┐ ┌──────────┐                   │
│  │API Server│ │Controller│ │Scheduler │ │  etcd   │                   │
│  │   Pod    │ │Mgr Pod   │ │   Pod    │ │   Pod   │                   │
│  └──────────┘ └──────────┘ └──────────┘ └──────────┘                   │
│                                                                          │
│  ⚠️ If kubelet is down, static pods don't restart!                      │
│  ⚠️ If manifest has syntax error, static pod won't start!              │
│                                                                          │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Control Plane Troubleshooting Commands 
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# 1. CHECK — Are control plane pods running?
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
kubectl get pods -n kube-system
# Expected: kube-apiserver, kube-scheduler, kube-controller-manager, etcd

# 2. CHECK — Control plane pod logs
kubectl logs kube-apiserver-controlplane -n kube-system
kubectl logs kube-scheduler-controlplane -n kube-system
kubectl logs kube-controller-manager-controlplane -n kube-system
kubectl logs etcd-controlplane -n kube-system

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# 3. IF API SERVER IS DOWN — kubectl won't work!
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Use container runtime directly:
crictl ps -a                               # List all containers
crictl ps -a | grep kube-apiserver         # Find API server container
crictl logs <container-id>                 # Read its logs

# 4. CHECK — Static pod manifests
cat /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml
cat /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-scheduler.yaml
cat /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-controller-manager.yaml

# 5. FIX — Edit manifest, kubelet auto-restarts the pod
vi /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-scheduler.yaml
# kubelet detects change → restarts the static pod automatically

# 6. VERIFY
kubectl get pods -n kube-system
kubectl get componentstatuses             # Quick cluster health check
💡 CKA Pro Tip: When API Server Is Down

If the API server won't start, kubectl doesn't work at all. You must use crictl (the container runtime CLI) to check container status and logs directly. This is a common CKA exam scenario — they break the API server manifest and you need to fix it.

✅ When kubectl Works
  • kubectl get pods -n kube-system
  • kubectl logs <pod> -n kube-system
  • kubectl describe node <name>
  • kubectl get events --sort-by='.lastTimestamp'
🚨 When kubectl Is Dead
  • crictl ps -a | grep kube
  • crictl logs <container-id>
  • journalctl -u kubelet --no-pager
  • cat /etc/kubernetes/manifests/*.yaml

🏋️ Practice: Break and Fix the Control Plane

1
Break the scheduler: sudo sed -i 's/--leader-elect=true/--leader-elect=BROKEN/' /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-scheduler.yaml

Watch: kubectl get pods -n kube-system -w — scheduler pod will restart/crash

2
Diagnose: kubectl logs kube-scheduler-controlplane -n kube-system

Find the error message pointing to the invalid flag

3
Fix: Edit the manifest back to --leader-elect=true

kubelet detects the change and restarts the scheduler automatically

32.4 Troubleshooting Networking

🎯 CKA EXAM TOPIC

Network problems manifest as pods that can't reach services, DNS failures, or external traffic not arriving. Systematic diagnosis is key.

Network Troubleshooting — Layer by Layer

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  NETWORKING TROUBLESHOOTING LAYERS                                           │
│                                                                              │
│  Layer 1: POD-TO-POD                                                         │
│  ┌──────────┐              ┌──────────┐                                     │
│  │  Pod A   │──── CNI ────▶│  Pod B   │                                     │
│  │ 10.244.1 │   network    │ 10.244.2 │                                     │
│  └──────────┘              └──────────┘                                     │
│  Debug: kubectl exec podA -- ping <podB-IP>                                 │
│  Fix:   Check CNI plugin pods (Calico/Weave/Flannel) running in kube-system │
│                                                                              │
│  Layer 2: POD-TO-SERVICE                                                     │
│  ┌──────────┐  ┌───────────────┐  ┌──────────┐                             │
│  │  Pod A   │─▶│  Service      │─▶│  Pod B   │                             │
│  │          │  │  (ClusterIP)  │  │(endpoint)│                             │
│  └──────────┘  └───────────────┘  └──────────┘                             │
│  Debug: kubectl get endpoints <svc>   ← Are there endpoints?               │
│  Fix:   Service selector must match pod labels                               │
│                                                                              │
│  Layer 3: DNS RESOLUTION                                                     │
│  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────────┐                              │
│  │  Pod A   │─▶│ CoreDNS  │─▶│ Service IP   │                              │
│  │nslookup  │  │(kube-dns)│  │  resolved    │                              │
│  └──────────┘  └──────────┘  └──────────────┘                              │
│  Debug: kubectl exec pod -- nslookup <svc-name>                             │
│  Fix:   Check CoreDNS pods, ConfigMap, and kube-dns Service                  │
│                                                                              │
│  Layer 4: EXTERNAL ACCESS                                                    │
│  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────┐                   │
│  │ External │─▶│ NodePort │─▶│ Service  │─▶│  Pod     │                   │
│  │  Client  │  │  :30080  │  │ ClusterIP│  │(backend) │                   │
│  └──────────┘  └──────────┘  └──────────┘  └──────────┘                   │
│  Debug: curl http://<node-ip>:<nodeport>                                    │
│  Fix:   Check firewall rules, kube-proxy, service type                      │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Network Debugging Commands 
# ═══ SERVICE NOT RESPONDING ═══
# Step 1: Check endpoints — are pods registered?
kubectl get endpoints <service-name>
# If <none>: service selector doesn't match any pod labels!

# Step 2: Compare service selector with pod labels
kubectl get svc <service-name> -o yaml | grep -A5 selector
kubectl get pods --show-labels

# ═══ DNS NOT WORKING ═══
# Step 1: Test DNS from inside a pod
kubectl run dnstest --image=busybox --rm -it --restart=Never -- \
  nslookup kubernetes.default

# Step 2: Check CoreDNS pods
kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns
kubectl logs -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns

# Step 3: Check CoreDNS ConfigMap for typos
kubectl get cm coredns -n kube-system -o yaml

# Step 4: Check kube-dns Service ClusterIP
kubectl get svc kube-dns -n kube-system
# This IP should match /etc/resolv.conf inside pods

# ═══ CNI PLUGIN ISSUES ═══
# Check CNI plugin pods (Calico, Weave, Flannel, etc.)
kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep -i "calico\|weave\|flannel\|cilium"
kubectl get ds -n kube-system            # CNI typically runs as DaemonSet

# ═══ KUBE-PROXY ISSUES ═══
kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep kube-proxy
kubectl logs -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-proxy
# kube-proxy manages iptables rules for Service → Pod routing

✅ Verify Your Node & Cluster Troubleshooting Skills

  • Can you check a node's kubelet status? → ssh node; systemctl status kubelet
  • Can you view static pod manifests? → ls /etc/kubernetes/manifests/
  • Can you debug when API server is down? → crictl ps -a; crictl logs <id>
  • Can you test DNS from a pod? → kubectl exec pod -- nslookup svc-name
  • Can you check if service has endpoints? → kubectl get endpoints svc-name

kube-proxy Modes — How Service Routing Works

Mode How It Works Performance Check Command
iptables (default) Creates iptables rules for each Service → Pod mapping. Kernel-level packet routing. Good — random pod selection. Scales to ~5K services. iptables -t nat -L -n | grep <service-clusterip>
ipvs Uses IPVS kernel module with hash tables. More efficient routing algorithms. Better — supports round-robin, least-conn, etc. Scales to 10K+ services. ipvsadm -Ln
nftables (v1.31+) Successor to iptables using nftables kernel subsystem. More efficient rule processing. Best — modern kernel-level routing with better rule optimization. nft list ruleset
💡 Diagnosing kube-proxy Issues

If services exist but traffic doesn't reach pods (and endpoints ARE populated), the problem may be kube-proxy. Check: kubectl logs -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-proxy. If kube-proxy is down, iptables rules become stale and services stop routing to new pods.

⚠️ CKA Gotcha: Node & Cluster Component Traps

  • Node NotReady top causes — (1) kubelet not running: systemctl status kubelet. (2) kubelet config error: journalctl -u kubelet -f. (3) CNI plugin missing: ls /etc/cni/net.d/. (4) Container runtime down: systemctl status containerd
  • Static pod manifests location — /etc/kubernetes/manifests/. If kube-apiserver.yaml has a typo, the API server crashes. Fix the YAML, kubelet auto-restarts the pod. The exam loves this pattern
  • crictl not docker — Modern clusters use containerd. Debug with crictl ps, crictl logs, crictl inspect. On the CKA exam, docker CLI is NOT available
  • kubelet runs as systemd service — systemctl restart kubelet to restart. journalctl -u kubelet --no-pager -l for full logs. Config file at /var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml
  • Control plane component logs — kubectl logs -n kube-system kube-apiserver-<node> for API server. Same pattern for controller-manager, scheduler. If API server is down, use crictl logs <container-id>

CKA Speed Tip — Node NotReady systematic fix:
1. SSH into the node
2. systemctl status kubelet — is it running?
3. journalctl -u kubelet -f — what's the error?
4. Common fixes: systemctl enable --now kubelet, fix /var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml, reinstall CNI
5. systemctl restart kubelet after any fix

⚡ Node & component debug commands: kubectl get nodes -o wide — node status + versions + IPs
kubectl describe node <name> — conditions, capacity, taints, events
ssh <node> journalctl -u kubelet --since "5 min ago" — recent kubelet logs
ssh <node> crictl ps -a — all containers including exited ones
kubectl get componentstatuses — (deprecated but still works) quick health check
📝 Chapter 32 Practice Drill

Timed challenge (target: 6 minutes):

  • 1. SSH to a worker node and stop kubelet: systemctl stop kubelet
  • 2. From control plane: kubectl get nodes — observe NotReady status (may take ~40s)
  • 3. Diagnose: ssh worker journalctl -u kubelet
  • 4. Fix: ssh worker systemctl start kubelet
  • 5. Verify: kubectl get nodes — node returns to Ready

📝 Chapter 32 — CKA Practice Questions

Q1
A node shows NotReady. Walk through the troubleshooting process.
Show Answer
Answer
# Step 1: Identify which node is NotReady
kubectl get nodes
# NAME       STATUS     ROLES    AGE  VERSION
# worker-2   NotReady   <none>   30d  v1.31.0

# Step 2: Describe the node for conditions
kubectl describe node worker-2 | grep -A20 Conditions
# MemoryPressure    False
# DiskPressure      False
# PIDPressure       False
# Ready             False  ← kubelet is NOT reporting
#   Last Heartbeat: 5 minutes ago

# Step 3: SSH to the node
ssh worker-2

# Step 4: Check kubelet status
systemctl status kubelet
# Active: inactive (dead)  ← Kubelet is stopped!

# Step 5: Check kubelet logs for errors
journalctl -u kubelet -n 50 --no-pager
# "failed to load kubelet config file"
# OR "unable to connect to API server"

# Step 6: Common fixes:
# kubelet stopped → systemctl start kubelet
# kubelet crashing → check config: /var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml
# cert expired → check /var/lib/kubelet/pki/
# wrong API server → check /etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf

# Step 7: Start and enable kubelet
systemctl start kubelet
systemctl enable kubelet

# Step 8: Verify from control plane
kubectl get nodes
# worker-2   Ready   <none>   30d  v1.31.0 ✓
Explanation

NotReady = kubelet stopped sending heartbeats. On the CKA: SSH to the node, check systemctl status kubelet, check journalctl -u kubelet for errors, fix the issue, restart kubelet. The most common CKA scenario: kubelet is stopped or has a config error. Always check the config file path and API server address. After fixing, verify with kubectl get nodes.

Q2
The kubelet is running but keeps restarting. How do you diagnose the config issue?
Show Answer
Answer
# Step 1: Check kubelet status
systemctl status kubelet
# Active: activating (auto-restart)
# ← kubelet keeps crashing and systemd restarts it

# Step 2: Check journal logs
journalctl -u kubelet -n 100 --no-pager
# Look for error messages:

# Error A: "failed to load kubelet config file"
cat /var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml
# Check YAML syntax, missing fields

# Error B: "unable to load client CA file"
ls -la /etc/kubernetes/pki/ca.crt
# Check if cert file exists and is readable

# Error C: "server.key: no such file or directory"
# Wrong cert/key path in kubelet config

# Step 3: Check kubelet systemd unit
cat /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10-kubeadm.conf
# Look for wrong flags or paths

# Step 4: Verify kubelet config
cat /var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml
# Common issues:
# - Wrong clusterDNS IP
# - Wrong clusterDomain
# - Invalid YAML syntax

# Step 5: Fix and restart
vi /var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml  # Fix the issue
systemctl daemon-reload
systemctl restart kubelet

# Step 6: Verify
systemctl status kubelet  # Should show "active (running)"
kubectl get nodes          # Node should be Ready
Explanation

When kubelet keeps restarting (activating/auto-restart), it's usually a configuration problem. On the CKA, check journalctl -u kubelet for the specific error message. Common causes: broken kubelet config YAML, missing/wrong cert paths, wrong systemd flags. Fix the config file, daemon-reload, restart kubelet. Always do daemon-reload if you changed the systemd unit file.

Q3
A static pod isn't starting on a node. How do you fix it?
Show Answer
Answer
# Step 1: Find static pod manifest directory
# Check kubelet config:
cat /var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml | grep staticPodPath
# staticPodPath: /etc/kubernetes/manifests

# OR check kubelet flags:
ps aux | grep kubelet | grep -- --pod-manifest-path

# Step 2: Check the manifest exists
ls -la /etc/kubernetes/manifests/
# etcd.yaml  kube-apiserver.yaml  kube-controller-manager.yaml
# kube-scheduler.yaml  my-static-pod.yaml

# Step 3: Validate the manifest YAML
cat /etc/kubernetes/manifests/my-static-pod.yaml

# Step 4: Check for syntax errors
# Common issues:
# - Wrong indentation
# - Missing required fields (apiVersion, kind, spec)
# - Wrong image name
# - Invalid port numbers

# Step 5: Check kubelet logs for static pod errors
journalctl -u kubelet | grep "my-static-pod"
# "failed to parse static pod manifest: ..."

# Step 6: Fix the manifest
vi /etc/kubernetes/manifests/my-static-pod.yaml
# Kubelet auto-detects changes — no restart needed!

# Step 7: Verify
kubectl get pods -A | grep my-static-pod
# my-static-pod-worker-1   1/1  Running  0  10s

# Remember: Static pod names have node name appended
# Static pods are managed by kubelet, NOT the scheduler
Explanation

Static pods are managed directly by the kubelet (not the API server). They read manifests from staticPodPath (default: /etc/kubernetes/manifests/). On the CKA: if a static pod isn't working, check the YAML in the manifest directory. Kubelet watches the directory and auto-restarts when manifests change — no need to restart kubelet. Static pod names have the node name appended (e.g., etcd-master). Control plane components (etcd, apiserver, scheduler, controller-manager) are static pods.

Q4
How do you use crictl to troubleshoot container runtime issues on a node?
Show Answer
Answer
# crictl = CRI Container Runtime Interface tool
# Works with containerd, CRI-O (not Docker)

# SSH to the node first
ssh worker-1

# List running containers (like docker ps)
crictl ps
# CONTAINER ID  IMAGE     CREATED    STATE    NAME     POD ID
# a1b2c3d4e5    nginx     5min ago   Running  nginx    f6g7h8

# List ALL containers (including stopped)
crictl ps -a

# Get container logs
crictl logs a1b2c3d4e5

# Inspect container details
crictl inspect a1b2c3d4e5

# List pods on this node
crictl pods
# POD ID    STATE  NAME         NAMESPACE  AGE
# f6g7h8    Ready  web-pod      default    30m

# List images
crictl images

# Check container runtime socket
crictl info
# If error: "connect: no such file or directory"
# → Container runtime is down!

# Fix container runtime:
systemctl status containerd  # check status
systemctl restart containerd  # restart if needed
systemctl restart kubelet     # restart kubelet after

# Pull an image manually (for debugging)
crictl pull nginx:1.25
Explanation

crictl interacts with the container runtime directly on the node. On the CKA, use it when kubectl commands timeout (API server issue) or to debug container-level problems. Key commands: crictl ps (list containers), crictl logs (container logs), crictl pods (list pods on node). If crictl info fails, the container runtime is down. Restart containerd first, then kubelet.

Q5
How do you check control plane component health?
Show Answer
Answer
# Method 1: Check control plane pods in kube-system
kubectl get pods -n kube-system
# NAME                              READY  STATUS   RESTARTS
# etcd-master                       1/1    Running  0
# kube-apiserver-master             1/1    Running  0
# kube-controller-manager-master    1/1    Running  0
# kube-scheduler-master             1/1    Running  0
# coredns-5d78c9869d-abcde          1/1    Running  0
# kube-proxy-12345                  1/1    Running  0

# Method 2: Component status (deprecated but still works)
kubectl get componentstatuses
# NAME                 STATUS    MESSAGE
# scheduler            Healthy   ok
# controller-manager   Healthy   ok
