# etcd-0               Healthy   ok

# Method 3: Check API server directly
kubectl cluster-info
# Kubernetes control plane is running at https://10.0.0.1:6443
# CoreDNS is running at https://10.0.0.1:6443/api/v1/namespaces/...

# Method 4: Check logs of control plane components
kubectl logs kube-apiserver-master -n kube-system | tail -20
kubectl logs kube-scheduler-master -n kube-system | tail -20
kubectl logs kube-controller-manager-master -n kube-system | tail -20
kubectl logs etcd-master -n kube-system | tail -20

# Method 5: On the master node directly
crictl ps | grep kube
journalctl -u kubelet | grep -i error
Explanation

Control plane components run as static pods on the master node (in kube-system namespace). On the CKA: check if all 4 components are Running, check logs for errors, and use crictl on the master if kubectl doesn't work (API server down). Common issue: if API server is down, kubectl won't work — SSH to master and use crictl to check the container, then look at the static pod manifest in /etc/kubernetes/manifests/.

Q6
How do you troubleshoot CNI (Container Network Interface) issues?
Show Answer
Answer
# Symptom: Pods stuck in ContainerCreating
kubectl describe pod stuck-pod
# Warning  FailedCreatePodSandBox
# "network plugin is not ready: cni config uninitialized"

# Step 1: Check CNI plugin is installed
ls /etc/cni/net.d/
# Should contain config files like:
# 10-calico.conflist  OR  10-weave.conflist

# If empty → CNI plugin not installed!

# Step 2: Check CNI plugin pods
kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep -E "calico|weave|flannel"
# calico-node-abc12   0/1   CrashLoopBackOff  ← Problem!

# Step 3: Check CNI plugin logs
kubectl logs calico-node-abc12 -n kube-system

# Step 4: Check CNI binary
ls /opt/cni/bin/
# Should contain: calico, bandwidth, bridge, etc.

# Fix 1: Install CNI plugin (if missing)
kubectl apply -f https://docs.projectcalico.org/manifests/calico.yaml

# Fix 2: Restart CNI pods
kubectl delete pods -n kube-system -l k8s-app=calico-node
# DaemonSet recreates them

# Fix 3: Check node network
ssh worker-1
ip link show  # Network interfaces
ip route     # Routing table

# Verify: Pods should move from ContainerCreating to Running
kubectl get pods -w
Explanation

CNI provides pod networking. Without CNI, pods can't get IP addresses and stay in ContainerCreating. On the CKA: check /etc/cni/net.d/ for config files and /opt/cni/bin/ for binaries. If no CNI is installed, you need to apply the CNI plugin manifest. CNI plugins run as DaemonSets in kube-system. Common exam question: "pods stuck in ContainerCreating on a new node" = CNI not configured.

Q7
A node shows MemoryPressure or DiskPressure. What happens and how do you fix it?
Show Answer
Answer
# Check node conditions:
kubectl describe node worker-1 | grep -A20 Conditions
# Type              Status
# MemoryPressure    True    ← Node running out of memory!
# DiskPressure      True    ← Node running out of disk!
# PIDPressure       False
# Ready             True    (can be True with pressure)

# What happens automatically:
# MemoryPressure → kubelet starts evicting pods (BestEffort first)
# DiskPressure   → kubelet reclaims dead containers, unused images
# Both           → Node gets tainted: node.kubernetes.io/memory-pressure:NoSchedule

# Check memory on the node:
ssh worker-1
free -h
# total: 4Gi  used: 3.8Gi  available: 200Mi ← Very low!

# Check disk:
df -h /
# /dev/sda1  50G  48G  2G  96% /  ← 96% full!

# Fix MemoryPressure:
# 1. Find memory-hungry pods:
kubectl top pods -A --sort-by=memory | head -10
# 2. Delete or reduce limits on heavy consumers
# 3. Add memory to the node (if possible)

# Fix DiskPressure:
ssh worker-1
# Clean up container logs:
journalctl --vacuum-size=100M
# Remove unused images:
crictl rmi --prune
# Clear unused containers:
crictl rm $(crictl ps -a -q --state exited)

# Eviction thresholds (defaults):
# memory.available < 100Mi
# nodefs.available < 10%
# imagefs.available < 15%
Explanation

Node pressure conditions trigger automatic eviction. The kubelet evicts pods in order: BestEffort (no limits) first, then Burstable, then Guaranteed last. Pressure also adds a NoSchedule taint so new pods won't be scheduled. On the CKA: check with describe node, SSH to the node to check free -h and df -h, fix by removing unused resources or moving pods. The kubelet's eviction thresholds are configurable in kubelet config.yaml.

Q8
The kube-proxy DaemonSet isn't running on a node. How do you fix it?
Show Answer
Answer
# Symptom: Services don't work on one specific node
# Pods can ping other pods but Service ClusterIP fails

# Step 1: Check kube-proxy pods
kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-proxy -o wide
# NAME              READY  STATUS   NODE       
# kube-proxy-abc12  1/1    Running  worker-1
# kube-proxy-def34  0/1    Error    worker-2  ← Problem!

# Step 2: Check logs
kubectl logs kube-proxy-def34 -n kube-system
# "failed to load config: couldn't read file"

# Step 3: Check kube-proxy ConfigMap
kubectl get configmap kube-proxy -n kube-system -o yaml | head -30

# Step 4: Check kube-proxy DaemonSet
kubectl get ds kube-proxy -n kube-system
kubectl describe ds kube-proxy -n kube-system

# Step 5: Check iptables/ipvs rules on the node
ssh worker-2
iptables -L -t nat | grep -i kube
# If empty → kube-proxy isn't creating rules

# Fix: Delete the erroring pod (DaemonSet recreates)
kubectl delete pod kube-proxy-def34 -n kube-system

# If still broken, check kube-proxy mode:
kubectl get configmap kube-proxy -n kube-system -o yaml | grep mode
# mode: "iptables" (default) or "ipvs"

# Restart ALL kube-proxy pods:
kubectl rollout restart ds kube-proxy -n kube-system
Explanation

kube-proxy runs as a DaemonSet (one pod per node) and maintains network rules (iptables/ipvs) for Service routing. If kube-proxy is broken on a node, Services won't work on that node. On the CKA: check the kube-proxy pod logs, delete the broken pod (DaemonSet auto-recreates), or rollout restart the DaemonSet. Config comes from the kube-proxy ConfigMap in kube-system.

Q9
How do you troubleshoot a kubelet that won't start after a cluster upgrade?
Show Answer
Answer
# After kubeadm upgrade, kubelet won't start:
systemctl status kubelet
# Active: activating (auto-restart)

# Step 1: Check journal
journalctl -u kubelet -n 50 --no-pager
# Error messages to look for:

# A: "mismatched kubelet version"
kubelet --version
# v1.30.0 ← Not upgraded to v1.31!
# Fix: Upgrade kubelet package
apt-get install kubelet=1.31.0-1.1

# B: "failed to read kubelet config"
cat /var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml
# Config may need updating
kubeadm upgrade node  # Updates kubelet config

# C: "certificate has expired"
ls -la /var/lib/kubelet/pki/
openssl x509 -in /var/lib/kubelet/pki/kubelet-client-current.pem \
  -noout -dates
# Fix: kubeadm certs renew all (on master)

# Step 2: Full fix sequence
apt-get update
apt-get install -y kubelet=1.31.0-1.1 kubectl=1.31.0-1.1
kubeadm upgrade node  # Only on worker nodes
systemctl daemon-reload
systemctl restart kubelet

# Step 3: Verify
systemctl status kubelet  # active (running)
kubectl get nodes          # Node Ready, correct version
Explanation

Post-upgrade kubelet issues are common on the CKA. Key checks: (1) kubelet binary version matches the cluster version, (2) kubeadm upgrade node was run to update config, (3) daemon-reload was done before restart, (4) certificates are valid. The most common mistake is forgetting kubeadm upgrade node on workers or not doing daemon-reload. Always check journalctl first — the error message tells you exactly what's wrong.

Q10
How do you find which pods are running on a specific node and what resources they're using?
Show Answer
Answer
# Method 1: Field selector (recommended)
kubectl get pods -A --field-selector spec.nodeName=worker-1
# NAMESPACE     NAME              READY  STATUS
# default       web-abc12         1/1    Running
# kube-system   kube-proxy-xyz    1/1    Running
# kube-system   calico-node-123   1/1    Running

# Method 2: Using -o wide shows NODE column
kubectl get pods -A -o wide | grep worker-1

# Method 3: Resource usage on the node
kubectl top node worker-1
# NAME       CPU(cores)  CPU%  MEMORY(bytes)  MEMORY%
# worker-1   350m        17%   1200Mi         60%

# Method 4: Resource usage per pod on the node
kubectl top pods -A --sort-by=cpu --field-selector spec.nodeName=worker-1

# Method 5: Node capacity vs allocatable vs used
kubectl describe node worker-1 | grep -A10 "Allocated resources"
# Allocated resources:
#   CPU Requests  CPU Limits  Memory Requests  Memory Limits
#   750m (37%)    2 (100%)    1Gi (50%)        2Gi (100%)

# Method 6: All resource details
kubectl describe node worker-1 | grep -A5 "Capacity"
kubectl describe node worker-1 | grep -A5 "Allocatable"

# CKA shortcut: See what's running and how much room is left
kubectl describe node worker-1 | grep -A30 "Non-terminated"
Explanation

--field-selector spec.nodeName= filters pods by node. On the CKA, kubectl describe node shows Capacity, Allocatable, and Allocated Resources — this tells you total vs available vs used. kubectl top node shows real-time usage. Key CKA scenario: scheduling failure because node is at capacity — use describe node to see Allocated vs Allocatable resources to confirm.

Chapter 33: Logging and Monitoring

Application logging, cluster-level logging architecture, Metrics Server, and monitoring resource usage. Essential for debugging and capacity planning.

🔴 Advanced ⏱️ ~1 hour 📊 Monitoring 📝 CKA EXAM

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ Application logging with kubectl logs — all flags and patterns
  • ✅ Cluster-level logging architecture — node agents, sidecar containers
  • ✅ Metrics Server installation and resource monitoring
  • ✅ Monitoring resource usage — kubectl top, capacity planning
🎓 CKA Relevance
Troubleshooting 30%

33.1 Application Logging

🎯 CKA EXAM TOPIC

Containers write to stdout/stderr, and the container runtime captures these streams as log files on the node. kubectl logs retrieves this output.

kubectl logs — Debugging Flow

1
Basic Logs

kubectl logs <pod> — Start with current container output. If pod is running, this shows real-time stdout/stderr.

2
Previous Crash

kubectl logs <pod> --previous — If CrashLoopBackOff, this shows output from the last crashed container instance.

3
Specific Container

kubectl logs <pod> -c <container> — Multi-container pods require specifying which container. Use --all-containers for everything.

4
Label Selector

kubectl logs -l app=name --tail=50 — Check logs across ALL replicas of a Deployment at once.

📋 kubectl logs — Complete Reference

Master every flag for the CKA exam:

Command Purpose When to Use
kubectl logs <pod> Current container stdout/stderr First check for running pods
kubectl logs <pod> --previous Previous crashed container output CrashLoopBackOff debugging
kubectl logs <pod> -f Follow / stream live Watch realtime during testing
kubectl logs <pod> --tail=100 Last N lines only Quick check without scrolling
kubectl logs <pod> --since=1h Logs from last hour Recent errors only
kubectl logs <pod> -c <container> Specific container Multi-container pods (sidecars)
kubectl logs <pod> --all-containers All containers in a pod Sidecar/init container logs
kubectl logs -l app=name All pods matching label Deployments with multiple replicas
Application Logging Examples 
# Basic logs
kubectl logs anihpj-web-xxxx -n anihpj

# Previous crashed container ⭐ (CKA critical)
kubectl logs anihpj-web-xxxx -n anihpj --previous

# Follow live (useful during testing)
kubectl logs anihpj-web-xxxx -n anihpj -f

# Multi-container pod: specify the container
kubectl logs anihpj-web-xxxx -n anihpj -c django
kubectl logs anihpj-web-xxxx -n anihpj -c nginx-sidecar

# All containers at once
kubectl logs anihpj-web-xxxx -n anihpj --all-containers

# Logs from all pods with label app=anihpj
kubectl logs -l app=anihpj -n anihpj --tail=50

# Combine flags: last 100 lines from last hour, follow live
kubectl logs anihpj-web-xxxx --tail=100 --since=1h -f
📖 Where Do Container Logs Actually Live?

On the node, container logs are stored at:
/var/log/containers/<pod-name>_<namespace>_<container>-<id>.log
/var/log/pods/<namespace>_<pod-name>_<uid>/<container>/
These are symlinks managed by the container runtime. kubectl logs reads these files via kubelet.

33.2 Cluster-Level Logging

Node-level logging only keeps logs on individual nodes. For production, you need a centralized logging architecture — a DaemonSet log agent that ships logs to a central store.

The 3 Logging Patterns

📋
Node-Level Agent (Recommended)

DaemonSet runs 1 log agent per node (fluentd, filebeat). Agent reads /var/log/containers/ and ships to central store. Lightweight, no pod modification needed.

🔀
Sidecar Streaming

Sidecar container reads app log files and streams to its own stdout. Node agent then picks up the sidecar's output. Use when app writes to files instead of stdout.

📡
Sidecar with Agent

Sidecar runs its own logging agent (fluentd) that ships directly to the backend. More flexible but resource-heavy — each pod gets its own agent.

💡
CKA Focus

Know the architecture concepts and kubectl logs flags. The exam won't ask you to deploy EFK/Loki stacks — but may ask you to create a sidecar logging container.

Kubernetes Logging Architecture — From Container to Central Store

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                  KUBERNETES LOGGING ARCHITECTURE                             │
│                                                                              │
│  ┌──────────── Worker Node 1 ─────────────────────────────┐                 │
│  │                                                         │                 │
│  │  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────┐             │                 │
│  │  │  Pod A   │  │  Pod B   │  │  Pod C   │             │                 │
│  │  │ stdout→  │  │ stdout→  │  │ stdout→  │             │                 │
│  │  └────┬─────┘  └────┬─────┘  └────┬─────┘             │                 │
│  │       │              │              │                   │                 │
│  │       ▼              ▼              ▼                   │                 │
│  │  /var/log/containers/*.log                              │                 │
│  │  /var/log/pods/<namespace>_<pod>/                       │                 │
│  │       │                                                 │                 │
│  │       ▼                                                 │                 │
│  │  ┌─────────────────────────────┐                       │                 │
│  │  │ 📋 DaemonSet Log Agent     │   fluentd /            │                 │
│  │  │    (1 per node)             │   filebeat /           │                 │
│  │  │                             │   fluent-bit           │                 │
│  │  └──────────────┬──────────────┘                       │                 │
│  └─────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────┘                 │
│                    │                                                         │
│  ┌─────────────────┼── Worker Node 2 ──────────────────────┐                │
│  │  (same pattern) │                                        │                │
│  │  Pods → /var/log/ → DaemonSet Agent ───────────────┤    │                │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼────┘                │
│                                                        │                     │
│              ┌─────────────────────────────────────────▼──────────┐          │
│              │  🗄️  CENTRAL LOG STORE                              │          │
│              │                                                     │          │
│              │  Elasticsearch / Loki / CloudWatch / Splunk        │          │
│              │         │                                          │          │
│              │         ▼                                          │          │
│              │  📊 Kibana / Grafana   (visualization & alerts)   │          │
│              └────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘          │
│                                                                              │
│  THREE LOGGING PATTERNS:                                                     │
│  ┌──────────────────┬──────────────────┬──────────────────────┐             │
│  │ Node-level Agent │ Sidecar Streaming│ Sidecar with Agent   │             │
│  │ (DaemonSet)      │ (sidecar → stdout│ (sidecar ships logs) │             │
│  │ ✅ Recommended   │  → node agent)   │ ⚠️ Resource heavy   │             │
│  │ 1 agent per node │ Multi-format logs│ Direct to backend    │             │
│  └──────────────────┴──────────────────┴──────────────────────┘             │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
💡 CKA Scope: Logging

The CKA exam tests kubectl logs commands, not deploying full logging stacks. Know the architecture concepts (DaemonSet agent, node-level logs, sidecar patterns) but focus your practice on the kubectl logs flags in section 33.1.

Sidecar Logging Pattern — When Apps Don't Log to stdout

📖 Why Sidecar Logging?

Some applications write logs to files instead of stdout. Since kubectl logs only captures stdout/stderr, you need a sidecar container that reads the log file and streams it to its own stdout — making it visible via kubectl logs.

sidecar-logging.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-web
  namespace: anihpj
spec:
  containers:
  # Main application — writes logs to a file
  - name: django
    image: anihpj:v1
    volumeMounts:
    - name: log-volume
      mountPath: /var/log/django

  # Sidecar — streams the log file to stdout
  - name: log-streamer
    image: busybox:1.36
    args: ['sh', '-c', 'tail -F /var/log/django/app.log']
    volumeMounts:
    - name: log-volume
      mountPath: /var/log/django

  volumes:
  - name: log-volume
    emptyDir: {}

# Now you can read the app logs via:
# kubectl logs anihpj-web -c log-streamer

Container Log Rotation

Setting Default Purpose
containerLogMaxSize 10Mi Max size of a single log file before rotation
containerLogMaxFiles 5 Max number of rotated log files kept per container
💡 Log Rotation Config

These are set in the kubelet configuration (/var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml). Default: each container can use up to 50Mi of disk for logs (10Mi × 5 files). If a node has disk pressure, check if log rotation is properly configured.

33.3 Monitoring with Metrics Server

🎯 CKA EXAM TOPIC

Metrics Server is a lightweight, in-cluster component that collects CPU and memory usage from kubelet on each node. It powers kubectl top and is required for HPA (Horizontal Pod Autoscaler).

Metrics Server — How It Works

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                     METRICS SERVER ARCHITECTURE                          │
│                                                                          │
│  ┌──────────┐   ┌──────────┐   ┌──────────┐                            │
│  │ kubelet  │   │ kubelet  │   │ kubelet  │                            │
│  │ (node 1) │   │ (node 2) │   │ (node 3) │                            │
│  │          │   │          │   │          │                            │
│  │ cAdvisor │   │ cAdvisor │   │ cAdvisor │   ← built into kubelet     │
│  │ collects │   │ collects │   │ collects │     collects container      │
│  │ metrics  │   │ metrics  │   │ metrics  │     CPU/memory metrics     │
│  └────┬─────┘   └────┬─────┘   └────┬─────┘                            │
│       │              │              │                                    │
│       └──────────────┼──────────────┘                                    │
│                      ▼                                                   │
│            ┌──────────────────┐                                         │
│            │  Metrics Server  │   ← Deployment in kube-system           │
│            │  (kube-system)   │     Scrapes /metrics/resource from      │
│            │                  │     each kubelet every 15 seconds       │
│            └────────┬─────────┘                                         │
│                     │ exposes                                            │
│                     ▼                                                    │
│            ┌──────────────────┐                                         │
│            │ metrics.k8s.io   │   ← Kubernetes API extension            │
│            │  API endpoint    │                                         │
│            └────────┬─────────┘                                         │
│                     │                                                    │
│           ┌─────────┼──────────────┐                                    │
│           ▼                        ▼                                    │
│  ┌──────────────┐       ┌──────────────────┐                           │
│  │ kubectl top  │       │ HPA Controller   │                           │
│  │ pods / nodes │       │ (auto-scaling)   │                           │
│  └──────────────┘       └──────────────────┘                           │
│                                                                          │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Metrics Server Installation & Usage 
# ═══ INSTALL METRICS SERVER ═══
# Download and apply the official manifest
kubectl apply -f https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/metrics-server/releases/latest/download/components.yaml

# For local clusters (kubeadm, kind, minikube) — add insecure TLS flag:
# Edit the Deployment and add to args:
# --kubelet-insecure-tls
kubectl edit deployment metrics-server -n kube-system

# Verify metrics server is running
kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep metrics-server
kubectl get apiservices | grep metrics     # Should show v1beta1.metrics.k8s.io

# ═══ KUBECTL TOP — RESOURCE USAGE ═══
# Node-level metrics
kubectl top nodes                          # CPU and memory per node
kubectl top nodes --sort-by=cpu            # Sort by CPU usage

# Pod-level metrics
kubectl top pods                           # CPU and memory per pod
kubectl top pods -A                        # All namespaces
kubectl top pods --sort-by=memory          # Sort by memory usage
kubectl top pods --sort-by=cpu             # Sort by CPU usage
kubectl top pods -n kube-system            # Specific namespace

# Pod with containers breakdown
kubectl top pods --containers              # Per-container metrics

Interpreting Resource Metrics

Metric Unit Example What It Means
CPU millicores (m) 250m 25% of one CPU core
CPU cores 1 100% of one CPU core
Memory Mi (mebibytes) 128Mi ~134 MB of RAM
Memory Gi (gibibytes) 2Gi ~2.1 GB of RAM

⚠️ Metrics Server Gotchas

  • ✗ Wrong Expecting kubectl top to work without Metrics Server installed
  • ✓ Right Install Metrics Server first — it's not included by default in kubeadm clusters
  • ✗ Wrong Thinking Metrics Server stores historical data
  • ✓ Right Metrics Server provides current snapshot only — for history use Prometheus
  • ✗ Wrong Forgetting --kubelet-insecure-tls in lab environments
  • ✓ Right Add this flag in kubeadm/kind/minikube — otherwise Metrics Server can't scrape kubelet

33.4 Monitoring anihpj Resource Usage

Let's apply monitoring to our anihpj project — find resource-hungry pods, identify OOM risks, and set appropriate limits.

Monitoring anihpj — Finding Resource Issues 
# Which pods are using the most CPU?
kubectl top pods -n anihpj --sort-by=cpu
# NAME                          CPU(cores)   MEMORY(bytes)
# anihpj-web-7d4f8c5-abc12      250m         128Mi
# anihpj-db-0                   100m         512Mi     ← DB uses most memory
# anihpj-worker-xxxx            50m          64Mi

# Which pods are using the most memory?
kubectl top pods -n anihpj --sort-by=memory

# Per-container breakdown (useful for sidecar pods)
kubectl top pods -n anihpj --containers
# NAME                  CONTAINER   CPU    MEMORY
# anihpj-web-xxx        django      200m   100Mi
# anihpj-web-xxx        nginx       50m    28Mi

# Node-level — is any node under pressure?
kubectl top nodes
# NAME           CPU(cores)  CPU%   MEMORY(bytes)  MEMORY%
# controlplane   350m        17%    1200Mi         62%
# worker01       800m        40%    3200Mi         80%     ← ⚠️ High memory!

# Compare against resource requests/limits
kubectl describe pod anihpj-web-xxxx -n anihpj | grep -A8 "Requests\|Limits"

Resource Monitoring — Requests vs Limits vs Actual Usage

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  anihpj-web pod: CPU usage over time                                     │
│                                                                          │
│  CPU (millicores)                                                        │
│  500m ┤                                                                  │
│       │                                                                  │
│  400m ┤                              ╔═══════════════╗ ← LIMIT (400m)   │
│       │                        ┌─────╨───┐           ║                   │
│  300m ┤                   ┌────┘         └───┐       ║                   │
│       │              ┌────┘                  │       ║                   │
│  250m ┤─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─│─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─│─ ─ ─ ─║─ ← REQUEST (250m)│
│       │         ┌────┘                       └───┐   ║                   │
│  200m ┤    ┌────┘                                └───║──┐                │
│       │────┘                                         ║  └───             │
│  100m ┤                                              ║                   │
│       │                                              ║                   │
│    0m ┤──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────▶ Time  │
│       │                                                                  │
│  REQUEST = guaranteed minimum (scheduler uses this for placement)        │
│  LIMIT   = maximum allowed (container is throttled/killed above this)    │
│  ACTUAL  = the wavy line (what kubectl top shows)                        │
│                                                                          │
│  ⚠️ If ACTUAL exceeds MEMORY LIMIT → OOMKilled (exit 137)              │
│  ⚠️ If ACTUAL exceeds CPU LIMIT → Throttled (not killed)               │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

🏋️ Practice Drill: Monitoring Commands

1
Install Metrics Server and verify: kubectl top nodes returns data
2
Find the pod using the most CPU: kubectl top pods -A --sort-by=cpu | head -5
3
Find the node with highest memory usage: kubectl top nodes --sort-by=memory

⚠️ CKA Gotcha: Logging & Monitoring Traps

  • Metrics Server must be installed — kubectl top nodes and kubectl top pods fail without it. The exam cluster usually has it pre-installed, but verify first: kubectl get deployment metrics-server -n kube-system
  • kubectl top shows CURRENT usage, not limits — A pod using 450Mi doesn't mean its limit is 450Mi. Compare with kubectl describe pod to see limits vs actual usage. Exam may ask "which pod is closest to its memory limit"
  • Log rotation — Node-level logs at /var/log/containers/ are rotated by the container runtime. If a pod restarted 5 times, only the current + previous logs are available via kubectl logs
  • Sidecar logging pattern — App writes to a file on a shared emptyDir volume. A sidecar container tails that file to stdout. This makes logs accessible via kubectl logs <pod> -c sidecar
  • --sort-by flag — kubectl top pods --sort-by=memory or --sort-by=cpu. The exam may ask to find the pod consuming the most resources. Know these flags

CKA Speed Tip — Resource usage commands:
kubectl top nodes — CPU/memory per node
kubectl top pods -A --sort-by=cpu — highest CPU across all namespaces
kubectl top pods -A --sort-by=memory | head -5 — top 5 memory consumers
kubectl logs <pod> -f --tail=50 — follow last 50 lines (live tail)

⚡ Logging & monitoring commands: kubectl logs <pod> --all-containers --since=10m — all containers, last 10 min
kubectl logs -l app=web --max-log-requests=10 — logs from multiple pods by label
kubectl top pods -n <ns> --containers — per-container resource usage
kubectl get events -A --sort-by=.lastTimestamp — recent cluster events
kubectl logs <pod> -c <container> --previous — previous crashed container's logs

📝 Chapter 33 — CKA Practice Questions

Q1
How do you get the last 100 lines of logs from a pod and stream new logs in real time?
Show Answer
Answer
# Last 100 lines:
kubectl logs web-pod --tail=100

# Stream (follow) logs in real time:
kubectl logs web-pod -f

# Combine: last 100 lines THEN stream:
kubectl logs web-pod --tail=100 -f

# Logs since a specific time:
kubectl logs web-pod --since=1h     # Last 1 hour
kubectl logs web-pod --since=30m    # Last 30 minutes
kubectl logs web-pod --since=5s     # Last 5 seconds

# Logs since a timestamp:
kubectl logs web-pod --since-time="2024-01-15T10:00:00Z"

# Add timestamps to log lines:
kubectl logs web-pod --timestamps=true
# 2024-01-15T10:30:00Z  [INFO] Server started on port 8080

# Previous container logs (after crash/restart):
kubectl logs web-pod --previous

# Combine for exam speed:
kubectl logs web-pod --tail=50 --timestamps -f
Explanation

--tail=N shows the last N lines (avoids flooding). -f follows/streams new logs. --since accepts durations (1h, 30m) or timestamps. --timestamps prepends timestamps to each line. On the CKA, use --tail=50 -f to quickly see recent logs and watch for new errors. --previous gets logs from the previous container instance (crucial after crashes).

Q2
Create a sidecar container that reads the main application's log file and streams it to stdout.
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: app-with-sidecar
spec:
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: busybox
    command: ['sh', '-c',
      'while true; do echo "$(date) - Processing request" >> /var/log/app.log; sleep 5; done']
    volumeMounts:
    - name: log-volume
      mountPath: /var/log

  - name: log-sidecar
    image: busybox
    command: ['sh', '-c', 'tail -f /var/log/app.log']
    volumeMounts:
    - name: log-volume
      mountPath: /var/log

  volumes:
  - name: log-volume
    emptyDir: {}

# Now you can view app logs via kubectl:
# kubectl logs app-with-sidecar -c log-sidecar
# 2024-01-15 10:30:00 - Processing request
# 2024-01-15 10:30:05 - Processing request
Explanation

Sidecar logging pattern: the main app writes logs to a file, the sidecar reads that file with tail -f and outputs to stdout. Both containers share a volume (emptyDir). This makes file-based logs accessible via kubectl logs. On the CKA, you may need to add a sidecar container to stream logs from a file. Key: shared volume, tail -f in the sidecar, and matching mountPath.

Q3
How do you install Metrics Server and get resource usage with kubectl top?
Show Answer
Answer
# Check if metrics-server is installed:
kubectl top nodes
# Error: Metrics API not available
# ← Metrics Server is NOT installed

# Install Metrics Server:
kubectl apply -f https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/metrics-server/releases/latest/download/components.yaml

# For lab environments (self-signed certs), add --kubelet-insecure-tls:
kubectl patch deployment metrics-server -n kube-system \
  --type='json' -p='[{"op":"add","path":"/spec/template/spec/containers/0/args/-","value":"--kubelet-insecure-tls"}]'

# Wait for it to be ready (~60 seconds):
kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep metrics-server

# Node resource usage:
kubectl top nodes
# NAME       CPU(cores)  CPU%  MEMORY(bytes)  MEMORY%
# master     250m        12%   1500Mi         75%
# worker-1   150m        7%    800Mi          40%

# Pod resource usage:
kubectl top pods -A
kubectl top pods -n default

# Sort by CPU or memory:
kubectl top pods --sort-by=cpu
kubectl top pods --sort-by=memory

# Per-container metrics:
kubectl top pods --containers

# Specific namespace, sorted:
kubectl top pods -n production --sort-by=memory
Explanation

Metrics Server collects CPU/memory metrics from kubelets. kubectl top requires Metrics Server to be installed. On the CKA, Metrics Server is usually pre-installed, but you should know how to verify. --sort-by=cpu or --sort-by=memory sorts the output — useful for finding resource-hungry pods. --containers shows per-container breakdown. In lab environments, --kubelet-insecure-tls is often needed.

Q4
How do you find the pod consuming the most CPU in a specific namespace?
Show Answer
Answer
# Sort by CPU usage (highest first):
kubectl top pods -n production --sort-by=cpu
# NAME          CPU(cores)  MEMORY(bytes)
# data-proc     950m        2Gi          ← Highest CPU!
# web-server    200m        512Mi
# api-gateway   150m        256Mi
# cache         50m         128Mi

# Sort by memory:
kubectl top pods -n production --sort-by=memory
# NAME          CPU(cores)  MEMORY(bytes)
# data-proc     950m        2Gi          ← Also highest memory!
# web-server    200m        512Mi

# All namespaces, top CPU consumers:
kubectl top pods -A --sort-by=cpu | head -10

# Per-container breakdown:
kubectl top pods -n production --containers --sort-by=cpu
# POD          CONTAINER    CPU(cores)  MEMORY(bytes)
# data-proc    worker       800m        1.5Gi
# data-proc    sidecar      150m        512Mi

# Node-level: which node is busiest?
kubectl top nodes --sort-by=cpu

# CKA tip: Redirect output to a file
kubectl top pods -A --sort-by=cpu > /opt/heavy-cpu.txt
# Or extract just the pod name:
kubectl top pods -n production --sort-by=cpu \
  --no-headers | head -1 | awk '{print $1}' > /opt/answer.txt
# data-proc
Explanation

On the CKA, you'll be asked to find the highest resource consumer and write the answer to a file. kubectl top pods --sort-by=cpu sorts by CPU. Use --no-headers | head -1 | awk '{print $1}' to extract just the pod name. Common exam question: "find the pod using the most CPU in namespace X and write its name to /opt/answer.txt".

Q5
How do you view and filter Kubernetes events?
Show Answer
Answer
# All events in current namespace:
kubectl get events

# All events, sorted by time (most recent first):
kubectl get events --sort-by=.lastTimestamp

# All events across all namespaces:
kubectl get events -A --sort-by=.lastTimestamp

# Only Warning events (errors):
kubectl get events --field-selector type=Warning

# Events for a specific object:
kubectl get events --field-selector involvedObject.name=web-pod
kubectl get events --field-selector involvedObject.name=my-deploy

# Events by reason:
kubectl get events --field-selector reason=FailedScheduling
kubectl get events --field-selector reason=BackOff
kubectl get events --field-selector reason=Killing

# Watch events in real time:
kubectl get events -w

# Custom output:
kubectl get events --sort-by=.lastTimestamp \
  -o custom-columns=\
'TIME:.lastTimestamp,TYPE:.type,REASON:.reason,OBJECT:.involvedObject.name,MESSAGE:.message'

# Events in describe (per-object):
kubectl describe pod web-pod | tail -20
# Events section shows events specific to this pod

# Note: Events expire after ~1 hour by default
# (controlled by --event-ttl flag on kube-apiserver)
Explanation

Events record what's happening in the cluster. On the CKA: --sort-by=.lastTimestamp shows newest first, --field-selector type=Warning filters to only errors. Events expire after ~1 hour, so check them early during troubleshooting. kubectl describe also shows events for a specific resource. Use -w to watch events in real time while reproducing an issue.

Q6
How do you check resource requests and limits for all pods and compare with node capacity?
Show Answer
Answer
# Pod resource requests/limits:
kubectl get pods -o custom-columns=\
'NAME:.metadata.name,CPU_REQ:.spec.containers[0].resources.requests.cpu,CPU_LIM:.spec.containers[0].resources.limits.cpu,MEM_REQ:.spec.containers[0].resources.requests.memory,MEM_LIM:.spec.containers[0].resources.limits.memory'
# NAME       CPU_REQ  CPU_LIM  MEM_REQ  MEM_LIM
# web-pod    100m     500m     128Mi    512Mi
# api-pod    250m     1        256Mi    1Gi

# Node capacity:
kubectl describe node worker-1 | grep -A5 "Capacity"
# Capacity:
#   cpu:     4
#   memory:  8Gi

# Node allocatable (after system reservations):
kubectl describe node worker-1 | grep -A5 "Allocatable"
# Allocatable:
#   cpu:     3800m
#   memory:  7500Mi

# What's allocated on the node:
kubectl describe node worker-1 | grep -A10 "Allocated"
# Allocated resources:
#   CPU Requests  (Limits)    Memory Requests  (Limits)
#   1500m (39%)   3000m (78%) 2Gi (26%)        4Gi (53%)

# Available = Allocatable - Allocated
# CPU: 3800m - 1500m = 2300m free for scheduling
# Memory: 7500Mi - 2048Mi = 5452Mi free

# Actual usage vs requests (are pods using what they asked for?):
kubectl top pods
# Compare kubectl top (actual) vs requests (reserved)
Explanation

Three levels: Capacity (total), Allocatable (after reservations), Allocated (sum of requests). On the CKA: scheduling uses requests (not limits or actual usage). If a pod requests 500m CPU and the node only has 300m allocatable, it won't schedule. kubectl describe node shows all three levels. kubectl top shows actual real-time usage. Common pitfall: pods requesting more than they use wastes resources.

Q7
How do you redirect pod logs to a file for a CKA exam question?
Show Answer
Answer
# Basic: All logs to a file
kubectl logs web-pod > /opt/web-pod-logs.txt

# Specific container in multi-container pod:
kubectl logs web-pod -c nginx > /opt/nginx-logs.txt

# Only error lines:
kubectl logs web-pod | grep -i error > /opt/errors.txt
kubectl logs web-pod | grep -i "ERROR\|WARN" > /opt/issues.txt

# Last 100 lines to file:
kubectl logs web-pod --tail=100 > /opt/recent-logs.txt

# With timestamps:
kubectl logs web-pod --timestamps > /opt/timestamped.txt

# Previous crashed container:
kubectl logs web-pod --previous > /opt/crash-logs.txt

# Multiple pods by label:
kubectl logs -l app=web > /opt/all-web-logs.txt

# CKA common question patterns:
# "Write the logs of pod X to /opt/logs.txt"
kubectl logs web-pod > /opt/logs.txt

# "Write only error logs of pod X to /opt/errors.txt"
kubectl logs web-pod | grep -i error > /opt/errors.txt

# "Write the name of the pod with most restarts to /opt/answer.txt"
kubectl get pods --sort-by='.status.containerStatuses[0].restartCount' \
  --no-headers | tail -1 | awk '{print $1}' > /opt/answer.txt
Explanation

CKA exam frequently asks you to write output to specific files. Use > to redirect. Key patterns: kubectl logs pod > file, kubectl logs pod | grep error > file, kubectl top pods --sort-by=cpu --no-headers | head -1 | awk '{print $1}' > file. Always verify: cat /opt/answer.txt to confirm the content is correct. Use pipe + grep + awk for extracting specific values.

Q8
A cluster has multiple pods with high restart counts. How do you investigate?
Show Answer
Answer
# Step 1: Find pods with high restart counts
kubectl get pods -A --sort-by='.status.containerStatuses[0].restartCount'
# Shows ascending — look at the bottom for highest

# Reverse sort trick (show highest first):
kubectl get pods -A --sort-by='.status.containerStatuses[0].restartCount' \
  -o custom-columns=\
'NS:.metadata.namespace,NAME:.metadata.name,RESTARTS:.status.containerStatuses[0].restartCount,STATUS:.status.phase'
# NS        NAME         RESTARTS  STATUS
# default   crash-app    45        Running  ← Lots of restarts!
# prod      buggy-api    23        Running
# default   web-app      0         Running

# Step 2: Check WHY it's restarting
kubectl describe pod crash-app | grep -A5 "Last State"
# Last State:  Terminated
#   Reason:    OOMKilled    ← Memory issue
#   Exit Code: 137

# Step 3: Check recent restarts with events
kubectl get events --field-selector involvedObject.name=crash-app \
  --sort-by=.lastTimestamp

# Step 4: Check liveness probe
kubectl describe pod crash-app | grep -A5 Liveness
# If liveness probe fails → pod gets killed and restarted

# Common restart causes:
# OOMKilled (137) → Increase memory limit
# Exit Code 1     → App error, check logs --previous
# Liveness failed → Fix probe path/port/threshold
# Readiness fail  → Doesn't cause restart, but indicates issues
Explanation

Sort by restart count to find the most unstable pods. --sort-by='.status.containerStatuses[0].restartCount' sorts ascending (highest at bottom). Check describe pod for "Last State: Terminated" with the reason. Top causes of restarts: OOMKilled (exit 137), liveness probe failures, and app errors (exit 1). Note: high restart count + Running status = the pod keeps crashing and recovering.

Q9
How do you monitor cluster-wide resource usage and identify capacity issues?
Show Answer
Answer
# Node-level overview:
kubectl top nodes
# NAME       CPU(cores)  CPU%  MEMORY(bytes)  MEMORY%
# master     250m        12%   1500Mi         75%
# worker-1   1800m       90%   6000Mi         80%  ← High!
# worker-2   100m        5%    400Mi          20%

# Identify overloaded node:
# worker-1 at 90% CPU and 80% memory → capacity issue!

# Check what's on the overloaded node:
kubectl get pods -A --field-selector spec.nodeName=worker-1 \
  -o custom-columns='NS:.metadata.namespace,NAME:.metadata.name,CPU:.spec.containers[0].resources.requests.cpu'

# Check if pods are unevenly distributed:
kubectl get pods -A -o wide | awk '{print $8}' | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn
#  15 worker-1    ← Too many pods!
#   3 worker-2
#   2 master

# Check node allocations:
for node in $(kubectl get nodes -o name); do
  echo "=== $node ==="
  kubectl describe $node | grep -A8 "Allocated resources"
done

# Check for resource quotas:
kubectl get resourcequotas -A

# Check for limit ranges:
kubectl get limitranges -A

# Quick cluster health:
kubectl get nodes
kubectl get pods -A | grep -v Running | grep -v Completed
kubectl top nodes
kubectl get events -A --field-selector type=Warning --sort-by=.lastTimestamp | head -10
Explanation

kubectl top nodes gives CPU% and Memory% per node — look for nodes above 80%. Check pod distribution with -o wide and count per node. If pods are unevenly distributed, it might indicate affinity rules or the scheduler choosing based on available resources. describe node shows allocated vs allocatable, which tells you what the scheduler sees. ResourceQuotas and LimitRanges control resource usage at the namespace level.

Q10
How do you get logs from all pods matching a label selector?
Show Answer
Answer
# Logs from all pods with label app=web:
kubectl logs -l app=web
# (shows logs from up to 5 pods by default)

# Increase the limit:
kubectl logs -l app=web --max-log-requests=20

# All containers in matching pods:
kubectl logs -l app=web --all-containers=true

# Stream logs from all matching pods:
kubectl logs -l app=web -f --max-log-requests=10

# With prefix (shows pod name before each line):
kubectl logs -l app=web --prefix=true
# [pod/web-abc12/nginx] 10.0.0.1 - GET /api/health 200
# [pod/web-def34/nginx] 10.0.0.2 - GET /api/users 500 ← Error!

# Only last 10 lines from each:
kubectl logs -l app=web --tail=10

# Filter specific namespace:
kubectl logs -l app=web -n production

# Combine with grep:
kubectl logs -l app=web --all-containers | grep -i error

# Labels with multiple selectors:
kubectl logs -l app=web,tier=frontend

# CKA tip: When you need to check multiple replicas
# Use -l with --prefix to identify which pod has the error
Explanation

-l (label selector) with kubectl logs fetches logs from all matching pods at once. Default limit is 5 concurrent pods — increase with --max-log-requests. --prefix=true prepends the pod/container name to each line, making it easy to identify which pod generated each log entry. On the CKA, this is faster than checking pods one by one when a Deployment has multiple replicas.

Chapter 34: CKA Troubleshooting Scenarios

10 must-practice scenarios with step-by-step solutions and a systematic troubleshooting methodology. Practice each scenario until you can solve it under 5 minutes — that's exam speed.

🔴 Advanced ⏱️ ~3 hours 🎯 Scenarios 📝 CKA EXAM

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ Troubleshooting methodology — the 5-step IDENTIFY → GATHER → ANALYZE → FIX → VERIFY flow
  • ✅ 10 must-practice scenarios — Node NotReady, Pod Pending, CrashLoopBackOff, DNS, etcd, certs, and more
  • ✅ Symptom-to-solution mapping — fast lookup from what you see to what you fix
  • ✅ CKA exam strategy — time management, speed tips, and common traps to avoid
  • ✅ Essential command reference — the exact commands for each troubleshooting step
CKA Exam Domains Covered:
Troubleshooting 30%
🎯 This Chapter = Your CKA Practice Lab

These 10 scenarios cover the most common troubleshooting tasks on the CKA exam. Each follows the 5-step method: IDENTIFY → GATHER → ANALYZE → FIX → VERIFY. Train yourself to follow this flow automatically.

34.1 Scenario Collection — 10 Must-Practice Scenarios

🎯 CKA EXAM TOPIC

Each scenario presents a broken cluster state and walks you through the exact commands and thinking process to diagnose and fix it. These mirror real CKA exam tasks.

The 5-Step Troubleshooting Method

1
IDENTIFY

What's broken? kubectl get pods/nodes/svc — find the failing resource and its status.

2
GATHER

kubectl describe + kubectl logs — collect Events, conditions, and error messages.

3
ANALYZE

Read the error messages. Match symptoms to known causes. Narrow down root cause.

4
FIX

Apply the fix — edit YAML, restart service, add missing resource. Make ONE change at a time.

5
VERIFY

Confirm the fix worked. kubectl get + kubectl describe — status should be healthy.

Troubleshooting Priority Layers

🏗️
Infrastructure Layer

Nodes NotReady, kubelet down, etcd unhealthy, certificates expired, kubeconfig issues. Fix infrastructure first — nothing else works if the cluster is broken.

📦
Workload Layer

Pod Pending, CrashLoopBackOff, PVC not binding, resource limits. Most common exam tasks — fix pod/deployment configs.

🌐
Networking Layer

Service not accessible, DNS failing, NetworkPolicy blocking. Hardest to debug — check selectors, endpoints, kube-proxy, CoreDNS.

⏱️
CKA Time Strategy

Easy tasks first (2-3 min each), hard tasks last. Skip if stuck >8 minutes. Return to skipped tasks at the end. Never zero a question.

Scenario Difficulty Map

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  10 MUST-PRACTICE CKA TROUBLESHOOTING SCENARIOS                             │
│                                                                              │
│  INFRASTRUCTURE LAYER                                                        │
│  ├── Scenario 1:  Worker Node NotReady        ████████░░  Difficulty: 8/10  │
│  ├── Scenario 6:  etcd Cluster Unhealthy      █████████░  Difficulty: 9/10  │
│  ├── Scenario 7:  Certificates Expired        ████████░░  Difficulty: 8/10  │
│  └── Scenario 10: kubeconfig Issues           ██████░░░░  Difficulty: 6/10  │
│                                                                              │
│  WORKLOAD LAYER                                                              │
│  ├── Scenario 2:  Pod Stuck in Pending        ██████░░░░  Difficulty: 6/10  │
│  ├── Scenario 4:  CrashLoopBackOff            ███████░░░  Difficulty: 7/10  │
│  └── Scenario 9:  PVC Not Binding             ██████░░░░  Difficulty: 6/10  │
│                                                                              │
│  NETWORKING LAYER                                                            │
│  ├── Scenario 3:  Service Not Accessible      ███████░░░  Difficulty: 7/10  │
│  ├── Scenario 5:  DNS Resolution Failing      ████████░░  Difficulty: 8/10  │
│  └── Scenario 8:  Network Policy Blocking     ███████░░░  Difficulty: 7/10  │
│                                                                              │
│  💡 Practice order: 2 → 4 → 3 → 10 → 9 → 1 → 8 → 5 → 7 → 6              │
│     (easiest first, build confidence before the hard ones)                   │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Scenario 1: Worker Node NotReady

⚠️ Problem

kubectl get nodes shows worker01 NotReady. Pods on this node are being evicted.

🔴 Worker Node NotReady — Step-by-Step Walkthrough

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  1. IDENTIFY:                                                           │
│     $ kubectl get nodes                                                 │
│     NAME       STATUS     ROLES    AGE   VERSION                        │
│     master     Ready      master   30d   v1.31.0                        │
│     worker01   NotReady   <none>   30d   v1.31.0    ◄── PROBLEM        │
│     worker02   Ready      <none>   30d   v1.31.0                        │
│                                                                         │
│  2. GATHER — Describe the node:                                         │
│     $ kubectl describe node worker01                                    │
│     Conditions:                                                         │
│       Ready   False   KubeletNotReady   container runtime not running   │
│                                                                         │
│  3. ANALYZE — SSH to the node:                                          │
│     $ ssh worker01                                                      │
│     $ systemctl status kubelet                                          │
│     → Active: inactive (dead)    ◄── kubelet is not running!            │
│                                                                         │
│     $ systemctl status containerd                                       │
│     → Active: inactive (dead)    ◄── container runtime also down!       │
│                                                                         │
│  4. FIX — Restart services:                                             │
│     $ systemctl restart containerd                                      │
│     $ systemctl restart kubelet                                         │
│     $ systemctl enable kubelet    (ensure it starts on boot)            │
│                                                                         │
│  5. VERIFY — Back on the master:                                        │
│     $ kubectl get nodes                                                 │
│     worker01   Ready   <none>   30d   v1.31.0   ← ✅ Fixed!            │
│                                                                         │
│  ⚠️  OTHER COMMON CAUSES:                                               │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐            │
│  │ • kubelet config wrong → /var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml   │            │
│  │ • swap enabled → swapoff -a                             │            │
│  │ • disk pressure → df -h (clear space)                   │            │
│  │ • CNI plugin missing → ls /etc/cni/net.d/               │            │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘            │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Scenario 2: Pod Stuck in Pending

⚠️ Problem

anihpj-web pod in the anihpj namespace is stuck in Pending state and won't schedule.

🟡 Pod Pending — Scheduling Failure Walkthrough

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  1. IDENTIFY:                                                           │
│     $ kubectl get pods -n anihpj                                        │
│     NAME                 READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE               │
│     anihpj-web-xxxx      0/1     Pending   0          5m                │
│                                                                         │
│  2. GATHER — Describe the pod (Events are KEY):                         │
│     $ kubectl describe pod anihpj-web-xxxx -n anihpj                    │
│     Events:                                                             │
│       Warning  FailedScheduling  0/3 nodes are available:               │
│         1 node(s) had taint {node-role.kubernetes.io/master: },    ◄──  │
│         that the pod didn't tolerate,                                   │
│         2 Insufficient cpu.                                        ◄──  │
│                                                                         │
│  3. ANALYZE — Two issues revealed:                                      │
│     a) Master node has taint → pod can't schedule there                 │
│     b) Worker nodes don't have enough CPU for the request               │
│                                                                         │
│  4. FIX — Reduce resource requests:                                     │
│     $ kubectl edit deployment anihpj-web -n anihpj                      │
│     resources:                                                          │
│       requests:                                                         │
│         cpu: "100m"       ← was "2000m", reduced to fit                 │
│         memory: "256Mi"                                                 │
│                                                                         │
│  5. VERIFY:                                                             │
│     $ kubectl get pods -n anihpj -w                                     │
│     anihpj-web-xxxx   1/1   Running   0   15s   ← ✅ Scheduled!        │
│                                                                         │
│  📋 COMMON PENDING CAUSES:                                              │
│  ┌──────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────┐           │
│  │ Event Message            │ Fix                           │           │
│  ├──────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────┤           │
│  │ Insufficient cpu/memory  │ Reduce requests or add nodes  │           │
│  │ pod didn't tolerate      │ Add toleration or remove taint│           │
│  │ no nodes match selector  │ Fix nodeSelector or label node│           │
│  │ PVC not found            │ Create the missing PVC        │           │
│  └──────────────────────────┴───────────────────────────────┘           │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Scenario 3: Service Not Accessible

⚠️ Problem

Can't reach anihpj via the NodePort service. Requests to http://<node-ip>:<nodeport> time out.

🔵 Service Not Accessible — Label Mismatch Walkthrough

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  1. IDENTIFY — Check the service:                                       │
│     $ kubectl get svc anihpj-svc -n anihpj                              │
│     NAME         TYPE       CLUSTER-IP    PORT(S)        AGE            │
│     anihpj-svc   NodePort   10.96.45.12   80:30080/TCP   2h             │
│                                                                         │
│  2. GATHER — Check if endpoints exist:                                  │
│     $ kubectl get endpoints anihpj-svc -n anihpj                        │
│     NAME         ENDPOINTS   AGE                                        │
│     anihpj-svc   <none>      2h   ◄── NO ENDPOINTS! Service has        │
│                                         no matching pods!               │
│                                                                         │
│  3. ANALYZE — Compare selector vs pod labels:                           │
│     $ kubectl get svc anihpj-svc -n anihpj -o yaml | grep -A3 selector │
│       selector:                                                         │
│         app: anihpj-web       ◄── Service looks for this label          │
│                                                                         │
│     $ kubectl get pods -n anihpj --show-labels                          │
│       anihpj-web-xxxx   Running   app=anihpj   ◄── Pod has THIS label  │
│                                                                         │
│     → MISMATCH! Service: "anihpj-web" vs Pod: "anihpj"                 │
│                                                                         │
│  4. FIX — Correct the service selector:                                 │
│     $ kubectl edit svc anihpj-svc -n anihpj                             │
│     selector:                                                           │
│       app: anihpj         ← changed from "anihpj-web" to "anihpj"      │
│                                                                         │
│  5. VERIFY:                                                             │
│     $ kubectl get endpoints anihpj-svc -n anihpj                        │
│     ENDPOINTS: 10.244.1.5:8000   ← ✅ Pod is now connected!            │
│                                                                         │
│     $ curl http://<node-ip>:30080                                       │
│     → ✅ anihpj responds!                                               │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Scenario 4: CrashLoopBackOff

⚠️ Problem

anihpj-db pod keeps crashing — restart count is climbing and backoff interval increasing.

🔴 CrashLoopBackOff — Missing Environment Variable

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  1. IDENTIFY:                                                           │
│     $ kubectl get pods -n anihpj                                        │
│     NAME         READY   STATUS             RESTARTS      AGE           │
│     anihpj-db-0  0/1     CrashLoopBackOff   7 (3m ago)    15m           │
│                                                                         │
│  2. GATHER — Logs from the PREVIOUS crash:                              │
│     $ kubectl logs anihpj-db-0 -n anihpj --previous                     │
│                                                                         │
│     Error: Database is uninitialized and superuser password              │
│     is not specified. You must specify POSTGRES_PASSWORD to a    ◄──    │
│     non-empty value for the superuser.                                  │
│                                                                         │
│  3. ANALYZE — Check pod spec:                                           │
│     $ kubectl describe pod anihpj-db-0 -n anihpj                        │
│     Containers:                                                         │
│       postgres:                                                         │
│         Image: postgres:15                                              │
│         Environment: <none>    ◄── No env vars set! Missing            │
│                                    POSTGRES_PASSWORD                     │
│     Last State: Terminated (Exit Code 1)  ← App error                   │
│                                                                         │
│  4. FIX — Add the required env var via Secret:                          │
│     $ kubectl create secret generic db-creds \                          │
│         --from-literal=POSTGRES_PASSWORD=securepass123 -n anihpj        │
│                                                                         │
│     Then patch the StatefulSet:                                          │
│     $ kubectl edit statefulset anihpj-db -n anihpj                       │
│     env:                                                                │
│     - name: POSTGRES_PASSWORD                                           │
│       valueFrom:                                                        │
│         secretKeyRef:                                                   │
│           name: db-creds                                                │
│           key: POSTGRES_PASSWORD                                        │
│                                                                         │
│  5. VERIFY:                                                             │
│     $ kubectl get pods -n anihpj -w                                     │
│     anihpj-db-0   1/1   Running   0   20s   ← ✅ No more crashes!      │
│     $ kubectl logs anihpj-db-0 -n anihpj                                │
│     → "database system is ready to accept connections"                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Scenario 5: DNS Resolution Failing

⚠️ Problem

Pods can't resolve service names. anihpj-web can't reach anihpj-db by hostname.

🔴 DNS Failure — CoreDNS Troubleshooting

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  1. IDENTIFY — Test DNS from inside a pod:                              │
│     $ kubectl run dns-test --image=busybox:1.36 --rm -it --restart=    │
│       Never -- nslookup kubernetes.default                              │
│     → ;; connection timed out; no servers could be reached     ◄──     │
│                                                                         │
│  2. GATHER — Check CoreDNS pods:                                       │
│     $ kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns              │
│     NAME                       READY   STATUS             RESTARTS     │
│     coredns-5644d7b6d9-abc12   0/1     CrashLoopBackOff   4            │
│     coredns-5644d7b6d9-def34   0/1     CrashLoopBackOff   4            │
│                                                                         │
│  3. ANALYZE — Check CoreDNS logs:                                      │
│     $ kubectl logs -n kube-system coredns-5644d7b6d9-abc12              │
│     → plugin/forward: no nameservers found                              │
│                                                                         │
│     $ kubectl get cm coredns -n kube-system -o yaml                     │
│     → Corefile has forward . /etc/resolv.conf                           │
│     → But /etc/resolv.conf in the pod is empty or wrong                 │
│                                                                         │
│  4. FIX — Correct the CoreDNS ConfigMap:                               │
│     $ kubectl edit cm coredns -n kube-system                            │
│     forward . 8.8.8.8 8.8.4.4    ← use specific DNS servers            │
│                                                                         │
│     $ kubectl rollout restart deployment coredns -n kube-system         │
│                                                                         │
│  5. VERIFY:                                                             │
│     $ kubectl run dns-test --image=busybox:1.36 --rm -it --restart=    │
│       Never -- nslookup kubernetes.default                              │
│     Name:    kubernetes.default.svc.cluster.local                       │
│     Address: 10.96.0.1   ← ✅ DNS is working!                          │
│                                                                         │
│  🔍 ALSO CHECK:                                                        │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐            │
│  │ • CoreDNS service IP:  kubectl get svc kube-dns         │            │
│  │   -n kube-system (must match pod's /etc/resolv.conf)    │            │
│  │ • Network plugin: is the CNI functional?                │            │
│  │ • Firewall rules blocking UDP port 53?                  │            │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘            │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Scenario 6: etcd Cluster Unhealthy

⚠️ Problem

API server is intermittently unavailable. kubectl commands sometimes time out. Suspected etcd issue.

🔴 etcd Unhealthy — Diagnosis and Recovery

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  1. IDENTIFY — Check etcd health:                                       │
│     $ ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl endpoint health \                           │
│       --endpoints=https://127.0.0.1:2379 \                              │
│       --cacert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt \                        │
│       --cert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt \                      │
│       --key=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key                         │
│                                                                         │
│     https://127.0.0.1:2379 is unhealthy: failed to commit    ◄──       │
│                                                                         │
│  2. GATHER — Check etcd member list and logs:                           │
│     $ etcdctl member list (with same cert flags above)                  │
│     $ kubectl logs etcd-master -n kube-system                           │
│     OR if API is down:                                                  │
│     $ crictl logs $(crictl ps -a | grep etcd | awk '{print $1}')       │
│                                                                         │
│  3. ANALYZE — Common causes:                                           │
│     → Disk too slow (WAL write latency)                                 │
│     → Member removed/added incorrectly                                  │
│     → Data corruption                                                   │
│                                                                         │
│  4. FIX — If data corruption, restore from backup:                     │
│     a) Move API server manifest out to stop it:                         │
│        $ mv /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml /tmp/         │
│                                                                         │
│     b) Restore etcd from snapshot:                                      │
│        $ ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot restore /backup/snapshot.db \   │
│          --data-dir=/var/lib/etcd-restored                              │
│                                                                         │
│     c) Update etcd manifest with new data-dir:                          │
│        $ vi /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml                         │
│        → Change --data-dir=/var/lib/etcd-restored                       │
│        → Update hostPath volume to /var/lib/etcd-restored               │
│                                                                         │
│     d) Move API server manifest back:                                   │
│        $ mv /tmp/kube-apiserver.yaml /etc/kubernetes/manifests/         │
│                                                                         │
│  5. VERIFY:                                                             │
│     $ kubectl get nodes        ← API responds                          │
│     $ etcdctl endpoint health  ← etcd is healthy                       │
│     ✅ Cluster recovered!                                               │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
⚠️ CKA Gotcha: etcd Restore Flags

When restoring etcd, you must use a new data-dir path (--data-dir=/var/lib/etcd-restored). Don't restore in-place to /var/lib/etcd — it will fail if the existing data conflicts. Also update both the --data-dir flag AND the hostPath volume in the etcd static pod manifest.

Scenario 7: Certificates Expired

⚠️ Problem

kubectl commands fail with "Unable to connect to the server: x509: certificate has expired".

🔴 Certificates Expired — Renewal Walkthrough

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  1. IDENTIFY — Check certificate expiry:                                │
│     $ kubeadm certs check-expiration                                    │
│                                                                         │
│     CERTIFICATE                EXPIRES                  RESIDUAL TIME   │
│     admin.conf                 Jan 15, 2024 09:30 UTC   expired    ◄── │
│     apiserver                  Jan 15, 2024 09:30 UTC   expired    ◄── │
│     apiserver-kubelet-client   Jan 15, 2024 09:30 UTC   expired        │
│     controller-manager.conf    Jan 15, 2024 09:30 UTC   expired        │
│     front-proxy-client         Jan 15, 2024 09:30 UTC   expired        │
│     scheduler.conf             Jan 15, 2024 09:30 UTC   expired        │
│                                                                         │
│  2. FIX — Renew all certificates:                                      │
│     $ kubeadm certs renew all                                           │
│                                                                         │
│     certificate embedded in admin.conf renewed                          │
│     certificate for serving the Kubernetes API renewed                  │
│     certificate for the API server to connect to kubelet renewed        │
│     ... (all renewed)                                                   │
│                                                                         │
│  3. RESTART — Control plane components:                                │
│     $ systemctl restart kubelet                                         │
│     (Static pods will automatically restart and pick up new certs)      │
│                                                                         │
│  4. UPDATE — kubeconfig with new certs:                                │
│     $ cp /etc/kubernetes/admin.conf ~/.kube/config                      │
│     $ chown $(id -u):$(id -g) ~/.kube/config                           │
│                                                                         │
│  5. VERIFY:                                                             │
│     $ kubectl get nodes                                                 │
│     → Should work without cert errors   ← ✅ Fixed!                    │
│                                                                         │
│     $ kubeadm certs check-expiration                                    │
│     → All certs show ~1 year residual time                              │
│                                                                         │
│  💡 PREVENTION: kubeadm auto-renews on control plane upgrade.          │
│     Set a calendar reminder before cert expiry!                         │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Scenario 8: Network Policy Blocking Traffic

⚠️ Problem

Frontend pods can't reach backend pods after a NetworkPolicy was applied. curl backend:80 from frontend times out.

🟠 Network Policy Blocking — Label Selector Fix

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  1. IDENTIFY — Confirm the connectivity issue:                          │
│     $ kubectl exec -n anihpj frontend-pod -- curl -s --max-time 3      │
│       http://backend-svc:80                                             │
│     → curl: (28) Connection timed out   ◄── blocked!                   │
│                                                                         │
│  2. GATHER — List and inspect NetworkPolicies:                          │
│     $ kubectl get networkpolicy -n anihpj                               │
│     NAME             POD-SELECTOR    AGE                                │
│     backend-policy   app=backend     10m                                │
│                                                                         │
│     $ kubectl describe netpol backend-policy -n anihpj                  │
│     Spec:                                                               │
│       PodSelector:   app=backend                                        │
│       Allowing ingress traffic:                                         │
│         From:                                                           │
│           PodSelector: role=api       ◄── Only allows "role=api" pods  │
│       Policy Types: Ingress                                             │
│                                                                         │
│  3. ANALYZE — Check frontend pod labels:                               │
│     $ kubectl get pods -n anihpj --show-labels                          │
│     frontend-pod   Running   app=frontend,tier=web   ◄── No "role=api" │
│                                                                         │
│     → The policy allows ingress from pods with "role=api"               │
│     → Frontend has "app=frontend,tier=web" — NO MATCH!                 │
│                                                                         │
│  4. FIX — Either add the label or update the policy:                   │
│     Option A: Add label to frontend pod                                 │
│     $ kubectl label pod frontend-pod -n anihpj role=api                 │
│                                                                         │
│     Option B: Fix the NetworkPolicy selector                            │
│     $ kubectl edit netpol backend-policy -n anihpj                      │
│     ingress:                                                            │
│     - from:                                                             │
│       - podSelector:                                                    │
│           matchLabels:                                                  │
│             app: frontend      ← match actual frontend labels          │
│                                                                         │
│  5. VERIFY:                                                             │
│     $ kubectl exec -n anihpj frontend-pod -- curl -s --max-time 3      │
│       http://backend-svc:80                                             │
│     → ✅ Response received! Traffic is flowing.                         │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Scenario 9: PVC Not Binding

⚠️ Problem

anihpj-db PVC is stuck in Pending state. The database pod can't start without its volume.

🟡 PVC Not Binding — StorageClass Mismatch

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  1. IDENTIFY:                                                           │
│     $ kubectl get pvc -n anihpj                                         │
│     NAME          STATUS    VOLUME   CAPACITY   STORAGECLASS   AGE      │
│     anihpj-data   Pending                       fast-ssd       10m ◄── │
│                                                                         │
│  2. GATHER — Describe PVC for events:                                  │
│     $ kubectl describe pvc anihpj-data -n anihpj                        │
│     Events:                                                             │
│       Warning  ProvisioningFailed  no persistent volumes available      │
│       for this claim and no storage class "fast-ssd" found     ◄──     │
│                                                                         │
│  3. ANALYZE — Check available storage classes and PVs:                 │
│     $ kubectl get storageclass                                          │
│     NAME              PROVISIONER          RECLAIMPOLICY               │
│     standard (def.)   rancher.io/local     Delete                      │
│     → "fast-ssd" doesn't exist! PVC asks for a non-existent class     │
│                                                                         │
│     $ kubectl get pv                                                    │
│     NAME     CAPACITY   ACCESS   RECLAIM   STATUS      STORAGECLASS    │
│     pv-01    10Gi       RWO      Retain    Available   standard        │
│     → PV exists but with "standard" class, not "fast-ssd"             │
│                                                                         │
│  4. FIX — Option A: Change PVC to use "standard":                      │
│     (PVC storageClassName is immutable — recreate it)                   │
│     $ kubectl delete pvc anihpj-data -n anihpj                          │
│     $ cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -                                    │
│     apiVersion: v1                                                      │
│     kind: PersistentVolumeClaim                                         │
│     metadata:                                                           │
│       name: anihpj-data                                                 │
│       namespace: anihpj                                                 │
│     spec:                                                               │
│       accessModes: ["ReadWriteOnce"]                                    │
│       storageClassName: standard                                        │
│       resources:                                                        │
│         requests:                                                       │
│           storage: 5Gi                                                  │
│     EOF                                                                 │
│                                                                         │
│  5. VERIFY:                                                             │
│     $ kubectl get pvc -n anihpj                                         │
│     anihpj-data   Bound   pv-01   10Gi   RWO   standard   ← ✅ Bound! │
│                                                                         │
│  💡 "WaitForFirstConsumer" is NOT an error — PV binds only when        │
│     a pod actually mounts the PVC. This is normal behavior.            │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Scenario 10: kubeconfig Issues

⚠️ Problem

kubectl can't connect to the cluster: "The connection to the server localhost:8080 was refused".

🟠 kubeconfig Issues — Connection Diagnosis

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  1. IDENTIFY — Check current config:                                    │
│     $ kubectl config current-context                                    │
│     error: current-context is not set   ◄── no context configured!     │
│                                                                         │
│     $ kubectl config view                                               │
│     apiVersion: v1                                                      │
│     clusters: null                                                      │
│     contexts: null       ◄── kubeconfig is empty/wrong                 │
│                                                                         │
│  2. GATHER — Check where kubectl looks for config:                     │
│     $ echo $KUBECONFIG                                                  │
│     → empty (uses default ~/.kube/config)                               │
│                                                                         │
│     $ ls -la ~/.kube/config                                             │
│     → No such file or directory   ◄── config file is missing!          │
│                                                                         │
│  3. ANALYZE — The kubeconfig wasn't copied after cluster setup.        │
│     On a kubeadm cluster, the admin config is on the master node.       │
│                                                                         │
│  4. FIX — Copy the kubeconfig from the master:                         │
│     $ mkdir -p ~/.kube                                                  │
│     $ cp /etc/kubernetes/admin.conf ~/.kube/config                      │
│     $ chown $(id -u):$(id -g) ~/.kube/config                           │
│                                                                         │
│     If on a different machine, SCP from master:                         │
│     $ scp master:/etc/kubernetes/admin.conf ~/.kube/config              │
│                                                                         │
