│  5. VERIFY:                                                             │
│     $ kubectl cluster-info                                              │
│     Kubernetes control plane is running at https://10.0.0.10:6443       │
│     CoreDNS is running at https://10.0.0.10:6443/api/v1/...            │
│                                                                         │
│     $ kubectl get nodes                                                 │
│     → All nodes listed   ← ✅ Connected!                               │
│                                                                         │
│  📋 OTHER KUBECONFIG ISSUES:                                            │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────┐         │
│  │ Symptom                      │ Fix                         │         │
│  ├──────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────┤         │
│  │ Wrong context active         │ kubectl config use-context  │         │
│  │ Wrong server URL             │ Edit server: in config      │         │
│  │ KUBECONFIG env overriding    │ unset KUBECONFIG            │         │
│  │ Permission denied            │ chown the config file       │         │
│  └──────────────────────────────┴─────────────────────────────┘         │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
🏋️ Practice Drill: Time Yourself on All 10 Scenarios

Set up a practice cluster (kubeadm or kind). Intentionally break it in each of these 10 ways, then fix it. Target: solve each scenario in under 5 minutes. On the CKA exam, speed matters.

  1. Stop kubelet on a worker node → diagnose and fix NotReady
  2. Set impossible resource requests → fix Pending pod
  3. Mismatch service selector → fix endpoints
  4. Remove required env var → fix CrashLoopBackOff
  5. Break CoreDNS config → fix DNS resolution
  6. Corrupt etcd data → restore from snapshot
  7. Let certs expire (kubeadm certs) → renew and recover
  8. Apply overly restrictive NetworkPolicy → fix traffic flow
  9. Create PVC with wrong StorageClass → fix binding
  10. Delete kubeconfig → recreate from admin.conf

Symptom → First Response Quick-Reference

Symptom You See Most Likely Cause First Command to Run Scenario #
Node NotReady kubelet stopped / misconfigured ssh <node>; systemctl status kubelet #1
Pod Pending Insufficient resources / node affinity kubectl describe pod <name> → Events #2
Service returns no response Selector mismatch / wrong port kubectl get endpoints <svc> #3
CrashLoopBackOff Missing config / bad entrypoint kubectl logs <pod> --previous #4
DNS not resolving CoreDNS crash / bad ConfigMap kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns #5
API server errors / cluster unstable etcd unhealthy or data loss etcdctl endpoint health (with certs) #6
x509: certificate has expired Cluster certificates expired kubeadm certs check-expiration #7
Pod-to-pod traffic blocked NetworkPolicy too restrictive kubectl get netpol -A #8
PVC Pending StorageClass mismatch / no provisioner kubectl get sc + kubectl describe pvc #9
connection refused from kubectl kubeconfig missing or corrupted ls -la ~/.kube/config #10
⚠️ Common Gotcha: Multiple Symptoms, One Root Cause

On the CKA exam, a single failure (e.g., kubelet down) can produce many symptoms at once — Pods in Terminating state, Services returning errors, DNS failing. Always start at the infrastructure layer (nodes → control plane → workloads) and work upward. Fixing the root cause often resolves several visible symptoms simultaneously.

34.2 Troubleshooting Methodology Checklist

🎯 CKA EXAM TOPIC

Every troubleshooting task — on the exam or in production — follows the same 5-step systematic method. Internalize this flow and you'll never freeze under pressure.

The 5-Step Troubleshooting Method

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                   THE 5-STEP TROUBLESHOOTING METHOD                          │
│                                                                              │
│  ┌──────────┐    ┌──────────┐    ┌──────────┐    ┌──────┐    ┌──────────┐  │
│  │    1.     │    │    2.    │    │    3.     │    │  4.  │    │    5.    │  │
│  │ IDENTIFY │───▶│ GATHER  │───▶│ ANALYZE  │───▶│ FIX  │───▶│ VERIFY  │  │
│  │          │    │         │    │          │    │      │    │         │  │
│  └──────────┘    └──────────┘    └──────────┘    └──────┘    └──────────┘  │
│       │               │              │              │             │         │
│       ▼               ▼              ▼              ▼             ▼         │
│  ┌──────────┐   ┌──────────┐   ┌──────────┐   ┌──────────┐ ┌──────────┐  │
│  │ What is  │   │ kubectl  │   │ What     │   │ Apply    │ │ Did the  │  │
│  │ broken?  │   │ describe │   │ changed? │   │ smallest │ │ fix      │  │
│  │ What's   │   │ kubectl  │   │ What's   │   │ possible │ │ work?    │  │
│  │ the      │   │ logs     │   │ diff-    │   │ change   │ │ Are      │  │
│  │ symptom? │   │ kubectl  │   │ erent?   │   │          │ │ there    │  │
│  │          │   │ exec     │   │          │   │          │ │ side     │  │
│  │ get pods │   │ events   │   │ Compare  │   │ Don't    │ │ effects? │  │
│  │ get nodes│   │ journal  │   │ working  │   │ change   │ │          │  │
│  │ get svc  │   │ systemctl│   │ vs broken│   │ too much │ │ Re-run   │  │
│  │ get events│  │ crictl   │   │          │   │ at once  │ │ step 1   │  │
│  └──────────┘   └──────────┘   └──────────┘   └──────────┘ └──────────┘  │
│                                                                              │
│  ⚠️  NEVER SKIP STEP 5!  Many CKA points are lost because candidates        │
│     fix the issue but don't verify, and the fix was incomplete.             │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Essential Troubleshooting Commands — Quick Reference

Step Command Purpose
1. IDENTIFY kubectl get pods -A -o wide See all pods across all namespaces with node placement
kubectl get nodes -o wide Node status, IP, OS, container runtime
kubectl get events --sort-by='.lastTimestamp' Recent cluster-wide events timeline
2. GATHER kubectl describe pod <name> Detailed pod info — Events section at bottom!
kubectl logs <pod> --previous Logs from the last crashed container
kubectl logs <pod> -c <container> Logs from a specific container in multi-container pod
kubectl exec -it <pod> -- /bin/sh Shell into a running container to inspect
3. ANALYZE kubectl describe node <name> Node conditions, taints, allocated resources
journalctl -u kubelet --no-pager -l kubelet logs on a node (SSH first)
crictl ps -a Container runtime status (when API is down)
4. FIX kubectl edit <resource> <name> Quick in-place fix of a resource
systemctl restart kubelet Restart kubelet after fixing node issues
5. VERIFY kubectl get pods -w Watch pods transition to Running
kubectl get events --field-selector reason=Started Confirm containers started successfully
💡 Part 9 Key Takeaways
  • Troubleshooting flow: kubectl get → describe → logs → exec → events. ALWAYS follow this order.
  • Pod statuses: CrashLoopBackOff (app crashes), ImagePullBackOff (wrong image/tag), Pending (no node/resources), Error (exit non-zero)
  • Node troubleshooting: Check kubelet (systemctl status), certificates (kubeadm certs), connectivity
  • Control plane down? Check static pod manifests in /etc/kubernetes/manifests/ and kubelet logs
  • 5-step method: IDENTIFY → GATHER → ANALYZE → FIX → VERIFY — never skip VERIFY!
  • Use --previous to see logs from crashed containers. Use -f to follow logs in real-time.
  • CKA weight: 30% — the heaviest domain! Practice every scenario until you can solve it in under 5 minutes.
✅ Part 9 Competency Checklist
  • Can you debug a CrashLoopBackOff pod in under 3 minutes?
  • Can you diagnose a NotReady node and bring it back?
  • Can you restore etcd from a snapshot backup?
  • Can you fix a broken service with no endpoints?
  • Can you troubleshoot DNS failure by inspecting CoreDNS?
  • Can you renew expired certificates and restore API access?
  • Can you identify and fix NetworkPolicy blocking traffic?
  • Do you follow IDENTIFY → GATHER → ANALYZE → FIX → VERIFY every time?

CKA Exam Time Management — Troubleshooting Questions

CKA Exam Strategy — 2 Hours, ~17 Questions

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  CKA EXAM TIME MANAGEMENT STRATEGY                                          │
│                                                                              │
│  Total: 2 hours (120 minutes) │ ~17 questions │ Pass: 66%                   │
│  Troubleshooting: ~30% = approximately 5-6 questions                        │
│                                                                              │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐                │
│  │  RECOMMENDED APPROACH                                    │                │
│  │                                                          │                │
│  │  1. FIRST PASS (0-90 min):                               │                │
│  │     → Answer every question you can solve quickly         │                │
│  │     → Target: ~5 min per question                        │                │
│  │     → Skip any question that takes > 8 min              │                │
│  │     → Flag skipped questions for second pass              │                │
│  │                                                          │                │
│  │  2. SECOND PASS (90-120 min):                             │                │
│  │     → Return to flagged/skipped questions                │                │
│  │     → Spend remaining time on highest-weight questions   │                │
│  │     → Partial answers still earn partial credit          │                │
│  │                                                          │                │
│  │  3. TROUBLESHOOTING SPEED TIPS:                           │                │
│  │     → Always check the NAMESPACE first (exam specifies)  │                │
│  │     → Use kubectl config set-context --current            │                │
│  │       --namespace=<ns> to avoid -n flag every time       │                │
│  │     → Have your debug sequence memorized:                 │                │
│  │       get → describe → logs → exec                       │                │
│  │     → Use kubectl explain <resource> for field reference │                │
│  │     → Copy-paste from kubernetes.io docs (allowed!)      │                │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘                │
│                                                                              │
│  ⚠️  COMMON TIME WASTERS:                                                   │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────┐       │
│  │ Trap                           │ Solution                        │       │
│  ├────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┤       │
│  │ Typing long YAML from memory  │ Use --dry-run=client -o yaml    │       │
│  │ Searching docs endlessly      │ Bookmark key pages before exam  │       │
│  │ Forgetting to switch context  │ Read question header FIRST      │       │
│  │ Not verifying after fix       │ Budget 30s for verification     │       │
│  │ Perfecting a low-score Q      │ Move on, return if time permits │       │
│  └────────────────────────────────┴─────────────────────────────────┘       │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Troubleshooting Quick-Reference Cheat Sheet

🔥 One-Page Troubleshooting Cheat Sheet

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  STATUS → FIRST COMMAND TO RUN                                               │
│  ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════  │
│                                                                              │
│  Pod Pending          → kubectl describe pod <name>     (check Events)     │
│  CrashLoopBackOff     → kubectl logs <pod> --previous   (last crash log)   │
│  ImagePullBackOff     → kubectl describe pod <name>     (check image name) │
│  CreateContainerError → kubectl describe pod <name>     (missing CM/Secret)│
│  Running but unhealthy→ kubectl logs <pod> -f           (app errors)       │
│  OOMKilled (exit 137) → increase resources.limits.memory                    │
│                                                                              │
│  Node NotReady        → ssh node; systemctl status kubelet                  │
│  API Server down      → crictl ps -a; crictl logs <id>                     │
│  Scheduler broken     → cat /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-scheduler.yaml   │
│  etcd broken          → etcdctl endpoint health (with cert flags)           │
│                                                                              │
│  Service no endpoints → kubectl get endpoints <svc>   (selector mismatch) │
│  DNS not resolving    → kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns│
│  NetworkPolicy block  → kubectl describe netpol <name> (check selectors)  │
│  PVC Pending          → kubectl get storageclass       (class mismatch)    │
│                                                                              │
│  USEFUL SHORTCUTS:                                                           │
│  ─────────────────                                                           │
│  alias k=kubectl                          # Already set on CKA exam         │
│  export do="--dry-run=client -o yaml"     # Quick YAML generation          │
│  export now="--force --grace-period 0"    # Instant pod deletion            │
│  k config set-context --current --namespace=X  # Set default namespace     │
│  k explain pod.spec.containers --recursive     # Field reference            │
│                                                                              │
│  FILE LOCATIONS TO REMEMBER:                                                 │
│  ─────────────────────────────                                               │
│  /etc/kubernetes/manifests/    → Static pod YAML files (control plane)      │
│  /etc/kubernetes/pki/          → Cluster certificates                       │
│  /var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml  → kubelet configuration                      │
│  /var/log/containers/          → Container log files on node                │
│  /etc/cni/net.d/               → CNI plugin configuration                  │
│  /var/lib/etcd/                → etcd data directory                        │
│  ~/.kube/config                → kubectl configuration (kubeconfig)         │
│                                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

⚠️ CKA Gotcha: Troubleshooting Scenario Traps

  • Always VERIFY after fixing — The exam checks the end state. If you fix a broken service but don't verify with curl or kubectl get endpoints, you might have missed something. Follow the 5-step method: IDENTIFY → GATHER → ANALYZE → FIX → VERIFY
  • Multiple issues per question — A single scenario may have 2-3 things broken (wrong label + wrong port + missing service). Fix one, test, then check for more. Don't assume one fix solves everything
  • Read the question TWICE — "Ensure the pod runs on node01" could mean nodeSelector, nodeName, or taint toleration. "Fix the deployment" could mean image, resources, selectors, or mount issues. Misreading costs 5-10 minutes
  • Use kubectl explain as your API reference — kubectl explain pod.spec.containers.resources --recursive. Faster than searching docs. Works offline (exam environment)
  • Time management — 17 questions in 2 hours = ~7 min per question. If stuck for 10 minutes, flag it and move on. Come back with fresh eyes. Easy questions first

CKA Speed Tip — Troubleshooting decision tree:
Pod not running? → kubectl describe pod → check Events
Service not reachable? → kubectl get endpoints → if empty, fix selector
Node NotReady? → SSH + systemctl status kubelet + journalctl -u kubelet
API server down? → Check /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml
DNS broken? → kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns

⚡ Exam-day aliases (set at start): alias k=kubectl (already set on exam)
export do="--dry-run=client -o yaml" → k run nginx --image=nginx $do > pod.yaml
export now="--force --grace-period 0" → k delete pod nginx $now
alias kn="kubectl config set-context --current --namespace" → kn prod
alias kx="kubectl explain" → kx pod.spec.volumes --recursive
🔥

Multi-Issue Scenario: "Application Not Reachable"

ADVANCED

Situation: A 3-tier app (frontend → backend → database) reports "Connection refused" from outside the cluster. Multiple things are broken.

Layer 1 — Infrastructure

Run kubectl get nodes. Worker node is NotReady. SSH in, find kubelet stopped → systemctl start kubelet. Node recovers. Pods reschedule.

Layer 2 — Workload

Backend pod is CrashLoopBackOff. kubectl logs backend --previous shows missing DB_HOST env var. Fix: add envFrom referencing the ConfigMap. Pod starts.

Layer 3 — Networking

Frontend Service has no Endpoints. kubectl get endpoints frontend-svc → empty. Selector says app: front but pod label is app: frontend. Fix selector → endpoints populate → app reachable.

Lesson: On the CKA, one question can have 2-3 issues across different layers. Always re-verify the whole stack after each fix. Don't stop at the first symptom.

📝 Chapter 34 — CKA Practice Questions

Q1
A Deployment's pods are running but the Service returns no response. Walk through the troubleshooting methodology.
Show Answer
Answer
# Systematic 5-step approach:

# Step 1: Check pods are running and ready
kubectl get pods -l app=web
# NAME        READY  STATUS   RESTARTS
# web-abc12   1/1    Running  0        ← Looks good

# Step 2: Check Service exists and has correct selector
kubectl get svc web-svc
kubectl describe svc web-svc
# Selector: app=web
# Port:     80/TCP
# TargetPort: 8080

# Step 3: Check Endpoints (MOST CRITICAL STEP)
kubectl get endpoints web-svc
# NAME      ENDPOINTS
# web-svc   <none>     ← NO ENDPOINTS! Selector mismatch!

# Step 4: Compare selector with pod labels
kubectl get pods --show-labels
# NAME        LABELS
# web-abc12   app=webapp    ← "webapp" not "web"!

# Step 5: Fix the selector
kubectl edit svc web-svc
# Change selector from app: web → app: webapp

# OR fix the pod labels:
kubectl label pod web-abc12 app=web --overwrite

# Verify:
kubectl get endpoints web-svc
# web-svc   10.244.1.5:8080   ← Endpoints populated!

# Test:
kubectl run test --rm -it --image=busybox -- wget -qO- web-svc
# <html>...</html> ✓
Explanation

The #1 CKA troubleshooting pattern: Service → Endpoints → Pods. If Endpoints shows <none>, the Service selector doesn't match any pod labels. Always: (1) check pods are Running+Ready, (2) check Service selector, (3) check Endpoints, (4) compare labels, (5) fix. This scenario appears in almost every CKA exam. The targetPort (container port) must match what the app listens on.

Q2
A Deployment isn't rolling out new pods after an image update. What's wrong?
Show Answer
Answer
# Check rollout status:
kubectl rollout status deployment web
# Waiting for deployment "web" rollout to finish:
# 1 out of 3 new replicas have been updated...
# ← Stuck! New pods aren't becoming ready

# Check the new ReplicaSet:
kubectl get rs
# NAME          DESIRED  CURRENT  READY
# web-abc123    3        3        3     ← OLD (still running)
# web-def456    1        1        0     ← NEW (0 ready!)

# Check the new pod:
kubectl get pods | grep web-def456
# web-def456-xyz   0/1   ImagePullBackOff   0   2m

# Root cause: Wrong image name
kubectl describe pod web-def456-xyz | grep Image
# Image: nginxx:latest  ← Typo! "nginxx"

# Fix:
kubectl set image deployment/web web=nginx:latest
# OR
kubectl rollout undo deployment web

# If rollout was paused:
kubectl rollout status deployment web
# "deployment is paused" ← Someone paused it!
kubectl rollout resume deployment web

# If maxUnavailable=0 and cluster is at capacity:
# New pod can't schedule → old pod can't terminate → deadlock
# Fix: Increase maxSurge or reduce replicas temporarily

# Verify:
kubectl rollout status deployment web
# deployment "web" successfully rolled out ✓
Explanation

Stuck rollouts happen when new pods can't become Ready. Common causes: (1) wrong image name/tag (ImagePullBackOff), (2) readiness probe fails, (3) resource constraints, (4) rollout paused. On the CKA: check `kubectl rollout status`, then check the new ReplicaSet's pods. kubectl rollout undo is the fastest way to revert. Deadlock scenario: maxUnavailable=0 + maxSurge=0 = can't add new or remove old pods.

Q3
DNS resolution isn't working inside pods. How do you debug?
Show Answer
Answer
# Step 1: Test DNS from inside a pod
kubectl run dns-test --rm -it --image=busybox -- nslookup kubernetes
# Server:    10.96.0.10
# nslookup: can't resolve 'kubernetes'  ← DNS broken!

# Step 2: Check CoreDNS pods
kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns
# NAME                      READY  STATUS
# coredns-5d78c9869d-abc12  0/1    CrashLoopBackOff  ← Problem!

# Step 3: Check CoreDNS logs
kubectl logs -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns

# Step 4: Check CoreDNS ConfigMap
kubectl get configmap coredns -n kube-system -o yaml
# Look for syntax errors in Corefile

# Step 5: Check kube-dns Service
kubectl get svc kube-dns -n kube-system
# Should be ClusterIP 10.96.0.10

# Step 6: Check pod DNS config
kubectl exec dns-test -- cat /etc/resolv.conf
# nameserver 10.96.0.10     ← Should point to kube-dns
# search default.svc.cluster.local svc.cluster.local cluster.local

# Common fixes:
# CoreDNS crash → Check ConfigMap for syntax errors
# Wrong nameserver → Check kubelet --cluster-dns flag
# Loop detected → Remove "loop" plugin from Corefile
# Upstream DNS → Check forward . directive in Corefile

# Fix CoreDNS ConfigMap:
kubectl edit configmap coredns -n kube-system
# Then restart:
kubectl rollout restart deployment coredns -n kube-system
Explanation

DNS is CoreDNS running in kube-system. Pods get the DNS IP (10.96.0.10) from kubelet config. On the CKA: test with nslookup kubernetes, check CoreDNS pods, check the Corefile ConfigMap. Common issue: loop detection plugin causes crashes on some setups — fix by editing the ConfigMap. The kube-dns Service name is kept for backward compatibility, but it routes to CoreDNS pods.

Q4
A user reports they can't create pods in a namespace. How do you check RBAC permissions?
Show Answer
Answer
# Step 1: Check what the user CAN do
kubectl auth can-i create pods -n dev --as=user1
# no  ← User can't create pods!

# Step 2: Check all permissions for the user
kubectl auth can-i --list --as=user1 -n dev
# Resources  Verbs
# (nothing relevant)  ← No roles bound!

# Step 3: Check existing RoleBindings
kubectl get rolebindings -n dev
# NAME       ROLE              SUBJECTS
# (empty)    ← No bindings!

# Step 4: Check ClusterRoleBindings
kubectl get clusterrolebindings | grep user1
# (nothing)

# Step 5: Create the necessary RBAC
# Option A: Create Role + RoleBinding
kubectl create role pod-manager -n dev \
  --verb=create,get,list,delete \
  --resource=pods

kubectl create rolebinding user1-pods -n dev \
  --role=pod-manager \
  --user=user1

# Option B: Use existing ClusterRole
kubectl create rolebinding user1-edit -n dev \
  --clusterrole=edit \
  --user=user1

# Step 6: Verify
kubectl auth can-i create pods -n dev --as=user1
# yes ✓

# Debug: Check which role is blocking
kubectl auth can-i create pods -n dev --as=user1 -v=5
# Shows API calls and authorization decisions
Explanation

kubectl auth can-i tests permissions. --as=user1 impersonates the user. --list shows all allowed actions. On the CKA: if a user can't do something, check RoleBindings in the namespace and ClusterRoleBindings. The fix is creating a Role + RoleBinding. Built-in ClusterRoles (view, edit, admin, cluster-admin) save time. -v=5 shows verbose authorization debugging.

Q5
A NetworkPolicy is blocking expected traffic. How do you debug it?
Show Answer
Answer
# Symptom: Pod A can't reach Pod B, but it worked before

# Step 1: Check NetworkPolicies in the namespace
kubectl get networkpolicies -n prod
# NAME           POD-SELECTOR  AGE
# deny-all       app=api       5m   ← Recently created!
# allow-web      app=web       30d

# Step 2: Examine the blocking policy
kubectl describe networkpolicy deny-all -n prod
# PodSelector: app=api
# Allowing ingress traffic: <none>   ← Blocks ALL ingress!
# Allowing egress traffic:  <none>   ← Blocks ALL egress!

# Step 3: Verify traffic is actually blocked
kubectl exec -n prod web-pod -- curl -s --max-time 3 api-svc:8080
# curl: (28) Connection timed out  ← Blocked!

# Step 4: Fix — Add allow rule for needed traffic
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-web-to-api
  namespace: prod
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: api
  policyTypes: ["Ingress"]
  ingress:
  - from:
    - podSelector:
        matchLabels:
          app: web
    ports:
    - port: 8080

# Step 5: Verify
kubectl exec -n prod web-pod -- curl -s api-svc:8080
# {"status": "ok"} ✓

# Key rules:
# - If ANY NetworkPolicy selects a pod, only allowed traffic works
# - No NetworkPolicy = all traffic allowed
# - Multiple policies are ADDITIVE (union of all allow rules)
Explanation

NetworkPolicies are default-deny once applied. If a pod has ANY NetworkPolicy selecting it, only explicitly allowed traffic passes. On the CKA: check which policies select the target pod (via podSelector), verify with curl/wget from source pod, then add an allow rule if needed. Multiple NetworkPolicies are additive — the union of all rules determines what's allowed. Common trap: creating a deny-all without corresponding allow rules.

Q6
You have 5 minutes left on the CKA exam and 3 questions remaining. What's your strategy?
Show Answer
Answer
Strategy Action
Triage Check weights — do highest % question first
Imperative First Use kubectl run/create/expose not YAML
Skip Stuck If stuck >60s, flag and move on
Partial Credit Create the resource even if incomplete — partial credit exists
Context! Always check kubectl config use-context — wrong context = 0 points 
# Speed aliases (should already be set):
export do="--dry-run=client -o yaml"
export now="--force --grace-period 0"

# Fast pod creation:
k run web --image=nginx --port=80 $do > pod.yaml
vi pod.yaml  # Quick edit
k apply -f pod.yaml

# Fast service:
k expose pod web --port=80 --target-port=80

# Fast delete and recreate:
k delete pod broken $now
k run broken --image=nginx
Explanation

Time management is critical on the CKA. With 5 minutes left: (1) do the highest-weight question, (2) use imperative commands exclusively, (3) don't debug — create from scratch if faster, (4) always verify the context with kubectl config use-context. Partial credit exists, so creating an incomplete resource is better than skipping entirely. The $do and $now variables save significant time.

Q7
A pod can't resolve an external domain (e.g., google.com) but can resolve cluster services. What's the issue?
Show Answer
Answer
# Step 1: Verify internal DNS works
kubectl exec test-pod -- nslookup kubernetes.default
# Name:   kubernetes.default.svc.cluster.local
# Address: 10.96.0.1  ← Internal DNS works!

# Step 2: Test external DNS
kubectl exec test-pod -- nslookup google.com
# ** server can't find google.com: SERVFAIL ← External fails!

# Step 3: Check CoreDNS forward config
kubectl get configmap coredns -n kube-system -o yaml
# Corefile:
#   .:53 {
#     forward . /etc/resolv.conf    ← Should forward to upstream DNS
#   }

# Step 4: Check the node's /etc/resolv.conf
# CoreDNS forwards external queries to the node's DNS
ssh worker-1
cat /etc/resolv.conf
# nameserver 127.0.0.53  ← systemd-resolved (might cause loops!)

# Step 5: Fix — Point CoreDNS to a real DNS server
kubectl edit configmap coredns -n kube-system
# Change: forward . /etc/resolv.conf
# To:     forward . 8.8.8.8 8.8.4.4

# Step 6: Restart CoreDNS
kubectl rollout restart deployment coredns -n kube-system

# Step 7: Verify
kubectl exec test-pod -- nslookup google.com
# Name:   google.com
# Address: 142.250.80.46 ✓

# Alternative: Check NetworkPolicy blocking DNS egress
kubectl get networkpolicies -n <namespace>
# If egress restricted, must allow UDP port 53 to kube-dns
Explanation

Internal DNS works (CoreDNS resolves .cluster.local) but external fails = upstream DNS forwarding issue. CoreDNS forwards external queries using the forward directive. If the node uses systemd-resolved (127.0.0.53), CoreDNS may loop. Fix by pointing to real upstream DNS (8.8.8.8). Also check NetworkPolicies — if egress is restricted, UDP port 53 to kube-dns must be explicitly allowed.

Q8
You need to fix a broken cluster where kubectl commands timeout. Where do you start?
Show Answer
Answer
# kubectl timeout = API server unreachable

# Step 1: SSH to the master node
ssh master

# Step 2: Check kubelet (manages static pods)
systemctl status kubelet
# If stopped → systemctl start kubelet

# Step 3: Check API server container
crictl ps | grep kube-apiserver
# If not listed → Check static pod manifest

# Step 4: Check API server manifest
cat /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml
# Look for:
# - Wrong image
# - Wrong port
# - Missing/wrong cert paths
# - Syntax errors

# Step 5: Check API server logs
crictl logs $(crictl ps -a | grep apiserver | awk '{print $1}')
# OR
journalctl -u kubelet | grep apiserver

# Step 6: Check etcd (API server depends on it)
crictl ps | grep etcd
# If etcd is down → API server can't work
# Check: /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml

# Step 7: Check certificates
openssl x509 -in /etc/kubernetes/pki/apiserver.crt -noout -dates
# notAfter=... ← Expired?

# Fix sequence:
# 1. Fix manifests in /etc/kubernetes/manifests/
# 2. kubelet auto-restarts static pods
# 3. Wait 30-60 seconds
# 4. Test: kubectl get nodes

# Common CKA causes:
# - Wrong port in kube-apiserver.yaml
# - Cert file path typo
# - etcd endpoint wrong
# - Image tag wrong
Explanation

kubectl timeout = API server down. On the CKA: SSH to master, use crictl (not kubectl!), check kubelet, check static pod manifests. API server is a static pod managed by kubelet. Common exam scenario: a deliberate error is placed in /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml. Fix the manifest → kubelet detects the change → API server restarts. Check etcd too — if etcd is down, API server won't work even if its config is correct.

Q9
How do you use kubectl explain to quickly find the correct YAML field paths during the exam?
Show Answer
Answer
# Basic: What fields does a Pod spec have?
kubectl explain pod.spec
# Lists all fields with descriptions

# Recursive: ALL nested fields
kubectl explain pod.spec --recursive
# Shows the full tree without descriptions
# Perfect for finding the exact path

# Specific field:
kubectl explain pod.spec.containers.resources
# Shows: limits, requests sub-fields

# Common exam lookups:
kubectl explain pod.spec.volumes --recursive
# Shows all volume types and their fields

kubectl explain pod.spec.containers.volumeMounts
# mountPath, name, subPath, readOnly

kubectl explain pod.spec.tolerations
# effect, key, operator, tolerationSeconds, value

kubectl explain pod.spec.affinity --recursive
# nodeAffinity, podAffinity, podAntiAffinity

kubectl explain deployment.spec.strategy
# type, rollingUpdate (maxSurge, maxUnavailable)

kubectl explain pv.spec --recursive
# All PV fields: capacity, accessModes, hostPath, nfs...

# CKA shortcut: Use with aliases
alias kx="kubectl explain"
kx pod.spec.securityContext --recursive
kx pod.spec.initContainers

# Can't remember the field name? Use recursive + grep:
kubectl explain pod.spec --recursive | grep -i "toleration"
#   tolerations  <[]Toleration>
Explanation

kubectl explain is your built-in documentation during the CKA — it's faster than searching docs. --recursive shows the full field tree. Use it when you can't remember the exact YAML path (e.g., "is it spec.containers.resources.limits or spec.resources.limits?"). grep with recursive output helps find field names. On the CKA, alias it as kx for speed.

Q10
A multi-issue scenario: pods are Pending, a Service has no endpoints, and a node is NotReady. Solve it systematically.
Show Answer
Answer
# Multi-issue diagnosis order: Infrastructure → Workloads → Networking

# PHASE 1: FIX INFRASTRUCTURE (Node)
kubectl get nodes
# NAME       STATUS     ROLES          AGE
# master     Ready      control-plane  30d
# worker-1   NotReady   <none>         30d  ← Fix this first!

ssh worker-1
systemctl status kubelet
# Active: inactive (dead)
systemctl start kubelet
systemctl enable kubelet
exit
# Wait: kubectl get nodes → worker-1 Ready ✓

# PHASE 2: FIX WORKLOADS (Pending Pods)
kubectl get pods
# NAME       READY  STATUS   RESTARTS
# web-abc    0/1    Pending  0
kubectl describe pod web-abc | tail -10
# "0/1 nodes available: 1 node had taint {NoSchedule}"
# (Before we fixed the node, no node was available)
# After node fix → pod auto-schedules!
kubectl get pods  # web-abc → Running ✓

# PHASE 3: FIX NETWORKING (Service)
kubectl get endpoints web-svc
# web-svc   <none>  ← Still no endpoints!

kubectl get svc web-svc -o yaml | grep -A2 selector
# selector:
#   app: website      ← Wrong label!

kubectl get pods --show-labels
# web-abc   app=web   ← Label is "web" not "website"

kubectl patch svc web-svc -p '{"spec":{"selector":{"app":"web"}}}'
kubectl get endpoints web-svc
# web-svc   10.244.1.5:80 ✓

# VERIFY ALL:
kubectl get nodes        # All Ready ✓
kubectl get pods         # All Running ✓
kubectl get endpoints    # All populated ✓
Explanation

Multi-issue scenarios test your ability to prioritize. Fix order: Infrastructure (nodes) → Workloads (pods) → Networking (services). Fixing the node first often auto-resolves the Pending pods (they were waiting for a schedulable node). Then fix the Service selector to populate endpoints. On the CKA, issues are often cascading — one root cause creates multiple symptoms. Fix the root cause first and check if downstream issues self-resolve before spending time on them.

🔴 Part 10: Production and Beyond Chapters 35-37

Chapter 35: High Availability Clusters

HA architecture, stacked vs external etcd topologies, setting up HA with kubeadm, etcd quorum, leader election, load balancer configuration, and a production HA checklist for the anihpj Django application.

🔴 Advanced ⏱️ ~2 hours 🏗️ HA 📝 CKA EXAM

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ HA architecture — why 3+ control plane nodes and how they work together
  • ✅ Stacked vs external etcd — topologies, trade-offs, and when to use each
  • ✅ HA setup with kubeadm — step-by-step multi-master initialization
  • ✅ etcd quorum — the math behind fault tolerance and why odd numbers matter
  • ✅ Leader election — how scheduler and controller-manager elect active leaders
  • ✅ Load balancer config — HAProxy/nginx in front of API servers
  • ✅ Production checklist — everything needed for anihpj HA deployment
CKA Exam Domains Covered:
Cluster Architecture 25% Troubleshooting 30%
🎯 HA Concepts Appear Throughout the CKA Exam

While you won't set up a full HA cluster on the exam, you must understand etcd quorum, backup/restore (covered in Part 7), and how control plane components achieve high availability. Questions about etcd member count, leader election, and API server load balancing are common.

35.1 HA Architecture

🎯 CKA EXAM TOPIC

A single control plane node is a single point of failure. If it goes down, the API server becomes unreachable, no new pods can be scheduled, and the cluster is essentially frozen. High Availability (HA) eliminates this by running multiple control plane nodes.

Single vs HA Control Plane — Why HA Matters

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                                                                              │
│  ❌ SINGLE CONTROL PLANE                ✅ HIGH AVAILABILITY (HA)            │
│  ════════════════════════               ═══════════════════════════           │
│                                                                              │
│  ┌──────────────┐                       ┌────────────────────┐              │
│  │ Control Plane│ ← Single point        │   LOAD BALANCER    │              │
│  │              │    of failure!         │  (HAProxy/nginx)   │              │
│  │ API Server   │                       └───────┬────────────┘              │
│  │ etcd         │                      ┌────────┼────────┐                  │
│  │ Scheduler    │                      ▼        ▼        ▼                  │
│  │ Ctrl-Manager │               ┌──────────┐┌──────────┐┌──────────┐       │
│  └──────┬───────┘               │  CP-1    ││  CP-2    ││  CP-3    │       │
│         │                       │ API ✅    ││ API ✅    ││ API ✅    │       │
│    ┌────┼────┐                  │ etcd ✅   ││ etcd ✅   ││ etcd ✅   │       │
│    ▼    ▼    ▼                  │ sched ✅  ││ sched ⏸  ││ sched ⏸  │       │
│  W-1  W-2  W-3                  │ c-mgr ✅  ││ c-mgr ⏸  ││ c-mgr ⏸  │       │
│                                 └──────────┘└──────────┘└──────────┘       │
│  If CP goes down:                     (leader)   (standby)   (standby)      │
│  ❌ No new scheduling                                                        │
│  ❌ No API access                ┌────┼────┐                                │
│  ❌ No self-healing              ▼    ▼    ▼                                │
│  ❌ Cluster is FROZEN           W-1  W-2  W-3                               │
│                                                                              │
│  Existing pods keep running     If CP-1 goes down:                          │
│  but nothing new can happen     ✅ LB routes to CP-2/CP-3                   │
│                                 ✅ New leader elected in seconds             │
│                                 ✅ Full cluster operations continue          │
│                                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
💡 Key HA Principle

Only ONE scheduler and controller-manager are active at any time (the "leader"). The others are on hot standby — they watch and are ready to take over in seconds. All API servers, however, handle requests simultaneously (active-active behind the load balancer).

HA Cluster Architecture — Complete View

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                       HA CLUSTER ARCHITECTURE                                │
│                                                                              │
│                    ┌──────────────────────────┐                              │
│                    │     LOAD BALANCER         │                              │
│                    │   (HAProxy / nginx)       │                              │
│                    │   VIP: 10.0.0.100:6443    │                              │
│                    └────────────┬─────────────┘                              │
│                     ┌──────────┼──────────┐                                  │
│                     │          │          │                                   │
│                     ▼          ▼          ▼                                   │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐     │
│  │                  CONTROL PLANE TIER                                  │     │
│  │                                                                     │     │
│  │  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐             │     │
│  │  │ cp-1 (10.0.1)│  │ cp-2 (10.0.2)│  │ cp-3 (10.0.3)│             │     │
│  │  ├──────────────┤  ├──────────────┤  ├──────────────┤             │     │
│  │  │ kube-apiserver│  │ kube-apiserver│  │ kube-apiserver│             │     │
│  │  │ (active)     │  │ (active)     │  │ (active)     │             │     │
│  │  ├──────────────┤  ├──────────────┤  ├──────────────┤             │     │
│  │  │ kube-scheduler│  │ kube-scheduler│  │ kube-scheduler│             │     │
│  │  │ ★ LEADER     │  │ ⏸ standby    │  │ ⏸ standby    │             │     │
│  │  ├──────────────┤  ├──────────────┤  ├──────────────┤             │     │
│  │  │ ctrl-manager │  │ ctrl-manager │  │ ctrl-manager │             │     │
│  │  │ ★ LEADER     │  │ ⏸ standby    │  │ ⏸ standby    │             │     │
│  │  ├──────────────┤  ├──────────────┤  ├──────────────┤             │     │
│  │  │ etcd         │  │ etcd         │  │ etcd         │             │     │
│  │  │ (peer)       │  │ (peer)       │  │ (peer)       │             │     │
│  │  └──────────────┘  └──────────────┘  └──────────────┘             │     │
│  │                                                                     │     │
│  │  etcd replication: cp-1 ◀──▶ cp-2 ◀──▶ cp-3 (Raft consensus)      │     │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘     │
│                                                                              │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐     │
│  │                   WORKER NODE TIER                                   │     │
│  │                                                                     │     │
│  │  ┌───────────┐  ┌───────────┐  ┌───────────┐  ┌───────────┐       │     │
│  │  │ worker-1  │  │ worker-2  │  │ worker-3  │  │ worker-N  │       │     │
│  │  │┌─────────┐│  │┌─────────┐│  │┌─────────┐│  │┌─────────┐│       │     │
│  │  ││ anihpj  ││  ││ anihpj  ││  ││ anihpj  ││  ││ jobpost ││       │     │
│  │  ││ web-1   ││  ││ web-2   ││  ││ db-0    ││  ││ worker  ││       │     │
│  │  │└─────────┘│  │└─────────┘│  │└─────────┘│  │└─────────┘│       │     │
│  │  │ kubelet   │  │ kubelet   │  │ kubelet   │  │ kubelet   │       │     │
│  │  │ kube-proxy│  │ kube-proxy│  │ kube-proxy│  │ kube-proxy│       │     │
│  │  └───────────┘  └───────────┘  └───────────┘  └───────────┘       │     │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘     │
│                                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
🎯 CKA Exam: Know the Components

You must know what runs on control plane nodes vs worker nodes. API server is active-active (all serve requests). Scheduler and controller-manager are active-passive (only the leader acts). etcd uses Raft consensus (all peers participate but one is the Raft leader).

35.2 Stacked vs External etcd

🎯 CKA EXAM TOPIC

There are two ways to deploy etcd in an HA cluster. The choice affects fault tolerance, complexity, and cost. You must understand both for the CKA exam.

Stacked etcd Topology — etcd on Control Plane Nodes

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  STACKED etcd TOPOLOGY (Default with kubeadm)                                │
│                                                                              │
│  Each control plane node runs BOTH Kubernetes components AND etcd            │
│                                                                              │
│  ┌───────────────┐    ┌───────────────┐    ┌───────────────┐                │
│  │  CP Node 1    │    │  CP Node 2    │    │  CP Node 3    │                │
│  │ ┌───────────┐ │    │ ┌───────────┐ │    │ ┌───────────┐ │                │
│  │ │API Server │ │    │ │API Server │ │    │ │API Server │ │                │
│  │ │Scheduler  │ │    │ │Scheduler  │ │    │ │Scheduler  │ │                │
│  │ │Ctrl-Mgr   │ │    │ │Ctrl-Mgr   │ │    │ │Ctrl-Mgr   │ │                │
│  │ ├───────────┤ │    │ ├───────────┤ │    │ ├───────────┤ │                │
│  │ │  etcd  📀 │ │    │ │  etcd  📀 │ │    │ │  etcd  📀 │ │                │
│  │ └───────────┘ │    │ └───────────┘ │    │ └───────────┘ │                │
│  └───────┬───────┘    └───────┬───────┘    └───────┬───────┘                │
│          │                    │                    │                          │
│          └─────── Raft Replication ───────────────┘                          │
│                                                                              │
│  ✅ Simpler setup — fewer nodes to manage                                   │
│  ✅ Default kubeadm topology                                                │
│  ⚠️ Risk: If a node dies, you lose both CP and an etcd member              │
│  📊 Minimum: 3 nodes (for etcd quorum)                                     │
│                                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

External etcd Topology — etcd on Dedicated Servers

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  EXTERNAL etcd TOPOLOGY                                                      │
│                                                                              │
│  etcd runs on SEPARATE dedicated nodes — decoupled from control plane        │
│                                                                              │
│  Control Plane Tier:                                                         │
│  ┌───────────────┐    ┌───────────────┐    ┌───────────────┐                │
│  │  CP Node 1    │    │  CP Node 2    │    │  CP Node 3    │                │
│  │ ┌───────────┐ │    │ ┌───────────┐ │    │ ┌───────────┐ │                │
│  │ │API Server │ │    │ │API Server │ │    │ │API Server │ │                │
│  │ │Scheduler  │ │    │ │Scheduler  │ │    │ │Scheduler  │ │                │
│  │ │Ctrl-Mgr   │ │    │ │Ctrl-Mgr   │ │    │ │Ctrl-Mgr   │ │                │
│  │ └───────────┘ │    │ └───────────┘ │    │ └───────────┘ │                │
│  └───────┬───────┘    └───────┬───────┘    └───────┬───────┘                │
│          └────────────┬───────┘                    │                          │
│                       │  Connect via TLS            │                          │
│          ┌────────────┼────────────────────────────┘                          │
│          ▼            ▼            ▼                                          │
│  ┌───────────┐  ┌───────────┐  ┌───────────┐                                │
│  │ etcd-1 📀 │  │ etcd-2 📀 │  │ etcd-3 📀 │  ← Dedicated etcd cluster    │
│  └─────┬─────┘  └─────┬─────┘  └─────┬─────┘                                │
│        └───── Raft Replication ───────┘                                      │
│                                                                              │
│  ✅ etcd survives control plane failures                                    │
│  ✅ Can scale etcd independently                                            │
│  ⚠️ More complex — requires 6+ nodes minimum (3 CP + 3 etcd)               │
│  ⚠️ More certs to manage (API→etcd TLS)                                    │
│                                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Topology Comparison

Aspect Stacked (default) External etcd
etcd location On control plane node Separate dedicated servers
Setup complexity Easier — kubeadm init handles it Harder — manual etcd cluster setup
Fault isolation ⚠️ CP failure = etcd member lost too ✅ etcd survives CP node failure
Minimum nodes 3 (combined CP + etcd) 6 (3 CP + 3 etcd)
Cost Lower — fewer servers Higher — 2× the infrastructure
CKA Exam Most tested topology ✅ Conceptual understanding needed
Best for Most production clusters Critical workloads with strict SLAs

⚠️ CKA Gotcha: Which Topology Is on the Exam?

The CKA exam uses stacked etcd topology (kubeadm default). When asked to backup/restore etcd, the etcd data dir is on the control plane node at /var/lib/etcd. The API server connects to etcd on localhost:2379. Know both topologies conceptually, but practice stacked for the exam.

35.3 Setting Up HA with kubeadm

🎯 CKA EXAM TOPIC

Setting up an HA cluster with kubeadm follows a specific sequence: configure the load balancer first, initialize the first control plane, then join additional control planes and workers.

HA Setup Sequence — Step by Step

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    HA CLUSTER SETUP SEQUENCE                                  │
│                                                                              │
│  Step 1          Step 2           Step 3           Step 4                    │
│  ┌──────────┐    ┌──────────┐    ┌──────────┐    ┌──────────┐              │
│  │ Setup    │    │ Init 1st │    │ Join     │    │ Join     │              │
│  │ Load     │───▶│ Control  │───▶│ More CP  │───▶│ Worker   │              │
│  │ Balancer │    │ Plane    │    │ Nodes    │    │ Nodes    │              │
│  └──────────┘    └──────────┘    └──────────┘    └──────────┘              │
│  HAProxy/nginx    kubeadm init    kubeadm join    kubeadm join              │
│  on :6443         --control-      --control-      (no flag)                 │
│                   plane-endpoint  plane                                      │
│                   =LB_IP:6443                                                │
│                                                                              │
│  Step 5          Step 6           Step 7                                     │
│  ┌──────────┐    ┌──────────┐    ┌──────────┐                               │
│  │ Install  │    │ Verify   │    │ Test     │                               │
│  │ CNI      │───▶│ etcd     │───▶│ Failover │                               │
│  │ Plugin   │    │ Health   │    │          │                               │
│  └──────────┘    └──────────┘    └──────────┘                               │
│  Calico/Cilium    etcdctl         Kill a CP node                             │
│                   endpoint        → verify LB                                │
│                   health          reroutes                                    │
│                                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Step 1: HAProxy Configuration

/etc/haproxy/haproxy.cfg 
# HAProxy configuration for Kubernetes API Server HA
frontend kubernetes-api
    bind *:6443
    mode tcp
    option tcplog
    default_backend kubernetes-api-backend

backend kubernetes-api-backend
    mode tcp
    option tcp-check
    balance roundrobin
    # Health check: API server /healthz endpoint
    server cp-1 10.0.0.1:6443 check fall 3 rise 2
    server cp-2 10.0.0.2:6443 check fall 3 rise 2
    server cp-3 10.0.0.3:6443 check fall 3 rise 2

Step 2: Initialize First Control Plane

Terminal — cp-1 (first control plane) 
# Initialize first control plane — point to the load balancer!
$ sudo kubeadm init \
    --control-plane-endpoint "10.0.0.100:6443" \
    --upload-certs \
    --pod-network-cidr=10.244.0.0/16

# Output includes TWO join commands:
# 1. For additional control plane nodes (has --control-plane flag)
# 2. For worker nodes (no --control-plane flag)

# Copy kubeconfig
$ mkdir -p $HOME/.kube
$ sudo cp /etc/kubernetes/admin.conf $HOME/.kube/config
$ sudo chown $(id -u):$(id -g) $HOME/.kube/config

Steps 3 & 4: Join Additional Nodes

Terminal — cp-2 and cp-3 
# Join as additional control plane (note --control-plane flag!)
$ sudo kubeadm join 10.0.0.100:6443 \
    --token <token> \
    --discovery-token-ca-cert-hash sha256:<hash> \
    --control-plane \
    --certificate-key <cert-key>
Terminal — worker nodes 
# Join as worker node (NO --control-plane flag)
$ sudo kubeadm join 10.0.0.100:6443 \
    --token <token> \
    --discovery-token-ca-cert-hash sha256:<hash>

Step 6: Verify Cluster Health

Terminal — verify HA cluster 
# Verify all nodes are Ready
$ kubectl get nodes
NAME   STATUS   ROLES           AGE   VERSION
cp-1   Ready    control-plane   10m   v1.31.0
cp-2   Ready    control-plane   8m    v1.31.0
cp-3   Ready    control-plane   6m    v1.31.0
w-1    Ready    <none>          4m    v1.31.0
w-2    Ready    <none>          3m    v1.31.0
w-3    Ready    <none>          2m    v1.31.0

# Verify etcd cluster health
$ ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl member list \
    --endpoints=https://127.0.0.1:2379 \
    --cacert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt \
    --cert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt \
    --key=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key
# Should show 3 members: cp-1, cp-2, cp-3

✅ Verify: HA Setup Checklist

  • kubectl get nodes — all control plane nodes show Ready
  • etcdctl member list — shows 3 etcd members
  • etcdctl endpoint health — all endpoints healthy
  • Load balancer health check — all backends UP
  • Kill one CP node → API still responds via LB

35.4 etcd Quorum and Leader Election

🎯 CKA EXAM TOPIC

etcd uses the Raft consensus algorithm. A majority of etcd members must agree before any write is committed. This majority is called the quorum. Understanding quorum math is essential for CKA.

etcd Quorum Formula: (N/2) + 1

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    ETCD QUORUM — THE MATH                                    │
│                                                                              │
│  Formula: Quorum = floor(N/2) + 1                                            │
│  Tolerated Failures = N - Quorum                                             │
│                                                                              │
│  ┌─────────┬────────┬─────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │ Members │ Quorum │ Tolerated Failures  │ Recommendation               │  │
│  ├─────────┼────────┼─────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────┤  │
│  │    1    │   1    │         0           │ ❌ Dev/test only              │  │
│  │    2    │   2    │         0           │ ❌ NEVER — worse than 1!     │  │
│  │    3    │   2    │         1           │ ✅ Standard production       │  │
│  │    4    │   3    │         1           │ ❌ Same tolerance as 3!      │  │
│  │    5    │   3    │         2           │ ✅ Large-scale production    │  │
│  │    6    │   4    │         2           │ ❌ Same tolerance as 5!      │  │
│  │    7    │   4    │         3           │ ⚠️ Rare — more latency       │  │
│  └─────────┴────────┴─────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────┘  │
│                                                                              │
│  🔑 KEY INSIGHT: Even numbers are WASTEFUL!                                 │
│  ─────────────────────────────────────────                                   │
│  3 members → tolerate 1 failure                                              │
│  4 members → STILL only tolerate 1 failure (need 3 for quorum)              │
│  → You added a node but got ZERO extra fault tolerance!                      │
│                                                                              │
│  ALWAYS USE ODD NUMBERS: 1, 3, 5, 7                                        │
│                                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Quorum in Action — What Happens During Failures

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  3-MEMBER CLUSTER FAILURE SCENARIOS                                          │
│                                                                              │
│  Normal (3/3 alive):         1 Node Down (2/3 alive):                        │
│  ┌────┐  ┌────┐  ┌────┐     ┌────┐  ┌────┐  ┌────┐                        │
│  │ E1 │  │ E2 │  │ E3 │     │ E1 │  │ E2 │  │ ╳╳ │  ← Node 3 down       │
│  │ ✅ │  │ ✅ │  │ ✅ │     │ ✅ │  │ ✅ │  │ ❌ │                        │
│  └────┘  └────┘  └────┘     └────┘  └────┘  └────┘                        │
│  Quorum: 2 of 3 = ✅         Quorum: 2 of 3 = ✅ (still works!)            │
│  Writes: ✅                  Writes: ✅                                      │
│  Reads:  ✅                  Reads:  ✅                                      │
│                                                                              │
│  2 Nodes Down (1/3 alive):   Split Brain (network partition):                │
│  ┌────┐  ┌────┐  ┌────┐     ┌────┐  │  ┌────┐  ┌────┐                     │
│  │ E1 │  │ ╳╳ │  │ ╳╳ │     │ E1 │  │  │ E2 │  │ E3 │                     │
│  │ ✅ │  │ ❌ │  │ ❌ │     │ ✅ │  │  │ ✅ │  │ ✅ │                     │
│  └────┘  └────┘  └────┘     └────┘  │  └────┘  └────┘                     │
│  Quorum: 1 of 3 = ❌         Side A: 1 (no quorum ❌)                       │
│  Writes: ❌ CLUSTER DOWN!    Side B: 2 (has quorum ✅)                       │
│  Reads:  ❌ (stale reads     → Side B continues serving                      │
│             may be possible)  → Side A becomes read-only                     │
│                                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
🎯 CKA Exam Quick Recall

If asked "how many etcd node failures can a 5-member cluster tolerate?" — the answer is 2. Formula: floor(5/2) + 1 = 3 needed for quorum, so 5 - 3 = 2 failures tolerated. Always use odd numbers for etcd cluster size.

💡 Why Even Numbers Are Dangerous

A 4-member etcd cluster tolerates only 1 failure — the same as a 3-member cluster — but uses more resources. Worse, with 4 members a network partition can create two equal groups (2 vs 2), and neither has quorum, causing a total outage. Odd numbers eliminate this symmetric split risk.

etcd Backup & Restore in HA — Critical CKA Skill

Backing up and restoring etcd is a guaranteed CKA exam topic. In an HA cluster, you must back up from one member and restore to all members.

Terminal — etcd backup & restore (CKA exam critical) 
# ═══════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 1: BACKUP — Take snapshot from any healthy member
# ═══════════════════════════════════════
$ ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot save /tmp/etcd-backup.db \
    --endpoints=https://127.0.0.1:2379 \
    --cacert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt \
    --cert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt \
    --key=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key

# Verify the backup
$ ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot status /tmp/etcd-backup.db --write-table
+----------+----------+------------+------------+
|   HASH   | REVISION | TOTAL KEYS | TOTAL SIZE |
+----------+----------+------------+------------+
| abc12def |   150234 |       1847 |     4.2 MB |
+----------+----------+------------+------------+

# ═══════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 2: RESTORE — Stop API server, restore, restart
# ═══════════════════════════════════════
# On EACH control plane node:
$ mv /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml /tmp/

# Restore with a NEW data directory (don't overwrite!)
$ ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot restore /tmp/etcd-backup.db \
    --data-dir=/var/lib/etcd-restored \
    --name=cp-1 \
    --initial-cluster="cp-1=https://10.0.0.1:2380,cp-2=https://10.0.0.2:2380,cp-3=https://10.0.0.3:2380" \
    --initial-advertise-peer-urls=https://10.0.0.1:2380

# Update etcd manifest to point to new data dir
$ sed -i 's|/var/lib/etcd|/var/lib/etcd-restored|g' \
    /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml

# Restart API server
$ mv /tmp/kube-apiserver.yaml /etc/kubernetes/manifests/

etcd Backup Strategy — When & Where

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  etcd BACKUP BEST PRACTICES                                                  │
│                                                                              │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐               │
│  │  BACKUP SCHEDULE:                                         │               │
│  │                                                           │               │
│  │  CronJob every 30 min ──▶ /backups/etcd-{timestamp}.db   │               │
│  │  Keep last 48 backups (24 hours of 30-min snapshots)      │               │
│  │  Store offsite: S3 / Azure Blob / GCS bucket ✅           │               │
│  │  NEVER store backups only on the etcd nodes! ❌            │               │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘               │
│                                                                              │
│  RESTORE SCENARIOS:                                                          │
│  ┌───────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────┐               │
│  │ Scenario          │ Action                               │               │
│  ├───────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────┤               │
│  │ 1 etcd member     │ Remove & re-add member (simpler)   │               │
│  │ lost              │ etcdctl member add                   │               │
│  ├───────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────┤               │
│  │ Quorum lost       │ Full restore from snapshot on ALL    │               │
│  │ (majority down)   │ remaining members                    │               │
│  ├───────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────┤               │
│  │ Data corruption   │ Full restore from known-good         │               │
│  │                   │ snapshot + verify cluster health      │               │
│  ├───────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────┤               │
│  │ Disaster recovery │ Restore snapshot to new cluster +    │               │
│  │                   │ update certificates if needed         │               │
│  └───────────────────┴─────────────────────────────────────┘               │
│                                                                              │
│  ⚠️  REMEMBER: etcd restore creates a NEW cluster ID — all members          │
│     must restore from the SAME snapshot to form a consistent cluster!        │
│                                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

⚠️ Common Mistake: Forgetting TLS Flags

The #1 reason etcdctl commands fail on the CKA exam: missing the --cacert, --cert, and --key flags. These certs are always at /etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/. Also remember to set ETCDCTL_API=3 — version 2 has different commands!

35.5 Leader Election Deep Dive

🎯 CKA EXAM TOPIC

In an HA cluster, only one instance of the kube-scheduler and kube-controller-manager is actively making decisions at any time. This is managed through leader election using the Kubernetes Lease API.

Leader Election — How It Works

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  LEADER ELECTION MECHANISM                                                   │
│                                                                              │
│  Component        │ Active Instances │ Election Method                       │
│  ─────────────────┼──────────────────┼────────────────────────               │
│  kube-apiserver   │ ALL (active-     │ No election needed —                  │
│                   │ active)          │ stateless, LB distributes             │
│  ─────────────────┼──────────────────┼────────────────────────               │
│  kube-scheduler   │ ONE (leader)     │ Lease object in kube-system          │
│                   │                  │ --leader-elect=true                   │
│  ─────────────────┼──────────────────┼────────────────────────               │
│  kube-controller  │ ONE (leader)     │ Lease object in kube-system          │
│  -manager         │                  │ --leader-elect=true                   │
│  ─────────────────┼──────────────────┼────────────────────────               │
│  etcd             │ ALL (Raft        │ Raft protocol — internal             │
│                   │ consensus)       │ leader for write ordering            │
│                                                                              │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐            │
│  │  LEASE-BASED LEADER ELECTION FLOW:                          │            │
│  │                                                              │            │
│  │  1. All instances try to acquire a Lease object              │            │
│  │  2. Winner writes its identity + timestamp to Lease          │            │
│  │  3. Leader renews Lease every ~2 seconds (leaseDuration)     │            │
│  │  4. Other instances watch — if Lease expires, they compete   │            │
│  │  5. Fastest to write wins → new leader in ~15 seconds        │            │
│  │                                                              │            │
│  │  Timeline:                                                   │            │
│  │  ─────────── Leader CP-1 ──────────── ╳ (dies)              │            │
│  │  ───── standby CP-2 ────────────────── ★ new leader ──────  │            │
│  │  ───── standby CP-3 ────────────────── standby ───────────  │            │
│  │                               ~15s gap                       │            │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘            │
│                                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Inspecting Leader Election

Terminal — check current leaders 
# List all leases in kube-system
$ kubectl get lease -n kube-system
NAME                      HOLDER       AGE
kube-scheduler            cp-1_xxx     3d
kube-controller-manager   cp-1_xxx     3d

# Describe scheduler lease — see who holds it
$ kubectl describe lease kube-scheduler -n kube-system
Name:         kube-scheduler
Namespace:    kube-system
Spec:
  Holder Identity:    cp-1_xxxxxxxx-xxxx  ← THIS is the active leader
  Lease Duration:     15s
  Renew Time:         2024-03-15T10:45:32Z

# Check leader-elect flags in scheduler manifest
$ cat /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-scheduler.yaml | grep leader
    - --leader-elect=true    ← Enables leader election (default)
💡 What Happens During Leader Failover

When the leader dies, there's a brief gap (~15 seconds) before a new leader is elected. During this time, no new pods are scheduled and no controller actions happen (like scaling Deployments). Existing pods continue running — they don't depend on the scheduler or controller-manager once scheduled.

🏋️ Practice Drill — Leader Election Investigation

Scenario: Your team reports that new pods aren't being scheduled. You suspect the scheduler leader may have failed over. Investigate and verify.

  1. Check which node currently holds the scheduler lease:
    kubectl get lease kube-scheduler -n kube-system -o yaml
  2. Check the controller-manager lease as well:
    kubectl get lease kube-controller-manager -n kube-system -o jsonpath='{.spec.holderIdentity}'
  3. Verify the lease renewTime is recent (within last ~15 seconds). If stale → the leader may have crashed
  4. Check scheduler pod logs for election events:
    kubectl logs -n kube-system kube-scheduler-cp-1 | grep -i "leader\|elected\|lost"
  5. Verify all control plane nodes are healthy:
    kubectl get nodes -l node-role.kubernetes.io/control-plane

Expected outcome: You can identify which control plane node is the active leader and confirm the lease is being renewed.

35.6 Load Balancer Configuration for HA

The load balancer is the front door to your HA cluster. All kubectl commands, worker node registrations, and internal API calls go through it. If the LB fails, the entire cluster is unreachable — making the LB itself another critical HA component.

Load Balancer Health Check Flow

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  LOAD BALANCER HEALTH CHECK & ROUTING                                       │
│                                                                              │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────┐                                        │
│  │        kubectl / kubelet        │                                        │
│  │        All API requests         │                                        │
│  └───────────────┬─────────────────┘                                        │
│                  │                                                            │
│                  ▼ :6443                                                      │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────┐                                        │
│  │        LOAD BALANCER            │      Health Check:                      │
│  │      (HAProxy / nginx)          │      GET /healthz on :6443              │
│  │                                 │      Every 5 seconds                    │
│  │   Rules:                        │                                        │
│  │   - TCP mode (Layer 4)          │      Response 200 → Backend UP ✅      │
│  │   - Round-robin distribution    │      No response  → Remove from pool ❌│
│  │   - 3 failed checks → remove   │                                        │
│  │   - 2 passed checks → re-add   │                                        │
│  └──┬──────────┬──────────┬───────┘                                        │
│     │          │          │                                                  │
│     ▼          ▼          ▼                                                  │
│  ┌──────┐  ┌──────┐  ┌──────┐                                              │
│  │CP-1  │  │CP-2  │  │CP-3  │                                              │
│  │:6443 │  │:6443 │  │:6443 │                                              │
│  │ ✅   │  │ ✅   │  │ ❌   │  ← CP-3 failed health check                 │
│  └──────┘  └──────┘  └──────┘    → removed from pool                       │
│  ▲ 50%     ▲ 50%     (0%)       → traffic split between CP-1 & CP-2       │
│                                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

nginx Stream Proxy Configuration (Alternative to HAProxy)

/etc/nginx/nginx.conf — stream proxy for K8s API 
stream {
    upstream kubernetes_api {
        # Round-robin between control plane nodes
        server 10.0.0.1:6443 max_fails=3 fail_timeout=30s;
        server 10.0.0.2:6443 max_fails=3 fail_timeout=30s;
        server 10.0.0.3:6443 max_fails=3 fail_timeout=30s;
    }

    server {
        listen 6443;
        proxy_pass kubernetes_api;
        proxy_timeout 10m;
        proxy_connect_timeout 1s;
    }
}

⚠️ LB Single Point of Failure?

Yes — a single load balancer is itself a SPOF. In production, use keepalived + VRRP for LB failover (two LB hosts sharing a virtual IP), or use a cloud-managed load balancer (AWS NLB, Azure LB, GCP TCP LB) which is inherently HA.

keepalived + VRRP — Making the Load Balancer HA

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  LOAD BALANCER HIGH AVAILABILITY WITH keepalived                             │
│                                                                              │
│  Problem: Single LB = Single Point of Failure                                │
│  Solution: Two LBs sharing a Virtual IP (VIP) via VRRP protocol             │
│                                                                              │
│                     ┌──────────────┐                                        │
│   kubectl ────▶     │  VIP:        │     ← Clients connect to VIP           │
│   kubelet           │  10.0.0.100  │        NOT individual LB IPs            │
│                     └──────┬───────┘                                        │
│                            │                                                 │
│              ┌─────────────┴─────────────┐                                  │
│              │                           │                                   │
│     ┌────────▼────────┐        ┌────────▼────────┐                          │
│     │  LB-1 (MASTER)  │  VRRP  │  LB-2 (BACKUP)  │                          │
│     │  10.0.0.10      │◄──────▶│  10.0.0.11       │                          │
│     │  Owns VIP ✅    │ health  │  Standby ⏳      │                          │
│     │  Priority: 101  │ checks  │  Priority: 100   │                          │
│     └────────┬────────┘        └────────┬─────────┘                          │
│              │                           │                                   │
│              └─────────┬─┬───────────────┘                                  │
│                        │ │ │                                                 │
│                   CP-1 CP-2 CP-3                                             │
│                                                                              │
│  Failover Flow:                                                              │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐            │
│  │  1. LB-1 sends VRRP advertisements every 1 second          │            │
│  │  2. LB-2 monitors — if no VRRP from LB-1 for 3 seconds    │            │
│  │  3. LB-2 claims the VIP → sends gratuitous ARP             │            │
│  │  4. Traffic redirects to LB-2 in <5 seconds ✅            │            │
│  │  5. When LB-1 recovers → reclaims VIP (preempt mode)       │            │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘            │
│                                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

35.7 Production HA Checklist for anihpj

Here's everything needed to deploy the anihpj Django application on a production HA Kubernetes cluster. Use this as your go-live checklist.

anihpj Production HA Architecture

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  anihpj PRODUCTION HA DEPLOYMENT                                             │
│                                                                              │
│                        ┌───────────────┐                                    │
│    Users ──────▶       │  Ingress /    │                                    │
│                        │  Cloud LB     │                                    │
│                        └───────┬───────┘                                    │
│                                │                                             │
│               ┌────────────────┼────────────────┐                            │
│               ▼                ▼                ▼                            │
│  ┌────────────────┐ ┌────────────────┐ ┌────────────────┐                   │
│  │  anihpj-web    │ │  anihpj-web    │ │  anihpj-web    │                   │
│  │  replica 1     │ │  replica 2     │ │  replica 3     │                   │
│  │  (worker-1)    │ │  (worker-2)    │ │  (worker-3)    │                   │
│  └───────┬────────┘ └───────┬────────┘ └───────┬────────┘                   │
│          └──────────┬───────┘                   │                            │
│                     ▼                           ▼                            │
│          ┌────────────────────────────────────────────┐                      │
│          │  anihpj-db (PostgreSQL StatefulSet)        │                      │
│          │  Primary + Read Replica                     │                      │
│          │  PVC: 50Gi on fast-ssd StorageClass         │                      │
│          └────────────────────────────────────────────┘                      │
│                                                                              │
│  Infrastructure:                                                             │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐          │
│  │  3× Control Plane (HA)  │  3× Workers  │  HAProxy LB (HA)    │          │
│  │  etcd backup: 6h cycle  │  autoscaler  │  keepalived + VIP   │          │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘          │
│                                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Production Readiness Checklist

Category Requirement Status
Control Plane 3+ CP nodes across availability zones ✅ Required
etcd backup CronJob every 6 hours ✅ Required
Load balancer with health checks on /healthz ✅ Required
Workloads PodDisruptionBudget on anihpj (minAvailable: 2) ✅ Required
Anti-affinity: spread anihpj pods across nodes ✅ Required
Resource requests/limits on all containers ✅ Required
Liveness and readiness probes configured ✅ Required
Monitoring Alerts for node NotReady and pod crash loops ✅ Required
Central logging (Fluentd/Fluent Bit → ELK/Loki) ✅ Required
Security RBAC: least-privilege ServiceAccounts ✅ Required
NetworkPolicies: restrict inter-namespace traffic ✅ Required
Secrets encrypted at rest in etcd ✅ Required
Anti-Patterns ❌ Never run single-replica Deployments in prod ⚠️ Avoid
❌ Never run without resource limits ⚠️ Avoid
❌ Never skip PodDisruptionBudgets ⚠️ Avoid
anihpj-pdb.yaml — PodDisruptionBudget 
apiVersion: policy/v1
kind: PodDisruptionBudget
metadata:
  name: anihpj-web-pdb
  namespace: anihpj
spec:
  minAvailable: 2          # At least 2 pods must always be running
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj-web
---
# Anti-affinity in Deployment to spread pods across nodes
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj-web
  namespace: anihpj
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj-web
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj-web
    spec:
      affinity:
        podAntiAffinity:
          preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
          - weight: 100
            podAffinityTerm:
              labelSelector:
                matchExpressions:
                - key: app
                  operator: In
                  values:
                  - anihpj-web
              topologyKey: kubernetes.io/hostname
      containers:
      - name: anihpj
        image: anihpj/web:v2.0
        resources:
          requests:
            cpu: "200m"
            memory: "256Mi"
          limits:
            cpu: "500m"
            memory: "512Mi"
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /health/
            port: 8000
          initialDelaySeconds: 10
          periodSeconds: 15
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /ready/
            port: 8000
          initialDelaySeconds: 5
          periodSeconds: 10

✅ Chapter 35 Competency Checklist

  • Can you explain the difference between stacked and external etcd topologies?
  • Can you initialize a multi-master cluster with kubeadm init --control-plane-endpoint?
  • Can you calculate etcd quorum for any cluster size?
  • Can you find the current scheduler leader using kubectl get lease?
  • Can you configure HAProxy/nginx to load-balance API servers?
  • Can you set up PodDisruptionBudgets and anti-affinity for production workloads?

⚠️ CKA Gotcha: High Availability Traps

  • Stacked vs External etcd — Stacked: etcd runs on control plane nodes (simpler, fewer machines). External: etcd on dedicated nodes (better isolation, harder to set up). The CKA uses stacked topology. Know both for conceptual questions
