  • etcd quorum formula — (N/2) + 1 members must be alive. 3 nodes = tolerate 1 failure. 5 nodes = tolerate 2. Always use ODD numbers. Even numbers waste a node without improving fault tolerance
  • Leader election — Only ONE controller-manager and ONE scheduler are active at a time (the leader). Others are standby. Check leader: kubectl get lease -n kube-system. If the leader dies, a new one is elected in ~15s
  • --control-plane-endpoint is critical — Must point to a load balancer, not a single node IP. Without this, adding control plane nodes later fails. Set during kubeadm init and it CANNOT be changed after
  • Pod anti-affinity for HA workloads — Without podAntiAffinity, all replicas could land on the same node. If that node dies, 100% downtime. Always spread critical workloads across nodes

CKA Speed Tip — HA concepts to know:
kubectl get lease -n kube-system — see leader election leases
kubectl get endpoints -n kube-system kube-scheduler -o yaml — current leader annotation
Quorum table: 1 node=no HA, 3=tolerate 1, 5=tolerate 2, 7=tolerate 3
Rule: Never run production with fewer than 3 control plane nodes

⚡ HA inspection commands: kubectl get nodes -l node-role.kubernetes.io/control-plane — list control plane nodes
kubectl get lease -n kube-system — leader election status
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl member list — etcd cluster members
kubectl get pdb -A — verify PDBs protect critical workloads
kubectl get pod -o wide -l app=<name> — verify pods spread across nodes

📝 Chapter 35 — CKA Practice Questions

Q1
How many etcd nodes are needed to tolerate 2 node failures? Explain the quorum formula.
Show Answer
Answer
Total Nodes Quorum Needed Failures Tolerated Useful?
1 1 0 ❌ No HA
2 2 0 ❌ Worse than 1
3 2 1 ✅ Minimum HA
4 3 1 ⚠️ Wastes a node
5 3 2 ✅ Answer!
7 4 3 ✅ Large clusters 
# Formula: Quorum = (N / 2) + 1 (integer division)
# Failures tolerated = N - Quorum

# 5 nodes: Quorum = (5/2) + 1 = 3
# Failures = 5 - 3 = 2 ← Answer!

# Check etcd members:
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl member list \
  --endpoints=https://127.0.0.1:2379 \
  --cacert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt \
  --cert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt \
  --key=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key
Explanation

etcd uses Raft consensus: a majority (quorum) must agree on every write. Formula: quorum = ⌊N/2⌋ + 1. To tolerate 2 failures, you need 5 nodes (quorum of 3, so 2 can fail). Always use odd numbers — 4 nodes tolerates the same failures as 3 but costs more. On the CKA, know this table cold. Two nodes is WORSE than one because you need both alive for quorum.

Q2
What's the difference between stacked etcd and external etcd topologies?
Show Answer
Answer
Aspect Stacked etcd External etcd
Where etcd runs On control plane nodes On dedicated separate nodes
Min machines (HA) 3 (total) 6 (3 CP + 3 etcd)
Failure coupling Coupled: node failure loses both CP + etcd Decoupled: CP failure ≠ etcd failure
Complexity Simpler setup More complex
CKA exam ✅ Default (kubeadm) Know conceptually
kubeadm init flag (default) --external-etcd-endpoints 
# Stacked: etcd is a local static pod
ls /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml  # Exists on CP nodes

# External: API server points to remote etcd
grep etcd /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml
# --etcd-servers=https://etcd1:2379,https://etcd2:2379,https://etcd3:2379
Explanation

Stacked = etcd runs on the same nodes as the control plane (simpler, fewer machines). External = etcd on dedicated nodes (better isolation, requires more machines). The CKA uses stacked topology (kubeadm default). In stacked, losing a control plane node also loses an etcd member. In external, control plane and etcd fail independently, providing better resilience at the cost of complexity.

Q3
How do you check which node is the current leader for kube-controller-manager and kube-scheduler?
Show Answer
Answer
# Method 1: Check Lease objects (modern way)
kubectl get lease -n kube-system
# NAME                          HOLDER            AGE
# kube-controller-manager       master-1_abc123   30d
# kube-scheduler                master-2_def456   30d
# ← HOLDER shows which node is the current leader

# Method 2: Detailed lease info
kubectl describe lease kube-controller-manager -n kube-system
# Spec:
#   Holder Identity:   master-1_abc123
#   Lease Duration:    15s
#   Renew Time:        2024-01-15 10:30:00

# Method 3: Check endpoint annotations (legacy)
kubectl get endpoints kube-controller-manager -n kube-system -o yaml
# metadata:
#   annotations:
#     control-plane.alpha.kubernetes.io/leader:
#       holderIdentity: master-1_abc123

# How leader election works:
# 1. Multiple instances compete for a Lease object
# 2. Winner writes its identity + holds the lease
# 3. Winner renews every ~10s (leaseDuration: 15s)
# 4. If leader stops renewing, another takes over (~15s)
# 5. Only the leader actively processes work

# On each control plane node, check:
kubectl logs kube-scheduler-master-2 -n kube-system | head -5
# "successfully acquired lease kube-system/kube-scheduler"
# ← This node is the scheduler leader
Explanation

In HA clusters, only ONE controller-manager and ONE scheduler are active (the leader). Others are standby. Leaders use Lease objects (in kube-system) for election. The HOLDER field shows which node is leading. If the leader dies, a new one is elected within the lease duration (~15s). On the CKA, kubectl get lease -n kube-system is the fastest way to check.

Q4
What is the --control-plane-endpoint flag and why is it critical for HA?
Show Answer
Answer
# kubeadm init with HA setup:
kubeadm init \
  --control-plane-endpoint="lb.example.com:6443" \
  --upload-certs \
  --pod-network-cidr=192.168.0.0/16

# --control-plane-endpoint sets the API server address for ALL nodes
# Must be a load balancer or DNS name that fronts all CP nodes

# What it configures:
# 1. All kubeconfigs point to lb.example.com:6443
# 2. All kubelet configs use this address
# 3. Workers join via this address (not a single master IP)

# WITHOUT --control-plane-endpoint:
# kubeadm uses the first master's IP (e.g., 10.0.0.1:6443)
# If that master dies → ALL kubeconfigs point to dead IP
# Workers can't reach API server → cluster is down
# CANNOT be changed after kubeadm init!

# Load balancer options:
# - HAProxy (common for bare-metal)
# - nginx (also common)
# - Cloud LB (ELB, ALB, Azure LB)
# - keepalived + virtual IP

# Verify current endpoint:
kubectl cluster-info
# Kubernetes control plane is running at https://lb.example.com:6443

# Check kubeconfig:
cat ~/.kube/config | grep server
# server: https://lb.example.com:6443
Explanation

--control-plane-endpoint must be a load balancer address that distributes traffic across all control plane nodes. It's set during kubeadm init and is IMMUTABLE — changing it later requires rebuilding the cluster. Without it, all configs point to a single master IP, making HA impossible. On the CKA, know this conceptually: it ensures API access survives individual master node failures.

Q5
How do you ensure critical workloads are spread across nodes for high availability?
Show Answer
Answer
# Problem: All 3 replicas land on the same node
kubectl get pods -l app=web -o wide
# NAME       NODE
# web-abc    worker-1
# web-def    worker-1
# web-ghi    worker-1  ← All on one node! Not HA!

# Solution: Pod Anti-Affinity
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: web
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: web
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: web
    spec:
      affinity:
        podAntiAffinity:
          requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
          - labelSelector:
              matchExpressions:
              - key: app
                operator: In
                values:
                - web
            topologyKey: kubernetes.io/hostname
      containers:
      - name: nginx
        image: nginx

# Result: 1 pod per node
# web-abc  worker-1
# web-def  worker-2
# web-ghi  worker-3  ← Spread across nodes!

# Softer version (preferred, not required):
# preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution
# → Best effort spread, won't block scheduling

# Topology Spread Constraints (newer API):
spec:
  topologySpreadConstraints:
  - maxSkew: 1
    topologyKey: kubernetes.io/hostname
    whenUnsatisfiable: DoNotSchedule
    labelSelector:
      matchLabels:
        app: web
Explanation

Pod anti-affinity prevents pods with the same label from running on the same node. requiredDuringScheduling = hard rule (pod stays Pending if no unique node). preferredDuringScheduling = soft rule (best effort). topologyKey: kubernetes.io/hostname means one pod per node. On the CKA, you might need to add anti-affinity to ensure HA. topologySpreadConstraints is the newer, more flexible alternative.

Q6
How do you add a new control plane node to an existing HA cluster?
Show Answer
Answer
# Prerequisites:
# - Initial cluster created with --control-plane-endpoint
# - Load balancer configured for existing + new CP node
# - New node meets prerequisites (OS, container runtime, kubeadm)

# Step 1: On existing control plane, generate join command
kubeadm token create --print-join-command
# kubeadm join lb.example.com:6443 --token abc123 \
#   --discovery-token-ca-cert-hash sha256:xyz789

# Step 2: Upload certificates (need key for new CP node)
kubeadm init phase upload-certs --upload-certs
# [upload-certs] Using certificate key: abc123def456...

# Step 3: On new node, join as control plane
kubeadm join lb.example.com:6443 \
  --token abc123 \
  --discovery-token-ca-cert-hash sha256:xyz789 \
  --control-plane \
  --certificate-key abc123def456

# Step 4: Update load balancer
# Add new node's IP to the LB backend pool

# Step 5: Verify
kubectl get nodes
# NAME       STATUS  ROLES          VERSION
# master-1   Ready   control-plane  v1.31.0
# master-2   Ready   control-plane  v1.31.0
# master-3   Ready   control-plane  v1.31.0  ← New!

# Check etcd is healthy:
kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep etcd
# etcd-master-1  1/1  Running
# etcd-master-2  1/1  Running
# etcd-master-3  1/1  Running  ← New etcd member!
Explanation

Adding a control plane node uses kubeadm join with the --control-plane flag and --certificate-key to download shared certificates. The cluster must have been initialized with --control-plane-endpoint. On the CKA, know this workflow conceptually. The key steps: generate token, upload certs, join with control-plane flag, update load balancer. Stacked etcd automatically adds a new member when a control plane node joins.

Q7
What is a PodDisruptionBudget and how does it protect HA during maintenance?
Show Answer
Answer
# Create PDB imperatively:
kubectl create pdb web-pdb --selector=app=web --min-available=2

# OR declaratively:
apiVersion: policy/v1
kind: PodDisruptionBudget
metadata:
  name: web-pdb
spec:
  minAvailable: 2          # At least 2 pods must be running
  # OR: maxUnavailable: 1  # At most 1 pod can be down
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: web

# What PDB protects against:
# ✅ kubectl drain (node maintenance)
# ✅ Cluster autoscaler removing nodes
# ✅ kubeadm upgrade
# ❌ Does NOT protect against: node crash, OOMKill, pod deletion

# Without PDB (3 replicas, draining 2 nodes):
kubectl drain worker-1 --delete-emptydir-data --ignore-daemonsets
kubectl drain worker-2 --delete-emptydir-data --ignore-daemonsets
# Both succeed → 0 pods running → DOWNTIME!

# With PDB (minAvailable: 2):
kubectl drain worker-1  # Succeeds (2 pods remain on worker-2,3)
kubectl drain worker-2  # BLOCKS! "Cannot evict pod: would violate PDB"
# Service stays up with 2 pods ✓

# Check PDB status:
kubectl get pdb
# NAME     MIN-AVAILABLE  MAX-UNAVAILABLE  ALLOWED-DISRUPTIONS  AGE
# web-pdb  2              N/A              1                    5m
# ALLOWED-DISRUPTIONS: 1 = can safely evict 1 more pod
Explanation

PDB sets limits on voluntary disruptions (drain, upgrades, autoscaler). minAvailable = minimum pods that must stay running. maxUnavailable = maximum pods that can be down. PDB blocks drain commands that would violate the budget. On the CKA, create PDBs to protect critical workloads during node maintenance. kubectl create pdb is the fastest imperative method. PDB does NOT prevent involuntary disruptions (node crashes, OOMKill).

Q8
An HA cluster has 3 control plane nodes and one goes down. What happens?
Show Answer
Answer
# 3 CP nodes → Quorum = 2 → Can tolerate 1 failure ✓

# What happens when master-2 goes down:

# API Server:
# master-1 API server: ✅ Still serving requests
# master-3 API server: ✅ Still serving requests
# Load balancer routes to remaining 2 nodes
# kubectl commands still work (via LB)

# etcd:
# 3 members, 1 down → 2 alive → Quorum (2) met ✅
# Reads work, writes work
# "etcdctl member list" shows member as unreachable

# Controller Manager:
# If master-2 was the leader → new leader elected (~15s)
# If master-2 was standby → no impact
kubectl get lease kube-controller-manager -n kube-system
# HOLDER changes to master-1 or master-3

# Scheduler:
# Same as controller manager — leader election kicks in
kubectl get lease kube-scheduler -n kube-system

# Impact on workloads:
# ✅ Existing pods keep running (kubelet is independent)
# ✅ New pods can be created (scheduler on remaining CP)
# ✅ Self-healing works (controller-manager re-elected)
# ⚠️ But now: ONE MORE failure = total cluster loss!

# Action: Fix/replace master-2 ASAP
# 1. Fix the node if recoverable
# 2. OR remove and re-add: kubeadm reset + re-join
Explanation

With 3 CP nodes and 1 down: quorum is still met (2/3 alive), so the cluster functions normally. API server (via LB), etcd, controller-manager, and scheduler all continue. Leader election replaces the lost leader in ~15 seconds. Critical: you now have ZERO fault tolerance. If another node fails, you lose quorum and the cluster becomes read-only (no writes to etcd). Fix the failed node immediately.

Q9
How do you verify an HA cluster is properly configured?
Show Answer
Answer
# 1. Check all control plane nodes:
kubectl get nodes -l node-role.kubernetes.io/control-plane
# NAME       STATUS  ROLES          VERSION
# master-1   Ready   control-plane  v1.31.0
# master-2   Ready   control-plane  v1.31.0
# master-3   Ready   control-plane  v1.31.0  ✓ 3 nodes

# 2. Check all control plane pods running:
kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep -E "apiserver|etcd|scheduler|controller"
# All should show 1/1 Running for each master node

# 3. Check etcd cluster health:
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl endpoint health \
  --endpoints=https://master-1:2379,https://master-2:2379,https://master-3:2379 \
  --cacert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt \
  --cert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/healthcheck-client.crt \
  --key=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/healthcheck-client.key
# master-1:2379 is healthy
# master-2:2379 is healthy
# master-3:2379 is healthy  ✓

# 4. Check leader election:
kubectl get lease -n kube-system
# Controller-manager and scheduler should have HOLDERs ✓

# 5. Check API server endpoint:
kubectl cluster-info
# Should show LB address, not single node IP

# 6. Check PDBs for critical workloads:
kubectl get pdb -A
# Critical apps should have PDBs ✓

# 7. Test failover (carefully!):
# Simulate by stopping kubelet on one master
# Verify cluster still works after ~30s
Explanation

HA verification checklist: (1) 3+ control plane nodes all Ready, (2) all CP pods running (3 of each), (3) etcd cluster healthy, (4) leader election active, (5) API endpoint is a load balancer, (6) PDBs protect workloads. On the CKA, these verification steps help you understand what a working HA cluster looks like, which helps you diagnose when something is broken.

Q10
What is the role of the load balancer in an HA Kubernetes cluster?
Show Answer
Answer
# Load Balancer sits in front of ALL API servers:
#
#   kubectl → Load Balancer → API Server 1 (master-1)
#                           → API Server 2 (master-2)
#                           → API Server 3 (master-3)
#
# Workers also connect through the LB:
#   kubelet → Load Balancer → API Server (any)

# Configuration (HAProxy example):
# /etc/haproxy/haproxy.cfg
# frontend k8s-api
#   bind *:6443
#   default_backend k8s-api-backend
#
# backend k8s-api-backend
#   balance roundrobin
#   option httpchk GET /healthz
#   server master-1 10.0.0.1:6443 check
#   server master-2 10.0.0.2:6443 check
#   server master-3 10.0.0.3:6443 check

# What the LB does:
# ✅ Distributes API requests across CP nodes
# ✅ Health checks — removes dead nodes from rotation
# ✅ Single endpoint for all clients (kubectl, kubelet, etc.)
# ✅ Survives individual CP node failures

# What the LB does NOT do:
# ❌ Doesn't handle etcd traffic (etcd peers talk directly)
# ❌ Doesn't load balance pod-to-pod traffic (that's kube-proxy)
# ❌ Doesn't replace Ingress controllers

# LB is a SPOF — must also be HA in production:
# keepalived + virtual IP (active/passive)
# Cloud LB (inherently HA)
Explanation

The load balancer provides a single stable endpoint for the Kubernetes API, distributing requests across all control plane nodes. It's what --control-plane-endpoint points to. Health checks automatically remove unresponsive masters from the pool. On the CKA, understand that the LB only handles API server traffic (port 6443). etcd uses separate peer-to-peer communication. The LB itself must be HA in production (keepalived, cloud LB).

Q11
Write an HAProxy configuration for load balancing across 3 API servers. What health checks ensure traffic only goes to healthy masters?
Show Answer
Answer
# /etc/haproxy/haproxy.cfg

frontend kubernetes-api
    bind *:6443
    mode tcp
    option tcplog
    default_backend api-servers

backend api-servers
    mode tcp
    option tcp-check
    balance roundrobin
    # Health check: TCP connect to port 6443
    server master1 192.168.1.10:6443 check fall 3 rise 2 inter 5s
    server master2 192.168.1.11:6443 check fall 3 rise 2 inter 5s
    server master3 192.168.1.12:6443 check fall 3 rise 2 inter 5s

# Alternative: HTTP health check against /healthz
backend api-servers-http-check
    mode tcp
    option httpchk GET /healthz
    http-check expect status 200
    server master1 192.168.1.10:6443 check check-ssl verify none
    server master2 192.168.1.11:6443 check check-ssl verify none
    server master3 192.168.1.12:6443 check check-ssl verify none

# Health check explanation:
# check        → enable health checking
# fall 3       → mark down after 3 consecutive failures
# rise 2       → mark up after 2 consecutive successes
# inter 5s     → check every 5 seconds

# Start HAProxy:
sudo systemctl enable --now haproxy

# Verify:
curl -k https://<haproxy-ip>:6443/healthz
# ok

# kubeadm init with LB:
kubeadm init --control-plane-endpoint="<haproxy-ip>:6443" \
  --upload-certs
Explanation

The load balancer sits in front of all API servers and is the address all clients (kubectl, kubelet, kube-proxy) connect to. HAProxy in TCP mode passes through TLS without terminating it mode tcp. Health checks (fall 3 rise 2) automatically remove a crashed master from the rotation and re-add it when it recovers. This is what --control-plane-endpoint points to during kubeadm init. On the CKA, you won't configure HAProxy, but understanding that the LB is needed for HA and what port it forwards (6443 only) is essential for HA cluster questions.

Q12
What is the production readiness checklist for an HA Kubernetes cluster? List the key items you should verify before considering a cluster production-ready.
Show Answer
Answer
# Production HA Readiness Checklist:

# 1. Control Plane HA
kubectl get nodes | grep control-plane
# At least 3 control plane nodes (odd number for quorum)

# 2. etcd Cluster Health
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl endpoint health --cluster \
  --cacert=... --cert=... --key=...
# All members healthy, leader elected

# 3. Load Balancer
curl -k https://<lb-endpoint>:6443/healthz
# ok — LB routing to healthy API servers

# 4. Automated etcd Backups
kubectl get cronjob -n kube-system | grep etcd-backup
# Backup CronJob running on schedule

# 5. Pod Disruption Budgets
kubectl get pdb -A
# Critical workloads have PDBs preventing total downtime

# 6. Resource Requests & Limits
kubectl get pods -A -o json | jq '.items[] |
  select(.spec.containers[].resources.requests == null) |
  .metadata.name'
# No pods without resource requests (prevents scheduling chaos)

# 7. Node Redundancy
kubectl get nodes
# Multiple worker nodes across failure domains

# 8. Monitoring & Alerting
kubectl get pods -n monitoring
# Prometheus/Grafana or equivalent running

# 9. RBAC Configured
kubectl get clusterrolebindings | grep -v system
# Custom roles set up, no default service account abuse

# 10. Certificate Expiration
kubeadm certs check-expiration
# All certs valid, auto-renewal configured

# 11. Network Policies
kubectl get networkpolicies -A
# Default deny + explicit allow rules

# 12. Storage
kubectl get sc
# StorageClass with appropriate reclaim policy (Retain for prod)
Explanation

A production HA cluster needs more than just multiple masters. The key areas: (1) etcd quorum — odd number of members (3 or 5), (2) load balancer with health checks, (3) automated backups, (4) PDBs for critical workloads, (5) resource requests on all pods for proper scheduling, (6) certificate management, (7) RBAC and network policies. On the CKA, you're tested on individual pieces (etcd backup, PDBs, RBAC), but understanding how they fit together as a production readiness checklist shows mastery of the full stack.

Chapter 36: Cloud Kubernetes — EKS, GKE, AKS

Managed vs self-managed, cloud storage/networking, identity, deploying anihpj.

🔴 Advanced ⏱️ ~1.5 hours ☁️ Cloud 🎯 Production

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ Compare managed Kubernetes services — EKS, GKE, AKS features, pricing, and trade-offs
  • ✅ Map CKA concepts to cloud — which skills transfer directly and which differ
  • ✅ Configure cloud-native storage — StorageClass with CSI drivers for AWS, Azure, GCP
  • ✅ Integrate cloud identity — IRSA, Workload Identity, Managed Identity for pod-level access
  • ✅ Deploy anihpj to any cloud — complete walkthrough from cluster creation to running app
  • ✅ Configure Cluster Autoscaler — automatically scale nodes based on pod demand
CKA Exam Relevance
Cluster Architecture, Installation & Configuration — 25% Storage — 10%
🎯 CKA Exam Note

The CKA exam uses a vanilla Kubernetes cluster, not managed cloud. However, understanding cloud Kubernetes helps you recognize what kubeadm automates vs what cloud providers abstract away. These are skills you need the day after passing CKA.

36.1 Managed vs Self-Managed Kubernetes

CONCEPT

All three major cloud providers offer managed Kubernetes — they handle the control plane while you manage the worker nodes and workloads. Here's how they compare.

Feature EKS (AWS) GKE (Google) AKS (Azure)
Control Plane Managed ($0.10/hr) Managed (free tier avail.) Managed (free!)
Worker Nodes Self-managed / Fargate Standard / Autopilot VMSS node pools
Networking VPC CNI (AWS native) VPC-native / Calico Azure CNI / kubenet
Load Balancer ALB / NLB GCP TCP/HTTP LB Azure LB / App GW
Storage EBS CSI driver PD CSI driver Azure Disk CSI
Identity IAM Roles (IRSA) Workload Identity Managed Identity
CKA Relevance Concepts apply ✅ Concepts apply ✅ Concepts apply ✅

Managed vs Self-Managed — Responsibility Model

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  SELF-MANAGED (kubeadm)          │  CLOUD-MANAGED (EKS/GKE/AKS)           │
│                                   │                                         │
│  YOU manage:                      │  CLOUD manages:                         │
│  ┌─────────────────────────┐      │  ┌─────────────────────────┐           │
│  │  Control Plane           │      │  │  Control Plane    ☁️    │           │
│  │  ├── API Server          │      │  │  ├── API Server (HA)    │           │
│  │  ├── Scheduler           │      │  │  ├── Scheduler          │           │
│  │  ├── Controller Mgr      │      │  │  ├── Controller Mgr     │           │
│  │  ├── etcd (backup/HA)    │      │  │  └── etcd (auto-backup) │           │
│  │  └── Certificates        │      │  └─────────────────────────┘           │
│  ├─────────────────────────┤      │                                         │
│  │  Worker Nodes            │      │  YOU manage:                           │
│  │  ├── kubelet             │      │  ┌─────────────────────────┐           │
│  │  ├── kube-proxy          │      │  │  Worker Nodes            │           │
│  │  └── Container runtime   │      │  │  ├── Node pool config    │           │
│  ├─────────────────────────┤      │  │  └── Autoscaling rules   │           │
│  │  Networking              │      │  ├─────────────────────────┤           │
│  │  ├── CNI plugin          │      │  │  Workloads              │           │
│  │  └── Load Balancer       │      │  │  ├── Deployments         │           │
│  ├─────────────────────────┤      │  │  ├── Services             │           │
│  │  Upgrades                │      │  │  └── Config/Secrets      │           │
│  │  └── kubeadm upgrade     │      │  └─────────────────────────┘           │
│  └─────────────────────────┘      │                                         │
│                                   │  Upgrade: Click button / az aks upgrade │
│  Effort: ████████████ HIGH       │  Effort: ████░░░░░░░░ LOW               │
│                                   │                                         │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
🟠
AWS EKS

Control plane: $0.10/hr. Nodes: EC2 or Fargate (serverless). Identity: IRSA (IAM Roles for Service Accounts). Storage: EBS CSI. Best for: AWS-heavy orgs.

🔵
Google GKE

Control plane: Free (standard). Nodes: Standard or Autopilot. Identity: Workload Identity. Storage: PD CSI. Best for: K8s-native features first.

🔷
Azure AKS

Control plane: Free! Nodes: VMSS pools. Identity: Managed Identity. Storage: Azure Disk CSI. Best for: Azure/enterprise shops.

🔧 Self-Managed (kubeadm)

  • ✅ Full control over every component
  • ✅ Deep understanding of K8s internals
  • ✅ No vendor lock-in
  • ❌ You manage etcd, certs, upgrades, HA
  • ❌ Responsible for node provisioning
  • ❌ Must run your own monitoring/alerting
  • CKA: This is what the exam tests

☁️ Cloud-Managed (EKS/GKE/AKS)

  • ✅ Control plane fully managed & HA
  • ✅ Auto-rotating certs, auto-backup etcd
  • ✅ 1-click upgrades, cluster autoscaler
  • ✅ Integrated identity, storage, networking
  • ❌ Less visibility into control plane
  • ❌ Vendor-specific tooling and patterns
  • CKA: Where you’ll apply exam skills

36.2 CKA Concepts in Cloud Kubernetes

🎯 CKA EXAM TOPIC

The good news: most CKA skills transfer directly to cloud Kubernetes. Your kubectl commands, YAML manifests, and troubleshooting skills work everywhere. Here's exactly what changes and what stays the same.

Self-Managed vs Cloud-Managed — What Changes?

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  CKA SKILL                SELF-MANAGED     CLOUD-MANAGED                    │
│  ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────          │
│  kubeadm init/join         You do it ✋      Not needed ☁️                   │
│  etcd backup/restore       You do it ✋      Auto-managed ☁️                 │
│  Certificate rotation      You do it ✋      Auto-rotated ☁️                 │
│  Cluster upgrades          kubeadm ✋       Cloud console / CLI ☁️          │
│  Node scaling              Manual ✋         Cluster Autoscaler ☁️           │
│  ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────          │
│  kubectl commands          Same ✅           Same ✅                          │
│  YAML manifests            Same ✅           Same ✅                          │
│  RBAC                      Same ✅           Same ✅ + Cloud IAM             │
│  NetworkPolicies           Same ✅           Same ✅                          │
│  PV / PVC                  Same ✅           Same ✅ + Cloud Disks           │
│  Troubleshooting           Same ✅           Same ✅                          │
│  Pod scheduling            Same ✅           Same ✅                          │
│  Services / Ingress        Same ✅           Same ✅ + Cloud LB              │
│  Resource limits           Same ✅           Same ✅                          │
│  ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────          │
│                                                                              │
│  🔑  KEY INSIGHT: ~80% of CKA skills are IDENTICAL on cloud K8s.           │
│      The 20% that differ are infrastructure setup tasks that cloud          │
│      providers handle FOR you. That's WHY companies use managed K8s.        │
│                                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
💡 CKA to Cloud Translation

When you see a CKA task like "backup etcd" — on EKS/GKE/AKS, this is handled automatically. But the concept of "etcd holds cluster state, and losing it means losing the cluster" still applies. The CKA teaches you why things matter; cloud just automates the how.

36.3 Cloud-Native Storage and Networking

Cloud providers use CSI (Container Storage Interface) drivers to connect Kubernetes PersistentVolumes to cloud block storage. You write the same PVC YAML — only the StorageClass provisioner changes.

CSI Architecture — How Cloud Storage Plugs In

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  CSI (Container Storage Interface)                                           │
│                                                                              │
│  ┌──────────┐      ┌──────────────┐      ┌──────────────────┐              │
│  │  Pod      │      │  Kubernetes  │      │  Cloud Provider  │              │
│  │  (anihpj)│─────▶│  PVC → PV    │─────▶│  CSI Driver      │              │
│  └──────────┘      └──────────────┘      └────────┬─────────┘              │
│                                                    │                         │
│                                           ┌────────▼─────────┐              │
│                                           │  Cloud Disk API  │              │
│                                           │  (EBS / PD / AD) │              │
│                                           └──────────────────┘              │
│                                                                              │
│  Your YAML (same pattern):       StorageClass (cloud-specific):             │
│  ┌──────────────────────┐       ┌────────────────────────────────┐          │
│  │ apiVersion: v1        │       │ provisioner:                   │          │
│  │ kind: PVC             │       │   AWS:   ebs.csi.aws.com      │          │
│  │ spec:                 │       │   GCP:   pd.csi.storage.gke.io│          │
│  │   storageClass: fast  │       │   Azure: disk.csi.azure.com   │          │
│  │   resources:          │       │ parameters:                    │          │
│  │     requests:         │       │   type: gp3 / pd-ssd / Premium│          │
│  │       storage: 20Gi   │       └────────────────────────────────┘          │
│  └──────────────────────┘                                                   │
│                                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

StorageClass Configurations by Cloud Provider

storageclass-aws.yaml — AWS EBS StorageClass 
apiVersion: storage.k8s.io/v1
kind: StorageClass
metadata:
  name: fast-ebs
provisioner: ebs.csi.aws.com      # AWS EBS CSI driver
parameters:
  type: gp3                        # General Purpose SSD (gp3)
  encrypted: "true"                # Encrypt at rest
reclaimPolicy: Delete
volumeBindingMode: WaitForFirstConsumer  # Schedule-aware binding
allowVolumeExpansion: true
storageclass-azure.yaml — Azure Disk StorageClass 
apiVersion: storage.k8s.io/v1
kind: StorageClass
metadata:
  name: fast-azure
provisioner: disk.csi.azure.com    # Azure Disk CSI driver
parameters:
  skuName: Premium_LRS             # Premium SSD
  cachingmode: ReadOnly
reclaimPolicy: Delete
volumeBindingMode: WaitForFirstConsumer
allowVolumeExpansion: true
storageclass-gcp.yaml — GCE Persistent Disk StorageClass 
apiVersion: storage.k8s.io/v1
kind: StorageClass
metadata:
  name: fast-gce
provisioner: pd.csi.storage.gke.io  # GCE PD CSI driver
parameters:
  type: pd-ssd                       # SSD persistent disk
reclaimPolicy: Delete
volumeBindingMode: WaitForFirstConsumer
allowVolumeExpansion: true
🎯 CKA Exam Relevance

The CKA tests you on PersistentVolume, PersistentVolumeClaim, and StorageClass concepts — the YAML structure is identical across all providers. Know the volumeBindingMode: WaitForFirstConsumer pattern — it ensures the volume is created in the same zone as the pod.

36.4 Cloud Identity Integration

In cloud Kubernetes, pods can assume cloud IAM roles without storing credentials. This is the production-grade way to give your application access to cloud resources like databases, storage buckets, and message queues.

Pod → ServiceAccount → Cloud IAM Role → Cloud Resource

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  CLOUD IDENTITY INTEGRATION — How Pods Access Cloud Resources               │
│                                                                              │
│  ❌ BAD: Hardcoded credentials (Secret with access keys)                    │
│  ┌──────┐   Secret: AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID      ┌──────────┐                    │
│  │ Pod  │──────────────────────────────────▶│ S3 Bucket│  ← Key leaked!    │
│  └──────┘                                   └──────────┘                    │
│                                                                              │
│  ✅ GOOD: IAM Role via ServiceAccount (no credentials stored)              │
│                                                                              │
│  ┌──────┐    ┌──────────────┐    ┌─────────────┐    ┌──────────┐          │
│  │ Pod  │───▶│ K8s Service  │───▶│ Cloud IAM   │───▶│ Cloud    │          │
│  │      │    │ Account      │    │ Role         │    │ Resource │          │
│  └──────┘    └──────────────┘    └─────────────┘    └──────────┘          │
│                                                                              │
│  How Each Cloud Does It:                                                     │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐       │
│  │  AWS (IRSA):                                                     │       │
│  │  Pod → ServiceAccount → IAM Role via OIDC → AWS Resources       │       │
│  │  (annotation: eks.amazonaws.com/role-arn)                        │       │
│  │                                                                  │       │
│  │  Azure (Workload Identity):                                      │       │
│  │  Pod → ServiceAccount → Managed Identity → Azure Resources      │       │
│  │  (annotation: azure.workload.identity/client-id)                │       │
│  │                                                                  │       │
│  │  GCP (Workload Identity):                                        │       │
│  │  Pod → KSA → GSA binding → GCP Resources                        │       │
│  │  (annotation: iam.gke.io/gcp-service-account)                   │       │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘       │
│                                                                              │
│  anihpj Example:                                                             │
│  Pod (anihpj-web) → SA (anihpj-sa) → IAM Role → Cloud Database Access     │
│  No passwords in YAML! No secrets to rotate! ✅                              │
│                                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
💡 Why This Matters for anihpj

Instead of storing database passwords in Kubernetes Secrets, anihpj pods can authenticate to a cloud-managed PostgreSQL database using IAM roles. The ServiceAccount annotation is the only config needed — no credentials ever appear in your YAML manifests or container environment.

ServiceAccount Annotations — Per Cloud Provider

serviceaccount.yaml — Cloud IAM integration (all 3 providers) 
# ═══════════════════════════════════════
# AWS EKS — IRSA (IAM Roles for Service Accounts)
# ═══════════════════════════════════════
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: anihpj-sa
  namespace: anihpj
  annotations:
    # This links the K8s SA to an AWS IAM Role
    eks.amazonaws.com/role-arn: arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/anihpj-db-role
---
# ═══════════════════════════════════════
# Azure AKS — Workload Identity
# ═══════════════════════════════════════
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: anihpj-sa
  namespace: anihpj
  annotations:
    azure.workload.identity/client-id: "abc-def-123-456"
  labels:
    azure.workload.identity/use: "true"    # Required label!
---
# ═══════════════════════════════════════
# GCP GKE — Workload Identity Federation
# ═══════════════════════════════════════
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: anihpj-sa
  namespace: anihpj
  annotations:
    iam.gke.io/gcp-service-account: anihpj-app@project-id.iam.gserviceaccount.com
deployment.yaml — Using the annotated ServiceAccount 
# Reference the SA in your Deployment — that's it!
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj-web
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      serviceAccountName: anihpj-sa    # ← Pod inherits cloud IAM role
      containers:
      - name: anihpj
        image: anihpj/web:v2.1
        env:
        - name: DATABASE_URL
          # No password needed — IAM auth handles it!
          value: "postgresql://anihpj-db.cluster-xyz.us-east-1.rds.amazonaws.com/anihpj"
🎯 CKA Focus — ServiceAccounts

The CKA exam tests serviceAccountName in pod specs. While cloud IAM annotations are provider-specific and not directly tested, understanding how ServiceAccounts bind to pods and that automountServiceAccountToken: false restricts API access is core CKA knowledge.

36.5 Deploying anihpj to Cloud Kubernetes

The same Kubernetes manifests you use locally work on cloud. The differences are how you create the cluster and how you connect kubectl. Here's the complete walkthrough for all three clouds.

Cloud Deployment Flow — 4 Steps on Any Cloud

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  DEPLOYMENT FLOW (same for all clouds)                                       │
│                                                                              │
│  Step 1               Step 2               Step 3              Step 4       │
│  ┌─────────────┐     ┌─────────────┐     ┌──────────────┐   ┌───────────┐ │
│  │ Create      │     │ Configure   │     │ Apply same   │   │ Expose    │ │
│  │ Managed     │────▶│ kubectl     │────▶│ YAML as      │──▶│ via Cloud │ │
│  │ Cluster     │     │ Context     │     │ local env    │   │ LB/Ingress│ │
│  └─────────────┘     └─────────────┘     └──────────────┘   └───────────┘ │
│                                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Step 1 & 2: Create Cluster + Configure kubectl

Terminal — create cluster and connect (all 3 clouds) 
# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# AWS EKS
# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════
$ eksctl create cluster --name anihpj-prod --region us-east-1 --nodes 3
$ aws eks update-kubeconfig --name anihpj-prod --region us-east-1

# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Azure AKS
# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════
$ az aks create --resource-group anihpj-rg --name anihpj-prod \
    --node-count 3 --generate-ssh-keys
$ az aks get-credentials --resource-group anihpj-rg --name anihpj-prod

# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Google GKE
# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════
$ gcloud container clusters create anihpj-prod --zone us-central1-a --num-nodes 3
$ gcloud container clusters get-credentials anihpj-prod --zone us-central1-a

# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Verify connection (same for ALL clouds!)
# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════
$ kubectl get nodes
NAME                  STATUS   ROLES   AGE   VERSION
node-pool-xxxx-001    Ready    <none>  2m    v1.29.0
node-pool-xxxx-002    Ready    <none>  2m    v1.29.0
node-pool-xxxx-003    Ready    <none>  2m    v1.29.0

Step 3 & 4: Deploy anihpj (Same YAML, Any Cloud)

Terminal — deploy anihpj to cloud cluster 
# Same commands whether on EKS, AKS, or GKE!
$ kubectl create namespace anihpj
$ kubectl apply -f anihpj-deployment.yaml -n anihpj
$ kubectl apply -f anihpj-service.yaml -n anihpj

# Expose via cloud LoadBalancer (auto-provisions cloud LB)
$ kubectl expose deployment anihpj-web --type=LoadBalancer \
    --port=80 --target-port=8000 -n anihpj

# Wait for external IP
$ kubectl get svc -n anihpj -w
NAME          TYPE           CLUSTER-IP    EXTERNAL-IP      PORT(S)
anihpj-web    LoadBalancer   10.100.0.52   52.186.xxx.xxx   80:31234/TCP

# Test it! 🎉
$ curl http://52.186.xxx.xxx/
<!DOCTYPE html><html>...anihpj is running!...
🎯 Focus on CKA Core Concepts

The CKA exam won't ask you to create an EKS/AKS/GKE cluster. But it will test Deployments, Services, PVCs, and troubleshooting — which are identical on cloud and on-prem. Master the core concepts, and they transfer everywhere.

36.6 Cluster Autoscaler

The Cluster Autoscaler automatically adjusts the number of worker nodes in your cluster. When pods can't be scheduled (no node has enough resources), it adds nodes. When nodes are underutilized for 10+ minutes, it removes them.

Cluster Autoscaler Decision Flow

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  CLUSTER AUTOSCALER — HOW IT WORKS                                          │
│                                                                              │
│  SCALE UP:                                                                  │
│  ┌────────────────────┐                                                     │
│  │ New pod created    │                                                     │
│  │ (e.g. HPA scales  │                                                     │
│  │  anihpj to 10)    │                                                     │
│  └────────┬───────────┘                                                     │
│           │                                                                  │
│           ▼                                                                  │
│  ┌────────────────────┐     ┌────────────────────┐                          │
│  │ Scheduler tries to │ NO  │ Pod stays PENDING   │                          │
│  │ place pod on node  │────▶│ (Insufficient CPU/  │                          │
│  └────────────────────┘     │  Memory)             │                          │
│                              └────────┬───────────┘                          │
│                                       │                                      │
│                                       ▼                                      │
│                              ┌────────────────────┐                          │
│                              │ Cluster Autoscaler  │                          │
│                              │ detects pending pod  │                          │
│                              └────────┬───────────┘                          │
│                                       │                                      │
│                                       ▼                                      │
│                              ┌────────────────────┐                          │
│                              │ Cloud API: Create   │                          │
│                              │ new node instance   │                          │
│                              └────────┬───────────┘                          │
│                                       │ (~2-5 min)                           │
│                                       ▼                                      │
│                              ┌────────────────────┐                          │
│                              │ Node Ready → Pod    │                          │
│                              │ scheduled ✅         │                          │
│                              └────────────────────┘                          │
│                                                                              │
│  SCALE DOWN:                                                                │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐             │
│  │  Node utilization < 50% for 10 minutes                     │             │
│  │       │                                                     │             │
│  │       ▼                                                     │             │
│  │  Check: Can all pods be rescheduled to other nodes?        │             │
│  │       │                                                     │             │
│  │       ▼  YES                                                │             │
│  │  Cordon → Drain → Terminate node instance                  │             │
│  │  (Respects PDB! Won't violate minAvailable)                │             │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘             │
│                                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

⚠️ Autoscaler & PodDisruptionBudgets

The Cluster Autoscaler respects PDBs during scale-down. If removing a node would violate a PodDisruptionBudget (e.g., anihpj's minAvailable: 2), the node won't be removed. This is why PDBs are important even with autoscaling!

HPA + Cluster Autoscaler — The Two-Level Scaling Loop

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  TWO-LEVEL AUTOSCALING — HPA (pods) + CA (nodes)                            │
│                                                                              │
│  Traffic increases → CPU hits 80% threshold                                  │
│                                                                              │
│  ┌─── Level 1: HPA (Horizontal Pod Autoscaler) ─────────────────────────┐   │
│  │                                                                       │   │
│  │  anihpj Deployment: replicas 3 → 5 → 8 → 12                         │   │
│  │                                                                       │   │
│  │  ┌───┐ ┌───┐ ┌───┐ ┌───┐ ┌───┐ ┌───┐ ┌───┐ ┌───┐ ┌───┐ ... ┌───┐ │   │
│  │  │Pod│ │Pod│ │Pod│ │Pod│ │Pod│ │Pod│ │Pod│ │Pod│ │???│     │???│ │   │
│  │  └───┘ └───┘ └───┘ └───┘ └───┘ └───┘ └───┘ └───┘ └───┘     └───┘ │   │
│  │  ──────── running ────────  ──scheduled──  ─── PENDING! ───────── │   │
│  │                                             no room on existing    │   │
│  │                                             nodes → triggers CA    │   │
│  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│                              │                                           │
│                              ▼                                           │
│  ┌─── Level 2: Cluster Autoscaler (CA) ──────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │                                                                    │  │
│  │  Node pool: 3 nodes → 4 nodes → 5 nodes                          │  │
│  │                                                                    │  │
│  │  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────┐  ┌─────┐│  │
│  │  │ Node-1   │  │ Node-2   │  │ Node-3   │  │ Node-4   │  │ N-5 ││  │
│  │  │ 4 pods   │  │ 4 pods   │  │ 4 pods   │  │ 2 pods   │  │ NEW ││  │
│  │  │ ████████ │  │ ████████ │  │ ████████ │  │ ████░░░░ │  │ ... ││  │
│  │  └──────────┘  └──────────┘  └──────────┘  └──────────┘  └─────┘│  │
│  │                                                                    │  │
│  │  CA calls cloud API → provisions new node → ~2-5 min warm-up     │  │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                                                                          │
│  SCALING TIMELINE:                                                       │
│  ─────┬───────────┬───────────────┬──────────────────┬──────────        │
│   0s  │ HPA       │ HPA creates   │ CA provisions    │ All pods         │
│       │ detects   │ new pods      │ new node         │ running ✅       │
│       │ high CPU  │ (15-30s)      │ (2-5 min)        │                  │
│  ─────┴───────────┴───────────────┴──────────────────┴──────────        │
│                                                                          │
│  KEY INSIGHT: HPA scales pods FAST (seconds), CA scales nodes SLOW      │
│  (minutes). Always ensure enough headroom or use "pause pods" to        │
│  pre-provision node capacity for faster scaling.                         │
│                                                                          │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
hpa-anihpj.yaml — HPA triggers pod scaling, which triggers CA 
apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: anihpj-web-hpa
  namespace: anihpj
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: anihpj-web
  minReplicas: 3            # Minimum for HA
  maxReplicas: 20           # Max pods — budget limit
  metrics:
  - type: Resource
    resource:
      name: cpu
      target:
        type: Utilization
        averageUtilization: 70   # Scale when CPU > 70%
  - type: Resource
    resource:
      name: memory
      target:
        type: Utilization
        averageUtilization: 80   # Also scale on memory pressure
  behavior:
    scaleUp:
      stabilizationWindowSeconds: 60    # Wait 1 min before scaling up
      policies:
      - type: Pods
        value: 4
        periodSeconds: 60               # Add max 4 pods per minute
    scaleDown:
      stabilizationWindowSeconds: 300   # Wait 5 min before scaling down
      policies:
      - type: Percent
        value: 25
        periodSeconds: 60               # Remove max 25% per minute
💡 Pause Pods — Pre-warming Node Capacity

Node provisioning takes 2-5 minutes. To avoid cold-start delays, some teams deploy "pause pods" — low-priority pods that reserve node capacity but do nothing. When real pods need space, the pause pods are evicted (they have PriorityClass: low), and real pods are scheduled instantly. The CA then provisions new nodes to replace the displaced pause pods in the background.

✅ Chapter 36 Competency Checklist

  • Can you explain the difference between managed and self-managed Kubernetes?
  • Can you identify which CKA skills transfer directly to cloud K8s?
  • Can you write a cloud-provider StorageClass with CSI provisioner?
  • Can you explain how pods access cloud resources via IAM without secrets?
  • Can you create a managed cluster and deploy workloads on any of the 3 clouds?
  • Can you describe how the Cluster Autoscaler scales nodes up and down?

⚠️ CKA Gotcha: Cloud Kubernetes Traps

  • CKA is cloud-agnostic — The exam tests vanilla Kubernetes (kubeadm clusters), NOT EKS/GKE/AKS specifics. But understanding cloud vs self-managed helps you know WHAT the CKA is actually testing (the control plane parts cloud providers hide)
  • StorageClass differences — Cloud clusters have default StorageClasses (gp2, standard, default). CKA kubeadm clusters may NOT have a default. Always check: kubectl get sc. If no (default) annotation, PVCs without storageClassName stay Pending
  • LoadBalancer Service type — Only works on cloud. On kubeadm/bare-metal, type: LoadBalancer stays Pending with no EXTERNAL-IP. Use NodePort or install MetalLB. The CKA exam uses NodePort
  • Cloud IAM vs Kubernetes RBAC — Cloud providers use their own IAM (AWS IAM, GCP IAM, Azure AD) to control WHO can access the cluster. Inside the cluster, Kubernetes RBAC controls WHAT they can do. Both layers matter
  • Cluster Autoscaler vs HPA — HPA scales pods. CA scales nodes (when pods are Pending due to insufficient resources). They work together: HPA creates more pods → pods go Pending → CA adds nodes → pods get scheduled

CKA Speed Tip — CKA skills that transfer to cloud:
✅ RBAC, NetworkPolicies, PV/PVC, ConfigMaps/Secrets — identical across all platforms
✅ Deployments, Services, Ingress, DaemonSets — same YAML everywhere
✅ Troubleshooting (logs, describe, exec) — universal toolkit
❌ kubeadm, etcd backup, static pods, certificate management — cloud providers handle these
The CKA tests everything including what cloud hides from you.

⚡ Cloud-relevant commands: kubectl get sc — check available StorageClasses and default
kubectl get svc -A — check for LoadBalancer services and their EXTERNAL-IP
kubectl top nodes — capacity planning (works on cloud and bare-metal with metrics-server)
kubectl get hpa -A — list HorizontalPodAutoscalers
kubectl autoscale deployment <name> --min=2 --max=10 --cpu-percent=70 — create HPA
1
Create Cluster

AWS: eksctl create cluster | GCP: gcloud container clusters create | Azure: az aks create

2
Get Credentials

aws eks update-kubeconfig | gcloud container clusters get-credentials | az aks get-credentials

3
Deploy Workloads

Standard kubectl from here — kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml. Same YAML works everywhere.

4
Expose Service

kubectl expose with type: LoadBalancer — cloud auto-provisions an external load balancer with public IP.

5
Enable Autoscaling

HPA for pods: kubectl autoscale. Cluster Autoscaler for nodes: deploy CA or enable via cloud CLI.

📝 Chapter 36 — CKA Practice Questions

Q1
What Kubernetes components does a managed service (EKS/GKE/AKS) handle vs what you manage?
Show Answer
Answer
Component Managed (EKS/GKE/AKS) Self-Managed (kubeadm)
API Server ☁️ Cloud manages 🔧 You manage
etcd ☁️ Cloud manages 🔧 You manage
Controller Manager ☁️ Cloud manages 🔧 You manage
Scheduler ☁️ Cloud manages 🔧 You manage
Certificates ☁️ Cloud manages 🔧 You manage
Worker Nodes 🔧 You manage* 🔧 You manage
Pods/Deployments 🔧 You manage 🔧 You manage
RBAC/NetworkPolicy 🔧 You manage 🔧 You manage
Upgrades ☁️ CP auto / 🔧 workers you 🔧 You manage everything

* Fargate/Autopilot = Cloud also manages workers

Explanation

Managed services handle the control plane (API server, etcd, scheduler, controller-manager, certificates). You handle workloads, RBAC, networking, and typically worker nodes. The CKA tests everything including what cloud hides: etcd backup, kubeadm upgrades, certificate management, static pod manifests. Understanding this split helps you know exactly why the CKA matters.

Q2
A PVC stays in Pending state on a kubeadm cluster. How does this differ from cloud and how do you fix it?
Show Answer
Answer
# Check PVC status:
kubectl get pvc
# NAME     STATUS   VOLUME  CAPACITY  ACCESS  SC       AGE
# data     Pending                            (none)   5m

# Why it's Pending:
kubectl describe pvc data | tail -10
# "no persistent volumes available for this claim"

# Cloud vs kubeadm differences:
# Cloud: Default StorageClass auto-provisions PVs
# kubeadm: NO default StorageClass → PVCs without SC stay Pending

# Step 1: Check StorageClasses
kubectl get sc
# (empty) ← No StorageClass!

# On cloud, you'd see:
# gp2 (default)  OR  standard (default)

# Fix Option 1: Create a PV manually
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolume
metadata:
  name: data-pv
spec:
  capacity:
    storage: 1Gi
  accessModes:
  - ReadWriteOnce
  hostPath:
    path: /data/pv
  persistentVolumeReclaimPolicy: Retain

# Fix Option 2: Specify storageClassName in PVC
# and create a matching StorageClass

# Fix Option 3: Create PV + PVC with matching labels
# PV: storageClassName: manual
# PVC: storageClassName: manual

# Verify:
kubectl get pvc
# NAME  STATUS  VOLUME   CAPACITY  SC      AGE
# data  Bound   data-pv  1Gi       manual  1m  ✓
Explanation

On cloud, StorageClasses with dynamic provisioning auto-create PVs. On kubeadm/bare-metal, you usually need to create PVs manually. CKA uses kubeadm, so expect to create PVs with hostPath. Common trap: PVC with no storageClassName looks for the default SC — if none exists, it stays Pending forever. Always check kubectl get sc first.

Q3
How do you create a HorizontalPodAutoscaler (HPA) and what prerequisites does it need?
Show Answer
Answer
# Prerequisite 1: Metrics Server must be installed
kubectl top pods  # If this works, metrics-server is ready

# Prerequisite 2: Pods must have resource REQUESTS defined
# HPA compares actual usage vs requested

# Create HPA imperatively:
kubectl autoscale deployment web \
  --min=2 --max=10 --cpu-percent=70
# Scales up when avg CPU > 70% of requests
# Scales down when avg CPU < 70%

# Check HPA:
kubectl get hpa
# NAME  REFERENCE        TARGETS        MINPODS  MAXPODS  REPLICAS
# web   Deployment/web   45%/70%        2        10       3
#                        ↑ current/target

# Declarative:
apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: web
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: web
  minReplicas: 2
  maxReplicas: 10
  metrics:
  - type: Resource
    resource:
      name: cpu
      target:
        type: Utilization
        averageUtilization: 70

# If TARGETS shows <unknown>/70%:
# → Metrics Server not installed
# OR → Pod has no resource requests defined!
# Fix: Add requests to pod spec
resources:
  requests:
    cpu: 100m
    memory: 128Mi
Explanation

HPA requires: (1) Metrics Server installed, (2) pods have CPU/memory requests defined. Without requests, HPA can't calculate percentage. kubectl autoscale is the imperative shortcut. If TARGETS shows <unknown>, check Metrics Server and resource requests. On the CKA, HPA is a practical topic — know how to create it imperatively and what the prerequisites are.

Q4
A LoadBalancer Service shows Pending EXTERNAL-IP on a kubeadm cluster. Why and what's the alternative?
Show Answer
Answer
# Check Service:
kubectl get svc web-lb
# NAME     TYPE          CLUSTER-IP    EXTERNAL-IP  PORT(S)
# web-lb   LoadBalancer  10.96.50.100  <pending>    80:31234/TCP

# Why Pending?
# LoadBalancer type requires a cloud provider's LB controller
# kubeadm has NO cloud LB controller → stays Pending forever

# Alternative 1: Use NodePort
kubectl expose deployment web --type=NodePort --port=80
kubectl get svc web
# NAME  TYPE      CLUSTER-IP   EXTERNAL-IP  PORT(S)
# web   NodePort  10.96.1.100  <none>       80:30080/TCP
# Access: http://<any-node-ip>:30080

# Alternative 2: Convert existing LB to NodePort
kubectl patch svc web-lb -p '{"spec":{"type":"NodePort"}}'

# Alternative 3: Install MetalLB (bare-metal LB)
# MetalLB assigns real IPs to LoadBalancer services
# Not CKA scope but useful to know

# Alternative 4: Use Ingress (for HTTP/HTTPS)
# Ingress controller (nginx) with NodePort
# Routes traffic by hostname/path

# CKA exam: NodePort is the expected answer
# Port range: 30000-32767 (default)
kubectl create svc nodeport web --tcp=80:80 --node-port=30080
Explanation

type: LoadBalancer needs a cloud provider's load balancer integration. On kubeadm/bare-metal, it stays Pending permanently. The CKA expects you to use NodePort instead (port range 30000-32767). NodePort makes the service accessible on every node's IP at the allocated port. MetalLB can provide LoadBalancer functionality on bare-metal but is not CKA scope.

Q5
What CKA skills transfer directly to cloud Kubernetes (EKS/GKE/AKS)?
Show Answer
Answer
Skill Category Transfers to Cloud? Notes
Pods, Deployments, Services ✅ 100% Identical YAML everywhere
RBAC, NetworkPolicy ✅ 100% Core Kubernetes API
ConfigMaps, Secrets ✅ 100% Same everywhere
PV/PVC, StorageClass ✅ 95% SC names differ per cloud
Troubleshooting (logs, describe) ✅ 100% Universal debugging toolkit
kubeadm, etcd backup ❌ No Cloud handles these
Certificate management ❌ No Cloud handles these
Static pod manifests ❌ No Cloud abstracts CP
Explanation

Most CKA skills (workloads, RBAC, networking, troubleshooting, storage) transfer directly to cloud. What doesn't transfer: kubeadm operations, etcd management, certificate handling, and static pod manifests — cloud providers handle these. The CKA tests BOTH: the universal Kubernetes skills you'll use daily AND the infrastructure skills that cloud hides but are essential for understanding how Kubernetes works.

Q6
How does cloud IAM interact with Kubernetes RBAC?
Show Answer
Answer
# Two authentication layers:

# Layer 1: Cloud IAM (WHO can access the cluster)
# AWS:   IAM Users/Roles → aws-auth ConfigMap → K8s users
# GCP:   GCP IAM → RBAC bindings
# Azure: Azure AD → AKS integration

# Layer 2: Kubernetes RBAC (WHAT they can do inside)
# Roles/ClusterRoles → RoleBindings/ClusterRoleBindings

# Flow:
# Developer → Cloud IAM (authenticated) → API Server → RBAC (authorized)
#                  ↓                                        ↓
#          "Yes, this is user1"              "user1 can list pods in dev ns"

# CKA focuses on Layer 2 (RBAC):
# Create Role:
kubectl create role pod-viewer -n dev \
  --verb=get,list,watch --resource=pods

# Create RoleBinding:
kubectl create rolebinding user1-view -n dev \
  --role=pod-viewer --user=user1

# Verify:
kubectl auth can-i list pods -n dev --as=user1
# yes ✓

kubectl auth can-i delete pods -n dev --as=user1
# no ← Only get,list,watch allowed

# On kubeadm (CKA exam):
# Authentication via X.509 certificates
# Certificate CN = username, O = group
# Then RBAC authorizes the authenticated user
Explanation

Cloud IAM handles authentication (WHO are you?), Kubernetes RBAC handles authorization (WHAT can you do?). Both must pass. On cloud: cloud IAM authenticates, then K8s RBAC authorizes. On kubeadm (CKA): X.509 certificates authenticate (CN=username, O=group), then RBAC authorizes. The CKA tests RBAC extensively — creating Roles, ClusterRoles, RoleBindings, ClusterRoleBindings, and verifying with kubectl auth can-i.

Q7
How does the Cluster Autoscaler differ from HPA and when do they work together?
Show Answer
Answer
# HPA: Scales PODS (horizontal pod autoscaler)
# CA:  Scales NODES (cluster autoscaler)

# HPA alone:
# Traffic increases → HPA adds replicas → BUT...
# If no node capacity → pods stay Pending → HPA can't help!

# CA alone:
# Detects Pending pods (due to insufficient resources)
# Adds a new node → Pending pods get scheduled

# Together (the magic):
# 1. Load increases
# 2. HPA: "CPU > 70%, need 10 replicas (currently 5)"
# 3. HPA creates pods 6-10
# 4. Pods 8-10: "Pending" — no room on existing nodes
# 5. CA: "Pending pods detected, adding a node"
# 6. New node Ready → pods 8-10 scheduled
# 7. Load decreases
# 8. HPA: "CPU < 70%, scale down to 5 replicas"
# 9. HPA removes pods 6-10
# 10. CA: "Node is underutilized, removing it" (after cooldown)

# CKA exam:
# HPA: kubectl autoscale deployment web \
#   --min=2 --max=10 --cpu-percent=70
# CA: NOT on CKA (it's a cloud add-on)

# But know conceptually:
# HPA = pod-level scaling (CKA ✅)
# CA = node-level scaling (cloud only)
# VPA = Vertical Pod Autoscaler (adjusts requests/limits)
Explanation

HPA scales pods based on metrics (CPU, memory, custom). CA scales nodes when pods are Pending due to resource shortage. Together: HPA creates demand, CA adds capacity. On the CKA, you'll work with HPA (imperative: kubectl autoscale). CA is a cloud add-on. Key: HPA needs Metrics Server + resource requests defined on pods. Without requests, HPA can't calculate percentages.

Q8
How do StorageClasses differ between cloud and bare-metal clusters?
Show Answer
Answer
# Cloud: Dynamic provisioning with cloud storage
kubectl get sc  # On GKE:
# NAME              PROVISIONER          DEFAULT
# standard (def)    kubernetes.io/gce-pd  Yes
# premium-rwo       pd.csi.storage...     No

# PVC automatically creates a cloud disk:
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: data-pvc
spec:
  accessModes: [ReadWriteOnce]
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 10Gi
  # storageClassName: standard  ← Optional, uses default

# kubectl get pv → Auto-created PV bound to cloud disk!

# Bare-metal/kubeadm (CKA):
kubectl get sc
# (empty or local-storage only)

# Must create PV manually:
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolume
metadata:
  name: data-pv
spec:
  capacity:
    storage: 10Gi
  accessModes: [ReadWriteOnce]
  hostPath:
    path: /mnt/data
  storageClassName: manual

# PVC must specify matching storageClassName:
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: data-pvc
spec:
  accessModes: [ReadWriteOnce]
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 10Gi
  storageClassName: manual

# CKA exam: Expect hostPath PVs with manual provisioning
# Check default SC: kubectl get sc
# Set default: kubectl patch sc <name> \
#   -p '{"metadata":{"annotations":{"storageclass.kubernetes.io/is-default-class":"true"}}}'
Explanation

Cloud clusters have dynamic provisioning: create PVC → StorageClass auto-creates a PV backed by cloud storage (EBS, GCE PD, Azure Disk). On kubeadm (CKA), there's typically no default StorageClass, so you must create PVs manually using hostPath or local volumes. Key CKA skill: creating both PV and PVC with matching storageClassName, capacity, and access modes.

Q9
How do you check if a cluster has a default StorageClass and set one?
Show Answer
Answer
# Check existing StorageClasses:
kubectl get sc
# NAME              PROVISIONER        RECLAIMPOLICY  DEFAULT
# local-storage     local              Delete         
# ← No "(default)" annotation!

# PVCs without storageClassName look for the default SC
# If no default → PVC stays Pending

# Set a StorageClass as default:
kubectl patch sc local-storage -p \
  '{"metadata":{"annotations":{"storageclass.kubernetes.io/is-default-class":"true"}}}'

# Verify:
kubectl get sc
# NAME                    PROVISIONER  RECLAIMPOLICY  DEFAULT
# local-storage (default) local        Delete         Yes ✓

# Remove default from a StorageClass:
kubectl patch sc local-storage -p \
  '{"metadata":{"annotations":{"storageclass.kubernetes.io/is-default-class":"false"}}}'

# Important: Only ONE SC should be default
# Multiple defaults → PVCs fail with "multiple default SCs"

# Create a new StorageClass:
apiVersion: storage.k8s.io/v1
kind: StorageClass
metadata:
  name: fast-storage
  annotations:
    storageclass.kubernetes.io/is-default-class: "true"
provisioner: kubernetes.io/no-provisioner
volumeBindingMode: WaitForFirstConsumer
reclaimPolicy: Retain
Explanation

The default StorageClass is marked by the annotation storageclass.kubernetes.io/is-default-class: "true". PVCs without storageClassName use the default SC. On the CKA, check with kubectl get sc and look for (default). If multiple SCs are marked default, PVC creation fails. WaitForFirstConsumer delays provisioning until a pod actually uses the PVC.

Q10
What's the difference between Ingress on cloud vs bare-metal?
Show Answer
Answer
# Cloud Ingress:
# 1. Install Ingress controller (nginx, ALB, etc.)
# 2. Controller creates: Service type=LoadBalancer → Cloud LB
# 3. External IP assigned automatically
kubectl get svc -n ingress-nginx
# NAME                  TYPE          EXTERNAL-IP
# ingress-controller    LoadBalancer  35.200.1.1  ← Cloud LB

# Bare-metal/kubeadm Ingress:
# 1. Install Ingress controller (nginx)
# 2. Controller creates: Service type=NodePort (or hostNetwork)
# 3. Access via node IP + NodePort
kubectl get svc -n ingress-nginx
# NAME                  TYPE      EXTERNAL-IP  PORT(S)
# ingress-controller    NodePort  <none>       80:30080/TCP

# Access: http://<node-ip>:30080

# Create Ingress resource (same on both):
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: web-ingress
  annotations:
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/rewrite-target: /
spec:
  ingressClassName: nginx
  rules:
  - host: web.example.com
    http:
      paths:
      - path: /
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service:
            name: web-svc
            port:
              number: 80

# CKA exam: Know Ingress resource YAML (same everywhere)
# The controller setup differs, but the Ingress object is identical
Explanation

The Ingress resource YAML is identical on cloud and bare-metal. The difference is how the Ingress controller is exposed: cloud uses LoadBalancer Service (gets external IP), bare-metal uses NodePort or hostNetwork. On the CKA, you create Ingress resources (the YAML) — know ingressClassName, rules, paths, backends. The controller is typically pre-installed on the exam cluster.

Q11
Walk through deploying the anihpj Django application to a managed Kubernetes cluster (EKS, GKE, or AKS). What cloud-specific resources are automatically provisioned when you create a LoadBalancer Service and a PVC?
Show Answer
Answer
# Step 1: Create cluster (example: AKS)
az aks create -g myRG -n anihpj-cluster --node-count 3
az aks get-credentials -g myRG -n anihpj-cluster

# Step 2: Deploy anihpj (same YAML as bare-metal!)
kubectl create deployment anihpj --image=anihpj:v1 --replicas=3

# Step 3: Expose with LoadBalancer
kubectl expose deployment anihpj --type=LoadBalancer \
  --port=80 --target-port=8000

# Cloud auto-provisions:
# AWS:   Elastic Load Balancer (ELB/ALB) + public IP
# GCP:   Google Cloud Load Balancer + external IP
# Azure: Azure Load Balancer + public IP

# Wait for external IP:
kubectl get svc anihpj -w
# NAME     TYPE           EXTERNAL-IP      PORT(S)
# anihpj   LoadBalancer   40.112.73.215    80:31234/TCP

# Step 4: Create PVC for PostgreSQL
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: postgres-data
spec:
  accessModes: ["ReadWriteOnce"]
  storageClassName: managed-premium   # Cloud StorageClass
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 50Gi

# Cloud auto-provisions:
# AWS:   EBS volume (gp3)
# GCP:   Persistent Disk (pd-ssd)
# Azure: Azure Managed Disk (Premium_LRS)

# Key difference from bare-metal:
# No manual PV creation! Cloud CSI driver handles it
kubectl get pv
# pvc-abc123   50Gi   RWO   Bound   default/postgres-data
# (PV auto-created by cloud provisioner)

# Verify application:
curl http://40.112.73.215/api/jobs/
Explanation

The beauty of Kubernetes is that the same YAML works everywhere. The difference is what happens behind the scenes: on bare-metal, type: LoadBalancer stays Pending forever (no cloud controller). On managed clusters, the cloud controller automatically provisions a real load balancer with a public IP. Similarly, PVCs with a cloud StorageClass trigger automatic disk provisioning via CSI drivers — no manual PV creation needed. On the CKA (which uses bare-metal/kubeadm), you create PVs manually. But understanding that cloud clusters automate this helps you grasp why StorageClasses and the CSI architecture exist.

Q12
What is the Cluster Autoscaler and how does it differ from HPA? Configure a node pool with autoscaling enabled (min 2, max 10 nodes). What triggers a scale-up vs scale-down?
Show Answer
Answer
# HPA vs Cluster Autoscaler:
#
# HPA (Horizontal Pod Autoscaler):
#   Scales PODS within existing nodes
#   Based on CPU/memory metrics
#   kubectl autoscale deployment anihpj --min=2 --max=20 --cpu-percent=70
#
# Cluster Autoscaler (CA):
#   Scales NODES (adds/removes VMs)
#   Based on pod scheduling failures
#   Configured per node pool via cloud CLI

# Enable Cluster Autoscaler:
# AWS EKS:
eksctl create nodegroup --cluster=anihpj-cluster \
  --name=workers --nodes-min=2 --nodes-max=10 \
  --asg-access

# GCP GKE:
gcloud container clusters update anihpj-cluster \
  --enable-autoscaling --min-nodes=2 --max-nodes=10

# Azure AKS:
az aks update -g myRG -n anihpj-cluster \
  --enable-cluster-autoscaler \
  --min-count 2 --max-count 10

# SCALE-UP triggers:
# 1. Pod is Pending due to "Insufficient cpu/memory"
# 2. No existing node can fit the pod's resource requests
# 3. CA adds a new node to the pool
# 4. Scheduler places the Pending pod on the new node

# SCALE-DOWN triggers:
# 1. Node utilization drops below threshold (~50%)
# 2. All pods on the node can be rescheduled elsewhere
# 3. No pods with PDBs that would be violated
# 4. CA drains the node and removes the VM
# 5. Default cooldown: 10 minutes after scale-up

# Verify autoscaler status:
kubectl get nodes
# Shows current node count

kubectl describe configmap cluster-autoscaler-status \
  -n kube-system
# Shows scaling decisions and events
Explanation

HPA and Cluster Autoscaler work together: HPA creates more pods when demand increases → eventually nodes fill up → new pods go Pending → Cluster Autoscaler detects Pending pods → adds nodes → pods get scheduled. For scale-down, CA checks if a node's pods can be moved elsewhere and respects PDBs before removing nodes. The CKA tests HPA directly (create with kubectl autoscale), while Cluster Autoscaler is conceptual knowledge. Key insight: resource requests on pods are what drive both systems — without requests, neither HPA nor CA can make informed decisions.

Chapter 37: CI/CD and GitOps

CI/CD pipelines, ArgoCD, Helm, Kustomize — automating Kubernetes deployments.

🔴 Advanced ⏱️ ~2 hours 🔄 CI/CD 🎯 Production

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ Build a CI/CD pipeline — from code push to deployed pods, end-to-end
  • ✅ Understand GitOps principles — Git as single source of truth with ArgoCD
  • ✅ Deploy anihpj with ArgoCD — practical GitOps setup and sync workflow
  • ✅ Package with Helm — create reusable, configurable Kubernetes charts
  • ✅ Customize with Kustomize — base + overlay pattern for multi-environment deployment
  • ✅ Choose Helm vs Kustomize — when to use each and how they complement each other
CKA Exam Relevance
Workloads & Scheduling — 15% Application Lifecycle Management
🎯 CKA Exam Note

CI/CD and GitOps are not directly tested on the CKA exam. However, Helm and Kustomize appear increasingly in CKA environments as deployment tools. More importantly, these are skills every CKA-certified engineer uses daily in production.

37.1 CI/CD Pipeline for Kubernetes

CONCEPT

CI/CD automates the path from code commit to running pods. Every push to Git triggers a pipeline: build, test, package as container image, push to registry, and deploy to Kubernetes.

CI/CD Pipeline — End to End

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  CI/CD PIPELINE FOR KUBERNETES                                               │
│                                                                              │
│  ┌──────┐  ┌──────┐  ┌──────┐  ┌──────┐  ┌──────┐  ┌──────┐  ┌──────┐    │
│  │  📝  │  │  🔨  │  │  ✅  │  │  🏷️  │  │  📦  │  │  ☸️  │  │  🔍  │    │
│  │ CODE │→│BUILD │→│ TEST │→│ TAG  │→│ PUSH │→│DEPLOY│→│VERIFY│    │
│  └──────┘  └──────┘  └──────┘  └──────┘  └──────┘  └──────┘  └──────┘    │
│                                                                              │
│  Git       Docker    Unit      Docker    Container  kubectl    Smoke        │
│  Push      Build     Tests     Tag       Registry   apply     Tests         │
│                      + Lint    v1.2.3    (ECR/ACR/  -f deploy  curl /health │
│                                          GCR/GHCR)                          │
│                                                                              │
│  ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════     │
│                                                                              │
│  EXAMPLE: anihpj CI/CD with GitHub Actions                                   │
│                                                                              │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐        │
│  │  on: push to main                                               │        │
│  │                                                                  │        │
│  │  jobs:                                                           │        │
│  │    build:                                                        │        │
│  │      ├── Checkout code                                           │        │
│  │      ├── Run Django tests (python manage.py test)               │        │
│  │      ├── Build Docker image                                      │        │
│  │      ├── Tag: ghcr.io/user/anihpj:${{ github.sha }}            │        │
│  │      └── Push to GitHub Container Registry                       │        │
│  │                                                                  │        │
│  │    deploy:                                                       │        │
│  │      ├── Update image tag in deployment YAML                    │        │
│  │      ├── kubectl apply -f k8s/                                   │        │
│  │      └── kubectl rollout status deployment/anihpj-web           │        │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘        │
│                                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
💡 CI vs CD vs CD

CI (Continuous Integration) = build + test on every commit. CD (Continuous Delivery) = automatically prepare a deployable artifact. CD (Continuous Deployment) = automatically deploy to production. Most teams do CI + Continuous Delivery, with manual approval before production.

Complete GitHub Actions Workflow for anihpj

.github/workflows/ci-cd.yaml — Full pipeline 
name: anihpj CI/CD Pipeline

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

env:
  REGISTRY: ghcr.io
  IMAGE_NAME: ${{ github.repository }}/anihpj-web

jobs:
  # ═══════════════════════════════════════
  # JOB 1: Test (CI)
  # ═══════════════════════════════════════
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
    - uses: actions/checkout@v4

    - name: Set up Python
      uses: actions/setup-python@v5
      with:
        python-version: "3.12"

    - name: Install dependencies
      run: pip install -r requirements.txt

    - name: Run Django tests
      run: python manage.py test --verbosity=2

    - name: Run linter
      run: flake8 . --max-line-length=120

  # ═══════════════════════════════════════
  # JOB 2: Build & Push Image (CD prep)
  # ═══════════════════════════════════════
  build:
    needs: test           # Only build if tests pass
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'   # Only on main
    permissions:
      packages: write     # Push to GHCR

    steps:
    - uses: actions/checkout@v4

    - name: Log in to Container Registry
      uses: docker/login-action@v3
      with:
        registry: ${{ env.REGISTRY }}
        username: ${{ github.actor }}
        password: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}

    - name: Build and push Docker image
      uses: docker/build-push-action@v5
      with:
        context: .
        push: true
        tags: |
          ${{ env.REGISTRY }}/${{ env.IMAGE_NAME }}:${{ github.sha }}
          ${{ env.REGISTRY }}/${{ env.IMAGE_NAME }}:latest

  # ═══════════════════════════════════════
  # JOB 3: Update manifest repo (GitOps trigger)
  # ═══════════════════════════════════════
  update-manifests:
    needs: build
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
    - name: Checkout manifest repo
      uses: actions/checkout@v4
      with:
        repository: youruser/anihpj-k8s-manifests
        token: ${{ secrets.MANIFEST_REPO_TOKEN }}

    - name: Update image tag
      run: |
