        cd overlays/production
        kustomize edit set image \
          anihpj-web=${{ env.REGISTRY }}/${{ env.IMAGE_NAME }}:${{ github.sha }}

    - name: Commit and push
      run: |
        git config user.name "GitHub Actions"
        git config user.email "actions@github.com"
        git commit -am "Deploy anihpj ${{ github.sha }}"
        git push    # ArgoCD picks this up automatically!
🎯 Key Takeaway for CKA

The CKA won't ask you to write CI/CD YAML, but understanding this pipeline helps you grasp why image tags matter (they trigger rolling updates), why container registries matter (image pull policies, imagePullSecrets), and how deployments update — all directly tested on the CKA.

37.2 GitOps with ArgoCD

CONCEPT

GitOps means Git is the single source of truth for your cluster state. Instead of running kubectl apply from a CI pipeline (push-based), a GitOps tool like ArgoCD watches your Git repo and syncs changes automatically (pull-based).

Traditional CI/CD (Push) vs GitOps (Pull)

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  TRADITIONAL CI/CD (Push-Based):       GITOPS (Pull-Based):                 │
│                                                                              │
│  Developer pushes code                 Developer pushes code                 │
│       │                                     │                                │
│       ▼                                     ▼                                │
│  CI builds & tests                    CI builds & tests                    │
│       │                                     │                                │
│       ▼                                     ▼                                │
│  CD runs kubectl apply ⚠️              CD updates manifest                   │
│  (push to cluster directly)            in Git repo                           │
│       │                                     │                                │
│       ▼                                     ▼                                │
│  Deployed. But...                      ArgoCD detects Git change             │
│  - Who ran it? 🤷                            │                                │
│  - What changed? 🤷                          ▼                                │
│  - How to rollback? 😬                 ArgoCD syncs to cluster ✅            │
│  - Drift undetected 😱                                                       │
│                                         Benefits:                            │
│                                         ✅ Full audit trail (Git log)       │
│                                         ✅ Rollback = git revert             │
│                                         ✅ Drift detection & auto-fix      │
│                                         ✅ PR-based deployments              │
│                                         ✅ No cluster creds in CI           │
│                                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

ArgoCD Architecture

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  ARGOCD SYNC LOOP                                                            │
│                                                                              │
│  ┌──────────────┐      Poll every 3min     ┌──────────────────┐            │
│  │              │   ◀─────────────────────  │                  │            │
│  │  Git Repo    │                           │    ArgoCD        │            │
│  │  (manifests) │   Detect diff?            │    Controller    │            │
│  │              │   ─────────────────────▶  │                  │            │
│  └──────────────┘                           └────────┬─────────┘            │
│                                                       │                      │
│         Git state ≠ Cluster state?                   │ Sync!                │
│         → OutOfSync status                           │                      │
│         → Auto-sync or manual approval               ▼                      │
│                                              ┌──────────────────┐           │
│                                              │   K8s Cluster    │           │
│                                              │   ┌──────────┐   │           │
│                                              │   │ anihpj   │   │           │
│                                              │   │ pods     │   │           │
│                                              │   └──────────┘   │           │
│                                              │   Status: Synced │           │
│                                              │   Health: Healthy │           │
│                                              └──────────────────┘           │
│                                                                              │
│  ArgoCD UI Dashboard:                                                        │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐             │
│  │ App: anihpj     │ Sync: ✅ Synced │ Health: 💚 Healthy    │             │
│  │ Repo: github.com/user/anihpj-k8s   │ Revision: abc1234   │             │
│  │ Target: anihpj namespace            │ Auto-Sync: ON       │             │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘             │
│                                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

37.3 ArgoCD for anihpj — Practical Setup

Here's a complete walkthrough of setting up ArgoCD to deploy anihpj using GitOps.

Step 1: Install ArgoCD

Terminal — install ArgoCD 
# Create namespace and install ArgoCD
$ kubectl create namespace argocd
$ kubectl apply -n argocd \
    -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/argoproj/argo-cd/stable/manifests/install.yaml

# Wait for all pods to be ready
$ kubectl -n argocd get pods -w
NAME                                  READY   STATUS    RESTARTS
argocd-server-xxxxx                   1/1     Running   0
argocd-repo-server-xxxxx              1/1     Running   0
argocd-application-controller-xxxxx   1/1     Running   0
argocd-redis-xxxxx                    1/1     Running   0

# Get the initial admin password
$ kubectl -n argocd get secret argocd-initial-admin-secret \
    -o jsonpath="{.data.password}" | base64 -d

# Access the UI (port-forward for local access)
$ kubectl port-forward svc/argocd-server -n argocd 8080:443

Step 2: Create ArgoCD Application for anihpj

anihpj-argocd-app.yaml — ArgoCD Application 
apiVersion: argoproj.io/v1alpha1
kind: Application
metadata:
  name: anihpj
  namespace: argocd
spec:
  project: default
  source:
    repoURL: https://github.com/youruser/anihpj-k8s-manifests
    targetRevision: main          # Track main branch
    path: overlays/production     # Kustomize overlay path
  destination:
    server: https://kubernetes.default.svc
    namespace: anihpj
  syncPolicy:
    automated:
      prune: true                 # Delete resources removed from Git
      selfHeal: true              # Fix manual changes (drift)
    syncOptions:
    - CreateNamespace=true        # Auto-create namespace

anihpj GitOps Workflow — Day-to-Day

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  DAILY GITOPS WORKFLOW FOR anihpj                                            │
│                                                                              │
│  1. Developer updates anihpj code                                            │
│     └── CI builds new image: anihpj/web:v2.1                                │
│                                                                              │
│  2. Developer updates manifest repo (PR)                                     │
│     └── Change: image: anihpj/web:v2.0 → image: anihpj/web:v2.1           │
│                                                                              │
│  3. PR reviewed & merged to main                                           │
│     └── Git log: "Bump anihpj to v2.1 — fix login bug"                     │
│                                                                              │
│  4. ArgoCD detects change (3 min polling or webhook)                        │
│     └── Status: OutOfSync → Syncing → Synced ✅                              │
│                                                                              │
│  5. ArgoCD applies changes to cluster                                        │
│     └── Rolling update: v2.0 pods → v2.1 pods                              │
│                                                                              │
│  6. Something wrong? Rollback in seconds!                                    │
│     └── $ git revert HEAD && git push                                     │
│     └── ArgoCD syncs back to v2.0 ✅                                         │
│                                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

ArgoCD Troubleshooting — Common Issues

Terminal — ArgoCD debugging commands 
# ═══════════════════════════════════════
# CHECK APPLICATION STATUS
# ═══════════════════════════════════════
$ argocd app get anihpj
Name:               anihpj
Project:            default
Server:             https://kubernetes.default.svc
Namespace:          anihpj
Sync Status:        OutOfSync     ← Drift detected!
Health Status:      Degraded       ← Something's wrong

# ═══════════════════════════════════════
# SEE WHAT'S DIFFERENT (drift detection)
# ═══════════════════════════════════════
$ argocd app diff anihpj
===== apps/Deployment anihpj/anihpj-web ======
  replicas: 3               # Git says 3
- replicas: 5               # Cluster has 5 (someone used kubectl scale!)

# ═══════════════════════════════════════
# FORCE SYNC (override manual changes)
# ═══════════════════════════════════════
$ argocd app sync anihpj --force --prune

# ═══════════════════════════════════════
# CHECK SYNC HISTORY  
# ═══════════════════════════════════════
$ argocd app history anihpj
ID  DATE                  REVISION
1   2024-03-10 09:00:00   abc1234 (Deploy v2.0)
2   2024-03-12 14:30:00   def5678 (Fix login bug)
3   2024-03-15 11:00:00   ghi9012 (Scale to 3 replicas)

# ═══════════════════════════════════════
# ROLLBACK TO PREVIOUS SYNC
# ═══════════════════════════════════════
$ argocd app rollback anihpj 2    # Go back to revision 2

ArgoCD Status Cheat Sheet

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  ARGOCD STATUS REFERENCE                                                     │
│                                                                              │
│  SYNC STATUS:                                                                │
│  ┌──────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
│  │ Synced     ✅    │ Cluster matches Git — everything is good         │    │
│  │ OutOfSync  ⚠️    │ Cluster differs from Git — needs sync            │    │
│  │ Unknown    ❓    │ ArgoCD can't determine state                      │    │
│  └──────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
│                                                                              │
│  HEALTH STATUS:                                                              │
│  ┌──────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
│  │ Healthy    💚    │ All resources are running correctly               │    │
│  │ Progressing ⏳   │ Resource is updating (rolling update in progress) │    │
│  │ Degraded   🟡    │ Resource is unhealthy (CrashLoop, pending)       │    │
│  │ Suspended  ⏸️    │ Resource is suspended (scaled to 0)              │    │
│  │ Missing    ❌    │ Resource exists in Git but not in cluster         │    │
│  └──────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
│                                                                              │
│  COMMON TROUBLESHOOTING:                                                     │
│  ┌────────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────┐        │
│  │ OutOfSync + Healthy        │ Someone used kubectl directly —    │        │
│  │                            │ sync to restore Git state          │        │
│  ├────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────┤        │
│  │ Synced + Degraded          │ Git state applied but pods         │        │
│  │                            │ crashing — check image/config      │        │
│  ├────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────┤        │
│  │ OutOfSync + Missing        │ New resource in Git not yet        │        │
│  │                            │ synced — run sync                  │        │
│  ├────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────┤        │
│  │ Unknown + Unknown          │ ArgoCD can't reach repo or         │        │
│  │                            │ cluster — check credentials        │        │
│  └────────────────────────────┴────────────────────────────────────┘        │
│                                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

37.4 Helm — Kubernetes Package Manager

CONCEPT

Helm packages Kubernetes manifests into charts — reusable, versioned, configurable bundles. Think of it as apt or pip for Kubernetes. Values files let you customize the same chart for dev, staging, and production.

Helm Chart Structure — anihpj Example

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  HELM CHART DIRECTORY STRUCTURE                                              │
│                                                                              │
│  anihpj-chart/                                                               │
│  ├── Chart.yaml              # Chart metadata (name, version, description)  │
│  ├── values.yaml             # Default configuration values                  │
│  ├── values-prod.yaml        # Production overrides                          │
│  ├── templates/                                                              │
│  │   ├── _helpers.tpl        # Template helper functions                     │
│  │   ├── deployment.yaml     # {{ .Values.replicas }}                       │
│  │   ├── service.yaml        # {{ .Values.serviceType }}                    │
│  │   ├── ingress.yaml        # {{ .Values.host }}                           │
│  │   ├── configmap.yaml      # {{ .Values.dbHost }}                         │
│  │   ├── secret.yaml         # {{ .Values.dbPassword }}                     │
│  │   └── hpa.yaml            # {{ .Values.autoscaling.enabled }}            │
│  └── charts/                 # Sub-chart dependencies                        │
│                                                                              │
│  ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════     │
│                                                                              │
│  values.yaml (defaults):       values-prod.yaml (overrides):                │
│  ┌─────────────────────────┐   ┌─────────────────────────┐                  │
│  │ replicas: 1              │   │ replicas: 3              │                  │
│  │ image:                   │   │ image:                   │                  │
│  │   tag: latest            │   │   tag: v2.1-stable       │                  │
│  │ serviceType: ClusterIP   │   │ serviceType: LoadBalancer│                  │
│  │ resources:               │   │ resources:               │                  │
│  │   requests:              │   │   requests:              │                  │
│  │     cpu: 100m            │   │     cpu: 500m            │                  │
│  │     memory: 128Mi        │   │     memory: 512Mi        │                  │
│  │ ingress:                 │   │ ingress:                 │                  │
│  │   enabled: false         │   │   enabled: true          │                  │
│  │   host: ""               │   │   host: anihpj.com      │                  │
│  └─────────────────────────┘   └─────────────────────────┘                  │
│                                                                              │
│  Same chart, different values → different environments! 🎯                  │
│                                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Helm Essential Commands

Terminal — Helm workflow 
# ═══════════════════════════════════════
# CHART REPOSITORIES
# ═══════════════════════════════════════
$ helm repo add bitnami https://charts.bitnami.com/bitnami
$ helm repo update
$ helm search repo nginx

# ═══════════════════════════════════════
# INSTALL / UPGRADE / ROLLBACK
# ═══════════════════════════════════════
# Install anihpj chart with production values
$ helm install anihpj ./anihpj-chart --values values-prod.yaml -n anihpj

# Upgrade (change image tag to v2.2)
$ helm upgrade anihpj ./anihpj-chart --values values-prod.yaml \
    --set image.tag=v2.2 -n anihpj

# Oops! Rollback to previous release
$ helm rollback anihpj 1 -n anihpj

# ═══════════════════════════════════════
# INSPECT / DEBUG
# ═══════════════════════════════════════
$ helm list -A                    # List all releases
$ helm history anihpj -n anihpj   # Release history
$ helm get values anihpj -n anihpj  # Current values
$ helm template ./anihpj-chart    # Render without installing (dry-run)

Helm Go Template Syntax — Crash Course

Helm templates use Go's text/template language. Here's how a real Helm deployment template looks for anihpj:

templates/deployment.yaml — Helm Go template syntax 
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  # {{ .Release.Name }} = helm install NAME → "anihpj"
  name: {{ .Release.Name }}-web
  labels:
    {{- include "anihpj.labels" . | nindent 4 }}
spec:
  replicas: {{ .Values.replicas | default 1 }}
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: {{ .Release.Name }}
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: {{ .Release.Name }}
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: {{ .Chart.Name }}
        image: "{{ .Values.image.repository }}:{{ .Values.image.tag }}"
        ports:
        - containerPort: {{ .Values.containerPort | default 8000 }}
        {{- if .Values.resources }}
        resources:
          {{- toYaml .Values.resources | nindent 10 }}
        {{- end }}
        {{- if .Values.env }}
        env:
        {{- range .Values.env }}
        - name: {{ .name }}
          value: {{ .value | quote }}
        {{- end }}
        {{- end }}
      {{- if .Values.nodeSelector }}
      nodeSelector:
        {{- toYaml .Values.nodeSelector | nindent 8 }}
      {{- end }}

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# GO TEMPLATE CHEAT SHEET:
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# {{ .Values.xxx }}           → Read from values.yaml
# {{ .Release.Name }}         → Helm release name
# {{ .Chart.Name }}           → From Chart.yaml
# {{ .Release.Namespace }}    → Target namespace
# {{- ... }}                  → Trim left whitespace
# {{ ... -}}                  → Trim right whitespace
# {{ if .Values.x }}...{{ end }}  → Conditional
# {{ range .Values.list }}        → Loop
# {{ include "tpl" . }}           → Include named template
# {{ toYaml .Values.x | nindent 4 }} → Render as YAML
# {{ .Values.x | default "val" }}     → Default value
# {{ .Values.x | quote }}        → Wrap in quotes

⚠️ Helm Debugging Tips

Template not rendering correctly? Use helm template ./chart --debug to see the rendered YAML. Common errors: missing {{- }} whitespace control causes extra blank lines, and forgetting | nindent N causes YAML indentation errors. Always validate with helm lint ./chart before installing.

37.5 Kustomize — Built-in Manifest Customization

CONCEPT

Kustomize is built into kubectl — no separate binary needed. It uses a base + overlays pattern: write your YAML once (base), and patch it per environment (overlays). No templates, no Go syntax — just strategic merge patches on plain YAML.

Kustomize Directory Structure — anihpj

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  KUSTOMIZE BASE + OVERLAYS PATTERN                                          │
│                                                                              │
│  anihpj-kustomize/                                                           │
│  ├── base/                          # Shared across ALL environments         │
│  │   ├── kustomization.yaml         # Lists resources to include             │
│  │   ├── deployment.yaml            # anihpj base: replicas: 1              │
│  │   ├── service.yaml               # ClusterIP service                     │
│  │   └── configmap.yaml             # Shared config values                  │
│  │                                                                           │
│  └── overlays/                      # Per-environment patches                │
│      ├── dev/                                                                │
│      │   ├── kustomization.yaml     # bases: [../../base]                   │
│      │   └── patch-replicas.yaml    # replicas: 1, cpu: 100m               │
│      │                                                                       │
│      ├── staging/                                                            │
│      │   ├── kustomization.yaml     # bases: [../../base]                   │
│      │   └── patch-resources.yaml   # replicas: 2, cpu: 250m               │
│      │                                                                       │
│      └── production/                                                         │
│          ├── kustomization.yaml     # bases: [../../base]                   │
│          ├── patch-replicas.yaml    # replicas: 5                           │
│          └── patch-resources.yaml   # cpu: 500m, memory: 512Mi             │
│                                                                              │
│  ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════     │
│                                                                              │
│  Deploy:   $ kubectl apply -k overlays/production/                           │
│  Preview:  $ kubectl kustomize overlays/production/                          │
│                                                                              │
│  Result: base/deployment.yaml MERGED with production patches                 │
│  → replicas: 5, cpu: 500m, memory: 512Mi                                    │
│                                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Kustomize Files for anihpj

base/kustomization.yaml & overlays/production/kustomization.yaml 
# ═══════════════════════════════════════
# base/kustomization.yaml
# ═══════════════════════════════════════
apiVersion: kustomize.config.k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: Kustomization
resources:
- deployment.yaml
- service.yaml
- configmap.yaml
commonLabels:
  app: anihpj

---
# ═══════════════════════════════════════
# overlays/production/kustomization.yaml
# ═══════════════════════════════════════
apiVersion: kustomize.config.k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: Kustomization
resources:
- ../../base
namePrefix: prod-              # All resources get prod- prefix
namespace: anihpj-production
patches:
- path: patch-replicas.yaml
  target:
    kind: Deployment
    name: anihpj-web
images:
- name: anihpj/web
  newTag: v2.1-stable          # Override image tag for prod

---
# ═══════════════════════════════════════
# overlays/production/patch-replicas.yaml
# ═══════════════════════════════════════
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj-web
spec:
  replicas: 5                  # Production: 5 replicas
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: anihpj
        resources:
          requests:
            cpu: "500m"
            memory: "512Mi"
          limits:
            cpu: "1000m"
            memory: "1Gi"

Kustomize Power Features — Generators & Transformers

kustomization.yaml — Generators, labels, and config management 
apiVersion: kustomize.config.k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: Kustomization
resources:
- ../../base

namespace: anihpj-production

# ═══════════════════════════════════════
# CONFIGMAP GENERATOR — auto-creates ConfigMaps from files/literals
# ═══════════════════════════════════════
configMapGenerator:
- name: anihpj-config
  literals:
  - DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE=anihpj.settings.production
  - ALLOWED_HOSTS=anihpj.com
  - DEBUG=false

# ═══════════════════════════════════════
# SECRET GENERATOR — auto-creates Secrets
# ═══════════════════════════════════════
secretGenerator:
- name: anihpj-db-creds
  literals:
  - DB_PASSWORD=supersecret   # In real life: use sealed-secrets or external-secrets
  type: Opaque

# ═══════════════════════════════════════
# COMMON LABELS — applied to ALL resources
# ═══════════════════════════════════════
commonLabels:
  app: anihpj
  env: production
  team: backend

# ═══════════════════════════════════════
# COMMON ANNOTATIONS
# ═══════════════════════════════════════
commonAnnotations:
  managed-by: kustomize

# ═══════════════════════════════════════
# IMAGE TRANSFORMER — override image tags
# ═══════════════════════════════════════
images:
- name: anihpj/web
  newName: ghcr.io/youruser/anihpj-web
  newTag: v2.1-stable

# ═══════════════════════════════════════
# PATCHES — strategic merge or JSON patches
# ═══════════════════════════════════════
patches:
- path: patch-replicas.yaml
  target:
    kind: Deployment
    name: anihpj-web
🏋️ Practice Drill — Kustomize Multi-Environment Deploy

Scenario: Deploy anihpj to staging and production using Kustomize overlays with different configurations.

  1. Preview what Kustomize will generate for production:
    kubectl kustomize overlays/production/
  2. Apply the production overlay:
    kubectl apply -k overlays/production/
  3. Verify resources were created in the correct namespace:
    kubectl get all -n anihpj-production
  4. Check that labels were applied correctly:
    kubectl get deploy -n anihpj-production --show-labels
  5. Verify the image tag matches the overlay:
    kubectl get deploy anihpj-web -n anihpj-production -o jsonpath='{.spec.template.spec.containers[0].image}'
  6. Compare with staging config:
    diff <(kubectl kustomize overlays/staging/) <(kubectl kustomize overlays/production/)

Key point: Same base YAML, but -k flag applies environment-specific patches. This is DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself) for Kubernetes!

🎯 CKA Exam — Kustomize

The CKA exam may reference Kustomize in the exam environment. Know these two commands cold: kubectl apply -k <directory> applies Kustomize configuration, and kubectl kustomize <directory> previews without applying. The -k flag is your friend!

37.6 Helm vs Kustomize — When to Use Each

This is one of the most common questions in Kubernetes. The short answer: they serve different purposes and complement each other.

Feature Helm Kustomize
Approach Templates + values Patches on base YAML
Learning Curve Moderate (Go templates) Low (plain YAML patches)
Package Sharing ✅ Chart repos (Artifact Hub) ❌ Not designed for sharing
Complexity Go template syntax Strategic merge patches
Built into kubectl No (separate binary) Yes (kubectl apply -k)
Versioning Chart versions + release history Git-based versioning
Rollback helm rollback git revert
Best For Shared / public apps (nginx, Redis) Internal config management
anihpj Use Case Package for distribution Multi-env deployments

Decision Tree — Helm, Kustomize, or Both?

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  DECISION TREE                                                               │
│                                                                              │
│  Need to share charts publicly?                                              │
│  ├── YES → Use Helm (chart repos)                                           │
│  └── NO                                                                      │
│      │                                                                       │
│      Need complex templating (conditionals, loops)?                         │
│      ├── YES → Use Helm (Go templates are powerful)                         │
│      └── NO                                                                  │
│          │                                                                   │
│          Just need different configs per environment?                        │
│          ├── YES → Use Kustomize (simplest solution)                        │
│          └── BOTH                                                            │
│              │                                                               │
│              Use Helm to render, Kustomize to patch!                        │
│              $ helm template ./chart | kubectl apply -k -                   │
│                                                                              │
│  ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════     │
│                                                                              │
│  🔑 PRO TIP: Many teams use BOTH together:                                 │
│                                                                              │
│  ┌──────────┐     ┌──────────┐     ┌──────────┐     ┌──────────┐          │
│  │  Helm    │────▶│ Rendered │────▶│Kustomize │────▶│ Applied  │          │
│  │  Chart   │     │  YAML    │     │  Patches │     │ to K8s   │          │
│  └──────────┘     └──────────┘     └──────────┘     └──────────┘          │
│                                                                              │
│  ArgoCD natively supports both Helm and Kustomize as sources!               │
│                                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

✅ Chapter 37 Competency Checklist

  • Can you explain the stages of a CI/CD pipeline for Kubernetes?
  • Can you write a GitHub Actions workflow that builds, tests, and pushes a container image?
  • Can you articulate why GitOps (pull-based) is preferred over push-based CD?
  • Can you set up an ArgoCD Application pointing at a Git repo?
  • Can you troubleshoot ArgoCD sync/health status issues?
  • Can you create a Helm chart with Go templates and values files?
  • Can you read and write Helm Go template syntax (conditionals, loops, defaults)?
  • Can you use kubectl apply -k with a base + overlay Kustomize structure?
  • Can you use Kustomize generators (configMapGenerator, secretGenerator)?
  • Can you explain when to choose Helm vs Kustomize vs both?
💡 Real-World Stack for anihpj — Putting It All Together

Here's how a production team would combine everything from this chapter:

  • GitHub Actions — CI: run Django tests, build Docker image, push to GHCR
  • Kustomize — Manage dev/staging/prod overlays for anihpj manifests
  • ArgoCD — Watch the manifest repo, auto-sync to cluster, detect drift
  • Helm — Install third-party tools (nginx-ingress, cert-manager, Prometheus)
  • HPA + Cluster Autoscaler — Scale pods and nodes based on traffic

This is the modern Kubernetes deployment stack used by most teams. You now understand every layer.

⚠️ CKA Gotcha: CI/CD & GitOps Traps

  • CKA doesn't test CI/CD directly — But understanding the deployment pipeline helps with troubleshooting questions. If a deployment is broken, knowing whether the issue is in the image (CI) vs the manifest (CD) vs the cluster saves time
  • Helm is NOT on the CKA exam — But Kustomize IS available via kubectl apply -k. Know that -k applies a kustomization directory. You may see it in exam documentation
  • kubectl apply vs kubectl create — apply is declarative (idempotent, can update). create is imperative (fails if resource exists). GitOps uses apply. The exam expects you to know both — use create for speed, apply for YAML files
  • Image pull policies — Always (default for :latest), IfNotPresent (default for tagged), Never. If a CI pipeline pushes a new image with the same tag, pods with IfNotPresent won't pull the update. This is a real-world trap
  • Rolling update strategy — maxSurge and maxUnavailable control deployment speed. Default is 25% each. For zero-downtime: maxUnavailable: 0, maxSurge: 1. Know how to set these on the exam

CKA Speed Tip — Deployment update commands:
kubectl set image deployment/<name> <container>=<image>:<tag> — update image
kubectl rollout status deployment/<name> — watch rollout progress
kubectl rollout history deployment/<name> — see revision history
kubectl rollout undo deployment/<name> — rollback to previous version
kubectl rollout undo deployment/<name> --to-revision=2 — rollback to specific revision

⚡ Deployment & rollout commands: kubectl apply -k ./overlays/prod/ — apply Kustomize overlay
kubectl diff -f manifest.yaml — preview changes before applying
kubectl annotate deployment <name> kubernetes.io/change-cause="reason" — record change reason
kubectl rollout pause deployment/<name> — pause mid-rollout
kubectl rollout resume deployment/<name> — resume paused rollout
📝 Chapter 37 Practice Drill

Timed challenge (target: 5 minutes):

  • 1. Create a deployment: kubectl create deploy webapp --image=nginx:1.24 --replicas=3
  • 2. Update the image: kubectl set image deploy/webapp nginx=nginx:1.25
  • 3. Watch the rollout: kubectl rollout status deploy/webapp
  • 4. Check history: kubectl rollout history deploy/webapp
  • 5. Rollback: kubectl rollout undo deploy/webapp
  • 6. Verify: kubectl describe deploy webapp | grep Image — should show nginx:1.24

📝 Chapter 37 — CKA Practice Questions

Q1
How do you update a Deployment's image and track the rollout?
Show Answer
Answer
# Method 1: kubectl set image (fastest)
kubectl set image deployment/webapp nginx=nginx:1.25
# Records the change automatically

# Method 2: Edit directly
kubectl edit deployment webapp
# Change image: nginx:1.24 → nginx:1.25

# Method 3: Patch
kubectl patch deployment webapp -p \
  '{"spec":{"template":{"spec":{"containers":[{"name":"nginx","image":"nginx:1.25"}]}}}}'

# Track the rollout:
kubectl rollout status deployment/webapp
# Waiting for deployment "webapp" rollout to finish:
# 2 out of 3 new replicas have been updated...
# deployment "webapp" successfully rolled out ✓

# Record the reason for the change:
kubectl annotate deployment webapp \
  kubernetes.io/change-cause="Updated nginx to 1.25 for security patch"

# Check rollout history:
kubectl rollout history deployment/webapp
# REVISION  CHANGE-CAUSE
# 1         <none>
# 2         Updated nginx to 1.25 for security patch

# View specific revision details:
kubectl rollout history deployment/webapp --revision=2
Explanation

kubectl set image is the fastest way to update a Deployment's image on the CKA. rollout status watches the rollout in real time. annotate with kubernetes.io/change-cause records why the change was made (shows in rollout history). On the CKA, set image + rollout status is the combo you'll use most. Each image update creates a new revision for rollback.

Q2
How do you rollback a Deployment to a previous version or a specific revision?
Show Answer
Answer
# Rollback to previous version:
kubectl rollout undo deployment/webapp
# deployment.apps/webapp rolled back

# Rollback to specific revision:
kubectl rollout history deployment/webapp
# REVISION  CHANGE-CAUSE
# 1         Initial deployment
# 2         Updated to nginx:1.25
# 3         Updated to nginx:1.26-broken  ← Current (broken!)

kubectl rollout undo deployment/webapp --to-revision=2
# Rolls back to revision 2 (nginx:1.25)

# Verify the rollback:
kubectl rollout status deployment/webapp
# deployment "webapp" successfully rolled out ✓

kubectl describe deployment webapp | grep Image
# Image: nginx:1.25  ← Back to working version!

# After rollback, revision numbers change:
kubectl rollout history deployment/webapp
# REVISION  CHANGE-CAUSE
# 1         Initial deployment
# 3         Updated to nginx:1.26-broken
# 4         Updated to nginx:1.25  ← Undo created rev 4

# How it works:
# - Each undo creates a NEW revision
# - Old ReplicaSet is scaled up, new one scaled down
# - Rolling update strategy still applies
# - Pods are NOT recreated all at once (gradual)
Explanation

kubectl rollout undo reverts to the previous revision. --to-revision=N targets a specific revision. On the CKA, this is the fastest way to fix a broken deployment. Key: undo creates a NEW revision (it doesn't delete history). The rollback uses the same rolling update strategy as a normal update. Always verify with describe deployment | grep Image after rollback.

Q3
What is the difference between kubectl apply and kubectl create?
Show Answer
Answer
Feature kubectl create kubectl apply
Style Imperative Declarative
If resource exists ❌ Error (AlreadyExists) ✅ Updates it
If resource doesn't exist ✅ Creates it ✅ Creates it
Idempotent ❌ No ✅ Yes
GitOps/CI/CD ❌ Not suitable ✅ Standard
CKA usage Generate YAML quickly Apply YAML files 
# CKA workflow:
# 1. Generate YAML with create + dry-run:
kubectl create deployment web --image=nginx \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml > web.yaml

# 2. Edit the YAML:
vi web.yaml  # Add resources, probes, etc.

# 3. Apply it:
kubectl apply -f web.yaml

# 4. Need to change? Edit and re-apply:
vi web.yaml  # Change replicas
kubectl apply -f web.yaml  # Updates existing resource ✓

# Using create for the same file twice:
kubectl create -f web.yaml
# Error: deployments.apps "web" already exists ✗

# Kustomize with apply:
kubectl apply -k ./overlays/prod/  # Apply kustomization
Explanation

create = imperative, fails if resource exists. apply = declarative, creates OR updates. On the CKA, use create --dry-run=client -o yaml to generate YAML, then apply -f to create/update. GitOps uses apply because it's idempotent (safe to run multiple times). apply stores the "last applied configuration" annotation for three-way merge on subsequent applies.

Q4
How do you configure a rolling update strategy with zero downtime?
Show Answer
Answer
# Zero-downtime rolling update:
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: web
spec:
  replicas: 3
  strategy:
    type: RollingUpdate
    rollingUpdate:
      maxSurge: 1          # Add 1 extra pod during update
      maxUnavailable: 0    # Never reduce below desired count
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: web
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: web
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: nginx
        image: nginx:1.25
        readinessProbe:      # CRITICAL for zero downtime!
          httpGet:
            path: /
            port: 80
          initialDelaySeconds: 5
          periodSeconds: 5

# maxSurge: 1, maxUnavailable: 0 means:
# 1. Create 1 new pod (4 total)
# 2. Wait for new pod to be READY
# 3. Terminate 1 old pod (3 total)
# 4. Create 1 new pod (4 total)
# 5. Wait for READY, terminate old...
# Always at least 3 healthy pods!

# Without readinessProbe:
# New pod is "Ready" immediately (before app starts)
# Old pod terminated → brief downtime before new pod serves traffic

# Verify strategy:
kubectl describe deployment web | grep -A3 Strategy
# RollingUpdateStrategy: 1 max unavailable, 1 max surge

# Set imperatively:
kubectl patch deployment web -p \
  '{"spec":{"strategy":{"rollingUpdate":{"maxSurge":1,"maxUnavailable":0}}}}'
Explanation

Zero downtime requires: (1) maxUnavailable: 0 — never reduce pod count below desired, (2) maxSurge: 1+ — allow extra pods during update, (3) readinessProbe — only send traffic when pod is actually ready. Without a readiness probe, Kubernetes considers pods Ready immediately, causing brief traffic failures. On the CKA, know how to set maxSurge/maxUnavailable and always add readiness probes.

Q5
What is kubectl apply -k (Kustomize) and how does it work?
Show Answer
Answer
# Kustomize: built into kubectl, no extra tools needed
# Customizes YAML without modifying original files

# Directory structure:
# base/
#   deployment.yaml
#   service.yaml
#   kustomization.yaml
# overlays/
#   prod/
#     kustomization.yaml  ← References base + adds overrides

# base/kustomization.yaml:
apiVersion: kustomize.config.k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: Kustomization
resources:
- deployment.yaml
- service.yaml

# overlays/prod/kustomization.yaml:
apiVersion: kustomize.config.k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: Kustomization
resources:
- ../../base
namePrefix: prod-
namespace: production
patches:
- patch: |-
    - op: replace
      path: /spec/replicas
      value: 5
  target:
    kind: Deployment
    name: web

# Preview what will be applied:
kubectl kustomize overlays/prod/

# Apply:
kubectl apply -k overlays/prod/

# Diff before applying:
kubectl diff -k overlays/prod/

# CKA relevance:
# - You may see -k in exam documentation
# - Know that -k applies kustomization directories
# - Not heavily tested but good to recognize
Explanation

Kustomize is built into kubectl via -k flag. It lets you customize YAML manifests without editing originals — using overlays for different environments. On the CKA, you likely won't need to create kustomization files, but know that kubectl apply -k applies a kustomization directory. kubectl kustomize previews the output. Kustomize can add prefixes, namespaces, labels, and patches on top of base resources.

Q6
How do you preview changes before applying a manifest with kubectl diff?
Show Answer
Answer
# See what will change before applying:
kubectl diff -f deployment.yaml
# --- /tmp/LIVE-123
# +++ /tmp/MERGED-456
# @@ -15,7 +15,7 @@
#      containers:
#      - name: nginx
# -      image: nginx:1.24
# +      image: nginx:1.25    ← Will change this!
#        resources:
# -        limits:
# -          memory: 256Mi
# +        limits:
# +          memory: 512Mi    ← And this!

# Exit codes:
# 0 = No differences (already in desired state)
# 1 = Differences found (changes will be made)
# >1 = Error

# Diff with kustomize:
kubectl diff -k overlays/prod/

# Diff a specific resource:
kubectl diff -f service.yaml

# CKA usage: Verify before applying
kubectl diff -f fix.yaml    # Review changes
kubectl apply -f fix.yaml   # Apply if correct

# dry-run alternatives:
kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml --dry-run=server
# Validates against the API server without persisting
# Shows what WOULD happen

kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml --dry-run=client -o yaml
# Client-side validation + outputs the result YAML
Explanation

kubectl diff shows a diff between the live state and what would be applied — like a preview before apply. On the CKA, use diff to verify your changes before applying (especially when editing manifests). Exit code 0 = no changes needed, 1 = changes will be made. --dry-run=server validates against the API server without persisting. Use these to avoid mistakes under time pressure.

Q7
What is imagePullPolicy and how does it affect deployments?
Show Answer
Answer
# Three policies:
# Always         → Pull image every time (default for :latest)
# IfNotPresent   → Pull only if not cached (default for tagged)
# Never          → Only use cached images (error if not cached)

# Example:
spec:
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx:1.25
    imagePullPolicy: Always  # Forces fresh pull every time

# Defaults:
# image: nginx          → imagePullPolicy: Always (implicit :latest)
# image: nginx:latest   → imagePullPolicy: Always
# image: nginx:1.25     → imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent

# Problem scenario:
# CI pushes new image with same tag "nginx:1.25"
# Existing pods have the OLD image cached
# imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent → won't pull the new version!

# Fix:
# Option 1: Use unique tags (nginx:1.25-build-456)
# Option 2: Set imagePullPolicy: Always
# Option 3: Delete pods to force re-pull

# CKA gotcha:
# Using :latest is BAD practice:
# - Not reproducible (you don't know which version is running)
# - Always pulls (slower restarts)
# - Can't rollback reliably

# Best practice:
image: nginx:1.25.3   # Specific tag
imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent  # Cache-friendly
Explanation

imagePullPolicy controls when the container runtime downloads the image. Always = pull every time (ensures latest), IfNotPresent = use cache if available (faster), Never = cache only. On the CKA, know the defaults: :latest tag → Always, specific tag → IfNotPresent. Using :latest is an anti-pattern because you can't reliably track or rollback versions. Use specific tags in production.

Q8
How do you pause and resume a Deployment rollout?
Show Answer
Answer
# Pause a rollout (stop mid-update):
kubectl rollout pause deployment/webapp
# deployment.apps/webapp paused

# While paused:
# - Current state is frozen
# - You can make MULTIPLE changes without triggering rollouts
kubectl set image deployment/webapp nginx=nginx:1.25
kubectl set resources deployment/webapp \
  --limits=cpu=500m,memory=512Mi
kubectl set env deployment/webapp APP_ENV=production
# None of these trigger a rollout yet!

# Resume to apply ALL changes at once:
kubectl rollout resume deployment/webapp
# deployment.apps/webapp resumed
# Now all 3 changes roll out in a SINGLE rollout! ✓

# Without pause, each change triggers a separate rollout:
# Change 1 → rollout starts → Change 2 → rollout restarts → slow!

# Check if paused:
kubectl rollout status deployment/webapp
# "deployment is paused"

# Use case on CKA:
# "Make multiple changes to a deployment and apply them together"
# 1. kubectl rollout pause deployment/webapp
# 2. Make all changes
# 3. kubectl rollout resume deployment/webapp

# Cancel a rollout (if something goes wrong):
kubectl rollout undo deployment/webapp  # Reverts changes
Explanation

Pausing batches multiple changes into a single rollout. Without pause, each set image, set env, or set resources triggers a separate rolling update (wasteful). Pause → make all changes → resume = one clean rollout. On the CKA, this saves time when you need to update image + env vars + resources simultaneously. rollout undo works even on paused deployments.

Q9
What is the Recreate strategy and when would you use it instead of RollingUpdate?
Show Answer
Answer
# RollingUpdate (default):
# Gradually replaces pods: old ↓ new ↑
# Zero downtime (with proper probes)
# Two versions run simultaneously during transition

# Recreate:
# Kills ALL old pods first, then creates new ones
# Brief downtime between old and new
# Only ONE version runs at a time

spec:
  strategy:
    type: Recreate  # No rollingUpdate sub-fields needed

# When to use Recreate:
# 1. Database migrations — can't have two schema versions
# 2. Persistent volume — RWO volume can't mount on two pods
# 3. Application can't handle two versions concurrently
# 4. Port conflicts — new and old both need the same port

# Timeline:
# RollingUpdate:  old-1 old-2 old-3
#                 old-1 old-2 new-1
#                 old-1 new-1 new-2
#                 new-1 new-2 new-3  ← Zero downtime
#
# Recreate:       old-1 old-2 old-3
#                 (none)              ← DOWNTIME
#                 new-1 new-2 new-3

# CKA: Know both strategies exist
# Default is RollingUpdate (most common)
# Set Recreate when asked "ensure only one version runs"

# Check current strategy:
kubectl describe deployment webapp | grep Strategy
# StrategyType: RollingUpdate
# OR
# StrategyType: Recreate
Explanation

Recreate = terminate all old pods before creating new ones. Use when: two versions can't coexist (DB schema changes, RWO volumes, port conflicts). RollingUpdate = gradually replace (zero downtime, two versions briefly coexist). On the CKA, default is RollingUpdate. Use Recreate only when specifically asked for single-version deployment. Key: Recreate has no maxSurge/maxUnavailable — it's all-or-nothing.

Q10
Complete this timed challenge: create a deployment, update it, check history, and rollback — all in under 2 minutes.
Show Answer
Answer
# Timer starts! ⏱️

# Step 1: Create deployment (15 sec)
kubectl create deploy webapp --image=nginx:1.24 --replicas=3
kubectl annotate deploy webapp kubernetes.io/change-cause="initial v1.24"

# Step 2: Verify running (10 sec)
kubectl rollout status deploy/webapp
# deployment "webapp" successfully rolled out ✓

# Step 3: Update image (10 sec)
kubectl set image deploy/webapp nginx=nginx:1.25
kubectl annotate deploy webapp kubernetes.io/change-cause="upgrade to v1.25" --overwrite

# Step 4: Watch rollout (15 sec)
kubectl rollout status deploy/webapp
# deployment "webapp" successfully rolled out ✓

# Step 5: Check history (10 sec)
kubectl rollout history deploy/webapp
# REVISION  CHANGE-CAUSE
# 1         initial v1.24
# 2         upgrade to v1.25

# Step 6: Rollback to revision 1 (10 sec)
kubectl rollout undo deploy/webapp --to-revision=1

# Step 7: Verify rollback (10 sec)
kubectl describe deploy webapp | grep Image
# Image: nginx:1.24  ← Back to original ✓

kubectl rollout history deploy/webapp
# REVISION  CHANGE-CAUSE
# 2         upgrade to v1.25
# 3         initial v1.24  ← Rollback created rev 3

# Total: ~80 seconds ✓ Under 2 minutes!
Explanation

This drill tests the complete deployment lifecycle. On the CKA: create deploy (imperative, fast), set image (update), rollout status (verify), rollout history (check revisions), rollout undo (rollback). Use annotate with kubernetes.io/change-cause to label each revision. Practice this workflow until it's muscle memory — 2 minutes or less. These commands appear in almost every CKA exam.

Q11
What is GitOps? How does ArgoCD implement the GitOps pattern? Write an ArgoCD Application manifest that watches a Git repo and auto-syncs deployments to the cluster.
Show Answer
Answer
# GitOps Principles:
# 1. Git is the SINGLE source of truth for desired state
# 2. All changes via Git commits (no kubectl apply manually)
# 3. Automated agent reconciles cluster state with Git
# 4. Drift detection: agent alerts/fixes when cluster ≠ Git

# ArgoCD Application CRD:
apiVersion: argoproj.io/v1alpha1
kind: Application
metadata:
  name: anihpj
  namespace: argocd
spec:
  project: default
  source:
    repoURL: https://github.com/org/anihpj-k8s-manifests.git
    targetRevision: main
    path: k8s/production       # Directory with YAML manifests
  destination:
    server: https://kubernetes.default.svc
    namespace: production
  syncPolicy:
    automated:
      prune: true              # Delete resources removed from Git
      selfHeal: true           # Fix manual changes (drift)
    syncOptions:
      - CreateNamespace=true

# How it works:
# 1. Developer pushes change to Git (e.g., update image tag)
# 2. ArgoCD detects the change (polls every 3 min or webhook)
# 3. ArgoCD compares Git state vs cluster state
# 4. If different: auto-applies the changes (sync)
# 5. If someone does kubectl edit: selfHeal reverts it

# Install ArgoCD:
kubectl create namespace argocd
kubectl apply -n argocd -f \
  https://raw.githubusercontent.com/argoproj/argo-cd/stable/manifests/install.yaml

# Access ArgoCD UI:
kubectl port-forward svc/argocd-server -n argocd 8080:443

# CLI: sync manually
argocd app sync anihpj
argocd app get anihpj
Explanation

GitOps treats Git as the single source of truth — the desired cluster state is defined in a Git repo, and an operator (ArgoCD) continuously reconciles the actual cluster state to match. automated.selfHeal: true means any manual kubectl edit or kubectl delete gets reverted automatically. prune: true means deleting a file from Git also deletes the resource from the cluster. The CKA doesn't test ArgoCD directly, but understanding GitOps principles helps you appreciate why declarative YAML and kubectl apply (vs imperative commands) are the recommended workflow in production.

Q12
Install a Helm chart, customize it with values, and show how to manage releases. What is the Helm chart structure, and how does helm template differ from helm install?
Show Answer
Answer
# Helm chart structure:
# mychart/
#   Chart.yaml          ← Metadata (name, version)
#   values.yaml         ← Default configuration values
#   templates/           ← Kubernetes manifests with Go templates
#     deployment.yaml
#     service.yaml
#     ingress.yaml
#   charts/              ← Dependencies (sub-charts)

# Add a repo and install:
helm repo add bitnami https://charts.bitnami.com/bitnami
helm repo update

# Install with default values:
helm install my-postgres bitnami/postgresql -n database
#  NAME: my-postgres
#  STATUS: deployed
#  REVISION: 1

# Install with custom values:
helm install my-postgres bitnami/postgresql -n database \
  --set auth.postgresPassword=secretpass \
  --set primary.resources.requests.memory=256Mi \
  --set primary.persistence.size=50Gi

# Or use a values file:
helm install my-postgres bitnami/postgresql -n database \
  -f custom-values.yaml

# helm template vs helm install:
helm template my-postgres bitnami/postgresql \
  --set auth.postgresPassword=secretpass
# ↑ Renders YAML locally (stdout) — does NOT apply to cluster
# Use this to preview or pipe into kubectl apply

helm install my-postgres bitnami/postgresql \
  --set auth.postgresPassword=secretpass
# ↑ Renders AND applies to cluster. Tracks as a "release"

# Manage releases:
helm list -A                    # List all releases
helm status my-postgres -n database  # Release details
helm upgrade my-postgres bitnami/postgresql \
  --set auth.postgresPassword=newpass  # Upgrade
helm rollback my-postgres 1     # Rollback to revision 1
helm uninstall my-postgres -n database  # Remove

# Show what Helm would render:
helm get manifest my-postgres -n database
Explanation

Helm is the Kubernetes package manager — charts bundle multiple YAML manifests with templated values. helm template renders the YAML locally without applying (useful for review, CI/CD pipelines, or piping into kubectl apply). helm install renders AND applies, tracking the deployment as a "release" with revision history (enabling helm rollback). Key distinction: Helm manages releases server-side (stored as Secrets in the namespace), while helm template is stateless. The CKA may ask you to install something with Helm or use helm template to generate manifests for review. Know: --set for inline values, -f for values files, and helm list to see what's installed.

Q13
Compare Helm and Kustomize. When would you use each? Show how to deploy the same anihpj application using both approaches.
Show Answer
Answer
# HELM approach — templating with values:
# Chart has templates with {{ .Values.image }} placeholders
helm install anihpj ./anihpj-chart \
  --set image.tag=v2 \
  --set replicas=5 \
  --set database.password=secret

# KUSTOMIZE approach — patching base manifests:
# base/deployment.yaml (plain YAML, no templates)
# overlays/production/kustomization.yaml:
apiVersion: kustomize.config.k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: Kustomization
resources:
  - ../../base
namePrefix: prod-
namespace: production
images:
  - name: anihpj
    newTag: v2
replicas:
  - name: anihpj
    count: 5
patchesStrategicMerge:
  - increase-resources.yaml

# Apply with kubectl (built-in!):
kubectl apply -k overlays/production/

# Comparison:
# ┌──────────────────┬────────────────────┬───────────────────┐
# │ Aspect           │ Helm                 │ Kustomize           │
# ├──────────────────┼────────────────────┼───────────────────┤
# │ Approach         │ Templating (Go tmpl) │ Patching (overlays) │
# │ Install needed?  │ Yes (helm binary)    │ No (built into      │
# │                  │                      │ kubectl)            │
# │ Base manifests   │ Templates (not valid │ Plain YAML (valid   │
# │                  │ YAML until rendered) │ as-is)              │
# │ Release tracking │ Yes (revisions)      │ No                  │
# │ Rollback         │ helm rollback        │ Git revert + apply  │
# │ Dependencies     │ Sub-charts           │ Remote bases        │
# │ CKA relevance    │ Know helm commands   │ kubectl apply -k    │
# └──────────────────┴────────────────────┴───────────────────┘

# When to use which:
# Helm:      3rd-party apps (PostgreSQL, Prometheus, nginx-ingress)
# Kustomize: Your own apps with per-environment overrides
# Both:      Helm for install, then Kustomize to patch Helm output
Explanation

Helm uses templating — base files have {{ .Values.x }} placeholders that get replaced. Kustomize uses patching — base manifests are valid YAML, and overlays modify them without touching the originals. Kustomize is built into kubectl (kubectl apply -k), while Helm needs a separate binary. Use Helm for installing third-party charts (databases, monitoring). Use Kustomize for your own apps with dev/staging/prod overlays. On the CKA, kubectl apply -k (Kustomize) is more likely to appear since it's built-in. Know both approaches conceptually.

Q14
What would an ArgoCD-managed deployment workflow look like for the anihpj application? Describe how a code change flows from commit to running in the cluster, and how drift is handled.
Show Answer
Answer
# ArgoCD GitOps Deployment Flow for anihpj:

# 1. COMMIT: Developer updates image tag in Git
#    k8s/production/deployment.yaml:
#    image: anihpj:v2    (was v1)
#    git commit -m "Upgrade anihpj to v2"
#    git push origin main

# 2. DETECT: ArgoCD detects change
#    - Polls Git repo every 3 minutes (or webhook triggers)
#    - Compares Git manifests vs live cluster state
#    - Status changes: Synced → OutOfSync

# 3. SYNC: ArgoCD applies the change
#    - With automated sync: applies immediately
#    - Without: shows OutOfSync in UI, waits for manual sync
#    argocd app sync anihpj

# 4. VERIFY: ArgoCD monitors rollout
#    - Watches Deployment rollout progress
#    - Status: Progressing → Healthy (or Degraded if fails)
#    argocd app get anihpj
#    Health Status: Healthy
#    Sync Status:   Synced

# 5. DRIFT DETECTION AND SELF-HEAL:
#    Someone does: kubectl scale deploy anihpj --replicas=1
#    ArgoCD detects: cluster state ≠ Git state
#    With selfHeal: true → ArgoCD reverts to Git's replica count
#    Without: Shows OutOfSync, alerts admin

# 6. ROLLBACK (if v2 is broken):
#    Option A: Git revert + push → ArgoCD syncs to v1
#    Option B: argocd app rollback anihpj
#    Git revert is preferred (maintains Git as source of truth)

# Verify the full pipeline:
argocd app list
# NAME     SYNC     HEALTH    PROJECT
# anihpj  Synced   Healthy   default

argocd app history anihpj
# ID  DATE                 REVISION
# 1   2024-01-15 10:00:00  abc1234
# 2   2024-01-15 14:30:00  def5678  ← current
Explanation

The ArgoCD workflow enforces a strict Git → Cluster pipeline: no manual kubectl apply changes survive because selfHeal reverts them. This is the core GitOps benefit — you always know what's running by looking at Git, and there's a full audit trail of every change. Rollbacks are just git revert. The CKA doesn't test ArgoCD directly, but understanding this CI/CD pattern explains why Kubernetes emphasizes declarative manifests, kubectl apply (vs create), and version control. In production, ArgoCD or Flux is the standard way to deploy to Kubernetes clusters.

🔧 Part 11: Extending and Hardening Chapters 38-40

Chapter 38: Extending Kubernetes — CRDs, Operators & API Extensions

Learn how to extend the Kubernetes API with Custom Resource Definitions, build automation with Operators, use kubectl plugins, and implement Admission Webhooks — all demonstrated with the anihpj Django project.

🔴 Advanced ⏱️ ~2.5 hours 🔧 Extensions 📝 CKA EXAM

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) — extend the K8s API with your own resource types
  • ✅ The Operator Pattern — CRD + custom controller for application lifecycle automation
  • ✅ API Aggregation Layer — extending the API server with additional API servers
  • ✅ kubectl Plugins — extend kubectl with krew and custom commands
  • ✅ Admission Webhooks — ValidatingWebhookConfiguration and MutatingWebhookConfiguration
  • ✅ ValidatingAdmissionPolicy — CEL-based policy enforcement without webhooks
CKA Exam Relevance
Cluster Architecture 25% Workloads 15%
CRDs and the CKA Exam

The CKA may ask you to create a CRD, create custom resources, or troubleshoot why a custom resource isn't working. Understanding how CRDs extend the API is also essential for comprehending tools you already use — like cert-manager's Certificate resource or Prometheus Operator's ServiceMonitor.

  • Know how to — kubectl get crd, kubectl explain <custom-resource>, create a CRD YAML
  • Understand — the Operator pattern (CRD + Controller), why it matters for day-2 operations
  • Bookmark — kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/extend-kubernetes/custom-resources/custom-resource-definitions/
📋
CRDs

Define new resource types stored in etcd — the simplest way to extend the API

🤖
Operators

CRD + Controller — automate complex application lifecycle management

🔌
Webhooks

Intercept API requests to validate or mutate resources before persistence

🧩
API Aggregation

Register external API servers for custom storage backends and real-time data

📝Define CRD
→
📤kubectl apply
→
✅API Registered
→
🏗️Create CRs
→
🤖Controller Acts

38.1 Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs)

CONCEPT 📝 CKA EXAM

A Custom Resource Definition (CRD) lets you create entirely new resource types in Kubernetes. Once registered, your custom resources behave just like built-in ones — you can kubectl get, describe, delete, and apply them.

💡 Why CRDs Matter

Every popular Kubernetes tool uses CRDs: cert-manager creates Certificate resources, Prometheus Operator creates ServiceMonitor resources, ArgoCD creates Application resources. Understanding CRDs is understanding how the K8s ecosystem works.

CRD vs Built-in Resources

 ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
 │                    Kubernetes API Server                  │
 ├──────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────┤
 │   BUILT-IN RESOURCES │       CUSTOM RESOURCES (CRDs)     │
 │   ─────────────────  │       ───────────────────────     │
 │   Pod          v1    │       Certificate   cert-manager  │
 │   Deployment   apps  │       ServiceMonitor prometheus   │
 │   Service      v1    │       Application   argocd        │
 │   ConfigMap    v1    │       DjangoApp     anihpj (ours!)│
 │   Secret       v1    │       VirtualService istio        │
 │                      │                                   │
 │   API Group: core,   │       API Group: custom defined   │
 │   apps, networking   │       e.g. apps.anihpj.io/v1     │
 └──────────────────────┴───────────────────────────────────┘

Creating a CRD — anihpj DjangoApp Example

Let's create a custom DjangoApp resource type that describes a Django application deployment:

djangoapp-crd.yaml — Custom Resource Definition 
apiVersion: apiextensions.k8s.io/v1
kind: CustomResourceDefinition
metadata:
  name: djangoapps.apps.anihpj.io    # Must match: plural.group
spec:
  group: apps.anihpj.io              # API group for our custom resources
  versions:
    - name: v1                        # Version of our API
      served: true                    # Enable this version
      storage: true                   # Store objects in this version
      schema:
        openAPIV3Schema:              # Validation schema (required!)
          type: object
          properties:
            spec:
              type: object
              required: ["image", "replicas"]
              properties:
                image:
                  type: string
                  description: "Container image for the Django app"
                replicas:
                  type: integer
                  minimum: 1
                  maximum: 20
                database:
                  type: object
                  properties:
                    engine:
                      type: string
                      enum: ["postgresql", "mysql", "sqlite3"]
                    host:
                      type: string
                port:
                  type: integer
                  default: 8000
            status:
              type: object
              properties:
                availableReplicas:
                  type: integer
                phase:
                  type: string
      subresources:
        status: {}                    # Enable /status subresource
      additionalPrinterColumns:       # Custom columns for kubectl get
        - name: Image
          type: string
          jsonPath: .spec.image
        - name: Replicas
          type: integer
          jsonPath: .spec.replicas
        - name: Phase
          type: string
          jsonPath: .status.phase
        - name: Age
          type: date
          jsonPath: .metadata.creationTimestamp
  scope: Namespaced                   # Namespaced (vs Cluster)
  names:
    plural: djangoapps                # kubectl get djangoapps
    singular: djangoapp               # kubectl get djangoapp
    kind: DjangoApp                   # kind: DjangoApp in YAML
    shortNames:
      - dja                           # kubectl get dja

Registering and Using the CRD

terminal — CRD lifecycle 
# Step 1: Register the CRD
kubectl apply -f djangoapp-crd.yaml
# customresourcedefinition.apiextensions.k8s.io/djangoapps.apps.anihpj.io created

# Step 2: Verify CRD is registered
kubectl get crd djangoapps.apps.anihpj.io
# NAME                          CREATED AT
# djangoapps.apps.anihpj.io     2026-04-02T10:00:00Z

# Step 3: The new resource type is now available!
kubectl api-resources | grep djangoapp
# djangoapps   dja   apps.anihpj.io/v1   true   DjangoApp

# Step 4: kubectl explain works on CRDs too!
kubectl explain djangoapp.spec
# KIND:     DjangoApp
# VERSION:  apps.anihpj.io/v1
# FIELD:    spec
# FIELDS:
#   database     Object   ...
#   image        string   Container image for the Django app
#   replicas     integer  ...

Creating a Custom Resource Instance

anihpj-djangoapp.yaml — Custom Resource instance 
apiVersion: apps.anihpj.io/v1
kind: DjangoApp
metadata:
  name: anihpj-web
  namespace: anihpj
spec:
  image: anihpj/jobpost:v2.1
  replicas: 3
  database:
    engine: postgresql
    host: postgres-svc.anihpj.svc.cluster.local
  port: 8000
terminal — managing custom resources 
# Create the custom resource
kubectl apply -f anihpj-djangoapp.yaml

# List custom resources (short name works!)
kubectl get dja -n anihpj
# NAME         IMAGE                  REPLICAS   PHASE   AGE
# anihpj-web   anihpj/jobpost:v2.1    3                  5s

# Describe it — just like any built-in resource
kubectl describe djangoapp anihpj-web -n anihpj

# Edit it
kubectl edit djangoapp anihpj-web -n anihpj

# Delete it
kubectl delete djangoapp anihpj-web -n anihpj

# Delete the CRD (WARNING: deletes ALL custom resources of this type!)
kubectl delete crd djangoapps.apps.anihpj.io
⚠️ CRD Deletion Warning

Deleting a CRD deletes ALL custom resources of that type across all namespaces. This is a cluster-wide destructive operation. Always back up custom resources before deleting a CRD: kubectl get djangoapps --all-namespaces -o yaml > backup.yaml

CRD Common Mistakes on the CKA

  • ❌ Wrong metadata.name — must be plural.group (e.g., djangoapps.apps.anihpj.io), not just the kind name
  • ❌ Missing openAPIV3Schema — required since K8s v1.16. Without it, the CRD won't be accepted
  • ❌ Forgetting served: true and storage: true — at least one version must have both set to true
  • ✅ Always verify — kubectl get crd | grep yourname after creating. If it's not listed, check kubectl describe crd for errors

38.2 The Operator Pattern

CONCEPT

An Operator = CRD + Custom Controller. The CRD defines what your custom resource looks like, and the controller watches for those resources and takes action to make reality match the desired state.

Operator Pattern — How It Works

 ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
 │                     THE OPERATOR PATTERN                     │
 ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
 │                                                              │
 │  YOU create:        DjangoApp YAML                          │
 │       │              (desired state)                         │
 │       ▼                                                      │
 │  ┌──────────┐     ┌────────────────────┐                    │
 │  │ API      │────▶│ Controller (Watch)  │                    │
 │  │ Server   │     │                    │                    │
 │  │ (stores  │     │ "DjangoApp created" │                    │
 │  │  CRD)    │     │  → Create Deployment│                    │
 │  └──────────┘     │  → Create Service   │                    │
 │                   │  → Create ConfigMap  │                    │
 │                   │  → Create Ingress    │                    │
 │                   │  → Run migrations    │                    │
 │                   │  → Update status     │                    │
 │                   └────────────────────┘                    │
 │                           │                                  │
 │                           ▼                                  │
 │             ┌─────────────────────────┐                     │
 │             │    RECONCILIATION LOOP   │                     │
 │             │  Watch → Compare → Act   │                     │
 │             │  (desired vs actual)     │                     │
 │             └─────────────────────────┘                     │
 └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Real-World Operators You Already Use

OperatorCRD It CreatesWhat It Automates
cert-managerCertificate, ClusterIssuerTLS certificate provisioning and renewal via Let's Encrypt
Prometheus OperatorServiceMonitor, PrometheusRuleMonitoring target discovery and alerting rules
PostgreSQL OperatorPostgresClusterDatabase provisioning, backups, failover, upgrades
ArgoCDApplication, AppProjectGitOps deployment sync and health monitoring
Ingress NGINXIngressClassHTTP routing based on Ingress resource specs
terminal — exploring Operators in your cluster 
# List ALL CRDs in the cluster — see what Operators installed
kubectl get crd
# NAME                                      CREATED AT
# certificates.cert-manager.io              2026-01-01T00:00:00Z
# clusterissuers.cert-manager.io            2026-01-01T00:00:00Z
# applications.argoproj.io                  2026-01-15T00:00:00Z
# servicemonitors.monitoring.coreos.com     2026-02-01T00:00:00Z

# List custom resources for a specific CRD
kubectl get certificates --all-namespaces
kubectl get servicemonitors --all-namespaces

# Check which controller manages a CRD
kubectl get deployment -n cert-manager
# NAME                      READY   AGE
# cert-manager              1/1     90d
# cert-manager-cainjector   1/1     90d
# cert-manager-webhook      1/1     90d
💡 Operator vs Controller

All Operators are controllers, but not all controllers are Operators. The built-in Deployment controller is a controller (it reconciles Deployments), but it's not called an "Operator" because it manages generic workloads. An Operator specifically manages a complex, stateful application with domain-specific knowledge (e.g., how to back up PostgreSQL, how to rotate TLS certificates).

🔄 Operator Reconciliation Loop

The controller continuously watches for changes and reconciles desired state with actual state — the same pattern Kubernetes uses internally for Deployments, Services, etc.

1
Watch

Controller subscribes to CRD events via the API server — create, update, delete notifications for DjangoApp resources

2
Compare

Read the DjangoApp spec (desired: 3 replicas, v2.1 image) and check current cluster state (actual: 2 replicas, v2.0)

3
Act

Create/update child resources — scale Deployment to 3, update image to v2.1, run database migrations

4
Update Status

Write back to DjangoApp .status — set phase to "Running", availableReplicas to 3

5
Repeat

Loop back to Watch — any drift (pod crash, manual change) triggers re-reconciliation automatically

38.3 API Aggregation Layer

CONCEPT

The API Aggregation Layer allows you to extend the Kubernetes API by registering additional API servers. Unlike CRDs (which store data in etcd via the main API server), aggregated APIs run their own backend — useful for resources that need custom storage or complex logic.

CRD vs API Aggregation — When to Use Each

 ┌──────────────────────────┐    ┌──────────────────────────┐
 │     CRD APPROACH          │    │   API AGGREGATION         │
 ├──────────────────────────┤    ├──────────────────────────┤
 │ ✅ Simple to create       │    │ ✅ Custom storage backend │
 │ ✅ No extra server needed │    │ ✅ Custom validation logic│
 │ ✅ Stored in etcd         │    │ ✅ Protocol translation   │
 │ ❌ Limited validation     │    │ ❌ Complex to build       │
 │ ❌ No custom storage      │    │ ❌ Requires running server│
 │                          │    │                          │
 │ USE FOR:                  │    │ USE FOR:                  │
 │ Most custom resources     │    │ metrics-server            │
 │ App configuration         │    │ Custom metrics API        │
 │ Operator CRDs             │    │ Complex sub-resources     │
 └──────────────────────────┘    └──────────────────────────┘

The most common example you interact with daily is metrics-server — it registers an aggregated API at /apis/metrics.k8s.io so that kubectl top pods and kubectl top nodes work. It doesn't store data in etcd; it collects real-time metrics from kubelets.

📋 Use CRDs When
  • You need simple CRUD operations on custom types
  • Data fits naturally in etcd (configs, specs, state)
  • You want easy setup with just a YAML file
  • Validation via OpenAPI schema is sufficient
  • Examples: cert-manager, ArgoCD, Prometheus Operator
🔌 Use API Aggregation When
  • You need a custom storage backend (not etcd)
  • Data is real-time/ephemeral (metrics, logs)
  • You need complex protocol translation
  • Sub-resources need custom behavior
  • Examples: metrics-server, custom metrics API
terminal — check registered API services 
# List all registered API services
kubectl get apiservice | head -20
# NAME                       SERVICE                      AVAILABLE
# v1.                        Local                        True
# v1.apps                    Local                        True
# v1beta1.metrics.k8s.io     kube-system/metrics-server   True  ← aggregated!

# "Local" = built-in API, handled by main API server
# A service reference = aggregated API, handled by separate server

# Check a specific aggregated API
kubectl get apiservice v1beta1.metrics.k8s.io -o yaml
# spec:
#   group: metrics.k8s.io
#   service:
#     name: metrics-server
#     namespace: kube-system

38.4 kubectl Plugins and krew

COMMANDS

kubectl supports a plugin mechanism — any executable named kubectl-PLUGINNAME in your PATH becomes a kubectl subcommand. krew is the plugin package manager (like apt/yum for kubectl).

terminal — kubectl plugins 
# List installed plugins
kubectl plugin list

# Install krew (the plugin manager)
# See: https://krew.sigs.k8s.io/docs/user-guide/setup/install/

# Install useful plugins via krew
kubectl krew install ctx       # Fast context switching
kubectl krew install ns        # Fast namespace switching
kubectl krew install neat      # Clean up kubectl output (remove managed fields)
kubectl krew install tree      # Show resource ownership hierarchy
kubectl krew install who-can   # RBAC analysis — who can do what?

# Usage examples
kubectl ctx production         # Switch to production context
kubectl ns anihpj              # Switch to anihpj namespace
kubectl neat get pod web-0 -o yaml   # Clean YAML output
kubectl tree deployment anihpj-web   # Show: Deployment → ReplicaSet → Pods
kubectl who-can create pods -n anihpj
📝 CKA Note

kubectl plugins (krew) are NOT available on the CKA exam. However, you should understand how the plugin mechanism works conceptually: any executable named kubectl-<name> in your PATH is automatically discovered as a plugin. This is relevant for understanding how organizations extend kubectl for their workflows.

38.5 Admission Webhooks

CONCEPT 📝 CKA EXAM

After a request passes authentication and authorization, it goes through admission control. Kubernetes has two types of dynamic admission webhooks:

API Request Flow — Where Webhooks Fit

 kubectl apply ──▶ API Server
                      │
            ┌─────────┼─────────┐
            ▼         ▼         ▼
       Authentication  Authorization  Admission Control
       (Who are you?)  (Can you do    ┌─────────────────┐
                        this?)        │ 1. Mutating      │
                                     │    Webhooks       │
                                     │    (modify req)   │
                                     │                   │
                                     │ 2. Schema         │
                                     │    Validation     │
                                     │                   │
                                     │ 3. Validating     │
                                     │    Webhooks       │
                                     │    (accept/reject)│
                                     └─────────────────┘
                                            │
                                            ▼
                                       etcd (persist)
FeatureMutatingWebhookConfigurationValidatingWebhookConfiguration
When it runsFirst — before validationAfter mutation + schema validation
Can modify request?✅ Yes — can add/change/remove fields❌ No — can only accept or reject
Use caseInject sidecars, add labels, set defaultsEnforce policies, block insecure configs
ExampleIstio sidecar injectionDeny pods without resource limits

Example: Validating Webhook — Require Labels on Pods

🎯 Admission Control — The API Server Gateway

Every resource mutation passes through admission control. Mutating webhooks run first (can change the request), then validating webhooks (can only accept/reject). This is your last chance to enforce policies before data hits etcd.

validating-webhook.yaml — require "team" label on all pods 
apiVersion: admissionregistration.k8s.io/v1
kind: ValidatingWebhookConfiguration
metadata:
  name: require-team-label
webhooks:
  - name: require-team-label.anihpj.io
    admissionReviewVersions: ["v1"]
    sideEffects: None
    clientConfig:
      service:
        name: webhook-server          # Your webhook service
        namespace: webhook-system
        path: /validate-pods
      caBundle: <base64-encoded-CA>   # CA cert for TLS
    rules:
      - apiGroups: [""]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        resources: ["pods"]
        operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
    namespaceSelector:                # Only in labeled namespaces
      matchLabels:
        webhook-enabled: "true"
    failurePolicy: Fail               # Reject if webhook unreachable
    timeoutSeconds: 5
⚠️ failurePolicy: Fail vs Ignore

Fail (default) — if the webhook is down, ALL matching requests are rejected. Safe but can break your cluster if the webhook crashes. Ignore — if the webhook is down, requests pass through without validation. Less safe but prevents cluster lockout. For production, use Fail with high availability for the webhook server.

CKA Exam Context — You probably won't build a webhook server on the exam, but you need to understand the ValidatingWebhookConfiguration and MutatingWebhookConfiguration resource structure. Know how to kubectl get validatingwebhookconfigurations and troubleshoot when a webhook blocks legitimate requests.
