38.6 ValidatingAdmissionPolicy (CEL-Based)

CONCEPT 📝 CKA EXAM

ValidatingAdmissionPolicy (GA in Kubernetes v1.30) lets you write validation rules using Common Expression Language (CEL) — no external webhook server needed! This is the future of in-cluster policy enforcement.

require-replicas-policy.yaml — No single-replica deployments 
# Step 1: Define the policy
apiVersion: admissionregistration.k8s.io/v1
kind: ValidatingAdmissionPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-minimum-replicas
spec:
  failurePolicy: Fail
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: ["apps"]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
        resources: ["deployments"]
  validations:
    - expression: "object.spec.replicas >= 2"
      message: "Deployments must have at least 2 replicas for high availability"
    - expression: "has(object.spec.template.spec.containers) && object.spec.template.spec.containers.all(c, has(c.resources) && has(c.resources.limits))"
      message: "All containers must have resource limits defined"
---
# Step 2: Bind the policy to namespaces
apiVersion: admissionregistration.k8s.io/v1
kind: ValidatingAdmissionPolicyBinding
metadata:
  name: require-minimum-replicas-binding
spec:
  policyName: require-minimum-replicas
  validationActions: [Deny]          # Deny, Warn, or Audit
  matchResources:
    namespaceSelector:
      matchLabels:
        environment: production       # Only enforce in production
terminal — testing the policy 
# Label namespace for policy enforcement
kubectl label namespace anihpj environment=production

# Apply the policy
kubectl apply -f require-replicas-policy.yaml

# Test — this should be DENIED
kubectl create deployment test --image=nginx --replicas=1 -n anihpj
# Error: Deployments must have at least 2 replicas for high availability

# This should SUCCEED
kubectl create deployment test --image=nginx --replicas=3 -n anihpj
# deployment.apps/test created

# List policies
kubectl get validatingadmissionpolicies
kubectl get validatingadmissionpolicybindings
💡 CEL Expression Cheat Sheet

object = the resource being created/updated. oldObject = the previous version (for UPDATE). has(object.spec.field) = check if field exists. object.spec.containers.all(c, expr) = check all items. object.metadata.labels.exists(k, k == "team") = check labels.

38.7 RBAC for Custom Resources

COMMANDS 📝 CKA EXAM

Custom resources follow the same RBAC model as built-in resources. You need to grant access to the CRD's API group and resource name.

crd-rbac.yaml — RBAC for DjangoApp CRD 
# Role for managing DjangoApp custom resources
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: djangoapp-manager
  namespace: anihpj
rules:
  - apiGroups: ["apps.anihpj.io"]     # CRD API group
    resources: ["djangoapps"]          # CRD plural name
    verbs: ["get", "list", "watch", "create", "update", "patch", "delete"]
  - apiGroups: ["apps.anihpj.io"]
    resources: ["djangoapps/status"]   # Status subresource
    verbs: ["get", "update", "patch"]
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: djangoapp-manager-binding
  namespace: anihpj
subjects:
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: anihpj-operator
    namespace: anihpj
roleRef:
  kind: Role
  name: djangoapp-manager
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io

Verify RBAC for Custom Resources

# Check if a service account can access CRDs
kubectl auth can-i get djangoapps --as=system:serviceaccount:anihpj:anihpj-operator -n anihpj
# yes

kubectl auth can-i delete djangoapps --as=system:serviceaccount:anihpj:anihpj-operator -n anihpj
# yes

# Note: to CREATE CRDs themselves (not instances), you need cluster-level access:
kubectl auth can-i create customresourcedefinitions --as=system:serviceaccount:anihpj:anihpj-operator
# no  ← only cluster admins should create/delete CRDs

📝 Chapter 38 — CKA Practice Questions

Q38.1
Create a CRD called backups.data.anihpj.io with a Backup kind. It should be namespaced, have a v1 version, and require spec.source (string) and spec.schedule (string) fields. Then create a Backup resource in the anihpj namespace.
Show Answer 
# CRD definition
apiVersion: apiextensions.k8s.io/v1
kind: CustomResourceDefinition
metadata:
  name: backups.data.anihpj.io
spec:
  group: data.anihpj.io
  versions:
    - name: v1
      served: true
      storage: true
      schema:
        openAPIV3Schema:
          type: object
          properties:
            spec:
              type: object
              required: ["source", "schedule"]
              properties:
                source:
                  type: string
                schedule:
                  type: string
  scope: Namespaced
  names:
    plural: backups
    singular: backup
    kind: Backup
---
# Backup instance
apiVersion: data.anihpj.io/v1
kind: Backup
metadata:
  name: db-daily
  namespace: anihpj
spec:
  source: postgresql
  schedule: "0 2 * * *"
Explanation

CRD metadata.name MUST be plural.group — so backups.data.anihpj.io. The schema is required and validates that source and schedule are strings. Once the CRD is registered, you create instances using the CRD's apiVersion (group/version) and kind. On the CKA, always apply the CRD first, verify with kubectl get crd, then create the custom resource.

Q38.2
List all CRDs in the cluster. Find which CRDs belong to cert-manager. Then check who can create certificates (cert-manager CRD) in the anihpj namespace.
Show Answer 
# List all CRDs
kubectl get crd

# Filter for cert-manager CRDs
kubectl get crd | grep cert-manager
# certificates.cert-manager.io
# certificaterequests.cert-manager.io
# clusterissuers.cert-manager.io
# issuers.cert-manager.io
# orders.acme.cert-manager.io
# challenges.acme.cert-manager.io

# Check who can create certificates
kubectl auth can-i create certificates.cert-manager.io \
  --as=system:serviceaccount:anihpj:default -n anihpj

# Use who-can (if available) for comprehensive RBAC analysis
kubectl who-can create certificates.cert-manager.io -n anihpj
Explanation

CRDs are cluster-scoped resources — kubectl get crd lists all of them regardless of namespace. To filter by a specific operator, grep for the group name (e.g., cert-manager). When checking RBAC for custom resources, use the full resource name including the API group: certificates.cert-manager.io, not just certificates. The kubectl auth can-i command also works with --as= to test permissions for other users/service accounts.

Q38.3
Create a ValidatingAdmissionPolicy that prevents creating Pods with privileged: true security context. Bind it to the anihpj namespace only.
Show Answer 
apiVersion: admissionregistration.k8s.io/v1
kind: ValidatingAdmissionPolicy
metadata:
  name: deny-privileged-pods
spec:
  failurePolicy: Fail
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: [""]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations: ["CREATE"]
        resources: ["pods"]
  validations:
    - expression: >-
        object.spec.containers.all(c,
          !has(c.securityContext) ||
          !has(c.securityContext.privileged) ||
          c.securityContext.privileged == false)
      message: "Privileged containers are not allowed"
---
apiVersion: admissionregistration.k8s.io/v1
kind: ValidatingAdmissionPolicyBinding
metadata:
  name: deny-privileged-pods-binding
spec:
  policyName: deny-privileged-pods
  validationActions: [Deny]
  matchResources:
    namespaceSelector:
      matchLabels:
        kubernetes.io/metadata.name: anihpj
Explanation

ValidatingAdmissionPolicy uses CEL expressions for in-cluster policy enforcement without external webhooks. The expression checks every container in the pod spec — if none has privileged: true, the pod is allowed. The binding uses namespaceSelector to limit enforcement to the anihpj namespace (using the automatic label kubernetes.io/metadata.name). This replaces the need for OPA Gatekeeper or Kyverno for simple policies. Available since K8s v1.30 GA.

Q38.4
Explain the difference between CRDs and API Aggregation. Give an example of when you'd use each approach. How does metrics-server use API aggregation?
Show Answer 
# Check if metrics-server uses API aggregation
kubectl get apiservice v1beta1.metrics.k8s.io -o yaml
# spec:
#   group: metrics.k8s.io
#   service:
#     name: metrics-server
#     namespace: kube-system

# Verify the aggregated API works
kubectl top pods -n anihpj
kubectl top nodes
Explanation

CRDs: Store data in etcd, no custom server needed, simple to create. Use for: app configuration, operator-managed resources (Certificates, Applications). API Aggregation: Runs a separate API server, can have custom storage backend, complex to build. Use for: real-time data that shouldn't be in etcd (metrics), protocol translation. metrics-server uses API aggregation because it serves real-time CPU/memory metrics collected from kubelets — storing this in etcd would be wasteful and slow. It registers at metrics.k8s.io and proxies kubectl top requests to its own server.

Q38.5
A ValidatingWebhookConfiguration is blocking pod creation with the error "webhook denied the request". The webhook service is down. How do you temporarily fix this?
Show Answer 
# List validating webhooks
kubectl get validatingwebhookconfigurations

# Check which webhook is failing
kubectl get validatingwebhookconfiguration <name> -o yaml

# Option 1: Change failurePolicy to Ignore (temporary!)
kubectl patch validatingwebhookconfiguration <name> \
  --type='json' -p='[{"op": "replace", "path": "/webhooks/0/failurePolicy", "value": "Ignore"}]'

# Option 2: Delete the webhook configuration entirely (if safe)
kubectl delete validatingwebhookconfiguration <name>

# Option 3: Fix the webhook service (proper fix)
kubectl get svc -n webhook-system
kubectl get pods -n webhook-system
kubectl logs -n webhook-system <webhook-pod>
Explanation

When a webhook with failurePolicy: Fail is unreachable, ALL matching API requests are rejected — this can lock you out of creating/updating resources. Quick fix: patch the failurePolicy to Ignore so requests pass through while you fix the webhook server. The proper fix is to ensure the webhook service and pods are running. On the CKA, if you see "webhook denied the request" errors, check kubectl get validatingwebhookconfigurations and kubectl get mutatingwebhookconfigurations.

Chapter 39: Security Hardening & Best Practices

A comprehensive security hardening guide covering RBAC best practices, Secrets management, API server security, supply chain security, runtime protection with seccomp/AppArmor, and multi-tenancy isolation — all demonstrated with the anihpj Django project.

🔴 Advanced ⏱️ ~3 hours 🔒 Security 📝 CKA EXAM

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ RBAC Hardening — principle of least privilege, avoiding dangerous permissions
  • ✅ Secrets Best Practices — encryption at rest, external secret stores, avoiding anti-patterns
  • ✅ API Server Security — audit logging, anonymous auth, admission control
  • ✅ Supply Chain Security — image scanning, signed images, allowlisting registries
  • ✅ Runtime Security — seccomp profiles, AppArmor, read-only filesystems
  • ✅ Network Hardening — default-deny policies, egress filtering, service mesh
  • ✅ Multi-Tenancy — namespace isolation, resource quotas, network boundaries
CKA Exam Relevance
Cluster Architecture 25% Workloads 15% Security 15%
Security on the CKA Exam

Security topics appear across multiple CKA domains. You may be asked to create RBAC rules, configure network policies, troubleshoot Secret issues, or apply Pod Security Standards. This chapter consolidates all security knowledge into a hardening checklist you can follow during the exam.

  • High Priority — RBAC (ClusterRole/Role, bindings), NetworkPolicy, Secrets encryption
  • Medium Priority — Pod Security Admission, SecurityContext, ServiceAccount tokens
  • Awareness — seccomp, AppArmor, audit logging, image security
🔐
RBAC

Principle of least privilege — who can do what in which namespace

🔑
Secrets

Encryption at rest, volume mounts over env vars, external stores

🛡️
Runtime

seccomp + AppArmor profiles, read-only filesystems, drop capabilities

🌐
Network

Default-deny policies, namespace isolation, egress filtering, mTLS

CKA Security Weight 15%
Cluster Architecture (Security Topics) 25%
This Chapter's Exam Coverage ~40%

39.1 The Kubernetes Security Hardening Checklist

CONCEPT 📝 CKA EXAM

Use this as a reference checklist when securing a cluster. Each item links to the detailed section below.

1
Infrastructure Layer

API server flags, etcd encryption at rest, audit logging, component TLS — secure the foundation

2
Auth & RBAC

Least-privilege roles, disable default SA token mounting, no wildcard permissions, audit bindings

3
Admission Control

Pod Security Standards (restricted), ValidatingAdmissionPolicy, image registry allowlisting

4
Workload Hardening

SecurityContext (runAsNonRoot, readOnlyRootFilesystem), drop ALL capabilities, seccomp profiles

5
Network Isolation

Default-deny NetworkPolicy per namespace, egress filtering, DNS rules, mTLS with service mesh

6
Data Protection

Secrets encrypted at rest, mount as files (not env), external secret stores, credential rotation

Security Layers — Defense in Depth

 ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
 │                  DEFENSE IN DEPTH                            │
 ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
 │                                                              │
 │  Layer 1: CLUSTER INFRASTRUCTURE                            │
 │  ├── API server flags (--anonymous-auth=false)              │
 │  ├── etcd encryption at rest                                │
 │  ├── Audit logging enabled                                  │
 │  └── Component TLS (kubelet, etcd)                          │
 │                                                              │
 │  Layer 2: AUTHENTICATION & AUTHORIZATION                    │
 │  ├── RBAC with least privilege                              │
 │  ├── Disable default SA token auto-mount                    │
 │  ├── No cluster-admin for workloads                         │
 │  └── Audit who has wildcard permissions                     │
 │                                                              │
 │  Layer 3: ADMISSION CONTROL                                 │
 │  ├── Pod Security Admission (restricted)                    │
 │  ├── ValidatingAdmissionPolicy (CEL)                        │
 │  ├── Image allowlisting                                     │
 │  └── Deny privileged containers                              │
 │                                                              │
 │  Layer 4: WORKLOAD SECURITY                                 │
 │  ├── SecurityContext (runAsNonRoot, readOnlyRootFilesystem) │
 │  ├── Resource limits (CPU/memory)                           │
 │  ├── seccomp / AppArmor profiles                            │
 │  └── No hostNetwork, hostPID, hostIPC                       │
 │                                                              │
 │  Layer 5: NETWORK SECURITY                                  │
 │  ├── Default-deny NetworkPolicy                             │
 │  ├── Egress filtering                                       │
 │  ├── Namespace isolation                                    │
 │  └── mTLS (service mesh)                                    │
 │                                                              │
 │  Layer 6: DATA SECURITY                                     │
 │  ├── Secrets encrypted at rest                              │
 │  ├── External secret management (Vault, etc.)               │
 │  ├── No secrets in environment variables                    │
 │  └── Rotate credentials regularly                           │
 │                                                              │
 └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

39.2 RBAC Hardening Deep Dive

CONCEPT 📝 CKA EXAM

Dangerous Permissions to Avoid

These RBAC rules grant far more access than they appear to. Treat them as red flags during security reviews.

Dangerous PermissionWhy It's DangerousWhat to Do Instead
verbs: ["*"] on any resourceGrants ALL operations including deleteList only the verbs you need: ["get", "list", "watch"]
resources: ["*"]Access to ALL resource types in the API groupList specific resources: ["pods", "services"]
apiGroups: ["*"]Access across ALL API groupsSpecify: ["", "apps", "networking.k8s.io"]
create on podsCan exec into nodes, mount secrets, escapeGrant create on deployments instead
escalate on rolesCan grant themselves cluster-adminNever grant to workload service accounts
bind on clusterrolesCan bind any role to any subjectLimit to specific roles with resourceNames
impersonate on users/groupsCan act as any user including cluster-adminNever grant unless absolutely required

RBAC Best Practices — anihpj Example

rbac-hardened.yaml — least-privilege for anihpj app 
# ❌ BAD — overly permissive
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRole
metadata:
  name: bad-example-do-not-use
rules:
  - apiGroups: ["*"]
    resources: ["*"]
    verbs: ["*"]           # This is basically cluster-admin!
---
# ✅ GOOD — least privilege for anihpj web app
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: anihpj-web-role
  namespace: anihpj
rules:
  # Read-only access to ConfigMaps (for Django settings)
  - apiGroups: [""]
    resources: ["configmaps"]
    resourceNames: ["anihpj-config"]  # Only THIS specific ConfigMap
    verbs: ["get", "watch"]
  
  # Read-only access to Secrets (for DB credentials)
  - apiGroups: [""]
    resources: ["secrets"]
    resourceNames: ["anihpj-db-secret"]
    verbs: ["get"]
  
  # No access to pods, services, etc. — the app doesn't need it
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: anihpj-web-binding
  namespace: anihpj
subjects:
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: anihpj-web-sa
    namespace: anihpj
roleRef:
  kind: Role
  name: anihpj-web-role
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io

Audit Existing RBAC Permissions

terminal — RBAC audit commands 
# Find all ClusterRoleBindings that reference cluster-admin
kubectl get clusterrolebindings -o json | \
  jq -r '.items[] | select(.roleRef.name=="cluster-admin") |
  .metadata.name + " → " + (.subjects[]? | .kind + "/" + .name)'

# Check what a specific ServiceAccount can do
kubectl auth can-i --list --as=system:serviceaccount:anihpj:anihpj-web-sa -n anihpj

# Find roles with wildcard permissions
kubectl get roles,clusterroles -A -o json | \
  jq -r '.items[] | select(.rules[]? | .verbs[]? == "*") |
  .kind + "/" + .metadata.name'

# Verify no default SA has extra bindings
kubectl get rolebindings,clusterrolebindings -A -o json | \
  jq -r '.items[] | select(.subjects[]? | .name == "default") |
  .metadata.namespace + "/" + .metadata.name'

RBAC Gotchas on the CKA

  • ❌ Role vs ClusterRole — Role is namespace-scoped, ClusterRole is cluster-wide. Use Role unless you need cross-namespace access
  • ❌ RoleBinding to ClusterRole — this is valid! It scopes the ClusterRole's permissions to a single namespace
  • ❌ Forgetting apiGroup — core resources (pods, services) use "", apps resources use "apps"
  • ✅ Use resourceNames — when possible, restrict access to specific named resources

39.3 Secrets Management Best Practices

CONCEPT 📝 CKA EXAM

Kubernetes Secrets are Base64-encoded, not encrypted by default. Anyone with RBAC access to Secrets or etcd can read them. Here's how to harden Secrets properly.

⚠️ Critical Security Truth

echo "cGFzc3dvcmQ=" | base64 -d → password. Base64 is encoding, NOT encryption. Without additional measures, Secrets in etcd are plaintext-equivalent. NEVER assume Secrets are safe by default.

Enable Encryption at Rest

encryption-config.yaml — encrypt secrets in etcd 
# /etc/kubernetes/encryption-config.yaml
apiVersion: apiserver.config.k8s.io/v1
kind: EncryptionConfiguration
resources:
  - resources:
      - secrets
    providers:
      - aescbc:                      # AES-CBC encryption
          keys:
            - name: key1
              secret: <base64-encoded-32-byte-key>
      - identity: {}                 # Fallback: read unencrypted (for migration)
terminal — enable encryption at rest 
# 1. Generate a 32-byte encryption key
head -c 32 /dev/urandom | base64

# 2. Add --encryption-provider-config flag to kube-apiserver
# Edit: /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml
# Add to spec.containers[0].command:
#   --encryption-provider-config=/etc/kubernetes/encryption-config.yaml

# 3. Mount the encryption config into the API server pod
# Add volume and volumeMount to kube-apiserver.yaml

# 4. Re-encrypt existing Secrets (they're still unencrypted!)
kubectl get secrets --all-namespaces -o json | kubectl replace -f -

# 5. Verify encryption is working
# Read a Secret directly from etcd — should be encrypted
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl get /registry/secrets/anihpj/anihpj-db-secret \
  --cert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt \
  --key=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key \
  --cacert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt | hexdump -C | head

Secrets Anti-Patterns vs Best Practices

Anti-Pattern ❌Best Practice ✅Why
Secrets in env varsMount as files via volumeMountEnv vars appear in logs, process listings, and crash dumps
Secrets in Git reposUse SealedSecrets or external secret storesGit history is permanent — secrets can never be truly removed
Using the default SACreate dedicated ServiceAccountsDefault SA is shared by all pods in a namespace
Auto-mounting SA tokensSet automountServiceAccountToken: falseMost pods don't need API server access
Hardcoding secrets in YAMLUse kubectl create secret or external toolKeeps secret values out of manifest files
How should I provide secrets to my pod?
Need K8s-native?
✅ Use K8s Secret mounted as volume file
Production/enterprise?
✅ External secret store (Vault, AWS SM)
Never use these approaches:
❌ Environment variables — leak in logs/crashes
❌ Hardcoded in YAML — committed to Git history
anihpj-secure-pod.yaml — proper secret handling 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-web
  namespace: anihpj
spec:
  serviceAccountName: anihpj-web-sa
  automountServiceAccountToken: false   # ✅ Don't mount SA token
  containers:
    - name: django
      image: anihpj/jobpost:v2.1
      volumeMounts:
        - name: db-creds
          mountPath: /etc/secrets/db     # ✅ Mount as files
          readOnly: true                 # ✅ Read-only mount
  volumes:
    - name: db-creds
      secret:
        secretName: anihpj-db-secret
        defaultMode: 0400                # ✅ Restrictive permissions

39.4 API Server & Control Plane Security

CONCEPT 📝 CKA EXAM

The API server is the front door to your cluster. Hardening it is your highest priority.

Critical API Server Flags

FlagRecommended ValuePurpose
--anonymous-authfalseDisable unauthenticated access to the API
--enable-admission-pluginsInclude NodeRestriction,PodSecurityRestrict node self-modification, enforce pod security
--audit-log-path/var/log/kubernetes/audit.logEnable audit logging for all API requests
--audit-policy-filePoint to policy YAMLDefine what events to record and at what level
--profilingfalseDisable profiling endpoints (information disclosure)
--insecure-port0 (disabled)No HTTP access — HTTPS only (deprecated, removed in 1.24)
--encryption-provider-configPoint to config fileEncrypt secrets at rest in etcd

Audit Logging — Tracking Who Did What

audit-policy.yaml — sample audit policy 
apiVersion: audit.k8s.io/v1
kind: Policy
rules:
  # Log Secret access at Metadata level (don't log Secret content!)
  - level: Metadata
    resources:
      - group: ""
        resources: ["secrets"]
  
  # Log all write operations at RequestResponse level
  - level: RequestResponse
    verbs: ["create", "update", "patch", "delete"]
    resources:
      - group: ""
        resources: ["pods", "services", "configmaps"]
      - group: "apps"
        resources: ["deployments", "statefulsets", "daemonsets"]
  
  # Log RBAC changes at RequestResponse level
  - level: RequestResponse
    resources:
      - group: "rbac.authorization.k8s.io"
        resources: ["roles", "rolebindings", "clusterroles", "clusterrolebindings"]
  
  # Don't log read-only requests to known non-sensitive endpoints
  - level: None
    resources:
      - group: ""
        resources: ["events"]
    verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
  
  # Catch-all: log everything else at Metadata level
  - level: Metadata

CKA Exam — You may be asked to configure audit logging. Remember the four audit levels: None (no logging), Metadata (log request metadata only), Request (log request body), RequestResponse (log both request and response body). Never log Secret content — use Metadata level for Secrets.

39.5 Runtime Security — seccomp & AppArmor

CONCEPT

seccomp Profiles

seccomp (Secure Computing Mode) restricts which Linux system calls a container can make. By blocking dangerous syscalls, you reduce the attack surface if a container is compromised.

anihpj-seccomp.yaml — seccomp profile for Django app 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-web-hardened
  namespace: anihpj
spec:
  securityContext:
    seccompProfile:
      type: RuntimeDefault          # ✅ Use container runtime's default profile
  containers:
    - name: django
      image: anihpj/jobpost:v2.1
      securityContext:
        runAsNonRoot: true           # ✅ Never run as root
        runAsUser: 1000
        readOnlyRootFilesystem: true # ✅ Prevent filesystem writes
        allowPrivilegeEscalation: false  # ✅ Block setuid/setgid
        capabilities:
          drop: ["ALL"]              # ✅ Drop ALL Linux capabilities
      volumeMounts:
        - name: tmp
          mountPath: /tmp            # Django needs a writable temp dir
  volumes:
    - name: tmp
      emptyDir: {}                   # ✅ Provide writable tmp via emptyDir

AppArmor Profiles

AppArmor is a Linux Security Module that restricts a program's capabilities — file access, network access, and raw socket creation.

apparmor-pod.yaml — apply AppArmor profile 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-web-apparmor
  namespace: anihpj
spec:
  containers:
    - name: django
      image: anihpj/jobpost:v2.1
      securityContext:
        appArmorProfile:
          type: RuntimeDefault       # Use runtime default AppArmor profile
          # type: Localhost           # Or use a custom profile:
          # localhostProfile: anihpj-web-profile
📝 seccomp vs AppArmor

seccomp restricts system calls (kernel-level operations). AppArmor restricts file and network access (path-based). They are complementary — use both for defense in depth. The RuntimeDefault profile for both is a good starting point that blocks obviously dangerous operations without breaking most applications.

🔒 seccomp (System Call Filter)
  • Layer: Kernel syscalls
  • Blocks: mount, reboot, ptrace, raw sockets
  • Config: securityContext.seccompProfile
  • Types: RuntimeDefault, Localhost, Unconfined
  • Best for: Preventing kernel exploits
🛡️ AppArmor (Application Armor)
  • Layer: File/network access paths
  • Blocks: /etc/shadow reads, raw network, specific file writes
  • Config: securityContext.appArmorProfile
  • Types: RuntimeDefault, Localhost, Unconfined
  • Best for: Preventing file-based attacks

39.6 Supply Chain Security

CONCEPT

Securing the container image supply chain prevents compromised or vulnerable images from running in your cluster.

📌
Pin Image Versions

Use specific tags (v2.1) or SHA digests — never :latest

🔍
Scan Images

Run vulnerability scanners (Trivy, Grype) in CI/CD before deployment

✍️
Sign Images

Use cosign/Notation to verify image provenance and integrity

🏢
Allowlist Registries

ValidatingAdmissionPolicy to restrict image sources to trusted repos only

📦
Minimal Base Images

Use -slim or distroless images — fewer packages = fewer vulnerabilities

image-security-best-practices 
# ✅ Best Practice 1: Use specific image tags (NEVER use :latest)
containers:
  - name: django
    image: anihpj/jobpost:v2.1        # ✅ Pinned version
    # image: anihpj/jobpost:latest     # ❌ Mutable, unpredictable
    # image: anihpj/jobpost             # ❌ Defaults to :latest

# ✅ Best Practice 2: Use image digests for maximum immutability
containers:
  - name: django
    image: anihpj/jobpost@sha256:abc123...  # ✅ Content-addressable

# ✅ Best Practice 3: Set imagePullPolicy
containers:
  - name: django
    image: anihpj/jobpost:v2.1
    imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent     # or Always for security-critical apps

# ✅ Best Practice 4: Use minimal base images
# Dockerfile:
# FROM python:3.12-slim              # ✅ Minimal — fewer vulnerabilities
# FROM python:3.12                    # ❌ Full — larger attack surface
# FROM ubuntu:24.04                   # ❌ General purpose — bloated

Restrict Image Sources with ValidatingAdmissionPolicy

restrict-registries.yaml — only allow trusted registries 
apiVersion: admissionregistration.k8s.io/v1
kind: ValidatingAdmissionPolicy
metadata:
  name: restrict-image-registries
spec:
  failurePolicy: Fail
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: [""]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
        resources: ["pods"]
  validations:
    - expression: >-
        object.spec.containers.all(c,
          c.image.startsWith('anihpj/') ||
          c.image.startsWith('registry.company.com/') ||
          c.image.startsWith('gcr.io/distroless/'))
      message: "Images must come from approved registries: anihpj/, registry.company.com/, gcr.io/distroless/"

39.7 Multi-Tenancy & Namespace Isolation

CONCEPT 📝 CKA EXAM

In multi-tenant clusters, namespaces are the primary isolation boundary. But namespaces alone aren't enough — you need RBAC, NetworkPolicy, ResourceQuota, and Pod Security working together.

Multi-Tenancy Isolation Stack

 ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
 │              MULTI-TENANCY ISOLATION STACK                 │
 ├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
 │                                                            │
 │  Namespace: team-a        │  Namespace: team-b             │
 │  ┌──────────────────┐    │  ┌──────────────────┐          │
 │  │ RBAC: team-a-role │    │  │ RBAC: team-b-role │          │
 │  │ (only team-a      │    │  │ (only team-b      │          │
 │  │  resources)       │    │  │  resources)       │          │
 │  ├──────────────────┤    │  ├──────────────────┤          │
 │  │ ResourceQuota:    │    │  │ ResourceQuota:    │          │
 │  │  CPU: 4 cores     │    │  │  CPU: 8 cores     │          │
 │  │  Memory: 8Gi      │    │  │  Memory: 16Gi     │          │
 │  ├──────────────────┤    │  ├──────────────────┤          │
 │  │ NetworkPolicy:    │    │  │ NetworkPolicy:    │          │
 │  │  deny-all +       │    │  │  deny-all +       │          │
 │  │  allow internal   │    │  │  allow internal   │          │
 │  ├──────────────────┤    │  ├──────────────────┤          │
 │  │ Pod Security:     │    │  │ Pod Security:     │          │
 │  │  restricted       │    │  │  restricted       │          │
 │  └──────────────────┘    │  └──────────────────┘          │
 │                           │                                │
 └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
tenant-namespace.yaml — complete tenant isolation 
# 1. Namespace with Pod Security Standard
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: anihpj
  labels:
    pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce: restricted
    pod-security.kubernetes.io/warn: restricted
    pod-security.kubernetes.io/audit: restricted
---
# 2. Default deny-all NetworkPolicy
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: default-deny-all
  namespace: anihpj
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes: ["Ingress", "Egress"]
---
# 3. Allow internal communication within namespace
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-internal
  namespace: anihpj
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes: ["Ingress", "Egress"]
  ingress:
    - from:
        - podSelector: {}            # Allow from same namespace
  egress:
    - to:
        - podSelector: {}            # Allow to same namespace
    - to:                            # Allow DNS resolution
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              kubernetes.io/metadata.name: kube-system
          podSelector:
            matchLabels:
              k8s-app: kube-dns
      ports:
        - protocol: UDP
          port: 53
---
# 4. ResourceQuota to limit resource consumption
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: anihpj-quota
  namespace: anihpj
spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: "4"
    requests.memory: 8Gi
    limits.cpu: "8"
    limits.memory: 16Gi
    pods: "20"
    services: "10"
    secrets: "20"
    configmaps: "20"

📝 Chapter 39 — CKA Practice Questions

Q39.1
Create a Role in the anihpj namespace called secret-reader that allows only get and list on Secrets. Bind it to a ServiceAccount called anihpj-app. Then verify the binding works.
Show Answer 
# Create the SA
kubectl create serviceaccount anihpj-app -n anihpj

# Create the role
kubectl create role secret-reader -n anihpj \
  --verb=get,list \
  --resource=secrets

# Create the binding
kubectl create rolebinding secret-reader-binding -n anihpj \
  --role=secret-reader \
  --serviceaccount=anihpj:anihpj-app

# Verify
kubectl auth can-i get secrets -n anihpj \
  --as=system:serviceaccount:anihpj:anihpj-app
# yes

kubectl auth can-i delete secrets -n anihpj \
  --as=system:serviceaccount:anihpj:anihpj-app
# no   ← correct! delete is not granted
Explanation

The imperative kubectl create role command is fastest for the CKA. The --verb=get,list flag sets allowed verbs, --resource=secrets sets the resource type. A RoleBinding connects the role to the service account. Verify with kubectl auth can-i using the --as flag to impersonate the service account. Note the ServiceAccount format: namespace:name.

Q39.2
Enable encryption at rest for Secrets. Create an EncryptionConfiguration using aescbc provider. Then update the API server to use it.
Show Answer 
# 1. Generate an encryption key
head -c 32 /dev/urandom | base64
# Output: Bz4w1F...==

# 2. Create the encryption config
cat <<EOF > /etc/kubernetes/encryption-config.yaml
apiVersion: apiserver.config.k8s.io/v1
kind: EncryptionConfiguration
resources:
  - resources:
      - secrets
    providers:
      - aescbc:
          keys:
            - name: key1
              secret: Bz4w1F...==
      - identity: {}
EOF

# 3. Edit kube-apiserver manifest
vim /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml
# Add to command args:
#   --encryption-provider-config=/etc/kubernetes/encryption-config.yaml
# Add volume:
#   - name: encryption-config
#     hostPath:
#       path: /etc/kubernetes/encryption-config.yaml
#       type: File
# Add volumeMount:
#   - name: encryption-config
#     mountPath: /etc/kubernetes/encryption-config.yaml
#     readOnly: true

# 4. API server restarts automatically (static pod)
# 5. Re-encrypt existing secrets
kubectl get secrets --all-namespaces -o json | kubectl replace -f -
Explanation

The EncryptionConfiguration lists providers in order — the first provider is used for new writes, remaining providers are tried for reads (enabling migration). The identity: {} fallback allows reading pre-existing unencrypted Secrets. After enabling encryption, you must re-encrypt existing Secrets with kubectl get ... | kubectl replace. This is a common CKA task — remember to mount the config file into the API server pod.

Q39.3
Create a Pod in the anihpj namespace that follows all security best practices: non-root user, read-only filesystem, drop all capabilities, seccomp profile, no SA token mounting.
Show Answer 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-hardened
  namespace: anihpj
spec:
  automountServiceAccountToken: false
  securityContext:
    runAsNonRoot: true
    runAsUser: 1000
    runAsGroup: 3000
    fsGroup: 2000
    seccompProfile:
      type: RuntimeDefault
  containers:
    - name: django
      image: anihpj/jobpost:v2.1
      ports:
        - containerPort: 8000
      securityContext:
        allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
        readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
        capabilities:
          drop: ["ALL"]
      resources:
        limits:
          cpu: 500m
          memory: 256Mi
        requests:
          cpu: 100m
          memory: 128Mi
      volumeMounts:
        - name: tmp
          mountPath: /tmp
  volumes:
    - name: tmp
      emptyDir:
        sizeLimit: 100Mi
Explanation

This pod follows all CKA security best practices: runAsNonRoot: true ensures no root processes, readOnlyRootFilesystem prevents filesystem writes (except mounted volumes), capabilities.drop: ["ALL"] removes all Linux capabilities, seccompProfile: RuntimeDefault restricts syscalls, automountServiceAccountToken: false prevents API access, and resource limits prevent resource abuse. The emptyDir volume provides a writable /tmp directory since the root filesystem is read-only.

Q39.4
Configure audit logging for the API server. The policy should: log all Secret access at Metadata level, log RBAC changes at RequestResponse level, and skip logging events and health checks.
Show Answer 
# /etc/kubernetes/audit-policy.yaml
apiVersion: audit.k8s.io/v1
kind: Policy
rules:
  # Skip health checks and events
  - level: None
    nonResourceURLs: ["/healthz*", "/readyz*", "/livez*"]
  - level: None
    resources:
      - group: ""
        resources: ["events"]
  
  # Log Secrets at Metadata only (never log content!)
  - level: Metadata
    resources:
      - group: ""
        resources: ["secrets"]
  
  # Log RBAC changes at full detail
  - level: RequestResponse
    resources:
      - group: "rbac.authorization.k8s.io"
        resources: ["roles", "rolebindings", "clusterroles", "clusterrolebindings"]
  
  # Catch-all
  - level: Metadata
# Add to kube-apiserver.yaml:
# --audit-policy-file=/etc/kubernetes/audit-policy.yaml
# --audit-log-path=/var/log/kubernetes/audit.log
# --audit-log-maxage=30
# --audit-log-maxbackup=10
# --audit-log-maxsize=100
#
# Mount both the policy file and log directory as volumes
Explanation

Audit policy rules are evaluated in order — the first matching rule wins. That's why the None rules (skip) come first. For Secrets, use Metadata level to avoid logging Secret values in the audit trail. RBAC changes need RequestResponse level for full forensic detail. The API server needs both --audit-policy-file and --audit-log-path flags, plus volume mounts for both the policy file and the log output directory.

Q39.5
Set up complete namespace isolation for the anihpj namespace: enforce the restricted Pod Security Standard, create a default-deny NetworkPolicy, and set a ResourceQuota limiting to 4 CPU cores and 8Gi memory.
Show Answer 
# 1. Label namespace for Pod Security Standard
kubectl label namespace anihpj \
  pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce=restricted \
  pod-security.kubernetes.io/warn=restricted \
  pod-security.kubernetes.io/audit=restricted

# 2. Default deny NetworkPolicy
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: default-deny-all
  namespace: anihpj
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes: ["Ingress", "Egress"]
EOF

# 3. ResourceQuota
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: anihpj-quota
  namespace: anihpj
spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: "4"
    requests.memory: 8Gi
    limits.cpu: "4"
    limits.memory: 8Gi
    pods: "20"
EOF

# Verify all three
kubectl describe namespace anihpj
kubectl get networkpolicy -n anihpj
kubectl describe resourcequota -n anihpj
Explanation

Complete namespace isolation requires three components: Pod Security Standards via namespace labels (enforce=restricted blocks privileged containers, host networking, etc.), NetworkPolicy with empty podSelector and both Ingress/Egress policyTypes creates a default-deny rule, and ResourceQuota limits total resource consumption. On the CKA, you'll often need to combine these. After default-deny, remember to create allow-rules for legitimate traffic (DNS, ingress controller, internal communication).

Chapter 40: Kubernetes Policies Deep Dive

Master the full range of Kubernetes policy enforcement — Pod Security Standards, ValidatingAdmissionPolicy with CEL, LimitRange vs ResourceQuota, PID limits, topology constraints, and an overview of external policy engines (OPA Gatekeeper & Kyverno) — all with anihpj project examples.

🔴 Advanced ⏱️ ~2 hours 📜 Policies 📝 CKA EXAM

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ Pod Security Standards — privileged, baseline, and restricted profiles with Pod Security Admission
  • ✅ ValidatingAdmissionPolicy — in-cluster CEL-based policy enforcement patterns
  • ✅ LimitRange vs ResourceQuota — per-pod defaults vs namespace-wide totals
  • ✅ PID Limits — protecting nodes from fork bombs
  • ✅ Topology Spread Constraints — policy-driven workload distribution
  • ✅ External Policy Engines — OPA Gatekeeper and Kyverno overview
CKA Exam Relevance
Workloads 15% Cluster Architecture 25%
Policies and the CKA Exam

The CKA tests your ability to enforce security policies at the namespace and cluster level. Pod Security Admission via namespace labels is the most likely exam topic. LimitRange and ResourceQuota questions also appear frequently. Know the difference between them and when to use each.

  • Must Know — Pod Security Standards (3 levels), LimitRange, ResourceQuota
  • Should Know — ValidatingAdmissionPolicy (CEL), PodDisruptionBudget
  • Awareness — OPA Gatekeeper, Kyverno (not on CKA but used in production)
🛡️
Pod Security

Three-level standard (privileged, baseline, restricted) via namespace labels

📏
LimitRange

Per-pod defaults and bounds — min, max, default CPU/memory for individual containers

📊
ResourceQuota

Namespace-wide totals — cap aggregate resource consumption across all pods

🌐
NetworkPolicy

Ingress/egress traffic rules — default-deny + allow-list per namespace

🛡️PSA Labels
→
📏LimitRange
→
📊Quota Check
→
🌐NetPolicy
→
✅Pod Runs

40.1 Pod Security Standards — The Three Levels

CONCEPT 📝 CKA EXAM

Pod Security Standards define three progressive security levels. They replaced the deprecated PodSecurityPolicy in Kubernetes v1.25.

LevelDescriptionWhat It BlocksUse Case
Privileged No restrictions Nothing — everything allowed System/infrastructure namespaces (kube-system)
Baseline Minimally restrictive Privileged containers, hostNetwork, hostPID, hostIPC, known dangerous capabilities General workloads with basic security
Restricted Heavily restricted Everything in baseline + must run as non-root, read-only filesystem encouraged, drop all capabilities, seccomp required Security-critical and multi-tenant workloads
⚠️
Privileged

No restrictions. Use for kube-system and infrastructure components only.

🟡
Baseline

Blocks known escalations. Good starting point for general workloads.

🟢
Restricted

Maximum security. Required for production and multi-tenant clusters.

Pod Security Admission — Enforcement Modes

ModeBehaviorLabel Key
enforceBlock pods that violate the policypod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce
warnAllow but show warning to userpod-security.kubernetes.io/warn
auditAllow but record in audit logpod-security.kubernetes.io/audit
terminal — applying Pod Security Standards 
# Apply restricted level with all three modes
kubectl label namespace anihpj \
  pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce=restricted \
  pod-security.kubernetes.io/warn=restricted \
  pod-security.kubernetes.io/audit=restricted

# Verify labels
kubectl get namespace anihpj --show-labels

# Test — this pod violates restricted (runs as root)
kubectl run test --image=nginx -n anihpj
# Error from server (Forbidden): pods "test" is forbidden:
#   violates PodSecurity "restricted:latest":
#   allowPrivilegeEscalation != false
#   unrestricted capabilities
#   runAsNonRoot != true

# Pin to a specific K8s version for consistent behavior
kubectl label namespace anihpj \
  pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce-version=v1.30

Pod Security Standard Decision Tree

 Is it a system namespace (kube-system, etc.)?
 ├── YES → Use "privileged" (system components need elevated privileges)
 └── NO
     ├── Does it run trusted, well-known images only?
     │   ├── YES → Use "baseline" (prevents obvious attacks)
     │   └── NO  → Use "restricted" (maximum security)
     └── Multi-tenant cluster?
         └── ALWAYS → Use "restricted" + "warn" on next level

Pod Security Admission Gotchas

  • ❌ Labels apply at namespace level only — you cannot apply PSA to individual pods, only entire namespaces
  • ❌ Existing pods aren't evicted — enforce mode only blocks NEW pods. Existing violating pods continue running
  • ✅ Use warn + audit first — apply warning mode before enforce to discover violations without breaking workloads
  • ✅ Check with dry-run — kubectl label --dry-run=server --overwrite ns anihpj pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce=restricted shows what would be blocked

40.2 LimitRange vs ResourceQuota — Complete Comparison

CONCEPT 📝 CKA EXAM

These two resources work together but solve different problems. Understanding their differences is critical for the CKA.

📏 LimitRange + ResourceQuota = Complete Resource Governance

LimitRange sets guardrails for individual pods (defaults, min, max). ResourceQuota caps the total namespace consumption. Use both together — LimitRange provides defaults so pods satisfy ResourceQuota's requirement that all pods have limits.

Pod Created
→
LimitRange Injects Defaults
→
LimitRange Validates Min/Max
→
ResourceQuota Checks Totals
→
Pod Scheduled
FeatureLimitRangeResourceQuota
ScopePer-container / per-pod defaultsNamespace-wide totals
What it controlsDefault, min, max for individual podsTotal aggregate consumption across all pods
Applies whenPod is created (sets defaults)Namespace hits capacity limits
If not setPods get no default limitsNo cap on total namespace usage
Primary purposePrevent one pod from being too large/smallPrevent one team from consuming all cluster resources
limitrange-vs-quota.yaml — both working together 
# LimitRange — defaults and bounds for INDIVIDUAL pods/containers
apiVersion: v1
kind: LimitRange
metadata:
  name: anihpj-limits
  namespace: anihpj
spec:
  limits:
    - type: Container
      default:              # Applied if container doesn't specify limits
        cpu: 200m
        memory: 256Mi
      defaultRequest:       # Applied if container doesn't specify requests
        cpu: 100m
        memory: 128Mi
      max:                  # No container can exceed these
        cpu: "2"
        memory: 2Gi
      min:                  # No container can request less than these
        cpu: 50m
        memory: 64Mi
    - type: Pod
      max:                  # Total pod limits (sum of all containers)
        cpu: "4"
        memory: 4Gi
---
# ResourceQuota — aggregate limits for the ENTIRE namespace
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: anihpj-quota
  namespace: anihpj
spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: "8"         # Total CPU requests across all pods
    requests.memory: 16Gi
    limits.cpu: "16"
    limits.memory: 32Gi
    pods: "30"                # Max 30 pods in namespace
    services: "10"
    persistentvolumeclaims: "10"
    secrets: "20"
    configmaps: "20"
    replicationcontrollers: "10"
    services.loadbalancers: "2"
    services.nodeports: "5"
terminal — checking limits and quotas 
# View LimitRange settings
kubectl describe limitrange anihpj-limits -n anihpj

# View ResourceQuota usage
kubectl describe resourcequota anihpj-quota -n anihpj
# Name:            anihpj-quota
# Resource         Used   Hard
# --------         ---    ----
# limits.cpu       600m   16
# limits.memory    768Mi  32Gi
# pods             3      30
# requests.cpu     300m   8
# requests.memory  384Mi  16Gi

# Test: try to create a pod that exceeds LimitRange max
kubectl run big --image=nginx -n anihpj \
  --overrides='{"spec":{"containers":[{"name":"big","image":"nginx","resources":{"limits":{"cpu":"5"}}}]}}'
# Error: cpu max constraint (2) exceeded

CKA Quick Reference — When a ResourceQuota exists in a namespace, ALL pods in that namespace MUST specify resource requests and limits. If they don't and no LimitRange provides defaults, the pod will be rejected. This is why LimitRange and ResourceQuota are often used together: LimitRange provides defaults so pods without explicit limits still get created.

40.3 PID Limits — Preventing Fork Bombs

CONCEPT

PID limits restrict the number of process IDs a pod can use, preventing fork bombs and runaway process creation from destabilizing the entire node.

kubelet-config.yaml — PID limits 
# Kubelet configuration — set per node
apiVersion: kubelet.config.k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: KubeletConfiguration
podPidsLimit: 1024           # Max PIDs per pod
# System reserved PIDs are configured separately:
# --system-reserved=pid=1000
terminal — checking PID usage 
# Check current PID allocation on a node
kubectl get --raw /api/v1/nodes/<node-name>/proxy/stats/summary | \
  jq '.pods[] | select(.podRef.name | startswith("anihpj")) |
  {name: .podRef.name, pids: .process_stats.process_count}'

# Check kubelet PID limit setting
kubectl get --raw /api/v1/nodes/<node-name>/proxy/configz | \
  jq '.kubeletconfig.podPidsLimit'
📝 PID Limits and the CKA

PID limits are configured at the kubelet level, not via YAML manifests. On the CKA, you may see this in troubleshooting scenarios where pods are evicted due to PID pressure. Check kubectl describe node for the PIDPressure condition.

40.4 Topology Spread Constraints

CONCEPT 📝 CKA EXAM

Topology Spread Constraints ensure pods are evenly distributed across failure domains (nodes, zones, regions). This prevents all replicas landing on one node or in one availability zone.

🎯 maxSkew

Maximum allowed pod count difference between topology domains. Lower = more even. 1 means perfect balance.

🗺️ topologyKey

Node label that defines domains: kubernetes.io/hostname (per-node) or topology.kubernetes.io/zone (per-zone).

⚖️ whenUnsatisfiable

DoNotSchedule (hard — block) or ScheduleAnyway (soft — best-effort). Choose per constraint.

🏷️ labelSelector

Selects which pods to count for skew calculation. Must match the pod template's own labels.

topology-spread.yaml — even distribution for anihpj 
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj-web
  namespace: anihpj
spec:
  replicas: 6
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj-web
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj-web
    spec:
      topologySpreadConstraints:
        # Spread evenly across zones
        - maxSkew: 1
          topologyKey: topology.kubernetes.io/zone
          whenUnsatisfiable: DoNotSchedule
          labelSelector:
            matchLabels:
              app: anihpj-web
        # Spread evenly across nodes within each zone
        - maxSkew: 1
          topologyKey: kubernetes.io/hostname
          whenUnsatisfiable: ScheduleAnyway
          labelSelector:
            matchLabels:
              app: anihpj-web
      containers:
        - name: django
          image: anihpj/jobpost:v2.1
FieldDescriptionValues
maxSkewMaximum difference between zone/node pod countsInteger ≥ 1 (lower = more even)
topologyKeyNode label to group topology domainskubernetes.io/hostname, topology.kubernetes.io/zone
whenUnsatisfiableWhat to do if constraint can't be metDoNotSchedule (hard), ScheduleAnyway (soft)
labelSelectorWhich pods to count in the topology calculationMust match the pod template labels

Topology Spread Visualization

 Without Topology Spread          With maxSkew=1, by zone
 ┌────────────────────┐          ┌────────────────────┐
 │ Zone A             │          │ Zone A             │
 │ ┌──────┐ ┌──────┐ │          │ ┌──────┐ ┌──────┐ │
 │ │Node1 │ │Node2 │ │          │ │Node1 │ │Node2 │ │
 │ │██████│ │      │ │          │ │██    │ │██    │ │
 │ │██████│ │      │ │          │ └──────┘ └──────┘ │
 │ └──────┘ └──────┘ │          │ 2 pods             │
 │ 6 pods             │          ├────────────────────┤
 ├────────────────────┤          │ Zone B             │
 │ Zone B             │          │ ┌──────┐ ┌──────┐ │
 │ ┌──────┐          │          │ │Node3 │ │Node4 │ │
 │ │Node3 │          │          │ │██    │ │██    │ │
 │ │      │          │          │ └──────┘ └──────┘ │
 │ └──────┘          │          │ 2 pods             │
 │ 0 pods             │          ├────────────────────┤
 ├────────────────────┤          │ Zone C             │
 │ Zone C             │          │ ┌──────┐          │
 │ ┌──────┐          │          │ │Node5 │          │
 │ │Node5 │          │          │ │██    │          │
 │ │      │          │          │ └──────┘          │
 │ └──────┘          │          │ 2 pods             │
 │ 0 pods             │          └────────────────────┘
 └────────────────────┘          Evenly distributed!
  All eggs in one basket!

40.5 External Policy Engines — OPA Gatekeeper & Kyverno

CONCEPT

For complex policy requirements beyond what ValidatingAdmissionPolicy and Pod Security Standards provide, organizations use external policy engines. These are not on the CKA exam but are critical for production environments.

FeatureOPA GatekeeperKyvernoValidatingAdmissionPolicy
Policy languageRego (custom DSL)YAML (native K8s)CEL (simple expressions)
Learning curveSteep (learn Rego)Low (YAML-based)Low (CEL expressions)
Mutation supportYes (v3.7+)Yes (native)No (validation only)
Generation supportNoYes (generate resources)No
Report/audit modeYes (audit constraint)Yes (audit mode)Yes (Audit action)
Requires webhook?Yes (runs as webhook)Yes (runs as webhook)No (built into API server)
CKA exam?NoNoYes (may appear)
Do you need only validation (no mutation/generation)?
YES → Is it a simple per-resource rule (e.g., require labels, enforce limits)?
YES → Use ValidatingAdmissionPolicy (built-in, no extra components)
NO → Need cross-resource or complex logic? → OPA Gatekeeper
NO → Do you need to mutate resources or auto-generate defaults?
YES → Use Kyverno (YAML-native, supports validate + mutate + generate)
NO → Combine ValidatingAdmissionPolicy (simple rules) + external engine (complex rules)

OPA Gatekeeper Example — ConstraintTemplate

gatekeeper-example.yaml — require labels (Rego) 
# ConstraintTemplate defines the policy logic in Rego
apiVersion: templates.gatekeeper.sh/v1
kind: ConstraintTemplate
metadata:
  name: k8srequiredlabels
spec:
  crd:
    spec:
      names:
        kind: K8sRequiredLabels
      validation:
        openAPIV3Schema:
          properties:
            labels:
              type: array
              items:
                type: string
  targets:
    - target: admission.k8s.gatekeeper.sh
      rego: |
        package k8srequiredlabels
        violation[{"msg": msg}] {
          provided := {label | input.review.object.metadata.labels[label]}
          required := {label | label := input.parameters.labels[_]}
          missing := required - provided
          count(missing) > 0
          msg := sprintf("Missing required labels: %v", [missing])
        }
---
# Constraint applies the template with specific parameters
apiVersion: constraints.gatekeeper.sh/v1beta1
kind: K8sRequiredLabels
metadata:
  name: require-team-label
spec:
  match:
    kinds:
      - apiGroups: [""]
        kinds: ["Namespace"]
  parameters:
    labels: ["team", "environment"]

Kyverno Example — ClusterPolicy

kyverno-example.yaml — require labels (YAML) 
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-team-label
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Enforce
  rules:
    - name: check-team-label
      match:
        any:
          - resources:
              kinds:
                - Pod
      validate:
        message: "All pods must have a 'team' label"
        pattern:
          metadata:
            labels:
              team: "?*"         # Must exist and be non-empty
💡 Which Policy Engine Should You Choose?

Start with ValidatingAdmissionPolicy (built-in, no extra components). If you need mutation or resource generation, add Kyverno (easiest to learn). If your organization already uses OPA or needs complex cross-resource policies, use Gatekeeper. In practice, many teams combine ValidatingAdmissionPolicy for simple rules with an external engine for complex ones.

40.6 Combining Policies — The Complete Stack

CONCEPT 📝 CKA EXAM

In production, you use multiple policy types together. Here's the complete policy stack for the anihpj namespace:

Complete Policy Stack for anihpj Namespace

 ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
 │              anihpj NAMESPACE — POLICY STACK                │
 ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
 │                                                              │
 │  1. Pod Security Admission (enforce=restricted)             │
 │     └─ Blocks: privileged, hostNetwork, root, no seccomp    │
 │                                                              │
 │  2. ValidatingAdmissionPolicy (CEL)                         │
 │     ├─ Require minimum 2 replicas                           │
 │     ├─ Only approved image registries                       │
 │     └─ All containers need resource limits                  │
 │                                                              │
 │  3. LimitRange                                              │
 │     ├─ Default: 200m CPU / 256Mi memory                     │
 │     ├─ Max per container: 2 CPU / 2Gi memory                │
 │     └─ Min per container: 50m CPU / 64Mi memory             │
 │                                                              │
 │  4. ResourceQuota                                           │
 │     ├─ Total: 8 CPU / 16Gi memory                           │
 │     ├─ Max 30 pods                                          │
 │     └─ Max 2 LoadBalancers                                  │
 │                                                              │
 │  5. NetworkPolicy                                           │
 │     ├─ Default deny-all                                     │
 │     ├─ Allow internal namespace traffic                     │
 │     └─ Allow DNS egress to kube-system                      │
 │                                                              │
 │  6. PodDisruptionBudget                                     │
 │     └─ minAvailable: 2 (at least 2 pods during disruptions) │
 │                                                              │
 └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

🎬 Scenario: What Happens When anihpj Deploys a Pod?

1. PSA Gate
→
2. Admission Policy (CEL)
→
3. LimitRange Defaults
→
4. ResourceQuota Check
→
5. Schedule + NetPolicy
→
6. PDB Protected

If the pod runs privileged: true → blocked at step 1 (PSA). If it has no resource limits → LimitRange injects defaults at step 3. If the namespace is over quota → rejected at step 4. Each layer adds defense-in-depth.

echo "=== Pod Security Standards ===" kubectl get namespace anihpj -o jsonpath='{.metadata.labels}' | jq . echo "=== LimitRange ===" kubectl get limitrange -n anihpj echo "=== ResourceQuota ===" kubectl describe resourcequota -n anihpj echo "=== NetworkPolicies ===" kubectl get networkpolicy -n anihpj echo "=== PodDisruptionBudgets ===" kubectl get pdb -n anihpj echo "=== ValidatingAdmissionPolicies ===" kubectl get validatingadmissionpolicybindings -o json | \ jq -r '.items[] | select(.spec.matchResources.namespaceSelector.matchLabels | to_entries[]? | .value == "anihpj" or .key == "kubernetes.io/metadata.name") | .metadata.name'

📝 Chapter 40 — CKA Practice Questions

Q40.1
Apply the baseline Pod Security Standard to the anihpj namespace in warn mode, and the restricted standard in enforce mode. Pin both to version v1.30.
Show Answer 
kubectl label namespace anihpj \
  pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce=restricted \
  pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce-version=v1.30 \
  pod-security.kubernetes.io/warn=baseline \
  pod-security.kubernetes.io/warn-version=v1.30 \
  --overwrite

# Verify
kubectl get namespace anihpj --show-labels
Explanation

You can mix enforcement levels and modes. Here, restricted is enforced (blocks violations), while baseline in warn mode shows warnings for baseline violations that pass restricted enforcement. Pinning to a version (e.g., v1.30) ensures consistent behavior even after cluster upgrades. Use --overwrite if labels already exist.

Q40.2
Create a LimitRange in the anihpj namespace that sets default container limits of 300m CPU and 512Mi memory, with a maximum of 1 CPU and 1Gi. Then create a pod WITHOUT specifying resources and verify the defaults were applied.
Show Answer 
# Create LimitRange
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: LimitRange
metadata:
  name: anihpj-defaults
  namespace: anihpj
spec:
  limits:
    - type: Container
      default:
        cpu: 300m
        memory: 512Mi
      defaultRequest:
        cpu: 100m
        memory: 256Mi
      max:
        cpu: "1"
        memory: 1Gi
EOF

# Create pod without resources
kubectl run test-defaults --image=nginx -n anihpj

# Verify defaults were injected
kubectl get pod test-defaults -n anihpj -o jsonpath='{.spec.containers[0].resources}' | jq .
# {
#   "limits": { "cpu": "300m", "memory": "512Mi" },
#   "requests": { "cpu": "100m", "memory": "256Mi" }
# }

# Cleanup
kubectl delete pod test-defaults -n anihpj
Explanation

LimitRange injects default resource requests and limits into containers that don't specify them. This is done by the LimitRanger admission controller. The default field sets limits, defaultRequest sets requests. If you only set default without defaultRequest, the request defaults to the limit value. The max field rejects pods that exceed the maximum even if they explicitly set higher values.

Q40.3
Create a ResourceQuota in the anihpj namespace limiting: 10 pods, 4 CPU requests, 8Gi memory requests, and 2 PVCs. Then check the current usage.
Show Answer 
# Create ResourceQuota
kubectl create quota anihpj-quota -n anihpj \
  --hard=pods=10,requests.cpu=4,requests.memory=8Gi,persistentvolumeclaims=2

# Check usage
kubectl describe resourcequota anihpj-quota -n anihpj
# Name:                   anihpj-quota
# Namespace:              anihpj
# Resource                Used  Hard
# --------                ----  ----
# persistentvolumeclaims  0     2
# pods                    3     10
# requests.cpu            300m  4
# requests.memory         768Mi 8Gi

# Try to exceed (if 10 pods exist)
kubectl run overflow --image=nginx -n anihpj
# Error: forbidden: exceeded quota: anihpj-quota,
# requested: pods=1, used: pods=10, limited: pods=10
Explanation

The kubectl create quota imperative command is fast for the CKA. The --hard flag sets limits. kubectl describe resourcequota shows both Used and Hard columns so you can see current consumption. When a quota is exceeded, new resource creation is denied with a clear error message. Remember: when ResourceQuota exists, all pods MUST have resource requests/limits (or a LimitRange must provide defaults).

Q40.4
Create a Deployment with topology spread constraints that distributes pods evenly across nodes (maxSkew=1, DoNotSchedule). Deploy 4 replicas of the anihpj app.
Show Answer 
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj-spread
  namespace: anihpj
spec:
  replicas: 4
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj-spread
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj-spread
    spec:
      topologySpreadConstraints:
        - maxSkew: 1
          topologyKey: kubernetes.io/hostname
          whenUnsatisfiable: DoNotSchedule
          labelSelector:
            matchLabels:
              app: anihpj-spread
      containers:
        - name: django
          image: anihpj/jobpost:v2.1
          resources:
            requests:
              cpu: 100m
              memory: 128Mi
            limits:
              cpu: 200m
              memory: 256Mi
# Verify distribution
kubectl get pods -n anihpj -l app=anihpj-spread -o wide
# NAME                     NODE      STATUS
# anihpj-spread-abc12      node-1    Running
# anihpj-spread-def34      node-2    Running
# anihpj-spread-ghi56      node-3    Running
# anihpj-spread-jkl78      node-1    Running  ← only if ≤3 nodes
Explanation

maxSkew: 1 means the difference in pod count between any two nodes is at most 1. With 4 pods and 3 nodes, distribution would be 2-1-1, not 4-0-0. DoNotSchedule makes it a hard constraint — if the skew can't be maintained, the pod stays Pending. The labelSelector MUST match your pod labels so the scheduler counts existing pods correctly. Always include resources in production deployments.

Q40.5
Compare the three Pod Security Standard levels: privileged, baseline, and restricted. Which level should you use for the kube-system namespace? Which for a production application namespace? Why?
Show Answer 
# kube-system — Use privileged (system components need elevated access)
kubectl label namespace kube-system \
  pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce=privileged

# Production app namespace — Use restricted (maximum security)
kubectl label namespace anihpj \
  pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce=restricted \
  pod-security.kubernetes.io/warn=restricted

# Check pods that would violate restricted in kube-system
kubectl label namespace kube-system \
  pod-security.kubernetes.io/warn=restricted --overwrite --dry-run=server
# Warning: would violate "restricted": kube-proxy, kube-apiserver, etc.
Explanation

Privileged: No restrictions — for kube-system because system components (kube-proxy, CNI plugins, etc.) need host networking, privileged mode, and special capabilities. Baseline: Prevents known privilege escalations — good for general workloads that haven't been hardened yet. Restricted: Maximum security — for production application namespaces. Requires non-root, drops all capabilities, requires seccomp, etc. On the CKA, remember: kube-system = privileged, app namespaces = restricted, staging/dev = baseline as a stepping stone.

⌨️ Appendix A: kubectl Admin Command Reference

APPENDIX 📋 REFERENCE

Complete kubectl admin command reference with CKA speed aliases — sections A.1 through A.7. Every command uses the anihpj project context. Bookmark this page for the exam.

Exam Day Setup — Run these aliases first when your exam terminal opens:

~/.bashrc — exam aliases 
alias k=kubectl
alias kgp='kubectl get pods'
alias kgs='kubectl get svc'
alias kgn='kubectl get nodes'
alias kd='kubectl describe'
alias kaf='kubectl apply -f'
alias kdel='kubectl delete'
export do='--dry-run=client -o yaml'
export now='--force --grace-period=0'

# Apply immediately:
source ~/.bashrc
⚠️ Context Switching — #1 Exam Killer

The CKA exam uses multiple clusters. Each question tells you which cluster to use. If you forget to switch, you'll modify the wrong cluster and lose points with no way to undo. Run the context command first for every question:

terminal — context switching (EVERY question!) 
# The exam tells you: "Use context: k8s-cluster-1"
kubectl config use-context k8s-cluster-1

# Verify you're on the right cluster
kubectl config current-context
# k8s-cluster-1

# List all available contexts
kubectl config get-contexts
# CURRENT   NAME              CLUSTER           AUTHINFO
# *         k8s-cluster-1     k8s-cluster-1     admin
#           k8s-cluster-2     k8s-cluster-2     admin

# Set default namespace (saves typing -n every time)
kubectl config set-context --current --namespace=anihpj

kubectl Command Flow — What Happens When You Run a Command

 ┌─────────────┐    ┌──────────────┐    ┌──────────────┐    ┌──────────────┐
 │  YOU type:   │    │  ~/.kube/    │    │  API Server  │    │    etcd      │
 │  kubectl ... │───▶│  config      │───▶│  :6443       │───▶│  (store)     │
 │              │    │  (auth+ctx)  │    │  (AuthN/Z)   │    │              │
 └─────────────┘    └──────────────┘    └──────┬───────┘    └──────────────┘
                                               │
                    ┌──────────────────────────┬┴──────────────────────┐
                    ▼                          ▼                      ▼
             ┌─────────────┐         ┌──────────────┐       ┌──────────────┐
             │  Scheduler  │         │  Controller  │       │   kubelet    │
             │  (assigns   │         │  Manager     │       │  (runs pods  │
             │   nodes)    │         │  (reconcile) │       │   on nodes)  │
             └─────────────┘         └──────────────┘       └──────────────┘

A.1 Cluster Management Commands

COMMANDS 📝 CKA EXAM

These commands help you inspect the overall health and capabilities of your cluster. Use them first when troubleshooting any cluster issue.

Cluster Health Check Decision Tree

                        🏥 Cluster Health Check
                        ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
                                │
                    ┌───────────┼───────────┐
                    ▼           ▼           ▼
              kubectl       kubectl     kubectl
             cluster-info  get nodes   get cs
                    │           │           │
                    ▼           ▼           ▼
              ✅ API server  ✅ Nodes    ⚠️ Deprecated
              reachable?     Ready?      in v1.19+
                    │           │           │
                    ▼           ▼           ▼
               If ❌:       If NotReady:  Use instead:
              Check         SSH to node,  kubectl get
              kubeconfig    check kubelet componentstatuses
              + API pod     + containerd  (still works)

Cluster Inspection Commands

CommandWhat It ShowsWhen to Use
kubectl cluster-info API server URL + CoreDNS URL First check — is the API reachable?
kubectl get componentstatuses scheduler, controller-manager, etcd health Control plane component health check
kubectl top nodes CPU/memory usage per node Resource pressure diagnosis
kubectl top pods --all-namespaces CPU/memory usage per pod Find resource-hungry pods
kubectl get events --sort-by=.metadata.creationTimestamp Recent cluster events (newest last) Troubleshooting — what just happened?
kubectl api-resources All resource types + short names + API group Discover resource types and their short names
kubectl api-versions All API group/versions available Check if an API version exists
terminal — cluster inspection 
# Check cluster is reachable
kubectl cluster-info
# Kubernetes control plane is running at https://10.0.0.10:6443
# CoreDNS is running at https://10.0.0.10:6443/api/v1/namespaces/kube-system/...

# Quick health check
kubectl get componentstatuses
# NAME                 STATUS    MESSAGE
# scheduler            Healthy   ok
# controller-manager   Healthy   ok
# etcd-0               Healthy   ok

# Resource usage (requires metrics-server)
kubectl top nodes
# NAME     CPU(cores)   CPU%   MEMORY(bytes)   MEMORY%
# cp-1     213m         10%    1024Mi          25%
# worker-1 156m         7%     768Mi           19%

# Find the most recent events
kubectl get events -A --sort-by=.metadata.creationTimestamp | tail -20

# Discover all resource types with short names
kubectl api-resources --sort-by=name | head -20
# NAME          SHORTNAMES   APIVERSION   NAMESPACED   KIND
# configmaps    cm           v1           true         ConfigMap
# deployments   deploy       apps/v1      true         Deployment
# pods          po           v1           true         Pod
# services      svc          v1           true         Service
💡 Short Names Save Time

Use short names on the exam: po (pods), svc (services), deploy (deployments), cm (configmaps), ns (namespaces), no (nodes), pv (persistentvolumes), pvc (persistentvolumeclaims), sa (serviceaccounts), netpol (networkpolicies), ing (ingresses).

A.2 Node Management

COMMANDS 📝 CKA EXAM

Node management is a core CKA skill — you'll cordon, drain, and uncordon nodes during upgrades and maintenance.

Node Maintenance Lifecycle

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                    NODE MAINTENANCE WORKFLOW                            │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │                                                                         │
  │   ┌──────────┐    ┌──────────┐    ┌──────────────┐    ┌──────────┐     │
  │   │  NORMAL  │    │ CORDON   │    │    DRAIN     │    │ UNCORDON │     │
  │   │          │───▶│          │───▶│              │───▶│          │     │
  │   │ Schedul- │    │ No new   │    │ Evict all    │    │ Allow    │     │
  │   │ able ✅  │    │ pods ⛔  │    │ pods 🚀      │    │ pods ✅  │     │
  │   └──────────┘    └──────────┘    └──────────────┘    └──────────┘     │
  │                                          │                              │
  │                                   ┌──────┴──────┐                      │
  │                                   │  PERFORM    │                      │
  │                                   │  MAINTENANCE│                      │
  │                                   │  (upgrade,  │                      │
  │                                   │   reboot)   │                      │
  │                                   └─────────────┘                      │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Node Management Commands

CommandEffectCKA Context
kubectl get nodes -o wide List nodes with IP, OS, kernel, container runtime First check during troubleshooting
kubectl describe node <name> Full node details: conditions, capacity, pods, taints Diagnose NotReady, resource pressure
kubectl cordon <node> Mark unschedulable (existing pods stay) Pre-maintenance — stop new pods
kubectl drain <node> --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data Cordon + evict all pods (except DaemonSets) Safely evacuate before maintenance
kubectl uncordon <node> Mark schedulable again After maintenance — resume scheduling
kubectl taint nodes <node> key=value:NoSchedule Add taint — only pods with matching toleration can schedule Isolate nodes for specific workloads
kubectl label nodes <node> disk=ssd Add label — used with nodeSelector for scheduling Target pods to specific nodes
terminal — node maintenance for anihpj 
# Step 1: Check current node status
kubectl get nodes -o wide
# NAME       STATUS   ROLES           VERSION   INTERNAL-IP   OS-IMAGE
# cp-1       Ready    control-plane   v1.31.0   10.0.0.10     Ubuntu 22.04
# worker-1   Ready    <none>          v1.31.0   10.0.0.11     Ubuntu 22.04
# worker-2   Ready    <none>          v1.31.0   10.0.0.12     Ubuntu 22.04

# Step 2: Prepare worker-1 for maintenance
kubectl cordon worker-1
# node/worker-1 cordoned

# Step 3: Drain it (move anihpj pods to worker-2)
kubectl drain worker-1 --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data
# evicting pod anihpj/anihpj-web-7d4b8c6f9-xk2vn
# pod/anihpj-web-7d4b8c6f9-xk2vn evicted

# Step 4: Verify pods moved
kubectl get pods -n anihpj -o wide
# NAME                          READY   NODE
# anihpj-web-7d4b8c6f9-m8p3q   1/1     worker-2  ← moved here

# Step 5: Perform maintenance (upgrade kubelet, etc.)

# Step 6: Bring node back
kubectl uncordon worker-1
# node/worker-1 uncordoned

⚠️ Drain Fails? Common Causes

  • Unmanaged pods — Pods not managed by a controller (bare pods). Fix: add --force flag (pods will be deleted, not rescheduled!)
  • PodDisruptionBudget violated — PDB prevents eviction. Fix: check PDB with kubectl get pdb -A, adjust minAvailable
  • emptyDir data loss — Need --delete-emptydir-data flag to acknowledge data loss
  • DaemonSet pods — Always need --ignore-daemonsets (they're present on every node)

A.3 Workload Commands

COMMANDS 📝 CKA EXAM

Deployment lifecycle commands — create, scale, update, rollback. These are your bread and butter on the CKA exam.

Deployment Rollout Lifecycle

  CREATE ──▶ SCALE ──▶ UPDATE ──▶ ROLLBACK
    │          │         │          │
    ▼          ▼         ▼          ▼
  kubectl    kubectl   kubectl    kubectl
  create     scale     set image  rollout undo
  deploy     deploy    deploy/    deploy/
  --image    --rep=5   name       name
    │          │         │          │
    ▼          ▼         ▼          ▼
  1 replica   5 reps   Rolling    Previous
  created     running  update     revision
                       started    restored

  📊 MONITOR: kubectl rollout status deploy/name
  📜 HISTORY: kubectl rollout history deploy/name

Deployment & Workload Commands

CommandEffectCKA Speed Tip
kubectl create deployment anihpj-web --image=anihpj:v1 --replicas=3 Create deployment with 3 replicas Fastest way — no YAML needed
kubectl scale deployment anihpj-web --replicas=5 Scale to 5 replicas Instant — no rolling update
kubectl set image deploy/anihpj-web web=anihpj:v2 Update container image (triggers rolling update) Container name must match spec
kubectl rollout status deploy/anihpj-web Watch rollout progress Blocks until complete or failed
kubectl rollout history deploy/anihpj-web Show revision history See all past versions
kubectl rollout undo deploy/anihpj-web Rollback to previous revision Instant rollback — saves exam time
kubectl rollout undo deploy/anihpj-web --to-revision=2 Rollback to specific revision When previous isn't enough
terminal — anihpj deployment lifecycle 
# Create deployment imperatively
kubectl create deployment anihpj-web --image=anihpj:v1 --replicas=3 -n anihpj

# Generate YAML without creating (exam trick!)
kubectl create deployment anihpj-web --image=anihpj:v1 --replicas=3 \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml > deploy.yaml

# Scale up for traffic
kubectl scale deployment anihpj-web --replicas=5 -n anihpj

# Update to new version (triggers rolling update)
kubectl set image deployment/anihpj-web web=anihpj:v2 -n anihpj

# Watch the rollout
kubectl rollout status deployment/anihpj-web -n anihpj
# Waiting for deployment "anihpj-web" rollout to finish: 2 of 5 updated...
# deployment "anihpj-web" successfully rolled out

# Something wrong? Rollback!
kubectl rollout undo deployment/anihpj-web -n anihpj
# deployment.apps/anihpj-web rolled back

The --dry-run=client -o yaml combo is your best friend. Use it to generate YAML for any resource, then edit as needed. With the $do alias: k create deploy anihpj-web --image=anihpj:v1 $do > deploy.yaml

A.4 Debugging Commands

COMMANDS 📝 CKA EXAM

Debugging is 30% of the CKA exam. Master these commands — they're your diagnostic toolkit.

Pod Debugging Decision Tree

                        🔍 Pod Not Working?
                        ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
                                │
               ┌────────────────┼────────────────┐
               ▼                ▼                ▼
          Pod Status?      Container        Can't reach
               │           crashing?        the service?
               │                │                │
    ┌──────────┼──────┐        ▼                ▼
    ▼          ▼      ▼   kubectl logs     kubectl get
  Pending  CrashLoop  Image  pod -c ctr    endpoints svc
    │      BackOff    Pull     │                │
    ▼        ▼       BackOff   ▼                ▼
  describe  logs     describe  Fix app      Empty? →
  pod →     pod →    pod →     crash or     Labels don't
  check     check    check     missing      match between
  events    app      image/    config       pod & service
            error    secret

Debug & Inspect Commands

CommandWhat It ShowsDebug Scenario
kubectl describe pod <name> -n anihpj Full pod details, events, conditions First stop for ANY pod issue
kubectl logs <pod> -n anihpj Container stdout/stderr App crash, startup failure
kubectl logs <pod> --previous Logs from previous (crashed) container CrashLoopBackOff — see WHY it crashed
