kubectl logs <pod> -c <container> Logs from specific container in multi-container pod Sidecar / init container issues
kubectl exec -it <pod> -- /bin/sh Open shell inside container Check files, env vars, network from inside
kubectl port-forward pod/<pod> 8080:8000 Forward local 8080 → pod 8000 Test pod directly without Service
kubectl get events --field-selector involvedObject.name=<pod> Events for specific pod only Targeted event search
kubectl auth can-i <verb> <resource> --as=<user> Check RBAC permissions Forbidden errors — who can do what?
terminal — debugging anihpj pods 
# Pod stuck in CrashLoopBackOff — investigate
kubectl describe pod anihpj-web-7d4b8c6f9-crash -n anihpj
# Events:
#   Warning  BackOff  kubelet  Back-off restarting failed container

# Get logs from the crashed container
kubectl logs anihpj-web-7d4b8c6f9-crash -n anihpj --previous
# ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'anihpj.settings'

# Shell into a running pod to investigate
kubectl exec -it anihpj-web-7d4b8c6f9-m8p3q -n anihpj -- /bin/sh
# $ env | grep DB          ← check environment variables
# $ cat /app/manage.py     ← check files exist
# $ python manage.py check ← test Django
# $ exit

# Test pod connectivity directly
kubectl port-forward pod/anihpj-web-7d4b8c6f9-m8p3q 8080:8000 -n anihpj
# Then: curl http://localhost:8080/jobs/

# Check who can create pods in anihpj namespace
kubectl auth can-i create pods -n anihpj --as=system:serviceaccount:anihpj:anihpj-sa
# yes

⚠️ Log Gotcha: Multi-Container Pods

If a pod has multiple containers (sidecar pattern), kubectl logs <pod> will fail with "a container name must be specified". Always use -c <container-name>. List containers with: kubectl get pod <name> -o jsonpath='{.spec.containers[*].name}'

A.5 RBAC Commands

COMMANDS 📝 CKA EXAM

RBAC (Role-Based Access Control) commands for managing who can do what in your cluster. These are frequently tested on the CKA exam.

RBAC Components — The Permission Chain

  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                    RBAC PERMISSION MODEL                           │
  ├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │                                                                    │
  │   WHO (Subject)         BINDING              WHAT (Role)           │
  │   ──────────────    ─────────────────    ──────────────────        │
  │                                                                    │
  │   👤 User            RoleBinding           Role                    │
  │   👥 Group           (namespace-scoped)    (namespace-scoped)      │
  │   🤖 ServiceAccount  ─────────────────    ──────────────────       │
  │                      ClusterRoleBinding    ClusterRole              │
  │                      (cluster-wide)        (cluster-wide)          │
  │                                                                    │
  │   Example for anihpj:                                              │
  │   ┌────────────┐  ┌─────────────────┐  ┌──────────────────┐       │
  │   │ anihpj-sa  │──│ anihpj-binding  │──│ anihpj-role      │       │
  │   │ (SA)       │  │ (RoleBinding)   │  │ get,list pods    │       │
  │   └────────────┘  └─────────────────┘  │ in ns: anihpj   │       │
  │                                         └──────────────────┘       │
  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

RBAC Imperative Commands

CommandCreatesScope
kubectl create role anihpj-role --verb=get,list --resource=pods -n anihpj Namespace-scoped Role anihpj namespace only
kubectl create clusterrole node-reader --verb=get,list,watch --resource=nodes Cluster-wide ClusterRole All namespaces
kubectl create rolebinding anihpj-binding --role=anihpj-role --serviceaccount=anihpj:anihpj-sa -n anihpj RoleBinding (links SA → Role) anihpj namespace
kubectl create clusterrolebinding admin-binding --clusterrole=cluster-admin --user=admin ClusterRoleBinding Cluster-wide
kubectl auth can-i --list --as=system:serviceaccount:anihpj:anihpj-sa Lists all permissions for that SA Debug RBAC issues
terminal — RBAC setup for anihpj 
# Create ServiceAccount for anihpj app
kubectl create serviceaccount anihpj-sa -n anihpj

# Create Role: can read pods and services
kubectl create role anihpj-role \
  --verb=get,list,watch \
  --resource=pods,services \
  -n anihpj

# Bind the role to the ServiceAccount
kubectl create rolebinding anihpj-binding \
  --role=anihpj-role \
  --serviceaccount=anihpj:anihpj-sa \
  -n anihpj

# Verify permissions
kubectl auth can-i list pods -n anihpj \
  --as=system:serviceaccount:anihpj:anihpj-sa
# yes

kubectl auth can-i delete pods -n anihpj \
  --as=system:serviceaccount:anihpj:anihpj-sa
# no  ← correct! We only granted get,list,watch

# List ALL permissions for the SA
kubectl auth can-i --list \
  --as=system:serviceaccount:anihpj:anihpj-sa \
  -n anihpj

A.6 Certificate Commands

COMMANDS 📝 CKA EXAM

Kubernetes uses TLS certificates extensively. Certificate management — checking expiry, renewing, and inspecting certs — is a core CKA topic.

Kubernetes Certificate Architecture

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │              KUBERNETES PKI — /etc/kubernetes/pki/                  │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │                                                                     │
  │  📁 /etc/kubernetes/pki/                                            │
  │  ├── ca.crt + ca.key               ← Cluster CA (root of trust)    │
  │  ├── apiserver.crt + apiserver.key  ← API Server cert               │
  │  ├── apiserver-kubelet-client.crt   ← API → kubelet auth           │
  │  ├── front-proxy-ca.crt            ← Aggregation layer CA          │
  │  ├── sa.key + sa.pub               ← ServiceAccount signing        │
  │  └── etcd/                                                          │
  │      ├── ca.crt + ca.key           ← etcd CA (separate!)           │
  │      ├── server.crt + server.key   ← etcd server cert              │
  │      ├── peer.crt + peer.key       ← etcd peer-to-peer             │
  │      └── healthcheck-client.crt    ← etcd health check             │
  │                                                                     │
  │  ⏰ Default validity: 1 year (except CA: 10 years)                  │
  │  🔄 kubeadm certs renew all ← renews all 1-year certs              │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Certificate Management Commands

CommandWhat It DoesWhen to Use
kubeadm certs check-expiration Show expiry dates for all certs Regular health check / before renewal
kubeadm certs renew all Renew all kubeadm-managed certificates Before expiry or after upgrade
openssl x509 -in <cert> -text -noout Inspect certificate details (CN, SANs, dates) Verify specific cert properties
terminal — certificate management 
# Check expiration dates for ALL certs
sudo kubeadm certs check-expiration
# CERTIFICATE                EXPIRES                  RESIDUAL TIME
# admin.conf                 Dec 15, 2026 10:30 UTC   364d
# apiserver                  Dec 15, 2026 10:30 UTC   364d
# apiserver-etcd-client      Dec 15, 2026 10:30 UTC   364d
# apiserver-kubelet-client   Dec 15, 2026 10:30 UTC   364d
# controller-manager.conf    Dec 15, 2026 10:30 UTC   364d
# etcd-healthcheck-client    Dec 15, 2026 10:30 UTC   364d
# etcd-peer                  Dec 15, 2026 10:30 UTC   364d
# etcd-server                Dec 15, 2026 10:30 UTC   364d
# front-proxy-client         Dec 15, 2026 10:30 UTC   364d
# scheduler.conf             Dec 15, 2026 10:30 UTC   364d
# CA                         Dec 12, 2035 10:30 UTC   10y  ← CA lasts 10 years

# Renew all certificates
sudo kubeadm certs renew all
# [renew] Reading configuration from the cluster...
# certificate apiserver renewed
# certificate apiserver-kubelet-client renewed
# ...

# IMPORTANT: Restart control plane after renewal!
sudo systemctl restart kubelet

# Inspect a specific certificate
openssl x509 -in /etc/kubernetes/pki/apiserver.crt -text -noout | grep -A2 "Validity"
#     Validity
#         Not Before: Dec 15 10:30:00 2025 GMT
#         Not After : Dec 15 10:30:00 2026 GMT

# Check Subject Alternative Names (SANs)
openssl x509 -in /etc/kubernetes/pki/apiserver.crt -text -noout | grep -A1 "Subject Alternative"
#     X509v3 Subject Alternative Name:
#         DNS:cp-1, DNS:kubernetes, DNS:kubernetes.default, IP:10.96.0.1, IP:10.0.0.10

⚠️ After Renewing Certs

You must restart the control plane components after renewing certificates. With kubeadm, simply restart kubelet: sudo systemctl restart kubelet. The static pods (API server, controller-manager, scheduler) will be recreated automatically. If you forget this step, the old (expired) certs remain loaded in memory!

A.7 etcd Commands

COMMANDS 📝 CKA EXAM

etcd backup and restore is guaranteed on the CKA exam. Every command requires TLS flags — memorize the cert paths.

etcd Backup & Restore Flow

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                 etcd BACKUP & RESTORE WORKFLOW                     │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │                                                                     │
  │  📸 BACKUP                          🔄 RESTORE                      │
  │  ──────────                         ─────────                       │
  │                                                                     │
  │  etcdctl snapshot save              etcdctl snapshot restore        │
  │       │                                   │                         │
  │       ▼                                   ▼                         │
  │  /opt/backup/                        /var/lib/etcd-restored/        │
  │  etcd-snapshot.db                    (new data dir)                 │
  │       │                                   │                         │
  │       ▼                                   ▼                         │
  │  etcdctl snapshot                    Update etcd pod manifest:      │
  │  status (verify)                     --data-dir=/var/lib/           │
  │                                      etcd-restored                  │
  │                                           │                         │
  │                                           ▼                         │
  │  ⚠️ ALL COMMANDS REQUIRE:            Restart: etcd auto-restarts    │
  │  --cacert, --cert, --key             from static pod manifest       │
  │  + ETCDCTL_API=3                                                    │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

etcd Admin Commands

CommandPurposeCKA Exam Note
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot save <file> Create backup snapshot Always specify full path: /opt/backup/snapshot.db
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot restore <file> Restore from snapshot Use --data-dir to set new directory
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot status <file> --write-table Verify backup integrity Shows hash, revision, total keys
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl member list List etcd cluster members Verify HA etcd cluster health
terminal — etcd backup & restore (CKA exam) 
# ═══ STEP 1: BACKUP ═══
# The TLS flags are MANDATORY — memorize these paths!
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot save /opt/backup/etcd-snapshot.db \
  --cacert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt \
  --cert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt \
  --key=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key \
  --endpoints=https://127.0.0.1:2379
# Snapshot saved at /opt/backup/etcd-snapshot.db

# ═══ STEP 2: VERIFY BACKUP ═══
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot status /opt/backup/etcd-snapshot.db \
  --write-table
# +----------+----------+------------+------------+
# |   HASH   | REVISION | TOTAL KEYS | TOTAL SIZE |
# +----------+----------+------------+------------+
# | 3c5d8a2f |    48291 |       1043 |     4.1 MB |
# +----------+----------+------------+------------+

# ═══ STEP 3: RESTORE ═══
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot restore /opt/backup/etcd-snapshot.db \
  --data-dir=/var/lib/etcd-restored

# ═══ STEP 4: UPDATE ETCD MANIFEST ═══
# Edit the static pod manifest:
sudo vi /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml
# Change: --data-dir=/var/lib/etcd
# To:     --data-dir=/var/lib/etcd-restored
#
# ALSO update the hostPath volume:
# path: /var/lib/etcd → path: /var/lib/etcd-restored

# etcd will auto-restart with restored data

# ═══ VERIFY ═══
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl member list \
  --cacert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt \
  --cert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt \
  --key=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key
# Shows: member ID, name, peer URLs, client URLs

⚠️ The 3 Cert Flags — Don't Forget Any!

  • --cacert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt — etcd CA certificate
  • --cert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt — etcd server certificate
  • --key=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key — etcd server private key

Also: ETCDCTL_API=3 is required. Without it, etcdctl defaults to API v2 which uses completely different commands. If your command returns an error about unknown flags, check this first!

⚠️ Restore Creates a NEW Data Directory

Never restore into the existing /var/lib/etcd directory. Always use a new path like /var/lib/etcd-restored, then update the etcd static pod manifest to point to the new directory. This prevents data corruption and allows you to keep the old data as a safety net.

📋 Appendix A — Quick Reference Card

  • ✅ A.1 — Cluster health: cluster-info, top nodes, get events, api-resources
  • ✅ A.2 — Node lifecycle: cordon → drain → maintain → uncordon
  • ✅ A.3 — Workloads: create deploy, scale, set image, rollout undo
  • ✅ A.4 — Debug: describe, logs --previous, exec -it, port-forward
  • ✅ A.5 — RBAC: create role, create rolebinding, auth can-i
  • ✅ A.6 — Certs: kubeadm certs check-expiration, renew all, openssl x509
  • ✅ A.7 — etcd: snapshot save, snapshot restore, always use TLS flags + ETCDCTL_API=3

📝 Appendix B: CKA YAML Templates

APPENDIX 📋 REFERENCE

12 fully annotated YAML templates covering all CKA exam resources — sections B.1 through B.12. Every template uses the anihpj project with the jobpost app. Copy, adapt, deploy.

YAML Template Map — Which Template for Which Task?

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │            CKA YAML TEMPLATE QUICK-FIND MAP                            │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │                                                                         │
  │  🏗️ WORKLOADS               🌐 NETWORKING          🔒 SECURITY         │
  │  ─────────────              ─────────────          ─────────────        │
  │  B.1  Pod                   B.3  Service (3 types) B.6  Role+Binding    │
  │  B.2  Deployment            B.8  NetworkPolicy     B.7  ClusterRole     │
  │  B.10 PDB                   B.11 Ingress           B.9  ServiceAccount  │
  │                                                                         │
  │  💾 STORAGE                 ⚙️ CONFIGURATION                            │
  │  ─────────────              ─────────────                               │
  │  B.4  PV + PVC             B.12 ConfigMap                               │
  │  B.5  StorageClass         B.12 Secret                                  │
  │                                                                         │
  │  💡 Exam tip: Generate base YAML with --dry-run=client -o yaml          │
  │     then ADD fields like resources, probes, volumes from memory         │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

YAML Generation Shortcut: Never write YAML from scratch on the exam. Generate it first, then add what's missing:

  • kubectl run anihpj-web --image=anihpj:v1 --dry-run=client -o yaml > pod.yaml
  • kubectl create deploy anihpj-web --image=anihpj:v1 --dry-run=client -o yaml > deploy.yaml
  • kubectl expose deploy anihpj-web --port=80 --target-port=8000 --dry-run=client -o yaml > svc.yaml

B.1 Pod Template (with all common fields)

📝 CKA EXAM

The Pod is the atomic unit in Kubernetes. This template shows every field you'll encounter on the CKA exam.

Pod — Complete Exam Template

Fields marked with ← are most commonly tested on the CKA exam

apiVersion: v1                          # Always v1 for pods
kind: Pod                               # Object type
metadata:
  name: anihpj-web                      # ← Required: unique within namespace
  namespace: anihpj                     # ← Omit for default namespace
  labels:                               # ← Key-value pairs for selection
    app: anihpj
    tier: frontend
    version: v1
spec:
  containers:                           # ← List of containers (at least 1)
  - name: web                           # Container name
    image: anihpj:v1                    # ← Image[:tag]
    ports:
    - containerPort: 8000               # Informational only — does NOT expose
    env:                                # ← Environment variables
    - name: DEBUG
      value: "False"
    - name: DB_HOST
      valueFrom:
        configMapKeyRef:                # ← From ConfigMap
          name: anihpj-config
          key: DB_HOST
    - name: SECRET_KEY
      valueFrom:
        secretKeyRef:                   # ← From Secret
          name: anihpj-secret
          key: SECRET_KEY
    resources:                          # ← ALWAYS set in production
      requests:                         # Scheduling guarantee
        memory: "128Mi"
        cpu: "250m"
      limits:                           # Hard ceiling
        memory: "256Mi"
        cpu: "500m"
    readinessProbe:                     # ← When pod is ready for traffic
      httpGet:
        path: /health
        port: 8000
      initialDelaySeconds: 5
      periodSeconds: 10
    livenessProbe:                      # ← When to restart container
      httpGet:
        path: /health
        port: 8000
      initialDelaySeconds: 15
      periodSeconds: 20
    volumeMounts:                       # ← Mount volumes into container
    - name: app-data
      mountPath: /app/data
  volumes:                              # ← Define volumes at pod level
  - name: app-data
    persistentVolumeClaim:
      claimName: anihpj-pvc
  restartPolicy: Always                 # Always | OnFailure | Never
  serviceAccountName: anihpj-sa         # ← Explicit SA (security best practice)

Pod Spec Structure — Visual Map

  Pod
   ├── metadata
   │    ├── name: anihpj-web
   │    ├── namespace: anihpj
   │    └── labels: {app: anihpj}
   │
   └── spec
        ├── serviceAccountName: anihpj-sa
        ├── restartPolicy: Always
        │
        ├── containers[]
        │    └── [0] web
        │         ├── image: anihpj:v1
        │         ├── ports: [{containerPort: 8000}]
        │         ├── env: [{name, value/valueFrom}]
        │         ├── resources: {requests, limits}
        │         ├── readinessProbe: {httpGet}
        │         ├── livenessProbe: {httpGet}
        │         └── volumeMounts: [{name, mountPath}]
        │
        └── volumes[]
             └── [0] {name: app-data, pvc: anihpj-pvc}

Multi-Container Pod (Sidecar Pattern) — Exam Favourite

The CKA frequently asks you to add a sidecar container to an existing pod

apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-web-with-logger
  namespace: anihpj
spec:
  containers:
  - name: web                           # Main application container
    image: anihpj:v1
    ports:
    - containerPort: 8000
    volumeMounts:
    - name: shared-logs
      mountPath: /var/log/app
  - name: log-sidecar                   # ← Sidecar: ships logs
    image: busybox:1.36
    command: ["sh", "-c", "tail -f /var/log/app/access.log"]
    volumeMounts:
    - name: shared-logs                 # ← Same volume = shared filesystem
      mountPath: /var/log/app
  initContainers:                       # ← Runs BEFORE main containers
  - name: init-db-check
    image: busybox:1.36
    command: ["sh", "-c", "until nslookup anihpj-db-svc; do echo waiting; sleep 2; done"]
  volumes:
  - name: shared-logs
    emptyDir: {}                        # ← Temporary shared storage

Multi-Container Pod Patterns

  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  Pod: anihpj-web-with-logger                                 │
  │                                                              │
  │  ┌─ INIT CONTAINER ──────────┐  Runs FIRST, must complete   │
  │  │  init-db-check            │  before main containers start │
  │  │  (waits for DB DNS)       │                               │
  │  └───────────────────────────┘                               │
  │           │ ✅ done                                           │
  │           ▼                                                  │
  │  ┌─ MAIN CONTAINERS (run in parallel) ───────────────────┐  │
  │  │                                                        │  │
  │  │  ┌─────────────┐    shared     ┌─────────────────┐    │  │
  │  │  │ web         │    volume     │ log-sidecar     │    │  │
  │  │  │ anihpj:v1   │◄────────────▶│ busybox:1.36    │    │  │
  │  │  │ :8000       │  /var/log/app │ tail -f logs    │    │  │
  │  │  └─────────────┘               └─────────────────┘    │  │
  │  │                                                        │  │
  │  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

B.2 Deployment Template (with strategy)

📝 CKA EXAM

Deployments manage ReplicaSets which manage Pods. The strategy field controls how updates are rolled out.

Deployment — Rolling Update Strategy

apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj-web
  namespace: anihpj
  labels:
    app: anihpj
spec:
  replicas: 3                           # ← Desired pod count
  strategy:
    type: RollingUpdate                 # ← RollingUpdate | Recreate
    rollingUpdate:
      maxSurge: 1                       # Extra pods during update (can be % or int)
      maxUnavailable: 0                 # ← Zero-downtime: never go below replicas
  selector:
    matchLabels:                        # ← MUST match template.metadata.labels
      app: anihpj
  template:                             # ← Pod template (same fields as B.1)
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj                     # ← MUST match selector.matchLabels
    spec:
      serviceAccountName: anihpj-sa
      containers:
      - name: web
        image: anihpj:v1
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8000
        resources:
          requests:
            memory: "128Mi"
            cpu: "250m"
          limits:
            memory: "256Mi"
            cpu: "500m"
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /health
            port: 8000
          initialDelaySeconds: 5
StrategyBehaviorWhen to UseDowntime?
RollingUpdate Gradually replaces pods one by one Production — zero-downtime deploys ❌ No
Recreate Kills ALL old pods, then creates new ones Dev/test, or when old+new can't coexist ✅ Yes

⚠️ Label Mismatch = Deployment Stuck

If spec.selector.matchLabels doesn't match spec.template.metadata.labels, the Deployment can't find its pods. Kubernetes will reject the manifest outright. This is the #1 YAML mistake — always ensure these labels are identical.

B.3 Service Templates (ClusterIP, NodePort, LoadBalancer)

📝 CKA EXAM

Services expose pods to the network. Three types, each for different access patterns.

Service Types — Access Levels

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                                                                     │
  │  ☁️ INTERNET                                                        │
  │     │                                                               │
  │     ▼                                                               │
  │  ┌─────────────────┐                                                │
  │  │  LoadBalancer    │ ← Cloud provider provisions external LB       │
  │  │  (external IP)  │   Port: 80 → targetPort: 8000                 │
  │  └────────┬────────┘                                                │
  │           │                                                         │
  │     ┌─────┴─────┐                                                   │
  │     ▼           ▼                                                   │
  │  ┌────────┐  ┌────────┐                                             │
  │  │NodePort│  │NodePort│ ← Every node opens port 30000-32767        │
  │  │ :30080 │  │ :30080 │   Access: <nodeIP>:30080                 │
  │  └───┬────┘  └───┬────┘                                             │
  │      │           │                                                  │
  │      └─────┬─────┘                                                  │
  │            ▼                                                        │
  │     ┌─────────────┐                                                 │
  │     │  ClusterIP   │ ← Internal only (default)                     │
  │     │  10.96.0.100 │   Access: anihpj-web-svc.anihpj.svc:80       │
  │     └──────┬──────┘                                                 │
  │            │                                                        │
  │     ┌──────┴──────┐                                                 │
  │     ▼             ▼                                                 │
  │  ┌──────┐     ┌──────┐                                              │
  │  │ Pod  │     │ Pod  │ ← selector: app=anihpj matches these        │
  │  │:8000 │     │:8000 │                                              │
  │  └──────┘     └──────┘                                              │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

ClusterIP Service (internal only — default)

apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: anihpj-web-svc
  namespace: anihpj
spec:
  type: ClusterIP                       # Default — can omit
  selector:
    app: anihpj                         # ← MUST match pod labels
  ports:
  - port: 80                            # Service port (what clients connect to)
    targetPort: 8000                    # Container port (where app listens)
    protocol: TCP

NodePort Service (external via node IP:port)

apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: anihpj-web-nodeport
  namespace: anihpj
spec:
  type: NodePort
  selector:
    app: anihpj
  ports:
  - port: 80                            # Service port
    targetPort: 8000                    # Container port
    nodePort: 30080                     # ← 30000-32767 range (omit for auto)

LoadBalancer Service (cloud provider LB)

apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: anihpj-web-lb
  namespace: anihpj
spec:
  type: LoadBalancer
  selector:
    app: anihpj
  ports:
  - port: 80                            # External port
    targetPort: 8000                    # Container port
  # Cloud provider assigns EXTERNAL-IP automatically
TypeAccessUse CaseImperative
ClusterIP Internal only DB, internal APIs kubectl expose deploy anihpj-web --port=80 --target-port=8000
NodePort External via nodeIP:port Dev/test, on-prem kubectl expose deploy anihpj-web --type=NodePort --port=80
LoadBalancer External via cloud LB Production, cloud kubectl expose deploy anihpj-web --type=LoadBalancer --port=80

B.4 PersistentVolume + PVC Template

📝 CKA EXAM

PVs are cluster-level storage resources; PVCs are namespace-scoped claims that bind to PVs. Admin creates PV, developer creates PVC.

PV ↔ PVC Binding Flow

  ADMIN creates:                  DEVELOPER creates:          Pod uses:
  ┌──────────────┐               ┌────────────────┐        ┌──────────┐
  │ PersistentVol│               │ PersistentVol  │        │   Pod    │
  │              │    BINDING    │ Claim          │ MOUNT  │          │
  │ capacity:10Gi│◄────────────▶│ request: 10Gi  │───────▶│ volumes: │
  │ accessMode:  │  (automatic) │ accessMode:    │        │  - pvc:  │
  │  RWO         │               │  RWO           │        │  anihpj- │
  │ storageClass:│               │ storageClass:  │        │  pvc     │
  │  manual      │               │  manual        │        │          │
  └──────────────┘               └────────────────┘        └──────────┘
     ↑                              ↑
     Must match:                    Must match:
     - accessModes                  - storageClassName
     - storageClassName             - capacity ≤ PV capacity

PersistentVolume (admin creates)

apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolume
metadata:
  name: anihpj-pv                       # PVs are cluster-scoped (no namespace)
spec:
  capacity:
    storage: 10Gi                       # ← Storage amount
  accessModes:
  - ReadWriteOnce                       # ← RWO | ROX | RWX
  persistentVolumeReclaimPolicy: Retain # ← Retain | Delete | Recycle
  storageClassName: manual              # ← MUST match PVC
  hostPath:                             # ← Dev only! Use cloud storage in prod
    path: /data/anihpj-db

PersistentVolumeClaim (developer creates)

apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: anihpj-pvc
  namespace: anihpj
spec:
  accessModes:
  - ReadWriteOnce                       # ← Must match PV
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 10Gi                     # ← Must be ≤ PV capacity
  storageClassName: manual              # ← Must match PV
Access ModeAbbreviationDescriptionUse Case
ReadWriteOnceRWORead-write by a single nodeDatabase (PostgreSQL)
ReadOnlyManyROXRead-only by many nodesShared config/assets
ReadWriteManyRWXRead-write by many nodesShared uploads (NFS)

B.5 StorageClass Template

📝 CKA EXAM

StorageClasses enable dynamic provisioning — no pre-created PVs needed. When a PVC references a StorageClass, Kubernetes automatically creates the PV.

Static vs Dynamic Provisioning

  STATIC Provisioning (B.4)              DYNAMIC Provisioning (B.5)
  ──────────────────────────             ──────────────────────────

  1. Admin creates PV manually           1. Admin creates StorageClass
     apiVersion: v1                          apiVersion: storage.k8s.io/v1
     kind: PersistentVolume                  kind: StorageClass
     capacity: 10Gi                          provisioner: ebs.csi.aws.com
           │                                       │
  2. Dev creates PVC                     2. Dev creates PVC (same)
     storageClassName: manual                storageClassName: fast-storage
           │                                       │
  3. K8s binds PVC → PV                  3. K8s AUTO-CREATES PV + binds!
     (must find matching PV)                 (provisioner handles it)
           │                                       │
           ▼                                       ▼
  ⚠️ If no PV matches → Pending          ✅ PV created on-demand = no Pending

  💡 Exam: Dynamic is preferred in cloud. Static for hostPath/local dev.

StorageClass — Dynamic Provisioning

apiVersion: storage.k8s.io/v1
kind: StorageClass
metadata:
  name: fast-storage
provisioner: kubernetes.io/no-provisioner  # ← Or: ebs.csi.aws.com, pd.csi.storage.gke.io
volumeBindingMode: WaitForFirstConsumer    # ← Delay until pod schedules (topology-aware)
reclaimPolicy: Delete                      # ← Delete PV when PVC is deleted
allowVolumeExpansion: true                 # ← Allow PVC resize
Binding ModeBehaviorBest For
ImmediatePV created as soon as PVC is createdSingle-zone clusters
WaitForFirstConsumerPV created when first pod using PVC is scheduledMulti-zone clusters (prevents zone mismatch)

B.6 Role + RoleBinding Template

📝 CKA EXAM

Namespace-scoped RBAC — grants permissions within a specific namespace.

Role (namespace-scoped permissions)

apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  namespace: anihpj                     # ← Scoped to this namespace
  name: anihpj-role
rules:
- apiGroups: [""]                       # ← "" = core API group (pods, services, etc.)
  resources: ["pods", "services"]       # ← What resources
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]       # ← What actions allowed
- apiGroups: ["apps"]                   # ← apps group (deployments, replicasets)
  resources: ["deployments"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch", "update"]

RoleBinding (assigns role to subject)

apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  namespace: anihpj                     # ← Same namespace as the Role
  name: anihpj-binding
subjects:                               # ← WHO gets the permissions
- kind: ServiceAccount
  name: anihpj-sa
  namespace: anihpj
roleRef:                                # ← WHICH role to assign
  kind: Role                            # Role or ClusterRole
  name: anihpj-role
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
💡 Common API Groups

"" (core): pods, services, secrets, configmaps, PVCs, namespaces, nodes, events. "apps": deployments, replicasets, statefulsets, daemonsets. "rbac.authorization.k8s.io": roles, rolebindings. "networking.k8s.io": networkpolicies, ingresses.

B.7 ClusterRole + ClusterRoleBinding Template

📝 CKA EXAM

Cluster-wide RBAC — grants permissions across all namespaces or on cluster-scoped resources (nodes, PVs).

ClusterRole (cluster-wide permissions)

apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRole
metadata:
  name: node-reader                     # ← No namespace (cluster-scoped)
rules:
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["nodes"]                  # ← Cluster-scoped resource
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["persistentvolumes"]      # ← Also cluster-scoped
  verbs: ["get", "list"]

ClusterRoleBinding

apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRoleBinding
metadata:
  name: node-reader-binding             # ← No namespace
subjects:
- kind: User
  name: jane
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
roleRef:
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: node-reader
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
Role + RoleBindingClusterRole + ClusterRoleBinding
ScopeSingle namespaceAll namespaces / cluster resources
Use forApp-level access (anihpj pods)Admin access (nodes, PVs)
Has namespace?Yes (in metadata)No
Exampleanihpj-sa can read pods in anihpj nsjane can read nodes cluster-wide

B.8 NetworkPolicy Template

📝 CKA EXAM

NetworkPolicies control pod-to-pod traffic. By default, all pods can talk to all pods. A NetworkPolicy restricts that — deny by default, allow explicitly.

NetworkPolicy — anihpj Traffic Flow

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  namespace: anihpj                                              │
  │                                                                 │
  │  ┌──────────────┐     ✅ port 5432    ┌──────────────────┐     │
  │  │  anihpj-web  │ ──────────────────▶ │  anihpj-db       │     │
  │  │  app: anihpj │                     │  app: anihpj-db  │     │
  │  └──────────────┘                     └──────────────────┘     │
  │                                               ▲                 │
  │  ┌──────────────┐     ❌ BLOCKED              │                 │
  │  │  other-pod   │ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ┘                 │
  │  │  app: hacker │    NetworkPolicy denies                       │
  │  └──────────────┘    non-matching pods                          │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

NetworkPolicy — Allow Only anihpj-web → anihpj-db

apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: db-restrict
  namespace: anihpj
spec:
  podSelector:                          # ← Apply to these pods
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj-db
  policyTypes:
  - Ingress                             # ← Control incoming traffic
  ingress:
  - from:
    - podSelector:                      # ← Only allow FROM these pods
        matchLabels:
          app: anihpj
    ports:
    - port: 5432                        # ← Only on PostgreSQL port
      protocol: TCP

⚠️ NetworkPolicy Requires a CNI That Supports It

NetworkPolicies only work if your CNI plugin enforces them. Calico, Cilium, Weave Net support them. Flannel does NOT. On the CKA exam, the cluster uses a supporting CNI. If you apply a NetworkPolicy and traffic still flows unrestricted, check your CNI plugin.

Deny-All Default Policy (Common Exam Question!)

Apply this FIRST, then allow specific traffic with additional policies

apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: deny-all-ingress
  namespace: anihpj
spec:
  podSelector: {}                       # ← Empty = applies to ALL pods in namespace
  policyTypes:
  - Ingress                             # ← Block all incoming traffic
  # No ingress rules = nothing allowed
💡 NetworkPolicy Logic

No policy = all traffic allowed. Policy with empty podSelector = applies to all pods. Policy with no ingress rules = deny all ingress. Combine deny-all + specific allow policies for a "zero trust" network model. The CKA exam loves this pattern.

B.9 ServiceAccount Template

📝 CKA EXAM

ServiceAccounts provide identity for pods. Each pod runs as a ServiceAccount — always create a dedicated one instead of using default.

ServiceAccount

apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: anihpj-sa
  namespace: anihpj
automountServiceAccountToken: false     # ← Security: don't auto-mount token
                                        #   Only set true if pod needs API access
⚠️ Security Best Practice

Set automountServiceAccountToken: false unless your pod actually needs to talk to the Kubernetes API. The default SA token mounted at /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/token gives the pod API access. If compromised, attackers could query the API. The CKA exam may ask you to configure this.

terminal — ServiceAccount lifecycle 
# Create SA imperatively
kubectl create serviceaccount anihpj-sa -n anihpj

# Assign to a pod (in pod spec)
# spec.serviceAccountName: anihpj-sa

# Verify which SA a pod uses
kubectl get pod anihpj-web -n anihpj -o jsonpath='{.spec.serviceAccountName}'
# anihpj-sa

# Check if token is mounted
kubectl exec anihpj-web -n anihpj -- ls /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/
# ca.crt  namespace  token

# Full RBAC chain: create SA → create Role → bind them
kubectl create sa anihpj-sa -n anihpj
kubectl create role anihpj-role --verb=get,list --resource=pods -n anihpj
kubectl create rolebinding anihpj-binding --role=anihpj-role \
  --serviceaccount=anihpj:anihpj-sa -n anihpj

B.10 PodDisruptionBudget Template

📝 CKA EXAM

PDBs protect your app during voluntary disruptions (node drain, cluster upgrade). They guarantee minimum availability.

PodDisruptionBudget

apiVersion: policy/v1
kind: PodDisruptionBudget
metadata:
  name: anihpj-pdb
  namespace: anihpj
spec:
  minAvailable: 2                       # ← At least 2 pods must stay running
  # OR: maxUnavailable: 1              # ← At most 1 pod can be down
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj                       # ← Protects pods with this label
FieldMeaningExample (3 replicas)
minAvailable: 2Keep at least 2 runningCan evict max 1 at a time
maxUnavailable: 1At most 1 can be downSame effect for 3 replicas
minAvailable: "50%"Keep at least 50% runningCan evict 1 of 3

⚠️ PDB Blocks kubectl drain!

If a PDB prevents eviction, kubectl drain will hang indefinitely. Common CKA trap: you try to drain a node but it just waits. Check PDBs: kubectl get pdb -A. If minAvailable equals the current replica count and there's only one node, the drain can never succeed. You must either scale up first or temporarily lower the PDB's minAvailable.

B.11 Ingress Template

📝 CKA EXAM

Ingress provides HTTP/HTTPS routing from external traffic to internal Services. Requires an Ingress Controller (e.g., nginx) to be installed in the cluster.

Ingress Routing — anihpj Example

  Internet
     │
     ▼
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │              Ingress Controller (nginx)                   │
  │                                                          │
  │  Rule 1: anihpj.example.com/                             │
  │           └──▶ Service: anihpj-web-svc:80                │
  │                                                          │
  │  Rule 2: anihpj.example.com/api/                         │
  │           └──▶ Service: anihpj-api-svc:80                │
  │                                                          │
  │  Rule 3: admin.example.com/                              │
  │           └──▶ Service: admin-svc:80                     │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
     │                    │                    │
     ▼                    ▼                    ▼
  ┌────────┐         ┌────────┐         ┌────────┐
  │anihpj  │         │anihpj  │         │ admin  │
  │web pods│         │api pods│         │ pods   │
  └────────┘         └────────┘         └────────┘

Ingress — Host & Path-Based Routing

apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: anihpj-ingress
  namespace: anihpj
  annotations:
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/rewrite-target: /
spec:
  ingressClassName: nginx               # ← Which Ingress Controller to use
  rules:
  - host: anihpj.example.com            # ← Domain name routing
    http:
      paths:
      - path: /                         # ← URL path
        pathType: Prefix                # ← Prefix | Exact | ImplementationSpecific
        backend:
          service:
            name: anihpj-web-svc        # ← Target Service name
            port:
              number: 80                # ← Target Service port
      - path: /api
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service:
            name: anihpj-api-svc
            port:
              number: 80
pathTypeMatchesExample
PrefixURL prefix match/jobs matches /jobs, /jobs/123
ExactExact URL match only/jobs matches only /jobs

Ingress with TLS (HTTPS)

The CKA may ask you to configure TLS termination — create the Secret first

# Step 1: Create TLS secret
# kubectl create secret tls anihpj-tls \
#   --cert=tls.crt --key=tls.key -n anihpj

# Step 2: Reference in Ingress
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: anihpj-ingress-tls
  namespace: anihpj
spec:
  ingressClassName: nginx
  tls:                                  # ← TLS termination
  - hosts:
    - anihpj.example.com
    secretName: anihpj-tls              # ← Secret with tls.crt + tls.key
  rules:
  - host: anihpj.example.com
    http:
      paths:
      - path: /
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service:
            name: anihpj-web-svc
            port:
              number: 80

B.12 ConfigMap + Secret Template

📝 CKA EXAM

ConfigMaps store non-sensitive config; Secrets store sensitive data (base64-encoded, not encrypted by default).

ConfigMap

apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: anihpj-config
  namespace: anihpj
data:                                   # ← Key-value pairs (plain text)
  DEBUG: "False"
  DB_HOST: "anihpj-db-svc"
  DB_PORT: "5432"
  ALLOWED_HOSTS: "*"
  DB_NAME: "anihpj_db"

Secret (base64-encoded values)

apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: anihpj-secret
  namespace: anihpj
type: Opaque                            # ← Most common type
data:                                   # ← Values MUST be base64-encoded
  SECRET_KEY: c2VjcmV0MTIz              # echo -n 'secret123' | base64
  DB_PASSWORD: cGFzc3dvcmQ=             # echo -n 'password' | base64
  DB_USER: YW5paHBq                     # echo -n 'anihpj' | base64
terminal — imperative ConfigMap/Secret creation 
# Create ConfigMap imperatively
kubectl create configmap anihpj-config \
  --from-literal=DEBUG=False \
  --from-literal=DB_HOST=anihpj-db-svc \
  --from-literal=ALLOWED_HOSTS='*' \
  -n anihpj

# Create Secret imperatively (auto-encodes to base64!)
kubectl create secret generic anihpj-secret \
  --from-literal=SECRET_KEY=secret123 \
  --from-literal=DB_PASSWORD=password \
  -n anihpj

# Decode a secret value
kubectl get secret anihpj-secret -n anihpj -o jsonpath='{.data.DB_PASSWORD}' | base64 -d
# password

ConfigMap/Secret → Pod — Three Injection Methods

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  METHOD 1: Environment Variables (most common)                  │
  │  ──────────────────────────────────────────────                 │
  │  env:                                                           │
  │  - name: DB_HOST                                                │
  │    valueFrom:                                                   │
  │      configMapKeyRef:                                           │
  │        name: anihpj-config                                      │
  │        key: DB_HOST                                             │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │  METHOD 2: envFrom (all keys at once)                           │
  │  ─────────────────────────────────────                          │
  │  envFrom:                                                       │
  │  - configMapRef:                                                │
  │      name: anihpj-config                                        │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │  METHOD 3: Volume Mount (as files)                              │
  │  ─────────────────────────────────                              │
  │  volumes:                                                       │
  │  - name: config-vol                                             │
  │    configMap:                                                    │
  │      name: anihpj-config                                        │
  │  volumeMounts:                                                  │
  │  - name: config-vol                                             │
  │    mountPath: /etc/config     ← Each key becomes a file         │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

⚠️ Secrets Aren't Encrypted by Default!

Kubernetes Secrets are only base64-encoded, not encrypted. Anyone with API access can decode them. For real security: enable encryption at rest (EncryptionConfiguration), use RBAC to restrict Secret access, and consider external tools like HashiCorp Vault or Sealed Secrets.

📋 Appendix B — Template Quick Reference

  • ✅ B.1 Pod — containers, env, resources, probes, volumes
  • ✅ B.2 Deployment — replicas, strategy (RollingUpdate/Recreate), selector must match labels
  • ✅ B.3 Service — ClusterIP (internal), NodePort (external:30000-32767), LoadBalancer (cloud)
  • ✅ B.4 PV+PVC — capacity, accessModes, storageClassName must match
  • ✅ B.5 StorageClass — dynamic provisioning, WaitForFirstConsumer
  • ✅ B.6 Role+RoleBinding — namespace-scoped permissions
  • ✅ B.7 ClusterRole+ClusterRoleBinding — cluster-wide permissions
  • ✅ B.8 NetworkPolicy — pod-level firewall, requires supporting CNI
  • ✅ B.9 ServiceAccount — pod identity, disable auto-mount token
  • ✅ B.10 PDB — minAvailable or maxUnavailable during disruptions
  • ✅ B.11 Ingress — HTTP routing by host/path, needs Ingress Controller
  • ✅ B.12 ConfigMap+Secret — env vars, envFrom, volume mount

🚨 Appendix C: Common Errors and Solutions

APPENDIX 📋 REFERENCE

Quick reference table — Error → Cause → Fix for the most common Kubernetes issues you'll encounter on the CKA exam and in production.

Troubleshooting Decision Tree — Where to Start

  Something is wrong!
        │
        ▼
  ┌───────────────────┐     YES     ┌──────────────────────────────────┐
  │ Pod running?       ├───────────▶│ Check: logs, exec, describe       │
  │ kubectl get pods   │            │ → CrashLoopBackOff (app crash)    │
  └────────┬──────────┘            │ → OOMKilled (memory limit)        │
           │ NO                     └──────────────────────────────────┘
           ▼
  ┌───────────────────┐     YES     ┌──────────────────────────────────┐
  │ Pod Pending?       ├───────────▶│ Check: describe pod events        │
  │                    │            │ → Insufficient resources          │
  └────────┬──────────┘            │ → No matching PV                  │
           │ NO                     │ → Taints/tolerations mismatch     │
           ▼                        └──────────────────────────────────┘
  ┌───────────────────┐     YES     ┌──────────────────────────────────┐
  │ ImagePullBackOff?  ├───────────▶│ Check: image name, tag, registry  │
  │                    │            │ → Wrong image name/tag            │
  └────────┬──────────┘            │ → Missing imagePullSecrets        │
           │ NO                     └──────────────────────────────────┘
           ▼
  ┌───────────────────┐     YES     ┌──────────────────────────────────┐
  │ Node NotReady?     ├───────────▶│ SSH to node, check kubelet        │
  │ kubectl get nodes  │            │ → systemctl status kubelet        │
  └────────┬──────────┘            │ → journalctl -u kubelet           │
           │ NO                     └──────────────────────────────────┘
           ▼
  ┌───────────────────┐
  │ API/Network issue? │─────▶ Check kubeconfig, API server, certs
  └───────────────────┘

Exam Debugging Shortcuts:

  • kubectl describe pod <name> — Always start here; the Events section at the bottom shows why things failed
  • kubectl logs <pod> --previous — See logs from the LAST crashed container
  • kubectl get events --sort-by='.lastTimestamp' — Cluster-wide timeline of issues
Error / Status Category Common Cause How to Diagnose Fix
CrashLoopBackOff 🏗️ Workload App crashes on startup — missing env var, wrong command, config error kubectl logs <pod> --previous
kubectl describe pod <pod>		 Fix app config/code. Check command, args, env vars. Ensure ConfigMap/Secret exists.
ImagePullBackOff 🏗️ Workload Wrong image name/tag, private registry without credentials kubectl describe pod <pod> → Events section Fix image name. For private registries: create imagePullSecrets and reference in pod spec.
Pending (Pod) 🏗️ Workload Insufficient CPU/memory, no node matches nodeSelector/affinity, all nodes tainted kubectl describe pod <pod> → Events
kubectl describe node <node>		 Reduce resource requests, add nodes, remove taints, or add tolerations.
Pending (PVC) 💾 Storage No PV matches accessMode/capacity/storageClass kubectl describe pvc <name>
kubectl get pv		 Create matching PV or fix StorageClass name. Ensure accessModes and storageClassName match.
NodeNotReady 🖥️ Node kubelet stopped, resource pressure (disk/memory), network partition ssh <node>
systemctl status kubelet
journalctl -u kubelet		 Restart kubelet: systemctl restart kubelet. Free disk/memory. Check network connectivity.
FailedScheduling 🖥️ Node All nodes tainted (no matching tolerations), nodeAffinity mismatch kubectl describe pod <pod> → Events
kubectl describe node <node> → Taints		 Add tolerations to pod spec, or remove taint: kubectl taint node <n> key:NoSchedule-
Forbidden 🔒 RBAC ServiceAccount/User lacks permissions kubectl auth can-i <verb> <resource> --as=system:serviceaccount:ns:sa Create or update Role/ClusterRole + Binding to grant required permissions.
connection refused 🌐 Network Target pod/service not running, wrong port, NetworkPolicy blocking kubectl get svc
kubectl get endpoints
kubectl get netpol		 Ensure target pod is running and healthy. Verify Service selector matches pod labels. Check NetworkPolicy rules.
certificate has expired 🔐 Certs Kubernetes PKI cert past its validity period (typically 1 year) kubeadm certs check-expiration
openssl x509 -in /etc/kubernetes/pki/apiserver.crt -noout -dates		 kubeadm certs renew all then restart control plane:
systemctl restart kubelet
etcd cluster is unavailable 🗄️ etcd etcd pod down, cert expired, disk full kubectl -n kube-system get pods -l component=etcd
crictl ps | grep etcd		 Check etcd logs. Verify certs in /etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/. Restore from backup if data corrupt.
unable to connect to server ⚙️ API Wrong kubeconfig, API server pod down, firewall blocking port 6443 kubectl cluster-info
cat ~/.kube/config — check server URL
crictl ps | grep kube-api		 Fix kubeconfig server URL. Restart API server. Check firewall rules for port 6443.
OOMKilled 🏗️ Workload Container exceeded its memory limit kubectl describe pod <pod> → Last State: OOMKilled Increase resources.limits.memory, or fix memory leak in application.

Top 5 CKA Exam Errors — Detailed Fix Walkthroughs

💡 CKA Debug Pattern (memorize this!)

describe → logs → exec → events. Start with describe for events, check logs for app errors, exec into the pod to test connectivity, and get events for cluster-wide issues.

terminal — CrashLoopBackOff deep debug 
# 1. Check pod status and restart count
kubectl get pod anihpj-web -n anihpj
# NAME         READY   STATUS             RESTARTS   AGE
# anihpj-web   0/1     CrashLoopBackOff   5          2m

# 2. See why it crashed (logs from PREVIOUS container)
kubectl logs anihpj-web -n anihpj --previous
# ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'django'
# → Fix: wrong image or missing dependency

# 3. Check events for scheduling/pull issues
kubectl describe pod anihpj-web -n anihpj | tail -20
# Events:
#   Warning  BackOff  kubelet  Back-off restarting failed container

# 4. If env var issue — check ConfigMap exists
kubectl get configmap anihpj-config -n anihpj
kubectl get secret anihpj-secret -n anihpj
terminal — Pending Pod / FailedScheduling debug 
# 1. Check WHY it's pending
kubectl describe pod anihpj-web -n anihpj | grep -A5 Events
# 0/3 nodes are available: 3 Insufficient memory

# 2. Check node resources
kubectl top nodes
kubectl describe node worker-1 | grep -A5 "Allocated resources"

# 3. If taint issue — check node taints
kubectl describe node worker-1 | grep Taints
# Taints: node-role.kubernetes.io/control-plane:NoSchedule

# 4. Fix: add toleration to pod OR remove taint
kubectl taint node worker-1 node-role.kubernetes.io/control-plane:NoSchedule-
terminal — RBAC Forbidden debug 
# 1. Test what the SA can do
kubectl auth can-i get pods --as=system:serviceaccount:anihpj:anihpj-sa -n anihpj
# no

# 2. Check existing roles/bindings
kubectl get roles,rolebindings -n anihpj
kubectl get clusterroles,clusterrolebindings | grep anihpj

# 3. Fix: create the missing Role + RoleBinding
kubectl create role anihpj-role --verb=get,list,watch --resource=pods -n anihpj
kubectl create rolebinding anihpj-binding --role=anihpj-role \
  --serviceaccount=anihpj:anihpj-sa -n anihpj

# 4. Verify
kubectl auth can-i get pods --as=system:serviceaccount:anihpj:anihpj-sa -n anihpj
# yes
terminal — NodeNotReady recovery 
# 1. Identify the problem node
kubectl get nodes
# NAME       STATUS     ROLES           AGE    VERSION
# cp-1       Ready      control-plane   30d    v1.31.0
# worker-1   NotReady   <none>          30d    v1.31.0  ← PROBLEM

# 2. SSH to the affected node
ssh worker-1

# 3. Check kubelet status
systemctl status kubelet
# Active: inactive (dead)  ← kubelet stopped!

# 4. Check kubelet logs for the root cause
journalctl -u kubelet --no-pager | tail -30
# "failed to run Kubelet: unable to load bootstrap kubeconfig"
# → OR: "eviction threshold met: memory.available"

# 5. Fix and restart
sudo systemctl restart kubelet
sudo systemctl enable kubelet

# 6. Back on control plane — verify recovery
kubectl get nodes
# worker-1   Ready   <none>   30d   v1.31.0  ← RECOVERED

# 7. If disk pressure — free space on the node
ssh worker-1
df -h                                   # Check disk usage
sudo crictl rmi --prune                 # Remove unused images
sudo journalctl --vacuum-size=100M      # Trim system logs
terminal — certificate expired recovery 
# Symptom: kubectl commands return "certificate has expired"
kubectl get nodes
# Unable to connect to the server: x509: certificate has expired

# 1. SSH to the control plane node (kubectl won't work!)
ssh cp-1

# 2. Check which certs are expired
sudo kubeadm certs check-expiration
# CERTIFICATE                EXPIRES                  RESIDUAL TIME
# apiserver                  Jan 15, 2026 10:30 UTC   EXPIRED  ← !
# apiserver-kubelet-client   Jan 15, 2026 10:30 UTC   EXPIRED

# 3. Renew all certificates
sudo kubeadm certs renew all
# certificate apiserver renewed
# certificate apiserver-kubelet-client renewed
# ...
# Done renewing certificates.

# 4. Restart kubelet (restarts static pods with new certs)
sudo systemctl restart kubelet

# 5. Update admin kubeconfig (your kubectl config)
sudo cp /etc/kubernetes/admin.conf ~/.kube/config
sudo chown $(id -u):$(id -g) ~/.kube/config

# 6. Verify everything works
kubectl get nodes
# NAME   STATUS   ROLES           AGE   VERSION
# cp-1   Ready    control-plane   30d   v1.31.0  ← WORKING!

Debug Command Quick Reference Card

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │              WHICH COMMAND FOR WHICH ERROR?                          │
  ├───────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │  ERROR                │  FIRST COMMAND TO RUN                       │
  ├───────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │  CrashLoopBackOff     │  kubectl logs <pod> --previous             │
  │  ImagePullBackOff     │  kubectl describe pod <pod>                │
  │  Pending (pod)        │  kubectl describe pod <pod>                │
  │  Pending (PVC)        │  kubectl describe pvc <name>               │
  │  NodeNotReady         │  ssh node → systemctl status kubelet       │
  │  FailedScheduling     │  kubectl describe pod → check taints       │
  │  Forbidden            │  kubectl auth can-i <verb> <res> --as=SA  │
  │  connection refused   │  kubectl get endpoints <svc>               │
  │  cert expired         │  ssh cp → kubeadm certs check-expiration   │
  │  etcd unavailable     │  crictl ps | grep etcd                     │
  │  can't connect to API │  cat ~/.kube/config (check server URL)     │
  │  OOMKilled            │  kubectl describe pod → Last State          │
  └───────────────────────┴─────────────────────────────────────────────┘

🚨 Appendix C — Quick Debug Cheat Sheet

  • ✅ CrashLoopBackOff → logs --previous → fix config/code
  • ✅ ImagePullBackOff → check image name, add imagePullSecrets
  • ✅ Pending Pod → describe pod → resources, taints, affinity
  • ✅ Pending PVC → match PV accessMode + storageClassName + capacity
  • ✅ NodeNotReady → SSH, systemctl restart kubelet
  • ✅ Forbidden → auth can-i → create Role + RoleBinding
  • ✅ connection refused → verify pod running + Service selector + NetworkPolicy
  • ✅ cert expired → kubeadm certs renew all
  • ✅ etcd unavailable → check etcd pod/certs, restore from backup
  • ✅ unable to connect → fix kubeconfig, check API server

📖 Appendix D: Kubernetes Terminology Glossary

APPENDIX 📋 REFERENCE

110+ Kubernetes terms organized alphabetically across 22 letter sections (A–W) with CKA-relevant definitions, object hierarchy diagrams, and resource scope reference. Bookmark this glossary — if you see an unfamiliar term on the CKA exam, this is your quick-reference.

🔤 Jump to Letter

A B C D E F G H I J K L M N O P R S T U V W

Where These Terms Fit — Kubernetes Architecture Map

  ┌──────────────────────────── CONTROL PLANE ────────────────────────────┐
  │                                                                       │
  │  ┌────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐  ┌─────────────┐  ┌────────────┐  │
  │  │ API Server │  │  Scheduler   │  │ Controller  │  │    etcd    │  │
  │  │            │  │              │  │  Manager    │  │  (key-val  │  │
  │  │ AuthN/AuthZ│  │ NodeSelector │  │ ReplicaSet  │  │   store)   │  │
  │  │ Admission  │  │ Affinity     │  │ Deployment  │  │            │  │
  │  │ RBAC       │  │ Taints       │  │ DaemonSet   │  │ Backup/    │  │
  │  │            │  │ Tolerations  │  │ StatefulSet │  │ Restore    │  │
  │  └─────┬──────┘  └──────────────┘  └─────────────┘  └────────────┘  │
  └────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
           │
  ┌────────┼──────────────── WORKER NODES ────────────────────────────────┐
  │        ▼                                                              │
  │  ┌──────────┐  ┌────────────┐  ┌─────────────────────────────────┐   │
  │  │ kubelet  │  │ kube-proxy │  │           PODS                  │   │
  │  │ (agent)  │  │ (network   │  │  ┌──────────┐  ┌─────────────┐ │   │
  │  │          │  │  rules)    │  │  │Container │  │  Sidecar    │ │   │
  │  │ Probes   │  │ Services   │  │  │ Image    │  │  Init Ctr   │ │   │
  │  │ cgroups  │  │ iptables   │  │  │ Volumes  │  │  emptyDir   │ │   │
  │  └──────────┘  └────────────┘  │  │ Env/CM/  │  │             │ │   │
  │                                │  │  Secret  │  │             │ │   │
  │  ┌──────────────────────────┐  │  └──────────┘  └─────────────┘ │   │
  │  │ Container Runtime        │  └─────────────────────────────────┘   │
  │  │ (containerd / CRI-O)     │                                        │
  │  │ OCI images, CNI plugins  │                                        │
  │  └──────────────────────────┘                                        │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Exam Tip: You won't be asked definitions directly, but understanding these terms helps you parse question wording correctly. The CKA uses precise terminology — knowing the difference between cordon vs drain, or Role vs ClusterRole saves critical seconds.

Kubernetes Object Ownership Hierarchy

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                        DEPLOYMENT                                │
  │          (Desired state: replicas, image, strategy)              │
  └───────────┬─────────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────┘
              │ creates & manages            │ creates on update
              ▼                              ▼
  ┌─────────────────────┐      ┌─────────────────────────┐
  │   ReplicaSet (v2)   │      │   ReplicaSet (v1)       │
  │   replicas: 3        │      │   replicas: 0 (scaled   │
  │   ✅ current         │      │   down after rollout)   │
  └───────┬──┬──┬────────┘      └─────────────────────────┘
          │  │  │ creates & maintains desired count
          ▼  ▼  ▼
  ┌─────┐ ┌─────┐ ┌─────┐
  │Pod-0│ │Pod-1│ │Pod-2│    Each pod gets:
  └──┬──┘ └──┬──┘ └──┬──┘    • Unique IP
     │       │       │        • Shared network namespace
     ▼       ▼       ▼        • ownerReference → ReplicaSet
  ┌──────┐┌──────┐┌──────┐
  │ Ctr  ││ Ctr  ││ Ctr  │   Container = running image
  │nginx ││nginx ││nginx │   (the actual process)
  └──────┘└──────┘└──────┘

  🔑 Ownership chain: Deployment → ReplicaSet → Pod → Container
  🗑️ Delete Deployment = cascading delete of ALL children
🔍 Resource Scope Quick Reference

Every K8s resource is either cluster-scoped or namespace-scoped. This determines where you can create it and how RBAC applies.

🌐 Cluster-Scoped (no namespace) 📁 Namespace-Scoped
Node, PersistentVolume, StorageClassPod, Deployment, ReplicaSet, StatefulSet
Namespace (yes, it's cluster-scoped!)Service, Endpoints, Ingress
ClusterRole, ClusterRoleBindingRole, RoleBinding, ServiceAccount
CustomResourceDefinition (CRD)ConfigMap, Secret, PVC
PriorityClass, RuntimeClassJob, CronJob, DaemonSet, HPA
CSIDriver, CSINodeNetworkPolicy, PodDisruptionBudget
IngressClassLimitRange, ResourceQuota

⚠️ CKA Trap: Forgetting -n namespace

If you create a Role but forget -n <namespace>, it goes to default. Always specify namespace explicitly on the exam: kubectl create role myrole --verb=get --resource=pods -n target-ns

A

TermCKA?Definition
Admission Controller📝Plugin that intercepts API requests after authentication/authorization but before persistence. Examples: LimitRanger, PodSecurity, MutatingWebhook. Can mutate or validate objects.
Affinity📝Rules controlling pod placement. nodeAffinity targets specific nodes; podAffinity/podAntiAffinity attracts/repels pods relative to other pods. Softer than nodeSelector.
Annotation📝Key-value metadata attached to objects. Unlike labels, annotations are NOT used for selection. Used for: build info, config hints (nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/rewrite-target), tool metadata.
API Group📝Logical grouping of API resources. Core group: "" (pods, services). Named groups: apps (deployments), batch (jobs), networking.k8s.io (networkpolicies), rbac.authorization.k8s.io.
API Server📝Central management hub of Kubernetes (kube-apiserver). All kubectl commands and internal components communicate through it. Runs on port 6443. Handles AuthN → AuthZ → Admission → etcd persistence.

B

TermCKA?Definition
Base64📝Encoding (NOT encryption) used by Secrets. echo -n "password" | base64 to encode; echo "cGFzc3dvcmQ=" | base64 -d to decode. Anyone with RBAC access to the Secret can decode it.
BindingAPI object that ties a pod to a node. The Scheduler creates a Binding when it assigns a pod. Rarely used directly — understanding helps when debugging Pending pods.
Blue-Green DeploymentDeployment strategy: run two identical environments (blue=current, green=new), switch traffic instantly via Service selector change. Not built-in — implemented using label swaps on Services.

C

TermCKA?Definition
Calico📝CNI plugin providing pod networking and NetworkPolicy enforcement. Most popular choice for CKA-style clusters. Supports BGP routing and IPIP tunneling.
CertificateSigningRequest📝API object to request a certificate signed by the cluster CA. Used for user authentication: create CSR → approve → extract cert. kubectl certificate approve <name>.
cgroup📝Linux kernel feature that limits, accounts for, and isolates resource usage (CPU, memory, I/O) of process groups. Kubernetes uses cgroups to enforce resources.limits.
CIDR📝Classless Inter-Domain Routing — notation for IP address ranges. Example: 10.244.0.0/16 = pod network, 10.96.0.0/12 = service network. Used in --pod-network-cidr and NetworkPolicy.
ClusterIP📝Default Service type — accessible only within the cluster. Gets a virtual IP from the service CIDR. Access via <svc-name>.<namespace>.svc.cluster.local.
ClusterRole📝Cluster-wide permission set (no namespace). Used for cluster-scoped resources (nodes, PVs) or cross-namespace access. Bound via ClusterRoleBinding.
CNI📝Container Network Interface — plugin standard for pod networking. Provides IP allocation and inter-pod connectivity. Examples: Calico, Flannel, Cilium, Weave Net.
ConfigMap📝Stores non-sensitive configuration as key-value pairs. Injected into pods via: environment variables (envFrom), individual env vars (configMapKeyRef), or volume mounts (files).
Container Runtime📝Software that actually runs containers. Kubernetes supports any CRI-compatible runtime. Standard: containerd. Alternative: CRI-O. Docker was removed in v1.24.
containerd📝Industry-standard container runtime. Manages the complete container lifecycle: pull images, create containers, manage storage, networking. Default runtime for most K8s distributions.
Control Plane📝The "brain" of the cluster running on master/control-plane nodes. Components: API Server, etcd, Scheduler, Controller Manager. Manages cluster state and makes scheduling decisions.
CoreDNS📝DNS server built into Kubernetes (runs as a Deployment in kube-system). Resolves service names: <svc>.<ns>.svc.cluster.local. Debug: kubectl run tmp --rm -it --image=busybox -- nslookup <svc>.
Cordon📝Mark a node as unschedulable — no new pods will be placed, but existing pods remain. kubectl cordon <node>. First step before maintenance. Reverse: uncordon.
CRIContainer Runtime Interface — protocol between kubelet and container runtime. Replaced Docker-specific Dockershim in K8s v1.24.
CronJob📝Runs Jobs on a schedule (cron syntax). Example: schedule: "0 2 * * *" = daily at 2 AM. Creates Job objects which create Pods. Good for backups, report generation.
CSIContainer Storage Interface — standard for exposing storage systems to Kubernetes. Drivers: ebs.csi.aws.com, pd.csi.storage.gke.io, disk.csi.azure.com.
CRD (CustomResourceDefinition)Extend the Kubernetes API with your own resource types. Combined with a custom controller creates an Operator. Example: kind: Certificate (cert-manager).

D

TermCKA?Definition
DaemonSet📝Ensures exactly one pod per node (or per matching node). Used for: log collectors (Fluentd), monitoring agents (node-exporter), CNI plugins, kube-proxy. Pods survive kubectl drain --ignore-daemonsets.
Deployment📝Manages ReplicaSets for declarative updates. Supports rolling updates, rollbacks, and scaling. The most common workload controller: kubectl create deployment anihpj-web --image=anihpj:v1.
Drain📝Evict all pods from a node for maintenance. Cordons the node first, then evicts pods. Flags: --ignore-daemonsets (required), --delete-emptydir-data. Managed pods are rescheduled elsewhere.

E

TermCKA?Definition
emptyDir📝Temporary volume created when a pod starts, deleted when pod is removed. Shared between containers in the same pod (sidecar pattern). volumes: [{name: shared, emptyDir: {}}].
Endpoint📝IP:port combination backing a Service. When a Service selector matches pods, Kubernetes auto-creates Endpoints. Debug: kubectl get endpoints <svc> — if empty, selector/labels mismatch.
etcd📝Distributed key-value store holding all cluster state. Runs on control plane (port 2379). Backup: etcdctl snapshot save. Restore: etcdctl snapshot restore. Always requires TLS flags.

F

TermCKA?Definition
Finalizer📝Metadata key that prevents object deletion until cleanup is done. When you delete an object with finalizers, it enters Terminating state. The controller removes the finalizer after cleanup, then K8s completes deletion.
FlannelSimple CNI plugin using overlay networking (VXLAN). Easy to set up but does NOT support NetworkPolicies. Use Calico or Cilium instead for production.
FQDN📝Fully Qualified Domain Name for Services: <svc>.<ns>.svc.cluster.local. Same-namespace pods can use just <svc>. Cross-namespace requires at least <svc>.<ns>.

G

TermCKA?Definition
Garbage CollectionAutomatic cleanup of orphaned objects. When a Deployment is deleted, its ReplicaSets and Pods are garbage collected via ownerReferences. Cascade modes: Foreground, Background, Orphan.
Gateway APINext-gen routing API (successor to Ingress). Resources: GatewayClass, Gateway, HTTPRoute. More expressive than Ingress — supports header-based routing, traffic splitting, and cross-namespace references.
GracePeriod📝Time Kubernetes waits for a pod to shut down gracefully (default: 30 seconds). Override: kubectl delete pod <name> --grace-period=0 --force. During this time, SIGTERM is sent, then SIGKILL after timeout.

H

TermCKA?Definition
HelmKubernetes package manager. Bundles YAML manifests into Charts. Install: helm install my-release chart-name. Not on CKA exam directly, but widely used in practice.
hostPath📝Volume that mounts a file/directory from the host node's filesystem into a pod. Used for: dev/test, static pods reading node configs. Not safe for production — data only on one node.
HPA (Horizontal Pod Autoscaler)📝Automatically scales pod replicas based on CPU/memory metrics (requires metrics-server). kubectl autoscale deploy anihpj-web --min=2 --max=10 --cpu-percent=70.

I

TermCKA?Definition
Ingress📝API object defining HTTP/HTTPS routing rules. Routes external traffic to Services based on host/path. Requires an Ingress Controller (nginx, traefik). Supports TLS termination.
Ingress Controller📝Component that implements Ingress rules. It's a pod running a reverse proxy (nginx, HAProxy). Without it, Ingress resources have no effect. Install: kubectl apply -f nginx-ingress.yaml.
Init Container📝Runs before main containers. Must complete successfully before app containers start. Used for: waiting for dependencies, database migrations, config generation. Defined in spec.initContainers[].

J

TermCKA?Definition
Job📝Run-to-completion workload. Pod runs once (or N times), then stops. restartPolicy: Never or OnFailure. Used for: backups, data processing, one-time migrations.
JSONPath📝Query language for extracting data from JSON output. kubectl get pods -o jsonpath='{.items[*].metadata.name}'. Powerful for scripting and CKA exam output formatting.

K

TermCKA?Definition
kubeadm📝Official tool to bootstrap Kubernetes clusters. kubeadm init (control plane), kubeadm join (workers), kubeadm upgrade (version upgrades), kubeadm certs (certificate management). Core CKA tool.
kubectl📝CLI for communicating with the Kubernetes API server. Every CKA task uses it. Config: ~/.kube/config. Structure: kubectl [verb] [resource] [name] [flags].
kubelet📝Agent running on every node. Receives pod specs from API server, ensures containers are running and healthy. Manages probes, reports node status. Not a pod — it's a systemd service: systemctl status kubelet.
kube-proxy📝Network proxy on each node maintaining network rules (iptables/IPVS). Implements Service abstraction — routes traffic to pod endpoints. Runs as a DaemonSet in kube-system.
kube-scheduler📝Control plane component that assigns pods to nodes. Considers: resource requests, nodeSelector, affinity, taints/tolerations, PV topology. If it can't find a node → pod stays Pending.
KustomizeKubernetes-native config management (built into kubectl apply -k). Overlays patches on base YAML without modifying originals. Alternative to Helm for simpler use cases.

L

TermCKA?Definition
Label📝Key-value pair attached to objects for identification and selection. Used by selectors, services, deployments: app: anihpj. Filter: kubectl get pods -l app=anihpj.
LeaseLightweight object for leader election and node heartbeats. kubelet creates a Lease in kube-node-lease namespace — if it stops updating, node is marked NotReady.
LimitRange📝Sets default/min/max resource constraints per pod/container in a namespace. If a pod doesn't specify resources, LimitRange can inject defaults. Prevents runaway resource consumption.
Liveness Probe📝Health check: "is the container alive?" If it fails, kubelet restarts the container. Types: httpGet, tcpSocket, exec. Configure initialDelaySeconds to avoid false kills on startup.

M

TermCKA?Definition
Manifest📝YAML or JSON file declaring the desired state of Kubernetes objects. Applied with kubectl apply -f manifest.yaml. The declarative approach — Kubernetes reconciles actual state to match.
Metrics Server📝Cluster add-on that collects CPU/memory usage from kubelets. Required for kubectl top nodes, kubectl top pods, and HPA autoscaling. Install: kubectl apply -f components.yaml from GitHub.
Multi-container Pod📝Pod with multiple containers sharing network and volumes. Patterns: Sidecar (helper alongside main), Ambassador (proxy), Adapter (transform output). Init containers run before app containers.

N

TermCKA?Definition
Namespace📝Virtual cluster within a cluster. Provides resource isolation, RBAC boundaries, and resource quotas. Default namespaces: default, kube-system, kube-public, kube-node-lease.
NetworkPolicy📝Firewall rules for pod traffic. Controls ingress (incoming) and egress (outgoing) at L3/L4. Requires supporting CNI (Calico, Cilium). No policy = all traffic allowed. Empty podSelector = all pods.
Node📝A worker machine (VM or physical) in the cluster. Runs kubelet, kube-proxy, and container runtime. Control plane nodes also run API server, etcd, scheduler, controller-manager.
NodePort📝Service type that opens a port (30000-32767) on every node's IP. External access via <nodeIP>:<nodePort>. Automatically creates a ClusterIP too. Good for dev/on-prem.

O

TermCKA?Definition
OCIOpen Container Initiative — industry standard for container image format and runtime specification. All K8s container images follow OCI spec.
OperatorPattern: custom controller + CRD that automates complex application lifecycle management. Examples: Prometheus Operator, cert-manager, PostgreSQL Operator. Extends Kubernetes capabilities.

P

TermCKA?Definition
PersistentVolume (PV)📝Cluster-level storage resource provisioned by admin (or dynamically by StorageClass). Cluster-scoped — no namespace. Bound to PVCs. Reclaim policies: Retain, Delete, Recycle.
PersistentVolumeClaim (PVC)📝User's request for storage. Namespace-scoped. Binds to a PV matching accessMode, capacity, and storageClass. Pod references PVC in volumes[].persistentVolumeClaim.claimName.
Pod📝Smallest deployable unit — one or more containers sharing network namespace (same IP) and volumes. Usually one container per pod. Created by Deployments, not directly. kubectl run nginx --image=nginx.
PodDisruptionBudget (PDB)📝Guarantees minimum availability during voluntary disruptions (node drain, upgrades). minAvailable: 2 or maxUnavailable: 1. Can block kubectl drain if violated.
PodSecurityAdmission📝Built-in admission controller enforcing Pod Security Standards at namespace level. Modes: enforce, audit, warn. Label: pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce: restricted.
PodSecurityStandard📝Three profiles: Privileged (unrestricted), Baseline (sensible defaults), Restricted (hardened). Applied via PodSecurityAdmission labels on namespaces.
Probe📝Health check mechanism. Three types: Liveness (restart if fail), Readiness (remove from service if fail), Startup (disable other probes until success). Methods: httpGet, tcpSocket, exec.

R

TermCKA?Definition
RBAC📝Role-Based Access Control — who can do what. Four objects: Role (ns-scoped permissions), ClusterRole (cluster-wide), RoleBinding (links subject→Role), ClusterRoleBinding.
Readiness Probe📝Health check: "is the pod ready for traffic?" If it fails, pod is removed from Service endpoints (no restart). Critical for zero-downtime deployments during rolling updates.
ReplicaSet📝Ensures a specified number of pod replicas are running. Managed by Deployments — rarely created directly. kubectl get rs shows ReplicaSets behind a Deployment.
ResourceQuota📝Limits total resource consumption per namespace (cpu, memory, pods count, PVCs). Prevents one team from consuming all cluster resources. Applied at namespace level.
RoleBinding📝Binds a Role (or ClusterRole) to subjects (users, groups, ServiceAccounts) within a single namespace. kubectl create rolebinding name --role=role --serviceaccount=ns:sa -n ns.
Rolling Update📝Default Deployment strategy: gradually replace old pods with new ones. Controlled by maxSurge and maxUnavailable. Zero-downtime when combined with readiness probes.

S

TermCKA?Definition
Secret📝Stores sensitive data (base64-encoded, not encrypted by default). Types: Opaque (generic), kubernetes.io/tls (certs), kubernetes.io/dockerconfigjson (registry auth). Protect with RBAC + encryption at rest.
SecurityContext📝Security settings at pod or container level. Controls: runAsUser, runAsNonRoot, readOnlyRootFilesystem, capabilities, allowPrivilegeEscalation. CKA tests pod-level and container-level contexts.
Selector📝Label-based mechanism to identify a set of objects. Used by Services (spec.selector), Deployments (spec.selector.matchLabels), and NetworkPolicies (spec.podSelector).
Service📝Stable network endpoint for a set of pods. Types: ClusterIP (internal), NodePort (external:30000+), LoadBalancer (cloud LB), ExternalName (DNS alias). Selector matches pod labels.
ServiceAccount📝Identity for pods to authenticate to the API server. Every namespace has a default SA. Best practice: create dedicated SA with automountServiceAccountToken: false unless needed.
Sidecar📝Additional container in a pod running alongside the main container. Pattern: log shippers, proxies (Envoy/Istio), config reloaders. Shares volumes and network with main container.
Static Pod📝Pod managed directly by kubelet (not API server). Defined in /etc/kubernetes/manifests/. Used for control plane components: API server, scheduler, controller-manager, etcd. Mirror pod appears in API.
StatefulSet📝Manages stateful applications with stable identity: ordered pod names (db-0, db-1), persistent storage per pod, ordered deployment/scaling. Used for: databases, ZooKeeper, Kafka.
