StorageClass📝Defines types of storage for dynamic provisioning. When a PVC references a StorageClass, K8s auto-creates the PV. Key fields: provisioner, volumeBindingMode, reclaimPolicy.

T

TermCKA?Definition
Taint📝Mark on a node that repels pods unless they have a matching toleration. Effects: NoSchedule, PreferNoSchedule, NoExecute. Control plane has default taint. kubectl taint nodes node key=val:NoSchedule.
Toleration📝Pod spec field allowing scheduling on tainted nodes. Must match taint key, value, and effect. spec.tolerations: [{key: "key", operator: "Equal", value: "val", effect: "NoSchedule"}].

U

TermCKA?Definition
Uncordon📝Make a cordoned node schedulable again. kubectl uncordon <node>. Final step after maintenance. Existing pods won't move back automatically — new pods may be scheduled there.
Upgrade (kubeadm)📝CKA-critical procedure: kubeadm upgrade plan → kubeadm upgrade apply vX.Y.Z on control plane, then upgrade kubelet/kubectl on each node. Always upgrade one minor version at a time (e.g., 1.29→1.30).

V

TermCKA?Definition
Volume📝Storage attached to a pod. Types: emptyDir (temp), hostPath (node fs), persistentVolumeClaim (PVC), configMap, secret, projected. Defined in spec.volumes[], mounted via volumeMounts[].
VolumeMount📝How a volume is mounted inside a container. Specifies: name (matches volume name), mountPath (path in container), optional readOnly and subPath.

W

TermCKA?Definition
WebhookHTTP callback for admission control. MutatingAdmissionWebhook can modify objects; ValidatingAdmissionWebhook can accept/reject. Used by tools like cert-manager, OPA/Gatekeeper.
Worker Node📝Node that runs application workloads (pods). Contains kubelet, kube-proxy, and container runtime. Joined to cluster via kubeadm join. Managed from control plane.

⚠️ Frequently Confused Term Pairs

  • Label vs Annotation — Labels are for selection (selectors use them); Annotations are metadata only
  • Cordon vs Drain — Cordon stops new pods; Drain evicts existing pods too
  • Role vs ClusterRole — Role is namespace-scoped; ClusterRole is cluster-wide
  • PV vs PVC — PV is the actual storage (admin); PVC is the request (developer)
  • Liveness vs Readiness — Liveness restarts container; Readiness removes from service
  • DaemonSet vs Deployment — DaemonSet: one pod per node; Deployment: specified replica count
  • ConfigMap vs Secret — ConfigMap: plain text config; Secret: base64 sensitive data
  • Taint vs Toleration — Taint is on nodes (repels); Toleration is on pods (allows)
  • NodePort vs LoadBalancer — NodePort: nodeIP:port; LoadBalancer: cloud-provisioned external IP

📖 Appendix D — Glossary Quick Stats

  • ✅ 110+ terms across 22 letter sections (A–W) covering all CKA exam domains
  • ✅ 📝 = CKA-relevant — terms you may encounter on the exam
  • ✅ Object hierarchy diagram — Deployment → ReplicaSet → Pod → Container ownership chain
  • ✅ Resource scope reference — cluster-scoped vs namespace-scoped at a glance
  • ✅ Definitions include commands, examples, and cross-references
  • ✅ Confused pairs — 9 most commonly mixed-up term pairs clarified

🐍 Appendix E: Complete anihpj Kubernetes Manifests

APPENDIX 📋 REFERENCE

Production-ready YAML manifests for the complete anihpj Django/jobpost application — E.1 through E.12. Each manifest follows CKA best practices: resource limits, security contexts, labels, and health checks.

anihpj Application — Complete Kubernetes Architecture

                          ┌──────────────────┐
                INTERNET ─│   Ingress (NGINX) │
                          │  anihpj.local     │
                          └────────┬─────────┘
                                   │  :80/:443
                          ┌────────▼─────────┐
                          │  Django Service   │─────── NetworkPolicy ──────┐
                          │  (ClusterIP)      │       (restricts traffic)  │
                          └────────┬─────────┘                             │
                  ┌────────────────┼────────────────┐                      │
                  ▼                ▼                 ▼                      │
          ┌──────────────┐ ┌──────────────┐ ┌──────────────┐               │
          │ Django Pod-0 │ │ Django Pod-1 │ │ Django Pod-N │  ◄── HPA      │
          │ ┌──────────┐ │ │              │ │              │  (2-10 pods)  │
          │ │Container │ │ │  Deployment  │ │  Deployment  │               │
          │ │anihpj:v1 │ │ │  replicas=3  │ │  replicas=3  │               │
          │ ├──────────┤ │ └──────────────┘ └──────────────┘               │
          │ │ConfigMap │ │                                                  │
          │ │ DB_HOST  │ │    ServiceAccount: anihpj-web-sa                │
          │ │ DB_NAME  │ │    PDB: minAvailable=2                          │
          │ ├──────────┤ │                                                  │
          │ │ Secret   │ │                                                  │
          │ │ DB_PASS  │ │                                                  │
          │ └──────────┘ │                                                  │
          └──────┬───────┘                                                  │
                 │                                                          │
          ┌──────▼──────────┐                                               │
          │  PostgreSQL Svc │  ◄────────────────────────────────────────────┘
          │  (ClusterIP)    │
          └──────┬──────────┘
                 │
          ┌──────▼──────────┐
          │ PostgreSQL       │   StatefulSet (replicas=1)
          │ StatefulSet-0    │   volumeClaimTemplates → PVC
          │ ┌──────────────┐ │
          │ │ pgdata volume│ │   StorageClass: anihpj-storage
          │ │ (PVC 5Gi)    │ │   PersistentVolume: 10Gi
          │ └──────────────┘ │
          └──────────────────┘

  ┌─────────── NAMESPACE: anihpj ─────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  RBAC: Role (anihpj-role) + RoleBinding (anihpj-web-sa)          │
  │  CronJob: db-backup (daily at 2 AM)                              │
  │  Job: db-migrate (one-time schema migration)                     │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

How to Use These Manifests: Apply in order (E.1→E.12). Each manifest depends on the previous ones. Or apply all at once: kubectl apply -f anihpj/ (place all YAML in one directory).

E.1 Namespace

Why First

Every other manifest references namespace: anihpj. Create this before anything else.

01-namespace.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: anihpj
  labels:
    app.kubernetes.io/name: anihpj
    app.kubernetes.io/part-of: anihpj-platform
    environment: production
  annotations:
    description: "anihpj Django/jobpost application namespace"
⚡ Imperative equivalent: kubectl create namespace anihpj

E.2 ConfigMap

Purpose

Non-sensitive configuration — database host, port, app settings. Referenced by Django Deployment pods via envFrom.

02-configmap.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: anihpj-config
  namespace: anihpj
  labels:
    app.kubernetes.io/name: anihpj
    app.kubernetes.io/component: config
data:
  # Database connection (non-sensitive)
  DB_HOST: "anihpj-postgres"
  DB_PORT: "5432"
  DB_NAME: "anihpj_db"

  # Django settings
  DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE: "anihpj.settings"
  DJANGO_ALLOWED_HOSTS: "*"
  DJANGO_DEBUG: "False"

  # Application
  APP_NAME: "anihpj-jobpost"
  LOG_LEVEL: "INFO"
⚡ Imperative equivalent: kubectl create configmap anihpj-config --from-literal=DB_HOST=anihpj-postgres --from-literal=DB_PORT=5432 --from-literal=DB_NAME=anihpj_db -n anihpj

E.3 Secret

⚠️ Secrets are base64-encoded, NOT encrypted

  • Anyone with RBAC access to Secrets can decode them: echo <value> | base64 -d
  • Enable encryption at rest in production (EncryptionConfiguration)
  • Use stringData (plain text, auto-encoded) instead of data (pre-encoded) for readability
03-secret.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: anihpj-secrets
  namespace: anihpj
  labels:
    app.kubernetes.io/name: anihpj
    app.kubernetes.io/component: secrets
type: Opaque
stringData:
  # Using stringData — Kubernetes auto-encodes to base64
  DB_PASSWORD: "your-strong-password-here"
  DJANGO_SECRET_KEY: "your-django-secret-key-here"
  POSTGRES_PASSWORD: "your-strong-password-here"
⚡ Imperative equivalent: kubectl create secret generic anihpj-secrets --from-literal=DB_PASSWORD=your-strong-password-here --from-literal=DJANGO_SECRET_KEY=your-django-secret-key-here -n anihpj

E.4 StorageClass & PersistentVolumeClaim

Storage Flow

StorageClass defines how storage is provisioned → PVC requests storage → StatefulSet (E.5) uses the PVC via volumeClaimTemplates.

Storage Binding Flow

  StorageClass                PVC                     StatefulSet
  ┌──────────────┐     ┌──────────────┐     ┌────────────────────┐
  │ anihpj-      │────▶│ pgdata-claim │────▶│ postgres-0 Pod     │
  │ storage      │     │ 5Gi RWO      │     │ mountPath: /data   │
  │              │     │              │     │                    │
  │ provisioner: │     │ storageClass │     │ volumeClaimTemplates│
  │  hostPath    │     │  =anihpj-    │     │  uses this class   │
  │ (dev/test)   │     │   storage    │     │                    │
  └──────────────┘     └──────────────┘     └────────────────────┘
04a-storageclass.yaml 
apiVersion: storage.k8s.io/v1
kind: StorageClass
metadata:
  name: anihpj-storage
  labels:
    app.kubernetes.io/name: anihpj
    app.kubernetes.io/component: storage
provisioner: docker.io/hostpath       # Dev/test only
reclaimPolicy: Retain                 # Keep data after PVC deletion
volumeBindingMode: WaitForFirstConsumer  # Bind when pod is scheduled
allowVolumeExpansion: true
04b-pvc.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: pgdata-claim
  namespace: anihpj
  labels:
    app.kubernetes.io/name: anihpj
    app.kubernetes.io/component: database
spec:
  accessModes:
    - ReadWriteOnce              # Single node read-write
  storageClassName: anihpj-storage
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 5Gi

CKA Tip: For the exam, remember the three access modes: ReadWriteOnce (RWO), ReadOnlyMany (ROX), ReadWriteMany (RWX). Databases almost always use RWO.

E.5 PostgreSQL StatefulSet & Service

Why StatefulSet?

Databases need: stable network identity (anihpj-postgres-0), persistent storage per pod, ordered deployment. A Deployment would lose data on pod reschedule.

05a-postgres-statefulset.yaml 
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: StatefulSet
metadata:
  name: anihpj-postgres
  namespace: anihpj
  labels:
    app.kubernetes.io/name: anihpj
    app.kubernetes.io/component: database
spec:
  serviceName: anihpj-postgres    # Headless service name
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj-postgres
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj-postgres
        app.kubernetes.io/name: anihpj
        app.kubernetes.io/component: database
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: postgres
          image: postgres:15-alpine
          ports:
            - containerPort: 5432
              name: postgres
          env:
            - name: POSTGRES_DB
              valueFrom:
                configMapKeyRef:
                  name: anihpj-config
                  key: DB_NAME
            - name: POSTGRES_PASSWORD
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: anihpj-secrets
                  key: POSTGRES_PASSWORD
            - name: PGDATA
              value: /var/lib/postgresql/data/pgdata
          volumeMounts:
            - name: pgdata
              mountPath: /var/lib/postgresql/data
          resources:
            requests:
              cpu: 250m
              memory: 256Mi
            limits:
              cpu: 500m
              memory: 512Mi
          livenessProbe:
            exec:
              command: ["pg_isready", "-U", "postgres"]
            initialDelaySeconds: 30
            periodSeconds: 10
          readinessProbe:
            exec:
              command: ["pg_isready", "-U", "postgres"]
            initialDelaySeconds: 5
            periodSeconds: 5
  volumeClaimTemplates:
    - metadata:
        name: pgdata
      spec:
        accessModes: ["ReadWriteOnce"]
        storageClassName: anihpj-storage
        resources:
          requests:
            storage: 5Gi
05b-postgres-service.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: anihpj-postgres
  namespace: anihpj
  labels:
    app.kubernetes.io/name: anihpj
    app.kubernetes.io/component: database
spec:
  type: ClusterIP
  clusterIP: None                 # Headless service for StatefulSet
  selector:
    app: anihpj-postgres
  ports:
    - port: 5432
      targetPort: 5432
      name: postgres

⚠️ StatefulSet Gotchas

  • clusterIP: None makes a headless service — required for StatefulSet DNS (anihpj-postgres-0.anihpj-postgres.anihpj.svc.cluster.local)
  • serviceName in StatefulSet must match the headless service name
  • PVCs from volumeClaimTemplates are NOT deleted on StatefulSet deletion — delete manually if needed

E.6 Django Deployment & Service

Core Workload

This is the main anihpj application. Uses Deployment (stateless) with ConfigMap/Secret injection, resource limits, probes, and security context.

06a-django-deployment.yaml 
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj-web
  namespace: anihpj
  labels:
    app.kubernetes.io/name: anihpj
    app.kubernetes.io/component: web
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj-web
  strategy:
    type: RollingUpdate
    rollingUpdate:
      maxSurge: 1
      maxUnavailable: 0           # Zero-downtime deployments
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj-web
        app.kubernetes.io/name: anihpj
        app.kubernetes.io/component: web
    spec:
      serviceAccountName: anihpj-web-sa
      securityContext:
        runAsNonRoot: true
        runAsUser: 1000
        fsGroup: 1000
      containers:
        - name: django
          image: anihpj/jobpost:v1.0.0
          ports:
            - containerPort: 8000
              name: http
          envFrom:
            - configMapRef:
                name: anihpj-config
            - secretRef:
                name: anihpj-secrets
          resources:
            requests:
              cpu: 100m
              memory: 128Mi
            limits:
              cpu: 500m
              memory: 512Mi
          livenessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /health/
              port: 8000
            initialDelaySeconds: 15
            periodSeconds: 20
            failureThreshold: 3
          readinessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /ready/
              port: 8000
            initialDelaySeconds: 5
            periodSeconds: 10
          securityContext:
            allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
            readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
            capabilities:
              drop: ["ALL"]
          volumeMounts:
            - name: tmp
              mountPath: /tmp
      volumes:
        - name: tmp
          emptyDir: {}            # Writable /tmp for Django
06b-django-service.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: anihpj-web
  namespace: anihpj
  labels:
    app.kubernetes.io/name: anihpj
    app.kubernetes.io/component: web
spec:
  type: ClusterIP
  selector:
    app: anihpj-web
  ports:
    - port: 80
      targetPort: 8000
      protocol: TCP
      name: http

CKA Pattern: maxUnavailable: 0 + readiness probe = zero-downtime rolling update. Old pods stay in the Service until new pods pass readiness. This is a common exam scenario.

E.7 Ingress

07-ingress.yaml 
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: anihpj-ingress
  namespace: anihpj
  labels:
    app.kubernetes.io/name: anihpj
    app.kubernetes.io/component: networking
  annotations:
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/rewrite-target: /
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/ssl-redirect: "false"
spec:
  ingressClassName: nginx
  rules:
    - host: anihpj.local
      http:
        paths:
          - path: /
            pathType: Prefix
            backend:
              service:
                name: anihpj-web
                port:
                  number: 80
          - path: /api
            pathType: Prefix
            backend:
              service:
                name: anihpj-web
                port:
                  number: 80

Ingress Traffic Flow

  Client Request                 Ingress Controller          Backend Pods
  ────────────                   ──────────────────          ────────────
  GET anihpj.local/      ──▶   nginx pod (DaemonSet)  ──▶  anihpj-web-xxx
  GET anihpj.local/api   ──▶   matches path rules     ──▶  anihpj-web-yyy
  GET other.local/       ──▶   404 (no matching rule)

⚠️ Ingress Gotchas

  • Ingress resource alone does nothing — you need an Ingress Controller installed (nginx, traefik)
  • ingressClassName replaced the deprecated kubernetes.io/ingress.class annotation in v1.19+
  • pathType: Prefix matches /api and /api/jobs; Exact matches only /api

E.8 NetworkPolicy

⚠️ Prerequisite

NetworkPolicies require a CNI that supports them (Calico, Cilium). Flannel does NOT enforce network policies.

08-networkpolicy.yaml 
# Default deny all ingress in namespace
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: default-deny-ingress
  namespace: anihpj
spec:
  podSelector: {}                 # All pods in namespace
  policyTypes:
    - Ingress
---
# Allow Django to receive traffic from Ingress
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-web-ingress
  namespace: anihpj
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj-web
  policyTypes:
    - Ingress
  ingress:
    - from:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              kubernetes.io/metadata.name: ingress-nginx
      ports:
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 8000
---
# Allow Django → PostgreSQL traffic only
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-postgres-from-web
  namespace: anihpj
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj-postgres
  policyTypes:
    - Ingress
  ingress:
    - from:
        - podSelector:
            matchLabels:
              app: anihpj-web
      ports:
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 5432

Network Policy Effect — What's Allowed & What's Blocked

  ┌── ingress-nginx namespace ───┐     ┌────── anihpj namespace ────────────┐
  │                               │     │                                    │
  │  ┌─────────────────┐         │     │  ┌──────────────┐                  │
  │  │ Ingress         │─── ✅ ──┼────▶│  │ anihpj-web   │                  │
  │  │ Controller      │  :8000  │     │  │ (Django)     │                  │
  │  └─────────────────┘         │     │  └──────┬───────┘                  │
  │                               │     │         │ ✅ :5432                 │
  └───────────────────────────────┘     │         ▼                          │
                                        │  ┌──────────────┐                  │
  ┌── any other namespace ────┐         │  │ anihpj-      │                  │
  │                            │        │  │ postgres     │                  │
  │  ┌─────────────────┐      │        │  └──────────────┘                  │
  │  │ Random Pod      │── ❌ ─┼───X   │                                    │
  │  └─────────────────┘      │        │  ❌ All other ingress denied       │
  └────────────────────────────┘        └────────────────────────────────────┘

E.9 RBAC (Role & RoleBinding)

09a-serviceaccount.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: anihpj-web-sa
  namespace: anihpj
  labels:
    app.kubernetes.io/name: anihpj
    app.kubernetes.io/component: rbac
automountServiceAccountToken: false   # Best practice: opt-in only
09b-role.yaml 
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: anihpj-role
  namespace: anihpj
rules:
  - apiGroups: [""]
    resources: ["configmaps", "secrets"]
    verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
  - apiGroups: [""]
    resources: ["pods"]
    verbs: ["get", "list"]
09c-rolebinding.yaml 
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: anihpj-web-binding
  namespace: anihpj
subjects:
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: anihpj-web-sa
    namespace: anihpj
roleRef:
  kind: Role
  name: anihpj-role
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
⚡ Imperative equivalents: kubectl create sa anihpj-web-sa -n anihpj kubectl create role anihpj-role --verb=get,list,watch --resource=configmaps,secrets -n anihpj kubectl create rolebinding anihpj-web-binding --role=anihpj-role --serviceaccount=anihpj:anihpj-web-sa -n anihpj

E.10 PodDisruptionBudget

Purpose

Guarantees at least 2 Django pods are always running during voluntary disruptions (node drain, cluster upgrades).

10-pdb.yaml 
apiVersion: policy/v1
kind: PodDisruptionBudget
metadata:
  name: anihpj-web-pdb
  namespace: anihpj
  labels:
    app.kubernetes.io/name: anihpj
    app.kubernetes.io/component: availability
spec:
  minAvailable: 2                 # Always keep 2+ pods running
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj-web

CKA Tip: Use minAvailable when you know the minimum OR maxUnavailable when you want flexibility. With 3 replicas and minAvailable: 2, only 1 pod can be disrupted at a time.

E.11 Horizontal Pod Autoscaler

11-hpa.yaml 
apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: anihpj-web-hpa
  namespace: anihpj
  labels:
    app.kubernetes.io/name: anihpj
    app.kubernetes.io/component: autoscaling
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: anihpj-web
  minReplicas: 2
  maxReplicas: 10
  metrics:
    - type: Resource
      resource:
        name: cpu
        target:
          type: Utilization
          averageUtilization: 70
    - type: Resource
      resource:
        name: memory
        target:
          type: Utilization
          averageUtilization: 80
  behavior:
    scaleDown:
      stabilizationWindowSeconds: 300   # Wait 5 min before scaling down
      policies:
        - type: Pods
          value: 1
          periodSeconds: 60             # Remove max 1 pod per minute

HPA Scaling Behavior

  CPU Load     Replicas     Action
  ────────     ────────     ──────
    20%          2          Minimum (idle)
    50%          2          Still within threshold
    70%          3          Scale up triggered (target: 70%)
    90%          5          Aggressive scale up
   100%         10          Maximum reached
    60%         10          Stabilization window (5 min wait)
    40%          9          Scale down: -1 pod/min
    20%          2          Back to minimum
⚡ Imperative equivalent: kubectl autoscale deployment anihpj-web --min=2 --max=10 --cpu-percent=70 -n anihpj

E.12 Jobs & CronJobs

Two Job Types

A one-time Job for database migration, and a CronJob for daily database backups.

12a-db-migrate-job.yaml 
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: Job
metadata:
  name: anihpj-db-migrate
  namespace: anihpj
  labels:
    app.kubernetes.io/name: anihpj
    app.kubernetes.io/component: migration
spec:
  backoffLimit: 3                 # Retry up to 3 times
  activeDeadlineSeconds: 300      # Timeout after 5 minutes
  template:
    spec:
      restartPolicy: Never
      containers:
        - name: migrate
          image: anihpj/jobpost:v1.0.0
          command: ["python", "manage.py", "migrate", "--noinput"]
          envFrom:
            - configMapRef:
                name: anihpj-config
            - secretRef:
                name: anihpj-secrets
          resources:
            requests:
              cpu: 100m
              memory: 128Mi
            limits:
              cpu: 250m
              memory: 256Mi
12b-db-backup-cronjob.yaml 
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: anihpj-db-backup
  namespace: anihpj
  labels:
    app.kubernetes.io/name: anihpj
    app.kubernetes.io/component: backup
spec:
  schedule: "0 2 * * *"          # Daily at 2:00 AM
  concurrencyPolicy: Forbid       # Don't overlap backup runs
  successfulJobsHistoryLimit: 3
  failedJobsHistoryLimit: 1
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      backoffLimit: 2
      template:
        spec:
          restartPolicy: OnFailure
          containers:
            - name: backup
              image: postgres:15-alpine
              command:
                - /bin/sh
                - -c
                - |
                  pg_dump -h $DB_HOST -U postgres -d $DB_NAME \
                    -Fc -f /backup/anihpj-$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M%S).dump
              envFrom:
                - configMapRef:
                    name: anihpj-config
              env:
                - name: PGPASSWORD
                  valueFrom:
                    secretKeyRef:
                      name: anihpj-secrets
                      key: POSTGRES_PASSWORD
              resources:
                requests:
                  cpu: 100m
                  memory: 128Mi
                limits:
                  cpu: 250m
                  memory: 256Mi

🐍 Appendix E — Manifest Deployment Order

  • Step 1: kubectl apply -f 01-namespace.yaml — Create namespace first
  • Step 2: kubectl apply -f 02-configmap.yaml -f 03-secret.yaml — Config & secrets
  • Step 3: kubectl apply -f 04a-storageclass.yaml -f 04b-pvc.yaml — Storage
  • Step 4: kubectl apply -f 05a-postgres-statefulset.yaml -f 05b-postgres-service.yaml — Database
  • Step 5: kubectl apply -f 12a-db-migrate-job.yaml — Run migrations
  • Step 6: kubectl apply -f 06a-django-deployment.yaml -f 06b-django-service.yaml — App
  • Step 7: kubectl apply -f 07-ingress.yaml — External access
  • Step 8: Apply remaining: NetworkPolicy, RBAC, PDB, HPA, CronJob
  • ✅ All at once: kubectl apply -f anihpj/ --recursive
✅ Post-Deployment Verification Checklist

After applying all manifests, run these commands to verify everything is healthy:

verify-deployment.sh 
# 1. Check all pods are Running
kubectl get pods -n anihpj -o wide

# 2. Verify Services have endpoints
kubectl get svc -n anihpj
kubectl get endpoints -n anihpj

# 3. Check PVC is Bound
kubectl get pvc -n anihpj

# 4. Verify Ingress has an address
kubectl get ingress -n anihpj

# 5. Check HPA is reading metrics
kubectl get hpa -n anihpj

# 6. Verify NetworkPolicy is applied
kubectl get networkpolicy -n anihpj

# 7. Test DNS resolution
kubectl run tmp --rm -it --image=busybox -n anihpj --restart=Never \
  -- nslookup anihpj-django-service

# 8. Test application endpoint
kubectl run tmp --rm -it --image=curlimages/curl -n anihpj --restart=Never \
  -- curl -s http://anihpj-django-service:8000/health/

# 9. Check RBAC is working
kubectl auth can-i get pods -n anihpj --as=system:serviceaccount:anihpj:anihpj-web-sa

# 10. View recent events for issues
kubectl get events -n anihpj --sort-by=.lastTimestamp | tail -20

Pod Not Running? — Troubleshooting Decision Flow

  Pod Status?
  │
  ├── Pending ──────────► kubectl describe pod <name> -n anihpj
  │   │                   Look at Events section:
  │   ├── "Insufficient cpu/memory" ──► Increase node resources or reduce requests
  │   ├── "no nodes match selector"  ──► Check nodeSelector/affinity/taints
  │   └── "PVC not bound"           ──► Check StorageClass exists, PV available
  │
  ├── CrashLoopBackOff ─► kubectl logs <pod> -n anihpj --previous
  │   │                   (--previous shows logs from crashed container)
  │   ├── "Connection refused DB" ──► PostgreSQL not ready yet (check ordering)
  │   ├── "Permission denied"     ──► SecurityContext too restrictive
  │   └── "Module not found"      ──► Wrong image or missing dependencies
  │
  ├── ImagePullBackOff ──► kubectl describe pod <name> -n anihpj
  │   ├── "repository not found"  ──► Wrong image name/tag
  │   └── "unauthorized"          ──► Missing imagePullSecrets
  │
  └── Running but ──────► kubectl logs <pod> -n anihpj -f
      not working         kubectl exec -it <pod> -n anihpj -- /bin/sh
                          Check: env vars, mounted configs, network connectivity

⚠️ Common Manifest Mistakes on CKA

  • Wrong namespace — Forgetting namespace: anihpj in metadata, so object lands in default
  • Label mismatch — Service selector doesn't match Deployment's template.metadata.labels → 0 endpoints
  • Port mismatch — Service targetPort doesn't match container containerPort → connection refused
  • Secret key typo — secretKeyRef.key doesn't match actual Secret data key → pod fails to start
  • Missing resources — No resources.requests means scheduler can't account for pod needs → OOM kills
  • PVC accessMode — Using ReadWriteMany with a StorageClass that only supports ReadWriteOnce → PVC stays Pending

🎓 Appendix F: CKA Exam Preparation Guide

APPENDIX 📋 REFERENCE

Exam format, domain weights, must-know tips, time management, practice labs, exam day checklist. Everything you need to pass the CKA on your first attempt.

F.1 Exam Format and Rules

CKA Exam Environment Overview

  ┌─────────────────────────── CKA EXAM ───────────────────────────────┐
  │                                                                     │
  │   Duration:    2 hours                                              │
  │   Questions:   15–20 performance-based tasks                        │
  │   Pass Score:  66%                                                  │
  │   Format:      Hands-on (real clusters, real kubectl)               │
  │   Retake:      1 free retake included                               │
  │   Validity:    2 years (was 3 years before 2023)                    │
  │   Proctored:   Online via PSI Secure Browser                        │
  │                                                                     │
  │   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐       │
  │   │  YOUR SCREEN DURING EXAM                                │       │
  │   │  ┌──────────────────┐  ┌────────────────────────────┐   │       │
  │   │  │ Question Panel   │  │ Terminal                    │   │       │
  │   │  │                  │  │ $ kubectl ...               │   │       │
  │   │  │ Task description │  │                             │   │       │
  │   │  │ Requirements     │  │ One or more pre-configured  │   │       │
  │   │  │ Context switch   │  │ cluster contexts available  │   │       │
  │   │  │                  │  │                             │   │       │
  │   │  └──────────────────┘  └────────────────────────────┘   │       │
  │   │  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │       │
  │   │  │ Built-in Browser Tab (allowed docs only)         │   │       │
  │   │  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │       │
  │   └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘       │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
AspectDetails
Kubernetes VersionLatest stable (currently v1.31 — always check CNCF site before exam)
Number of ClustersMultiple pre-configured clusters. Each question specifies which context to use: kubectl config use-context <name>
Root AccessYou get sudo on nodes when needed (e.g., for static pods, kubelet troubleshooting)
ClipboardCopy/paste works within the exam terminal. Use Ctrl+Shift+C/V in the terminal
NotepadBuilt-in notepad provided — use it to track flagged questions
IDE/Editorvim, nano, and emacs available. Set export EDITOR=vim if needed

✅ Allowed Documentation During Exam

You may open ONE extra browser tab with these domains only:

  • https://kubernetes.io/docs/ — Official documentation
  • https://kubernetes.io/blog/ — Official blog
  • https://helm.sh/docs/ — Helm documentation (since CKA 2024)

NOT allowed: GitHub, Stack Overflow, any other website, personal notes, second monitor.

F.2 CKA Exam Domain Weights

CKA Domain Weight Distribution (2024/2025)

  Domain                               Weight    Visual
  ─────────────────────────────────    ──────    ──────────────────────────────
  🔧 Troubleshooting                    30%     ████████████████████████████████
  🏗️ Cluster Architecture & Setup       25%     ██████████████████████████
  🌐 Services & Networking              20%     █████████████████████
  📦 Workloads & Scheduling             15%     ████████████████
  💾 Storage                             10%     ███████████

                              Pass threshold: 66% ─────────▶ ┃
  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────┃──────────┐
  0%                   33%                  66%   ┃      100%
  ■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■□□□□□□□□□□□□
  ◀── FAIL zone ──▶                   ◀── PASS ─▶
%DomainKey Topics to MasterChapters
30% 🔧 Troubleshooting Application failure, pod debugging, cluster component failure, node issues, networking issues. Highest weight — practice logs, events, describe. Ch 27–31
25% 🏗️ Cluster Architecture RBAC, kubeadm upgrades, etcd backup/restore, HA setup, node management (cordon/drain/uncordon). Ch 1–6, 22–26
20% 🌐 Services & Networking Service types (ClusterIP, NodePort, LB), Ingress, NetworkPolicy, DNS resolution, CNI. Ch 16–21
15% 📦 Workloads & Scheduling Deployments, DaemonSets, resource limits, scheduling (nodeSelector, affinity, taints), scaling. Ch 7–12
10% 💾 Storage PV/PVC, StorageClass, volume types, accessModes, dynamic provisioning. Ch 13–15

Strategy: Troubleshooting (30%) + Cluster Architecture (25%) = 55% of the exam. Master these two domains first — they alone can nearly pass you.

F.3 Tips, Tricks & Time Management

⏱️ Time Management Strategy (120 Minutes)

With 15–20 questions in 2 hours, you have ~6–8 minutes per question. Not all questions are equal weight.

Recommended Time Allocation

  ┌────── FIRST PASS (90 min) ──────┐  ┌──── SECOND PASS (25 min) ────┐  ┌─ REVIEW ─┐
  │                                  │  │                               │  │  (5 min)  │
  │  Answer ALL questions you can    │  │  Return to flagged/skipped    │  │  Verify   │
  │  do quickly (<5 min each)       │  │  questions                    │  │  contexts │
  │                                  │  │                               │  │  Check    │
  │  FLAG and SKIP hard ones         │  │  Spend remaining time on      │  │  answers  │
  │  (come back in pass 2)           │  │  highest-weight questions     │  │           │
  │                                  │  │                               │  │           │
  └──────────────────────────────────┘  └───────────────────────────────┘  └───────────┘
   0 min                         90 min                             115 min          120 min
⚡ First Thing — Set Up Your Aliases

Paste these at the exam terminal start. They save hundreds of keystrokes.

~/.bashrc exam setup 
# Paste at start of exam (takes 30 seconds, saves 10+ minutes)
alias k=kubectl
alias kgp='kubectl get pods'
alias kgs='kubectl get svc'
alias kgn='kubectl get nodes'
alias kaf='kubectl apply -f'
alias kdel='kubectl delete'
alias kd='kubectl describe'
alias kl='kubectl logs'
alias kx='kubectl exec -it'

# Auto-complete
source <(kubectl completion bash)
complete -o default -F __start_kubectl k

# Default editor
export EDITOR=vim
export KUBE_EDITOR=vim

# Quick dry-run shortcut
export do="--dry-run=client -o yaml"

# Usage: k run nginx --image=nginx $do > pod.yaml
#TipDetails
1Read context switchEvery question starts with kubectl config use-context <name>. DO NOT SKIP THIS — wrong context = wrong cluster = zero points.
2Use imperative commandskubectl create deploy, kubectl run, kubectl expose are 5x faster than writing YAML from scratch.
3Generate YAML skeletonskubectl run nginx --image=nginx --dry-run=client -o yaml > pod.yaml — then edit the YAML.
4Use kubectl explainkubectl explain pod.spec.containers.resources — built-in docs when you forget field names.
5Bookmark docs pagesBefore exam, bookmark: cheat sheet, kubectl reference, API reference, tasks page.
6Don't get stuckIf a 4% question takes >10 min, flag it and move on. An 8% question waiting is worth more.
7Verify your workAfter creating a resource: kubectl get, kubectl describe, check events. Confirm it's in the right namespace.
8Know vim basicsi (insert), Esc (normal), :wq (save+quit), dd (delete line), /search (find), :set number (line numbers).

⚠️ Common Exam Mistakes

  • Wrong namespace: Always check -n <namespace> — many questions use non-default namespaces
  • Wrong context: Run the context switch command at the start of EVERY question
  • YAML indentation: Spaces only (no tabs). A single wrong indent breaks everything
  • Forgetting to save: In vim, :wq — many have lost work by exiting without saving
  • Over-engineering: The exam wants minimal correct answers, not production-grade YAML

🔖 Must-Bookmark kubernetes.io Pages (Before Exam Day)

Open these before the exam starts and bookmark them in your allowed browser tab:

  • kubectl Cheat Sheet — kubernetes.io/docs/reference/kubectl/cheatsheet/
  • kubectl Reference — kubernetes.io/docs/reference/generated/kubectl/kubectl-commands
  • API Reference (v1.31) — kubernetes.io/docs/reference/kubernetes-api/
  • Tasks Index — kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/ (copy-paste YAML snippets)
  • kubeadm upgrade — kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/administer-cluster/kubeadm/kubeadm-upgrade/
  • etcd backup/restore — kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/administer-cluster/configure-upgrade-etcd/
  • NetworkPolicy — kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/services-networking/network-policies/
  • RBAC — kubernetes.io/docs/reference/access-authn-authz/rbac/
📝 Vim Survival Guide for the CKA Exam

You will use vim on the exam. Master these 15 commands:

KeyActionWhen to Use
iEnter insert modeStart typing/editing text
EscReturn to normal modeStop editing, run commands
:wqSave and quitDone editing — save file
:q!Quit without savingMessed up — discard all changes
ddDelete entire lineRemove unwanted YAML lines
5ddDelete 5 linesRemove a block of YAML
yy / pCopy line / Paste belowDuplicate a YAML block
uUndo last changeRevert a mistake
/textSearch forwardFind a field in large YAML
nNext search matchJump to next occurrence
GGo to end of fileJump to bottom of file
ggGo to start of fileJump to top of file
:set numberShow line numbersDebug YAML indentation errors
:set pastePaste mode (no auto-indent)Before pasting YAML from docs
Shift+V → select → dVisual line deleteSelect and delete multiple lines
⚡ Essential kubectl Patterns You MUST Know

These patterns cover ~80% of CKA tasks. Practice until they're muscle memory.

essential-exam-patterns.sh 
# Generate YAML skeleton (most-used pattern)
k run nginx --image=nginx $do > pod.yaml
k create deploy web --image=nginx --replicas=3 $do > deploy.yaml
k expose deploy web --port=80 --target-port=8080 $do > svc.yaml

# Context switching (FIRST thing every question)
k config use-context <context-name>
k config get-contexts    # verify current

# Quick troubleshooting chain
k get pods -A            # all namespaces overview
k describe pod <name>   # events + conditions
k logs <pod> -c <ctr>   # container logs
k logs <pod> --previous  # crashed container logs
k exec -it <pod> -- sh  # shell into container

# RBAC creation chain
k create sa mysa -n <ns>
k create role myrole --verb=get,list --resource=pods -n <ns>
k create rolebinding myrb --role=myrole --serviceaccount=<ns>:mysa -n <ns>
k auth can-i get pods --as=system:serviceaccount:<ns>:mysa -n <ns>

# Node maintenance
k cordon <node>
k drain <node> --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data
k uncordon <node>

# etcd backup (know this exact command)
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot save /tmp/backup.db \
  --endpoints=https://127.0.0.1:2379 \
  --cacert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt \
  --cert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt \
  --key=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key

F.4 Practice Lab Recommendations

PlatformCostCKA Sim?Notes
killer.shFree (2 sessions with exam purchase)⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐Most realistic. Included with CKA exam purchase. Harder than real exam. Use both sessions in final week.
KodeKloud CKA~$15-25/mo⭐⭐⭐⭐Structured course with hands-on labs. Great for beginners. Mock exams included. Lightning labs for speed practice.
KillercodaFree⭐⭐⭐Free browser-based K8s scenarios. Good for daily 15-min practice. By the makers of killer.sh.
Kubernetes the Hard WayFree⭐⭐⭐Kelsey Hightower's manual cluster setup. Deepens understanding but NOT exam format. Do once for learning.
Play with KubernetesFree⭐⭐4-hour free browser clusters. Good for quick experiments when you don't have local setup.
Local (kubeadm/kind/minikube)Free⭐⭐⭐Practice kubeadm installs, upgrades, etcd backup locally. Closest to exam for cluster-admin tasks.

Recommended Study Path: KodeKloud (learn concepts + labs) → Killercoda (daily practice) → killer.sh (final 2 sessions before exam). Practice until you can complete killer.sh with 20+ minutes to spare.

F.5 Exam Day Checklist

Exam Day Timeline

  ─── BEFORE EXAM (Day Before) ───────────────────────────────────────────

  ☐  Test PSI Secure Browser (download + system check)
  ☐  Verify webcam + microphone work
  ☐  Prepare government-issued photo ID
  ☐  Clear desk — nothing except computer, monitor, keyboard, mouse
  ☐  Close all applications, disconnect extra monitors
  ☐  Review your bookmarked kubernetes.io pages

  ─── EXAM DAY (30 min before) ──────────────────────────────────────────

  ☐  Close ALL apps except PSI Secure Browser
  ☐  Clear browser cache
  ☐  Ensure stable internet (wired recommended)
  ☐  Check-in opens 15 min before scheduled time
  ☐  Show room to proctor via webcam (360° sweep)
  ☐  Show desk surface and under desk

  ─── DURING EXAM (first 2 minutes) ────────────────────────────────────

  ☐  Set up aliases (paste your prepared .bashrc snippet)
  ☐  Test: k get nodes (verify cluster access)
  ☐  Skim all questions — identify easy wins
  ☐  Start with highest-confidence questions first

  ─── DURING EXAM (ongoing) ────────────────────────────────────────────

  ☐  READ context switch command for EVERY question
  ☐  Flag difficult questions, move on after 8 min
  ☐  Verify work: kubectl get / describe after each task
  ☐  Use notepad to track flagged question numbers

  ─── LAST 10 MINUTES ──────────────────────────────────────────────────

  ☐  Return to flagged questions (highest weight first)
  ☐  Verify all resources are in correct namespaces
  ☐  Don't make last-minute changes without checking

⚠️ Environment Requirements

  • Clean room: No papers, books, phones, smartwatches, or second screens
  • Alone: No other people in the room during the exam
  • No headphones/earbuds — even noise-canceling
  • Browser: Only PSI Secure Browser + one kubernetes.io tab allowed
  • Talking: Do not read questions aloud or mumble — proctor may flag it
Post-Exam

Results arrive within 24 hours via email. If you don't pass, you get one free retake (must use within 12 months). Review weak areas using the domain breakdown in your score report.

🎓 Appendix F — Exam Prep Quick Reference

  • ✅ Format: 2 hours, 15-20 tasks, 66% to pass, 1 free retake
  • ✅ Top domains: Troubleshooting (30%) + Cluster Architecture (25%) = 55%
  • ✅ Must-do first: Set up aliases + completion (saves 10+ min)
  • ✅ 8 must-bookmark kubernetes.io pages for instant reference
  • ✅ 15 vim commands — the only ones you need on exam day
  • ✅ Essential kubectl patterns covering ~80% of exam tasks
  • ✅ Best practice platform: killer.sh (2 free sessions with exam)
  • ✅ Key rule: Read context switch EVERY question, verify EVERY answer

📅 Appendix G: 10-Week CKA Learning Path

APPENDIX 📋 REFERENCE

Week-by-week study roadmap from complete beginner to CKA-certified administrator. Designed for 8–12 hours of study per week alongside a full-time job.

10-Week CKA Learning Roadmap

  WEEK   PHASE            DIFFICULTY    FOCUS AREA
  ─────  ───────────────  ──────────    ─────────────────────────────────
   1     🟢 Foundation    ★☆☆☆☆        Docker basics, K8s concepts, setup
   2     🟢 Foundation    ★★☆☆☆        kubectl mastery, pods, namespaces

   3     🟡 Intermediate  ★★☆☆☆        Deployments, ReplicaSets, Services
   4     🟡 Intermediate  ★★★☆☆        ConfigMaps, Secrets, Volumes, PV/PVC
   5     🟡 Intermediate  ★★★☆☆        Scheduling: taints, affinity, DaemonSets
   6     🟡 Intermediate  ★★★☆☆        Networking: Services, Ingress, DNS, NetworkPolicy

   7     🟠 Advanced      ★★★★☆        RBAC, Security Contexts, ServiceAccounts
   8     🟠 Advanced      ★★★★☆        Cluster admin: kubeadm, upgrades, etcd backup

   9     🔴 Expert        ★★★★★        Troubleshooting deep-dive (30% of exam!)

  10     🎯 EXAM READY    ★★★★★        Mock exams, killer.sh, review weak areas

                    ──────── TAKE THE CKA EXAM ────────
                    66% to pass │ 1 free retake included

🟢 Week 1 — Foundation: Containers & Kubernetes Concepts

DayTopicChaptersHands-On
Day 1–2Docker fundamentals: images, containers, DockerfilesCh 1Build anihpj Docker image
Day 3–4Kubernetes architecture: control plane, worker nodes, etcdCh 2–3Install minikube or kind
Day 5–6kubectl basics: get, describe, create, delete, applyCh 4Deploy first pod, explore cluster
Day 7Review + Lab: Deploy a multi-container podCh 1–4Killercoda beginner scenarios

Goal: Comfortable running kubectl commands, understand pods, can explain K8s architecture on a whiteboard.

🟢 Week 2 — Foundation: kubectl Mastery & Core Objects

DayTopicChaptersHands-On
Day 1–2Namespaces, labels, selectors, annotationsCh 5Organize resources by namespace
Day 3–4Pod lifecycle, restart policies, init containersCh 6Create pods with init containers
Day 5–6Imperative vs declarative, dry-run, YAML generationCh 4, App BGenerate all resource YAML imperatively
Day 7Speed drill: Create 10 different resources in 15 minutesReviewTime yourself with App B commands

Goal: Can create any basic resource imperatively in under 30 seconds. Comfortable editing YAML.

🟡 Week 3 — Workloads: Deployments, Scaling & Updates

DayTopicChaptersHands-On
Day 1–2Deployments, ReplicaSets, rolling updates, rollbacksCh 7–8Deploy anihpj, perform rolling update
Day 3–4Services: ClusterIP, NodePort, LoadBalancer, ExternalNameCh 16Expose anihpj via each service type
Day 5–6Probes: liveness, readiness, startupCh 9Add health checks to anihpj deployment
Day 7Lab: Rolling update with zero downtime using probesCh 7–9, 16Full deployment cycle end-to-end

Goal: Can deploy, scale, update, and rollback, with services and health checks. Covers 15% of exam (Workloads).

🟡 Week 4 — Configuration & Storage

DayTopicChaptersHands-On
Day 1–2ConfigMaps and Secrets: creation, injection, mountingCh 10–11Inject DB config into anihpj pods
Day 3–4Volumes: emptyDir, hostPath, PV, PVC, StorageClassCh 13–14Set up PostgreSQL with persistent storage
Day 5–6Resource requests/limits, LimitRange, ResourceQuotaCh 12Configure resource constraints for anihpj namespace
Day 7Lab: Complete anihpj storage setup (E.1–E.5)App EApply manifests E.1 through E.5

Goal: Storage (10% of exam) mastered. Can create PV/PVC, mount ConfigMaps, manage Secrets.

🟡 Week 5 — Scheduling & Workload Controllers

DayTopicChaptersHands-On
Day 1–2nodeSelector, node affinity, pod affinity/anti-affinityCh 15Schedule pods to specific nodes
Day 3–4Taints & tolerations, DaemonSets, static podsCh 15Create DaemonSet, taint/untaint nodes
Day 5–6Jobs, CronJobs, HPA (Horizontal Pod Autoscaler)Ch 8, 12Run backup CronJob, set up HPA for anihpj
Day 7Lab: Multi-constraint scheduling scenarioCh 15KodeKloud scheduling labs

Goal: Scheduling domain complete. Can control pod placement with any constraint type.

🟡 Week 6 — Networking Deep Dive

DayTopicChaptersHands-On
Day 1–2Kubernetes networking model, CNI, pod-to-pod communicationCh 17Trace network traffic between pods
Day 3–4Ingress: controllers, rules, TLS terminationCh 18Install nginx ingress, route to anihpj
Day 5–6NetworkPolicies: ingress/egress rules, default denyCh 19Implement anihpj network policies (E.8)
Day 7Lab: DNS debugging + NetworkPolicy scenariosCh 17–19CoreDNS troubleshooting, nslookup tests

Goal: Networking (20% of exam) mastered. Can create Ingress, NetworkPolicies, debug DNS.

🟠 Week 7 — Security: RBAC & Pod Security

DayTopicChaptersHands-On
Day 1–2RBAC: Roles, ClusterRoles, RoleBindings, ClusterRoleBindingsCh 22Create least-privilege RBAC for anihpj SA
Day 3–4ServiceAccounts, token mounting, user certificates (CSR)Ch 23Create user cert, approve CSR, test access
Day 5–6SecurityContexts, Pod Security Standards, admissionCh 24Enforce restricted PSS on anihpj namespace
Day 7Lab: Full RBAC scenario + security auditCh 22–24KodeKloud security labs

Goal: Can create RBAC policies, manage SAs, enforce pod security. Critical for Cluster Architecture domain (25%).

🟠 Week 8 — Cluster Administration

DayTopicChaptersHands-On
Day 1–2kubeadm: cluster init, token management, node joinCh 25Build a 3-node cluster from scratch with kubeadm
Day 3–4Cluster upgrades: control plane + worker node upgradeCh 26Upgrade cluster from v1.30 → v1.31
Day 5–6etcd backup & restore, node maintenance (cordon/drain/uncordon)Ch 26Practice etcd snapshot + restore cycle
Day 7Lab: Full cluster lifecycle: build → upgrade → backup → restoreCh 25–26End-to-end cluster admin practice

Goal: Cluster Architecture (25%) mastered. Can init, upgrade, backup, and restore clusters.

🔴 Week 9 — Troubleshooting (30% of Exam!)

DayTopicChaptersHands-On
Day 1–2Application failures: CrashLoopBackOff, ImagePullBackOff, OOMKilledCh 27–28Debug broken deployments (logs, events, describe)
Day 3–4Cluster component failures: API server, scheduler, etcd, kubeletCh 29Break and fix control plane components
Day 5–6Networking issues: Service not routing, DNS failure, NetworkPolicy blockingCh 30–31Debug service connectivity, trace traffic path
Day 7Lab: "Fix 10 broken clusters" challengeCh 27–31KodeKloud troubleshooting labs

Goal: Troubleshooting (30%!) mastered. This is the HIGHEST-WEIGHT domain. Can diagnose and fix any cluster issue systematically.

🎯 Week 10 — Mock Exams & Final Review

DayTopicResourceTarget
Day 1killer.sh Session 1 — Full mock examkiller.shComplete in <2 hours, score 70%+
Day 2Review killer.sh mistakes, study weak areasNotesZero knowledge gaps
Day 3–4Speed drills: imperative commands, YAML generationApp B, CAll common tasks in <2 min each
Day 5killer.sh Session 2 — Second mock examkiller.shScore 80%+ with time to spare
Day 6Light review: aliases, bookmarks, exam checklist (App F)App FFeel confident and prepared
Day 7🎯 TAKE THE CKA EXAMPSI Secure BrowserPass with 66%+!

Goal: Walk into the exam confident. You've built clusters, deployed apps, debugged failures, and aced mock exams.

Cumulative Exam Coverage by Week

  Week     Domains Covered                              Cumulative %
  ─────    ─────────────────────────────────            ─────────────
   1-2     Foundation (no exam domain yet)                     0%
     3     + Workloads & Scheduling (partial)                 10%
     4     + Storage (complete!)                               20%
     5     + Workloads & Scheduling (complete!)               25%
     6     + Services & Networking (complete!)                45%
     7     + Cluster Architecture (partial: RBAC)             55%
     8     + Cluster Architecture (complete!)                  70%  ◀── PASS LINE
     9     + Troubleshooting (complete!)                      100%
    10     Mock exams + polish                                100%

  ████████████████████████████████████████████████████████████████████
  0%              25%              50%            ▲75%           100%
                                                 │
                                           66% pass line

Pacing Tip: If you can only study 5 hrs/week instead of 10, extend to a 15-week plan — but don't rush Weeks 8-9 (Cluster Admin + Troubleshooting = 55% of the exam).

🏁 Milestone Checkpoints — "Am I On Track?"

At each milestone, you should be able to complete every item without looking at notes. If not, review before moving forward.

AfterYou Should Be Able To...Self-Test Command
Week 2Create any pod/deployment imperatively in <30 seck run test --image=nginx --port=80 $do
Week 4Create PV+PVC+Pod with mounted volume from memoryk explain pvc.spec → write YAML without docs
Week 6Write a NetworkPolicy that allows only specific ingressk create -f netpol.yaml → test with curl
Week 8Upgrade a cluster and backup/restore etcd blindfoldedFull kubeadm upgrade + etcdctl snapshot cycle
Week 9Diagnose a broken pod in <2 minutesk get/describe/logs → identify root cause → fix
Week 10Score 80%+ on killer.sh with 20+ min remainingComplete all killer.sh tasks under exam conditions

⚠️ Falling Behind? — Recovery Strategies

  • 1 week behind: Skip the review lab days — focus on core content only, do labs on weekends
  • 2+ weeks behind: Extend to a 12-week plan, but never skip Weeks 8 (Cluster Admin) or 9 (Troubleshooting)
  • Struggling with a topic: Watch a KodeKloud video on it + do 3 Killercoda scenarios. Don't spend more than 2 extra days per topic
  • Not confident at Week 10: Postpone the exam (you can reschedule). It's better to take an extra 2 weeks than to burn your free retake

🎯 CKA Readiness Self-Assessment (Score Yourself)

Rate each skill 0-3: 0=Can't do it, 1=Need docs, 2=Can do it slowly, 3=Muscle memory. Target: 45+ out of 60 (75%)

DomainWeightSkillScore /3
🔧 Troubleshoot30%Debug CrashLoopBackOff / ImagePullBackOff pods___
Fix broken control plane (API server, scheduler, etcd)___
Debug Service connectivity (no endpoints, wrong selector)___
Read and interpret kubectl logs / events / describe___
🏗️ Cluster25%Create RBAC (Role, RoleBinding, ClusterRole) from scratch___
Upgrade cluster with kubeadm (control plane + workers)___
Backup and restore etcd snapshot___
Cordon, drain, uncordon nodes for maintenance___
🌐 Network20%Create Services (ClusterIP, NodePort) and Ingress___
Write NetworkPolicy with specific ingress/egress rules___
Debug DNS with nslookup / dig from a pod___
Understand CoreDNS FQDN format for cross-namespace access___
📦 Workloads15%Create Deployments, scale, rolling update, rollback___
Schedule pods with nodeSelector, affinity, taints/tolerations___
Create DaemonSets, Jobs, CronJobs___
Configure resource requests/limits, LimitRange, ResourceQuota___
💾 Storage10%Create PV + PVC + Pod that mounts the volume___
Use StorageClass for dynamic provisioning___
Mount ConfigMap and Secret as volumes___
Understand accessModes (RWO, ROX, RWX) and reclaimPolicy___
📊 How to Interpret Your Score

50-60: You're ready — schedule the exam with confidence.
40-49: Almost there — spend 1 more week on weak areas, then take it.
30-39: Need more practice — focus on the domain(s) where you scored lowest.
<30: Keep studying — you'll get there, but don't rush the exam.

📅 Appendix G — Learning Path Summary

  • ✅ Weeks 1-2: Foundation — Docker, architecture, kubectl basics (green)
  • ✅ Weeks 3-6: Core domains — Workloads, Storage, Networking (yellow)
  • ✅ Weeks 7-8: Advanced — RBAC, Security, Cluster Admin (orange)
  • ✅ Week 9: Troubleshooting — highest exam weight at 30% (red)
  • ✅ Week 10: Mock exams + CKA exam day! (purple)
  • ✅ 6 milestone checkpoints with self-test commands to verify progress
  • ✅ 20-skill readiness self-assessment mapped to exam domain weights
  • ✅ Total effort: ~80-120 hours over 10 weeks

🎯 Appendix H: CKA Exam Simulator — 20 Performance-Based Tasks

APPENDIX 📝 EXAM SIMULATOR

Simulate the real CKA exam: 20 performance-based tasks, weighted by domain, using the anihpj project. Each task matches the difficulty, format, and time pressure of the actual exam. Target: complete all 20 in under 120 minutes. Answers use <details> — try each task before revealing the solution.

CKA Exam Simulator — Domain Distribution

  Domain                                    Weight    Tasks    Questions
  ──────────────────────────────────────    ──────    ─────    ─────────
  🔧 Cluster Architecture, Install & Config  25%      5       H.1-H.5
  📦 Workloads & Scheduling                  15%      3       H.6-H.8
  🌐 Services & Networking                   20%      4       H.9-H.12
  💾 Storage                                  10%      2       H.13-H.14
  🔍 Troubleshooting                          30%      6       H.15-H.20

  Total: 20 tasks | Time: 120 minutes | Pass: 66% (≥14 correct)

  ⏱️ Suggested Pace:
  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │ Easy (2-3 min each)  │ Medium (5-7 min)  │ Hard (8-10 min)    │
  │ H.1, H.6, H.9, H.13 │ H.2-H.5, H.7-H.8 │ H.15-H.20          │
  │        ~12 min       │ H.10-H.12, H.14   │ (troubleshooting)  │
  │                      │     ~56 min        │     ~52 min        │
  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Before You Start: Set up your aliases first (just like the real exam): alias k=kubectl, export do="--dry-run=client -o yaml", export now="--force --grace-period 0". Then set your context: kubectl config use-context k8s.

🔧 Domain 1: Cluster Architecture, Installation & Configuration (25%)

Tasks H.1 through H.5 — RBAC, kubeadm upgrades, etcd backup/restore, cluster components.

H.1 RBAC — Create a Scoped ServiceAccount [5 min]

📋 Task

Create a ServiceAccount deploy-sa in namespace anihpj. Create a Role named deploy-role that allows get, list, watch, create, update on deployments in the apps API group. Bind the Role to the ServiceAccount using a RoleBinding named deploy-binding.

💡 Hints
Approach

Use kubectl create imperatively for speed. Remember: --resource=deployments needs --verb= and the API group is apps (not empty string).

✅ Solution
solution-h1.sh 
# Step 1: Create the ServiceAccount
kubectl create sa deploy-sa -n anihpj

# Step 2: Create the Role (note --resource and API group)
kubectl create role deploy-role \
  --verb=get,list,watch,create,update \
  --resource=deployments.apps \
  -n anihpj

# Step 3: Create the RoleBinding
kubectl create rolebinding deploy-binding \
  --role=deploy-role \
  --serviceaccount=anihpj:deploy-sa \
  -n anihpj

# Step 4: Verify
kubectl auth can-i create deployments --as=system:serviceaccount:anihpj:deploy-sa -n anihpj
# Expected: yes

kubectl auth can-i delete deployments --as=system:serviceaccount:anihpj:deploy-sa -n anihpj
# Expected: no (delete was not granted)

⚠️ Common Mistakes

  • Forgetting the .apps API group suffix — --resource=deployments alone targets the wrong API group
  • Using --serviceaccount=deploy-sa without the namespace prefix — must be anihpj:deploy-sa
  • Creating a ClusterRole instead of Role — the task says namespace-scoped

H.2 etcd Backup [7 min]

📋 Task

Create a snapshot of the etcd database and save it to /opt/etcd-backup/snapshot.db. The etcd server is running on the control plane node with the following endpoints and certificates:

etcd connection details 
# Endpoint
--endpoints=https://127.0.0.1:2379

# Certificates (find these in /etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/)
--cacert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt
--cert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt
--key=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key
💡 Hints
Approach

Use etcdctl snapshot save. Set ETCDCTL_API=3 first. If you forget the cert paths, check the etcd static pod manifest: cat /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml.

✅ Solution
solution-h2.sh 
# Ensure etcdctl API version 3
export ETCDCTL_API=3

# Create backup directory
mkdir -p /opt/etcd-backup

# Take the snapshot
etcdctl snapshot save /opt/etcd-backup/snapshot.db \
  --endpoints=https://127.0.0.1:2379 \
  --cacert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt \
  --cert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt \
  --key=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key

# Verify the snapshot
etcdctl snapshot status /opt/etcd-backup/snapshot.db --write-table

⚠️ CKA Trap

  • Forgetting ETCDCTL_API=3 — version 2 uses different commands
  • Using wrong cert files — server.crt/server.key for etcdctl, NOT peer.crt
  • Not creating the output directory first — the command will fail silently

H.3 etcd Restore [8 min]

📋 Task

A previous etcd snapshot exists at /opt/etcd-backup/snapshot.db. Restore etcd from this snapshot to a new data directory /var/lib/etcd-restored. Then update the etcd static pod to use the restored data directory.

💡 Hints
Approach

Two steps: (1) etcdctl snapshot restore to a new directory, (2) edit /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml to point --data-dir and the hostPath volume to the new path. The kubelet will auto-restart etcd.

✅ Solution
solution-h3.sh 
# Step 1: Restore snapshot to new directory
export ETCDCTL_API=3
etcdctl snapshot restore /opt/etcd-backup/snapshot.db \
  --data-dir=/var/lib/etcd-restored

# Step 2: Edit the etcd static pod manifest
# Change --data-dir flag AND the hostPath volume
sudo vi /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml

# In the container spec, change:
#   - --data-dir=/var/lib/etcd
# To:
#   - --data-dir=/var/lib/etcd-restored

# In the volumes section, change:
#   hostPath:
#     path: /var/lib/etcd
# To:
#   hostPath:
#     path: /var/lib/etcd-restored

# Step 3: Wait for etcd to restart (kubelet detects manifest change)
# Verify:
kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep etcd
kubectl get nodes  # Should show Ready

⚠️ Critical: TWO Changes Required

  • You must change BOTH the --data-dir flag AND the hostPath.path in the volume mount
  • If you only change one, etcd will fail to start and the entire cluster goes down
  • Don't panic if kubectl stops working temporarily — the API server depends on etcd
  • Wait 1-2 minutes for the kubelet to detect the manifest change and restart the pod

H.4 Cluster Upgrade — Control Plane [10 min]

📋 Task

Upgrade the control plane node controlplane from Kubernetes v1.30.0 to v1.31.0. Upgrade kubeadm first, then the control plane components, then kubelet and kubectl. Ensure the node is schedulable after the upgrade.

💡 Hints
Approach

Order matters: (1) upgrade kubeadm, (2) kubeadm upgrade plan, (3) kubeadm upgrade apply, (4) drain node, (5) upgrade kubelet+kubectl, (6) restart kubelet, (7) uncordon. On Debian/Ubuntu, use apt-mark unhold → install → apt-mark hold.

✅ Solution
solution-h4.sh 
# Step 1: Upgrade kubeadm
sudo apt-mark unhold kubeadm
sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get install -y kubeadm=1.31.0-*
sudo apt-mark hold kubeadm

# Step 2: Verify upgrade plan
sudo kubeadm upgrade plan

# Step 3: Apply the upgrade
sudo kubeadm upgrade apply v1.31.0

# Step 4: Drain the control plane node
kubectl drain controlplane --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data

# Step 5: Upgrade kubelet and kubectl
sudo apt-mark unhold kubelet kubectl
sudo apt-get install -y kubelet=1.31.0-* kubectl=1.31.0-*
sudo apt-mark hold kubelet kubectl

# Step 6: Restart kubelet
sudo systemctl daemon-reload
sudo systemctl restart kubelet

# Step 7: Uncordon the node
kubectl uncordon controlplane

# Step 8: Verify
kubectl get nodes
# controlplane should show v1.31.0 and Ready

⚠️ Upgrade Order is Critical

  • Always upgrade kubeadm before running kubeadm upgrade apply
  • Always upgrade kubelet after the control plane components
  • Never skip minor versions — upgrade one version at a time (1.30→1.31, not 1.29→1.31)
  • Don't forget systemctl daemon-reload before restarting kubelet

H.5 Cluster Upgrade — Worker Node [7 min]

📋 Task

Upgrade worker node worker-1 to Kubernetes v1.31.0. The control plane has already been upgraded. Ensure workloads are safely evicted during the upgrade and the node is schedulable afterwards.

💡 Hints
Approach

From the control plane: drain the worker. SSH to the worker: upgrade kubeadm, run kubeadm upgrade node (NOT apply), upgrade kubelet+kubectl, restart. Back on control plane: uncordon.

✅ Solution
solution-h5.sh 
# FROM CONTROL PLANE:
# Step 1: Drain the worker node
kubectl drain worker-1 --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data

# SSH TO WORKER NODE:
ssh worker-1

# Step 2: Upgrade kubeadm
sudo apt-mark unhold kubeadm
sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get install -y kubeadm=1.31.0-*
sudo apt-mark hold kubeadm

# Step 3: Upgrade node config (NOT "apply" — that's for control plane only)
sudo kubeadm upgrade node

# Step 4: Upgrade kubelet and kubectl
sudo apt-mark unhold kubelet kubectl
sudo apt-get install -y kubelet=1.31.0-* kubectl=1.31.0-*
sudo apt-mark hold kubelet kubectl

# Step 5: Restart kubelet
sudo systemctl daemon-reload
sudo systemctl restart kubelet

# Step 6: Exit back to control plane
exit

# FROM CONTROL PLANE:
# Step 7: Uncordon the worker
kubectl uncordon worker-1

# Verify
kubectl get nodes
# worker-1 should show v1.31.0 and Ready

⚠️ Worker vs Control Plane Difference

  • Workers use kubeadm upgrade node — NOT kubeadm upgrade apply
  • You must SSH to the worker to run upgrade commands — kubectl runs on control plane
  • Drain happens from control plane, upgrade happens on the worker node itself

🏷️ Appendix I: Well-Known Labels, Annotations & Taints

APPENDIX 📋 REFERENCE

Complete reference of Kubernetes well-known labels, annotations, and taints that are automatically applied by the system or recognized by core components. Essential for scheduling, topology, and node management — all referenced with the anihpj project context.

🏷️ Node Labels

Auto-populated by kubelet and cloud providers. Used for affinity, topology spread, and zone-aware scheduling.

📝 Annotations

Non-identifying metadata recognized by K8s components. Record change causes, last-applied configs, and tool hints.

🚫 Taints

Applied by node lifecycle controller or kubelet. Control scheduling and eviction — critical for troubleshooting Pending pods.

📋 App Labels

Recommended app.kubernetes.io/* convention used by Helm, ArgoCD, and kubectl for identifying resources.

I.1 Well-Known Node Labels

These labels are automatically populated by the kubelet or cloud provider. They are used by the scheduler for node affinity, topology spread constraints, and zone-aware scheduling.

LabelExample ValuePurposeSet By
kubernetes.io/hostnamenode-1Node hostname — used for per-node affinity/anti-affinitykubelet
kubernetes.io/oslinuxOperating system (linux/windows)kubelet
kubernetes.io/archamd64CPU architecture (amd64/arm64)kubelet
topology.kubernetes.io/zoneus-east-1aCloud availability zone — zone-aware schedulingcloud provider
topology.kubernetes.io/regionus-east-1Cloud region — region-aware schedulingcloud provider
node.kubernetes.io/instance-typem5.xlargeCloud instance type — schedule by machine sizecloud provider
kubernetes.io/metadata.nameanihpjAuto-set to namespace/node name — used in selectorsAPI server
terminal — using well-known labels 
# View all labels on a node
kubectl get node node-1 --show-labels

# Schedule anihpj pods only on Linux amd64 nodes
kubectl get nodes -l kubernetes.io/os=linux,kubernetes.io/arch=amd64

# Find nodes in a specific availability zone
kubectl get nodes -l topology.kubernetes.io/zone=us-east-1a

# Use in node affinity for anihpj deployment
# spec.affinity.nodeAffinity.requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
#   nodeSelectorTerms:
#     - matchExpressions:
#         - key: topology.kubernetes.io/zone
#           operator: In
#           values: ["us-east-1a", "us-east-1b"]

I.2 Well-Known Annotations

Annotations hold non-identifying metadata. These well-known annotations are recognized by Kubernetes components and common tools.

AnnotationUsed OnPurpose
kubernetes.io/change-causeDeploymentsRecords why a rollout happened — shown in kubectl rollout history
kubernetes.io/descriptionAny resourceHuman-readable description of the resource
kubectl.kubernetes.io/last-applied-configurationAny resourceStores the last kubectl apply manifest — used for 3-way merge
scheduler.alpha.kubernetes.io/defaultTolerationsNamespacesDefault tolerations applied to all pods in namespace
storageclass.kubernetes.io/is-default-classStorageClassMarks the default StorageClass ("true")
kubernetes.io/ingress.classIngressLegacy ingress controller selector (replaced by ingressClassName)
node.kubernetes.io/exclude-from-external-load-balancersNodesExclude node from cloud load balancer backends
terminal — working with annotations 
# Record change cause for rollout history
kubectl annotate deployment anihpj-web -n anihpj \
  kubernetes.io/change-cause="Updated to v2.1 with new job filters"

# View rollout history (shows change-cause)
kubectl rollout history deployment/anihpj-web -n anihpj
# REVISION  CHANGE-CAUSE
# 1         Initial deployment
# 2         Updated to v2.1 with new job filters

# Or use --record flag (deprecated but still works)
kubectl set image deployment/anihpj-web django=anihpj/jobpost:v2.2 -n anihpj --record

# Check the default StorageClass
kubectl get storageclass -o json | \
  jq -r '.items[] | select(.metadata.annotations["storageclass.kubernetes.io/is-default-class"]=="true") | .metadata.name'

I.3 Well-Known Taints

Taints are automatically applied by the node lifecycle controller or kubelet. Understanding them is critical for troubleshooting scheduling issues.

Pod Submitted
→
Node Has Taint?
→
Pod Tolerates It?
→
✅ Scheduled
NoSchedule → Blocked
|
NoExecute → Evicted
|
PreferNoSchedule → Soft Avoid
Taint KeyEffectWhen AppliedImpact
node-role.kubernetes.io/control-plane NoSchedule By kubeadm on control plane nodes Prevents workloads on control plane (add toleration to override)
node.kubernetes.io/not-ready NoSchedule + NoExecute Node condition NotReady Stops scheduling; evicts pods after tolerationSeconds
node.kubernetes.io/unreachable NoSchedule + NoExecute Node condition Unknown (network issue) Same as not-ready but for network partition
node.kubernetes.io/disk-pressure NoSchedule Disk usage exceeds eviction threshold Stops new pods from scheduling; does NOT evict existing
node.kubernetes.io/memory-pressure NoSchedule Memory usage exceeds eviction threshold Stops new pods from scheduling
node.kubernetes.io/pid-pressure NoSchedule PID usage exceeds eviction threshold Stops new pods from scheduling
node.kubernetes.io/network-unavailable NoSchedule Node's network is not configured (pre-CNI) Removed once CNI plugin sets up networking
node.kubernetes.io/unschedulable NoSchedule kubectl cordon or kubectl drain Prevents new pods; existing pods continue running
terminal — working with taints 
# View taints on a node
kubectl describe node node-1 | grep -A5 Taints

# Check all taints across all nodes
kubectl get nodes -o json | \
  jq -r '.items[] | .metadata.name + ": " + 
  ([.spec.taints[]? | .key + "=" + (.value // "") + ":" + .effect] | join(", "))'

# Tolerate control-plane taint (e.g., for single-node clusters)
# In pod spec:
# tolerations:
#   - key: "node-role.kubernetes.io/control-plane"
#     operator: "Exists"
#     effect: "NoSchedule"

# Schedule anihpj pods with custom tolerations
kubectl run anihpj-debug --image=anihpj/jobpost:v2.1 -n anihpj \
  --overrides='{"spec":{"tolerations":[{"key":"dedicated","operator":"Equal","value":"anihpj","effect":"NoSchedule"}]}}'

# Remove a taint
kubectl taint nodes node-1 dedicated=anihpj:NoSchedule-

# Common troubleshooting: pod stuck Pending due to taints
kubectl describe pod <pending-pod> | grep -A3 Events
# 0/3 nodes are available: 3 node(s) had untolerated taint {key: value}

Taint Troubleshooting on the CKA

  • ❌ Pod stuck Pending — check kubectl describe pod Events for "untolerated taint" messages
  • ❌ Pods evicted after drain — NoExecute taints evict pods that don't tolerate them. DaemonSet pods tolerate everything by default
  • ✅ Toleration matching — operator: Exists matches any value; operator: Equal requires exact value match
  • ✅ Empty key + Exists — tolerates ALL taints (used by DaemonSets): {"operator": "Exists"}

I.4 Recommended Labels for Applications

These labels follow the Kubernetes recommended labeling convention (app.kubernetes.io/*) and are used by tools like Helm, ArgoCD, and kubectl.

🧬 Anatomy of anihpj Labels

Think of these labels as a hierarchy: part-of (project) → name (app) → component (role) → instance (deployment) → version (release)

part-of: anihpj
→
name: jobpost
→
component: web
→
instance: anihpj-web
→
version: v2.1
LabelExample ValuePurpose
app.kubernetes.io/namejobpostThe name of the application
app.kubernetes.io/instanceanihpj-webA unique instance name (for multiple deployments)
app.kubernetes.io/versionv2.1Current application version
app.kubernetes.io/componentfrontendComponent within the architecture (frontend, backend, db)
app.kubernetes.io/part-ofanihpjThe larger application this is part of
app.kubernetes.io/managed-byhelmTool managing this resource (helm, argocd, kubectl)
anihpj-labels.yaml — recommended labels in practice 
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj-web
  namespace: anihpj
  labels:
    app.kubernetes.io/name: jobpost
    app.kubernetes.io/instance: anihpj-web
    app.kubernetes.io/version: "v2.1"
    app.kubernetes.io/component: web
    app.kubernetes.io/part-of: anihpj
    app.kubernetes.io/managed-by: kubectl
spec:
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app.kubernetes.io/name: jobpost
      app.kubernetes.io/instance: anihpj-web
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app.kubernetes.io/name: jobpost
        app.kubernetes.io/instance: anihpj-web
        app.kubernetes.io/version: "v2.1"
        app.kubernetes.io/component: web
        app.kubernetes.io/part-of: anihpj
💡 Label Quick Reference

app.kubernetes.io/name + app.kubernetes.io/instance together uniquely identify an application deployment. Use part-of to group related components (web, db, cache) under one project name. Helm automatically sets managed-by: Helm and helm.sh/chart: chartname-version.
